<story>
Halfway down the Rue Saint-Denis, near the Rue du Petit-Lion, a charming old house allowed historians to reconstruct Paris. Dilapidated walls exposed timber-and-plaster patterns; a warped triangular roof jutted over the street, sheltering the entrance and dormer. The upper story used overlapping planks to lighten the load on the fragile structure.On a rainy March morning a young man in a cloak stood beneath a shop awning opposite the house, studying it with antiquarian zeal. He ignored the first floor’s narrow boarded windows and examined the third floor’s modest sash windows, dimmed by green-tinted glass. Occasionally he glanced at the shop below, amused by its details: a large wooden beam, supported by four pillars, bore peeling layers of paint and a comical depiction of a cat playing with a racket and an oversized ball. The faded sign read “Guillaume, Successor to Master Chevrel,” a whimsical remnant meant to attract customers.Dressed with stylish care—cloak, white silk stockings and gloves, hair slightly dishevelled as if returning from a ball—he seemed spirited yet calm. Market gardeners hurried by, but the street was unusually still; his pale face, dazzling white cravat and expressive eyes drew the attention of the shopkeepers at the “Cat and Racket.”Suddenly three apprentices at the dormer window teased him, even sprinkling shaving cream; he shrugged off the annoyance. A young girl’s face, pure and unembarrassed like a Raphael Virgin, peered then withdrew, leaving him disappointed. The shop’s shutters opened, a manservant hung the cloth sign, and behind iron bars packages of brown-linen-wrapped cloth were visible. Monsieur Guillaume, old-fashioned but famed for having Paris’s best-stocked cloth house and an honest reputation that earned steady profit, was nevertheless warned about his notary’s notorious discounts.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The old merchant stood at his shop threshold on the Rue Saint-Denis, waking as if from a long slumber. Monsieur Guillaume, in traditional dress, watched a curious stranger who seemed overly interested in his business. His patient, shrewd face showed experience. A strict traditionalist, he lamented lost customs and enforced a rigorous regime for his three apprentices, who feared his constant vigilance.As the apprentices began the day, Guillaume noticed the stranger’s anxious glances and suspected ulterior motives. The eldest, Joseph Lebas, stepped outside and thought he saw Mademoiselle Augustine leaving hastily; he hailed a cab, which eased their worries about a prank on her.Guillaume reprimanded them for idleness, recalling how hard he had worked in youth. Though the apprentices came from fairly comfortable families and paid for their board, Madame Guillaume’s frugality even deprived them of family desserts. He demanded strict industry, contrasting his order and economy with modern shops where young men expected quick wealth. The shop itself was meticulously kept, a testament to his values.The household followed firm traditions: they attended Mass together, and after years of service Joseph earned a modest salary. Guillaume saw Joseph as a fitting husband for his elder daughter Virginie, though Joseph secretly loved the younger Augustine. Virginie resembled her mother in piety and sternness; Augustine, eighteen, was delicate and charming but constrained by maternal rule. Augustine’s yearning for more—fueled by secret novels—deepened Joseph’s affection, while Virginie, lacking suitors, found herself drawn to him. Guillaume mused about his daughters’ futures in a world where suitors might grow scarce.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################From that day the old merchant, grieving his eldest daughter’s decline, remembered marrying Mademoiselle Chevrel under circumstances like Joseph Lebas and Virginie’s. Marrying his daughter would repay a sacred debt—an orphan’s past kindness. Joseph Lebas, thirty-three, acutely felt the fifteen-year gap with Augustine and Monsieur Guillaume’s rule that the elder daughter marry first. The warm‑hearted, tall assistant suffered in silence.How it began: one evening a young painter paused before the dark Cat and Racket and saw a scene any artist would cherish. The unlit shop framed a lamp-lit dining room that gave Augustine—pensive, scarcely touched by food—an almost angelic face. Fresh from Rome and craving simple nature, the painter left overwhelmed, unable to eat or sleep.He returned to his studio resolved to capture that moment, visiting the Cat and Racket repeatedly, sometimes in disguise, to study Augustine. For eight months he devoted himself to love and work, neglecting friends and former pursuits. One morning his friend Girodet pierced his isolation to ask about his Salon submission. The painter revealed two masterpieces; Girodet embraced him, recognizing their depth and asking, “You are in love?” They agreed great work springs from passion, and Girodet warned the public might not grasp these subtle pieces. The artist exhibited them anyway; they caused a sensation. The Interior sparked a genre shift, while the portrait became unforgettable, earning acclaim and offers the painter refused.These public successes felt remote from the monastic life on Rue Saint‑Denis. During a visit, Madame Guillaume mentioned the exhibition, rousing Augustine’s curiosity. With her cousin’s help she obtained permission to visit the Louvre. Seeing the portrait, she trembled—it was herself. The artist stood nearby and whispered, “You see how love has inspired me,” leaving her stunned.Desperate to escape the crowd, she tried to leave, but the throng carried them toward the celebrated painting; overwhelmed, she wept. In a brief silent exchange she gestured to the artist; he acknowledged her with a nod. Guilt and exhilaration followed. The Salon’s chaos and the painter’s gaze awakened feelings she had not known. She feigned a headache to avoid talk of the exhibition; that night she longed for the elegance she’d always desired and felt hope at the thought of being the wife of a genius. The next day her parents returned disappointed—the painter had removed the pictures, and Madame Guillaume had lost her shawl—but Augustine had discovered a new sensitivity.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The morning after a ball, Theodore de Sommervieux—who had won Augustine’s heart—found it hard to declare his love because the household watched her closely. Augustine sat in the counting-house between Mademoiselle Virginie and Madame Guillaume under her mother’s eye, so Theodore bribed a maid to exchange notes and arranged a discreet meeting during Mass at Saint-Leu.The Cat and Racket household was absorbed in stock-taking, with Monsieur Guillaume overseeing inventory while the family verified goods and barely noticed Augustine’s inward turmoil. After weeks of labor the accounts were settled and the family enjoyed a rare evening at Cendrillon while the apprentices took the night off.The next morning Guillaume reviewed the profitable accounts and summoned his assistant Joseph Lebas, proposing to make him a partner. Overwhelmed, Joseph tried to answer but confessed his love for Augustine; Guillaume at first thought he meant Virginie. Corrected, Guillaume agreed to consider Joseph’s feelings and suggested he accompany Augustine to Mass.At breakfast tension rose as Madame Guillaume and Virginie eyed a nervous Joseph. Madame Guillaume, unaware Augustine loved Theodore, chided her for inattention during church and ordered her to her room, prompting a heated parental debate about Augustine’s future. Summoned, Augustine openly declared her love for Theodore and defended his respectable background against Monsieur Guillaume’s old suspicions of artists.Madame Roguin then arrived to report that Theodore had recently received honors and gained wealth, urging the match. Her account swayed the parents. By evening the household shifted from conflict to acceptance: Guillaume informed Joseph, the family celebrated the prospective union, Theodore presented a painting of the Cat and Racket, and Guillaume consented—cautious about finances—while the lovers found solace in mutual affection.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few months after a memorable Sunday, the high altar of Saint-Leu saw two contrasting weddings. Augustine and Theodore glowed with happiness while Virginie, simply dressed, followed her sister. Monsieur Guillaume had wanted Virginie married first, but the priests attended the more elegant bride. Neighbors praised Virginie for a sensible match; others envied Augustine for marrying an artist of rank. An old fan-maker warned such extravagance would invite ruin, and Monsieur Guillaume felt justified in his cautious settlements. After a splendid ball and supper the couples returned home, and the artist carried Augustine to their beautifully adorned apartment.Their first year was blissful. Theodore showered Augustine with affection; she, lost in love, believed her devotion would always charm him. Naïve about the wider world, she spoke the language of love and sometimes revealed her innocence. As a child was expected, Theodore felt the need to return to work. He reconnected with friends and began frequenting the Duchesse de Carigliano’s gatherings, eager for the social validation of being seen with a beautiful wife.Augustine enjoyed society but soon realized she lacked the sophistication of Theodore’s artistic circle. Her admiration for his work was genuine yet superficial, and a disconnect grew. Theodore hoped the environment would inspire her, but she struggled to adapt; her innocence clashed with the more liberated attitudes at gatherings.Over time Theodore’s affection waned, and Augustine felt the weight of his indifference. Three years in, once vibrant, she became consumed by sorrow. Attempts to rekindle his love failed as the artist’s attachment to the Duchesse deepened. Determined, Augustine tried to educate herself in poetry and art, but she could not acquire the natural wit Theodore admired; her earnestness often backfired.In desperation she returned to her childhood home, finding Virginie and Joseph Lebas running their drapery with steady, practical happiness. Joseph’s commerce-minded advice intensified her conflict. Ultimately she returned home, burdened by uncertainty and longing, trying every remedy to heal their fractured marriage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The elderly couple welcomed their daughter Augustine; her visit brightened their monotonous lives. For four years they had drifted, dwelling on past business successes and failures like the Lecocq bankruptcy. Monsieur Guillaume inspected his shop, the Cat and Racket, trading memories with former competitors, while Madame Guillaume—now on the Army's clothing consulting board thanks to her son-in-law—filled their home with extravagant, often tasteless decor. Their nostalgia and stagnation struck Augustine as a sharp contrast to her own turbulent existence, leaving her both proud and appalled.Augustine spoke of recent achievements and aired marital grievances, which her parents eagerly absorbed. Madame Guillaume was especially curious about life with Theodore, Augustine’s husband, a painter. Augustine mentioned his late nights and unconventional habits, prompting her mother’s disapproval of his profession. The couple’s incredulity grew as Augustine defended Theodore, sparking a heated debate about genius and marital responsibility.When Augustine hinted at divorce, her parents were shocked; Monsieur Guillaume offered support. Overwhelmed by their concern and unable to convey the depth of her suffering, Augustine rejected separation, realizing her pain could scarcely be understood by them. She felt isolated, convinced only those with similar experience could empathize.Determined to win back Theodore by other means, Augustine resolved to consult the Duchess de Carigliano—not to reclaim his heart outright but to learn how to captivate him. At the Duchess’s opulent home, Augustine admired the elegance and felt rivalry. The Duchess, beautiful and shrewd, advised a composed facade: cultivate mystery, never reveal the full measure of one’s affection, and use guardedness as strategy. She gave Augustine a portrait Theodore had painted of the Duchess, believing it might rekindle his interest.When Augustine presented the portrait, Theodore’s reaction was fury and jealousy, igniting a violent confrontation that exposed his passion and insecurity. The aftermath left Augustine shattered; her hopes of mending their marriage lay in ruins. The next morning her mother found her consumed by despair amid the remnants of the quarrel. Augustine died young, her fate a poignant reminder of the precarious balance between passion and stability in relationships with artistic genius.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##################################################################### MIDDLE OF STORY############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY. MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Comte de Fontaine, a loyal royalist from Poitou, had served the Bourbon cause during the La Vendée and sacrificed much; his estate was confiscated and he was left ruined. He married Mademoiselle de Kergarouet, a noblewoman without fortune, and after the second revolution moved to Paris with a large family hoping for better prospects. Initially disillusioned, he unexpectedly received honors—a military promotion and royal decorations—which revived hopes for favor.He soon felt his sacrifices little recognized as new aristocrats rose, and though he considered returning to his estate he reentered royal service in the turmoil of 1815. His loyalty was rewarded: he became a trusted adviser to Louis XVIII, valued for wit and political skill. The Fontaines began to prosper—sons won posts and daughters made advantageous, if lesser, matches.Emilie, the youngest, was spoiled and central to Parisian attention. Beautiful and charming, she remained indifferent and scornful of suitors she deemed beneath her, provoking family conflict. The Count’s repeated attempts to secure a suitable husband were frustrated by her caprice and petty dismissals. As the political climate shifted and the King’s health declined, he pressed harder to marry her into respectability; her unreasonable standards and dismissive winter behavior left the family’s future and honor uncertain.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Nature had endowed Emilie de Fontaine with every quality suited to play Célimène. Tall and slender, she could assume dignity or coquettishness; her long neck and arched brows expressed scorn and pride. She had mastered gestures that conveyed contempt or charm and a melodious voice that could win a heart or cut off indiscretion. Her alabaster complexion remained usually calm, roused only by quick flashes of feeling. Jilted admirers accused her of acting a part; she insisted she merely inspired desire and enjoyed subjecting suitors to her whims.Monsieur de Fontaine realized too late that constant adulation had spoiled his daughter. Praise had inflated her pride and self-confidence, fostering the selfishness of a pampered child. Her youthful charms masked these defects; his attempts to teach practical wisdom met only caprice and ironic replies, his counsel sliding off her like water from marble. He came to regard her affections as often condescending, like a child demanding attention before returning to play.Emilie sometimes showed affection for her parents but often withdrew, jealous of any shared fondness even among siblings. She blamed solitude on fate, failing to see that happiness must originate within. Determined to avoid her sisters’ marriages, she nevertheless envied their contentment; her unpredictability led her mother to suspect a touch of madness.Such pride is common among wealthy young beauties, who imagine mothers cannot understand youthful dreams and suspect maternal envy. These conflicts yield secret tears and rebellions against perceived tyranny, and girls devise elaborate life plans, picturing an ideal partner who must meet exacting conditions. With experience, many discover happiness unexpectedly and learn that ideals often fall short.Emilie’s terms for a suitor were rigid: he must be a peer of France, a soldier, and bear an Order so they could be treated with respect; he must also be slender—she deemed corpulence incompatible with romance. Her disdain for those who failed her standards was clear, and Monsieur de Fontaine feared her eccentricities would invite ridicule and leave her indecisive.During the winter of Charles X’s reign he redoubled his matchmaking, hosting gatherings to attract suitors. Though his reputation as a corrupter of legislative integrity grew, he remained earnest. After a season of efforts he felt hopeful, only to meet Emilie’s unyielding whims.One morning he summoned her to discuss her future, arranging his study to present a serious front. Emilie greeted him with a light kiss. He spoke of marriage as essential to her happiness and urged wise choice; she interrupted, recalling their agreement about suitors. He pressed the matter, noting the family’s endeavors; she dismissed his proposals, mocking the idea of settling for anything less than a peer. Exasperated, he declared he had fulfilled his duty and that the choice was hers, warning that marital happiness rests on mutual respect rather than mere wealth or title. Emilie, touched by his sincerity, feigned warmth but kept her pride.At a family gathering she reiterated her refusal to marry except a peer. Her uncle joked she awaited the Duc de Bordeaux’s majority; she forbade further talk.As summer neared, at their country house Emilie sought the anonymity of the Bal de Sceaux. There she noticed a striking young man apart from the crowd, whose elegance stirred her imagination. She approached him; he remained aloof, tending instead to a pale dancer named Clara. Jealous and curious, Emilie followed them. Days later, riding with her uncle and meeting Lady Dudley and Monsieur Vandenesse, she learned the man was not what she had imagined. Disappointed yet still drawn to him, she concealed her feelings as her uncle promised an introduction. That promise intensified her infatuation: her fancy swelled, fueled by imagination and the romance of the unknown, blind to marriage’s complexities and compromises.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Before Emilie woke, her uncle hurried to Chevreuse, recognized the young man he'd insulted, and greeted him with polished old‑court courtesy. He professed contrition—calling himself a vice‑admiral, blaming grog, and insisting, “Shake hands; I’d sooner endure a hundred rebuffs from a Longueville than bring his family regret.” The two rode together; the admiral joked about duels, fired a pistol into a birch to show he feared none, praised the young man’s shooting, and quizzed him on gentlemanly trifles—debts, pleasures, heraldry—teasing about mistresses. Longueville answered with composed worldliness: he paid punctually, lived independently, and showed the polish of a fine education. The Count announced he would introduce him at the Pavillon Planat.At the Villa Planat, the name “Monsieur de Longueville” shifted curiosity to attention. He arrived simply but elegantly, at ease in the Receiver‑General’s house, well spoken and informed—even on naval architecture, a subject the admiral raised. His manners suggested École Polytechnique training; pressed about his profession, he said he had renounced the Engineers to preserve his independence. He declined dinner courteously, citing a delicate sister.Emilie, who had dressed to attract him, found his measured attention both pleasing and wounding. Usually flaunting her charms, she seemed simpler and more natural; when prodded she quipped, “Those who please everybody please nobody,” revealing pride and caution.Over the next fortnight Longueville called repeatedly. His modesty and discretion drew Emilie—partly from pride—and he skillfully evaded questions about his family. In music and conversation he neither revealed nor flaunted his rank; once they sang a duet and company could not tell if he were professional. His reserve both tormented and deepened Emilie’s interest.A dance was arranged to meet his sister Clara. When the siblings arrived, Emilie felt genuine pleasure for Clara but also envy: Clara combined ease with modesty and gently reproved Emilie’s earlier disdain for commoners. Clara’s frank courtesy reassured Emilie, bringing the girls closer.With autumn, Emilie and Longueville’s attachment deepened through small intimacies—aimless conversations, sunsets, daisy‑gatherings, passionate duets—learning each other while withholding much. His silence was both balm and barrier; she cherished his attention and resented the mystery. The household watched and speculated, charmed by his discretion and talent, while unanswered questions about his rank and history lingered.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY. MIDDLE OF THE STORY. MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################For effusive souls, twilight invites reflection on art. One such hour finds a young painter, finishing a large canvas, falling from his ladder, striking his head and losing consciousness.He awakens to the gentle voice of a young woman and her mother, who had rushed to his aid. The painter, Hippolyte Schinner, is immediately struck by the girl's beauty, which matches the feminine ideal he has long imagined. The older woman, resembling a refined marquise, tends him kindly and advises on his recovery. They depart with polite reserve.Afterwards the porter’s wife tells him the visitors are Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother. Hippolyte, newly installed in the attic studio after winning some recognition, is determined to provide better for his own mother and keeps his private life guarded despite growing success.During convalescence he returns repeatedly to the image of the two women, especially the girl, imagining a bond born of shared misfortune. When he is well enough he visits them, finding poverty in their home but feeling empathy rather than contempt. Mademoiselle Leseigneur greets him politely; the contrast between their humble surroundings and the remnant elegance they display—the faded trappings of past luxury meticulously kept—strikes him.Conversation begins stiffly but warms when it turns to art. Madame Leseigneur speaks of a cherished but deteriorating portrait of her late husband, a naval officer. Hippolyte offers to paint a new portrait, both to help and to forge a closer connection; they accept his offer with gratitude, and he senses the emotional weight behind it.As they speak, Adelaide steps out and returns with candles to light the room. An unexpected visitor arrives—a man who knows Adelaide. His presence provokes Hippolyte’s jealousy. The newcomer, an older gentleman in an outmoded coat, combines an air of nobility and libertinism, hinting at a complicated past and revealing layers of the women’s lives.Throughout the evening Hippolyte struggles with admiration for Adelaide and uncertainty about their differing social standings, clinging to the slender hope of connection amid the fraught circumstances.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A remarkable figure stood beside the “Louis XIV’s light infantry” specimen — the Bonapartists’ nickname for those remnants of the Monarchy. Lean and aged, he was a faded echo of the first: a worn coat, dulled powder, a feebler intellect — the living reflection of Rivarol’s quip on Champcenetz. The painter was puzzled: the old man neither spoke nor was spoken to; his relation to the gallant man was unclear — friend, relative, or mere companion — and he answered no curiosity, especially about any past tie to the gallant’s deceased wife.The least dilapidated of the group approached Madame de Rouville, kissed her hand, and sat near her. Adelaide leaned over the old gentleman’s chair in a pose recalling a famous painting, though his familiarity seemed to irritate her. “Are you sulky with me?” he asked, casting a shrewd glance at the painter. “This is our neighbor,” Madame de Rouville introduced Hippolyte, the celebrated painter. The old man acknowledged him, noting Hippolyte’s red ribbon against his own harder-earned honors.Hippolyte, captivated by Adelaide’s beauty, lost himself in admiration and for a moment forgot their squalor. The old gentleman lamented card losses; Adelaide offered help but he insisted she remain where he could see her. When the game ended the gentleman complained about his bad luck, the silent figure echoed the hour like a clock, and Hippolyte left still entranced by the stirrings of love.The next day Hippolyte felt an overwhelming urge to see Adelaide. He hesitated until afternoon, then awkwardly requested the Baron’s portrait. Adelaide, alone, sent him to fetch it, revealing a modesty that spoke more than words. Their glance conveyed what they would not say, and Hippolyte promised a swift return.In the following days their relationship blossomed through laughter and shared thought. Another amateur at the table tried to learn piquet but often lost; meanwhile an unspoken bond between Hippolyte and Adelaide deepened. They became inseparable: Adelaide’s affection grew, Hippolyte’s jealousy flared when the old Count showed undue familiarity, and she fretted that his social life harmed his health. Their love matured without grand gestures, built on trust and delicate exchanges; within two months their lives were intertwined, Hippolyte visiting daily.As the old gentleman’s visits waned, Hippolyte took his place at the card table and shared in the losses. He spent two months on the Baron’s portrait, calling it one of his best; the Baroness’s silence about payment or thanks puzzled him. When Adelaide came to his studio, awestruck by the portrait, their charged moment overflowed with unspoken feeling. Overcome, Hippolyte declared his desire to marry her — at which moment the old vice-admiral arrived, praised the work, and hinted at funds to pay for both portraits.That evening, however, Hippolyte noticed Madame de Rouville’s unusual excitement during cards and began to suspect a gambling habit. After a night of torment he discovered his purse missing and, blinded by jealousy, suspected Adelaide and her mother of theft. Tormented, he confronted them and, seeing Adelaide pale and sad, felt ashamed. Their conversation revealed mutual longing and cleared his doubts. When Adelaide quietly set a new purse before him during the game, his fears dissolved; he impulsively declared his intention to marry her. The vice-admiral returned, announcing a promotion and openly supporting the young couple. The evening ended with unity as families came together, dispelling the last doubts. The tale closes on love restored: trust, misunderstanding, and ultimately a promise of a brighter future.MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In October 1800 a foreigner, Bartolomeo di Piombo, stood outside the Tuileries with his wife and young daughter. Over sixty, grave and troubled, his wife tried to stay calm for their child; their despair drew attention, though his fierce gaze discouraged sympathy.After a moment’s thought Bartolomeo handed a dagger to his wife and declared he would confront the Bonapartes. A soldier stopped him at the palace, demanding a written request. Following a tense standoff, Lucien Bonaparte, minister of the interior, recognized him and brought him to meet his brother Napoleon.Napoleon dismissed his aides and spoke privately. Bartolomeo sought asylum and confessed he had killed the Portas—seven members of a family who had burned his vineyard and murdered his son—saying he had avenged his child. He complained the Bonapartes had been indifferent despite his earlier protection of their family.Now a national leader, Napoleon felt bound by law and could not endorse personal vengeance, yet he acknowledged the Corsican vendetta. He offered Bartolomeo a chance to live in Paris with financial assistance, warning the law would not tolerate private justice. Bartolomeo accepted, pledged loyalty, and appeared hopeful. Lucien, learning they had arrived with nothing, gave them money and promised further help, noting their Corsican property wouldn’t sustain them in Paris. Fifteen years later events made this context necessary.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Servin, an upright, respected artist of about forty married to a general’s dowerless daughter, was the first to open a studio for young girls who wished to paint. Mothers at first accompanied their daughters, then trusted Servin’s methods and sent them alone. He admitted only young ladies from wealthy families—refusing aspiring professionals who would demand more thorough instruction—to avoid class disputes. Careful and exacting, he gained a stellar reputation among the aristocracy; when a girl showed aptitude, mothers recommended him without hesitation. He became synonymous with feminine art as Herbault was with bonnets or Leroy with gowns, and though well connected to Parisian elites he kept an independent, patriotic wit.The studio, arranged for pupils, sat above his apartment; the attic entrance was sealed and reached by a small spiral staircase. The large room, lit by broad panes veiled with green linen shades, had dark-gray walls hung with caricatures and plaster models—a controlled disorder suited to study.On a bright July day the studio hummed with young women at easels, each with distinct expressions and styles. They split into two cliques: daughters of wealthy merchants and the aristocratic set led by Amelie Thirion, vain and pretentious. Among them sat Ginevra Piombo, modestly dressed and somewhat apart, appearing anxious. Though the girls laughed and chatted, Ginevra’s talent and beauty inspired jealousy; to ostracize her, Amelie slid Ginevra’s easel away from the group, provoking whispers that the slight carried political overtones as well.The political climate after the Bourbons’ second return sharpened such tensions. Ginevra, a devoted Bonapartist, met disdain from royalist peers; Amelie’s rebuke was both personal and political. Yet Ginevra remained absorbed in her work.A mysterious sound behind a partition revealed that Servin was sheltering a political fugitive, an officer of the Imperial Guard. Excitement and concern followed; Ginevra chose to protect him, sitting near the hiding place rather than risk his exposure. Madame Servin’s calm arrival suggested she knew of the guest. To mask the faint breathing, Ginevra hummed a Corsican song and sketched the officer’s head with fervor. Servin praised her; curious classmates, including Amelie, were drawn to her sheet, but Ginevra deftly deflected attention.Servin moved among his pupils watching Ginevra. Their shared knowledge created a tacit understanding; her resolve to keep the secret and aid the officer’s escape grew firmer. Outside, political turmoil pressed on, but within the studio the young women navigated rivalries and friendships, divided and united by art, ambition, and allegiance.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the painter Servin and Ginevra believed themselves alone, Servin opened the door to a tall young officer in an Imperial uniform, his arm in a sling and his face pale with pain. He started at Ginevra, but Servin reassured him, introducing her as the daughter of a loyal friend of the Emperor, the Baron di Piombo. Recognizing her patriotism, the soldier visibly relaxed."You are wounded," Ginevra observed."It's nothing, mademoiselle," he said. "The wound is healing."Cries in the street announced a death sentence—"Condemned to death!"—and at the name "Labedoyere" the officer turned ashen and sank onto a stool, despairing that Labedoyere faced execution for their cause while he hid. Ginevra barred his exit and asked bluntly whether he expected to restore the Emperor and could raise again the giant who had failed to maintain himself. Overwhelmed by shame after losing his protector, he declared himself ready to die, insisting an honest man's life was worth more than two traitors', even speaking of selling his life to the executioner.Alarmed, Ginevra offered her savings, explaining her father's wealth and saying he would not blame her for aiding a soldier. She urged patience and escape until the political storm passed, suggesting he serve elsewhere rather than desert. Her practical kindness, framed with maternal grace and hope, moved him. He admired her beauty and called her "an angel of goodness," though thoughts of Labedoyere and the cause returned to torment him. In the ensuing silence their acquaintance deepened into an unspoken bond. When Servin asked if the officer could save Labedoyere, the soldier said he could only avenge him. Ginevra, stirred by the idea of vengeance and recognizing his passionate devotion to Napoleon, felt a magical connection as the sun set on his face and left smiling, a spark of hope kindled between them.The next day she returned to the studio. The officer, now called Luigi, spoke freely of the retreat from Moscow and the disaster at Waterloo; Ginevra listened with growing admiration. Discovering they both spoke Corsican tightened their closeness—shared language and memory reinforcing their bond. Days passed, and Ginevra’s absences from gatherings provoked gossip. Mademoiselle Thirion withdrew from class, attendance declined, and Laure confided that her mother insisted she leave because of the scandal. Ginevra promised they would meet again; Laure departed, and Ginevra felt the loss.Ginevra and Luigi’s intimacy quietly grew into mutual love. When Servin, absorbed in an exhibition, dismissed rumors, Luigi entered and half-jokingly claimed responsibility for the lost students; the painter playfully suggested marriage. Their shared silence that followed was the first open recognition of their love. Servin encouraged their union, and Ginevra pledged to use her father's influence to seek a pardon for Luigi.At home, her parents grew anxious over her late returns. Bartolomeo, devoted and politically loyal, worried for his only daughter. When Ginevra finally came home, her father embraced her with relief; the warm reunion confirmed that her parents remained her greatest supporters.Back in the studio, despite scandal and politics, Ginevra and Luigi’s love, forged in adversity and rooted in shared ideals, became a source of strength that united them against the world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Here she is, my Ginevra, Ginevrettina, Ginevrola, mia Ginevra bella!” the old man cried.“Oh, father, you hurt me!” Ginevra exclaimed, but was soon set down and reassured that it was a jest. The baroness revived; Piombo beamed; the parents seemed recovered.“Now, to dinner!” Piombo announced, taking Ginevra’s hand. When he teased that she spent more time at the studio than with them, Ginevra protested. Her mother hinted at a surprise—perhaps a portrait—but Ginevra, pale and hesitant, confessed she had been busy for another reason: she loved a young man. Her parents were astonished.He is no prince, she said; he had no fortune and was a comrade of Labedoyere, proscribed and sheltered by Servin. Piombo praised Servin yet scolded her for loving any man but her father. Ginevra answered gently that love was not a choice. She reminded him she had borne his long absences while he served Napoleon without complaint; she did not love him only for favors, and his reproaches revealed selfishness. He flared; she replied she did not blame him—each had motives: he for ego, she because, raised under his protection, she sought a husband who could protect her in turn.Piombo bristled at talk of owing benefits. “Oh! stay with us! I cannot bear to have you love another man,” he pleaded. Ginevra assured him she need not leave: they could both love her, and he would come to know the man she intended to make part of the family. Piombo recalled an old chance to marry offered when Napoleon had ordered it, which she had declined to spare him being alone. This enraged him: “You don’t wish to leave me alone, yet you marry! I know you, my daughter; you would cease to love us.” Elisa sat stunned. “We have no longer a daughter; she wishes to marry,” he moaned, half in despair, half in theatrical grief.Ginevra expected fury but found him gentler; she implored him to love her for herself and insisted the man loved her truly and would never make her unhappy. Any comparison set him ablaze: “If he loved you as you deserve, he would kill me; if he does not, I would kill him,” he raged. She met his fury calmly: she loved him, though she felt unworthy; love for her father and for her lover were different feelings that couldn't simply be ranked. Piombo vowed she would not marry him; she vowed she would.The baroness feared ruin. Piombo begged her to say nothing that night and let him think, even kneeling; she refused: “Ginevra Piombo does not break her word.” Elisa urged compromise—“We are sent into the world to marry”—but Piombo insisted no paternal order could be unjust. Ginevra argued a refusal to obey an unjust order was not disobedience, and that forbidding her marriage would only make her hate them. He accused her of not loving them; she denied it, yet said honoring them only while they lived would be empty. Anger cooled; she clung to him, weeping that she could not outlive him. His rage melted into affection; he played with her like a child but still refused any introduction to Luigi.Days passed. Ginevra grew tender, thanking her father as if for consent, singing and playing. A week later her mother whispered she had persuaded Piombo to receive the man. Ginevra, elated, returned home with Luigi on her arm. Luigi, an officer who had fought beside the Emperor, had emerged from hiding after Ginevra’s appeals to the Duc de Feltre restored his name; he was anxious to meet Piombo. His pallor and fear deepened Ginevra’s love.At home Piombo sat stern. Ginevra announced she had brought a soldier who fought at Mont-Saint-Jean. Piombo sneered that the man bore no decoration; Luigi, modest and shy, said he no longer wore such honors. His simple, respectful manner seemed to appease Piombo. Madame Piombo, noting the softening, observed that Monsieur resembled someone known in Corsica—Nina Porta. Luigi confirmed Nina was his sister. Then Piombo asked bluntly, “Are you Luigi Porta?”“Yes,” Luigi answered.The name staggered Piombo and Elisa; they left the room in silence, abandoning Ginevra in shock. The revelation implied a vendetta. Ginevra, too young to have been told the family’s tragic past, listened as her mother revealed that the Portas had once killed Ginevra’s brother and burned their house, and that Piombo had retaliated, massacring their family. Luigi had been tied to a bed before the house was set on fire. He had survived, taken to Genoa at six, raised by an old man named Colonna, warned of enemies, and told to take his present name to elude them; he had no other memory.Ginevra warned Luigi to leave: he was safe only under her father’s roof; once he left, Piombo’s Corsicans would pursue him. She realized their love must be purer and stronger because of the peril. Luigi, shaken, squeezed her hand; she led him to his lodging and saw him safely housed. Returning, she greeted her parents with calm resolve and observed her mother’s tears. They dined in silence; in the dim salon Piombo tried to speak but doubted himself, requesting the fire be lit because he felt cold. Both suffered, pride and pain masking their love.On Ginevra’s birthday the baroness, desperate to heal the rupture, gathered them in Bartolomeo’s study and summoned two notaries with witnesses to perform the legal formalities for Ginevra’s marriage to Luigi Porta. Bartolomeo watched them with foreboding. When the older notary announced their business—to observe the law relative to the daughter’s marriage—Piombo erupted, denouncing any law that would destroy paternal authority or rob an old man of his last consolation.In fury he seized a dagger from the wall and rushed toward Ginevra. Witnesses intervened, but he shoved them aside with the force of a man whose world had toppled. Seeing him advance, Ginevra knelt and implored him. Overcome, he threw the dagger aside. She begged his mercy, kneeling and asking for consent with a trembling voice. Bartolomeo, unable to accept the union, pushed her away: the wife of Luigi Porta could not be a Piombo; she was, in effect, dead to him. “Leave my house, unhappy girl,” he declared, turning her out.Ginevra fled to Luigi’s humble lodging. “My Luigi, we have no fortune but our love,” she said. “Then I am richer than kings!” he replied. She told him her parents had cast her off; he vowed to love her in their place. They resolved to be happy despite the shadow over them—joy and dread mingling as she declared, “Then let us be happy—we WILL be happy!”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day after Ginevra was expelled from her father's house she sought refuge with Madame Servin, who received her coldly, resentful of the trouble Ginevra had caused her husband. Proud but stunned, Ginevra rented a room near Luigi. He devoted himself to her and painted a bright future that lifted her spirits despite her father’s harshness.One morning Ginevra received trunks of essentials from her mother and a loving, sorrowful letter urging her to reconsider the marriage. Deeply moved and tempted to return, she nonetheless abandoned the thought when Luigi arrived; she resolved to bury her grief for the sake of their love.On the wedding day Ginevra was essentially alone. Luigi brought two simple witnesses in ordinary clothes; their quiet presence and Ginevra’s beauty made the mood somber. Passing other celebratory parties only highlighted their difference. At the mayor’s office the ceremony felt like a legal formality: the clerk noted the absence of her parents and confirmed a protest, an oppressive reminder of their disapproval. The ritual was reduced to signatures on a register.After signing, they left with mixed relief and sadness. Luigi expressed gratitude, and Ginevra felt some happiness. In a modest chapel an old priest married them; his remarks about their parents’ absence stung, but a sergeant’s simple blessing comforted her.They moved into a modest apartment Luigi called their true wedding. He had thoughtfully arranged artistic supplies for her; she delighted in the details, and together they found a small paradise in their compact home.At first they indulged in love, but soon had to work. Ginevra copied paintings and found clients; Luigi, after initial struggle, offered copying and handwriting services, then secured enough work to hire assistants. This period, filled with mutual labor and quiet evenings, became the happiest of their lives. Ginevra rarely complained though she sometimes felt pangs of remorse for the family she had left; to mark an anniversary she painted a portrait of herself for Luigi, and another year passed in contentment.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At the beginning of 1819, picture-dealers urged Ginevra to produce originals instead of copies as competition intensified. Madame Porta regretted not taking up genre painting earlier, which might have improved her standing. Ginevra tried portraiture but faced stiff competition from established artists. With only modest savings, she and Luigi grew anxious about their future.As winter progressed, Luigi labored relentlessly against a shrinking market for writing. Unable to afford help, he worked longer hours for the same pay. Ginevra painted several commendable works, but dealers shunned lesser-known artists, and even low prices failed to sell her paintings.Their household deteriorated: love and happiness were eclipsed by looming poverty. They concealed worries from each other—Ginevra lavished affection while Luigi hid his fears—clinging to the hope that love would sustain them. One night Ginevra woke to find Luigi gone and discovered him working late to avoid burdening her. Moved, she took up coloring lithographs, laboring through the night while Luigi toiled in his garret. Both pretended to sleep to spare the other their anxieties.On another sleepless night, feverish and exhausted, Luigi opened his window and saw Ginevra’s lamp across the courtyard, realizing she too was working. Confronted, she said she could not be idle while he suffered. They shared a tender moment, watching the dawn together and feeling hopeful.Their struggles deepened when Ginevra gave birth to a son, Bartolomeo. The joy of parenthood briefly masked their financial burdens, but soon they began selling possessions and jewels. Poverty stripped them of comfort and pride, escalating their desperation. Months later, frail and worn, Ginevra struggled to care for her sickly child. Luigi, feeling helpless, sought aid but found none; he sold Ginevra’s paintings and remaining furniture for a meager sum. Ginevra showed remarkable strength, managing the household and often starving herself to provide for Luigi.One evening she offered Luigi her last piece of bread; realizing her sacrifice, he left to find help and decided to sell himself as a military substitute. After securing an advance, he hurried home, only to find Ginevra asleep with their child. When he roused her, Bartolomeo was cold and lifeless. In delirium Ginevra spoke her love and sorrow, then died in Luigi’s arms.As Luigi mourned, Ginevra’s parents—who had not seen her in years—reflected on her absence. Elisa Piombo hoped to rekindle Bartolomeo’s love for Ginevra, but a messenger soon revealed the tragedy. The old couple was shattered; the cycle of suffering had claimed another victim, leaving only memories and grief.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY #### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #### ######## MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Many tales keep their charm, but some human experiences demand a delicate touch. Certain details—almost anatomical—require nuance. Portraits must carry a soul; some truths are expressed only through secret harmonies shaped by time, circumstance, or inner moral inclination.These revelations are needed for this simple story, offered to the grave and reflective who find sustenance in tender emotions. If the writer, like a surgeon at a dying friend’s side, feels reverence for the subject, should not the reader share it? Those who have mourned a dear one, sit alone, or watch the day decline may continue; others should set this book aside. Readers who have never buried a beloved will not grasp these pages, which will seem fragrant to some and colorless to others. The reader must have known the luxury of tears and the silent pangs of memory. The writer assures this is a true story, worthy of feeling if the reader possesses it.In contemporary France, speech reflects as many idioms as there are individuals; it is amusing to hear differing interpretations of the same person from various Parisians. Madame Firmiani, for example, is sketched in many partial but revealing ways:- The Practical man: she is wealthy—fine house in the rue du Bac, well-furnished salons, an income of one hundred thousand francs, a husband once receiver-general.- The Lounger: values her exclusive, respectable salon and excellent tea.- The Egotist: dismisses such gatherings as trivial.- The Student: finds her enchanting and eager to introduce you.- The Fop: sees faded beauty but lingering charm.- The Connoisseur: notes a fine collection of paintings.- The Spiteful woman: calls her a dangerous siren.- The Attache: remembers her beauty from ten years earlier.- The Savvy observer: points to connections with the elite despite modest household circumstances.- The Duke: claims not to know her, recognizing only court-presented names.- The Ninny: wrongly insists she used to sing at the Opera.- Two old ladies: discuss her ties to the noble Cadignan family.- The Original: assures you her salon is safe—any scoundrels are of quality.- Another observer: depicts a beautiful woman who rarely rises to greet guests, married to a mythical husband, admired by many but favored by none.- The Contradictor and the Envious: spread rumor (Murat’s mistress) or reduce her wealth to coquetry that ruined a young man’s prospects.Each portrait is partial: observers measure property, hospitality, intellect, romance, faded beauty, art, rivalry, memory, social ties, court recognition, ignorance, lineage, and rumor. Each view reveals as much about the speaker’s habits and biases as it does about Madame Firmiani. In sum, she appears complex, refracted through many lenses, inviting readers to consider these interpretations while reflecting on reverence, loss, memory, and the quiet harmonies that connect them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In January 1824 opinion about Madame Firmiani was so divided that no clear portrait emerged: widow or wife, wise or foolish, virtuous or not, rich or poor, beautiful or plain. Such conflicting views showed how society distorts reputation, like lithographic blocks producing different images from slight variations.That winter Monsieur de Bourbonne, an elderly Touraine landowner, became intrigued. Practical and observant from managing an estate, he came to Paris to learn more, but gossip only deepened his confusion. A man of honor, he valued his nephew Octave de Camps and resented that Octave had sold an estate without consulting him. Worse, a relative claimed Octave had squandered his fortune on Madame Firmiani and was reduced to teaching mathematics—a report that alarmed Monsieur de Bourbonne.Refusing to assume the worst, he resolved to investigate. Using connections in the faubourg Saint-Germain and assuming the name de Rouxellay, he sought the truth of Madame Firmiani’s relation to Octave. Choosing a moment when Octave would be occupied, he soon confirmed that Octave’s financial troubles were real.De Rouxellay was no simple provincial: a former member of the King’s Guard, he knew society’s manners. Entering Madame Firmiani’s salon with the Marquise de Listomère, he lamented how a woman of such luxury could be linked to a man in such straits. Yet on seeing her his anger softened. Thought to be twenty-five but nearer twenty-eight, married at sixteen, she combined beauty, wealth, musical talent, intelligence, and wit. Admired even by rivals, her popularity invited envy and malicious gossip—transforming success into fodder for unfounded rumors and revealing a complex, intriguing nature.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The former guardsman lingered after the other guests had left, and Madame Firmiani found him still sitting before her; the clock struck two.“Madame,” he began as she rose, “I am the uncle of Monsieur Octave de Camps.”She sat again, affected. Monsieur de Bourbonne, despite his experience, could not tell whether her pallor was shame or pleasure.“Well, monsieur?” she asked.“People in my province say my nephew has ruined himself for you, living in a garret while you enjoy wealth. Forgive my rustic frankness; you should know these rumors.”“Stop, monsieur,” she interrupted, hand raised. “I know of those rumors. You are too polite to press me if I ask you not to, and too gallant not to admit you have no right to question me. It would be absurd to defend myself. Believe me: I scorn money; if I married into wealth it was not for its sake. Octave’s fortune or poverty matters not: I welcome him as a friend, and I cannot accept anyone I do not esteem. My guardian angel has kept me from gossip.”Her tone rose then softened into composed teasing.“Madame,” Monsieur de Bourbonne said, trembling slightly, “I am almost a father to Octave. I implore your pardon for the question I will ask. I swear as a gentleman your answer will stay here.” He placed his hand on his heart. “Do you love Octave?”“To another man I would answer with a look,” she replied. “To you, I ask what you would think of a woman who answered such a question. To confess love is an effort, a reward for him. To say it to another—” She stopped, rose, bowed, and withdrew to her private rooms, leaving the uncle bewildered.“Ah! the mischief!” he thought. “What a woman!” He left in his hired coach.Next morning at eight Monsieur de Bourbonne climbed to Octave’s garret. The young professor, who had been up late, was astonished to see him so early.“You rascal!” the uncle began, sitting down. “Since when is it fashionable to mock uncles with twenty-six thousand francs a year? Have I not been good to you? I’m making you an estate in Touraine. And what do you do? Sell your property and live like a lackey! Have you been gambling? Lost at the Bourse? Say ‘Uncle, I’m a wretch,’ and I’ll hug you. But if you lie, I’ll sell my property and go back to my youth.”“Uncle—” Octave began.“I saw your Madame Firmiani yesterday,” the old man continued. “She is charming. You have my approval if that helps. But have you ruined yourself for her?”“Yes, uncle.”“Ha! I’d have wagered it.” He laughed. “In my day women ruined men cleverly; this one is of another order.”“My dear uncle, here is a letter that explains nearly everything.” Octave took a letter from the elegant portfolio Madame Firmiani had given him. “Read it and I’ll tell you the rest. You’ll know a Madame Firmiani unknown to the world.”“I don’t have my spectacles; read it aloud.”Octave read, faltering with feeling: “‘My beloved—’” The uncle interrupted, surprised by the intimacy.“You are still intimate with her?” he asked.“Of course. We are married.”“Heavens!” cried the uncle. “Then why the garret?”“Let me finish.” Octave continued: “‘My beloved Husband, you ask why I am sad. I cannot deceive you; a wife should be gay and caressing. I long to love you forever; a woman’s glory is in the man she loves. But a tale you told tarnished my joy. I feel humiliated by you—whom I thought most honorable—because of your father’s having unjustly deprived others of their property. I must have deep confidence to avow this: how can you knowingly keep such property? I have wept to feel that I have more conscience than love. If you had committed a crime I would hide you from justice, but my devotion could go no farther. Love, for a woman, is boundless confidence plus reverence. I have never conceived of it otherwise. Come to me poor, and my love shall be redoubled. If not, renounce it. Consult your conscience. An act of justice should not be a sacrifice made to love. I am your wife, not your mistress. If I am mistaken, if you can rightly claim the property, reflect and decide from your own conscience. I know I have preached; scold me if you will.’”“What!” the uncle exclaimed. “Surely you didn't tell her about your father’s affair with the Bourgneufs? Women know more about wasting a fortune than making one.”“They know about integrity,” Octave answered, and resumed reading a passage in which she insisted that ill-gotten gains, whether five francs or five times a hundred thousand, were equally dishonoring. She begged him to act from conscience.“There’s more; finish it,” Monsieur de Bourbonne urged.“Oh, the rest is for lovers’ eyes,” Octave smiled.After a moment, Octave explained. The letter had changed him. He felt ashamed of living on his father’s questionable gains—“riding someone else’s horse, eating food not honestly mine.” He rushed to Madame Firmiani; together they calculated what was due the Bourgneufs and condemned themselves to pay interest. What he had was insufficient; she offered her savings, which he accepted.“What! Besides her other virtues, does that adorable woman save money?” his uncle exclaimed.“Don’t laugh; her position forced her to be careful. Her husband went to Greece in 1820 and died three years later. Legal proof of his death and his will—in which he left everything to her—were lost or stolen amid the Greek troubles; records there are not like France’s. Unsure of her title, she lived prudently. I want to buy property of my own to provide for her if she loses hers.”“But why didn’t you tell me? I love you enough to pay such debts. I’ll play the traditional uncle and help!”“Ah! uncle, I want to be rich by my own industry. If you help, let it be an allowance of two or three thousand francs a year until I find a small enterprise needing capital. I teach myself to live honestly. If you’d seen my happiness in making restitution! The Bourgneufs lived miserably: an old father working as a lottery agent, daughters tending house by lamplight, a sick mother. My visit was like bringing Providence. My conscience felt cleansed; my father, if there is a Paradise, is happy there. Madame Firmiani gives me more than happiness; she inspires a delicacy of feeling I lacked. I call her my dear conscience. I find honesty profitable; I shall get rich by my own industry. I have an industrial scheme and, if it succeeds, shall earn millions.”“Ah! my boy, you have your mother’s soul,” said the old man, eyes filling with memories.At that moment, despite the garret’s distance from the street, a carriage halted.“There she is!” he exclaimed. “I know her horses by the way they pull up.”Madame Firmiani entered.“Ah!” she said, surprised to see Monsieur de Bourbonne. “But our uncle is not in the way,” she added quickly, smiling. “I came to humbly entreat my husband to accept my fortune. The Austrian Embassy has sent me a document proving Monsieur Firmiani’s death and the will, which his valet kept safe for me. Octave, you can accept it all; you are richer than I, for you have treasures here”—she laid her hand on his heart—“to which none but God can add.” Overcome, she laid her head on his breast.“My dear niece,” said the old man, “in my day we made love; in yours, you love. You women are the best in humanity; you are hardly guilty of fault, for they come through us.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Middle of the story — the central, pivotal moment.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Rue du Tourniquet-Saint-Jean, a dark winding lane ending at Rue Martroi, was shut by a turnstile until 1823. Under six feet wide, it gathered gutter water and mud; sunlight touched it briefly, and dampness lingered. Residents trimmed lamps in summer, kept them burning through winter. Passing the Marais felt like moving through cellars, and at the corner of Rue de la Tixeranderie iron rings from safety chains jutted from the walls.A corner house built to combat damp—raised cellars, three steps up—had a closed-arch door crowned by a carved keystone and three high iron-barred windows. Through them passersby glimpsed two beds and an old woman tending a brazier, preparing a simple porter’s stew. The kitchen was sparse but kept clean and thrifty.The old woman, pale and wrinkled, sat by the hearth with resignation while her daughter worked at an embroidery frame. The mother, with slow fingers and worn spectacles, often glanced at the street; the daughter bent over her work with a downcast gaze. Their home mirrored working life, yet the girl kept youthful charm—rosy cheeks and bright eyes—while the mother dreamed of a better future for her.In August 1815 the mother noticed a new face: a tall thin man in black, solemn—the sort she guessed worked in the Prefect’s office. Excited, she awaited his return. When he returned, Caroline, usually indifferent, felt sudden sympathy at the suffering in his face and began to look forward to his passing, as if he might alter her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For over two months the “Black Gentleman” behaved oddly, no longer passing the Rue du Tourniquet at his usual hour. Madame Crochard noticed his distracted air—he was often lost in thought and seldom saw her or her daughter, Caroline. One mid-September morning Caroline’s liveliness caught him; he paused to watch her at the window, and a silent exchange of furtive glances began, signaling a growing understanding.After that he came twice daily, and their brief meetings became a quiet routine. Their guarded exchanges showed mutual concern: Caroline sensed his troubles, and he knew when she worried about work. Both remained cautious, fearing closer ties might lead to complications; Madame Crochard, frustrated by Caroline’s indecision, grew displeased with the Gentleman.As winter neared, family hardships weighed on Caroline. Hearing a revealing mother-daughter conversation one night, the Gentleman was moved to leave them money in secret; Caroline found it the next day, and a wordless gratitude passed between them.In early May Caroline suggested a trip to Montmorency, and the Gentleman—more somber than usual—accompanied them. During the carriage ride Madame Crochard feigned sleep, allowing a light conversation that brightened him and changed how he saw Caroline. Sharing personal stories—her sacrifices, his own—deepened their bond. The day brimmed with laughter, and as they left they playfully held hands, a discreet yet pivotal sign of unspoken promises and hope for a brighter future together.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The adventures of Roger and Caroline continue in modern Paris, where new apartments suit honeymooners. In a pristine building on Rue Taitbout, Caroline has lived over a month in a charming second-floor apartment, tastefully furnished in English style. Her bedroom, with gray hangings edged in green silk, cozy seating, and a writing table for love letters, is warmed by a bronze clock showing Love crowning Psyche and a Gothic-patterned carpet. As she prepares for Roger’s visit, her hairdresser flatters her and confesses admiration.On a chilly September day in 1816, Caroline slips into a green grenadine gown trimmed with chinchilla and watches from the balcony, heart racing. After a quarter-hour she sees Roger approaching; they embrace passionately after three days apart. He then must attend a meeting with the Attorney-General, disappointing her. She protests the theatre without him, reaffirms her devotion, vows to be Caroline de Bellefeuille and help the poor, plays a sonata, and watches him drive away, longing for his return.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years after settling on Rue Taitbout, twenty-four-year-old Mademoiselle Caroline de Bellefeuille is quietly content. Embracing motherhood, she spends her days with her four-year-old son Charles and infant daughter Eugénie, caring little for luxury or social ambition and remembering happy visits to Bellefeuille with Roger, whose secrecy she wonders at. On May 6, 1822—an anniversary she marks—Roger arrives with a document detailing Eugénie’s marriage portion, sparking a discussion about unequal provisions: Roger insists Charles should learn self-reliance while Eugénie, as a girl, must be more securely provided for. Their warm evening ends with a lantern show for Charles, but Roger is soon called away on duty.Shortly after, Caroline hurries to her mother, Madame Crochard, gravely ill. Unaware acquaintances—who do not know Caroline is her daughter—summon a priest for last rites while scheming old women hope to profit from the widow’s wealth. Caroline’s devoted care contrasts with their greed. In her final moments Madame Crochard laments their estrangement and reveals the priest pressed her to confess about Caroline’s benefactor, hinting at a tangle of relationships and secrets.A flashback to November 1805 explains some connections. Young barrister Monsieur de Granville, returning from a gathering at the High Chancellor, faces a career opportunity aided by an uncle but receives a letter urging him home: a proposed marriage to Mademoiselle Angélique Bontems would secure his future despite the Bontems’ social stigma. He recalls a childhood fondness for Angélique, travels to Bayeux where his father escorts him to the piously strict household, and, though dreading the match, glimpses Angélique in church—her beauty rekindling his attachment and prompting him to approach her. The intertwined stories probe love, duty, and social expectation as characters confront desire and obligation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For about two weeks after the Vicomte de Granville’s engagement to Mademoiselle Bontems, he frequented her dreary drawing-room and watched Angelique mark her trousseau. She never initiated religious talk; when Granville playfully handled her rosary or mocked practice she quietly returned it to its bag with a serene smile. Firmly believing in the Church, she insisted a mother must have a religion and questioned how one could judge between disbelievers and God. Granville, captivated by her conviction, sometimes mistook desire for love. Angelique, reconciling feeling with duty, left him unsure of his own sentiments. He thanked Madame Bontems for instilling religion in Angelique, thinking it would sustain her in adversity.At the marriage-contract signing Madame Bontems made Granville promise to respect Angelique’s religious practices and freedom of conscience; the Abbe Fontanon approved. They married and moved to Paris, where Granville became public prosecutor. Angelique persuaded him to take a ground-floor apartment in the Marais, near a church and chapel, arguing it would be good for future children. Granville, who preferred a more fashionable district, yielded and concentrated on his work, leaving decoration to Angelique, who delighted in furnishing their new home. He soon found the apartment cold and solemn, reflecting her austere upbringing.Though he tried to understand her choices, Granville grew frustrated by the lack of warmth and charm and attributed the sober decor to her Bayeux background. He attempted to comfort Angelique, believing happiness would come from her love and gentleness rather than material comforts. From April through the winter their life seemed outwardly harmonious: Granville kept busy with work, accompanied her to church on Sundays, and failed to notice many strict observances she enforced—Lenten meals and other austerities she passed off as carelessness.In November 1808 the Canon of Bayeux, who had once guided Angelique, arrived in Paris alarmed and sought to reclaim her from what he saw as laxity; she instead adopted stricter practices and began avoiding social obligations, especially balls, which enraged Granville. After a tense evening he confronted her; arguments escalated into theology. Angelique wrote to the Pope, whose reply sided with Granville—saying a wife should accompany her husband even if it risked compromising her virtue—but she and her director dismissed it.Frustrated, Granville asserted his preferences—ordering meat daily despite her objections—and withdrew from social life, feeling weighed down by her bigotry. The household grew oppressive; Angelique’s beauty was overshadowed by austerity, duty replaced affection, and the children suffered. Granville sent his sons to school early, resolved to marry off his daughters, and sought refuge in work, while Angelique tried to convert him. Their relationship deteriorated, leaving their home a place of coldness and rigidity rather than warmth and companionship.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between 1806 and 1825 the de Granville household displayed little outward drama but suffered deep estrangement. Madame de Granville, unchanged after losing her husband's affection, devoted herself to Masses and prayer to restore their relationship. Granville, a prominent judge, withdrew to the entresol of their house to avoid her; every morning a housekeeper relayed messages while he habitually declined breakfast, citing urgent court business. At thirty-five she looked older and made narrow-minded remarks meant to engage him. When he refused the presidency of a provincial court, friends and her confessor suspected an illicit affair—an idea she found inconceivable.In Lent of 1822 her health declined with stress and a low fever, worsened by Granville’s indifference. She tried to be more affectionate, but her piety and rigidity undermined her efforts. In late May she consulted the Abbé Fontanon, who revealed that Granville had lived with a concubine for seven years, fathering two children and squandering much of their fortune. Stunned and torn between faith and betrayal, she struggled to accept the news. The Abbé urged forgiveness and patience, but she left home determined to confront him.At that moment Roger and Mademoiselle de Bellefeuille were in a tense exchange—she declared hatred for Roger’s wife and blamed her for their unhappiness; Roger spoke of a future together. Their conversation was interrupted when Madame de Granville fainted. Revived, she demanded whether Granville loved her and had been faithful. He retorted that she had sacrificed his happiness to piety and failed as a loving wife, arguing love needs sacrifice and understanding. He admitted seeking happiness elsewhere and acknowledged his affection for another, leaving her devastated. The confrontation exposed the irreparable gulf: their marriage was effectively over, and Madame de Granville was left to face the consequences and an uncertain future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Early in December 1833 a white-haired, worn man, aged more by grief than years, paced the Rue Gaillon at midnight. He stopped before a modest two-story house; a dim light flickered in the attic. A young physician, Horace Bianchon, recognized him and approached.The Comte de Granville answered Bianchon’s teasing offers of escort with a weary pride in his fifty-five years and a touch of melancholy. He confessed he had been loitering to watch a shadow at a third-floor window, indulging idle conjectures. For nine years he had ceased to expect affection from people; life’s events had taught him to attach himself only to animals, plants, and superficial pleasures. He declared himself bored and incapable of interest in human feeling.Bianchon pressed him, noting that such indifference was a malady he could treat. Granville scoffed but allowed the doctor to speak. Bianchon described the garret’s occupant: a woman of about thirty whose love had become fanaticism for a young scoundrel named Solvet—a gambler and drunkard, morally ruined, and perhaps criminal. This woman, Caroline Crochard, had sacrificed ease, a devoted husband, and the father of her children to him; she had let him squander her savings, even the money for her children’s clothes and food. She had worked and sold her hair to support him; her children’s cries had broken her heart; she was ill and, that evening, her house had nothing to eat. Bianchon had been up to her garret and found the children too weak to cry.Granville, despite himself, put his hand into his waistcoat pocket. Bianchon’s story moved him: the living feeling in Caroline stirred a longing in Granville for what his life lacked. Yet his bitterness rose. He insisted he would not give a sou to rescue her; he would not risk hope or compassion again. He mocked the idea that his children might care for him, recalling their positions and suggesting they were likely thinking of his money. He admitted to helping others for his own sensations, like gambling for a thrill; gratitude was not his expectation. He said he had grown old in a day and would attach himself only to things that could not betray him.At the name Caroline Crochard Granville shuddered violently and gripped Bianchon’s arm. When Bianchon confirmed the woman’s name and that the lover was Solvet, Granville’s restraint snapped: he called the emotion this knowledge aroused “a gift from hell” and spoke of paying debts to that hell. They reached the Rue de la Chaussée-d’Antin where a shriveled night scavenger stood by the curb. Granville asked if scavengers ever found large banknotes and if they returned them. When the man replied that it depended on the reward, Granville handed him a thousand-franc note with grotesque instructions: spend it at the wine-shop, get drunk, fight, beat your wife, black your friends’ eyes—create trouble to keep the city’s servants of order busy. With this cynical, almost theatrical gift, Granville claimed he had “squared accounts with hell” and had bought himself some pleasure.He then told Bianchon that Caroline might starve listening to her children’s cries, convinced of her lover’s baseness, and that he would not aid her; because Bianchon had helped her, Granville declared he would see the doctor no more. He left abruptly and took a carriage to his house on the Rue Saint-Lazare.At home his servant announced that his son, the attorney-general, had been there an hour earlier and was waiting in the bedroom. Granville dismissed the servant and confronted his son with a brusque question about why he had broken the order forbidding the children to visit unbidden. The younger man, tremulous, explained that a very young man arrested that afternoon for robbing a friend had claimed to be Granville’s son, and that the man’s name was Charles Crochard.Granville waved the name away at first, then, after a pause, turned mild and fatherly. He admitted Charles had spoken the truth. The revelation transformed his tone; he was glad his son Eugène had come. He handed Eugène a considerable bundle of banknotes and entrusted him with discretion and whatever arrangements were needed. He revealed that he might be leaving France: he would ask the King the next day to dismiss him from his post and was going to Italy.Granville then gave paternal counsel. He said a father owes his children not an account of his life but the experience Fate sells dearly; that he must warn them before marriage, the most momentous act demanded by society. He urged Eugène to study the character of any woman he might marry and to consult him; lack of union between husband and wife inevitably leads to misfortune and punishment, he insisted. He promised to write about such matters from Florence and closed with a final, tender gesture: asking to be kissed by his son and saying farewell, as if they might part for the last time.Throughout, Granville embodied a mingling of hauteur and vulnerability: bitterly disillusioned, capable of cruelty to others and himself, yet capable too of an involuntary tenderness when confronted with the possibility of filial connection. Bianchon’s tale of Caroline Crochard had momentarily roused in him the very feeling he condemned. His reaction—refusing help yet secretly moved enough to waste money on a night scavenger’s riot—revealed both the depth of his despair and the theatricality of his attempts to master emotion. His final exchange with Eugène suggested that despite his proclamations of indifference, he still wished to transmit what he had learned and to salvage some dignity and guidance in his declining years.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY, CONTINUED HERE. ########### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In late November 1809, at the height of Napoleon’s empire and amid the aftershocks of Wagram, Austria still felt the war’s reverberations while peace talks continued. Napoleon basked in power and Parisian spectacle around his marriage to an Austrian archduchess. The newly ostentatious French aristocracy—diamonds and gold on women and uniforms—contrasted sharply with the Republic’s former austerity.
