The atmosphere was intoxicating: military leaders indulged in wealth earned by countless soldiers’ sacrifices, and society’s hedonism, whether revived from the fallen monarchy or urged by the Imperial family, seemed to foreshadow disaster.Women adopted a frivolous, Louis XV–like ease and a frenzy for military men, since frequent calls to arms made peace feel provisional and could turn mistresses into widows within weeks. Soldiers’ appeal rested on the grim prospect of early widowhood, the lure of historical renown, and the romantic ideal of courage; bravery became a prized trait, often assumed to remain secret and sealed on the battlefield. Alongside valor ran a mania for glittering possessions—diamonds, fireworks, flamboyant ornamentation—with figures like Murat setting extravagant examples.Comte de Gondreville, once Citizen Malin, had risen socially and delayed a grand entertainment to outshine rivals and impress Napoleon. His salons filled with ambassadors, influential men, and princes expecting the Emperor, who was absent following a private quarrel with Josephine that hinted at divorce. The most beautiful women of Paris flocked there: the financial elite used balls to present daughters to Napoleon’s circle, masking ambition beneath smiles while distrusting acquaintances as competitors for wealth and favor.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################These remarks are necessary to explain the incidents surrounding the little imbroglio that is the subject of this study, reflecting the tone prevalent in Paris drawing-rooms at the time.The scene opens with a group of men at a Gondreville ball, staring toward a candelabrum where a small, pale young woman sits in a corner. Her hair is à la Chinoise, adorned with bluebells; her almond-shaped blue eyes seem made for tears. She leans forward to watch Madame de Vaudremont below the crowd. Conversation immediately turns to guessing who she is. One man remarks on her exquisite complexion and delicate shoulders; another calls her either a prude or a coquette. They have all noticed her isolation—buried in a gloom of candles and surrounded by dazzling coquettes—and they wonder how anyone could spot her.Speculation runs through the group. Is she an aristocrat? A widow? A companion? Montcornet, the Colonel of Cuirassiers, teases, proposes various romantic backstories—widow of a gambler, German princess—while Martial, a young lawyer (Maitre des Requetes), analyzes her face with the dry connoisseurship of a man used to judging social signs: too innocent to be married, yet saddened as if already in love. The conversation is more about the men’s curiosity and rivalry than about the woman herself; each interpretation of her looks reveals the observer’s prejudices.The lawyer and the Colonel exchange barbs about rivalries and conquests. Montcornet jibes at Martial for behaving like a lover, reminding him of his impending marriage to the young widow Madame de Vaudremont, who brings four thousand napoleons a year—and who, Montcornet hints, slipped a diamond ring on Martial’s finger. Martial bristles at the advice but hides his ambitions beneath a cavalier demeanor; he is a diplomat by temperament, adept at masking desire with the show of indifference. Montcornet warns Martial not to flutter about the unknown lady; Martial answers with the boldness of a man trained to seize opportunity. The tone is light, mocking, but edged with the anxieties of men who measure their standing by conquests and by how they are seen in society.Their whispered conversation, taking place in the interval between dances, is visible to Madame de Vaudremont herself and to the unknown stranger—those silent observers whose light scrutiny is one of the petty enjoyments of idle social life. To understand why the chatter matters, we must follow an incident that binds these characters: the entrance, at around eleven, of Madame de Vaudremont and the Comte de Soulanges, which stirs the assembly.Madame de Vaudremont, the queen of fashion, makes it a rule to arrive at the precise hour when a ball is at its most brilliant, avoiding the later slump when dresses and faces lose their freshness. She enters with calculation, instantly scanning the room to ensure she outshines the other women. On this occasion she arrives with the Comte de Soulanges, a brave colonel of the Guards’ Artillery favored by the Emperor. Their joint appearance causes an immediate stir—some rise, men press forward—and the pair are observed like a spectacle. Soulanges, pale and nervous, is not outwardly handsome but has vivacious, black eyes; he gives off a reserved intensity that presages capabilities beyond his present posture. Madame de Vaudremont, tall and somewhat plump but exquisitely carried, embodies the cultivated charm that enlists affection rather than astonishes.Martial, who aspires to ambition disguised as indolence, feels a prick of jealousy at their entrance. He approaches Madame de Vaudremont with a bow; Soulanges casts Martial a covert glance and then, suddenly flushing scarlet, moves away toward a cardroom. The company assumes Soulanges has been humiliated, perhaps forced to withdraw from a contest for the Countess’ favor. Martial, seated by Madame de Vaudremont, fails to hear her whispered request not to wear that ring she had given him—she hints at private reasons and asks him for his arm to go to the Princess de Wagram’s. Martial doesn’t respond as he is distracted by the unknown lady. The Countess observes Martial’s furtive looks with unease.While Martial yields more and more to the charm of the fair stranger, the Colonel of Cuirassiers, unable to bear being outdone, threads through the crowd to the chair beside her. Too gentlemenlike to address her at once, he engages the woman on her left to coax the stranger into conversation. The woman to Montcornet’s left is only a screen; the lady in blue remains absorbed in watching Madame de Vaudremont and has tears in her eyes. When finally addressed, she answers in a mournful, measured way: yes, she is married; yes, her husband is present; she does not intend to dance. The Colonel’s bland compliments and offers to take her as his partner are met by the quiet, sad refusal.Martial, watching this from the dance, smiles, stroking his chin and showing the diamond on his finger—an emblem of the tie between him and Madame de Vaudremont that he still thinks secure. The stranger, however, notices the ring and asks the Colonel who the owner is. When told it is Martial de la Roche-Hugon, one of Montcornet’s intimate friends, she thanks him and comments on Martial’s agreeable appearance. The Colonel confesses Martial’s fickleness and remarks on a rumor that Madame de Vaudremont had long been devoted to Soulanges—though lately, the Countess has been inattentive to the Count. The unknown instantly pales and recovers, but believes Soulanges’ public reaction at their entrance may explain something of her own interest in him.The quadrille ends; the men draw aside to breathe fresh air in a window-bay and trade wagers. Montcornet, moral and conventional, declares that because the stranger is married he will not pursue her—he is a decent man. Martial, more daring, bets a hundred napoleons to the Colonel’s grey horse that the stranger will dance with him that evening, and the Colonel, confident he has been properly rebuffed, accepts. There is also the confession that the stranger seems fond of diamonds—a hint at motives that, to men like Montcornet and the Colonel, is both an insult and a challenge. The two men agree to part without malice but each with the intent of testing his luck.Montcornet moves to the cardroom and finds Soulanges at a bouillotte table, playing with a fury that alarms the Colonel. Soulanges is engaged in reckless, high-stakes play; piles of gold and banknotes accumulate at the table, and a circle of silent men watches with tense interest. The Count is winning—sweeping up piles of stakes with a harsh curl of his lips, not rejoicing but defying fortune. His behavior suggests that his passion and despair are driving him to gamble as if to annihilate something within himself. The scene of the bouillotte table, with its strained silence and the dramatic piles of money, contrasts sharply with the glittering lightness of the ballrooms: here are men who gamble their fortunes to silence their inner storms.Back in the drawing-room the unknown’s passive sadness sets her apart from the usual coquettes. Surrounding ladies, jealous or cautious, form a silent rampart around her, unwilling to let any man claim her; yet no one speaks to her. The Countess’s watchful glances and the anxious whispers of the men show how social performance and private motives intersect. Martial is torn between the charm of the stranger and the advantages of his alliance with Madame de Vaudremont. Montcornet teases him about the ring—this diamond symbolizes not only a sentimental tie but a social contract, and Martial’s flirtations might compromise a prosperous future. Martial, who has the diplomat’s apathy, is nonetheless visibly moved when he meets the unknown’s eyes.The unknown’s answers—short, melancholic—make it clear that she is not seeking to charm; her beauty and stillness provoke the imaginations of men accustomed to being desired. They interpret her tears as evidence of fresh sorrow or secret loves; the lawyer assumes she dressed for this evening with intent and might already be in love. The Colonel, by contrast, takes the socially proper stance: he won’t pursue a married woman. Yet he allows himself to be baited into a wager, not by desire but by esprit de corps and the gambler’s love of a stake. Martial, ever the strategist, both suspects and relishes the risks.Madame de Vaudremont herself continues to manage her display. She has the habitual tactic of arriving late to appear always at her best and to measure others; she has an artful face and dress that she plans carefully to outshine rivals. Her reaction to Martial’s distraction—sorrowful then teasing—is both a personal complaint and a public performance, for she needs to maintain her precedence and ensure nothing undermines the alliance that benefits her as well as her suitor. When she begs Martial to remove the ring, it is with reasons she reserves for private revelation; for now, she must endure his absent-mindedness in public.As the evening progresses, multiple tensions converge: Martial’s daggers of ambition and flirtation; Montcornet’s protectiveness and conventionalism; the Colonel’s pride and curbed passion; Soulanges’ self-destructive gambling; the stranger’s inscrutable sorrow; Madame de Vaudremont’s calculated management of her advantage. The ball’s brightness hides these private struggles, but attentive observers—those who know how to read the smallest glances—detect the threads tying these lives together.Thus the imbroglio begins: the men’s idle curiosity about a sad, pale woman becomes a test of character and will, revealing the social codes and private urgencies that govern behavior in salons where appearances are everything. The candelabrum’s little blue stranger, the diamond ring, the wagers, the entrance with Soulanges—all set the scene for events in which personal honor, ambition, and the fragile inventions of society will collide. The bouillotte table’s piles of gold—Soulanges’ reckless winnings—offer the darker underside of the evening’s light displays, a reminder that beneath frivolity often lurk despair and ruin.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Courage,” the Colonel cried, tugging Soulanges away from the gaming table as if to help him. “Come with me. I have good news, but on one condition.”“That you will answer a question,” Montcornet added.The Comte de Soulanges, gathering his winnings with a grim expression, rose abruptly. Montcornet drew him aside. “The Emperor praised you this morning; your promotion to field-marshal is certain.”Soulanges only scowled. “The Master does not love the Artillery.”“No, but he adores nobility, and you are an aristocrat. He said men who married in Paris during the campaign are not disgraced. Well?”Montcornet then asked whether Soulanges knew the charming woman beneath a huge candelabrum. The Count’s face brightened; he gripped Montcornet’s hand. “If anyone else had asked me that, I would have cracked his skull,” he said, then, with sudden despair: “Leave me. I feel more like blowing out my brains tonight than— I hate everything I see. This gaiety, this music, these stupid faces, all laughing, are killing me!”Montcornet tried to soothe him. “You are too vehement. What would you say if I told you Martial is so taken with that little lady that he is smitten?”“If he speaks to her,” Soulanges shouted, trembling with rage, “I will thrash him flat, even if he’s in the Emperor’s lap!” He sank into a chair; Montcornet, seeing him beyond consolation, left him to his fury.Back in the ballroom Montcornet found Madame de Vaudremont visibly agitated and sat beside her. She feigned irritation but was deeply distressed: she intended to go on to other balls but had to stop at the Princesse de Wagram’s; Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon was amusing himself by flirting with dowagers. Montcornet teased her and guessed the truth—she was watching Martial and feared he would fall at the feet of the pale stranger under the candelabrum. The Countess admitted it. Old Madame de Lansac had told her that de la Roche-Hugon was flirting with that pale, cruelly beautiful woman, likening her to a skeleton. She called the stranger her evil genius and threatened Martial with vengeance if he made her unhappy.Montcornet confessed he had just left a man who promised to blow his brains out if he spoke to that lady—Soulanges, a man to keep his word. But he also knew Martial: threats only encouraged him. He lowered his voice—there was a wager in play. The Countess, eager to know the stranger’s identity, agreed to dance with Montcornet later; for now she would watch what happened.At such entertainments there are always women like Madame de Lansac, older observers who take a perverse interest in the young. The Duchess—sharp-eyed and mischievous—saw through the Countess’s bravado. Madame de Vaudremont put on gaiety while feeling as much sorrow as she feigned: she had hoped Martial would bring power and love but realized painfully she had been mistaken. The Duchess, detecting every subtle movement, read the Countess’s heart and, amused, resolved to intervene.Madame de Lansac sat rouged and diamonded in a large armchair, her sharp nose and ironical smile promising epigrams. Her politeness concealed a mischievous mind; she watched the drama with a triumphal glance that said “I told you so,” and signaled toward the stranger. That look crossed the room and lit some hope in the pale young woman beneath the chandelier. The Comtesse de Vaudremont noticed the alliance forming between the Duchess and the unknown and, wary, wanted to know more.The Baron de la Roche-Hugon, having questioned the dowagers without success, turned to the Comtesse de Gondreville and learned only that the Duchess had introduced the blue lady. Seizing the chance when the Duchess sent a meaningful look, the Baron approached despite past quarrels with the old woman. Sensing his motive, the Duchess smiled with sardonic irony and glanced at Madame de Vaudremont, making Montcornet laugh.“Madame,” the Baron said, “you have, I am told, undertaken the charge of a very precious treasure.”“Do you take me for a dragon?” she replied, then sweetly asked whose treasure he meant. “Of that little lady, unknown to all, whom the jealousy of these coquettes has imprisoned in that corner. You, no doubt, know her family?”“Yes,” the Duchess said easily. “But what concern have you with a provincial heiress, married some time ago, a woman of good birth, who goes nowhere?”The Baron begged: Why does she not dance? She is so pretty. Will the Duchess tell him who she is in exchange for his helping petition the Commissioners of Crown Lands for restitution of the woods of Navarreins? It was a crafty bait: the Baron invoked the Duchess’s past influence at Court—her liaison with Louis XV—and dangled political advantage to open her lips.“Monsieur,” she answered, with feigned gravity, “bring the Comtesse de Vaudremont to me. I promise to reveal the mystery. Every man here is curious; all eyes turn to my protégée. Happy the man she chooses for her partner!” She fixed the Countess with a look that plainly said, “We are talking of you.” “I imagine you would rather hear the stranger’s name from your own lips than mine.”There was defiance in the Duchess’s tone. Madame de Vaudremont rose, took the chair Martial offered, and, without acknowledging him, said to the Duchess that she suspected as much but did not know if it was good or bad. The Duchess, pressing the young woman’s hand, replied softly, “Poor child!”They spoke privately. Madame de Vaudremont dismissed Martial with a commanding “Leave us.” The Baron, annoyed to see the Countess under the duchess’s influence, gave one of those potent looks men use to sway hearts—effective to the blinded, laughable to the newly critical woman.Martial was a man of the world—witty, urbane—and would not needlessly quarrel with a woman favored at Court. He hoped the Emperor wanted her married; he counted on stirring jealousy to uncover her reasons for coolness and withdrew willingly as a new quadrille began. The Baron, folding his arms against a marble console, watched the two ladies. Torn between a near fortune and the thrill of caprice, his face took on a sullen, anxious look; observers might have called him an evil genius relishing the spectacle.Montcornet, leaning in the doorway between the ballroom and the cardroom, smiled beneath his mustache. He enjoyed the agitation—the Countess’s distress, Martial’s intrigue, Soulanges’ gloom tied to the fair unknown whose expression shifted between hope and dread. As a Colonel of the Imperial Guard he stood like the king of the feast, receiving inviting smiles and counting as one of the best matches in the army.It was nearly midnight. The ballroom swelled with conversation, gambling, dancing, flirtations, rivalries, and schemes. Madame de Lansac, playing the indulgent old woman who knows youth errs, chose this moment to give the Countess harsh counsel. “You are at the age when I made many mistakes,” she began. “To go wrong at twenty-two is to spoil your future. Make clever enemies and friends without a sense of conduct, and you will see what a pleasant life you lead!” The Countess, eager for guidance, asked earnestly whether it is very hard for a woman to be happy.The Duchess answered with cold, practical frankness. “At your age you must choose between pleasure and happiness. You want to marry Martial, who is neither wise enough to be a good husband nor passionate enough to remain a lover. He’s in debt; he’ll squander your fortune. He may be ill; he is making the most of what remains. In three years he will be a wreck. He may be ambitious later—perhaps—but I doubt it. What is he? A man of intrigue who gossips agreeably, but he is too presumptuous for real merit. Besides, look at him; is it not written on his brow that what he sees in you is not your youth or beauty but your two million francs? He doesn’t love you; he values you as an investment. If you are bent on marrying, find an older man with an assured position, one halfway in his career. A widow’s marriage should be a sound alliance; it should be a good speculation. Marry well, and at least hope to be addressed as Madame la Marechale.”Her counsel was blunt and strategic: Martial is short-term comfort and long-term danger to the Countess’s fortune and happiness. Marry an older, secure man whose position promises steadiness and respect rather than the risky charm and debts of Martial.Thus, at the ball’s climax, intrigue centered on the pale stranger beneath the candelabrum and the private maneuverings around her: Soulanges’ desperate jealousy and threat, Montcornet’s teasing and protective manipulations, Madame de Vaudremont’s hurt and anger, the Baron’s opportunism, and Madame de Lansac’s deliberate orchestration. The Duchess used the social stage to control disclosures and steer a young woman’s destiny, offering ruthless but practical wisdom about love, marriage, and social advantage in a world where appearances mask calculation and fortunes hinge on prudent choices.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As she spoke, both women turned to Colonel Montcornet’s handsome face.“If you prefer to flirt rather than marry again,” the Duchess said bluntly, “you, better than any woman, know how to stir up trouble and then calm it. But don’t destroy families or ruin wives’ happiness. I’ve played that dangerous game. For a moment of vanity, a virtuous woman can be destroyed—there are truly virtuous women—and we can become mortally hated. I learned too late that, as the Duc d’Albe said, one salmon is worth a thousand frogs! Genuine affection brings far more happiness than fleeting passion. I came to preach to you; you are the reason for my visit to this lower-class house. Have I not just seen actors here? We used to receive them in our boudoir, but never in the drawing-room! If you want to play with men, toy only with those whose lives are unsettled and who have no duties. The others won’t forgive us for the errors that make them happy. Take this advice from my long experience. Look at Soulanges, whom you’ve made lovesick for the past fifteen months. Do you realize what you’ve done? You’ve affected his entire life. He’s been married two years and is adored by a charming wife whom he loves but neglects; she lives in tears. Soulanges has felt remorse far worse than any pleasure you’ve given him. And you, you little minx, have abandoned him. Come see the result of your actions.”The old lady took Madame de Vaudremont’s hand and they rose.“There,” Madame de Lansac said, pointing to a pale, trembling figure under the candlelight, “that is my grandniece, the Comtesse de Soulanges. Today she finally yielded to my persuasion and left the sorrowful room where her child brings her little consolation. You think her charming? Imagine how she must have looked when happiness and love illuminated that now-blighted face.”The Countess looked away, lost in sad thought.The Duchess led her to the card-room door, then, scanning the room, said, “And there is Soulanges!”The Countess shuddered at the sight of Soulanges, pale and rigid in an easy chair. His indifference and the tension in his brow revealed his suffering. The players passed him by, ignoring him as if he were dead. The image of the tearful wife and the morose husband, separated amidst festivity, struck the Countess with a sense of foreboding about her own future. Though her heart was not entirely hardened, she pressed the Duchess’s hand, thanking her with a childlike smile.“My dear child,” the old lady whispered, “remember that we can repel a man’s attentions just as easily as we can attract them.”“She is yours if you’re not a fool,” Madame de Lansac murmured to Colonel Montcornet while the Countess remained absorbed in her compassion for Soulanges, still loving him enough to wish to restore his happiness.“Oh! I will talk to him!” the Countess declared.“Do nothing of the sort!” the Duchess exclaimed as she returned to her chair. “Choose a good husband and shut your door to my nephew. A wife cannot accept her husband’s heart as a gift from another woman; she is far happier believing she has won it back. By bringing my niece here, I believe I’ve given her a chance to regain her husband’s affection. All I need from you is to play the Colonel.” She pointed to Montcornet, and the Countess smiled.Later, when alone with Martial, she amusedly confirmed she finally knew the name of the unknown. Her smile and bright eyes played on him; believing she still loved him, Martial adopted a charming demeanor.“Will you be annoyed if I seem to care about that name?” he asked.“Will you be annoyed if a remnant of affection prevents me from telling you? And if I forbid you to approach that young lady? It could be dangerous.”“To lose your favor would be worse than losing my life.”“Martial,” the Countess said sternly, “she is Madame de Soulanges. Her husband would kill you if he knew.”“Ha! So the Colonel can leave the man in peace who has taken his love, yet would fight for his wife! What a reversal of principles! I beg you to let me dance with her. You’ll see how little love that icy heart could feel for you; if the Colonel allows me to dance with his wife after letting me—”“But she loves her husband.”“An even greater obstacle I shall enjoy overcoming.”“But she is married.”“A whimsical objection!”“Ah!” the Countess said with a bitter smile, “you punish us for both our faults and our repentance!”“Do not be angry!” Martial exclaimed eagerly. “Forgive me, I beseech you. I will think no more of Madame de Soulanges.”“You deserve that I should send you to her.”“I’m off then,” he laughed, “and I’ll return more devoted to you than ever. You’ll see that the prettiest woman cannot capture the heart that is yours.”“Meaning you want to win Colonel Montcornet’s horse?”“Ah! Traitor!” he said, playfully threatening his friend. The Colonel smiled and joined them; the Baron gave him a seat near the Countess, saying with a sardonic tone, “Here, madame, is a man who boasted he could win your favor in one evening.”He left, thinking he had cleverly piqued the Countess’ pride and done Montcornet a disservice, unaware of the irony in Madame de Vaudremont’s words, as both were unconsciously moving toward each other.At that moment, the lawyer approached Madame de Soulanges, who sat pale and timid, seemingly alive only in her eyes. Her husband entered the ballroom, his eyes flashing with anger. The Duchess, ever watchful, rushed to her nephew, asking for his arm to find her carriage, feigning boredom to prevent a scene. Before leaving, she shot a knowing glance at her niece, signaling her to take revenge.Madame de Vaudremont noticed the exchange and felt a sudden realization; she feared being the target of the Duchess’s cunning.“That duplicitous Duchess,” she thought, “may be preaching morality while plotting against me.”This thought stirred her pride more than curiosity. In her distraction she lost composure; the Colonel misread her embarrassment and became more ardent. The diplomats, entertained by the drama, had never seen so many intrigues at once. The passions of the two couples became evident in the crowded rooms, reflected on the faces around them, amplifying their own feelings of helplessness.Finally, the Baron found a seat beside Madame de Soulanges. He admired her beauty close at hand, captivated by her pale neck and graceful figure. Women's sashes were tied under the bosom then, a style flattering to perfect figures. As he stole glances at the Countess, Martial was enchanted by her perfection.“You haven’t danced once this evening, madame,” he said in soft, flattering tones. “Not for lack of a partner, I presume?”“I never go to parties; I’m quite unknown,” Madame de Soulanges replied coldly, unaware of her aunt’s signal to attract the Baron.Martial, adjusting the diamond on his hand, felt a spark of hope as he caught the Countess’s eye. She blushed and looked at him with an indefinable expression.“Do you enjoy dancing?” he asked to continue the conversation.“Yes, very much, monsieur.”Their eyes met, and the earnestness in her voice stirred him. “Then, am I too bold in offering to be your partner for the next quadrille?”Artless confusion colored her cheeks.“But, monsieur, I have already refused one partner—a military man—”“Was it that tall cavalry colonel over there?”“Precisely.”“Oh! He’s a friend of mine; don’t worry. Will you grant me the favor I hope for?”“Yes, monsieur.”Her tone revealed a new and deep emotion that touched Martial’s world-weary heart. Overcome with shyness, he lost his confidence, and his southern charm ignited. He tried to speak, but his words felt clumsy compared to Madame de Soulanges’ bright replies. Fortunately, the quadrille was forming, and standing beside her eased his nerves. For many men, dancing is a way to express themselves, believing they can win a woman’s heart through physical grace. Martial likely intended to showcase all his charms, judging by his pretentious movements and gestures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################He led his conquest to the quadrille, where the brightest women prized dancing above all. As the orchestra began, the Baron felt proud surveying the ladies. Madame de Soulanges’ dress rivaled Madame de Vaudremont’s opposite her. Eyes turned to Madame de Soulanges; a flattering murmur followed and her blush only increased her charm. When she raised her eyes she sought her partner, seeming to transfer the glory of admiration to him; she combined innocence, modesty, and the artful glance of youth, knowing when to look at Martial.When a new figure, invented by a dancer named Trenis, brought Martial face to face with Colonel Montcornet, he joked, “I have won your horse.” Montcornet retorted, “Yes, but you have lost eighty thousand francs a year!” Martial waved it off: “What do I care? Madame de Soulanges is worth millions!”After the quadrille whispers circulated. Less attractive women moralized about Martial and the Comtesse; the prettier admired the Countess’s apparent ease in surrendering attention to him. The men, unable to explain the Baron’s luck, found him unremarkable. A few women defended the Countess, arguing that young wives would suffer if a single graceful dance could stain a woman’s reputation.Only Martial understood the depth of his happiness. During the last figure, as the ladies formed the moulinet, he clasped the Countess’s hand and felt her response through the thin glove. “Madame,” he said as the quadrille ended, “do not return to that corner where you’ve been hiding. Is admiration the only reward you gain from your jewels? Come and enjoy the scene and yourself.”She yielded to his assurance, and they crossed the crowded rooms. She hesitated at each doorway, peering into the next room and reassured only by his repeated “He is not here.” They reached a grand picture gallery, where a lavish collation awaited three hundred guests. As supper neared, Martial led her to an oval boudoir overlooking the garden, fragrant with flowers and hung in blue. The hubbub faded; startled, the Countess at first resisted but finally sat on an ottoman after glancing in a mirror, perhaps reassured they might be seen.“This room is charming,” she said. “All here is love and delight!” Martial watched her—unease, modesty, and eagerness that captivated him. She smiled, calming her inner struggle. Playfully she took his left hand and removed his ring, exclaiming, “What a fine diamond!”Intoxicated, Martial looked at her with eyes bright as the gem. “Wear it,” he said, “in memory of this hour, and for the love of—” He stopped, caught by her rapture. She asked, “You give it to me?” “I wish I had the whole world to offer you!” he replied. “Will you accept only my diamond?” She pressed, “You will never take it back?” “Never,” he assured.She placed the ring on her finger, and as Martial prepared to embrace her she suddenly stood and said, “I accept the diamond, monsieur, with less scruple because it belongs to me.” The Baron was taken aback. “Monsieur de Soulanges took it from my dressing-table and told me he had lost it.”“You are mistaken, madame,” Martial said, irritated. “It was given to me by Madame de Vaudremont.” “Precisely,” she replied, smiling. “My husband borrowed it, gave it to her, and she gifted it to you. This ring has made a little journey, that is all. It may reveal secrets to me.” She touched a spring in the ring; a tiny compartment opened, showing a lock of M. de Soulanges’ hair. Startled and affronted, she fled into the crowded rooms, leaving Martial confounded. Nearby the Colonel and Madame de Vaudremont laughed; the Colonel teased, “Will you have my horse to chase after your prize?” Martial accepted their banter gratefully, relieved they kept silent about the evening’s other matters, as he exchanged a horse for a young wife.Crossing Paris to the Faubourg Saint-Germain, the Comtesse’s anxiety deepened. Before leaving the Hôtel Gondreville she had searched for her aunt and husband, who had departed without her. Disturbing suspicions plagued her. She had silently witnessed her husband’s torment since Madame de Vaudremont ensnared him and had hoped for his repentance. Now she feared she had agreed to her aunt’s scheme and ruined herself.The evening had darkened her innocence. Alarmed first by her husband’s suffering, she grew more distressed seeing her rival’s beauty and feeling the weight of social corruption. Crossing the Pont Royal, she cast away the lock of hair found in the ring—what she had kept as a remnant of pure affection. Tears came as she remembered long-standing grief and the thought that a woman in search of peace must bury her pain without complaint.“Alas!” she thought, “What can women do when they do not love? What sustains their devotion? I cannot believe, as my aunt claims, that reason alone is enough.”Still sighing, she arrived home and rushed upstairs, startled to find her husband by the fire. “How long has it been since you attended balls without telling me?” he asked, his voice strained. “You know a woman is out of place without her husband. You compromised yourself by hiding in the dark corner.”“Oh, my dear Léon,” she replied coaxingly, “I couldn’t resist the joy of seeing you without your knowing. My aunt took me to the ball, and I was very happy there!”Her words softened the Count’s sternness; he had feared she would learn of his infidelity and had tried to be the first to blame her. Delighted to see him smile, the Countess looked at him tenderly; her simplicity and grace restored his mood. He took her hand and kissed it gratefully—gratitude often accompanies love. She explained the diamond’s story; he laughed at the bruise it had made on his lip and the scene closed on reconciliation rather than scandal.General Montcornet did not marry Madame de Vaudremont; she later perished in the tragic fire that marred the ball given by the Austrian ambassador for Napoleon’s marriage to the daughter of Emperor Joseph II.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##### — repeated frame############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In September 1835 Mademoiselle du Rouvre, the Marquis du Rouvre’s only daughter, married Comte Adam Mitgislas Laginski, a Polish exile. Though her father had squandered much of his fortune, Clémentine remained a substantial heiress through her mother’s family; relatives’ settlements ensured her a steady income after marriage.Adam, while exiled, was not a charge on the French government. He belonged to an illustrious Polish family but encountered prejudice in Paris, where Polish nobility was often poorly regarded. At first he seemed like a student enjoying Paris after losing political prospects. The Polish community in France was split between republican and noble exiles, complicating social relations and inviting jokes and speculation about his wealth and status.In 1834 Adam bought a house in the rue de la Pépinière, and by 1836 he had established himself in Parisian society. His marriage to Clémentine elevated his standing and made him fashionable. Their home was luxurious, blending architectural styles and fine decor reflecting rising bourgeois tastes.Clémentine noticed Captain Paz, a discreet factotum who managed finances, horses, and household affairs. Adam explained Paz was a noble from a poor branch who had fought for Poland and become a close friend through shared hardship. Paz’s loyalty, discretion, and refusal of money underlined their bond. Preparing for a day out, Clémentine’s affection for both men grew—an account of loyalty, friendship, and the complexities of social status in 19th‑century Paris.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Paz (Thaddeus) lived reclusively in Paris until the Comtesse Laginska, Clementine, was seen riding with him in the Bois de Boulogne and insisted he dine with her and Adam. Dressed for the occasion, he behaved like a subordinate—listening more than speaking—because his reserve concealed a deeper struggle: he was in love with Clementine and tried to hide it. Adam urged him to be livelier; at the opera Clementine was offended by his detachment, and Adam excused him as obdurate. Inwardly tormented by unrequited love, Thaddeus’s longing only deepened when she returned from a trip. He took a quiet satisfaction in being indispensable to their household—secure that their domestic life depended on his management—even as that role intensified his private yearning.Clementine, growing more beautiful and confident in Parisian society, focused on status and left little room for thoughts of Thaddeus. Still, she occasionally noticed him—once inviting him into their carriage, an act that flustered him and charged their brief interactions with unspoken feeling. Thaddeus found solace in duties and the stability they provided, yet his devotion remained a source of inner turmoil. He reflected on the nature of his love: profound, unrewarded, and sustained by watching Clementine’s happiness from a distance.At one reception Clementine hosted, his absence passed unnoticed; he returned to solitary habits, content with silent devotion but aware his love was both joy and suffering. He admired her as she sought attention, contended with rivals, and often neglected Adam. Throughout, Thaddeus quietly sustained their household while privately enduring the ache of desire.In May, as Clementine prepared to go to her uncle’s estate, she again insisted he join for dinner. He came, tried to be more engaging, but shyness and duty restrained him; still their talk grew intimate and Clementine voiced discontent with Adam and a wish for greater passion. Thaddeus wanted to confess but held back, fearing the peril to their triangle.To disguise his attachment he invented a love for a circus performer named Malaga—graceful, candid, a simpler affection than the polished Parisian world. He became her benefactor and refuge without real romance. When Malaga, suspicious and hurt, sent a pleading letter, its despair echoed his ache for Clementine and crystallized his realization: he was bound to an unwavering devotion that left him torn among silent adoration, practical duty, and the sacrifices their intertwined lives demanded.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Clementine returned home, and Paz saw her again, more beautiful than ever. After dinner the count and countess discussed Thaddeus, who had left a letter from Malaga with Adam. Adam lamented Thaddeus’s misfortune, fearing he would lose everything because of his involvement with a circus performer. Clementine expressed disdain for such women, while Adam defended Thaddeus, noting his financial sacrifices for Malaga. The countess viewed Paz—who had asked Adam for money for Malaga—as a potential ruin for Adam; Adam trusted that Thaddeus would abandon Malaga if it suited his interests.Days later Clementine’s manner toward Paz changed; he perceived her contempt and fell into despair, convinced he’d earned her scorn. Still, he found solace in silently admiring her at social events and dedicating himself to her well‑being and the household. His love was deep but left him feeling unworthy; the anguish made him ill for a time before he recovered.Meanwhile Adam lost a large sum gambling and asked Thaddeus to conceal it by blaming Malaga. Thaddeus, feeling obliged, agreed. When Clementine discovered Thaddeus’s financial generosity to Paz she protested his loyalty. Burdened by expectations, Thaddeus resolved to manage Clementine’s affairs and taught her household management so she might run things independently.As spring neared, Adam fell seriously ill. Thaddeus devoted himself to nursing him while Clementine sought the best medical care. Thaddeus’s care kept the count alive, earning praise, yet he remained tormented by unrequited love and the certainty he could never have Clementine. He decided to leave for the Caucasus to escape his pain.Before leaving he wrote Clementine a heartfelt letter confessing his feelings and sacrifices; Clementine, reflecting, mourned the loss of a devoted friend. Adam, unaware of Thaddeus’s full sacrifices, lamented only the departure. Years later Clementine often thought of Thaddeus, still unaware of his depth of devotion; she moved into a new romance, but his memory lingered as a bittersweet, irrevocable loss.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY — CONTINUING HERE. MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Marquise de Listomere embodies the Restoration spirit, mixing religious devotion with worldly elegance. Married seven years to the unremarkable Marquis de Listomere, she maintains a virtuous facade that also supports his political ambitions. While some women reserve judgment until he becomes a peer, her conduct remains strictly principled.The marquis is notable only for his lack of qualities: a silent deputy who votes properly but lacks charisma, nicknamed “dull weather.” His mediocrity makes him a safe match for a virtuous wife, since he cannot lead either to folly or scandal.Though reserved, the marquise provokes both respect and scorn from would‑be flirts, whom she answers with contempt. She speaks pleasantly with agreeable men without stirring gossip and does not affect coyness—her probity is sincere.The narrator admires her presence: neither plain nor conventionally pretty, she has a captivating air and animated eyes that suggest depth; she charms without trying. This emerges in a recent encounter with Eugène de Rastignac, a clever, lively young man. At a ball they converse from opera to women’s duties, an exchange hinting at mutual interest before he abruptly departs. The next morning Rastignac, distracted while writing letters, grows absorbed in feelings nearing romance until his friend Horace interrupts talk of an expedition to Morea.Dressing, the marquise receives Rastignac’s affectionate letter. Her impulse is to discard it, then to decipher his intent; the note leaves her fatigued as she ponders its impertinence and implications. The episode reveals tension between her outward virtue and inward stirrings, foreshadowing complex relationships.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame de Listomere forbade her porter to admit Monsieur de Rastignac and resolved to treat him with more than disdain; his recent insolence surpassed previous offenses. She had thought to keep a letter he sent but burned it. "Madame received a fine love‑letter," Caroline told the housemaid, who was surprised.That evening Madame de Listomere went to a party at the Marquis de Beauseant expecting Rastignac; by two in the morning she realized he would not come, despite intending to confront him with coldness. Stendhal called this mental process "crystallization."Four days later Rastignac scolded his valet Joseph for misplacing letters. Joseph, distracted, remembered one was for Madame de Listomere and the other for Rastignac's lawyer. When Eugène learned the letter had not been for Madame de Nucingen, he laughed at his own wandering heart. His errors began when, planning to amuse Madame de Listomere by discussing the mix‑up, he then stayed away for several days, letting her resentment harden.When he finally called, the porter said she was out. The Marquis, unaware of tensions, invited him in. As they climbed the stairs Rastignac noted social faux pas. On seeing him, Madame de Listomere blushed; he thought, "What! that fortress, too?" and adjusted his cravat. Monsieur de Listomere remained absorbed in his newspaper, oblivious.Madame de Listomere quickly composed herself. When Eugène asked after her health she assured him she was well. He suggested his visit might be unwelcome and blamed the marquis's introduction; she hinted that her husband did not know all her secrets. Eugène tried to lighten the mood, proposing silence as his best excuse. She promised to forget, maintaining dignity. When he mentioned the letter she feigned offense but could not hide amusement; he insisted he meant no harm.Monsieur de Listomere interrupted to announce a mutual acquaintance's death. The conversation revealed the marquise's irritation. When Eugène named Madame de Nucingen her demeanor shifted: she rang for wood, signaling the visit should end, and coldly confronted him about how his pen could have written her name. Discomfited, Eugène stammered and departed.Days later Madame de Listomere confirmed Eugène's claim about the letter's true recipient. She withdrew from society; the marquis explained her absence as a stomach ailment, her physician a nervous condition. The episode highlights the complexities of social interaction, misunderstanding, and romantic pride.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Story middle — continued here.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# ################################################ ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In Paris, balls often split into two gatherings: an official party of polite small talk and an intimate follow-up — salons hosted by women like Madame d’Espard and Mademoiselle des Touches — where genuine wit and free conversation flourish.At Mademoiselle des Touches’ salon one evening, the Minister de Marsay recounted a youthful passion: a secret affair with a woman six years his senior. To conceal it he kept a mask of indifference; their private signals—riding past her carriage, exchanging silent messages through the flowers she carried—sustained the thrill. Blind to any betrayal, he was shaken when illness led him to cancel a meeting by note but he visited nonetheless and found her with another man, igniting jealous fury. Her calm, tender concern briefly soothed him, yet the episode darkened his view: the end of innocence and the rise of a more cynical outlook on love. He grappled with revenge and passion, ultimately arguing that true love should transcend vengeance. His tale left the salon reflecting on trust’s fragility and love’s transformative power.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Cured of my cold and my idealized love, I set out with a charming new heroine whose beauty sharply contrasted with that of my deceptive angel. I avoided quarrels with this clever woman, suspecting true love might not match the pleasures of her practiced deceit; refined hypocrisy, I thought, could pass for virtue.I visited a hair-worker in the Rue Boucher to have a keepsake made for my new angel. He specialized in hair mementos and explained fashionable uses of hair in linen and accessories. When I recognized an embroidered handkerchief he showed me, my doubts about the sincerity of women’s affections hardened. I left fond of pleasure but skeptical about love.Two months later I was in Charlotte’s boudoir, holding her hand as we explored sentiment. In a tender moment I tried to draw out her most beguiling falsehoods. Charlotte professed love, declaring I was the only man in her world and that she had sought ways to bind me to her forever.When I bluntly asked, “When are you to marry the Duke?” her startled reaction betrayed guilt. She feigned astonishment, then, as I persisted and suggested she should marry him because he was rich and pious, she fell to her knees swearing before God she loved no one but me. I admitted my doubts; she instantly accused me of no longer loving her. She tried to escape the subject, but curiosity made her ask what I had seen. I told her I had spotted her attending mass with the Duke several times; she dismissed it as jealousy.I argued that marrying the Duke would elevate her and that he, being virtuous, would expect a serious wife. Charlotte, weeping, lamented that if I had spoken sooner we might have been happy. I insisted it was too late; she had gone too far with the Duke. She fretted over his possible suspicions; I suggested he likely had none, though she would need to prove her devotion. As she paced, her demeanor shifted: she extended her hand, praised my nobility and charm, and vowed never to forget me.Her tactics were admirable; I played the distressed lover. Then she asked what would become of me, and I disclosed advances from another woman. This shocked Charlotte, who accused me of unfaithfulness; I countered that she had too much wit for her heart to suffer. She declared disillusionment with love, claiming women could never be truly loved. I argued treating love as a solemn social bond was like romanticizing an actress. She accused me of betrayal; I feigned submission and asked if she would remember her grievances once she became a duchess. Her continued bitterness confirmed she still felt wronged, and I realized I had successfully detached myself—having studied the lives of successful men with women, I believed I had mastered the art of retreat.The women in the room sympathized with Charlotte while de Marsay smiled slightly. Conversation turned to the decline of social distinctions. Lady Dudley observed that women often did not view marriages as acts of inconstancy. De Marsay lamented the erosion of aristocracy: duchesses and marquises were disappearing, and the Civil Code had leveled hierarchies. Blondet noted aristocracy now often began with viscountesses; de Marsay thought countesses might survive in reduced form.The Comte de Vandenesse and Lord Dudley complained that property and nobility were no longer organized, hastening decay. Joseph Bridau declared the beauty of womanhood had dimmed; Daniel d’Arthez said aristocracy’s retreat left behind women who once might have shaped society. Discussion turned to the modern “perfect lady,” a product of the new era: she embodied good taste, grace, and effortless elegance but lacked the grandeur of former great ladies. Mademoiselle des Touches questioned whether this was progress; Madame de Nucingen argued a woman’s worth now rested more on character and presentation than lineage. Émile Blondet summarized her as graceful and poised across social settings, skilled at navigating expectations—captivating in a changed world where old orders and grandeurs fade.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Now, you will not talk for half an hour with a bourgeoise without her mentioning her husband,” Blondet began gravely. “Yet a married lady will hide her husband so skillfully you might never find him. You may only see her glance at a middle-aged man with a decoration who bows and goes; she has ordered her carriage and departs.“You are not the rose, but you have been with the rose, and you go to bed under a golden canopy of a delightful dream that may linger even after Sleep has opened the ivory gates of dreams.“At home she sees no one before four; she keeps you waiting. Her house is tasteful; her luxury is daily and renewed. There are no dusty glass shades or rags—warm stairs, flowers everywhere—flowers being the only permissible gift, and only from certain people since they mean a promise. Fashionable trifles are scattered, but not museum-fashion. She sits by the fire in a low chair and does not rise to greet you. At the ball she was our creditor; at home she owes you the pleasure of her wit. These are her arts. She seeks in you someone to expand her sphere, an object for her attention. To lure you into her drawing-room she is enchantingly charming. This shows how isolated women are and why they want a little world where they can shine like constellations. Conversation tends to generalities.“Yes,” de Marsay agreed, “you have found the flaw of our age. The epigram—an entire volume in a word—no longer attacks people or things but petty events that fade quickly.”“Hence,” Blondet went on, “a lady’s intelligence, if she has any, lies in casting doubt on everything. Here is the contrast between two women: the townswoman is virtuous, the lady hesitates and wrestles with virtue. That uncertainty is one of the last graces left her. She hardly goes to church but will discuss religion; if you show free thought, she’ll try to convert you with a stock of platitudes understood by these women: ‘For shame! I thought you had too much sense to attack religion. Society totters, and you deprive it of support. Religion is property and the future of our children! Individualism is the disease of the age, religion the only remedy; it unites families your laws sunder,’ and so on. She then glides into a neo-Christian, politically tinged discourse that is neither Catholic nor Protestant—but very moral—a muddle of modern doctrines at odds with each other.”The women laughed at Blondet’s satire.“This explanation, Count Adam,” Blondet told the Pole, “shows that the ‘perfect lady’ embodies both intellectual and political muddle, surrounded by flashy, ephemeral products of an industry that replaces itself. When you leave her you think: she certainly has superior ideas! You believe it because she has probed your heart delicately and asked your secrets; she feigns ignorance to learn everything. There are things she never knows even when she does. You alone feel uneasy, knowing nothing of her heart. The great ladies of old flaunted affairs; today’s lady arranges her passions like a musical score, with rests and notes, avoiding anything that would compromise love, her husband, or her children’s future. Name, place, and fortune no longer shield her; the aristocracy no longer protects. She lacks the lofty antagonism of the past; instead of crushing, she risks being crushed. She masters Jesuitical compromises between propriety and anonymous passion. She fears her servants as an Englishwoman fears a divorce trial. So free at a ball, charming in public, she is a slave at home; she is never independent except in secrecy or in theory. Her task is to remain a lady.“For today, a repudiated woman, reduced to a meager allowance without a carriage, luxury, or opera box is neither wife nor maid nor townswoman; she drifts into chattelhood. The Carmelites will not take a married woman; it would be bigamy. Would her lover want her then? That is the question. Thus your perfect lady may invite calumny but not slander.”“It is all horribly true,” said the Princesse de Cadignan.“And so,” Blondet continued, “our ‘perfect lady’ stands between English hypocrisy and eighteenth-century frankness—a bastard system symptomatic of an age where nothing that emerges resembles what has vanished, where transition leads nowhere and everything is graduated; the great figures fade into the background, and distinction becomes merely personal. I doubt any woman, even near a throne, can, before twenty-five, acquire that encyclopedic knowledge of trifles, the practice of maneuvering, the small yet important arts—the musical tones and harmony of dress, angelic mischief and innocent cunning, speech and silence, wit and obtuseness, diplomacy and ignorance—that make a perfect lady.”“And where,” Mademoiselle des Touches asked, “would you place the female author? Is she a perfect lady, a woman comme il faut?”“When she has no genius, she is a woman comme il n’en faut pas,” Blondet replied, looking in a way that might praise Camille Maupin. “That epigram is not mine but Napoleon’s.”“You need not hate Napoleon for that,” Canalis interposed. “He was weakly jealous of literary genius—he had his faults. Who can explain Napoleon? A man with arms folded who did everything—the greatest force, most concentrated, most caustic; a singular will who spread armed civilization without fixing it. He could do everything because he willed everything: a prodigious phenomenon of will, conquering illness by battle yet destined to die sick in bed; a man with a code and a sword in his brain, word and deed; a clear spirit seeing everything but his own fall; a capricious politician risking men, yet sparing three diplomats—Talleyrand, Pozzo, Metternich—whose deaths might have saved the Empire. Nature gave him, as a rare gift, a heart in a bronze frame; mirthful and kind among women at midnight, the next morning he moved Europe as a girl might splash water. Hypocritical and generous; liking tawdriness and simplicity; without taste, yet protecting the arts; truly great in everything by temperament: Caesar at twenty-five, Cromwell at thirty. Then, like my grocer in Père Lachaise, a good husband and father. He improvised public works, empires, kings, codes, verses, a romance—with breadth over precision. He aimed to make all Europe France; having changed laws of gravitation, he left us poorer. He who took an empire by his name lost it at its frontier in blood. A man of thought and action who understood Desaix and Fouché.”“All despotism and all justice at the right moments. The true king!” de Marsay declared.“Ah! what a pleasure to digest what you talk about,” Baron de Nucingen remarked.“But is our banquet of talk a common one?” Joseph Bridau asked. “If you paid for the charms of conversation as for music or dancing, your fortune would be inadequate! There is no second performance of the same flash of wit.”“And are we really so diminished as these gentlemen think?” the Princesse de Cadignan asked the women with a skeptical smile. “Because under a regime that makes everything small you prefer small dishes, small rooms, small pictures, small articles, small newspapers, small books, does it follow that women have grown smaller? Why should the human heart change because you change your coat? Passions remain the same in all ages. I know of beautiful devotion and sublime sufferings that lack the publicity—the glory—that once lent luster to some women’s errors. But even without saving a king one is no less an Agnès Sorel. Do you think our Marquise d’Espard is inferior to Madame Doublet or Madame du Deffand, in whose rooms so much evil was done? Is not Taglioni a match for Camargo? Malibran for Saint-Huberti? Are our poets not superior to those of the eighteenth century? If now, under merchant rule, we lack a defining style, did the Empire not have its stamp as Louis XV’s age did, and was its splendor not fabulous? Have the sciences lost anything?”“I agree, madame; the women of this age are truly great,” Comte de Vandenesse replied. “When posterity looks back, will not Madame Récamier appear as fine as any past beauty? We have made so much history that historians will be lacking. The age of Louis XIV had but one Madame de Sévigné; we have a thousand in Paris who write better privately than she published. Whether called ‘perfect lady’ or great lady, she will always be the woman among women.“Blondet painted the fascinations of a woman of the day; but when needed, this creature who bristles or shows off, who echoes Mr. This and Mr. That, can be heroic. Your faults, mesdames, are more poetic because they are surrounded by greater perils. I have seen much—perhaps too late—but when the illegality of your feelings might be excused, I always saw what you call Providence inevitably overwhelming those we deem light women.”“I hope,” Madame de Vandenesse said, “that we can be great in other ways—”“Oh, let the Comte preach to us!” Madame de Serizy cried.“With all the more reason because he has preached by example,” the Baronne de Nucingen added.“On my honor!” General de Montriveau said, “in all the dramas—a word you like,” he added, glancing at Blondet—“where God’s finger has been visible, the most frightful I ever knew was almost by my act—”“Tell us!” Lady Barimore cried. “I love to shudder!”“It is the taste of a virtuous woman,” de Marsay observed, looking at Lord Dudley’s lovely daughter.“During the 1812 campaign,” General de Montriveau began, “I was the involuntary cause of a terrible disaster which may interest Doctor Bianchon, since while you study the body, you care for the mind; it may help solve will’s problems.“I was on my second campaign, enjoying danger like a young foolish lieutenant. At the Beresina the army lost discipline and obedience; it was a medley of nations making their way south. Men would drive off a general in rags if he brought no wood or food. After crossing the river, disorder worsened. I had wandered from Zembin’s marshes seeking shelter. Finding none, I finally saw a wretched little Polish farm—think of the meanest wooden houses of Lower Normandy. These dwellings were a single room, one end partitioned as a forage store.“In twilight I saw faint smoke; hoping to find kinder comrades, I approached. Inside, the table was laid. Several officers and a woman—common enough—were eating potatoes, some horseflesh grilled, frozen beetroots. I recognized artillery captains from my regiment. I was welcomed with shouts that would have amazed me on the other side of the Beresina; but at that moment the cold was less keen; my comrades had food, warmth, and straw; we asked for little then. I sat on a bundle of straw.“At the table’s end, by the door to the hay partition, sat my old colonel—one of the most extraordinary men I met among the mixed ranks. He was Italian. When the South is truly fine, it is sublime. His fairness under lamplight was exquisite. My colonel, like many officers borrowed from Eugene’s army, was tall—well proportioned, perhaps stout, but prodigiously powerful and greyhound-like. His abundant black curls contrasted with a white, almost feminine complexion; small hands, a shapely foot, a pleasant mouth, and an aquiline nose that would turn white with anger, which happened often. His irascibility was beyond belief; no one could remain calm near him. He had taken a fancy to me and thought all I did was right. When angry his brow knit into a horseshoe delta; the muscles quivered and his strength became boundless.“He spoke with a guttural roll that, though sometimes a grace, conveyed incredible force when excited. When calm his blue eyes were angelic and his brow charming. In parade he eclipsed others; even d’Orsay paled before him. Passion thrives on contrast, so you need not ask whether he was irresistible to women—he could be—but singularly he was not a lady-killer.“To show his violence: we once were dragging guns up a narrow road when another artillery regiment crossed us. Their colonel wanted our captain to give way; our captain refused. Their nearest gun wheel struck our captain’s right leg, breaking it and throwing him off his horse. Our Italian colonel, nearby, sensed a quarrel; he galloped among the guns, risking everything, and reached the captain shouting ‘Help!’ No, our colonel was no longer human—foam rose, he roared. Incapable of speaking, he beckoned his opponent into the wood and drew his sword. In two seconds the other colonel lay with his skull split. The soldiers of the opponent’s regiment backed away—yes, quickly.“The injured captain had an Italian wife, a beautiful Sicilian from Messina, who was not indifferent to our colonel. This aggravated his rage; he felt pledged to protect the husband as he would the woman.“In the Zembin hovel where I was received that night the captain sat opposite me; his wife sat at the other end facing the Colonel. Rosina was small, dark, with almond eyes full of Southern fire; though dust-covered and ragged, exhausted, she retained womanly graces—rose mouth, white teeth, an outline suggesting love despite misery. Rosina’s frame was fragile-looking but wiry. Her husband, a Piedmontese gentleman, had an ironical simplicity on his face that betrayed a quiet cruelty; a thin, restless underlip that turned down at the corners made me uneasy.“The conversation was flat when I arrived. We exchanged campaign misfortunes. After the Colonel finished he wiped his mustache, gave a black look at the Italian woman, said ‘Rosina?’ and went into the hay to sleep. The meaning was clear. The young wife’s gesture expressed the annoyance she could not show—an offense to her dignity and to her husband—but also a presentiment. She stayed seated.“A minute later, apparently when the Colonel was snug, he repeated, ‘Rosina?’ The tone was more brutally demanding. Rosina turned pale, rose, passed behind us, and went to him. We all sat in absolute silence; I began to laugh, and then they laughed too. ‘Tu ridi?—you laugh?’ said the husband. ‘On my honor, old comrade,’ I replied, serious again, ‘I confess I was wrong; I ask your pardon a thousand times, and if you are not satisfied with my apologies I am ready to give you satisfaction.’ ‘Oh! it is not you who are wrong; it is I!’ he answered coldly. We all lay down and slept.“Next morning each set out alone, with the selfishness that made our rout one of the most horrible dramas of self-seeking, melancholy, and horror. Yet about seven or eight hundred paces away we met again and walked on together like geese. Necessity drove us all.“At a knoll where we could still see the farmhouse we heard sounds like lions and bulls—a noise like no known cry—and a woman’s feeble scream. We looked around in terror; we no longer saw the house, only a huge bonfire. The farmhouse was barricaded and ablaze. Swirls of smoke carried hoarse cries and a pungent smell. A few yards behind, the captain quietly joined our caravan; we gazed at him in silence; he pointed to his breast with his right forefinger and, waving his left toward the fire, said, ‘Son’io.’ We all walked on without asking a word.”“There is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep,” de Marsay remarked.“It would be frightful to leave with that vision in mind,” Madame de Montcornet said. “I shall dream of it—”“And what was the punishment of Monsieur de Marsay’s ‘First’?” Lord Dudley asked, smiling.“When the English jest, their foils have the buttons on,” Blondet replied.“Monsieur Bianchon can tell us, for he saw her dying,” de Marsay said, turning to me.“Yes,” I answered; “and her death was one of the most beautiful I ever witnessed. The Duke and I had spent the night by the dying woman’s pillow; pulmonary consumption in its last stage left no hope; she had taken the sacrament the day before. The Duke fell asleep. The Duchess woke about four, and with a friendly smile signed to me to let him rest while she faced death. She had grown incredibly thin, but her face kept its sublime outline; the pallor made her skin like porcelain lit from within. Her bright eyes contrasted with this languid beauty; her countenance showed expressive calm and pity for the Duke, a lofty tenderness that death seemed only to enlarge. The silence was complete; the room, softly lamp-lit, resembled any sickroom at the hour of death.“At that moment the clock struck. The Duke awoke, despairing at having slept through a few of the last moments; his gesture might have been misread by any but the dying woman. A busy statesman, always thinking of France’s interests, had strange odd ways that make genius seem mad to the unseeing. He sat by her, looked fixedly. The dying woman took his hand weakly and said in a low, agitated voice, ‘My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?’ Then she died looking at him.”“The stories the doctor tells always leave a deep impression,” Comte de Vandenesse remarked.“But a sweet one,” Mademoiselle des Touches said, rising.PARIS, June 1839–42.ADDENDUMSelected characters and notable other appearances in the Human Comedy:- Bianchon, Horace: Father Goriot; MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“Ah! madame,” said the doctor, “I have appalling stories; but each has its proper hour—Chamfort told the Duc de Fronsac, ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”“But it is two in the morning, and Rosina’s story has prepared us,” said the mistress of the house.“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” cried everyone.The doctor bowed and began.About a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the Loir, stands an old brown house with very high roofs, isolated without even a tannery or tavern nearby. In front is a garden sloping to the river; neat box borders and espaliered fruit trees have run wild; willows in the stream half hide the house. An arbor’s wreck and a table remain. One wall bears a sundial with the motto “Ultimam cogita.” The roof is dilapidated; shutters closed; balconies hung with swallows’ nests; doors forever shut. Weeds and rust mark the stonework; moon and weather have eaten the wood. Only birds, cats, polecats, rats, and mice disturb the silence. The whole scene seems stamped with a single word: Mystery.From the street a gate riddled with holes reveals a courtyard of cracked walls, disjointed steps, and broken gutters. What calamity befell it? What curse rests here? The empty house is an enigma.Once a small fief called La Grande Bretèche, it became the doctor’s secret pleasure during his stay in Vendôme. While attending a rich patient he found the unrevealed thought of the dwelling irresistible. He often climbed the wild hedge into the ownerless garden, scratched by briars, lingering for hours, inventing romances and melancholy to match the scene; had he known the real cause of the neglect the unwritten poetry would have been lost. The house suggested provincial life, contemplative and grave-like; sometimes he imagined lepers there, sometimes the Atridae; he wept there; he never laughed.Often involuntary terrors seized him—a wood-pigeon’s hum sounding ominous; damp earth and reptiles requiring caution; an icy chill settling as if a hand had lain on his shoulder. One evening a weathercock’s creak made him shudder; finishing a gloomy drama, he returned to his inn lost in shadowy thought. After supper his hostess entered with an air of mystery and announced, “Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.”“Who is Monsieur Regnault?” he asked.The hostess left and a tall, slim man in black appeared—hat in hand, with a receding forehead and a colorless face. His coat was shabby, but he wore a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.“Monsieur,” the doctor asked, “whom have I the honor of addressing?”“Dear me, it is very cold. I am Monsieur Regnault,” the man answered, rubbing his hands. “I am notary at Vendôme.”Regnault warned the doctor that walking in La Grande Bretèche’s garden was trespass and, as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret, begged him to discontinue the practice. Bound to the testatrix’s directions, he could not allow repairs or entry. He had never set foot in the house since the will was read; they had merely verified doors and windows to assess taxes. “Ah! my dear sir,” he said, “her will made a great commotion in the town.”Once a head-clerk in a Paris office ruined by bankruptcy, Regnault had come to Vendôme after failing to buy a Paris practice. He married into local ties; three months after being licensed he was summoned one evening by Madame la Comtesse de Merret to her château. She had been living at Merret after the Count went to Paris and died miserably two months before Regnault arrived. The Count and Countess had behaved eccentrically for months, admitting no visitors; Madame lived on the ground floor and Monsieur on the first; she was little seen except at church and had become altered. People whispered she was not quite right. When she left La Grande Bretèche for Merret she had dismantled the house and, it was said, burned furniture.Curiosity brought Regnault to the château that night. The maid had told him the Countess had received the Sacrament and might not live through the night. He crossed great, lofty, dark, cold rooms to the state bedroom. The chamber was old-fashioned, papered in brown, diabolically cold and thick with dust; a single old Argand lamp lighted it and a high tester bed hung with chintz dominated the scene. A small table bore an “Imitation of Christ” and the lamp; a deep armchair and two small chairs completed the scant furnishing. There was no fire. The room seemed a stage for a funeral.Under the lamp, Madame de Merret lay in the bed. She was yellow as wax and narrow as two folded hands; her lace cap showed abundant but white hair. Sitting up with difficulty, her large black eyes were dimmed by fever; her forehead clammy; her fleshless hands like bones beneath soft skin—veins and muscles visible. She had once been handsome; now she was a phantom. Those who wrapped her later said none had ever lived so emaciated. Her pale violet lips barely moved when she spoke.Although Regnault had often tended the dying, the lonely, silent woman in her vast château affected him deeply. He stood motionless, absorbed in her. At last her large eyes moved; with great effort she tried to raise her right hand and murmured in a breath, “I have waited for you with the greatest impatience.” A bright flush rose on her cheeks; speaking was a great effort.The housekeeper whispered, “Do not speak; Madame la Comtesse is not in a state to bear the slightest noise.” Regnault sat. After a few moments the Countess summoned strength to slide her right hand under the bolster and, with a last effort, withdrew a sealed paper, drops of perspiration on her brow. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” Her hand seized a crucifix; she lifted it to her lips and died.The expression in her eyes haunted Regnault; joy had been in her last glance. He brought away the will. When opened, it named him executor and left the whole property to the Vendôme hospital except for a few legacies. As for La Grande Bretèche, it ordered that the house be left, for fifty years from her death, in the state it then occupied: absolutely no entry into the apartments, no repairs of any sort, and watchmen to enforce the decree if necessary. After fifty years, if her will had been faithfully executed, the house would become the property of Regnault’s heirs; otherwise it reverts to legal heirs only after complying with conditions in a codicil sealed until the expiration of the fifty years. The will had not been contested.Regnault’s recital made the doctor feel he still saw the dying woman. When asked whether he knew the motives behind the testatrix’s strange directions, Regnault, with comic reticence, refused to criticize a benefactor who had given him a diamond. Still, the doctor soon coaxed from him the town’s gossip—contradictory and diffuse opinions that nearly put him to sleep. The notary left pleased, making a wry joke on the stairs about living forty-five years longer and taking a pinch of snuff.Later, the innkeeper, Madame Lepas, a buxom Flemish woman with an innkeeper’s quick scrutiny, asked if Regnault had told the story of La Grande Bretèche. When the doctor repeated the tale, she leaned forward eagerly. He suspected she knew more than she first let on; she confessed she had a secret that tormented her conscience and wished to consult him, believing he was a man of judgment and incapable of betraying her. Though she swore she would not tell what could compromise him, she asked him to listen.Madame Lepas then related another thread. When the Emperor sent Spaniards and prisoners to the region, she had been required to lodge, at the government’s expense, a young Spaniard on parole who had to present himself daily to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee—Bagos de Férédia, she recorded his name in her books. He was a handsome little man, barely five-foot-two or three, well made, with small, delicate hands he groomed carefully; thick black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion she admired despite prejudice against Spanish looks. He wore the finest linen she had ever seen—even finer than that of the nobles and princes she had lodged. He ate little but had polite, amiable ways. He spoke little and rarely answered, which she attributed to a Spanish mannerism.He read his breviary like a priest and attended mass regularly, always taking a place within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. At first no one suspected a purpose. He never looked up from his book. In the evenings he walked among the ruins of the old castle on the hill—his only amusement, which reminded him, people said, of his native Spain. “They say that Spain is all hills!” she concluded.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening soon after he arrived the Spaniard stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we had learned to accept his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys thought he had seen the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he did come back, I warned him about the weeds, and he seemed annoyed at being seen.Eventually, one morning we discovered he hadn’t returned at all. Searching his belongings, I found a note in his drawer and fifty doubloons—about five thousand francs—plus a sealed box of diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note said that if he didn’t come back, he left this money and the diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.My husband went looking for him and, curiously, returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near La Grande Bretèche. He went early, unseen. After reading the letter, he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced the Spaniard had escaped.The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas suspected the Spaniard had drowned.
