I, however, thought he was linked to the affair of Madame de Merret, especially since Rosalie told me the crucifix her mistress had cherished and been buried with was ebony and silver; early in his stay Monsieur Férédia had a similar crucifix, which I never saw again. Now, monsieur, should I feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs? Aren’t they truly mine?“Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?” I asked.“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.”After a brief talk my hostess left me with vague sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread—the feeling one has in entering a dark church at night, with a faint light under a high vault. La Grande Bretèche—with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and empty rooms—appeared vividly. I felt compelled to explore that mysterious house to reach the heart of this solemn story, this drama that had claimed three lives.Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme to me. Watching her, I sensed something inward beneath her robust health: an element of sorrow or hope, as if her soul held a secret. Yet she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile free of criminality; she looked innocent in her large checked kerchief and lilac-and-white-striped gown. I resolved not to leave Vendôme without learning La Grande Bretèche’s full story. To do so I would make advances with Rosalie if necessary.“Rosalie!” I called one evening.“Your servant, sir?”“You’re not married?” She flinched.“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!” she laughed, quickly regaining composure.“Yes; you’re attractive enough not to lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?”“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.”Her reply seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared central to this romance; she seemed bound to its core. It wasn’t an ordinary courtship affair—this girl held the last chapter of a romance. From that moment my attention fixed on her. By studying her I noticed many admirable qualities; she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying all the charms desire can give a woman, whatever her station. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one morning I said to Rosalie:“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.”“Oh!” she replied, “I will tell you, but keep it a secret.”“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.”“If it’s all the same to you,” she said, “I’d prefer it be with your own.”She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled to tell the tale; one needs a certain attitude to tell a story. The best tales are told at a proper hour—just as we are here at table. No one tells a story well standing or fasting.If I reproduced Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence, a whole volume would scarce suffice. Since the event she recounted sits between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s account, I will summarize it succinctly.Madame de Merret’s bedchamber at La Grande Bretèche was on the ground floor. A small built-in cupboard about four feet deep served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question, Madame de Merret had been so ill that her husband had begun sleeping on the first floor. One evening he returned two hours later than usual from the club, delayed by animated discussions and losses at billiards. His wife assumed he was in bed. For some time Monsieur de Merret had relied on Rosalie to tell him if his wife was in bed; her affirmative replies led him to his room, trusting her completely. That evening, however, he went to check on his wife to share his misfortunes and perhaps find comfort; he had noticed her especially lovely at dinner and thought her recovery had enhanced her beauty.Upon entering, he thought he heard the closet door shut, but when he entered, Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace. He felt a suspicion, and noticed anxiety in her eyes.“You’re very late,” she said, her voice slightly husky.Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing with folded arms.“Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?” his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He did not answer.“You can go, Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said; “I can manage my curl-papers myself.” She wanted to be alone with her husband, sensing disaster. Once Rosalie left—or was thought to have left, for she lingered in the passage—Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, declaring coldly, “Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!” She calmly replied, “No, monsieur.”This “No” pierced Monsieur de Merret’s heart; he did not believe it, yet his wife looked more saintly than ever. He moved toward the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand and stopped him. In strange emotion she said, “Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.”The dignity of her stance filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve that could be described as fierce.“No, Josephine,” he said, “I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.” At these words Madame de Merret looked haggard. “Here is your crucifix,” he continued. “Swear to me before God that there’s no one in that closet; I will believe you—I will never open that door.”Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, “I swear it.”“Louder,” he insisted; “repeat: ‘I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.’” She repeated the words without flinching.“That will do,” said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a pause he added, “You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before,” examining the ebony-and-silver crucifix.“I found it at Duvivier’s; last year when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme he bought it from a Spanish monk.”“Indeed,” said Monsieur de Merret, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.He waited for her to come. “Go to bed, all of you,” he told the household, and called Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, close. He whispered, “When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.”Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife as he gave orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss billiards and the club. When Rosalie returned she found them conversing amiably.Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings put in all the ground-floor reception rooms. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme, and he had ordered a large quantity, knowing he could sell any surplus. This circumstance supplied the means for his plan.“Gorenflot is here, sir,” Rosalie whispered.“Tell him to come in,” he said aloud.Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason.“Gorenflot,” her husband said, “go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.” Pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered, “Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning I’ll give you a passport to go abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, paid on your return if you fulfill the conditions. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, payable on your wedding day, provided you marry Gorenflot; but to marry, you must stay silent. If not, no wedding gift!”“Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said, “come and brush my hair.”Her husband paced quietly, watching the door, the mason, and his wife without revealing suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise. Madame de Merret caught a moment when he was unloading bricks to whisper to Rosalie: “My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.” Then she added casually, “You’d better help him.”Monsieur and Madame de Merret kept silent while Gorenflot began walling the cupboard. Their silence was deliberate; the husband did not want to give his wife any ambiguous chance. From her side it was either pride or prudence. When the wall was half built, the crafty mason, taking advantage of his master’s back being turned, broke one of the two panes at the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood Rosalie had urged this. They all then saw the face of a dark, gloomy man with black hair and fiery eyes.Before her husband turned around, the poor woman nodded to the stranger, signaling “Hope.”At four o’clock, as dawn broke in September, the work was finished. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.The next morning he mentioned casually, “Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.” He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy; “He will go to Duvivier’s,” she thought.As soon as he left, Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrifying voice, “The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.”In an instant Rosalie brought her mistress a cleaver; with a frenzy she began demolishing the wall. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.“Lay madame on her bed,” he said coldly.Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set a trap; he had only written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder was being repaired.“Duvivier,” Monsieur de Merret asked, “didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?”“No, monsieur.”“Very well; thank you,” he said, casting a fierce glance at his wife. “Jean,” he added, turning to his valet, “you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.”The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. In the early days, when there were noises from the cupboard and Josephine wished to intercede for the man dying inside, he silenced her: “You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.”After this story all the ladies rose from the table, breaking the spell Bianchon had cast over them. Some of them had shivered at those final words.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################During the Restoration the Archbishop of Besançon was among the few who frequented the devout Baronne de Watteville’s salon. Monsieur de Watteville, though descended from the notorious Watteville, was gentle and unassuming. After living quietly in La Franche-Comté he married Mademoiselle de Rupt, heiress of the Rupt family; the match, arranged in 1802 and formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration, brought substantial income. After her grandparents’ deaths Madame de Watteville grew more devout, and her piety influenced Besançon’s upper circles.Monsieur, thin and unremarkable, worked as a turner and collected geological specimens; some admired his noble spirit. Realizing he would never dominate his wife, he threw himself into work and hobbies. The Rupt house, well furnished and hospitable, contrasted with his modest estate, and their ties to the Archbishop attracted notable abbés.At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834 guests welcomed Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable verdict. The Vicar-General, calling the victory unexpected, credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron; when asked, he said Savaron lived nearby but of unknown origin, a detail that piqued interest. Amedée de Soulas, present that evening, would later prove significant.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################France and England have long exchanged cultural influences—especially in language and fashion—so that what seems "English" in Paris becomes "French" in London. Examples include Lulli composing "God Save the King" for a French play, hoop skirts that passed from ridicule to European fashion, and English words like "dandy" and "lion" entering French via Alfred de Musset, sparking a vogue for stylish young men from Paris outward.In 1834 Besançon, Amedée-Sylvain de Soulas, of Spanish descent, embodied this cosmopolitan affectation. Ill at ease in the dull garrison town, he kept a modest, sparsely furnished apartment while cultivating an extravagant appearance. He employed a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger," dressed him fashionably, and lavished expense on wardrobes he could barely afford—often dining out to avoid household costs. His grooming, Parisian knowledge, and distinctive touches (like trouser-straps) made him a local "lion," admired by the town's aristocratic "Colony" eager to seem modern before visiting Parisians.Amedée’s aim was to impress the elite and win Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville. Raised strictly and modestly under her mother’s watchful, jealous supervision, Rosalie was sheltered, skilled in domestic duties but lacking social polish; she had an inner strength yet was forced to repress feelings as her mother's protectiveness complicated any courtship. Though Amedée charmed the elite and engagement rumors circulated, Rosalie’s upbringing and Madame de Watteville’s influence made the match fraught. His story illustrates social ambition, cultural exchange, and the constraints of provincial society.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Madame,” Monsieur de Soulas said to the Baroness as he waited for his soup to cool, “one morning a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and took the first‑floor rooms in Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered at the mairie as ‘ALBERT SAVARON’ and was listed among the barristers.”“The Savaron name is famous,” Mademoiselle de Watteville observed.“He’s a Frenchman, not of the Brabant line,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he claims the Savaron arms he must add a bar‑sinister. The only remaining Brabant heir is a rich Miss de Savarus.”“The bar‑sinister denotes a bastard, but even a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble,” Rosalie added.“Enough,” the Baroness interrupted. “You insisted she learn heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville said with a smile. “She knows it well.”“Please continue,” the Baroness urged.“In a town like Besançon everything is catalogued,” Soulas went on. “Albert Savaron was received without fuss by local lawyers, who wondered who had sent him and what he hoped to do—especially since he left his card with judges instead of calling. Three days later no one paid him much mind. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. Savaron kept to himself; no one had seen much of him.”“Does he go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“He attends the early service at Saint‑Pierre on Sundays. He rises very early, works until eight, breakfasts, walks in his garden—fifty or sixty turns—dines, and is in bed by six or seven.”“How do you know all this?” Madame de Chavoncourt wanted to know.“I live on Rue Neuve, opposite his house; my servant speaks with Jerome.”“And you gossip with Babylas?” someone laughed.“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked the Vicar‑General.“The President of the Court assigned Savaron to defend a half‑witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man innocent and exposed the real culprits, who were arrested. That defense impressed the Court and the jury. Then a merchant put a difficult case in his hands, which he won. Monsieur Berryer advised us to employ him, promising success. I met him and had confidence in him—and I was right.”“Is he extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“Certainly,” the Vicar‑General replied.“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.“The first time I met him,” the Abbé de Grancey recounted, “he received me in an outer room lined with books, modestly furnished. I had not expected a young advocate. His dress was odd—black dressing gown, red cord, red slippers, red waistcoat, red smoking cap.”“The devil’s colors!” Madame de Watteville exclaimed.“Yes,” the Abbé continued, “but he has a magnificent head. Thick black hair streaked with gray, a round white throat, a broad forehead marked by thought, an olive complexion, a square nose, and eyes like an eagle. He is passionate yet calm; his voice is sweet but can thunder.”“Monsieur Savaron is of average height, with hands like a prelate,” the Abbé added.“The second time I saw him he received me in his shabby bedroom; the third I found him having a frugal breakfast in his library. He is no ordinary man; there is something heavy on him. His face is terrible and gentle at once; he seems to carry a great burden.”“Why would such an eloquent man leave Paris for Besançon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little a stranger can do here?”“After seeing his head and hearing him reply to a leading Paris lawyer,” the Abbé said, “I believe he will yet make a name.”The Abbé’s portrait of Savaron took root in Rosalie. For the first time she encountered someone who seemed truly exceptional, unlike the dull Count who had courted her. He presented an enigma: eloquent, serious, isolated, and apparently burdened. She replayed the Abbé’s words—especially about his forehead and hands—and felt a restless curiosity.“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.“I’m not hungry, mamma,” she answered, lost in thought.She realized she had seen a lighted window from her bed that belonged to him. “I must see him,” she decided.“Is the Chapter’s lawsuit settled?” she asked the Abbé, trying to appear casual.“It might go to the Court of Appeal, but our adversaries will hesitate,” he said.After dinner she listened for any mention of Savaron, but the talk moved on to other matters. Eager for bedtime, she planned to wake between two and three to glimpse his windows. When the hour came she felt a thrill watching the candlelight through the trees and thought of the furniture and color of his rooms. “While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God,” she imagined.At home the question of educating girls appeared in a whisper: too much enlightenment risks precociousness, too much suppression breeds rebellion. For Rosalie, the Abbé’s portrait had pierced restraint and kindled a desire—part romantic, part intellectual—to know this man.The next morning, while dressing, she saw Albert walking in the garden beside the Hotel de Rupt. “At least I can see him here,” she thought, and resolved to engineer an opportunity to enter his house and hear his voice.She spent the day embroidering with distracted air. She timed a confession to leave just before the eight o’clock mass, hoping to encounter Savaron. She visited her father in his workshop and suggested he carve larger pieces and build a grotto in the garden to display his work. He delighted in the idea; she warned him not to tell her mother.She imagined the grotto with an observatory that would give her a view into Savaron’s private room. Young girls often contrive elaborate lengths for the sake of a man, even if he never knows.When Sunday arrived Rosalie dressed with care; her maid Mariette noticed her nervousness. She arrived at church before mass and took a place where she might see Savaron enter. He had a solemn, burdened air; his gaze was intense. To Rosalie, everything about him now seemed filled with meaning. Boldly she stood in his path to exchange glances, and a warmth surged through her.After confession she found a seat to watch him; by the time Mass ended he had gone. “The Vicar‑General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besançon? I must know everything!” With renewed resolve she turned her thoughts to the grotto plan.“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has an idea for a Belvedere in the garden. Do you approve?”“I approve of whatever amuses your father,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly.“From there we’ll be able to see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms, and he may see us—perhaps remarks will be made—”“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie?” her mother snapped.“I’ll say no more, mamma,” she answered. But the plan pressed on. Monsieur de Watteville and their carpenter devised a grotto hidden from one neighbor yet giving a view toward Savaron’s windows. Periwinkle, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper would frame a rustic interior with a mirror, ottoman, and bark‑inlaid table. A chandelier carved by Monsieur de Watteville himself completed the idea. The garden project was commissioned and rumors spread: the Wattevilles were building a lovely belvedere—“a thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.Rosalie felt a flush of pride. No one suspected that the supposedly simple‑witted girl had planned the arrangement chiefly to see Savaron’s private study.Meanwhile Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten amid envy from other lawyers. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings, which increased his anonymity. Yet he kept winning complex cases: three successes at the Commercial Tribunal brought him four prominent merchant clients who recognized his legal skill.On the day the Wattevilles inaugurated their Belvedere, Savaron was launching an enterprise of his own. Quietly, through merchant connections, he planned a fortnightly paper, the Eastern Review. It was financed by forty shares of five hundred francs each subscribed by his first ten clients, who saw the need to promote Besançon as a trade center.To compete with Strasbourg and Dijon, Besançon needed commerce and enlightenment. Savaron wanted to challenge the literary dominance of the West and bring light to Eastern France. His ideas appealed to merchants who fancied they were furthering their own interests.Savaron kept his name out of the public foreground. He let his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, handle accounts, while Savaron retained editorial control and a share of any profits. The Review invited contributions from various regions and, with a low subscription price, quickly gained five hundred subscribers.To avoid bruising local pride, Savaron invited Alfred Boucher, Monsieur Boucher’s eager son, to manage the literary side. Savaron subtly guided Alfred’s contributions while letting him believe he was in charge. The merchants were pleased: with a few more subscribers the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the nominal editor remained unpaid.After the third issue the Review began to be noticed by other French publications; Savaron read them at home. The third number contained a tale attributed to him that stirred talk in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, though some of Besançon’s higher circles dismissed it for its liberal sentiments.“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a Review published in Besançon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room—mamma won’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie, subscribed and lent her the four issues. That night she devoured the tale—the first she had ever read. Given her limited reading experience, its effect on her was profound. She hoped to find in it the sentiments or even the life of Albert. From the opening pages she felt convinced it reflected him; critics would later note that the author had drawn from his own experiences as if confessing.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1823 two young men left Lucerne for a holiday around the lake of the Four Cantons. Passing the hamlet of Gersau, Rodolphe was captivated by a new house and a woman at the window and decided to stay; his friend Leopold continued the trip.Rodolphe and Leopold had studied law together: Leopold was headed for a notary’s post in Paris, while Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more unsettled past. His mother had sacrificed everything for his education; passionate and impulsive, he contrasted with Leopold’s steady calm.Rodolphe lodged with the Bergmanns, who told him the woman was Fanny Lovelace, recently moved with her elderly father for his health. Moderately well off, they lived simply; Fanny supported her father by translating and writing and was noted for her beauty and musical talent. Exploring their gardens, Rodolphe noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, whose presence suggested a distinctive past. The Bergmanns were protective, but Rodolphe was determined to meet Fanny.That evening he heard Fanny sing and was enchanted. When he later encountered Fanny and Gina, an unknown assailant attacked and wounded him; Fanny and Gina aided him and he was taken to safety. While recovering, Rodolphe learned his mother had died, a blow that plunged him into deep grief; he found solace in Fanny’s company. They bonded over shared interests; he confessed his feelings, only to learn she was married to an older man, Emilio.Despite the marriage, Fanny’s affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments. She revealed she had married Emilio under duress and that he was not as old as he seemed. Torn between love and reality, Rodolphe was encouraged by Fanny to pursue his ambitions, but jealousy and insecurity crept in—exacerbated by the appearance of Tito, a man from Fanny’s past. Their dreams of freedom and a future together clashed with Rodolphe’s ambitions and Fanny’s loyalty, the beautiful setting reflecting intense but precarious emotions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For twenty days Rodolphe stayed with the Bergmanns, watching Francesca almost unconsciously. He saw in her a mix of youthful imprudence and an unbent spirit: she sometimes resisted love, sometimes yielded. She treated the old man like a father; her gratitude revealed a native nobility that intrigued him and made her an enigma.Those days combined secret joys and melancholy; their quarrels were as delightful as their reconciliations. Rodolphe grew attached to Francesca’s steady tenderness, which often showed as jealous exactness over trifles.One evening, when Francesca declared she wanted to leave Gersau because it lacked luxury, Rodolphe asked if she cared for luxury. She answered passionately, equating it with an appreciation for art and beauty and insisting she had never complained in hardship. With a mischievous air she argued it was natural for ordinary people to aspire to greatness, revealing her character.She then begged Rodolphe not to accompany them to Geneva, citing town gossip and her wish to protect her husband’s reputation. Though she affirmed her love for Rodolphe, she demanded it remain concealed; she kissed him and left him astonished.The next day the Bergmanns’ lodgers departed early. Restless, Rodolphe went to Vevay and then to Geneva at the end of October, taking a long route. To avoid the town’s discomforts he rented a room in Eaux‑Vives. He inquired about Italian refugees, expecting Lamporani; the landlord said the only new arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini.On his first walk to the Villa Diodati the beauty of the place struck him. Approaching, he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady and felt dizzy with surprise. Learning they were at the Colonna residence lifted a veil: the implications of their past interactions became clear. He feared he had been a mere whim for Francesca and struggled with the reality of her rank.He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was received by a man posing as Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from requesting an audience with the Princess and returned to Eaux‑Vives with mingled anticipation and anxiety, feeling he must elevate his position to match hers.At the concert she was dazzling; their eyes met in an electric instant. Francesca’s beauty and grace captivated him, and he felt convinced she had remained faithful despite her rank. After the concert he was introduced to the Colonnas and to Francesca, who playfully styled herself “Princess Rodolphini.” The jest revived memories of Gersau; he was overwhelmed by her voice and presence.In the following days Francesca perceived Rodolphe’s deep love, which for her felt natural. She said their affection should be faithful and enduring, whatever the distance. Rodolphe, however, was driven by ambition: he wanted greatness so Francesca could be proud.Francesca combined gaiety and tenderness. Once destined for a cloister, family misfortunes had forced her to marry Prince Gandolphini to preserve her family’s standing. Despite noble birth, she had a taste for independence and liberal ideas, inclining her toward revolutionary sentiments.Rodolphe spent the winter in Geneva where time flew in her company. With spring came inner turmoil: Francesca’s calmness contrasted with his unrest. He wished she would show more feeling. One day on the terrace he made a bitter complaint about the happiness of others, wounding Francesca, who withdrew in tears. He found her, kissed her in a moment of passion; she repelled him and asserted her dignity. She then ordered him to leave, declaring they would not meet again until Naples. Rodolphe, recognizing the strength of her will, complied.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On his return to Paris, Rodolphe received a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner, who had refused many women. Rodolphe doubted the Prince could persuade him, but Francesca charmed Schinner into painting an original for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio, announcing it in a delightful letter — the start of a regular correspondence between Rodolphe and Francesca, their sole indulgence.Ambitious and in love, Rodolphe invested everything in a venture that failed through deceit, costing him three years' struggle. The Villele ministry fell just as he faced ruin. Seeking solace, he turned to politics but first went to Naples to recover at Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored. A three-month respite healed him and renewed his courage.He tried to rebuild his fortune; talents noticed and a high post promised were dashed by the July 1830 storm. Only Francesca and God witnessed his brave, unlucky efforts. Sustained by love, he returned to new challenges, guided by a faithful presence and devoted heart. Lovers! Pray for him!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Mademoiselle de Watteville finished her tale, Rosalie blushed and wept. It was her first encounter with such a love story—told through a man's experiences—and she became consumed by jealousy toward Francesca Colonna, whom she believed to be Albert's beloved.Driven by curiosity and a fierce desire to win Albert, Rosalie, insecure about her looks and lack of musical talent, feared he could never love her. Her imagination spun schemes typical of a sheltered girl: sneaking into his garden, writing to him, or contriving meetings at her father's land dispute to draw him into her drawing-room.While watching Albert's window she saw something that might expose his private life: Jérôme, Albert's servant, being helped over a wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie noted Mariette's plainness but also her steadiness and means—explanations for Jérôme's interest—and learned a dangerous lesson: her mother's strict upbringing could be undermined by a servant's choices.The next morning Rosalie shadowed Mariette to Mass and confronted her about the secret meetings. Offering support in exchange for access to letters Albert passed through Jérôme, Rosalie found Mariette tempted and willing.Obsession deepened as Rosalie read Albert's correspondence. A letter to his friend Léopold revealed noble lineage, past struggles, and ambitious plans, while portraying a man devoted to the woman he loved—Francesca. Entranced, Rosalie felt both admiration and acute inadequacy. She wrestled with guilt for spying but rationalized it as proof of love, taking on penances and self-imposed suffering to demonstrate devotion.When Mariette later brought a letter meant for Francesca, Rosalie, despite hesitation, read it. It confirmed Albert's enduring love and hopes for a shared future. Struck by their fidelity and her own longing, she chose to step back, return the letter with Mariette, and begin to accept the sorrow of unrequited affection.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Rosalie spent the winter of 1834–35 in turmoil; by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. In solitude that imagined struggle fed her passion and dark plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist, and her story is a cautionary tale.That winter Albert de Savarus made notable advances in Besançon, eagerly awaiting the Chamber’s dissolution. Backed by a wealthy contractor allied with moderates, he championed a contested water supply issue: while the Doubs flowed nearby, he argued the river water was undrinkable and promoted bringing water from Arcier, a distant hill, even proposing the restoration of a Roman aqueduct. Local Review articles echoed him, and townspeople across factions bought the romance of drinking like the Romans. The campaign, driven by hidden interests and lucrative contracts, dismissed opponents as old-fashioned. After eighteen months Savarus had stirred the stagnant town and gained influence without genuine popularity.He also won several lawsuits for local priests over the winter, and his ambition consumed him. His apparent indifference to money masked a form of moral usury: he expected far greater returns. In October 1834 he financed a struggling merchant with funds from Léopold Hannequin, which also aided his electability. The Abbé de Grancey, who saw Savarus’s ambition clearly, remarked on his formidable nature and hinted he’d chosen a wrong turn in life.Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hôtel de Rupt, at first content to watch him from afar. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, produced ten thousand francs a year, but management was neglected by the Baron, who left it to an old servant, Modinier. The Baron’s indifference sprang from his wife’s substantial income; the family often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot shaped by the famous Watteville. Watteville had built a dam and lake, enhancing the estate, but his heirs maintained a dubious claim over the village of Riceys, which historically held rights to the land. The Baron’s actions left a legacy of usurpation that his descendants continued.Rosalie’s efforts were complex and subtle. In May 1835 she left Besançon with her father for les Rouxey; the lake’s beauty captivated her and she associated it with love. On a suggested boat trip her father confided legal troubles with Riceys over land rights. Rosalie urged him to hire Savarus as their lawyer, insisting the Dent de Vilard must be secured and that a lawsuit would enliven their lives. She pressed to bring Savarus to les Rouxey, revealing her determination to secure her future and create beautiful gardens.Four days later the Abbé de Grancey visited Savarus to press him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing such alignment would compromise his neutrality. The Abbé urged him to consider the political advantages, especially votes. Rosalie meanwhile tried to keep Savarus in Besançon and prevent his election, even writing to the préfet, who ignored her.As the election approached, influential men in Besançon nominated Savarus—surprising him. He delivered a passionate speech outlining his vision and ambitions, yet faced opposition: the préfet worked to undermine him and his support eroded. The first voting day disappointed him; he returned in despair and thought of Francesca. Just then the Abbé arrived with encouraging news: he had secured some support, promising that winning the Rouxey lawsuit could bring crucial votes and urging Savarus to visit the Wattevilles. The Abbé even suggested marriage to Sidonie de Chavoncourt to solidify Savarus’s standing. Initially resistant, Savarus recognized the advantages. With the Abbé’s guidance, he prepared to navigate the intertwined complexities of love and politics, aware that his future hinged on imminent choices.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By nine the next evening Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were full of Besançon’s aristocracy, assembled over the unusual decision to go to the polls for the daughter of the Rupts. The man to be presented was the former Master of Appeals, secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch. Madame de Chavoncourt arrived with her younger daughter Sidonie, elegantly dressed; her elder daughter, secure in her relationship, had made no effort—an omission noted in a small town. The Abbe de Grancey moved among the groups, listening and directing the talk.“If the Elder Branch returns,” he asked an elderly statesman, “what politicians would they find?” They spoke of Berryer, isolated on his bench and likely to unsettle the Government, of the Duc de Fitz-James in Toulouse, and how a vote for Monsieur Savarus might draw Republicans toward them instead of the Moderates.By half-past nine Albert had not arrived, and Madame de Watteville took his lateness as a personal slight. Madame de Chavoncourt urged calm, suggesting he might be detained by a consultation. Rosalie, watching the two, noted her mother’s indulgence toward Albert, which hinted at a possible match between him and Sidonie.At ten the delay grew embarrassing; some gentlemen began to play cards. The Abbe de Grancey, perplexed, went to a window where Rosalie stood and exclaimed, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville, and Rosalie went into the garden to check Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent. “Jérôme!” Rosalie called; the servant confirmed Albert had left in a post-chaise. The Abbe cried, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s face, however, betrayed a joy the Vicar-General pretended to ignore.Back in the drawing-room Monsieur de Watteville announced Albert’s unexplained departure. By eleven-thirty only about fifteen guests remained: Madame de Chavoncourt, the Abbe de Godenars, the two Chavoncourt girls, Monsieur de Vauchelles, the Abbe de Grancey, Rosalie, Amedée de Soulas—and a few others. The Abbe stationed himself near the Baroness to watch Rosalie, visibly agitated.A servant then brought a letter for the Abbe de Grancey on a silver tray. Urged to read it, he watched Rosalie turn pale, recognized the handwriting, folded the letter and slipped it into his pocket. He understood she loved Albert. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie stopped him. “Monsieur de Grancey, it was from Albert!” she cried, and, when pressed, burst out, “I love him! What is the matter?” He told her Albert was withdrawing from the election. Stunned, she begged him to keep the news secret, knowing that if the election failed so might her sister’s chance with Albert.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On her way to Mass, Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette that Albert had abruptly disappeared. An elderly Parisian had arrived at the Hotel National, summoned Monsieur Savaron, and left with a large package after a long meeting; Savaron, pale and distraught, bought an old carriage, ordered post-horses, left his keys, told his servant to find other employment, and fled. The Prefect quickly granted him a passport. The episode stirred Besançon prejudice against outsiders; most soon forgot Albert, except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who alone knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.By April 1836 there was still no news. The Baroness de Watteville pressed Rosalie to marry; the Baron offered her the family estate, les Rouxey, but the Baroness, worried about finances, arranged a match with Monsieur de Soulas. Rosalie firmly refused. The refusal became public during a summer at les Rouxey; after Mariette and Jerome married the Baron improved the estate, which the Baroness resented. The Vicar-General tried to mediate.Rosalie then confessed to him that she had interfered in Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo — intercepting letters and engineering their separation. The Vicar-General, horrified, stressed the moral gravity of her actions and urged her to seek forgiveness.Her guilt deepened when a Paris clerk arrived to sell Albert’s effects and revealed that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote and received a letter from Albert expressing complete forgiveness and his wish to remain in solitude. He urged Rosalie to reconcile with her mother and accept de Soulas, but she refused to sacrifice her mother’s happiness. After the Vicar-General’s death, Madame de Watteville married de Soulas, believing Rosalie’s support.Now of age and wealthy, Rosalie encouraged that marriage to secure her independence, went to Paris determined to confront the Duchess, and at a ball publicly confessed her role in Albert’s downfall, shocking the Duchess. She returned to Besançon burdened by the emotional cost. The story closed as a lasting tale of love, betrayal, and the consequences of ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone at les Rouxey—riding, hunting and improving her estate—and was well known in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, mother of two, looks younger than her husband, who has grown old; he told young Chavoncourt, "My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!"Known for eccentric "vagaries," she yearly visited the walls of the Grande Chartreuse, perhaps imitating a grand-uncle who once burst through its walls. In 1841 she left Besançon—rumored to be marrying—but returned after a Loire steamboat boiler explosion that cost her a right arm and a left leg and left disfiguring facial scars. Her health declined; in constant pain, she now lives in religious seclusion at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.PARIS, May 1842.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Beauseant, Vicomtesse de: Father Goriot; The Deserted Woman- Genovese: Massimilla Doni- Hannequin, Léopold: Béatrix; Cousin Betty; Cousin Pons- Jeanrenaud: The Commission in Lunacy- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman- Rhetore, Duc Alphonse de: A Bachelor’s Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Letters of Two Brides; The Member for Arcis- Savaron de Savarus and Albert Savaron de: The Quest of the Absolute- Schinner, Hippolyte: The Purse; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierre Grassou; A Start in Life; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; The Imaginary Mistress; The Unconscious Humorists- Tinti, Clarina: Massimilla Doni############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Monsieur de Manerville was a respectable Norman gentleman whom the Maréchal de Richelieu married to a wealthy Bordeaux heiress when the old duke administered Guienne. The Norman sold his Bessin estate, adopted a Gascon life at his wife’s château de Lanstrac, and bought the post of major of the Gate Guards toward the end of Louis XV’s reign. He escaped the Revolution and lived on until 1813.In late 1790 he went to Martinique, leaving his Guienne property under the care of Mathias, a notary’s clerk who sympathized with revolutionary ideas. On his return he found his estates intact and well managed—the Norman steadiness united to the Gascon energy. Madame de Manerville died in 1810. The old man, hardened by youthful dissipation, became penurious and stingy, giving almost nothing to his only son.Paul de Manerville returned from the college of Vendôme in 1810 and spent three formative years under his seventy-year-old father’s strict rule. That despotism stunted the development of resistance in a naturally bold Gascon; Paul learned to suppress feeling and to yield outwardly. His suppressed impulses hardened inwardly, and later, when they met social norms, he could reason correctly but act wrongly: impulsive in crises yet timid in sustained opposition, simple and credulous, a dupe and voluntary victim of circumstances. Confined in his father’s mansion and without the means to live among equals, he envied their pleasures without sharing them.Each evening the old man took him in a shabby carriage to royalist salons where the remnants of parliamentary and military nobility gathered—landed men humbled by mercantile wealth, who took pride in scorning commerce. Too young to grasp those social distinctions, Paul was bored, unaware that such connections would later secure a man’s preeminence. He found some solace in the manual exercises his father insisted on—fencing, riding, pistol practice, tennis, and courtly manners—believing these accomplishments a gentleman’s essentials. So Paul fenced, rode, fired at a pistol gallery, and read novels when not occupied by the enforced frivolities of nobility. Such monotony would have crushed him had not his father’s death set him free. He then became possessor of the considerable capital amassed by paternal avarice and of well-kept estates. Yet Bordeaux displeased him, and Lanstrac did not charm.Once his inheritance was settled, the young heir, eager for enjoyment, invested, entrusted estate administration to Mathias, and spent six years abroad: attached to the French embassy at Naples, secretary at Madrid, then in London—making a European tour. After seeing the world and dissipating his father’s savings, Paul returned to draw on the territorial revenues Mathias had been conserving. He resolved, in a seemingly virtuous impulse, to leave Paris, return to Bordeaux, regulate his affairs, live as a country gentleman at Lanstrac, improve his property, marry, and seek a deputy’s seat.Paul was a count; nobility again had matrimonial value. For seven hundred thousand francs squandered in six years he had bought what money cannot: the social experience of life—the knowledge drawn from love affairs, duels, lost bets, disillusions, and pleasures. He had become what is called elegant. He was not a mere man of fashion; in that burlesque army of worldlings, the man of elegance ranks above the mere fop. Paul maintained that reputation: his servants were well dressed, his carriages noted, his suppers in vogue; his bachelor establishment rivaled the best Paris houses. He had not seduced any woman permanently; he gambled openly, was honest in manner, left no incriminating billets-doux, and kept no chest of love-letters. Not wishing to touch his Guienne estates, he practiced no reckless extravagance; he did not borrow, though he foolishly lent to friends who deserted him. His dissipations had a method, the secret of a character molded by a tyrannical father into a kind of social mongrel.One morning he told his friend Henri de Marsay, already destined for celebrity, “My dear fellow, life has a meaning.”“You must be twenty-seven to find it,” de Marsay laughed.“Well, I am twenty-seven; and because I am twenty-seven I mean to live the country-gentleman life at Lanstrac. I’ll move my belongings to Bordeaux, and spend three months a year in Paris in this house I shall keep.”“Will you marry?”“I will marry.”De Marsay, after a pause, gave a devastating counternarrative. He warned that settling into the respectable roles of father and husband would make Paul ridiculous and miserable. Marriage, he said, shoves a man into a narrow social destiny: merchants marry for capital, peasants for labor, brokers and notaries for wives’ dots, kings for dynasties. To join this pack was to forgo the freedoms and chances of bachelor life. A bachelor with even modest income preserves an elegant reputation and many opportunities; marriage is social immolation, limiting ambition and exposing one to the petty cares and compromises that make men small. Even if Paul married a wealthy Englishwoman to preserve some independence, he would still be hampered by domestic obligations and the inevitable compromise of his habits.De Marsay outlined a compromise—marry “en grand seigneur”: entail the estate, have two legitimate children, give the wife a separate household, meet her only in society, keep distinct spheres. Two hundred thousand francs a year could support such aristocratic life, which preserves dignity and social advantage while retaining bachelor privileges. Yet he doubted Paul could manage even that: he questioned whether Paul, shaped by a childhood under a tyrant and by his own gentleness, could become the necessary head of a household. Children, de Marsay argued, love a weak or prodigal father whom they later despise; to be a good paterfamilias requires not mere will but inherent strength. Marriage, he insisted, produces many men who dishonor their names.Paul defended himself with a different logic. Tired of living for appearances—keeping horses and carriages that exist only to be shown—he longed for sincere domestic feeling. He did not envy de Marsay’s cool detachment from society’s prejudices; he belonged to the great majority of men made of emotions and needed the peaceful chances and changes of life. Often a man courts many women and wins none; Paul wanted a woman always at his side, a confidante to share interests and secrets. He wanted a close union so affection would not hinge on the turns of passion and would be steady against disillusion. He believed he had the courage to be a worthy husband and father, to submit to society’s conditions, to accept family joys.De Marsay ridiculed the sentiment as naive. He argued that the problem of marriage is not merely a matter of love but of human psychology and the social framework that courtship disguises. Youthful conquest thrives on obstacles and the thrill of the pursuit; marriage removes those obstacles and often produces repulsion instead of intimacy. A lover can sometimes convert a reluctant woman, but that success recollects an earlier defeat—like Napoleon winning but unable to erase his retreat. The ardor a woman admires in a lover appears brutal in a husband. Moreover, the Civil Code treated women as wards and minors; to govern such a person effectively requires the rod of fear. Do you, de Marsay asked, have the strength to govern by fear? Paul’s gentleness and confiding nature made him ill-suited to play the necessary tyrant. Marriage’s daily realities—calculating dowries, discoursing on morality, becoming a domestic man—would turn him into what he most feared: ordinary and ridiculous.He offered final counsel: remain a bachelor for thirteen years, enjoy life, and at forty, when gout and the tedium of youth set in, marry a widow of thirty-six; then you might be happy. If one insisted on youth, one would risk becoming a madman. De Marsay confessed amusement at life’s follies but hinted that he might have written a book on modern marriages’ paradoxes had he not chosen to spend his energies on immediate pleasures and on ensnaring hearts rather than theorizing.Paul was not swayed. He laughed at de Marsay’s statecraft and declared his desire for domestic consolation sincere: “I shall feel happy when my valet says in the morning, ‘Madame is awaiting monsieur for breakfast’; happier still at night, to return to find a heart—” De Marsay mocked the sentiment as “indecorous,” but pledged to continue treating Paul politely, proposing, as if in a final jest, to meet the Comtesse de Manerville when she appeared.They agreed to remain friends—Paul steadfast in his modest destiny of becoming a worthy father and husband, and de Marsay still convinced that marriage was a social trap requiring strength Paul might lack. In their banter and counsel Balzac lays out two competing philosophies: the romantic longing for domestic union and the cynical, pragmatic calculation that marriage confines and corrupts, with society’s laws and human nature conspiring to make husbands either tyrants or fools. The scene closes with the pair poised between practical counsel and sentimental resolve, the future introduction of Paul’s intended hinted at as the next movement in this moral and social drama.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Though the foregoing conversation affected the Comte de Manerville, he carried out his intentions and returned to Bordeaux in the winter of 1821.Restoring and furnishing his family mansion sustained the elegant reputation that preceded him. Reintroduced by old connections to Bordeaux’s royalist society—which his opinions, name, and fortune bound him to—he quickly took a fashionable pre‑eminence. The Faubourg Saint‑Germain of Bordeaux admired his knowledge of life and Parisian accomplishments. An old marquise revived a courtly phrase, calling him “the pink of fashion”; the liberal clique used it satirically, the royalists sincerely.Paul de Manerville fulfilled his flowery title. In public he seemed superior, and at ease displayed the fine qualities that accompanied his defects. His wit was mild; his manners not supercilious; his intercourse with women expressed a temperate respect they liked. His foppery was merely a carefulness of appearance that pleased. He respected rank, granted youth a certain freedom, and though adept with sword and pistol, was noted for a feminine gentleness. Medium height and plumpness did not prevent his playing the part of a Bordelais Brummell: fair complexion, handsome hands and feet, blue eyes fringed by long lashes, black hair, graceful motions, and a chest voice that vibrated in listeners’ hearts harmonized with his sobriquet. Paul was a delicate flower needing careful culture—one more to receive than to give happiness, with something of the woman in his nature—one to whom conjugal love should come as a providence.Such a character creates private difficulties but commands public attraction. Consequently Paul succeeded greatly in the narrow provincial circle, where his half‑tinted mind was better appreciated than it would have been in Paris.Restoring his town house and the château de Lanstrac—into which he introduced English country‑house comfort—consumed the capital the notary had saved over six years. Reduced now to his strict income of about forty thousand a year, he believed himself prudent in regulating his household accordingly.After flaunting his equipages, entertaining the town’s most distinguished young men, and giving hunting parties at Lanstrac, Paul saw that provincial life would not do without marriage. He was too young for stinginess or for speculative improvements provincials undertake to secure children; he needed the variety of Parisian life. A name to preserve, property to transmit, a household to create social relations, and a weariness of irregular connections were motives, but the chief was his secret love for Bordeaux’s queen, the great beauty Mademoiselle Evangelista.Around the century’s beginning a rich Spaniard named Evangelista had settled in Bordeaux; letters of recommendation and large fortune opened noble salons to him. His wife, a Creole from the Casa‑Reale, lived as a great lady, ignorant of money’s value and indulged by an adoring husband. From 1800 to 1812 the Evangelistas were objects of interest. The Spaniard died in 1813, leaving Madame Evangelista a thirty‑two‑year‑old widow with an immense fortune and a prettiest little girl of eleven who became an accomplished young woman. The Restoration shifted social positions; royalist ranks cleared and old families left Bordeaux. Lacking her husband’s direction, Madame Evangelista resolved not to change her mode of living: by squandering large sums on brilliant fêtes and a quasi‑regal life she maintained the public belief in her wealth while her capital steadily diminished.Natalie, now nineteen, seemed the richest match in Bordeaux, but proposals had not come. Accustomed to indulgence, she wore cashmeres and jewels and lived in a luxury that alarmed the calculating provincials. A fatal remark circulated: “None but a prince can afford to marry Mademoiselle Evangelista.” Mothers jealous of Natalie’s beauty and tyrannical elegance spread treacherous warnings—her costly toilette, horses, maid, Mechlin laces—deterring suitors. Pampered as she was, Natalie knew nothing of the value of money or household maintenance; she bounded through life unbridled and unshod.Six months after Paul’s arrival the Pink of Fashion and the Queen of Balls met in Bordeaux’s highest society. They exchanged apparently cold glances, each thinking the other charming. Madame Evangelista muttered, “He will be my son‑in‑law.” Paul thought, “She will be my wife.”The Evangelistas’ reputed wealth lingered in Paul’s memory, so their pecuniary condition was known to him. When society’s flattering remarks about Natalie ended with warnings about extravagance, Paul replied with disdain that shut down provincial pettiness. He brought to Bordeaux a certain Britannic reserve and Byronic pessimism, together with English plate, boot‑polish, ponies, yellow gloves, cigars, and a habit of galloping—thus avoiding discouragements that deterred other suitors. Madame Evangelista asked him to formal dinners; he accepted, pleased to breathe the grandeur and luxury he loved.When he rode by in his tilbury or on his English horse, young men said, “There’s a lucky man. He is rich and handsome, and is to marry, so they say, Mademoiselle Evangelista.” Society, which never does good without doing evil, credited Natalie with a dowry of a million and bestowed her, almost before consent, upon Paul. Their fortunes were thought to match: Paul’s habits of luxury and his newly arranged house made him seem uniquely fitted to meet the pecuniary difficulties of marrying such a daughter of a Creole great lady. Royalist friends flattered him: “Everyone gives you Mademoiselle Evangelista. You could not find, even in Paris, a more delightful girl. Her mother is intelligent and adroit; she will be a great help in public life. You must soon bring the matter to a close.”“That is all very well,” replied Paul, anxious to keep freedom, “but I must be sure that the conclusion shall be a happy one.”He therefore went often to the Hôtel Evangelista, partly to occupy idle hours harder for him than for most men. Madame Evangelista, at forty, had the beauty of a glorious summer sunset. Her spotless reputation was much discussed; her temperament combined the force and seductiveness of Spanish and Creole women. She had black eyes and hair, a swaying form and a complexion like muslin over crimson—white suffused with color. Full and rounded, she possessed the nonchalance of strength united with vivacity, sometimes the carriage of a queen. Men were taken by her conversation; she had the genius for schemes necessity bestows, advancing from concession to concession and retreating when asked for too much. Though ignorant of facts, her familiarity with courts and illustrious families gave a superficial but impressive education. She received society with natural grace and dignity, and her reputed virtue lent authority to her actions.Mother and daughter had a true friendship that went beyond maternal and filial bonds; perpetual contact produced no jar. Madame Evangelista’s perpetual widowhood was not explained solely by maternal devotion: she had once loved a man who recovered titles and property under the Restoration, courted her to marry in 1814, and severed the connection by 1816. She nursed a resentment—“Odiate e aspettate”—and relished a revengeful triumph when he later ruined himself. This misadventure and pride kept her from remarrying and turned her motherly devotion into an ambition to place her daughter at the heart of Parisian society.Madame Evangelista divined Paul’s nature and concealed her own. He was precisely the man she wanted for a son‑in‑law, an instrument for future influence. Through his mother he belonged to the Maulincour family; his connection to the Baronne de Maulincour and her grandson Auguste’s army position made Paul an excellent introducer for the Evangelistas into Parisian society. The widow, who disliked residing in Bordeaux, longed to shine in Restoration Paris, where political fortune lay. She looked to Paul as a means to that end; many men serve as screens for secret feminine ambitions, and Madame Evangelista had more than one interest in holding her daughter’s husband.Paul was captivated. The mother charmed him by seeming to seek no influence while subtly magnifying herself and her daughter in his eyes. He valued himself more for their appreciation; he fancied himself clever when his remarks pleased Natalie and flattered when the mother’s praise appeared involuntary. So kind and welcoming were they that he spent most of his time at the Hôtel Evangelista.A year after his return, though Paul had not declared himself, his attentions made the world consider him a suitor. Neither mother nor daughter pressed marriage. Natalie preserved the reserve of a great lady who can charm without allowing intimacy—a reserve that pleased Paul, timid and disliking sudden proposals. He therefore committed himself inwardly all the more. The mother’s remark that superior women sometimes place ambition above earlier emotions seduced him: “That woman is fitted,” he thought, “to advance me in diplomacy before I am even made a deputy.”Paul was an optimist; he saw everything to advantage and did not suspect that an ambitious mother‑in‑law might become a tyrant. He pictured himself married each evening and embraced wedlock cheerfully—bachelor life seemed dull. “Marriage,” he mused, “is disagreeable for people without means, but half its troubles disappear before wealth.” Every day some favorable consideration swelled his sense of advantage.“No matter what position I attain, Natalie will always be on the level of her part,” he told himself. “How many Empire men I’ve seen who suffered horribly through their wives! It is a great condition of happiness not to feel one’s pride or vanity wounded by the companion we have chosen. A man can never be really unhappy with a well‑bred wife; she will never make him ridiculous.” He taxed his memory among Faubourg Saint‑Germain women to see Natalie as their equal; all comparisons favored her, resting on his imagination, which followed his desires. Paris would have shown other types to balance his mind; in Bordeaux Natalie had no rivals and appeared when Paul was under an age‑old tyrant: the domination of an idea.Thus propinquity, self‑love, and a real passion whose outlet was marriage led Paul into an irrational love he prudently kept to himself. He tried to study Natalie, mindful of de Marsay’s warnings. But luxury breeds indifference; Paul never imagined the gulf of ruin beneath the ladies’ habits. Moreover, there are no rules to predict conjugal harmony; young people display favorable weather only, oblivious to life’s common gray days. Each shows a favorable mask; later they blame marriage for evils inherent in life.To detect in Natalie any sign of future imperfections would have required not only physiognomy but a kind of personal science few possess. Her face, like most young girls’, was impenetrable. The sculptural calm of virgin faces—Justice, Innocence—marked purity; such placid expression had not been clouded by passion or betrayal. If that expression were assumed, then no young woman remained behind it.Yet any clever analyst might find in Natalie indications of future difficulties. Her beauty arose from extreme regularity of feature and proportion—perfection that often foreshadows coldness of organization. Superior nature usually shows slight imperfections that become luminous attractions; perfect harmony often indicates a mixed, colder nature. Her round waist signaled strength and a will liable to obstinacy; statue‑like hands suggested an inclination for illogical domination; meeting eyebrows hinted at jealousy that in inferior minds becomes hatred. The “hate and wait” of her mother lived in her. Her eyes were blackish brown with orange streaks, her hair a ruddy auburn—an external contrast giving charm but not necessarily soul. Where outlines lack soft roundness, beauty is not of the soul. Such roses of youth will drop their leaves, revealing hardness and dryness beneath admired nobility.Though her face had some august outlines, her slightly full chin suggested passions that might grow violent; her astringent mouth expressed haughty pride consistent with hand, chin, brows, and figure. Her pure voice, though seductive, had metallic tones hinting at Casa‑Reale temperament. These marks suggested violent passions without tenderness,—sudden devotions, irreconcilable dislikes, a mind not deeply intelligent, and a desire to rule natural in those who feel their station inferior to their pretensions.These defects lay in Natalie like ore in a mine and would only surface under life’s shocks. Meantime grace, freshness, distinction, sacred ignorance, and youthful sweetness gave a glamour that misled unthinking minds. Her mother taught her the art of agreeable talk, replying to arguments with clever jests and concealing the subsoil of her mind with volubility. Natalie had the charm of those who never knew suffering: frank, gay, natural, expecting pleasure from marriage and answering objections with jest.How could Paul, dazzled by such beauty, foresee the woman she might be at thirty? Besides, happiness in such a marriage was not impossible. Embryo defects could be stifled by proper training, especially in a young girl who loved him. But to render ductile an intractable woman the iron wrist de Marsay recommended was necessary. Fear inspired by love is an instrument to manage women; he who loves fears, and thus is nearer affection than hatred.Had Paul the coolness, firmness, and judgment required for this struggle? Did Natalie love him? Like most young girls, she mistook instinct and the pleasure of his external appearance for love; she knew nothing of marriage or its demands. Would she have the tact to distinguish his noble qualities among lesser defects, or would she magnify the latter and forget the former, as inexperienced wives often do? There comes a time when wives pardon defects in a husband who spares them annoyances; what conciliating power or wise experience would uphold this home? Paul, weak where even a strong man runs risks, might yield to tyranny instead of establishing his rule. Could he say “No”?The subject here is not merely the change from bachelor to married man—a picture that would itself offer rich drama—but the great comedy that precedes all conjugal pairing in France: the discussion before a notary about the marriage contract, played in families noble and bourgeois, where human passions are excited by small interests as by large ones. This scene, neglected by dramatists though full of wit, controlled Paul’s future life and was awaited by Madame Evangelista with terror. It is to this comedy—the notarial negotiation—that we now turn.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At the start of the winter of 1822 Paul de Manerville, urged by his great-aunt the Baronne de Maulincour, formally asked for Mademoiselle Natalie Evangelista’s hand. The baroness, back from Médoc with encouragement, insisted he marry: Natalie’s dowry was her patrimony and a man of name and estate must marry to continue the family. She advised consulting Maitre Mathias to draw the marriage contract.Paul, uneasy about hints that Madame Evangelista was a “sly cat,” followed his aunt’s counsel and engaged Mathias. He approached Madame Evangelista with customary courtesy. She accepted notarial oversight but concealed a crucial fact: legally she owed Natalie one-third of Monsieur Evangelista’s fortune—about 1,156,000 francs—while having little ready cash. If Paul learned this through his notary he might withdraw and foil her plans; confident she could secure Natalie’s future or find an escape, she resolved to hide the difficulty.Madame Evangelista called Maitre Solonet, a young, ambitious royalist notary noted for gallantry and zeal. Solonet, flattered by her reception, heard the situation and advised theatricality: deportment and appearance at the marriage-contract signing mattered more than law. He urged that Natalie be presented at her best that evening. Madame Evangelista dressed her daughter in white tulle and pink ribbons; Natalie, coiffed à la Sévigné, appeared charming. When pressed about love and finances, Natalie declared she loved Paul and promised to shoulder blame alone if property problems forced them apart.Paul arrived with a bouquet. He and Natalie spoke fondly of a future at Lanstrac, Paris, and children while awaiting the notaries. But the marriage’s legal and financial complexities soon intruded. The two notaries embodied different eras and temperaments. Maitre Mathias, Paul’s notary, was an elderly, painstaking professional of forty years’ practice, respected for uprightness and caution. Solonet was young, fashionable, politically engaged, eager for profit and advancement. At Madame Evangelista’s salon they represented “former times and the present day.”Solonet had prepared a contract favorable to the widow; Mathias insisted on detail and clarity. Mathias catalogued Paul’s assets—Lanstrac (rent 23,000 francs), farms, vineyards, houses in Bordeaux and Paris, furniture—emphasizing substantial landed wealth. Solonet countered that the Evangelista inheritance had been invested in Five-per-cent funds and effectively amounted to about 800,000 francs, that the Paris house added value, and that the mother could convey titles while reserving a life interest. Mathias challenged vague claims and demanded inventories. Solonet explained that, being Spanish and Creole, the family had not followed French formalities at the father’s death and had reconverted English securities in Paris, complicating strict accounting.As arguments grew heated, Natalie privately told Paul she would accept blame if property difficulties separated them. Mathias, alarmed, warned Paul aside that there was little immediate dowry and recommended postponement. Solonet quickly proposed a remedy: sell the Paris house, value the Five-per-cent funds nearly at par, and convey titles into Natalie’s name while reserving Madame Evangelista a life interest. This would present an immediate, large patrimony and close the guardianship account.Mathias saw a trap. Such an arrangement would acknowledge receipt of roughly 1,156,000 francs, obliging Paul to account to his heirs for that sum later while the family might not possess that secure capital—risking impoverishing Paul and his children. Madame Evangelista, provoked, threatened to break off the marriage and leave Bordeaux, appealing to Spanish sensibilities and the sentimental worth of her name. Paul, torn between love and prudence, nearly yielded to emotion. Madame Evangelista also harbored ambition: she wanted Paul to become a peer of France.Suddenly Mathias conceived a legal compromise. He proposed to secure Paul’s public prospects and protect his estate by entailing Lanstrac and the Paris house and including eight hundred thousand francs of Natalie’s patrimony in that entail. Practically, this required purchasing adjoining domains and invoking the royal ordinance needed to create an entail, converting liquid patrimony into inalienable landed capital that would strengthen the family estate. This would effectively use the convertible part of Natalie’s fortune to create inalienable capital, satisfying Madame Evangelista’s wish to release Natalie’s patrimony while assuaging fears of immediate impoverishment and allowing Mathias to accept the guardianship account’s shortfall.Solonet, surprised, recognized that an entail would allay distrust: making property inalienable reassured Paul and his advisers that the capital could not be dissipated. Madame Evangelista, unfamiliar with the technicalities, accepted Mathias’s plan because it appeared to secure Natalie’s patrimony and her own safety. Both she and Paul agreed that, if the entail were created, the marriage should proceed.They arranged that Mathias would draw the marriage contract and Solonet the guardianship account and its release, to be signed days before the wedding. The notaries left together, each satisfied professionally: Solonet for guiding the match under a plausible appearance of security, Mathias for devising a means to protect Manerville.Left alone, Paul and Madame Evangelista reconciled. Paul dreamed of a combined household yielding near one hundred thousand francs and of a peerage; Madame Evangelista, relieved, promised Natalie her diamonds and to sell her house to secure herself without injuring her daughter’s patrimony. Natalie, generous and emotional, accepted the possible sacrifices. Madame Evangelista fell asleep convinced she had achieved her ends: Natalie married into influence, the household assured, and Paul’s advancement plausible. Yet beneath the harmony remained seeds of distrust and competing designs—Madame Evangelista assured of triumph, Mathias guarding his client’s legacy, Solonet enjoying his victory, and Paul dreaming of love and ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Elie Magus, summoned by Madame Evangelista to Bordeaux, found that the talk of Comte Paul’s marriage had prompted expectations of jewels for the bride; instead she wanted Magus to value the mother-in-law’s diamonds, thinking their worth might appear in the marriage contract. She asked him to appraise them as if for a private buyer, though she did not plan to sell. Magus explained how experts tell Asiatic (Golconda, Visapur) from Brazilian stones—Asiatics have superior whiteness and brilliance, Brazilian stones a yellow tinge—and valued the necklace and earrings (entirely Asiatic) at 250,000 francs and the “Discreto” diamond at 100,000. He warned that dealers’ buying prices are much lower because rare gems tie up capital; a dealer would pay about 75,000 for the Discreto and 160,000 for the necklace and earrings. He valued the rest—Brazilian diamonds, pearls, rubies—at roughly 100,000 to a dealer (resalable at 150,000). Madame Evangelista declared she would keep them; Magus noted that investing the income could replace similar diamonds within five years while preserving the principal.News of the appraisal leaked. Servants overheard raised voices; gossip amplified the practical commerce into scandal: had Madame Evangelista sold her house? Was the marriage off? Notaries denied collapse, citing only delay on the entail’s constitution, but society persisted. Envious provincial mothers and officious neighbors speculated and embroidered the story; the prefecture even hinted that the notaries’ language implied trouble. Bets were laid; yet Paul and Madame Evangelista were not told. Paul rode twelve miles to Lanstrac and gave a hunting-party, which society took as proof of domestic strife.Madame de Gyas, hoping to marry her daughter to Paul, approached the Evangelistas with a worried face as if to disclose misfortune. Natalie and her mother laughed off the gossip but recognized its motives: provincial mothers thwarted by lack of dowry often invent such plots. Madame Evangelista, preparing to leave the city, mocked both friends and enemies, enjoying barbed remarks at public judgment.When Paul arrived—he had ridden to spend an hour with Natalie—he dismissed the rumors laughingly. He suggested a public ball on the day the contract was signed to refute the gossip. Madame Evangelista took him at his word and invited all Bordeaux to witness the signing; she intended the fête to silence rumor.Preparations for the camellia fête took a month: camellias banked staircases and supper-rooms, partitions were removed, estates near Lanstrac were bought, banns published, doubts eased. The earlier stormy conference seemed only a notarial affair—until the morning of signing, when a memory returned to Madame Evangelista: Mathias’s earlier remark, made when he had accepted Solonet’s conditions—“Questa coda non e di questo gatto”—had hinted that someone else would bear the cost. If Mathias had seen compensation in the terms, it could not be Paul; might her daughter’s fortune be pressed to pay the price? She decided to seek explanations; the day would profoundly affect Paul’s conjugal life.Despite winter, the house was adorned and crowded. About eight, as the final contract was discussed, a dense crowd assembled to see the ladies in full dress. The notaries had dined with the bridal pair and their mother; Mathias’s head-clerk was there to receive signatures and prevent surreptitious readings. Natalie was superb in her bridal toilette; Madame Evangelista, in cherry velvet, wore her pearl necklace clasped with the Discreto—an ostentatious contradiction to the ruin stories. Paul and Natalie sat close, whispering and touching; Natalie toyed with a peacock-feather fan Paul had given her.The reading began. Solonet read the guardianship account: Natalie’s share of Monsieur Evangelista’s estate, of more than three million francs, amounted to 1,156,000 francs. The ordinary clerical formalities followed—property statements, deeds of gift in case of death without issue, life-interest gifts permitted by law, constitution of a common fund, settlement of the diamonds on the wife, library and horses on the husband—and finally the constitution of the entail. When all but signatures remained, Madame Evangelista asked what an entail would mean.
