Solonet offered a general explanation, but Mathias bluntly stated the entail’s practical effect: if the wife died first leaving several children including a son, the children would inherit only 360,000 francs, from which the husband would deduct his fourth in life-interest and his fourth in capital, leaving a net debt of about 160,000 francs—excluding savings and profits from the common fund. Conversely, if the husband died leaving a male heir, the wife’s right would be 360,000 francs plus any deeds of gift not in the entail, the diamonds settled on her, and profits and savings from the common fund.The consequence struck Madame Evangelista: she whispered, “My daughter is ruined.” Both notaries heard. Mathias, with gentle irony, replied that constituting an indestructible fortune for the family could hardly be called ruin. The junior notary, reading her face, bluntly said they had attempted to deprive the family of 300,000 francs but that the children had actually secured 800,000—arguing that any supposed loss was only 400,000 from their interests for the children’s benefit—and advised her either to break the match immediately or proceed with it.Silence followed. Natalie, unaware she had lost half her fortune, and Paul, ignorant that the Manerville house had gained, chatted lightly. Solonet and Madame Evangelista exchanged glances—one masking indifference, the other suppressing bitter feeling. Madame Evangelista’s remorse at compromising her honor for Natalie’s sake turned to fury when she realized she would be the loser and that Mathias must have foreseen this and informed Paul. The thought that Mathias might be lauded for his sagacity and turn Paul against her was intolerable. She faced a dilemma: three months earlier she might have broken the match; now banns were published, friends had come for the fête, and postponing would cause scandal. She felt trapped and knew she must yield.Her anger surfaced—her chin trembled; Solonet observed the beginning of mortal hatred. She accused Mathias of incapacity or worse, of seeing the entail’s effect and not warning her sooner. The younger notary drew her aside, thinking of fees but speaking coolly: she had overreached legal craft in her own interest; if she did not want Comte Paul she could refuse him—the contract was not yet signed and could be postponed under some pretext. He argued that Natalie’s happiness mattered above titles, that as wife she could dominate the home and guide Paul; if she played her cards well, her daughter’s influence would secure the marriage’s benefits. This mollified Madame Evangelista; she agreed to proceed.Mathias and Solonet then amended the contract to protect the wife’s interests further. The younger notary proposed—and Mathias concurred—clauses ensuring that if Manerville died without children or left only daughters, the entailed property should pass under the general deed of gift between husband and wife or that the entail be void. Solonet added the clause in the margin; Mathias read the added sentence aloud. Madame Evangelista, her eyes dropped, consented.“We will now sign,” she said, voice strained. She resolved she would not let her daughter be ruined; she intended that Natalie should keep name, title, and fortune. If Natalie later loved another, Paul would be driven out of France. Her mind set on means to preserve Natalie’s wealth.Mathias took Paul aside and explained the saving stipulation and the rescript for the diamonds: Paul must ask for them and could use them toward payments. He spoke practically: the first payment of 200,000 francs would be due after formalities; use the diamonds; the house could be sold to pay the remainder; plant vineyards to raise income. Paul gratefully pressed Mathias’s hand; Madame Evangelista noticed and suspected collusion. Rage overwhelmed her.After signatures and initials, Mathias looked at the mother-in-law. Seeing Paul say nothing of the diamonds, Mathias remarked there could be no doubt about their transfer now they were family. Solonet suggested it was regular for Madame Evangelista to make them over immediately since Monsieur de Manerville had become responsible for guardianship funds and one never knew who might live or die. Paul protested immediate transfer would be insulting; Madame Evangelista, angered she interpreted as an insult and collusion, cried she would tear up the contract if he did not take the diamonds.She left in fury, nearly mad with impotence. Natalie whispered to Paul to accept them: her mother was angry and would learn why that night; they must pacify her. Calmed, Madame Evangelista kept the necklace and earrings she wore and handed over the other jewels Magus had valued at 150,000 francs. Mathias and Solonet inspected them and found they would meet the first payments. Solonet assured Paul he would lose nothing; Mathias said the jewels would cover the first payment on the estate purchase and contract costs.Madame Evangelista, however, took Paul’s hesitation as hypocrisy; his reluctance to accept the jewels seemed to her a masked insult, and a look she flung appeared to say, “Take your property.” Paul, eager to end the dispute, told Natalie to lock the jewels away as her gift. Natalie stowed them in a console drawer. At that moment carriages arrived, salons filled, and the fête began.During the ball guests whispered and judged: some speculated on future domestic friction, others doubted whether the merchant’s daughter could hold her place socially. Card-players settled to tables, young people danced, supper was served, and the ball lasted till morning.After Paul left, Madame Evangelista and Natalie retired. Though overtired, they exchanged a few words. Madame Evangelista confessed she had acted out of tender devotion and unschooled business ignorance to secure Natalie’s happiness; she had mortified her pride. Natalie, half-asleep, praised Paul’s innocence; she had no idea of her mother’s fears or the contract’s implications. In the dark, Madame Evangelista felt the first stirrings of avarice born of passion and hate: a resolve to secure Natalie’s entire fortune by reclaiming the husband’s property for her. She told herself honor required it; devotion to Natalie made her shrewd and calculating. She planned to invest capital and manage affairs closely. Passion and hatred had turned the once-prodigal woman into a miser and a plotter. She fell asleep muttering, “To-morrow!”—her mind already arranging measures to control Paul’s life, which she began to put into action the next day.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Though the banquet had eased Paul’s anxieties, they returned in bed. He turned over Mathias’s warning about the marriage contract and Madame Evangelista’s motives. He wondered whether, with Mathias’s help, he had been spared deceit—or whether suspicion was needless. In this second struggle with the contract his fate had already shifted unknowingly: of the two people he was marrying, the sharper—Madame Evangelista—had already become his adversary, resolved to protect her own interests from the common fund. Paul, unacquainted with Creole temperament, did not perceive her craft.Madame Evangelista’s character had been dormant for years of social ease; but the impending loss of Natalie set her passions aflame. In three days her daughter would leave; this last day together offered a decisive chance to influence Natalie, who trusted her implicitly. Words could shape two lives; social law lacks the power to stop crimes that begin with suggestion. The mother understood that subtle influence could accomplish what force could not.Midday found Madame Evangelista on Natalie’s bed, affectionate and mournful, rehearsing intimate counsel. She warned that conjugal love evaporates unless a wife carefully establishes influence at the start. Natalie, playful and naive, protested that Paul loved her; her mother countered that to rule the home she must appear to obey him while actually making him yield. If Madame Evangelista remained with them, the subtle acts that should form a wife’s authority might be ascribed to the mother, making Natalie's household into "a hell"; better, she advised, that the daughter be sole mistress and sovereign.Natalie pleaded to keep her mother near, but Madame Evangelista insisted on separating: she would not expose Natalie to the risk of maternal interference, and she claimed practical reasons—poverty, the need to manage their rural property at Lanstrac and to restore her fortune by economy. She promised to write often, visit yearly, and return when her presence no longer threatened the marriage. Natalie, alarmed at the prospect of being alone with her husband, begged for concrete instruction. The mother had been waiting for this moment.Her counsel was a compressed manual on feminine power in marriage. She blamed modern perpetual intimacy—born of revolutionary bourgeois manners and Rousseau’s ideas—for the failure of many marriages: constant contact breeds satiety, contempt, and "spleen." To maintain value, a wife must preserve distance and novelty. Go out, entertain, be ever new and amusing; let social life keep you desirable. If happiness is made to depend entirely on a man, the woman loses authority; conversely, a woman who amuses and keeps a man happy will retain him.Madame Evangelista cautioned Natalie not to yield her whole will. Use the power of the first hours—when desire is still fresh—to establish habits of obedience by asking concessions, even on unreasonable points. A husband who has yielded once is more likely to yield again; making him do a foolish thing for you is the first step to ruling him. Keep reserve: coldness can be a maintained posture; the phrase "It is not becoming in your wife to do thus" is a talisman. The words "I will not" are feminine power when used sparingly. Inspire him to believe you understand him; if he believes in you, you can govern. In short: harmonize two wills by arranging from the outset that there shall be but one—the wife’s.Her advice went broader: women are born to play their part in society as men are born to be generals or poets. Natalie, Madame Evangelista insisted, was made to charm salons and represent luxury, not to be a housekeeper or immediate mother. If children come only after a few years, governesses and tutors will preserve the wife’s figure and social role; display of vanity, carefully managed, flatters a man and keeps him pleased. Practical decisions would be learned from experience, the mother said, but a woman must cultivate the instinct to act on these principles.When Natalie asked how to begin, the mother gave specific strategic precepts: a woman ardently desired before marriage must remain so afterward; do not give yourself wholly to his power; use reserve in speech and action; never demonstrate complete dependence—pride and calculation are tools of mastery. The mother’s final counsel emphasized that a wife should control events rather than only bear them.Natalie received the lesson with gratitude and tears and, still childlike, asked why the mother had once claimed she alone could promote Paul’s advancement yet now planned to leave. Madame Evangelista lied about debts and obligations—an explanation that hid her real motive. She promised to manage matters from Lanstrac and to resume promoting Paul later if circumstances permitted. They rose to prepare for Paul’s arrival, conscious that they must salvage every asset—Mon Dieu, the "Discreto," the cherished jewel.Paul arrived about four, attempting cordiality but showing signs of the earlier confrontation. Madame Evangelista seized the opportunity to turn matters to advantage: she declared that she would have to sell her pearl necklace and ear-rings to meet immediate payments and proposed exchanging the "Discreto"—a historic diamond said to have belonged to Philip II—for the jewels she had already given to Natalie. She framed this as generosity: the young wife would have modern diamonds she could enjoy, while the family would keep the historic stone. Paul protested, but the mother pressed the sentimental angle; Paul’s benevolent nature and desire for freedom inclined him to accept.At that moment Solonet, a friend and speculator, arrived with news about contractors offering to buy three acres of grounds. He could raise the price if they wished. Natalie, forced into business by circumstance, agreed to sell part of the estate while reserving furniture and mirrors. Solonet suggested many were expected at the midnight mass; Madame Evangelista used the exchange with him to mention privately that she expected two hundred and fifty thousand francs of personal property and might raise more by selling her share of the house—funds she would invest at Lanstrac. The notary Mathias kissed her hand, misled into thinking the alliance might still serve familial and business ties.When she returned to the bridal couple, Madame Evangelista reconciled her earlier claim to leave with her insistence that she would be useful as bailiff at Lanstrac. She suggested therefore it was proper she not go to Paris, reasoning that living with the young couple would oblige them and obstruct their independence, and that a mother’s presence sometimes breeds jealousy. Paul’s secret relief at being rid of his mother—he neither admitted nor fully understood it—showed itself in his smile at Natalie's sadness; he expected Paris to cure whatever misgivings she had. For Madame Evangelista this meant success: she had gotten the diamonds for Natalie and won separation, the prerequisites for her private revenge. She could leave the couple alone with their fragile love and watch events unfold.The wedding itself was a nocturnal affair. After a small dinner for the witnesses—hosted by Madame Evangelista—the bridal couple and friends attended a torchlit mass before a crowded, curious audience. A midnight wedding suggested ill omen to some; as guests whispered, the couple’s separation and the mother’s decision to live elsewhere prompted gossip that the choice to shun the mother would open the marriage to lovers and other dangers. Elegant observers found the scene bare and indecent compared to more ostentatious nuptials.After the ceremony Madame Evangelista accompanied Natalie to Paul’s house. Her parting counsel was the same: "Be his wife, not his mistress." She made an ostentatious display of grief to gain concessions from Paul—a theatrical maternal appeal that elicited typical concessions from men. The next day she accompanied them to the ferry for Paris; with a look and a word she signaled to Natalie that, though Paul believed he had won the contract and his freedom, the mother’s revenge had quietly begun. By obtaining the jewels and ensuring her absence, she had set the conditions for Natalie to begin reducing her husband to obedience by the very principles she had recommended—reserve, social preeminence, strategic concessions—turning the naive bride into an instrument of her design.Paul’s future now depended on his own ability to make his wife truly love him; if he could arouse devotion rather than mere desire, he might escape. But Madame Evangelista’s craft, born of Creole temperament—a cultured, passionate, and calculating blend—was already at work: she had positioned herself to manage the estates at Lanstrac, to rebuild a fortune, and to influence events from afar. Natalie, instructed but inexperienced and trusting, left for Paris troubled and determined to follow her mother’s precepts. The mother believed she had preserved the family’s interests and secured her revenge; the couple, unmoored from maternal guidance, entered marriage under the shadow of the mother's doctrines—an alliance in which subtle domination and social strategy would reshape their lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years later, on an autumn afternoon, Comte Paul de Manerville arrived at the house of his old friend Maître Mathias in Bordeaux, cloaked and bearing a bowed, mysterious air.Mathias, retired and too old to practice, was away on urgent business; his housekeeper Josette showed the count to Madame Mathias’s room. Fatigued by his journey, Paul slept until evening. When Mathias returned he climbed to the room and watched him sleep, as a mother watches a child; Josette followed, hands on hips.“It is a year today, Josette, since I heard my dear wife’s last sigh; I little thought then I should see the count half dead,” Mathias murmured. Observing Paul’s moans and aged appearance, he counted the consequences of their mutual acquaintance and the passage of years. Leaning on his cane and pacing the garden, the gouty old notary reflected on the time since Paul’s aunt had proposed a match that had contributed to the count’s ruin.At supper Mathias, struck by Paul’s outward calm but noting signs of mental strain that had aged him, urged him to sit. Paul replied that Mathias had his own troubles and proposed they speak over the meal. Mathias, aghast at placards notifying seizure of Paul’s estates—Grassol, Guadet, the vineyard of Belle-Rose, and the family mansion—read aloud the public notice naming Dame Natalie Evangelista, wife of the count, as separated from him as to worldly goods and chattels by the court. Paul answered that he and his wife were now separated in body as well.Paul admitted he had left Paris without his wife’s knowledge. He had taken passage to Calcutta on the Belle-Amélie, sailing in two days, intending to earn a fortune in seven years and return to rebuild his wealth. To pursue commerce he would sail under the name Camille. He expected a friend, Henri de Marsay, to send a bill of exchange for 150,000 francs to Maître Mathias the next day to finance his voyage.Mathias, dismayed, said he had never known a ruined man whose friend would lend him money. Paul insisted de Marsay would, claiming the man had already sold investments to oblige him. Mathias brightened: if true, might the friend settle matters and allow Paul to remain quietly at Lanstrac until his affairs recovered? Paul answered that no assignment or economy could pay off the approximately fifteen hundred thousand francs of debt that ruined him. He explained how events had compounded: he had given his wife diamonds, spent 150,000 francs from selling Madame Evangelista’s house on their Paris residence, used other money to meet marriage-contract payments, and sold twenty-seven thousand francs’ worth in the Funds at eighty-seven (a calculation that had left him two hundred thousand francs in debt within a month of marriage). Their income of sixty-seven thousand francs a year had been spent at three times that rate; usurers’ interest and other payments totaled a million. Then he had purchased a coronet of diamond wheat-ears for a hundred thousand, bringing the total to eleven hundred thousand, and discovered he also owed his wife’s fortune—three hundred and sixty-six thousand francs of her dot. Notes and protests added further pressure.Mathias proposed selling properties, pressing Paul to pledge his income or sell Belle-Rose, worth perhaps six hundred thousand francs, possibly seven hundred under negotiation, and to consider the Bordeaux house, which might fetch two hundred thousand or more from buyers like Solonet. But Paul explained that such schemes were impracticable when his property was mortgaged and his wife’s claims took precedence; the multitude of notes and the seniority of his wife’s rights prevented easy settlement. Natalie’s mother, Madame Evangelista, had already improved Lanstrac and repaid Paul for its use; she had bought Grainrouge between Lanstrac and Grassol, paying one hundred thousand francs down. Paul spoke of both women who loved him—his wife and her mother—and of his unwillingness to force Natalie into a life stripped of her charms and pleasures; he preferred exile in India and honest labor to sacrifice her happiness. Mathias, moralistic, urged that a wife should share good and evil and that children might have consolidated the entail and prevented dissipation; Paul defended his indulgent view of love and begged Mathias not to preach.The next day Mathias received the bill of exchange for 150,000 francs from de Marsay. Paul seized on this as proof his friend would help. Mathias tried to deter him from leaving but found Paul resolute after long arguments. Fortune favored them: the wind was fair and the Belle-Amélie prepared to sail. The quay filled with relations, friends, and curious onlookers; Paul’s ruin made him as celebrated as his former wealth. Mathias accompanied Paul to the quay and suffered the cruel comments of those who recalled the former dandy now heading for India.Paul went aboard and leaned against the shrouds, defying the crowd with a contemptuous glance. As sailors weighed anchor, Mathias signaled with his handkerchief and Josette hurried to him. Paul asked the captain to wait for a boat; too feeble to go himself, Mathias gave two letters—one important packet just arrived by courier from Paris in thirty-five hours—into the sailor’s hands, with the imprudent suggestion that Paul might come ashore. The Breton sailor, unwilling to lose time, preferred not to return and handed the letters to Paul without stating their urgency. Recognizing the handwriting of his wife and de Marsay, Paul suspected their contents and, anxious not to be influenced, put the letters unread into his pocket.At sea, consumed by sad thoughts and doubts, Paul reflected on the uncertain future and on Natalie alone. Sea-sickness then dissolved his will: he was confined to his cabin three days, vomiting and sleeping. On the first morning he felt better, went on deck, and found the letters in his pocket. He opened his wife’s letter first.Paul’s parting letter to Natalie, written as he left Paris, had been ardent and confiding: he admitted he lacked the courage to explain his departure and had deceived her for the best; he could not accept the sacrifice of her fortune and vowed to go to India to repair his shattered fortune, promising to return rich. He asked her to trust de Marsay with the liquidation of his affairs and to keep his memory alive. He called himself her “poor Pink of Fashion,” a man blown away by mischance, but vowed to rebuild for her.The Comtesse de Manerville’s reply poured out tenderness, grief, and resolution. She reproached him for leaving without consulting her, asserting that he belonged to her and she should have shared his decision. Yet she praised his courage, called his enterprise sublime, and vowed to support him. Her mother had already brought thirty thousand francs; Augustine had shared the distress; she and her mother would economize, pledge diamonds if needed, live at Lanstrac, and await him. She believed herself pregnant and hoped for a son. She promised to obey his wish to trust de Marsay, to be prudent and devout during his absence, and to send daily prayers, closing with passionate assurances of fidelity and love. Paul read and re-read Natalie’s ardent words: they alternately assailed him with memories and reassured him; her exclusive tenderness provoked jealousy and unease even as it revived his faith.To understand what followed, one must know the contents of the letter Paul had written to de Marsay and de Marsay’s reply, which Mathias had received.Paul’s letter to Henri de Marsay had been practical and desperate. He explained his ruin, begged de Marsay to sign a lease of his Paris house on nominal terms and to lend him 150,000 francs by bill of exchange to be sent to Maître Mathias—funds necessary to go to India and which Paul expected in Bordeaux the night before sailing. He confessed having hidden his ruin and having gambled in a last attempt, then failed. He admitted a weakness for lavishness in marriage but justified his departure as a manly enterprise: he would return rich or never return at all. He entrusted Natalie to de Marsay’s guardianship, requesting that Henri be her adviser, banker, and protector, given the danger of rivals while a husband was abroad.De Marsay’s reply struck like a blow. He told Paul bluntly that his ruin was total: notes had been protested at Lecuyer’s instigation; Solonet and others had maneuvered to secure Natalie’s and her mother’s hold on the property. Belle-Rose and other estates were effectively in the hands of Madame Evangelista via intermediaries. De Marsay called it a domestic conspiracy and blamed Paul for secrecy: had Paul confided in him at marriage he could have defended him. He blamed Paul’s yielding temperament and hinted that Félix Vandenesse had touched Natalie in ways Paul never did; Paul’s indulgence and dissipation, de Marsay said, invited the wife and mother-in-law to “get rid of” him by ruining him financially.But de Marsay did not merely upbraid; he offered a course of action and a future promise. He outlined his own political ambitions, aimed at forming an oligarchy to direct the nation, and said he was near to a successful marriage to an English heiress, Miss Dinah Stevens, whose fortune and prudence would secure him. He spoke of investments and an eventual large landed income. He assured Paul that, if Paul renounced his plan to go to India and returned at once to Paris, de Marsay would arrange a duel with Vandenesse in which Paul could wound or kill his rival—thereby restoring his standing. In France, a husband who shoots his rival regains respectability. De Marsay claimed to hold 100,000 francs to apply to Paul’s principal debts and promised to help Paul obtain offices and funds that would make redemption possible. He proposed that the ship sail without Paul: stay, come back, and they would “play the game again” in the political arena, with de Marsay’s support to control Natalie and make her mother pliable. In short, de Marsay urged Paul to renounce exile, return, and let politics and a staged vindication restore him.Paul, sailing beyond the Azores, read de Marsay’s letter and felt as if struck by a pickaxe. It demolished his hopes, illusions, and love. Where Natalie’s letter had aroused tenderness and faith, de Marsay’s brutal realism and proposal for a political and personal struggle invoked anger and despair. He cried aloud the name “Henri!” Many men would have been driven mad; Paul went to bed and slept that heavy sleep which follows immense disasters—the stupefied, overfull sleep that comes after utter collapse.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.- Casa-Real, Duc de — The Quest of the Absolute- Claes, Josephine de Temninck, Madame — The Quest of the Absolute- Magus, Elie — The Vendetta; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierre Grassou; Cousin Pons- Manerville, Paul François-Joseph, Comte de — The Thirteen; The Ball at Sceaux; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris- Manerville, Comtesse Paul de — The Lily of the Valley; A Daughter of Eve- Marsay, Henri de — The Thirteen; The Unconscious Humorists; Another Study of Woman; The Lily of the Valley; Father Goriot; Jealousies of a Country Town; Ursule Mirouet; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Letters of Two Brides; The Ball at Sceaux; Modeste Mignon; The Secrets of a Princess; The Gondreville Mystery; A Daughter of Eve- Maulincour, Baronne de — The Thirteen- Stevens, Dinah — Cousin Pons- Vandenesse, Comte Felix de — The Lily of the Valley; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; César Birotteau; Letters of Two Brides; A Start in Life; The Secrets of a Princess; Another Study of Woman; The Gondreville Mystery; A Daughter of EveEnd of Project Gutenberg’s The Marriage Contract, by Honoré de Balzac############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Railroads were beginning to transform travel to and from Paris, making many small, traditional enterprises seem antiquarian. The picturesque "coucous" that had run from the Place de la Concorde and the Cours-la-Reine a century earlier were becoming scarce by 1842; in 1820 not all environs of Paris enjoyed regular stage-coach service. The Touchards had built a prosperous messagerie from the rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis, but short-run coucous still competed on nearby routes. Parisian demand for country trips sustained local operators—often innkeepers along the road—who retained custom by familiarity, lower charges, and accommodating practices the Touchards’ formal system could not match. Travelers preferred a known coachman and would wait for a familiar old vehicle rather than take a stranger.One much-contested line was Paris to Beaumont-sur-Oise, a lucrative route that in 1822 supported competing enterprises. Touchard tried to monopolize it by lowering fares and improving coaches, but short-run lines serving towns like Saint-Denis and Pierrefitte remained productive. On the main road toward England, a turning at a place called La Cave leads to the delightful Oise valley and the little town of Isle-Adam. Isle-Adam, near Nogent and Parmain—villages famed for quarries whose stone built many Paris structures—had fine sites and châteaux but was reached only by a detour: from La Cave the paved road made by the Princes of Conti turned six miles off the main line and ended at Isle-Adam, a divergence a speculator would not take. Thus, in 1822 the Conti highway was the only road to Isle-Adam, and two local proprietors reigned on the route, beloved by the countryfolk and often preferred even by those on the main road as far as La Cave.These two proprietors maintained a friendly rivalry; when one left Isle-Adam the other returned from Paris. In the capital they economized by sharing the same yard, hotel, stable, coach-house, office, and clerk. Their hotel, at the corner of the rue d’Enghien—the Lion d’Argent—was long accustomed to lodging coachmen and vehicles. Although their lines advertised fixed hours, both proprietors were indulgent—a practice winning peasant gratitude and sometimes embarrassing strangers. The afternoon trip listed for four might not go until half-past; the morning at eight seldom left before nine. In summer the rules relaxed further for country customers; this elasticity sometimes let a driver take two fares for one place if a countryman arrived early and sat in a seat booked by a late traveler. They excused such practices by citing hard times, winter losses, the need for new coaches, or adherence to printed tariffs—documents displayed only when a traveler demanded them.Pierrotin, about forty and a father, had been discharged from the cavalry at the great demobilization of 1815 and succeeded his father, who had long driven a coucou between Paris and Isle-Adam. He married an inn-keeper’s daughter and expanded the business into a regular service noted for intelligence and a certain military precision. Active and decisive, Pierrotin’s weather-worn face betrayed sly shrewdness; he had learned the art of speech from life and could soften his rough voice for bourgeois passengers. His military schooling left him respect for social superiority and obedience to the upper classes; he mingled little with the lesser bourgeoisie and always respected women. By "trundling the world," he came to regard his human passengers as parcels requiring less attention than cargo—the essential object of a coaching business.Alert to postwar changes, Pierrotin refused to be outclassed. Since the previous summer he had talked of a large coach ordered from Farry, Breilmann, and Company—renowned makers who had adopted square English springs—an addition prompted by an influx of travelers. Pierrotin’s establishment consisted of two vehicles. One, used in winter and the only one he reported to the tax-gatherer, was the coucou inherited from his father. Its rounded body provided two hard metallic seats that, by stretching the law, might hold six; a removable wooden bar Pierrotin called "a back" divided the seats and annoyed passengers. Though each seat was legally for three, it was not uncommon to see eight persons jammed onto the two seats. Pierrotin maintained travelers were more comfortable in a compact mass; when fewer occupied a seat they bumped and risked hitting their hats against the roof on jolting roads.In front was a wooden bench for the driver, where three travelers—called "rabbits"—could perch; Pierrotin sometimes placed four rabbits on the bench and sat on a box beneath the coach-body, which he kept full of straw and parcels. The coucou was painted yellow with a barber’s-blue band and bore in silver "Isle-Adam, Paris" and "Line to Isle-Adam." On great occasions it could carry three more passengers in a square compartment under an awning—allowed only for regulars and avoided near gendarmerie posts where overloads might be reported. Thus on busy nights Pierrotin’s coucou could "trundle" fifteen travelers, pairing Rougeot, his stout old horse, with Bichette, a small mare of which he was fond.The other vehicle was a comical four-wheel coach holding seventeen though legally allowed fourteen. It rattled so that Isle-Adam folk would say, "Here comes Pierrotin!" when he had barely emerged from the forest. It had an "interior" for six, a front cabriolet called the "coupe" closed with awkward sashes, and an "imperial" holding six under leather curtains. Pierrotin sat on a nearly hidden seat below the coupe. He paid tax only on the coucou, rated for six, taking out special permits when he ran the four-wheeler—common practice, since taxes on vehicles had been timidly enforced and proprietors often cheated. Often in summer the two coaches ran together, carrying thirty-two passengers though Pierrotin paid tax on six. On lucky days the convoy left Faubourg Saint-Denis at half-past four and reached Isle-Adam by ten at night, proclaiming "We went at a fine pace!" To do the twenty-seven miles in five hours he sometimes omitted stops at smaller villages like Saint-Brice, Moisselles, and La Cave.The Lion d’Argent’s deep plot extended back through a long courtyard to the stables; its passage under the second floor could shelter several coaches. In 1822 the offices of all lines starting there were kept by the innkeeper’s wife, who received fares, booked passengers, and stowed packages in her vast kitchen—a patriarchal system travelers accepted. If early they waited beneath the kitchen chimney, in the passage, or in the nearby Café de l’Echiquier.On an early autumn Saturday in 1822 Pierrotin stood with hands in his blouse beneath the Lion d’Argent porte-cochere, watching the courtyard and stables. Daumartin’s diligence had just gone, and it was past eight. Pierrotin fretted: only four passengers were booked on a fine Saturday. He told his hostler to harness anyway, lamenting the hard fate of coachmen. The hostler noted there was scarcely room left but the cabriolet; Pierrotin reminded him of the new coach arriving the next day and insisted he needed at least eighteen passengers for it. He boasted it would make Touchard envious—painted red and gold and drawing three horses; he had bought a mate for Rougeot and planned a showy team.Yet Pierrotin’s buoyant talk disguised anxiety. The coach-builders required a two-thousand-franc preliminary payment; Pierrotin had exhausted resources and credit—his wife, father-in-law, and friends had bled for him. He had already paid two thousand and still owed another thousand plus five hundred for Rougeot’s mate and three hundred for new harnesses on three months’ credit. Driven by despair and vanity, he had publicly announced the coach would start the next day, hoping the builders would accept fifteen hundred instead of the two thousand five hundred still due. He considered asking Monsieur Moreau, steward at Presles, to take his note for six months.At that moment a footman in livery, carrying a leather portmanteau and returning from the Touchard establishment where he had gone too late for places, asked whether Pierrotin would wait a quarter-hour to take his master. Pierrotin agreed and received the trunk—well buckled, with a brass plate bearing a coat of arms. He wrapped it in hay and put it in the boot, then invited the valet into the Café de l’Echiquier for two absinthes. The valet explained that his master came only once a year to the district and preferred to travel in his own carriage; this time he was going to Presles to see Monsieur Moreau. Pierrotin recognized the name: Monsieur le Comte de Serizy."But listen!" said the footman. "There’s a special order. If you have any of the country neighbors in your coach you are not to call him Monsieur le comte; he wants to travel 'en cognito,' and will pay a handsome pourboire if not recognized."Pierrotin wondered whether the secret journey related to recent local affairs. The valet mentioned a countermanded order: the Daumont carriage had been ordered to leave at seven for Presles, but Monsieur le Comte had changed his mind when a lady called the previous night and again that morning—perhaps from the country. Pierrotin speculated whether the lady had said anything against Monsieur Moreau, whom Pierrotin greatly admired as an honest and capable steward; he had done errands for the Moreaus, received fees, and even fetched and returned their children, and every time he carried someone to the château he was allowed to drive up to the gate—a benefit he valued.The valet, however, was more cynical. They said Monsieur Moreau had little when Monsieur le Comte made him steward of Presles; the valet hinted that masters were annoying and advised Pierrotin, "Every man for himself. Do what Monsieur le Comte asks, and you will be paid well." He stretched his arm to indicate a generous tip. This prudent counsel cooled Pierrotin’s ardor for borrowing from Moreau. The footman’s presence and his mention of the Comte and his steward introduced the social actors central to the drama about to unfold in Pierrotin’s vehicle, and a rapid glance at the lives of the Comte de Serizy and his steward, Moreau, is necessary to understand what is to follow.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Monsieur Huguet de Serisy descends from the celebrated président Huguet, ennobled under François I; the family arms and motto—“i, semper melius eris,” a pun yielding Serisy—reflect the restrained pride of bourgeois magistrates who rose into the nobility.The present Comte de Serizy’s father was a parlement president before the Revolution. At twenty-two, in 1787, the count became councillor of State at the Grand Council and wrote important memoranda on diplomatic questions. He did not emigrate during the Revolution, remaining on the Serizy estate near Arpajon where his father’s rank protected them. After nursing his dying father in 1794, he was elected to the Council of Five Hundred and took legislative duties to distract his grief. After 18th Brumaire, like many old parliamentary families, he accepted Napoleon’s overtures: appointed to the Council of State, he reformed a disordered department and became useful to the regime.Soon he was called to a ministry, raised to the rank of count and senator by the Emperor, and served as proconsul to two kingdoms. In 1806, at forty, he married the sister of the former Marquis de Ronquerolles, a young widow of General Gaubert who had left her property. The marriage suited his rank, increased his fortune, and linked him to the former Marquis de Rouvre, later a count and chamberlain under the Emperor.Weary and ill in 1814, the Comte de Serizy resigned and came to Paris; Napoleon at first suspected him of defection but accepted his illness as cause. Though never disgraced, Serizy felt wronged by Napoleon. When the Bourbons returned, Louis XVIII—whom the count regarded as his rightful sovereign—trusted him with private affairs and made him a cabinet minister. On March 20 he refused to go to Ghent; he told Napoleon he remained faithful to the Bourbons, would not accept a peerage during the Hundred Days, and stayed at his estate. After Napoleon’s final fall he resumed office as privy councillor, became vice-president of the Council of State, and acted as liquidator of claims and indemnities demanded by foreign powers.Unambitious personally but influential, little of importance occurred without his counsel; he avoided court and salons. A life of incessant work had formed his habit: rising at four to toil until midday, attending duties in the afternoon, and sleeping by nine. He held the grand cross of the Legion of Honor and several foreign orders; public honors mattered little to him—his devotion to duty had a deeper, private motive.He had loved his wife before marrying her; that passion endured through the private unhappiness of wedding a composed widow who continued to live with great liberty while he treated her with indulgent deference. His constant toil shielded him from pain he kept secret with a diplomat’s care. He knew jealousy would seem ridiculous in a society that would not credit an old statesman’s conjugal passion. One painful fact was that his labor had caused a skin malady that repelled his wife. Kind and considerate, he let her be mistress of herself and their home. She received all Paris, passed long hours in the country, and returned as if still a widow. He managed her fortune and supplied her luxury like a steward. The countess admired his mind and could soothe him by approving his endeavors; an hour of talk in his study made him pliable. Like old seigneurs, he protected her so loyally that any disrespect toward her would have seemed an unforgivable injury. The world admired him and his wife benefited; she was often ungrateful, yet sometimes mingled charm and soft kindness that patched his wounds.This private humiliation and that devotion explain why a minister of State would undertake a sudden incognito journey.A dispute about land near Presles precipitated the journey. A rich farmer of Beaumont-sur-Oise named Lég­er leased and cultivated the Moulineaux farm, whose ninety-six parcels projected into and troubled the Comte de Serizy’s Presles estate. The farm belonged to a bourgeois, Margueron, and the lease of Lég­er—made in 1799—was about to expire; Margueron refused Lég­er’s offers to renew. Monsieur de Serizy wished to buy the farm to remove the annoyance of enclosed fields. He was told Margueron’s chief ambition was to have his only son—then merely a tax-gatherer—appointed special collector at Beaumont. Lég­er, knowing he could resell parcels to the count at a large profit, planned to buy and resell.Two days before the narrative, anxious to settle the matter, Monsieur de Serizy summoned his notary Alexandre Crottat and his lawyer Derville to examine the situation. Though Derville and Crottat distrusted the count’s steward Moreau—prompted by a disturbing letter—the count defended Moreau, who had served him faithfully for seventeen years.Derville advised direct action: go to Presles himself and invite Margueron to dinner; Crottat would prepare a deed with blanks for the property description and titles; bring part of the purchase money as a Bank of France check; and secure the appointment of the son to the collectorship. If not settled immediately, the farm would slip away. Crottat agreed, and at his valet Pierrotin’s suggestion the count adopted the plan. The preceding evening he had sent Moreau a line by diligence to Beaumont, telling him to invite Margueron to dinner so they might conclude the purchase.Before this affair, the count had ordered the château of Presles restored and refurnished; for a year Grindot, a fashionable architect, had visited weekly. The count intended, while concluding the purchase, to examine restorations and new furniture as a surprise for his wife when he brought her to Presles; he had put personal pride into the work. Why the count, who planned to drive there in his own carriage in the evening, set out early the next morning incognito in Pierrotin’s coucou merits explanation.Some words on the steward Moreau are necessary. Moreau was the son of a provincial attorney who became syndic-attorney at Versailles during the Revolution; his efforts almost saved the Serizys’ lives and property. The elder Moreau belonged to Danton’s party, was pursued by Robespierre, and was executed at Versailles. The son, inheriting his father’s connections and doctrines, was implicated in a conspiracy against the First Consul. Monsieur de Serizy, wishing to repay past services, enabled Moreau—then under sentence of death—to escape; in 1804 he sought and obtained his pardon, offered him a government post, and finally made him private secretary.Some time after the count’s marriage Moreau fell in love with the countess’s waiting-woman and married her. To avoid the annoyances of court life, he asked to manage the Presles estate, where his wife could act as lady and neither would suffer wounded pride. The count wanted a trustworthy man at Presles because his wife preferred Serizy, an estate only fifteen miles from Paris. For three or four years Moreau thus held the key of the count’s affairs; he was intelligent, had studied law in his father’s office, and the count granted his request.“You can never advance in life,” the count told Moreau, “for you have broken your neck; but you can be happy, and I will take care that you are so.”He gave Moreau a salary of three thousand francs, a lodge near the château, all wood needed from estate timber, oats, hay, and straw for two horses, and a right to the garden’s produce. A sub-prefect was not as well provided.For the first eight years Moreau managed the estate conscientiously and took interest in it. The count, visiting now and then, admired Moreau’s loyalty and rewarded him liberally. But after the birth of Moreau’s third child he felt secure and ceased to see his advantages as gifts. About 1816 the steward, who had until then taken only what he needed, accepted twenty-five thousand francs from a wood-merchant as inducement to lease timber cutting for twelve years. Moreau argued he had no pension, was father of a family, and the count owed him that sum as a gift after ten years’ management; with sixty thousand francs saved, this would let him buy a farm worth 125,000 francs in Champagne, above Isle-Adam on the Oise. Political events prevented the count and neighbors from noting this investment, made in Madame Moreau’s name as if she had inherited from an aunt.Once he tasted possession, Moreau—while keeping an appearance of integrity—seized opportunities to increase his fortune clandestinely; the interests of his three children served as a poultice to his conscience. By local custom he shared with the count in profits due to his management, but this swelled his resources. Presles returned a net revenue of seventy thousand francs; locally people said, “Monsieur de Serizy has a second self in Moreau.” Prudent, Moreau invested yearly after 1817, piling up savings secretly. He often played the poor man so well that the count sent his sons to the school Henri IV. At present Moreau held 120,000 francs invested in Consolidated thirds, paying five percent and quoted at eighty francs. These hidden 120,000 and his Champagne farm, enlarged by purchases, amounted to about 280,000 francs, giving an income of some 16,000 francs.Such was the steward’s position when the Comte de Serizy wished to buy the Moulineaux farm—ownership indispensable to his comfort. Père Lég­er wished to buy only to resell it to the count at a handsome advance. To secure that sum he sought an understanding with Moreau. Three days before the crucial Sunday Lég­er talked with the steward and showed that Moreau could persuade the count to invest at two and a half percent and thus pocket forty thousand francs off Lég­er’s offer.“I tell you what,” Moreau said to his wife, “if I make fifty thousand francs out of the Moulineaux affair—and I certainly shall, for the count will give me ten thousand as a fee—we’ll retire to Isle-Adam and live in the Pavillon de Nogent.”“That will suit me,” his wife replied. “The Dutchman there has put it in order and now returns to India; he might let us have it for thirty thousand francs.”“We shall be close to Champagne,” said Moreau. “I hope to buy the farm and mill of Mours for a hundred thousand francs. That would give us ten thousand a year. Nogent is one of the most delightful residences in the valley; and we should still have an income of ten thousand from the Grand-Livre.”“But why not ask for the post of juge-de-paix at Isle-Adam? That would give influence and fifteen hundred a year.”“Well, I did think of it.”With such plans, Moreau—hearing the count was coming to Presles and had asked him to invite Margueron to dinner—sent an express to the count’s head-valet enclosing a letter to his master, which the messenger failed to deliver before the count retired. Augustin, however, placed it on his master’s desk in the morning. In the letter Moreau begged the count not to come, and to trust him entirely; he added that Margueron was no longer willing to sell the whole at once and spoke of cutting the farm into lots. It was necessary to circumvent this plan, perhaps by employing a third party to make the purchase.Everybody has enemies. Moreau and his wife had offended a retired officer, Monsieur de Reybert, and his wife, who lived near Presles. From pin-prick speeches matters had advanced to dagger thrusts. Monsieur de Reybert sought vengeance; he aimed to make Moreau lose his situation and gain it himself. Reybert had spied on the steward for two years. The same conveyance that took Moreau’s letter to the count carried Madame de Reybert to Paris, whom her husband had despatched. There she so earnestly asked to see the count that, though sent away at nine, she was ushered into his study the next morning at seven.“Monsieur,” she said, “we cannot write anonymous letters, so I have come in person. I am Madame de Reybert, née de Corroy. My husband is a retired officer with a pension of six hundred francs, and we live at Presles, where your steward has offered us insult after insult, though we are persons of good station. Monsieur de Reybert, who retired in 1816 after twenty years, has watched your steward for three years, aware of his dishonesty and intending to have him dismissed. Moreau plans to get an extra hundred thousand francs out of you, to be divided between the notary, the farmer Lég­er, and Moreau. You have written Moreau to invite Margueron, and you are going to Presles today; but Margueron will be ill, and Lég­er is so certain of buying he is now in Paris with the money. If we have enlightened you, and you want an upright steward, take my husband; though noble, he will serve you as he has served the State. Your steward has made two hundred and fifty thousand francs from his place; he is not to be pitied.”The count thanked Madame de Reybert coldly, for he despised backbiting; but he remembered Derville’s doubts and felt shaken. He then saw Moreau’s letter and read it. In its assurances of devotion and its implied blame of the count for distrust, he read the truth.“Corruption has come to him with fortune—as it always does!” he said.The count questioned Madame de Reybert, partly for information but more to observe her, and wrote a short note to his notary telling him not to send his head-clerk to Presles as requested, but to come himself in time for dinner.“Though Monsieur le comte,” Madame de Reybert concluded, “may have judged me unfavorably for acting without my husband, he ought to be convinced we obtained this information in a natural and honorable manner; the most sensitive conscience cannot take exception to it.”Madame de Reybert stood erect: a face seamed with smallpox, a flat spare figure, eager eyes, fair hair plastered on an anxious forehead, and a plain dress. The count recognized the wife of some poor, half-pay captain: a puritan, earnest in virtue but mindful of comfort and eager for a good situation. He had thought of entrusting estate management to some retired officer, about whom he could obtain exact information from the minister of war.“Madame,” he resumed, ringing for his valet, “return to Presles this afternoon with my notary, who is going down for dinner and to whom I have recommended you. Here is his address. I am going myself secretly to Presles, and will send for Monsieur de Reybert to come and speak to me.”Thus Monsieur de Serizy’s journey by public conveyance and his valet’s injunction to conceal his name and rank had reason: a minister intent on confronting a steward’s possible treachery and protecting his estate and honor—both public and painfully private.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Pierrotin emerged from the Café de l’Échiquier after tending the valet, he noticed in the gateway of the Lion d’Argent a lady and a young man he recognized as patrons. The lady, neck stretched and anxious, searched for him; she wore a black-dyed silk gown, a brown bonnet, an old cashmere shawl, raw-silk stockings, low shoes and carried a straw bag. Once beautiful and about forty, her blue eyes had lost the fire of happiness and bore the look of long renunciation. Her dress and manner betrayed a mother wholly devoted to home and son: the bonnet’s faded strings and the shawl fastened with a broken needle revealed thrift. She waited impatiently to entrust her son—no doubt travelling alone for the first time—to Pierrotin’s care.Her son completed the picture: an olive-green coat with sleeves too short, blue patched trousers, and a bright patch visible when the coat-tails parted. “Don’t rub your gloves that way, you’ll spoil them,” she admonished as Pierrotin appeared. “Is this the conductor? Ah! Pierrotin, is it you?” she cried, leaving the boy to speak with the coachman. Pierrotin greeted her with a mixture of respect and familiarity. She asked him to take good care of Oscar—he was travelling alone to Monsieur Moreau. Pierrotin, curious, probed whether Madame Moreau was willing to receive the boy; Madame Clapart answered, with resignation, that life would not be all roses for Oscar but his future required the journey. This reply struck Pierrotin: he feared for the steward, felt some unease, but did not voice it to Madame Clapart, who feared to ask too much lest she harm her son.As they talked lightly of weather and stopping places, Pierrotin’s relationship with the Claparts required explanation. Several times a month he met the steward Moreau near La Cave. When the carriage arrived, Moreau signalled a gardener who, with Pierrotin’s help, loaded baskets of fruit, vegetables, poultry, eggs, butter and game. The steward paid fares and added money for tolls if the baskets contained dutiable goods. These parcels were never addressed; the first time, Moreau had given Madame Clapart’s address verbally to the discreet Pierrotin, requesting secrecy. Expecting to find a pretty intrigue, Pierrotin had gone to number 7 rue de la Cerisaie and found Madame Clapart instead.Public drivers see many homes and learn many secrets; social accident often places them in intimate proximity. Pierrotin, not a close observer, remained puzzled for months about Moreau and Madame Clapart’s exact relation. Although lodgings were not dear in the Arsenal quarter, Madame Clapart lived on the third floor at the end of a courtyard in a house once belonging to a great family. Her flat, panelled with ancient carvings, had three rooms—dining-room, salon, and bedroom; above were the kitchen and Oscar’s attic room. Opposite the entrance on the landing was a door into a rough stone tower and staircase; that back room was where Moreau slept when in Paris.The room where Pierrotin deposited the hampers contained six wooden chairs with straw seats, a table, and a sideboard; the curtains were discolored. The salon held shabby Empire furniture; the bedroom matched the salon and dining-room. Painted woodwork blurred the mouldings; floors, never waxed, had the gray tone of boarding schools. Pierrotin noticed the china and glass betrayed poverty, yet forks and spoons were silver—some relic of a vanished past. Monsieur Clapart, in a shabby surtout and broken slippers, always wore green spectacles and revealed a pointed skull with a few dirty threads of hair. Timorous yet tyrannical, he looked wan. In that north-facing apartment, with only a vine and a well to look on, Madame Clapart bore herself like a queen and moved like a woman unaccustomed to walking. Often she slipped Pierrotin a twelve-sous piece with charming thanks. Pierrotin had never seen Oscar at home because the boy was always at school when his business took him there.The sad story Pierrotin could not learn from the portress—who only knew the Claparts paid 250 francs a year and had no servant but a charwoman—was as follows. There seldom exists a criminal wholly criminal, nor a dishonest nature entirely dishonest; Moreau, if guilty in some ways, was persistently kind to a woman whose favours he once claimed and in whose house he had hidden when condemned to death in 1797. This woman, famed under the Directory for a liaison with a powerful protector, had married through that protection a purveyor named Husson, who made millions from government contracts but whom Napoleon ruined in 1802. Husson went insane and threw himself into the Seine, leaving Madame Husson pregnant. Moreau, allied with her, had been condemned to death and could not marry her; forced to leave France, he nevertheless watched over her.Madame Husson, at twenty-two, later married a clerk named Clapart, aged twenty-seven, reputed to be promising. Clapart, coarse and handsome but lacking intelligence, expected wealth but found none; his Treasury post provided only a modest salary. When Moreau returned as secretary to the Comte de Serizy he learned of Madame Husson’s plight and obtained, before his own marriage, an appointment for her as head waiting-woman to Madame Mère, the Emperor’s mother. Still, Clapart was never promoted. Ruined in 1815 by the fall of the Empire, the once brilliant Aspasia of the Directory had no resources beyond Clapart’s meagre clerk’s salary and the small help Moreau could discretely provide. Moreau, the only protector of a woman he had once known in opulence, obtained a half-scholarship for her son, Oscar Husson, at the collège Henri IV, and sent her regularly, via Pierrotin, supplies from his estate at Presles as he could decently offer to a household in distress.Oscar was his mother’s whole life and future. The poor woman’s only fault was exaggerated tenderness for her boy—the bête-noire of his stepfather. Oscar unfortunately had a foolish, overweening conceit his mother did not perceive despite her husband’s sarcasm. This conceit worried Moreau so much that he begged Madame Clapart to send Oscar to him for a month so he might judge the lad’s character and find a suitable career; Moreau even thought of proposing Oscar as his successor to the count. The sources of Oscar’s vanity were evident: he had been born in Madame Mère’s household and had childhood impressions of imperial splendour. His imagination retained those images, and maternal tales of a golden past nurtured his boastfulness. As a scholarship pupil among paying students he had borne humiliations that hardened the conceit unless a scholar could command respect by physical superiority. This mixture of departed splendour, faded beauty, blind maternal love, and heroic suffering made Madame Clapart a pathetic figure in Paris.Pierrotin, incapable of understanding Moreau’s real attachment to her and of the past intimacy that bound her to him, did not communicate his sense of danger to the steward. He preferred to think of his own livelihood and obey the “chefs de file”—men of rank. His routine, deferential “Very good, madame,” hid a desire to escape long instructions.“You will be sure to place the packages so they can’t get wet,” Madame Clapart said.“I’ve a hood,” Pierrotin replied. “Besides, see with what care they are placed.”“Oscar, don’t stay more than two weeks, no matter how much they may ask you,” she continued. “You can’t please Madame Moreau; besides, be home by the end of September. We go to Belleville to your uncle Cardot.”“Yes, mamma,” Oscar answered, evidently annoyed at being lectured on the threshold of the Lion d’Argent. The mother, forgetful of the street, embraced Oscar and took a little roll from her basket. “Tiens! you were forgetting your roll and the chocolate! My child, don’t take anything at the inns; they’ll charge you ten times the value.”Two well-dressed young men a few years older—without a mother clinging to them—witnessed the scene and mocked the word “mamma,” as if infantilizing Oscar. Oscar cried “Good-bye, mother!” in a tone of impatience. Madame Clapart, perhaps lacking tact because all absorbing sentiments contain egoism, spoke too loudly and seemed anxious to display her tenderness. “What is the matter with you, Oscar?” she asked. “Listen—do not talk and show off; keep your tongue in check. You are not advanced enough to judge persons; there is nothing more dangerous than speaking in public conveyances. In a diligence well-bred persons always keep silence.”The two young men had walked to the other end of the establishment and returned with tapping heels. To rid himself of his mother, Oscar took a measure that revealed how vanity could stimulate cunning. “Mamma,” he said, “you are standing in a draught, and you may take cold. Besides, I am going to get into the coach.” The lad touched a tender spot; his mother caught him to her bosom, kissed him as if he were starting on a long voyage, and went with him to the vehicle with tears. “Don’t forget to give five francs to the servants when you come away,” she said; “write me three times during the fifteen days; behave properly, and remember all I have told you. You have linen enough; don’t send any to the wash. Above all, remember Monsieur Moreau’s kindness; mind him as you would a father, and follow his advice.”As Oscar got into the coach his blue woollen stockings showed as his trousers pulled up, and the new patch showed through his parted coat-tails. The two young men’s smiles, noticing these signs of respectable indigence, wounded his vanity. “The first place was engaged for Oscar,” said his mother to Pierrotin. “Take the back seat,” she told the boy with a fond smile.Oscar regretted that poverty had destroyed his mother’s beauty and subdued her dress. One young man, wearing top-boots and spurs, nudged the other to notice Oscar’s mother; the other twirled his moustache as if to say, “Rather a pretty figure!” Oscar wished they would leave. The two young men—one named Amaury—began to chatter and taunt. “He said mamma!” one cried, and their half-toned remarks cut him. Oscar compared his companion’s fine clothes with his own and felt that his mother’s shabby toilet counted for much. He fancied the other’s pockets full of francs when he noticed a gold chain and watch.Living in rue de la Cerisaie since 1815, brought to and from school by his stepfather and seeing only his mother’s poverty, Oscar had little comparison. Brought up on Moreau’s advice, he seldom went to the theatre except to the Ambigu-Comique. His stepfather still wore his watch with a heavy gold chain and old finery Oscar called the “ne plus ultra.” He was bewildered by the revelation of superior, negligent elegance: spotless gloves and a gold-headed switch cane twirled with grace. Oscar had reached the sensitive quarter of adolescence when little things cause immense joys and miseries—a period when youth prefers appearance and torments itself about dress. A nineteen-year-old only child adored at home envying a twenty-two-year-old in a frogged surtout is a peccadillo common to many.But Oscar’s peccadillo deepened into ill feeling. He felt humiliated, angry with the youth he envied, and secretly desired to prove himself as good. The two young fellows paced the gate and stables, looking at Oscar curled in his corner. He pretended indifference and began to hum a liberal chorus, provoking Amaury to sneer that perhaps Oscar was one of the Opera chorus. This exasperated Oscar, who burst up and called to Pierrotin, “When do we start?”“Presently,” Pierrotin answered, whip in hand, looking toward rue d’Enghien. At that moment a young man arrived with a true gamin followed by a porter with a hand-cart. The cart’s contents—trunks, buckets, brushes, oddly shaped boxes and innumerable packages—were stowed so quickly Oscar, smiling across the street at his mother, noticed none of the paraphernalia that might reveal his new companion’s trade.The gamin, about sixteen, wore a gray blouse buckled with a leather belt, a jaunty cap, curly brown hair, a black-silk cravat around a very white neck, bright gray eyes and a vivacious, mischievous face—Figaro-like. His gestures and mocking eye betrayed a lively intellect born of early vocation. He called himself “in style.” The companion, a slight pale young man with thick black hair worn fantastically and an enormous forehead suggesting precocious intellect, looked like an artist: a shabby American-green surtout, a black waistcoat buttoned to the throat, a scarlet neckerchief peeking, black trousers and muddy boots. His face seemed harassed and hollowed as if by chronic sickness or grief. With a rapid look he took in the Lion d’Argent scene and glanced at Mistigris’s satirical stare.“Charming!” said Mistigris.“Yes, very,” replied the master.“We seem to have got here too early,” Mistigris continued. “Couldn’t we get a mouthful somewhere? My stomach, like Nature, abhors a vacuum.”“Have we time for a cup of coffee?” the artist asked Pierrotin.“Yes, but don’t be long,” Pierrotin replied. “Good; that means a quarter of an hour,” said Mistigris, and they disappeared. Nine o’clock chimed in the hotel kitchen. Georges, seated inside handling a portfolio under the seat cushion, complained about the wheels and the horse. Pierrotin explained they were going to harness Bichette while the gentlemen took coffee. Georges worried about places; he and Oscar had claimed seats.As they prepared, Père Léger arrived—a stout man of perhaps two hundred and fifty pounds, square-backed with a protuberant stomach, powdered pigtail, little blue linen coat, white gaiters above the knee, velveteen breeches with silver buckles, and hob-nailed shoes. He carried a polished stick with a large knob. “At your service,” he said with a face like Louis XVIII, fat rubicund cheeks and a large nose. He was good-humored and only travelling to La Cave.Pierrotin still looked rue d’Enghien up and down, whip in hand, reluctant to take his hard seat where Mistigris fretted. “If you expect someone else, I am not the last,” said the count, as Monsieur de Serizy—whom Pierrotin had fetched—arrived another way. “Père Léger,” Pierrotin suggested, “will you give your place to Monsieur le comte? That will balance the carriage better.” Georges protested the habit of moving for late comers. Mistigris and his master bantered about respect and seats; Mistigris quipped and sprang about like a frog. The Comte de Serizy, taken for a bourgeois named Lecomte by the coach’s company, answered little and assumed a good-natured air. He sat beside Pierrotin in front.The count had a burning red face with inflamed blotches set against white hair that harmed his noble air; his green-gray eyes revealed shrewdness and wisdom. His flat face and depressed nose, hat hiding his forehead, made him odd. He wore a long blue overcoat buttoned to the throat, a white cravat, cotton wool in his ears, and a high shirt-collar. No decoration adorned him; nothing betrayed him as a peer and a useful statesman. Père Léger had never seen the count; the count knew the farmer only by name. When the count entered the carriage he glanced about, affronting Georges and Oscar but truly looking for the head-clerk of his notary, in case he, too, had been forced into Pierrotin’s vehicle. Feeling reassured by Oscar, Père Léger assumed his note had reached his notary Alexandre Crottat in time to keep the clerk from leaving.When they reached the steep faubourg Saint-Denis at rue de la Fidélité, Pierrotin suggested the farmer might disembark. “Ten louis for you if you keep the secret of my being here as I told you before,” the count whispered to Pierrotin, who winked, thinking of the thousand francs he might earn. Oscar and Georges stayed in the coach. Georges threatened to pay his fare and take a post-horse if Pierrotin did not go faster. Père Léger reassured him they would arrive by mid-day. Pierrotin promised punctuality, and Mistigris mocked the count’s supposed preferences. Thus, amid joking, impatience and the shuffle of passengers, the diligence prepared to set off with its motley company: Oscar inwardly wounded, his mother left behind with last admonitions, Pierrotin balancing duty and profit, a sly artist with his rapin, a complacent farmer and the discreet Comte de Serizy hidden among them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Those who travel in public conveyances know strangers do not at once begin to talk; there is first a period of mutual examination. Once each thinks he has sized up his companions, the most talkative starts and the conversation livens—especially in French stage-coaches, where everyone is eager to laugh and show wit.A twenty-two-year-old, Georges, was tempted to display his cleverness. He had sized up his fellow passengers: the count as a second-rate manufacturer or chandler; the shabby young man with Mistigris as insignificant; Oscar as a ninny; Pere Léger as an excellent dupe. He resolved to amuse himself at their expense and, as the coucou descended from La Chapelle toward Saint-Denis, ran over possible personae—Etienne, Béranger, a Carbonaro, the son of Marshal Ney, a disguised Russian prince, Cousin, Lord Byron—finally choosing a swaggering, Eastern-adventurer role. Mistigris’s banter and Pierrotin’s exclamations opened the talk.Assuming the air of someone just returned from the Levant, Georges said he had been ill three months and had only just left his bed after a “germ” of suppressed plague. This produced alarm and gibes. He launched into tales of Egypt and Greece: service under Ali, pacha of Janina, a quarrel with him, and Oriental living that had disordered his liver.When Pere Léger asked if he had been a soldier and how old he was, Georges answered twenty-nine and sketched a patched military history: enlisted in 1813, present at Hanau, promoted to sergeant-major, made sub-lieutenant at Montereau and decorated by the Emperor; he hinted at service at Mont-Saint-Jean in 1815, expressed disgust with France, then said he went abroad with comrades to serve a pacha in Egypt. He declared he wouldn’t renounce his religion for Islam and told of receiving a thousand talari from the pacha.The passengers were captivated. Georges boasted of being grandson of the famous Czerni-Georges, who had fought the Porte; his father had died in Paris leaving his mother pregnant. Their property had been seized; his mother had remarried a purveyor, Monsieur Yung, now dead; she had left Georges estranged from his stepfather—supposed reasons for his enlisting as a private in 1813. Back in Greece, Ali Tebelen welcomed him as descendant of Czerni-Georges; Georges claimed Ali gave him a harem—five wives and ten slaves—but the women were stupid and the harem little better than a herd of goats; he preferred French grisettes.They were entering Saint-Denis and stopped at a tavern famed for its cheese-cakes. The count, suspecting the youth had left something in the coach, returned and found beneath the cushion a portfolio labeled “Maitre Crottat, notary.” He took out the deed of sale for the farm at Moulineaux, pocketed it, and went into the inn, concluding Georges was Crottat’s second clerk sent as head-clerk. Georges meanwhile had paid for cheese-cakes and wine for Pere Léger and Oscar, basking in their admiration, and tried to pick up Mistigris and his master.When Pierrotin returned, Georges piled on: he had been made commander of Ali’s cavalry and had distinguished himself against Chosrew pacha, cutting his line and receiving Ali’s gratitude and costly gifts. Back at Janina Ali proposed degrading acts—drowning a wife, renouncing his religion—but Georges refused and fled. He claimed Chosrew later captured Ali by sea with English help; Chosrew had become a naval commander and, when he recognized Georges at Smyrna, nearly executed him. Monsieur de Rivière, the French ambassador, intervened, saving him and recovering two thousand talari, though most treasures were taken by the admiral.Georges said he had witnessed the execution of Smyrna’s governor and then gone to Spain, becoming aide-de-camp to Mina with the rank of colonel for the constitutional cause. The count reacted sharply, warning that the Court of Peers was then investigating conspiracies and punishing French soldiers who bore arms against France or engaged in foreign intrigues. He added that if he were a magistrate he would have ordered the gendarmes at Pierrefitte to arrest the supposed aide-de-camp and summon the coach’s occupants to testify. The remark silenced Georges as they passed the gendarmerie guard-house.Conversation turned to cultivation in Eastern lands. Georges, with his habitual flippancy, described them as too busy cutting each other’s heads off to farm like the French: onions, rice, tobacco, dates, rugs at Smyrna, Latakia tobacco, opium from poppies—customs that left the fellahs barely enough after taxes. Pere Léger, puzzled, asked about sheep and fields; Georges offered evasive explanations about crops growing with little cultivation. He mocked bureaucracy and contrasted despotic plunder with French stamped papers. The count, drawing from this talk, advised landowners to sell, warning taxes in Italy were heavy too.Mistigris and Schinner—the supposed painter—joined in. Schinner claimed to be a humble painter returned from Rome after winning the grand prix and sent to Italy at the government’s expense. He insisted he’d go merely as a decorator to a château. The count indulged this mock-importance of artists, arguing that great ceilings and the love that inspired them were worth sacrifices. Oscar, jealous and awkward, listened, eager to be somebody.Pierrotin, the driver, joked about carrying sovereigns and the pourboires he expected, and fretted about a debt for a new coach. The passengers exchanged banter about ceilings, Venice, Rome, and a near-murder Schinner claimed to have endured there. Mistigris hinted Lord Byron had been involved; Schinner demurred, not wishing to speak of Byron.Schinner then related a long account of Dalmatia, Zara, and a Greek beauty named Zena. He had stayed with an apothecary, seen Zena on a neighboring balcony, and, despite an ugly duenna and a jealous seventy-year-old husband—an Uscoque pirate with millions, a missing ear and eye—had exchanged looks and stolen walks with her. He detailed the dangerous delicacy of their flirtation: ramparts, the duenna who wouldn’t leave, and growing tension. He finally entered the pirate’s house and found Zena; they walked the ramparts together. One day he returned to find the street full of people; he was seized, garroted, gagged, and dragged before magistrates accused of murdering the pirate. He was stoned and nearly killed by a maddened populace.He learned that Zena had poisoned the pirate with opium to free herself for walks with him; the duenna had overdone the dose, implicating him. He insisted he knew nothing of the murder. The mayor and Austrian commissary of police released him with orders to return to Rome, while Zena, who had let the heirs take most of the Uscoque’s fortune, was sentenced to two years’ seclusion in a convent; Schinner hoped to paint her portrait someday. Mistigris complained that Schinner had left him penniless in Venice, forcing him to paint cheap portraits to reach Rome.Georges chimed in with comments on Dalmatian maraschino and the follies of youth. Pere Léger repeatedly asked about farming and crops, receiving brief, often evasive replies. The count maintained a guarded attitude, sometimes smiling at fashionable braggadocio and sometimes revealing surprising knowledge—for example, when Georges mentioned names tied to Smyrna that the count recognized, making the youth seem less sure of his invented recollections.Throughout the journey the characters parried between bluster and information: Georges’s ostentatious Eastern adventures mixed fact and fabrication; the count, discreet but observant, suspected a clerk posing as a colonel; Schinner’s romance showed the dangers beneath exotic talk; Mistigris supplied cynical comments; Pierrotin kept an eye on profit; Pere Léger asked homely questions; Oscar gaped and yearned to be more. Conversation, prompted by the coach’s enforced intimacy and the French habit of talk, ran on—at times diverting, at times revealing—until they moved on toward Pierrefitte and beyond.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Pierrotin’s coach stopped at Saint-Brice about half-past one for the usual rest. At the innkeeper’s cry—“Here’s Pere Leger”—the travelers prepared for breakfast. Georges, twirling his cane, produced a straw case of cigars and lit one on the threshold, provoking poor Oscar, who coughed and tried to look jaunty. Schinner and Georges showed him how to smoke; the painter and Georges mocked his awkwardness. Mistigris seized Oscar’s cigar and smoked with relish; when Oscar fumed at Georges’s expenditures—Alicante, cheesecakes, cigars—he felt his shabby clothes and patched stockings betrayed him. Mistigris, sharing the embarrassment, revealed his patched socks; the count, standing apart beneath the porte-cochere, smiled at the youngsters’ folly while envying their easy gaiety.In the kitchen the innkeeper and Pere Leger spoke low about Les Moulineaux. The innkeeper guessed Pere Leger had arranged to buy it and offered to lend money. Pere Leger calmly said he had done it for the count and explained his plan: the King had given him forty thousand “pictures on coin” to present to Moreau; he would buy Les Moulineaux outright for 260,000 francs and then sell it to the Count de Serizy for 360,000, making a neat profit while preserving appearances by letting the count propose him as tenant. The farm currently yielded 6,000 francs; Pere Leger would take it at 7,500 for eighteen years and show it as a sound investment. The innkeeper approved; the count, listening unseen, believed he had found incriminating evidence but wondered whether steward Moreau was implicated or if this was mere talk.When Pierrotin came out to water the horses, the count, fearing indiscretion, whispered to him to keep his name secret in Saint-Brice and Isle-Adam and promised Pierrotin a thousand francs next morning if he did. Pierrotin, flattered and pale with happiness, agreed.
