Few provincial towns—perhaps Vitre and Avignon excepted—preserve the medieval type so thoroughly.Guerande is still ringed by strong walls, its moats full, battlements and loopholes unovergrown; even ivy has not mantled the towers. Three gates show rings of portcullises and an iron-clamped drawbridge that could still be raised. In 1820 the mayor was reproached for planting poplars along the moat to shade the promenade—though the esplanade had long been a mall where citizens strolled beneath elms. The houses have not altered their fronts, added stories, or been stuccoed; arcades rest on wooden columns, floor planks bend but do not break. Merchant houses are low, slate-faced, with carved wooden figures—grotesque faces and fantastic beasts—relics painters prize for their patina.The streets remain as they were centuries ago, though the population has thinned so much a traveler may walk alone through lanes where mullioned windows are boarded up against the window-tax. A postern opens onto a wooded tract—one of Brittany’s luxuriant spots—seen through casemates once occupied by halberdiers. Every step recalls long-continued habits; medieval ideas and superstitions persist. Modern objects and fashions are rare and adapt to immutable customs. The public square still swarms with Breton costumes that attract artists: the white linen of the paludiers (salt-gatherers), blue and brown peasant clothes, the sacred jewelry of women, sailors’ jerkins and varnished caps. Castes remain distinct—bourgeoisie, nobility, clergy, peasants, paludiers—so that revolutionary leveling found the masses too rugged to plane. Even after 1830 Guerande remained fervently Catholic, silent, and self-contained, where modern ideas have little access.Geography explains much. Guerande overlooks salt-marshes producing the celebrated Guerande salt—whose quality Bretons link to their butter and sardines—and is linked to the rest of France by only two roads: one from Savenay ending at Saint-Nazaire, and another from Vannes. Land travel is limited; the more frequented route is by water from Saint-Nazaire. Between Guerande and Saint-Nazaire the mail-coach does not run; the road sees almost no passengers. These obstacles discourage travelers and encourage inhabitants to accept separation. Guerande, at the edge of the continent, leads nowhere and is glad to be left alone. Much of its salt production is managed at Croisic, which communicates with Guerande across dangerous quicksands and an arm of the sea; Croisic’s port handles commerce.Guerande is like the Herculaneum of feudality—preserved rather than destroyed. Approaching from Croisic across salt-marshes, its vast fortifications strike the traveler; from Saint-Nazaire its position and naive grace charm. The surrounding country is ravishing: hedges full of honeysuckle, roses, box, and plants that make the landscape seem an English garden. This rich nature, framed by an African-like belt of ocean-banked dunes, creates striking contrasts—the paludiers in white scattered among melancholy swamps recall Arabs in burrows. Guerande’s sleep-like silence, likened to Venice, lulls one; public conveyances are rare, reduced to a carrier’s springless wagon. Bernus, the carrier in 1829, was the town’s factotum, rising and setting the pace of arrivals; the coming of a carriage or a sea-bathing lady was an event that drew peasants to barter and to buy the distinctive jewels and linens of Breton brides and households.For a ten-mile circuit it is always Guerande—the town of the treaty of 1365, the key of the coast, still claiming a lost splendor. Artists and some bourgeois visitors feel, as in Venice, the desire to spend days amid its peace, walking along the mall from gate to gate toward the sea. Its image lingers like that of an enchanting woman: towered, girdled, floating in a robe of dunes, scented with wild paths.Near Guerande’s church stands a mansion that epitomizes the town’s past—a symbol of a greatness destroyed. It belongs to the du Guaisnics, the noblest family of the province, as ancient as Brittany’s granite. In du Guesclin’s time they were among the utmost nobility; perhaps Druids in origin, they now subsist on a small income. The family possesses the manor in Guerande, the little castle of Guaisnic, and nominally holds lands once bringing substantial revenue. But their estates have long been pledged to farmers; despite the barony's lands grossing perhaps sixty thousand francs under mismanaged cultivation, the family receives only two thousand francs a year. For two centuries they have been unable to repay advances and remain titular rather than actual proprietors—analogous to the ancien régime’s crown toward its engagistes. Before 1789 the Guaisnic fiefs were worth fifty thousand francs annually, but the National Assembly’s suppression of seigneurial dues on inheritance further reduced their claims. In Paris the family is little regarded and laughed at, yet in Guerande the Baron du Guaisnic remains a great baron, a man standing beneath only the King.At the end of a damp, gloomy lane stands the mansion’s granite archway—wide enough for a horseman, indicating antiquity. The oak gate, studded with enormous nails in geometric patterns, opens under a semicircular arch carved with the Guaisnics’ arms: Gules, a hand proper gonfaloned ermine, a sword argent in pale, with the motto FAC. The coronet above proclaims baronial rank. The hand, holding the sword with vigor, expresses chivalrous pride and a family’s readiness for action—“To DO,” a motto that still arouses feeling among those who know the Guaisnics.The gate opens into a courtyard with stables on the right and kitchen offices on the left. The house is of freestone from cellar to garret. Its courtyard façade has a portico with double steps; carved vestiges—now worn—display the Hand bearing the sword. Granite steps are disjointed and mossy, yet solidity remains. The oak door, nail-studded and likely by a Venetian-school artisan, opens into a noble hall leading to a portico and the garden.The hall is marvelously preserved: chestnut wainscot three feet high, gilded and faded Spanish leather hangings, a painted wooden ceiling with traces of gold and floral designs, and an enormous stone chimney-piece with giant wrought-iron andirons capable of holding a cart-load of wood. Furniture is all oak, each piece bearing the family arms. Hunting arms—English guns, sabres, game-bags—hang among fishermen’s utensils. A dining-room opens to the kitchen via a door cut through a corner tower; at the opposite corner a spiral staircase rises to the upper stories.The dining-room is hung with fourteenth-century tapestries; their inscribed banderols, in the naive language of the fabliaux, attest their age. These tapestries, spared much light and framed in ebony-toned oak, set the room’s medieval tone. On shelves, as in 1200, sit four old goblets, an embossed soup-tureen, two silver salt-cellars, and pewter plates and pitchers marked with the du Guaisnics’ arms and covered with hinged pewter lids. The fireplace here has been modernized in Louis XV taste—an anachronism that would distress a poet but to the family is indifferent. On the mantel stands a tortoise-shell clock inlaid with brass flanked by silver candelabra. A small round table by a garden window holds an odd lamp—an ostrich-egg-sized globe attached to a candlestick by a glass tube, its wick drawing nut-oil like a coiled tape-worm.Windows to the garden and courtyard are mullioned with hexagonal panes and draped with heavy brocatelle curtains of ancient crimson silk with gold reflections. The two upper stories contain only two rooms each—the master’s bedroom and the children’s—while guests lodged under the roof and servants above kitchens and stables. The pointed roof, with noble windows nearly to the ridge, bears slender casings whose carvings have suffered from salty air; above each arch a vane grinds.A spiral-stair tower at the corner descends to a little arched door opening on a gravel yard between house and stables. A companion, five-sided tower ending in a cupola with a bell-turret, repeats the form on the garden side. These towers connect on the first floor by a stone gallery supported by brackets ending in grotesque human heads; the gallery’s delicate balustrade and a stone dais hanging from the gable recall statues at church portals. One might imagine a woman walking this balcony to gaze over Guerande at sunlight gilding sands and ocean.The garden, remarkable for so old a place, covers half an acre: espaliered walls divide vegetable squares bordered by cordons of fruit trees tended by Gasselin, the man-of-all-work. At the far end is a grotto with a seat, a central sun-dial, and gravelled paths. The garden façade lacks the courtyard towers but shows a slender spiral column from ground to roof that once bore the family banner—its iron socket now supporting only some weak plants. Certain sculptural details, trefoils with four leaves rather than three, suggest Venetian influence in the architecture—Venetian art, corrupted by eastern contacts, sometimes altering Christian symbolism into quartet forms.This ancient dwelling’s charm prompts the question why such houses are no longer built. The reason is social change: mansions are sold, demolished, or parceled for streets; fathers fear the next generation will not retain the paternal home. Formerly, building was an act done for perpetuity; that faith—both in self and God—produced prodigies: durable architecture, carefully ordered interiors, and continuity of manners that now fade. The du Guaisnics still live by those traditions. Their rooms, courtyard, and external accessories preserve the spirit, grace, and candor of old Brittany. To understand the family’s surprising figures, one must first see the town and the house; things shape beings. There are public buildings whose presence molds nearby character; under the shadow of a cathedral, irreligion is harder to maintain. So too the Guaisnic mansion sustains a way of life. Were the Baron not still ready to take sword in hand, all this faithful portraiture would be untrue.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Early in May 1836 the household of Guenic comprised Monsieur and Madame du Guenic, Mademoiselle du Guenic (the baron’s elder sister), and an only son of twenty-one, Gaudebert-Calyste-Louis—called Calyste by family habit. The father bore the old family names Gaudebert-Calyste-Charles; saintly names watched over the Guenics as much by habit as piety.The Baron du Guenic had abandoned Guerande when La Vendée and Brittany rose, fighting with Charette, Cathelineau, La Rochejaquelein, d’Elbée, Bonchamps, and the Prince de Loudon. Prudently he had sold his estate to his sister Zéphirine before leaving, to shield the family fortune from confiscation. After the leaders’ deaths he refused to submit to Napoleon and continued resistance until 1802. Defeated and nearly captured, he went to Croisic and crossed to Ireland, true to the Breton habit of mistrust toward England.For twenty years Guerande pretended ignorance of his whereabouts; silence and discretion protected him. Mademoiselle collected rents and sent them by fishermen. The baron returned quietly in 1813 as if from a season in Nantes. During his Irish exile, well past fifty, he fell in love with an Irishwoman, Fanny O’Brien, daughter of an impoverished but noble family. She was twenty-one. He procured French marriage papers, returned to Ireland, and early in 1814 brought his wife to Guerande, where she gave him Calyste on the very day Louis XVIII landed at Calais—hence the boy’s final name, Louis.The baron was now seventy-three, aged prematurely by guerrilla warfare, exile, and hazardous sea crossings. He matched the worn grandeur of their mansion, built when a court still reigned at Guerande. Tall, straight, wiry, and lean, his oval, wrinkled face had the hollowed look of Rembrandt’s subjects; the sun and patriotic vigilance had carved him, yet enough remained to move the soul. His firm outline, solemn lines, and a rigid, slightly wounded nose declared intrepidity without calculation, faith without reserve, obedience without question, fidelity without compromise, and love without constancy. In him, Breton granite had taken human form.The baron had no teeth; his lips were violet and retracted, giving an expression both threatening and proud. His chin seemed to seek his hooked nose—signs of energy and Breton stubbornness. Abundant white hair fell in curls on his shoulders. Black eyes still glittered with the last flames of a generous spirit. Eyebrows and lashes were gone; shaving proved difficult, so he let his beard grow fan-shaped. His hands—large, broad, hairy—were those of a soldier and guerrilla, shaped by sword and oar and by fights among thorns and marshes; close inspection might show fresh marks from renewed Vendée service. They illustrated the Guenic motto: Fac!His high, narrow brow, broadened by hair loss, lent majesty to that noble ruin. His countenance reflected a certain stolid calm and even savagery—a Huguenot-like impassibility where animal nature dominates and thought is rare. Thinking seemed effort; its seat appeared the heart rather than the head. He had inborn faiths and sentiments that made deep speculation unnecessary; institutions and religion did his thinking. He conserved his mind for action, drawing thought from his heart as he drew his sword; sincerity shone on his scutcheon. This temperament explained the sale to his sister before the war—prudent provision for exile, death, or confiscation.The beauty of character displayed by him and his sister—who lived only by and for her brother—cannot be judged by the selfish standards of a later age. When the rector suggested the baron go to Paris in 1814 to seek recompense from the Bourbons, Mademoiselle cried, “Oh, fy! does my brother need to hold out his hand like a beggar?” “It would be thought I served a king from interest,” said the baron. “Besides, it is for him to remember. Poor king! he must be weary indeed of those who harass him.”That devotion did not go unrewarded by courtly forms: the baron received the rank of colonel, the cross of Saint-Louis, and a pension of two thousand francs a year—news he accepted as the king’s remembrance, though in truth the Duc de Feltre had arranged it. In gratitude the baron held a siege at Guerande against General Travot; refusing to surrender the fortress, he and a band of Chouans evacuated into the woods and stayed armed until the second Bourbon restoration. Guerande still recalls that siege.The Baron du Guenic was as illiterate as a peasant in many respects; he could read, write a little, cipher, and knew military art and heraldry, but beyond his prayer-book he had read hardly three volumes. He dressed always the same: stout shoes, ribbed stockings, greenish velveteen breeches, a cloth waistcoat, and a loose coat with a collar that displayed the cross of Saint-Louis. A noble serenity sat on his face; for a year or so sleep—prelude to death—seemed to be gathering him for eternal rest. These pauses alarmed neither his blind sister nor his wife—medical knowledge was small; to them these pauses were natural: the baron had done his duty.In that ancient mansion the absorbing interests were the fortunes of the dispossessed elder branch, the future of the exiled Bourbons, the Catholic religion, and Brittany politics. The sole personal interest was the devotion of all to the only son, Calyste, heir and hope of the du Guenic name.Some years earlier the old Vendean had a return of youth and began training his son in manly exercises for a gentleman always ready to take arms. At sixteen Calyste was taken to marshes and forest, taught by his father through the chase and small warfare—firm in saddle, steady at the shot, indifferent to fatigue, intrepid before obstacles. So, when the Duchesse de Berry landed to attempt a restoration for her son, the father judged it right to take his boy and join her, practicing the family motto. The baron left at night without informing his wife, taking his son under fire as if to a fête, accompanied by Gasselin, his sole vassal. They were absent three months without sending news to the baroness, who read the Quotidienne with trembling, nor to the blind sister, whose nerve did not falter. Considering the enterprise useless, the baron left the region before the affair of La Pénissière; otherwise the house might have been ruined.On a stormy night after parting from Madame, the father, son, and servant returned, surprising the baroness and Mademoiselle du Guenic—though the blind sister recognized their steps in the lane. The baron looked around the anxious circle seated by the antique lamp and said, in a tremulous voice while Gasselin replaced guns and sabres, in words of feudal simplicity: “The barons did not all do their duty.” He then kissed his wife and sister, sat in his old arm-chair, and ordered supper for his son, Gasselin, and himself. Gasselin had thrown himself before Calyste to protect him and received a sabre cut on his shoulder; the women scarcely thanked him. The baron and guests uttered neither curses nor complaints about conquerors—silence being a Breton trait. In forty years no one ever heard the baron rail against his adversaries; it was for them to do their duty as he had done his. This silence marked an unalterable will.That last effort, the flash of waning energy, had produced the present weakness and somnolence of the old man. The fresh defeat and exile of the Bourbons, driven out as miraculously as restored, weighed bitterly on him.About six on the evening when this tale begins, the baron, accustomed to dining at four, fell asleep while his wife read the Quotidienne. His head leaned back against the armchair beside the fireplace on the garden side.Near a gnarled tree and before the fire sat the baroness—the type of those admirable women often found in England, Scotland, or Ireland—milk-white, golden-haired, her curls wound as if by angels. Fanny O’Brien was one of those sylphs—strong in tenderness, invincible under misfortune, soft in voice, and pure in her blue eyes; her skin silken, her beauty exquisite. Still beautiful at forty-two, she might have graced any suitor’s dream. Half reclining with feet out to warm, she wore a black velvet gown against the chill; the corsage rose to the throat. Her hair was in English ringlets, the rest twisted to the crown and pinned; burnished gold gleamed. A short nape-lock, braided and hidden, was a coquettish racial sign that pleased the baron. When a woman displays such coquetry at home, it bespeaks noble devotion: she loves without calculation, does good in secret, and worships. In Fanny the Virgin’s halo seemed to preserve her beauty. Her serene face and pure blue eyes expressed boundless sympathy and angelic tenderness.At the other hearth sat the octogenarian sister, like her brother in all but dress, knitting stockings—the work needing no sight. Both eyes had cataracts; she refused an operation despite entreaties. Secretly she said it was want of courage; truly she would not let her brother spend twenty-five louis on her. That sum saved would be for the household’s good.These two old people set off the baroness’ beauty. Mademoiselle Zéphirine, deprived of sight, was unaware of eighty years’ effect on her face. Her pale hollow countenance and white sightless eyes produced a deathlike aspect; three or four projecting teeth showed when she smiled. She wore a brown printed cotton hood quilted like a petticoat, a cotillon over a quilted petticoat that hid double louis-d’or, and pockets sewn to a belt she fastened each morning and night. She clung to her casaquin and pleated collar, changing the collar only once a week—a point of domestic dispute between her and her sister-in-law. From large wadded sleeves came withered yet vigorous arms, brownish-red hands moving like stocking-machines; now and then she thrust a knitting-needle between hood and hair to poke her white locks, then pushed it back without fear. Straight as a steeple, erect and imposing, her carriage suggested pride—an old-age coquetry proving pride a necessary passion of life. Her smile was gay. She, too, had done her duty.When the baroness saw her husband asleep, she ceased reading. A ray of sunshine burnished black furniture, ceiling carvings, time-worn chests, and the old oak table; Fanny’s voice and the light made music in the blind sister’s heart. The ray sank into twilight; the baroness slipped into deep meditation, a silence noticed by Mademoiselle for two weeks. The elder continued to knit; the silence deepened until the clicking of needles was plainly heard.“You have dropped the paper, sister, but you are not asleep,” Mademoiselle remarked slyly.Mariotte then entered to light the lamp, which she placed on a small table by the fire, fetched her distaff and ball of thread, and sat on a stool in the window recess to spin. Gasselin busied himself about the offices, tended the horses, readied the stable, and fed the two hunting-dogs; their joyful barking was the last noise to awaken echoes in the dark walls. The two dogs and two horses were the household’s only remaining vestiges of chivalry. An imaginative man on the portico might have shivered at the hounds’ baying and the horses’ neighing.Gasselin was a short, thick Breton with black hair and sun-browned face, silent, slow, and obstinate as a mule but steady. Forty-two, he had served twenty-five years, hired by Mademoiselle at fifteen on hearing of the baron’s marriage and probable return. He considered himself family, had played with Calyste, loved the horses and dogs, petted them, and wore a blue linen jacket with flapping pockets, matching waistcoat and trousers, blue stockings, and hob-nailed shoes; in cold weather he donned a goatskin.Mariotte, also over forty, was to woman what Gasselin was to man—same complexion, figure, lively black eyes. Curiously they had never married; perhaps it seemed immoral because they resembled brother and sister. Mariotte’s wages were ninety francs a year; Gasselin’s, three hundred. Yet thousands would not have tempted either to leave the Guenic household. Both were under Mademoiselle’s orders, who, from war to the baron’s return, had ruled the house. When she learned the baron would bring home a mistress, she feared losing her household sceptre, yet she was agreeably surprised to find in Fanny a woman of high birth who disliked petty cares and preferred plain fare to costly dishes. Fanny could perform all duties, endure privations when necessary, but had no taste for commonplace work. When the baron begged his sister, in his wife’s name, to keep charge, the old maid kissed the baroness like a sister, made a daughter of her, adored her, and rejoiced to retain control. She ran the house with an almost inconceivable economy, relaxing only for great occasions—lie-in, Calyste’s needs, and similar events.Though masters and servants observed a stern regime, they required few orders: masters’ interests were their own. Mademoiselle insisted on overseeing all; she knew without looking how many nuts were in the barn or oats in the bin, carrying a boatswain’s whistle to summon Mariotte with one note and Gasselin with two.Gasselin’s greatest joy was tending the garden, producing fine fruits and vegetables; with so little to do he would have been lost without it. After grooming horses and cleaning the ground-floor rooms, he weeded and watched for field-mice or cockchafer grubs, joyfully presenting his catch. He liked going to Croisic on fast-days to buy fish cheaper than at Guerande.Thus no household was more united, more bound together than this noble family devoted to duty. Masters and servants seemed made for one another. For twenty-five years there had been neither trouble nor discord. The only griefs were the boy’s petty ailments, the sole terrors the events of 1814 and 1830. If the same tasks were performed at the same hours and food obeyed seasons and times, this monotony—like nature’s—varied only by cloud and sun, and was sustained by affection that sprang from natural causes and made life fruitful.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When night had fallen, Gasselin entered the hall and asked respectfully if he was still needed.“You can go out, or go to bed, after prayers,” replied the baron, waking, “unless Madame or my sister—”The two ladies signaled assent. Gasselin knelt as his masters rose to kneel on their chairs; Mariotte also knelt before her stool. Mademoiselle du Guenic then said the prayer aloud. When it ended, someone rapped at the lane-door and Gasselin opened it.“I dare say it is Monsieur le curé; he usually comes first,” said Mariotte.Monsieur Grimont’s foot sounded on the portico. The rector, with the unctuous civility of a priest, bowed to the three occupants and answered the mistress’s absent-minded greeting with an ecclesiastical question.“Are you anxious or ill, Madame la baronne?” he asked.“Thank you, no,” she replied.Grimont, about fifty and of middle height, seemed swallowed by his cassock, from which two stout shoes protruded. His plump, pale, slightly yellow-spotted face, dimpled hands, and placid, abbatial look suggested a Dutch burgomaster; his straight black hair and lively brown eyes recalled a Breton peasant. He had the joking gaiety of a man at peace with his conscience and the untroubled confidence of a rector in a town where his authority was undisputed. Yet in the du Guenics’ salon he lowered his spiritual superiority and acted as a chaplain in his seigneur’s house; in church his blessing favored their chapel, where their armorial device adorned the keystone.“I thought Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had already arrived,” Grimont said, taking the baroness’s hand to kiss it. “She is getting unpunctual. Can it be that dissipation is contagious? I see Monsieur le chevalier is again at Les Touches this evening.”“Don’t say anything about those visits before Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel,” cried the old maid eagerly.“Ah! mademoiselle,” Mariotte said, “you can’t prevent the town from gossiping.”“What do they say?” asked the baroness.“The young girls and the old women all say he is in love with Mademoiselle des Touches.”“A lad of Calyste’s make is playing his proper part in making the women love him,” said the baron.“Here comes Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel,” said Mariotte.Gravel crackled in the courtyard and a page with a lantern ushered the lady in. Mariotte moved her stool to the great hall to talk by the lantern’s gleam, economizing their single oribus candle.Jacqueline de Pen-Hoel was an elderly demoiselle: thin, dried, yellow as parchment, wrinkled, with gray eyes, prominent teeth, and the hard hands of a man. Short and slightly crooked, she stirred a mass of petticoats to reach a pocket; keys and coins jingled among her garments. Dangling from one side were the keys of a good housekeeper; from the other a silver snuff-box, thimble, knitting-needles and other resonant implements. She wore a green bonnet, yellowing with age, and gowns made by her nieces to an immutable pattern renewed every five years at Nantes. Using an old cane with a short hook, she bore the ermine of ancient Breton dukes. In her and her sister the illustrious house of the Pen-Hoels ended; the younger sister had married a Kergarouet who, against disapproval, added Pen-Hoel to his name and styled himself Vicomte de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel.“Heaven has punished him,” said the old lady; “he has nothing but daughters, and the Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel name will be wiped out.”Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had about seven thousand francs a year from rents. She had come into her property at thirty-six and managed it herself, inspecting it on horseback and showing the firmness often seen in deformed persons. Her celebrated avarice was local legend: she kept one servant and the page, and her expenses, excluding taxes, rarely exceeded a thousand francs a year. Thus she attracted the attentions of the Kergarouet-Pen-Hoels, who spent winters in Nantes and summers at their Loire estate. Supposedly ready to leave her fortune to whatever niece pleased her best, she received one of the four demoiselles de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel every three months.A friend of Zephirine du Guenic, Jacqueline had planned since Calyste’s birth to transmit her property to the chevalier by marrying him to whichever niece the Vicomtesse favored. She dreamt of buying back Guenic land from tenant engagistes; when avarice has an object it ceases to be vice and becomes a means to an end. Perhaps Zephirine knew Jacqueline’s plan; perhaps the baroness, devoted to her son and fond of his father, suspected it as well, seeing how persistently the old maid brought her favorite niece, Charlotte, now sixteen. The abbe Grimont was certainly in her confidence; he helped Jacqueline invest her savings.Yet however wealthy Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel might have been, the du Guenics never showed behavior that could be read as seeking her fortune. True Breton pride kept Jacqueline glad of the esteem accorded to Zephirine and the du Guenics; she seemed honored by her relations with them. She even concealed the personal discomfort of burning the singular gingerbread-colored oribus candle that night in the Guenic hall.At the moment of this narrative, the abbe Grimont revealed that on the evening when the old baron, the young chevalier, and Gasselin secretly departed to join Madame (to the baroness’s terror and all Bretons’ joy), Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had given the baron ten thousand francs in gold—an immense sacrifice—to which the abbe added another ten thousand from a tithe he collected—charging the old hero to offer the whole, in the name of the Pen-Hoels and of the parish of Guerande, to the mother of Henri V. This generosity showed how Jacqueline treated Calyste: as if her intentions gave her rights over him; her plans seemed to authorize supervision.Not strict in gallantry—indulgent like women of the old school—she held modern ways in horror. Calyste might earn her esteem by a few Breton adventures but would lose it if seen entangled in what she called innovations. She might disinter a little gold to pay the result of a love-affair, but if Calyste drove a tilbury or spoke of Paris she would deem him dissipated and spendthrift. To her, novel ideas meant the overthrow of crop succession, ruin under the name of improvements, and mortgaged lands as the inevitable consequence of experiments. Prudence, filling granaries and waiting for a rise, was the true method of making fortune. By chance she often made advantageous sales that confirmed her principles. Thought maliciously clever but not quick-witted, she was methodical as a Dutchman, prudent as a cat, and persistent as a priest—qualities in routine-bound Brittany nearly equivalent to intellect.“Will Monsieur du Halga join us this evening?” Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel asked, taking off her knitted mittens after the usual greetings.“Yes, mademoiselle; I met him walking his dog on the mall,” replied the rector.“Ha! then our mouche will be lively to-night. Last evening we were only four.”At the word mouche the abbe rose, fetched a small round osier basket, a pile of ivory counters yellowed with use, and a greasy pack of cards. He arranged counters before each player and set the basket beside the lamp with infantine eagerness and the practiced air of a man accustomed to this service.A firm knock at the outer gate brought in the long, lean, methodically clothed Chevalier du Halga, former flag-captain to Admiral de Kergarouet, outlined in black on the portico.“Welcome, chevalier!” cried Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel.“The altar is raised,” said the abbe.The chevalier, a man in poor health, wore flannel for rheumatism, a black-silk skull-cap to protect his head from fog, and a spencer to guard his chest from sudden gusts. He always carried a gold-headed cane and a little bitch named Thisbe. Fussy as a fine lady and speaking low to spare his voice, he had once been among the most intrepid officers of the old navy, winning de Suffren’s confidence and the Comte de Portenduere’s friendship; his splendid conduct as flag-captain to Admiral Kergarouet was written on his scarred face. Now no one would imagine the voice that had ruled storms and the eye that compassed the sea.The chevalier never smoked or swore; he was gentle and tranquil as a girl and as concerned about Thisbe’s caprices as an elderly dowager. He moved as an invalid, walking as if stepping on eggs, complaining of wind, sun, or mist, yet showed the whitest teeth in the reddest gums—a reassuring sign that his ailments were mostly expensive comforts involving four ample meals a day. Like the baron, his frame was bony and indestructibly strong, covered with parchment-like skin bearing a tawny color from India, though he brought back no facts or ideas from there. He had emigrated with his friends, lost his property, and served in the Russian navy until Emperor Alexander ordered him employed against France; he resigned, lived at Odessa near the Duc de Richelieu, and returned to France with the duke’s help, who secured for him the pension that enabled his present life. On Louis XVIII’s death he returned to Guerande and eventually became mayor.For the last fifteen years the rector, the chevalier, and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had regularly passed their evenings at the hôtel de Guenic, where other noble neighbors also came. The du Guenics led the faubourg Saint-Germain of the old Breton province, into which none of the new administration’s appointees were ever permitted. For six years their prayers had included Domine, salvum fac regem with a cough at the crucial words, and politics in Guerande remained faithful to that point.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mouche was played with five cards each and one card turned up to show trumps. On a turn a player might play or abstain; abstaining cost only his stake unless forfeits already filled the basket. If he played and won at least one trick he was paid pro rata; if he won none he was “mouche,” paying the whole stake and enlarging the next basket. Those who declined threw down their hands and their play was void. Players could exchange cards with the remainder of the pack in turn, so the first exchangers sometimes took most of it. The turned-up trump belonged to the dealer, who played last and could exchange it for any card in hand. The knave of clubs, a powerful card, was nicknamed Mistigris.The game fostered cupidity, diplomacy, and poker faces. At the Hôtel du Guénic each player put up twenty counters representing five sous, so the stake amounted to five farthings—small but meaningful to these guests; a run of luck could yield fifty sous, more than most in Guérande spent in a day. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël played with the zeal of a big-game hunter; Mademoiselle Zéphirine, who shared funds with the baroness, treated each farthing as a serious investment.An 1825 convention forbade continuing when any player losing ten sous wished to retire; politeness required stopping. The chevalier and the baron, however, skirted this: when most wanted to quit, Du Halga, the rich free-spending sailor, would bribe Zéphirine or Jacqueline with ten counters to keep playing, promising to recompense them if they won. The baron made similar offers, ostensibly to preserve the evening. The misers accepted after a show of reluctance—the old-maid custom—but only after the baron and chevalier had won, or the offer would have been an insult.Mouche grew more spirited when a Demoiselle de Kergarouet arrived with her aunt. The Kergarouets insisted on the single name; the aunt treated the niece at Du Guénic and told her to look amiable—easy near Calyste, whom the Kergarouet girls adored. Brought up in modern ways, they cared little for five sous; stakes rose. The aunt’s presence animated the blind elder sister, who signaled stakes to the baroness by pressing her foot a number of times. Deciding whether to play when the basket was full became a private conflict—cupidity against fear—and Charlotte de Kergarouet’s luck invited chiding lectures on reserve from her aunt.These nightly games, carried on for twenty years, were sometimes interrupted by gossip or politics; players often sat half an hour gabbing with cards fanned on their stomachs. If a counter was missing from the basket, everyone swore they had paid; usually the chevalier made up the deficiency and was accused of being absent-minded about his health. When he paid, Zéphirine and Jacqueline fussed that they themselves might have forgotten. Such small suspicions amused the company. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël would almost always accuse the rector of cheating when he won, to which he dryly replied, “It is singular that I never cheat except when I win the trick.”Talk sometimes drifted to royal misfortunes, and evenings could end without gain but with emotion and complaints that mouche was dull. Once, after the Kergarouets arrived, the baroness urged the group to try whist and boston; they failed to understand the new games, voted them head-splitters, and returned to the tradition of mouche—Brittany triumphing over novelty.While the rector dealt, the baroness asked the Chevalier du Halga the same health questions as the night before. The chevalier made a comic point of inventing ailments; he never complained of real wounds but fancied pains—his stock of comic maladies resisting medical cure. Light banter from Zéphirine and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël displayed the chevalier’s old wit and suggested a life rich in memories.Conversation turned to the recent death of the Duc de Lenoncourt; the baron remarked he would be next and his wife gently rebuked him. The company noted the baron’s melancholy and afterward would say, “Monsieur du Guénic was sad to-night.” The phrase “The Baron du Guénic fails” became a conversational opener.When asked about Thisbé, the chevalier mimicked the dog to expose his own hand—an attempt to make neighbors reveal whether they held Mistigris or the trump. The baroness observed the rector’s nose grow pale—a sure sign he had Mistigris—because that card pleased him.“You had company today,” the chevalier said. The baroness replied it had been a cousin of her brother-in-law and told of the marriage of the Comtesse de Kergarouet, a Demoiselle de Fontaine, whom the chevalier recognized through the admiral. Then Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël astonished them with tales about Mademoiselle des Touches—tales so strange that, if true, Calyste would not be constantly with her. The baron pricked up his ears.Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël suddenly had Mistigris and could not continue. “Your play, monsieur le baron,” she said. Zéphirine returned to knitting; the rector, informed about Calyste and Mademoiselle des Touches, refrained. The baron inquired again: “What is so extraordinary?” “She smokes,” Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël said, and added that the woman was squandering her estate.The baroness then announced a long run of trumps and Mistigris; the victory horrified Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël, who for a moment forgot Calyste and Mademoiselle des Touches. By nine only the baroness and rector remained; the others had retired. The chevalier accompanied Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël home as usual, remarking on Zéphirine’s delight in devouring her gains, and noting the baroness’s abstraction.At Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël's door she confided the cause: “Calyste is lost unless we marry him promptly. He loves Mademoiselle des Touches, an actress!” “Then send for Charlotte,” the chevalier advised. “I have; my sister will receive my letter tomorrow,” Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël replied.That declaration set the household abuzz; any stranger’s arrival or stay in Guérande provoked talk. When silence fell in the baron’s and his sister’s rooms, the baroness looked at the rector, who toyed pensively with counters. “I see that you begin to share my anxiety about Calyste,” she said. “Did you notice Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël’s displeasure to-night?” he asked. She loved Calyste as a son; his conduct in the Vendée and Madame’s praise of his devotion had increased her affection. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël intended to execute a deed of gift leaving her property to whichever niece Calyste married. The rector acknowledged this hope for a richer Irish match but counseled prudence: Pen-Hoël’s savings and well-managed land were not to be scorned.He then described Mademoiselle des Touches’ life: a stage-woman and writer who frequented theatres and artists, squandered her fortune on pamphleteers, painters, and musicians under the name Camille Maupin, and turned Les Touches into an improper house of dissipation where more wine was drunk in weeks than in all Guérande in a year. Last year the Demoiselles Bougniol had let lodgings to men suspected of being Blues who sang wicked songs and spent time at Les Touches. This was the woman Calyste adored; he would gallop to Nantes for an infamous book but not for the Church, and if called to fight for the royalist cause he might not answer if she wished him to stay.“Oh, no!” cried the baroness. The rector feared to test Calyste outright—the mother would suffer too much—and promised to see Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël the next day and find out the truth. Jacqueline trusted him. “Calyste is our child and pupil—he will not let the devil inveigle him,” he said; “one fault is not vice.”“You are only telling me details,” the baroness answered. “Was I not the first to notice the change in my Calyste? A mother feels keenly being second in her son’s heart. This is a trial of motherhood. I had hoped he would love someone noble and beautiful—not a stage-player, a masquerader, an author who feigns sentiments, a creature who will deceive and make him unhappy! She has had adventures—”“With several men,” added the rector. “Yet this impious creature was born in Brittany! She dishonors her land. I shall preach about her next Sunday.”“Don’t do that!” the baroness begged. “The peasants and paludiers might rush to Les Touches. Calyste is Breton; something dreadful might happen if he defended her as he would the Blessed Virgin.”“It is now ten o’clock; I must bid you good-night,” said the abbé, lighting his lantern. “Who could have told me that a young man raised by you, trained by me in Christian ideas, fervent and spotless, would plunge into such mire?”“But is it certain?” the baroness asked. “How could any woman help loving Calyste?”“What proof other than her staying so long at Les Touches? In twenty-four years since coming of age she has never stayed so long,” the rector replied.“A woman over forty!” exclaimed the baroness. “In Ireland they say such a woman is the most dangerous mistress for a young man.”“As to that I cannot say,” the rector answered.“I wish I had loved deeply, so I might counsel and comfort Calyste,” the baroness sighed. The rector did not cross the little courtyard alone; she accompanied him to the gate, hoping to hear Calyste’s step. They heard his tread fade and the parsonage door close behind the abbé.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The poor mother returned to the salon in deep distress: all the town now knew what she had thought hers alone. She trimmed the lamp-wick, took up her worsted-work, and waited for Calyste. The baroness hoped to make her son come home earlier and spend less time at Les Touches, but his visits had grown more frequent and later; the night before he arrived at midnight.Anxious, she stitched rapidly. Piqued by a curiosity common to pure women, she wondered what charms the daughters of Baal could possess to lure men from family and duty. Sometimes she longed to meet Mademoiselle des Touches to judge her soberly. Her mind measured the dangers that the era’s innovations—so menacing to young souls, the rector warned—might work on her only child, who until now had been guileless and beautiful with fresh innocence.Calyste, of Breton and Irish blood, had been raised with the utmost care. After infancy and maternal guidance, she entrusted him to the rector of Guerande for strict Christian instruction: Latin, Greek, cautious history, geography, and a limited range of French writers. He knew little of modern literature or current science. The Church continued the maternal work: Calyste remained a fervent Catholic, neither over-pious nor ridiculous.For this noble son the baroness desired a quiet, provident life. She expected to inherit a few thousand pounds from an aunt; combined with the Guenics’ fortune she hoped to find a wife with a suitable income. A wealthy heiress like Charlotte de Kergarouet would have suited her; she regarded marriage pragmatically, ignorant of passionate love. Thus the thought that Mademoiselle des Touches threatened their prudential plans filled Fanny with foreboding.Hearing midnight strike with tears, the baroness feared her son might not return; but his step soon sounded in the lane and she opened the gate joyfully.“Oh!” cried Calyste, grieved, “my darling mother, why did you sit up for me? I have a pass-key and the tinder-box.”“You know very well, my child, that I cannot sleep when you are out,” she said, kissing him.In the salon she studied his face for signs of the evening. Calyste stirred maternal pride: except for the dark, energetic eyes from his father, he resembled his mother—golden hair, lovable mouth, curving fingers, soft white skin; though slightly girlish in look, his strength was Herculean. His fatigue looked like painful thought; his mother blamed passing excitement. She feared that “those beautiful pure cheeks” would be given to another woman and that passion would tarnish her son’s innocence.It may seem odd that in a family of limited means the son had a velvet coat and the mother a velvet gown, but Fanny had wealthy London relations who sent gifts. Several sisters, married into wealth, took an interest in Calyste and hoped to find him an heiress.“You stayed at Les Touches longer than last night, my dear one,” said the mother.“Yes, dear mother,” he answered, offering no explanation. The curt reply clouded her brow. Mothers who love as Fanny did tremble before their sons; they perceive what love will take and yet feel joy in their children’s happiness.“Go to bed, dear child,” she said, repressing her tears.The next day Calyste slept until mid-day; his mother would not wake him. Mariotte served breakfast in his bed. At one o’clock the family sat in the salon to read and prepare for the three-o’clock dinner. The baroness read the Quotidienne; as she finished a paragraph she heard Calyste’s steps above and dropped the paper.“Calyste must be going to dine again at Les Touches; he has dressed himself.”“He amuses himself, the dear boy,” said the old sister.Mariotte appeared.“What is it?” she said; “anything wanted?”“The chevalier dines at Les Touches; don’t cook the fish.”“But we are not sure yet,” said the baroness.“You seem annoyed, sister; I know it by your voice.” Fanny informed them that Monsieur Grimont had heard grave charges against Mademoiselle des Touches, who had so changed Calyste. The baron, amused, tried to dismiss it.“Suppose Monsieur le chevalier does love Mademoiselle des Touches, where’s the harm?” said Mariotte. “She has thirty thousand francs a year and is very handsome.”“A Guenic marry a des Touches!” exclaimed the old baron. “A des Touches could hardly be an actress,” added the old man with ancestral pride. The baroness answered that Camille Maupin wrote plays and books; the baron, surprised, shrugged. Mariotte, not inquisitive, left when Calyste entered.“Are you going again to Les Touches, my Calyste?” the baroness asked.“Les Touches is not respectable. Its mistress leads an irregular life; she will corrupt our Calyste. Already Camille Maupin has made him read many books; you knew all that and never said one word to your best friends!”“The chevalier is discreet,” said his father.“Too discreet,” said the jealous mother, noting a flush on her son’s brow.“My dear mother,” said Calyste, kneeling beside her, “I didn’t think it necessary to publish my defeat. Mademoiselle des Touches, or Camille Maupin, rejected my love more than eighteen months ago during her last stay at Les Touches. She laughed at me gently, saying she might very well be my mother and that a woman of forty loving a minor were a crime against nature; she tried to comfort me and offered her friendship nobly. She has more heart than talent; she is generous. I am now her child. On her return lately, hearing she loves another, I have resigned myself. Do not repeat calumnies against her. Camille is an artist; she has genius; she leads an exceptional life not to be judged like ordinary lives.”“My child,” said Fanny, “nothing can excuse a woman for not conducting herself as the Church requires. She fails in her duty to God and society by abjuring the gentle tenets of her sex. A woman sins even in going to a theatre; but to write the impieties that actors repeat, to roam the world, first with an enemy to the Pope, and then with a musician—Calyste, you can never persuade me such acts are deeds of faith, hope, or charity. Her fortune was given by God to do good; what good does she do with hers?”Calyste sprang up. “Mother,” he said, “Camille is my friend; I cannot hear her spoken of this way; I would give my very life for her.”“Your life!” said the baroness. “Your life is our life, the life of all of us.” The old sister, knitting rapidly, declared that education had caused the change: “Educated people did the evil—be educated if you choose!”“Calyste,” she added later in a voice from her heart, “your father has never opened books; he speaks Breton, he fought for God and king.” The family’s conflicting views left the baroness agitated. Calyste whispered that it was impossible to explain now but promised a night’s talk after which she would bless Mademoiselle des Touches. He took his leave. The baron offered to visit Les Touches himself and report; the remark hinted at future confrontation.Guerande had seen Calyste’s frequent visits to Les Touches and concluded Mademoiselle Félicité des Touches was passionately in love with the youth and worked sorceries. Rumor swelled; the rector and local notables, alarmed, related the strange life of the author who hid behind the masculine name Camille Maupin. To Abbé Grimont she was monstrous—a cross between a siren and an atheist—violating the social laws that bound womanhood.Camille Maupin was the mask of Félicité des Touches, a Breton whose literary virility long led the world to suppose her a man. Her two volumes of plays published in 1822 provoked literary revolution in the clash of classicism and romanticism; she later wrote more plays and a novel that sustained her success. To understand why Félicité chose a man’s name, kept her freedom, and differed from Madame de Staël, one must recall her life.Félicité des Touches became an orphan in 1793. Her property escaped confiscation though her father and brother were killed for the king; her mother died soon after, leaving her to a sister, a nun of Chelles. Madame de Faucombe took the child to Faucombe, near Nantes. The populace of Nantes seized the nuns and Félicité on a false charge; Thermidor released them. Her aunt died of fear; another sister left France; the third entrusted Félicité to her great-uncle, Monsieur de Faucombe, an old archaeologist in Nantes.Monsieur de Faucombe, fond of amusement, left Félicité’s education to chance. She kept him company in his library and read widely. Thus she acquired theoretical knowledge of life without the innocence of ordinary upbringing. Her intellect was crammed with undigested learning; imagination ran riot while her personal life remained chaste. By eighteen she was as well-informed as a young man entering literary circles. This prodigious reading formed an unusual intellect without corrupting chastity—a surprising outcome to those who knew only convent routine.She helped Faucombe by writing three of his books; the strain affected her health, and doctors prescribed horseback and society. She became an admirable horsewoman and recovered. At eighteen she entered society and produced sensation, called “the beautiful Mademoiselle des Touches.” Seeking the pleasures of a woman’s triumph, she discovered coldness toward tribute and contempt for trivial talk; resentment at family ridicule led her to cultivate coquetry and music. She studied with Steibelt and later in Paris perfected harmony, composing music for two operas whose true authorship is concealed.Provincial mediocrity soon bored her; she returned to books, piano, Beethoven, and Faucombe. In 1812, at twenty-one, Faucombe gave her the guardianship accounts and she took control of fifteen thousand francs from Les Touches, twelve thousand from Faucombe, and a saved capital. She learned finance and local administration, invested wisely at the right moments, and by young adulthood had fifty thousand francs a year—force of will equal to a man of thirty. Habits of criticism enabled sober judgments about art and public affairs. Faucombe’s later illness kept her until his death as Europe convulsed under Napoleon.A Royalist, she hastened to Paris with the Bourbons’ return. Political commotions preserved her mind from personal entanglements. In 1815 she bought a fine house in rue Mont Blanc and settled as an independent woman. Accustomed to controlling her life, she adopted modes of thought and action considered masculine. By 1816 she knew nothing of marriage as a life-scheme; her superior mind refused the abdication married life requires. She valued independence and felt disgust for maternity’s duties. These facts explain Camille Maupin’s anomalies: having known no normal parental education and raised by an archaeologist among books, she had not played with dolls.Toward the close of 1817 Félicité felt bodily lassitude and feared aging. Between marriage and love she wanted freedom; she was still susceptible to admiration. At forty she retained twenty-five’s vivacity; Nature favored her, and meditation preserved youth.A Breton type with darker features, Félicité’s face was longer than oval, like an Isis in bas-relief; olive in daylight, luminous by candlelight, it changed little with emotion. Heavy black hair, a broad forehead, and incisive eyes—pupils ringed with an orange circle—gave severity and depth to her glance. In passion her eyes could be sublime; in repose they were often calm. Her nose and mouth could express disdain and tenderness in turn; the chin was firm, the bust ample, waist slim; arms and hands were strong and delicate. The head’s carriage was majestic; lips and nostrils offered expressive mobility. Yet overall the face seemed tinged with sadness from constant meditation; she listened more than she spoke, startling by silence and a deep, attentive look; no educated spectator could see her without thinking of Cleopatra.This anatomy of character suggested ambiguity: did she judge and not feel, or feel and judge at once? Her great mind made her often unpossessable, feared as a living symbol rather than an object of ordinary affection. Adventures of her life, partly rumored and not disavowed by Camille, reinforced such questions.Her beauty, fortune, and position aided her fame. In 1817 she opened her house to artists, authors, scientists, and publicists; her salon resembled Baron Gérard’s: men of rank mingled with distinguished talent and Parisian women. Ambitious mothers hoped to marry in; diplomats and men of distinction came for intellectual amusements. Mademoiselle des Touches studied comedies of passion and ambition on display. She despised accepted flatteries and laughed at things others took seriously.Ordinarily a woman feels, enjoys, and judges in succession; in Félicité this order reversed. Youth had been wrapped in knowledge and reflection. This transposition explained her life’s strangeness and talent and led inevitably to a first love late in life. A first secret affair had shown her the folly of valuing physical beauty as soul-beauty; she recovered with the help of a friend—a singular man, poet, critic, traveler—who took her to Italy to teach her Art and gave her frankness and literary grace. He roused her taste for German and English literature; in Rome in 1820 he deserted her for an Italian. That betrayal hardened her contempt for men and helped shape Camille Maupin’s public persona.Back in Paris, she collaborated with Conti, the great musician, for whom she wrote librettos. Her two volumes of plays made Camille Maupin famous; she then published a short novel, one of the period’s masterpieces and a dangerous work akin to “Adolphe.” Her pseudonym’s motives remain obscure; perhaps a woman’s wish to escape fame while using a man’s name for criticism played a part.Her celebrity grew through salon influence, wit, judgment, and learning. She became an authority; her sayings were quoted and she could not abandon the social role Paris bestowed. She was an exception: women admired her mind, men her beauty. Her friendships seemed platonic; far from Madame de Staël’s politics, she joked about Camille Maupin, admiring rivals with composure.From 1817 to 1834 she visited Les Touches occasionally. Her first return followed the 1818 disillusion; she took lodgings in the village to contemplate herself, then had furniture sent from Paris to Les Touches. The public’s curiosity about her luxury filled the porter’s purse; visitors came. Later, after returning from Italy with Conti, appearances excited less interest. By the time of this story, news of her double existence had spread in Guerande; letters addressed to Camille Maupin at Les Touches arrived. In a region so Catholic and prejudiced, her singular life provoked rumors that alarmed Abbé Grimont; such a life seemed improper to him.During her present stay at Les Touches she was not alone: Claude Vignon, a scornful, powerful writer, had been her guest for years. Knowing his laziness and penury, she still appeared inclined to marry him—ambition, dread of solitary old age, and a desire to secure a superior man with her fortune may have influenced this. Yet she was misleading both Calyste and Claude; her heart was undergoing the most violent convulsion possible for a soul as strong as hers. Too late she saw life lighted by youthful love, shining again in a heart she thought past such fires.Let us now see Camille’s convent where this was happening.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few hundred yards from Guérande the Breton soil gives way to salt‑marshes and dunes. We descend into a sea‑left desert along a rugged ravine where carriages have never passed: irregular ponds, muddy banks where salt is made, and a narrow arm of the sea separating the mainland from Croisic. Geographically a peninsula, Croisic clings to Brittany only by quicksands joining it to Batz and seems more an island.Where the road to Croisic branches from the main road stands a country house with a large garden and a grove of trimmed, wind‑twisted pines—some umbrellalike, some denuded, their trunks reddened by peeled bark—trees that prepare the eye for the marshes and dunes beyond, looking like a stiffened ocean. The house, of slaty stone with granite courses, is plain: a wall pierced by evenly spaced windows—small leaded panes below, larger ones above—attics under an immense pointed roof with two gables and dormers; circular windows under each gable, one facing the sea, the other toward Guérande. One façade faces the Guérande road, the other toward the desert ending at Croisic, and a brook escapes through the park wall, crossing the road to be lost in the sands.The house’s gray tones harmonize with the bleak landscape. The park is an oasis; at its entrance a mud hut shelters custom‑house officials. Though not large in land, most of the estate lying in Guérande, Les Touches yields over ten thousand francs a year from marshes and farms. Once a fief reduced by the Revolution, it is still called “the château” by the paludiers and would still harbor “seigneur” if the fief had not passed in the female line. When Félicité set about restoring Les Touches she, an artist in taste, kept the desolate exterior that gives it a prison‑like air, altering only by placing an entrance gate enlivened by two brick columns supporting an arch and planting trees in the court.The ground floor follows the plan of many eighteenth‑century country houses, built on ruins of an older castle: a large paneled entrance hall—turned by Félicité into a billiard room—opens into an immense salon with six windows and the dining room; the kitchen communicates with the dining room through an office. Camille has shown noble simplicity in arranging this floor, avoiding ostentation. The salon, painted gray, is furnished in old mahogany with green silk coverings; the dining room contains great mahogany buffets, horsehair chairs, and fine Audran engravings in mahogany frames. A heavy wooden staircase, covered with a green carpet, leads up.Above are two suites separated by the staircase. Mademoiselle des Touches has taken the one facing sea and marshes, arranging it as a little salon, a large chamber, and two cabinets—one for dressing, the other a study and writing room. The other suite she made into guest apartments; servants’ rooms occupy the attic. The guest rooms are simply furnished; a certain fantastic luxury is reserved for her own apartment. In that somber, melancholy habitation looking over the austere landscape she gathered the most imaginative art she could find. The little salon is hung with Gobelin tapestry set in carved oak. Heavy silken hangings—brocades shot with crimson and gold against green and yellow—are gathered into mighty pleats and trimmed with fringes, cords, and tassels worthy of a church. The salon contains a valuable chest or cabinet, a carved ebony table, a Venetian secretary inlaid with ivory arabesques, and other noble Gothic furniture. Her bedroom is strictly Louis XV: a carved white bedstead with Cupids, a canopy, blue silk hangings, a Pompadour dressing table, a duchesse, a chaise‑longue, a small sofa, silk window curtains lined with pink satin, and a Savonnerie carpet—the elegant dainties of eighteenth‑century women.Her study, entirely contemporary and resembling a boudoir, contrasts with the Louis‑Quinze gallantries and contains a charming collection of mahogany furniture. The bookshelves are full, but the room is cluttered with the trivialities of a woman’s existence mingled with masculine articles: pistols, a narghile, a riding‑whip, a hammock, a rifle, a man’s blouse, tobacco, pipes, a knapsack—an odd combination that paints Félicité in bold strokes.Beyond the park stretches the desolate landscape—the last vegetation of the continent—whose peculiar beauty any great soul entering that room would notice. Melancholy squares of water, divided by white salt crust paths where paludiers, dressed in white, rake and gather salt into mulons; saline exhalations that keep birds away and stifle botanical growth; sands brightened only by one hardy plant bearing rosy flowers and the Chartreux pansy; a salt lake, sandy dunes, the little town of Croisic afloat like a Venice, and the mighty ocean flinging foam upon granite rocks—this sight uplifts and saddens. The sublime here produces a longing for unknown things perceived by the soul; these wild harmonies belong to great spirits and great sorrows.This desert scene, where the sun’s rays reflected on water and sand shine on Batz and ripple on Croisic’s roofs, occupied Camille’s thoughts for days; she seldom cared for the cool groves about Guérande. Her soul struggled with a deep inward anguish.When Calyste first saw the house’s gables beyond the roadside furze and distorted pines, the air seemed lighter to him. Guérande had been a prison; his life was at Les Touches. A young man full of ardor but inexperienced, Calyste found in Félicité more than a woman—he found a grand presence before knowing her merely as a woman. His adoration, impulsive and needy, survived Félicité’s repeated, inexplicable refusals; it was more the impulse to love than love itself. Camille had analyzed her refusal as generosity rather than cruelty—a foresight Calyste did not perceive.At Les Touches the young countryman encountered riches of a new world: a different language, modern music and painters, contemporary imaginative literature; in short, the great Nineteenth Century in all its critical spirit and desire for renovation. Félicité initiated him into these things. For him she was the mother of his intellect: kind, elevated, inspiring. A woman is always adorable to a man in whom she stirs love, even when she seems not to return it.At that time Félicité was giving him music lessons. Her grand apartments and coquettish private rooms were vivified by a strange, undefinable spirit. The modern world’s poesy sharply contrasted with Guérande’s dullness: on one side the thousand developments of art, on the other the sameness of uncivilized Brittany. No wonder the poor lad, bored by provincial pleasures like his mother, quivered as he approached the house, rang, and crossed the court—reactions not felt by a mature man.One morning, hearing the piano, Calyste supposed Camille was in the salon, but the music ceased in the billiard hall. He ran up the thick‑carpeted stairs, slowed as he neared the top, and perceived something unusual in the music. Félicité was playing for herself, communing with her being. Instead of entering, he sat on a Gothic seat beneath a carved window. Camille’s improvisation was mysteriously melancholy—like a cry from the depths, the prayer of a hidden plaint. She had varied and extended the introduction to the cavatina “Mercy for thee, mercy for me!” from Robert le Diable, then suddenly sang the words heart‑rendingly and broke off. Calyste entered and saw why: Camille turned to him with a face wet with tears, wiped them with a handkerchief and said simply, “Good‑morning.” She was beautiful in her morning gown: a red chenille net letting coils of black hair escape; a short upper garment like a Greek peplum revealing cambric trousers with embroidered frills and Turkish slippers red and gold.“What is the matter?” cried Calyste.“He has not returned,” she answered, looking out at the sands, sea, and marshes.That answer explained all: Camille was awaiting Claude Vignon. Calyste wanted to find him.“Where are you going?” she asked.“To find him,” he replied.“Dear child!” she said, taking his hand and drawing him toward her with one of those moist glances that reward a youthful soul. “You are distracted! Where could you find him on that wide shore?”“I will find him.”“Your mother would be in mortal terror. Stay. Besides, I choose it.” She seated him. “Don’t pity me. The tears you see are the tears a woman likes to shed. We have a faculty not in man—that of abandoning ourselves to our nervous nature and driving our feelings to an extreme. By imagining certain situations we end in tears, and sometimes illness. Women’s fancies are of the heart more than the mind. You have come just in time; solitude is bad for me. I am not the dupe of his professed desire to go to Croisic and see the rocks without me. He meant to leave us alone together; he is jealous, or pretends to be—he is young and handsome.”“Why not have told me this before? What must I do? Must I stay away?” Calyste asked, his own tears betraying him.“You are an angel!” she cried, then gaily sang the “Stay! stay!” of Matilde in Guillaume Tell, turning a grave answer into a dainty jest. “He only wants to make me think he loves me more than he does. He knows how much I desire his happiness. Perhaps he feels humiliated to be inferior to me; perhaps he suspects you. But even if his only crime is to take his pleasure without me and not share the ideas this new place gives him, is that not enough? Ah! I am no more loved by that great brain than I was by the musician, the poet, the soldier! Sterne is right; names signify much; mine is a bitter sarcasm. I shall die without finding in any man the love that fills my heart, the poesy that is in my soul—”She stopped, arms pendent, head back, eyes fixed on a carpet pattern. The pain of great minds is imposing; it reveals a vast soul whose affections belong to a people and thus affect the world.“Why did you reject my—” Calyste began, but Camille’s beautiful hand laid on his stopped him.“Nature changed her laws in granting me youth beyond my due,” she said. “I rejected your love from egoism. Sooner or later the difference in our ages would have parted us. I am thirteen years older than he, and even that is too much.”“You will be beautiful at sixty,” cried Calyste.“God grant it,” she smiled. “Besides, dear child, I want to love. In spite of his cold heart, lack of imagination, cowardly indifference, and consuming envy, I believe greatness hides there; I hope to galvanize him, to save him from himself, to attach him to me. Alas! I have a clear mind, but a blind heart.”She analyzed herself with terrible honesty, suffering and dissecting her feelings as a scientist might explain disease. “I have brought him here to judge him, and he is already bored,” she went on. “He pines for Paris; the nostalgia of criticism is on him; he has no author to pluck, no system to undermine. He dares not commit some debauch in this house that might lift his ennui. Alas! my love is not intoxicating enough to soothe his brain. Make him drunk at dinner tonight and I’ll know if I am right. I will say I am ill and stay in my room.”Calyste flushed from neck to brow; even his ears burned.“Oh! forgive me,” she cried. “How can I heedlessly deprave your girlish innocence! Forgive me, Calyste—” She paused. “There are some noble natures who say: ‘If I had my life to live again, I would do the same.’ I, who do not think myself weak, say, ‘I would be a woman like your mother, Calyste.’ To have a Calyste—oh! what happiness! I could be humble and submissive—and yet I have harmed only myself. But a woman cannot stand alone in society except in a primitive state. Affections not in harmony with social or natural laws, not obligatory, escape us. Suffering for suffering, we might as well be useful where we can. What care I for those children of my cousin Faucombe? I have not seen them these twenty years and they are married to merchants. You are my son who never cost me the miseries of motherhood; I shall leave you my fortune and make you happy—so far as money can, dear treasure of beauty and grace.”“You would not take my love,” said Calyste, “and I shall return your fortune to your heirs.”“Child!” Camille cried in a guttural voice, letting tears fall. “Will nothing save me from myself?” she added after a moment.“You said you had a history to tell me, and a letter to—” the generous youth began, wishing to divert her; she would not let him finish.“You are right to remind me. I will be honest before all else. I will sacrifice no one—Yes, it was too late yesterday, but today we have time,” she said cheerfully. “I will keep my promise; while I tell you that history I will sit by the window and watch the road to the marshes.”Calyste drew a great Gothic chair near the window and opened a sash. Camille, sharing an oriental taste with her illustrious sister, took a magnificent Persian narghile given by an ambassador. She filled the nipple with patchouli, cleaned the bochettino, perfumed the goose‑quill attached to the mouthpiece, lit the yellow leaves, set the blue‑and‑gold enameled vase at some distance, and rang for tea.“Will you have cigarettes?—Ah! I am always forgetting you do not smoke. Purity such as yours is rare! The hand of Eve herself, fresh from the hand of her Maker, alone is innocent enough to stroke your cheek.”Calyste colored; sitting on a stool at Camille’s feet, he did not perceive the deep emotion that for a moment seemed to overwhelm her.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“I promised you this tale of the past, and here it is,” Camille began.She told Calyste the letter came from the Marquise de Rochefide. The Rochefide family, less ancient than the Casterans, sought a prestigious match for their son; the Comtesse de Montcornet recommended Mademoiselle Béatrix-Maximilienne-Rose de Casteran, the youngest daughter of a father who aimed to marry his two daughters without dowries to preserve the estate for his son. Béatrix, raised at the château de Casteran, had married in 1828 at twenty. Camille called her what provincials call original—independent, enthusiastic, absorbed by art—a temperament dangerous for a woman, since only men have the support to keep balance at those edges.The dowager de Casteran approved the match. Rochefide appeared superior: the marriage linked his family to other nobles and he had nearly obtained a peerage under Charles X, lost after the July revolution. Vain and foolish, he mistook Béatrix’s refinement and reserve for frigidity and soon returned to bachelor habits. He assumed a proud, artistic wife would content herself with salon pleasures; but he combined vanity with petty jealousy and sudden cruelty. Camille analyzed how this temperament and Béatrix’s admiration bred rivalry: Béatrix wanted her own salon and tried to draw Camille’s circle away, attracting only superficial acquaintances. Camille thought Béatrix thirsted for celebrity yet conceded the marquise had grandeur of soul, regal pride, clear ideas, and an uncommon facility in art, theology, and music—though sometimes affected by arcane learning.Camille described Béatrix’s appearance in spare strokes: an ethereal blonde, slender and erect, with a long, pointed face and pale sea-green eyes; a bold forehead, a disdainful mouth, pale cheeks, fine shoulders, and an exquisite waist though not a faultlessly beautiful figure. In certain dress — cherry velvet and lace — her carriage and nobility could seem divine. Camille admitted Béatrix left an ineffaceable impression.Félicité showed Calyste a painting by Mieris; Camille joked about the charms of blondes and warned Calyste not to fall for Béatrix from her portrait. Béatrix, she said, was severe and elegant, sometimes revealing Southern passion; an angelic flash would appear and vanish. For three years after her marriage she judged the world from her height; the July revolution and social changes unsettled her. She embraced some new intellectual currents while wishing to protect the nobility; finding limited place for individual superiority, she chose personal happiness over fading social power.At Camille’s salon she met Gennaro Conti, a celebrated Neapolitan composer whose voice people adored as a soul rather than mere sound. Béatrix fell madly in love and took him from Camille. The act, painful for Camille, was fair play: Béatrix confided to Camille and made her arbiter of her choice. Camille admired Béatrix’s conduct—she preserved her dignity and displayed a grandeur in womanhood that men often cannot appreciate. Yet Camille confessed she had been fascinated by Conti: she had tested him, knowing him charming but vain and hypocritical in love.Camille portrayed Conti as a consummate charlatan of feeling—insincere, theatrical, insatiable for applause; he posed as devoted to art but was often self-centered. His warmth and chivalry deceived many women; Camille had probed and seen his false sentiment, and Conti now hated her for seeing through him. She had told Béatrix nothing.Camille had received Conti and Béatrix together in her salon to keep the affair discreet, forcing Conti to dissemble admirably. Béatrix soon told Camille she was leaving for Italy with Conti. Camille was not surprised: Béatrix felt united to him and, weary of social constraints, chose to escape into love. Conti was elated; Camille warned that he loved himself more than any woman. After a scene of fury he borrowed money from Camille for the journey. Béatrix left Rochefide a letter and went to Italy for two years; she wrote charming letters to Camille, the only woman she trusted. Conti, short of funds, accepted an offer to write a French opera, and Béatrix wrote from Genoa that they might soon return to Paris. Camille handed Calyste Béatrix’s letter.Claude Vignon then entered unexpectedly, casting an ominous presence. He was thirty-seven, prematurely bald, his once-handsome face grown haggard; he resembled a youthful Raphael but now his features had thickened and his expressions were worn by solitude. His pale blue eyes were clouded; his face suggested deep thought but little action. Camille described him as a critic whose intellect penetrated everything but whose energy failed in outward life: he demolishes but does not create. He was simple yet wily, indulgent in excesses, prone to paralysis—an arch-critic who contemplated and did not act. Camille liked the idea of making Claude more active; he fancied himself a politician and writer but habitually let opportunity slip away.Small talk followed, with veiled jests and loverly badges about entrances “by the door” or window-scaling. Claude’s sardonic manner disturbed Calyste, who felt uneasy. Claude interrupted Calyste’s earnestness about the letter with indifferent, mocking comments about love, angering the young man. Calyste, rash and sincere, was reproached by Camille and Félicité, who tried to shield him. He left in a storm of emotion, kissed Camille’s hand, and wept; Claude smirked that the youth would love much. Félicité announced that Madame de Rochefide might arrive, perhaps with Conti, which interested Claude, who had seen a vessel offshore near Croisic that could bring them.Calyste, returning home, brooded on Béatrix’s letter and Camille’s portrait. At home, his mother Fanny, Aunt Zephirine, and servants created a cozy domestic scene. Calyste unfolded the letter again and imagined Béatrix as Camille had described. He told his mother how Béatrix had opened his mind, teaching him about Paris, music, literature, Italian, and social nuances; she had become, in effect, the mother of his intellect. Fanny feared the change but, moved by her son’s devotion, softened. Aunt Zephirine urged a prudent, bourgeois path: marriage to a good Breton girl, a tranquil life to secure the estate and heirs; practical counsel that contrasted with Calyste’s romantic ardor.Calyste flared against such domestic plans, insisting he could not be confined to a married monk’s life; his imagination had been inflamed by Parisian civilization, books, and women; he longed for passion, beauty, freedom. His mother, seeing his earnestness, embraced him and blessed his pursuit of happiness while pleading prudence.After dinner Calyste returned to Les Touches under pretext of returning the letter. Claude Vignon and Félicité remained at table; Félicité indulged Claude’s gourmandise and the dinner displayed Camille’s Parisian taste, a contrast to the Guénic family’s simpler comforts.
