Calyste presented the letter; Claude read only the first page and observed that Béatrix’s love for Conti seemed bound more by pride than passion: “She is held to Conti now by pride only; she is condemned to love him quand même.” Camille protested that Béatrix’s nature was too grand to change, and reminded them Conti would accompany her.Claude’s cynical remarks pierced Calyste. When Claude casually suggested Béatrix was the right match for Calyste, the youth was wounded. Félicité admonished Claude not to plant dangerous ideas in the impressionable young man. Claude, enjoying wordplay and satire, pursued discussions about youth’s infatuations with older women—arguing that young men often adore older, semi-dowager women because they possess experience, savoir-faire, dignity, and absolute devotion that captivates youth: their love, he said, is vast and unrelieved by vanity.The evening continued with wit and anecdotes; Claude and Félicité described Parisian life to Calyste, who was charmed—intellect had immense seduction for someone “all heart.” Claude predicted the Marquise and Conti might land at Croisic the next day, having possibly come on the Danish vessel he’d seen. Camille colored at the suggestion.Calyste arrived late that night to his anxious mother, insisting he’d never had so delightful an evening: genius, he rhapsodized, could create life and choice among women. His mother tenderly kissed him and reminded him that love might suffice. Calyste pledged he would love in her stead for those who might have adored him. Fanny counseled him to choose noble women if he must pursue love, and bade him be prudent though she could not bind his heart.Thus the scene closed with Calyste torn between domestic duty and the passionate horizons opened by Béatrix’s letter and Camille’s salon—a youth intoxicated by ideas, love, and the promise of Paris, watched over by a family that loved him and feared for his future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################What young man, brimming with restrained feeling, would not slip off to Croisic to see Madame de Rochefide and watch her incognito? Calyste surprised his parents by leaving at dawn, moving with nervous agility and hugging the walls of Les Touches to avoid being seen. Ashamed of his ardor and afraid of ridicule—Félicité and Vignon were so observant—he felt his very forehead transparent.At the shore, by a stone embankment near the ferry shelter, two boats arrived loaded with trunks that announced distinguished travellers. One held only a maid in a straw bonnet with a green veil and a footman. When a boatman called him by name, Calyste, annoyed, denied expecting anyone. Yet a chest stamped MME. LA MARQUISE DE ROCHEFIDE caught his eye like a talisman; in the desert of his newly awakened desire he fastened on the first woman presented. Béatrix thus inherited the love Camille had rejected.As the luggage landed Calyste searched for another boat that might reveal Béatrix; he stood absorbed, arms folded. Often we submit to our own inventions; chance has less to do with fate than we suppose. The maid, fretting about the marquise’s dressing if no one came to Les Touches, told him Mademoiselle des Touches had fetched her that morning. Calyste, fearing he’d blundered, rushed toward Guérande, doubling to avoid servants of Les Touches. At the mall he walked and reflected, convinced the marquise had no inkling of his agitation.His fancy fastened on the marquise. He had not known such terror and joy with Camille; these vague emotions rise like poems in an untutored soul. Fired by imagination, youth can reach the heights of love in silence before ever meeting its object.On the mall he saw the Chevalier du Halga with Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël, who mentioned Calyste. The chevalier advised that if Charlotte de Kergarouët came she should be kept near Calyste for months so the girl might fall in love by daily contact and they could marry in winter. A girl of sixteen, he claimed, could carry off the prize from a woman of forty. Calyste, overhearing, felt repugnance at the notion of being imposed a girl; his thoughts were fixed on the marquise, not on fortune or a match. He returned home at five, where his mother gave him a letter from Camille:My dear Calyste,—The beautiful marquise has come; we count on you to help us celebrate her arrival. Claude says you will play Bice and she Dante. It is for our honor as Bretons, and yours as a du Guenic, to welcome a Casteran. Come soon. Your friend, Camille Maupin. Come as you are, without ceremony.Curious about the marquise’s family, Fanny asked her husband who the Casterans were; the baron, dabbling in heraldry, identified them as an old Norman house. He admitted ignorance about the Rochefides, which partly reassured Fanny but also made her fear her son’s exposure to seduction.Calyste went to Les Touches with violent but inward movements: tight throat, swelling heart, feverish haste that he tried to slow but could not. He found the company assembled in Camille’s little salon at about six, twilight spreading through the room. Camille, lifting the tapestry, announced him as “the future deputy of Brittany,” punctual as a king. He bowed to the marquise, who inclined her head; he did not at first look at her but took Claude Vignon’s proffered hand.Camille presented Gennaro Conti, a medium-height, thin man with chestnut hair and a freckled complexion whose head resembled Lord Byron’s; Conti was proud of the likeness. The marquise murmured to Félicité that Calyste was “handsome as an angel.” Sitting between the sofa and the two ladies, Calyste observed the marquise in the half-light: reclining like a white serpentine shape, more beautiful than Camille’s portrait had suggested. Félicité had done her friend a kindness; Béatrix was superior to Camille’s sketch. Calyste took in her delicate, diaphanous features—diaphanous forehead, long neck, waist seemingly graspable, bare shoulders like camellia petals, a transparent fichu revealing her outline. Her white muslin gown with blue flowers, silver filigree earrings, and curls starred with bluebells completed an image that he carved into his soul. Between Béatrix’s fair, aristocratic force and Félicité’s darker gentleness, Calyste was seized at first sight by a love that crowned his hopes and fears. He felt sudden power and rivalry, especially toward Conti.Dinner was announced. Camille gave Calyste the marquise’s arm; descending the old staircase felt like going into battle. He trembled, sweat forming as his arm shook; the marquise asked if anything was the matter. He stammered that he had never seen any woman so beautiful except his mother. Félicité, predicting his forgetfulness, prodded him to sit beside the marquise on her right, giving Claude the left. Camille retained Conti by her side to keep an eye on “coquetries.” The marquise, removing her gloves, remarked on the good opportunity with a poet.By candlelight Béatrix seemed even more lovely: satiny forehead, spangled eyes, curls like gold, a revealed nape and graceful neck—unlike Camille’s, the mark of another character. Calyste could barely pretend to eat; nervousness drained his appetite. The ardor of his heart, restrained by moral reserve, left him in inward conflict — long hesitations and tender debate typical of the pure-hearted. Félicité’s eyes, noticing the youth’s adoration, issued repressive glances like avalanches of snow; Calyste tried to control his tears and, when Félicité asked why he ate nothing, forced himself to join the conversation.But Madame de Rochefide paid him little attention. Camille, narrating her Italian journey, amused the men and women, while Claude Vignon’s sarcastic remarks about love—its root in desire and artifice—turned the table pensive. Calyste felt joy in looking at Béatrix mingled with pain at her apparent indifference. Conti searched the marquise’s eyes to guess her thoughts; his presence deepened Calyste’s unease. After dinner Camille drew Calyste aside.“My dear Calyste,” she said, “you are acting in a manner that embarrasses the marquise; she may be delighted with your admiration, but she cannot accept it. Pray control yourself.”“He was hard to me; she will never care for me,” Calyste replied. “If she does not I shall die.”“Die! you! My dear Calyste, you are a child. Would you have died for me?”“You have made yourself my friend,” he answered.After coffee, Vignon asked Conti to sing. Camille sat at the piano. Together they sang the duet Dunque il mio bene tu mia sarai from Zingarelli’s Romeo e Giulietta, a passage of tragic modern poignancy whose phrase “Di tanti palpiti” expresses love’s grandeur. Calyste, sitting in the armchair where Félicité had related the marquise’s story, listened rapt. Béatrix and Vignon were on either side of the piano; Conti’s sublime voice, mingling with Félicité's, turned the music into a poem of divine melancholy—the farewell of two swans to life. The room fell under feelings beyond vulgar applause.“Ah! music is the first of arts!” exclaimed the marquise.“Camille thinks youth and beauty the first of poesies,” retorted Claude, whose sarcasm had already struck Camille. Béatrix, searching the room’s faces for the music’s effect, saw Calyste’s white, tear-wet face. Moved, and for Conti’s sake as much as the scene, she looked quickly toward the musician. Music had pierced Calyste’s heart, and Conti’s genius overwhelmed him; despite Camille’s warnings, he now believed in the soul’s beauty expressed by such an artist. How could he compete with a voice that entered the soul like another soul?Calyste was left in despair and reverence. His ingenuous admission of his own nothingness showed on his face mingled with admiration. Camille, touched, cried that Conti would never obtain applause equal to that child's homage, and urged them to sing a trio. Béatrix joined. Overwhelmed, Calyste rose softly, retreated to an open-door bedroom, flung himself on a sofa, and sat with his head in his hands, plunged in meditation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################X. DRAMAClaude Vignon enters Calyste’s room and tells him what he believes Calyste most needs to hear: Camille loves him. Calyste, who thinks himself pledged to the marquise Beatrix, is stunned. Claude, weary of life and unable yet to leave it, confesses he has suspected this for days. He explains he did not loiter in the marshes for pleasure and that when he returned to find Calyste with Camille his bitterness came from wounded pride. He plans to leave Les Touches—perhaps with Conti—preferring to witness from afar the passions he finds so rare in France, but advises Calyste to be cautious: youth entering such dangerous contests can be ruined.Calyste, bewildered and unable to reconcile Camille’s declared love with Beatrix’s hold on him, listens while Claude confronts Felicite Maupin. Claude accuses her of coming to Paris last year desperately in love with Calyste and using him as a screen to conceal her adoration. He condemns her scheme as grand and ignoble, saying she should have chosen a common man or a distracted one to conceal her designs. He tells her she has never been loved and will not be, for her strength repels those who might love her. Felicite asks why love flees her; he answers she is not lovable because she does not yield.When Calyste appears, Camille, deeply moved and more beautiful than before, betrays fear. Claude tells Calyste plainly that Felicite loves him—but not tenderly, rather in the peculiar way of a woman who delights in the suffering of longing, “in some sense a man,” he adds sardonically. He counsels the young man to use Beatrix’s attraction as a shield for Camille, arguing that Calyste’s passion now endangers the older woman. Humiliated and weeping, Camille cannot meet their eyes. Calyste, struck, falls at her feet and kisses her hands; she implores Claude not to abandon her.Claude suggests there is no danger because Calyste is already enamored of the marquise, which will be a better barrier. Camille, with a dominating look, answers that she will accept loving without hope: “Do not love me, Calyste; but I will love you as no woman will!” Claude kneels and kisses her hand.Calyste returns to Guerande dazed. At home the household notices his agitation; he tells his mother with unexpected candor, “Camille loves me, but I love her no longer.” The next day he hastens to the port but arrives as Camille and Beatrix wave to a departing steamer. Beatrix gives him an imperious look; Camille’s smile makes the marquise conscious of the vulgarity of such public behavior. After a drive where Calyste escorts the ladies and bids Camille and Beatrix goodbye, he joins a dinner at the du Guenic’s, where others assume he is free and nearly engaged to Charlotte de Pen-Hoel. Calyste is inwardly silent, torn between provincial ties and the allure of Parisian wit.XI. FEMALE DIPLOMACYCalyste reaches Les Touches as Camille and Beatrix leave the salon. He offers his arm to Felicite, who playfully chides him for abandoning the viscountess. Conversation reveals social links and sends subtle signals: Beatrix suggests the Kergarouet girl would suit him, which Camille quickly denies. Mortified by Beatrix’s coolness toward Charlotte, Calyste is silent; Camille accuses Beatrix of coldness and of driving him toward the marquise.Taking him into her chamber, Camille says plainly what must be done: if Calyste wishes to win Beatrix, he must appear to still love Camille. He knows he is too candid to feign, so she will take the deceit upon herself. He must come every day, spend mornings studying with her four hours, and be with her in the evenings—obey her blindly. If he speaks privately with the marquise, he will lose her forever. Trusting Camille, Calyste submits, convinced that within a week Beatrix will love him. He goes home restored to gaiety; the card players praise him, and he returns to Les Touches to act the ruse.After he leaves Camille, exhausted, confesses to Beatrix: “I am forty and I love him!” She laments lost youth and the shame of being loved from pity, and asks Beatrix—who loves Conti—to be noble and help keep Calyste. Flattered, the marquise resolves to behave generously; she will give him a cool shoulder while secretly enjoying the power of inspiring a man’s passion.Camille orchestrates a series of staged scenes to draw Beatrix’s attention: small signals, arranged glances, and invitations offered and declined in ways that display regret and restraint. These feminine maneuvers become a duel of snares and feints. Camille plays the jealous, self-effacing woman who praises Calyste’s purity and nobility, making Beatrix perceive both the worth and the danger of the young man. Beatrix, vacillating between pride and curiosity, affects gentle indifference but is inwardly stirred; her etiquette and hauteur mask a private satisfaction in being coveted.For six days Camille concentrates every art toward her end. Calyste, obeying but increasingly tortured, feels the plan’s deception nearly undoing him; he craves to speak with Beatrix and read her eyes. One morning he slips into the garden and surprises the marquise. Their talk is awkward: she tells him he has neither pleased nor displeased her, and when he says he no longer loves Camille she asks why he visits her every morning. Camille appears, furious, and drags him away, reproaching him for flouting their agreement. The marquise, stunned by Camille’s dramatic self-abnegation and the extremity of her proposed generosity, cannot quite grasp the part she is asked to play—yet the abnegation is typical of Camille’s grandeur.At breakfast Beatrix resolves to suppress any rising love. She remains gently indifferent—tormenting Calyste without harshness. Camille proposes an excursion, but Beatrix refuses, fearing complications: Conti is returning to fetch her. The prospect of Conti’s return turns Calyste pale. Despite Camille’s urging and his entreaties, Beatrix remains inflexible. Calyste leaves Les Touches plunged into despair, brooding on how to force an explanation with the marquise and caught between arranged appearances and the tumult of real feeling.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Calyste returned home he shut himself in his room until dinner and afterwards retreated there again. At ten his mother, uneasy at his absence, found him among blotted, half-torn sheets: he was composing a letter to Béatrix, tormented by jealousy about Camille after the marquise’s manner in the garden. As with all first love-letters, it poured from the heart and repeated itself; still, the baroness, hearing her son read it aloud, felt their old mansion glow with his passion.In this letter Calyste declares that seeing Béatrix has made his love stronger than any dream; he needs not praise her beauty, for he has studied and “penetrated” her delicacy and believes he understands every trait of hers. He asks whether she has ever been loved, understood, and adored as she deserves, and suggests that his comprehension makes him worthy of her notice. He insists his passion concerns her more than himself: he has aged by a week of suffering caused innocently by her; he is no common lover and is drawn to her like a child to light. Absolute devotion, unbounded faith—these merit nothing unless returned; her cold, stern eye turns him to ice, and yet her disdain cannot outweigh his love.Calyste confesses he once loved Félicité but forgot her at sight of Béatrix; he calls Béatrix his truth. He speaks of Camille dismissing stagnant love as wretched, of her mingling tears with his and rejecting a hopeless passion, and so he claims he is free to love Béatrix on earth and in heaven. If Béatrix loved him she would not make Camille’s arguments; they are young and could fly together. He pledges submission, patience, mute adoration, only begging not to be needlessly wounded. He believes Béatrix cannot love him without losing self-esteem, so he asks no return; still he cannot bear sustained coldness, which would make him fear she detests him. He imagines her passing through his life like Dante’s Beatrice—distant, white, cold—and asks for the music of pity as his crown. He cites his past behavior when Camille loved Claude Vignon as proof of his restraint, promising to bury his feelings unless driven to despair. A word of praise would make him bear martyrdom; a continued silence would make him think she feared him.The letter continues in this repetitive, impassioned strain: he calls her his Light and Sun, asks to be allowed to see her, suggests an excursion to Croisic and Batz as a test—absence of an invitation would be an answer. Though unlikely to sway the marquise, the letter moved his mother to tears. “My child, you are not happy,” she said, terrified and gentle. Calyste, perplexed how to send the letter, followed his mother into the salon with it burning in his pocket. The Chevalier du Halga was present; Charlotte de Kergarouet tried to use Calyste’s distraction for her own aims. Calyste wandered like a distracted butterfly. When a card game ended he asked the chevalier in a whisper how best to deliver a letter secretly to a mistress. “Slip it into the maid’s hand with a louis or two,” the chevalier suggested—though he admitted it was better to give it oneself. Calyste snatched his hat and ran to Les Touches.Appearances there were electric: Camille and Béatrix sat together in the little salon. Calyste flung himself impulsively beside the marquise, took her hand, and so rapidly slipped the letter into it that Félicité did not perceive it. His heart tingled as Béatrix pressed his hand and, without interrupting her words, slipped the letter into her glove. “You fling yourself on a woman’s dress without mercy,” she laughed. “Calyste is wanting in common sense,” said Félicité, openly rebuking him. He kissed Camille’s hand and, after a brief, embarrassed conversation, left Les Touches at eleven, having met Camille’s piercing glance.That wakeful night Calyste searched Guérande’s main street many times for an answer. At last the marquise’s waiting-woman brought a note, which he read in the garden grotto. Béatrix’s reply is careful, proud, and composed: she calls him a noble child but still a child and reminds him that he is bound to Camille, who adores him. She insists he would not find in her the perfections that distinguish Camille, nor the happiness Camille can give. She speaks plainly of her situation: she loves and is beloved, and the circumstances of her life forbid accepting homage. For a woman in her position, a man declaring love is not a flattering thing but an insult; yielding would lower her and shame Camille, whose love must not be robbed a second time. The thought of being betrayed, humiliated, or abandoned provokes in Béatrix a horror she does not conceal; she admits she might find courage to kill a man who, seeing her thus, spoke to her of love.Béatrix explains that, after receiving his letter and making a frank reply, she could at ease be what he asks in Camille’s house; but the bitter ridicule that would follow if she softened is dreadful. A second theft from Camille would prove her want of power and no woman could forgive such treachery. There are motives delicate women keep to themselves which men cannot understand. She praises Calyste’s mother for purity and nobility and insists she will not mock Calyste by sending him back to little Charlotte; rather she commends him to a divine young girl worthy of his love. Béatrix avows that if she were his, his life would be blighted: she is jealous, trifles would make her wretched, and inexorable thoughts of hers would poison their existence. She cannot promise gentleness; she fears becoming a tyrant of temper and resentment. She believes “true love” as Calyste imagines it is a dream; his passion, she thinks, may be mere joy of desire whose end he does not yet see.Béatrix praises Camille as inexhaustibly tender, likely to marry Calyste to a younger woman even at her own cost; she mentions practical help—selling an acre in Paris to free the Brittany estate—and says Camille will smooth difficulties and make him her heir by adoption. For Béatrix herself there is only a friendly hand: she wishes to be a sister to him and to end a correspondence between Les Touches and Guérande that she finds absurd. Her letter is a model of prudent sacrifice: generous to Calyste but immovable, refusing to reward his passion at the price of violating Camille and her own honor.Moved by her son’s strange exhibitions, the baroness could not sit quiet. She heard Béatrix’s reply and, with moist eyes, declared the marquise “a noble, grand woman,” worthy of pardon. “I will pray for her,” she said. Calyste cried, “Have I not every reason to adore her?” His mother, practical and anxious, warned of where such love might lead and urged a safer course: marry Charlotte de Kergarouet and release two-thirds of the estate. By selling a few farms, Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël could secure a marriage contract and help manage the property so their children would inherit a great name and estate. “Forget Béatrix!” she begged; Calyste muffled the entreaty and left to write an answer.The baroness, unable to bear inaction, went out to seek counsel and found the Chevalier du Halga on the mall. Certain to meet him, she recited Calyste’s letter and Béatrix’s reply without naming names, asking his opinion. The chevalier, vain and gallant, treated the affair like an adventurous leap for a thoroughbred horse and rambled into old exploits, escorting her to the alley with the courtesy of his sort.Back home, curiosity and a mother’s fear betrayed the baroness: she found an unsealed letter addressed to the marquise on Calyste’s table and, compelled, read it. Where Béatrix’s reply had been composed and dignified, Calyste’s second letter revealed a deeper plunge into madness: a clear-eyed resolution mingled with instinctive feeling that made it seem as if some worldly chevalier had dictated it. In frantic, arrogant strokes he renounced family names, fortunes, and obligations. He wrote that the Guénic name was nothing to him now; Béatrix’s happiness was his reality, her heart his only fortune. Estates mortgaged for two centuries might remain so; he considered his love his property, object, and religion.He rejected the thought of marrying any other woman: he would marry no one but Béatrix, and would wait twenty years if need be. He insisted his mother is a saint and has never loved; Béatrix had taught him what his mother had lost and what sacrifices she had made. Camille, he declared, had become a sister the moment he saw Béatrix—“the mother of my intellect, nothing more.” He recounted how he had taken Camille for a woman until Béatrix revealed what womanhood truly was, with all the grace and attractions he desired. From the first moment he felt Béatrix was his, he wrote, and it now seemed unnatural they should be apart.Calyste proposed elopement rather than patient prudence: let them fly to a country where Béatrix knows no one, where only God and he could reach her heart; his mother might follow, Ireland had castles, his mother’s family would lend one, and in a boat and with a few sailors they could disappear before anyone knew. He told her she had never been loved as he could love her, and that sacred love could wipe out the past; indeed, he said, he could wish her doubly shamed if such disgrace would make him prove his love by making her a saint. He begged for a single day to walk with her by the sea and called any passing alms of tenderness eternal riches.The baroness let the letter fall before finishing and, kneeling, prayed to God to save her son’s reason, to remove his madness and error. When Calyste entered she responded simply and tearfully that she had been praying and had committed the sin of reading his letter. “My Calyste is mad!” she said. He kissed her and called it “a sweet madness.” He even suggested: “Tomorrow let us take a boat and cross to Croisic. If you are on the jetty you can see her.”What especially terrified the baroness was that Calyste’s second letter showed both instinctive feeling and a clear-eyed resolution that read as if a worldly adviser might have dictated it; the danger was no longer only youthful folly but a persistent, possibly ruinous, determination. She saw in her son the surrender of prudence to passion—an ardor that would not be soothed by Béatrix’s dignity nor by appeals to honor. The chapter closes with Calyste sealing his letter and departing for Les Touches, resolved and unyielding, while his mother, half-humbled and wholly anxious, looked toward God to deliver him from his craze.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Perhaps one of the basest delights for small minds is ensnaring a great soul. Beatrix knew herself inferior to Camille Maupin—not only in talent but in the deeper matter of passion.When Calyste rushed to Les Touches with the ardor of first love, the marquise reveled in being its object. She would not share the feeling; she fancied herself heroic in repressing a caprice and persuaded herself she matched Camille’s devotion, imagining she sacrificed for a friend. Her vanities—her coquetry—were flattered by Calyste’s ardor. Assailed by such seduction, she resisted outwardly while inwardly exulting in her own virtue.Camille, who had set in motion the situation she now regretted, guarded herself against the clever, friendly rival she had admitted. To preserve secrets and remain aloof she contemplated nature, even turning for consolation to the idea of God; in the glow of those thoughts, Calyste appeared to her like a celestial image, a guardian angel lifting her above temporal suffering. Yet Beatrix carried an air of triumph that disturbed Camille. Between them a silent struggle played out—each pretending to magnanimity while hiding private wounds.Calyste arrived with a letter tucked between glove and hand, intending to slip it to Beatrix. Camille watched the instant when a woman’s glance betrays what she would conceal; women, she thought, have a genius for nuance and read rivals in a look. She perceived in Beatrix and Calyste a mutual understanding and felt discomposed.Beatrix no longer feigned indifference; she regarded Calyste as her own. Calyste, too, was transparent—blushing like the guilty and the happy. He said he had come to arrange a trip for the next day.“Then you really intend to go, my dear?” Camille asked.“Yes,” Beatrix answered.Camille proposed details of the excursion and sent Calyste away to make arrangements, then, privately, chided him for some unspecified folly. When he left to hire boatmen, anxieties assailed him; Camille’s grave words had forecast danger. Four hours later he returned, tired and expecting dinner, but found a maid telling him neither her mistress nor the marquise could receive him that evening. Shut out, Calyste went home, bewildered and distressed. At his house his family noticed he was altered: his gaiety extinguished by despair, the fall from elation feeling dreadful.While Calyste brooded at home, a scene between Camille and Beatrix unfolded that would shape the next day. Calyste’s letter had stirred emotions in Beatrix she had seldom experienced: the ardor and innocence of his affection made her suddenly desire control. Yet rereading his words bred a cruel suspicion. What had Calyste and Camille been doing together while Claude Vignon was away? If Calyste did not love Camille, and Camille knew it, how had they spent their mornings? Memory cast certain of Camille’s speeches in a new light; Beatrix pictured Camille’s intimacy with the young man and felt belittled, imagining herself a mere toy in a woman’s indulgence of a child she loved extraordinarily.The thought was a thunderclap. Beatrix reviewed the past week, scrutinizing the roles each had played; fury and jealous anger convinced her that Camille’s conduct might be vengeance for long-nursed resentments. Uncertain yet resolved to act, she went downstairs for dinner without dressing, her taciturn manner chilling Camille, who realized a crisis had come. Camille withdrew to fight alone; she feared that Beatrix would collapse under assault and wanted to be the sole adversary.Dinner was gloomy. Camille remained gentle but felt superior; Beatrix was hard and cutting, as though managed like a child. The battle began in glances and half-spoken sentences. When they rose, Camille offered her arm maliciously; Beatrix pretended not to see and stormed upstairs alone. After coffee came a flinty exchange—Camille’s dry wit meeting Beatrix’s venom—until Mademoiselle des Touches dismissed the footman, their signal for a private quarrel.“You make novels more dangerous than those you write,” Beatrix said.“They are unpublished, my angel,” Camille replied with a cigarette.Beatrix launched into a tirade: women know each other’s pride and virtue, she said, but Camille had something of the man about her—unrestrained, contemptuous of women, cruel. She accused Camille of trickery toward Calyste and of descending to depths unbecoming of her. Camille smiled, amused by the sphinx-like barbs, and with ironic composure suggested Beatrix was capable neither of foolish love nor of profound passion—too cool-headed, too calculating.Beatrix, stung, struck back about age and advantage; Camille, composed, retorted that she loved Calyste—“far too much for my peace of mind”—and that if he had a passing fancy for Beatrix, so be it: Beatrix was fair and young, while Camille was dark and dignified. Camille claimed she had done her best to prevent their situation but would not be humiliated by a rival unless she chose to allow it. She urged Beatrix to be frank with Calyste if he courted her, promising to aid her or to offer a refuge near Paris if the affair continued. “The purest love lies twenty times a day,” she said with a sting—suggesting love survives, even thrives, on its deceptions.Beatrix, unsettled by Camille’s disdain and her apparent willingness to accept the situation, produced Calyste’s last letter as a weapon. Camille read it and wept—true, violent tears. The letter revealed how deeply Calyste loved Beatrix; Camille’s grief was the genuine suffering of a woman mortally wounded by being loved but not fully returned.“Do you love him?” Camille demanded, searching Beatrix’s face. “Have you that infinite worship which triumphs over pain and survives betrayal?”Beatrix, with naïveté and honesty, confessed she did love him but in a way that made her ashamed of herself. She felt purified by his love and refused to corrupt his destiny. “If I cannot be his wife, I shall not be his mistress,” she declared. She would not be “an eternal regret” or a millstone round his neck. To love, for her, had become a moral test; she would not spoil his life.Camille, horrified, cried that this was not love. “To love and calculate!” she exclaimed. The closeness between them ruptured. Camille accused Beatrix of having words that would separate them forever: “We are no longer friends. Here begins a terrible conflict between us. You will either succumb or fly.” She left the room like a maddened lioness, then paused at the portière to ask coldly whether Beatrix would go to Croisic the next day. Beatrix, proud, replied she would not flee or yield. Camille answered she would write to Conti, a threat that made Beatrix pale.That night the violence subsided, both women arguing with themselves and returning to the art of temporizing—an approach often effective between men and women but dangerous among rivals. In the background, Mademoiselle des Touches heard the great Voice whose counsels can subdue the strongest will; Beatrix heard only worldly prudence and feared social contempt.Thus Félicité’s last deception had, for the moment, succeeded: Calyste’s mistake was repaired—he had escaped from immediate involvement—but a fresh indiscretion might yet prove fatal. The two women, bound in rivalry and wounded by different truths, had staked fortunes of feeling and honor on a hazardous game. Each returned to her defenses: Camille with the bitter certitude of a love that might cost her everything, Beatrix with a new-found rigid moralism that would not let her be the cause of Calyste’s ruin. The next day’s excursion and the letter left in tension the fates of all three, the outcome poised on pride, sacrifice, and the dangerous art of feminine strategy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It was late August; the sky was clear, the sea near the horizon gleamed like molten silver, and the shore rippled with little waves. Sunlight made the sands almost tropical; salt shone white on the marsh. Paludiers in white with long rakes had been at their posts since morning; some leaned on mud-walls watching the harvest, others stood with family. Custom-house officers smoked calmly. The scene was exotic enough that a Parisian might not guess it was France.The baron and baroness, who had pretended interest in the salt harvest, stood on the jetty admiring the quiet landscape where only the sea sounded and the cultivated green belt and boats lent charm. A party came along a narrow path from Les Touches: the marquise walked first alone; Calyste and Camille followed arm-in-arm, Gasselin bringing up the rear. The marquise saw Madame du Guénic and felt a repulsion for Béatrix, though Béatrix was attractively dressed in a Leghorn hat with bluebells, crimped hair, a gray gown, and a blue sash that made her seem a princess disguised as a milkmaid. Calyste introduced Camille to his parents; the paludier noted that Mademoiselle was the largest landowner and a good lady. They exchanged bows and the party boarded a waiting boat in silence.The marquise remained cold and dignified, having lectured Calyste for his disobedience; Calyste, in despair, cast tormented glances at Béatrix, where anger and love struggled. The passage from Guérande to Croisic was wordless. At Croisic, salt was discharged and peasant-women carried it in earthen jars on their heads; barefoot carriers watched a little Danish vessel unload. To avoid remarks and stay near Calyste, Camille sprang toward the rocks, leaving him to follow with Béatrix. Gasselin kept a distance.The Croisic peninsula is flanked by granite carved by the sea into arabesques and vaults. Under a vaulted roof is a hollow like a marble bathtub, floored with white sand and tepid water safe for bathing. Cool covered hollows, roughly carved but majestic, recall palaces copying nature. One rare tree stood on a granite promontory—a great box-bush whose roots suggested three hundred years’ life—above a cliff whose base formed a semicircle of rocks. It took nerve to climb this little Gibraltar; from its summit one sees clock towers, arid fields of Croisic, and dunes stretching to Batz.Calyste led Béatrix to this vantage point where the view was magnificent and the granite imposing. Camille, like a wounded wild animal craving solitude, had dressed in male attire—trousers edged with frills, a short blouse, a peaked cap—and courted danger among fissures and caves. She reached the tree’s shade and sat lost in thought.Alone, Camille considered old age and what a woman like her should do after having drunk too eagerly of fame. She ate strawberry lozenges and mused that strawberries no longer existed but lived in their qualities; perhaps people survive in their spiritual qualities too. Facing the ocean’s vast suggestion, she felt that only conviction of future holiness could give immortality meaning. A noble inward change came: the worldly woman in her died, and a more spiritual being rose. She resolved to withdraw from society, to transform the loves she once pursued into a divine impulse and renounce social ambition forever.After walking in silence, Calyste compared the sea’s depth and purity to his love. Béatrix remarked on the grandeur, saying it differed from the Mediterranean, and laughed that “it is met by a rock.” He answered with a look that he could summon the patience of the angels when he saw her; yet alone he suffered so much his mother wept for him. The marquise used that barren plain dried by salt winds as an image of her inner desolation. She told Calyste she loved him but would never be his; she would remain bound, a sandy plain without fruit. If abandoned, she would ask pardon of the man she had offended; she would never risk taking a happiness she believed would quickly end.Calyste replied passionately, offering to sacrifice everything—family, name, future—but she recoiled from the word “sacrifice,” seeing it as an insult. He cried that love cannot argue, it simply is; he would do the impossible. Moved but firm, she said she had done wrong and asked him not to tempt her further. They walked on in tense silence, his inward fury visible only to those who love without hope.At the base of the rock by the box-bush, Calyste helped Béatrix ascend the steep slope; for him it was a precious privilege. He clasped her round the waist. When he asked whether she would never be his, she replied she could only be Béatrix—a dream—and that that sweet, tender dream could be without bitterness or repentance. When he, in frenzy, cried “You shall never belong to any man!” and pushed her, she slipped eight or ten feet into the cavity where the box-bush grew; her gown caught on a rock point and tore slowly, lowering her onto the bush.Mademoiselle des Touches, horrified, signaled to Gasselin. Calyste leaned forward, savage; he saw Béatrix’s precarious position and shuddered. Her lips moved as if praying; the bush began to give. With youthful agility, Calyste slid down to the ledge below, grasped the marquise and held her though both risked sliding into the sea. She had fainted; in that aerial spot he fancied her wholly his, and his first emotion was pleasure. “Open your eyes,” he begged; she murmured “Die?” and when she opened them he kissed her, feeling her tremble convulsively with passionate joy.Gasselin’s hob-nailed shoes sounded above them; Camille followed. They sought a way to save the lovers. Gasselin declared he must slide down so they could climb on his shoulders and be pulled up; Camille was to fetch a ladder from Croisic. Béatrix asked to be laid down; Calyste placed her on a narrow ledge where, faint and then weakly revived, she seemed ready to faint again. Camille insisted the incident must be seen as an accident whether Béatrix lived or died. Calyste feared she would hate him; Camille reassured him she would adore him. Gasselin fetched a ladder, and with a contrivance involving Camille’s red shawl, Béatrix reached the top; the Breton took her in his arms and carried her to the shore like an infant.She murmured to Félicité that she had not wished for death, but for suffering. Camille had her taken to a farmhouse from which they had borrowed the ladder and a doctor was summoned from Croisic. Calyste, Gasselin, and Camille made a litter from spare clothes on the ladder and carried Béatrix; farmers gave her a bed while Gasselin rode for a doctor and sent a boatman for aid.Calyste sat on a stool answering in monosyllables; Camille grew uneasy at his unnatural state. The physician bled Béatrix; she improved and consented to embark, and by five o’clock they reached the jetty at Guérande whence she was carried to Les Touches. News of the accident had spread.Calyste spent the night at Les Touches beside Béatrix’s bed with Camille. The doctor had assured them only slight stiffness would remain. Through despair there came a gleam of joy: he could watch her sleep or wake, study her pallid face and expressions. Camille recognized in him a passion one feels but once—a consuming fire that blinds a man to everything else. She saw he would never know the real woman in Béatrix; he adored instead an image fashioned by love.Calyste was guileless; when he saw Béatrix’s green eyes turned to him with love, confusion, and mischief, he blushed and looked away. Béatrix jested sweetly about men promising happiness and ending by flinging women down precipices. He knelt, took her moist hand, and kissed it humbly, telling her she had the right to reject his love. Félicité soothed him, asserting that unless a woman were a monster she must be flattered by such devotion.The incident marked Calyste. That night he murmured Béatrix’s name as he slept. The hôtel du Guénic was calm; Mademoiselle des Touches had written to the baroness about the accident. Calyste returned to breakfast at Les Touches and found Béatrix up and dressed though pale and feeble. The harshness and coldness that had guarded her were gone. Camille played music, leaving Calyste free to take Béatrix’s hands; no storms erupted; Félicité effaced herself.Cold, fragile, hard women like Madame de Rochefide—women with pale souls—need a thunderbolt to melt; to Béatrix, Calyste’s fury of love and his mad action were that thunderbolt. She felt humbled inwardly; a pure love bathed her heart. She breathed a genial air of feelings hitherto unknown and felt raised to the ideal of Woman that Bretons honor. The respectful adoration of youth cost her little; a gesture, a look, a word satisfied him. The value he placed on trifles touched and magnified her; to hold her gloved hand meant more to him than possession to another man.Few women resist such constant deification. Béatrix felt sure of being obeyed and could command Calyste to risk his life for a caprice. This gave her a noble and imposing air; she sought to remain the loftiest of women in his eyes. Her coquetries grew persistent because of inward weakness; she played the invalid for a week with charming hypocrisy, leaning on Calyste’s arm in languid dependence on walks between house and garden.Before the Croisic excursion the two women had laughed at men’s declarations of love and agreed that clever men often go straight to the point. Madame de Rochefide could restrain Calyste by reminding him of his violence on the rocks; a look from her filled his eyes with tears and stilled his prayers and arguments. By infernal coquetry she brought him to seek Camille’s advice. Béatrix, armed with Calyste’s own letter and his axiom that to love is the first happiness and to be loved comes later, restrained his passion within respectful idolatry. She liked the hymns of praise and adoration youth offers; she gave no definite answer. He asked only permission to love, which suited his search after love’s spiritual side.Driven by despair, Calyste complained to Camille. She told him she had intended to cure him by making him quickly understand Béatrix, but he had spoiled it; once he was her master, now her slave. She advised he provoke a quarrel—let Béatrix ill-treat him, then stay away until she recalled him. In extreme illness one accepts cruel remedies, and Calyste followed her counsel, staying away two days. On the third he scratched at Béatrix’s door to say he and Camille awaited her for breakfast. Camille lamented he had lost another chance.During his absence Béatrix often looked through the window toward the road to Guérande. Camille found her watching the gorse’s golden blooms along the roadway and spoke of them, trying to distract her. The marquise kept them waiting long for breakfast; when she appeared her dress showed intention to fascinate and prevent another absence. After breakfast she walked with Calyste in the garden and expressed a wish to return to the rock where she had nearly perished.“Will you go with me alone?” Calyste asked.“If I refused, I should give you reason to think I found you dangerous. Alas! as I have told you, I belong to another, and I must be his only. I chose him knowing nothing of love. The fault was great, and bitter is my punishment,” she said with moist eyes. Calyste was filled with compassion that tempered his ardor; he adored her as if she were a Madonna. Béatrix was torn between herself and Calyste—between reentering the world and the young happiness he offered. She began to listen to his blind passion and allowed his pity to soothe her. He spoke of understanding her past, of making her feel his comprehension day by day; she admitted silently she was indeed an angel misunderstood. “I am too humiliated,” she said. “My past has robbed the future of all security.”It was a glorious morning for Calyste when he saw Béatrix at a window watching for him, wearing the same straw hat from their memorable first excursion. Small things of passion magnified the world for him. They left Les Touches through the garden gate onto the dunes. Béatrix loved the sands and gathered hardy plants with rose-colored flowers to mix with Chartreux pansies—a little relic for them. “We’ll add a bit of box,” she said, smiling.They sat on the jetty awaiting the boat; Calyste told her of a youthful escapade on the day of her arrival. “I knew of your little escapade,” she said, and that had been the cause of her sternness the first night. During their walk her tone was lightly jesting with tenderness; Calyste had reason to think himself beloved. At a creek strewn with brilliant pebbles, when Calyste, at the summit of happiness, asked her plainly to fly with him to Ireland, she resumed her distant air, asked for his arm, and continued in silence toward her Tarpeian rock.“My friend,” she said as she mounted the granite pedestal, “I have not the courage to conceal what you are to me. For ten years I have had no happiness comparable to searching for shells with you and exchanging pebbles for the necklace I will treasure more than diamonds. I have been a little girl again... The love I have inspired in you has raised me in my own eyes. Understand these words.”They reached the rock’s summit, seeing the vast ocean on one side and Brittany on the other. “But,” she continued, “I do not belong to myself; I am more bound by my own will than by law. You must be punished for my misdeed, but be satisfied to know we suffer together. Dante never saw his Beatrice again; Petrarch never had his Laura. Such disasters fall only on noble souls. But if I should be abandoned, if I fall lower into shame and ignominy and your Béatrix is cruelly misjudged, then, my adored child,” she said taking his hand, “to you she will still be first of all; you will see her rise to heaven as she leans on you; but then, if you wish to cast her down do not fail of your blow; after your love, death!”Calyste clasped her round the waist and pressed her to his heart. As if to confirm her words, Béatrix laid a tender, timid kiss on his brow. Walking back like those who perfectly understand each other—she thinking she had gained a truce, he not doubting his happiness—both were deceived. At a turn, Béatrix was seized with a horrible trembling—the contagion of seeing a serpent—which Calyste felt before he saw the cause: on a bench beneath a weeping ash sat Conti, talking with Camille Maupin.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The marquise’s convulsive trembling made the tragic situation plain to everyone.“You did not expect me so soon, I fancy,” Conti said, offering his arm to Beatrix.Unable to refuse, the marquise let Calyste’s arm fall and took Conti’s. Humiliated, Calyste flung himself on a bench beside Camille after exchanging the coldest salutations with his rival. He thought at first to attack Conti, but Beatrix’s violent trembling showed she suffered and paid for her faults; the sight struck him dumb, as if immobilized by fate.Madame de Rochefide and Conti passed before Calyste’s seat; Beatrix shot Camille a look that said everything and avoided Calyste’s eyes, turning instead to Conti, who appeared to jest.“What will they say to each other?” Calyste asked Camille.“Dear child,” Camille replied, “you don’t yet know the terrible rights an extinguished love gives a man over a woman. Beatrix could not refuse Conti’s arm. He’s surely teasing her about her new love; he may have guessed it from your manner and his observation.”“Teasing!” cried Calyste.“Be calm,” Camille counseled. “If he wounds her pride, she will crush him. But Conti is cunning; he’ll act more cleverly. He’ll pretend not to suspect proud Madame de Rochefide of betrayal; he’ll paint you as an ambitious child wanting a marquise in love with him, setting himself as arbiter. He’ll launch insinuations; Beatrix will be forced to deny, and he’ll become her master again.”“He does not love her. I would leave her free. True love must be chosen anew each moment. Why did he not stay away longer? What brought him back?”“The jest of a journalist,” Camille said. “His opera failed. Some critic—likely Claude Vignon—said: ‘It is hard to lose fame and mistress at the same moment,’ and it stung him. He seemed weary, disgusted; he regrets his public alliance with the marquise, and spoke to me of happier past days in a melancholy way. Perhaps he hoped to learn your secret amid my flatteries.”Calyste watched Beatrix and Conti approach but hardly listened further. Camille, careful when talking to Conti, revealed neither Calyste’s secret nor Beatrix’s. She warned Calyste that Conti was capable of treachery.“My dear friend,” she said, “this is the most critical moment for you. You need caution and cleverness you do not possess; I fear you will be tricked by the wiliest man I have known, and I can do nothing to help you.”The dinner bell rang. Conti offered his arm to Camille; Calyste gave his to Beatrix. Camille stepped back to let the marquise pass, and Beatrix found a moment to put her finger to her lips at Calyste, urging discretion.At dinner Conti was extremely gay—perhaps to probe Madame de Rochefide, who played her part poorly. Had her conduct been mere coquetry she might have deceived Conti, but her new love was real and betrayed her embarrassment. The wily musician pretended not to notice. At dessert he steered talk to women and praised their constancy—women who abandon men in prosperity would sacrifice all in misfortune; they cling to their first lover as honor requires. His praises, meant to wound, fell like incense on the altar of one bleeding heart. Camille and Beatrix alone saw the bitter sting beneath the eulogy; they flushed but controlled themselves. After dinner they took each other’s arms and, by mutual consent, turned aside into the great salon to be alone in darkness.“It is dreadful to let Conti ride over me; yet I can’t defend myself,” Beatrix said in a low voice. “The galley-slave is always a slave to his mate. I am lost; I must go back to my galleys! And it is you, Camille, who have cast me there! Ah! you brought him back a day too soon, or a day too late. I recognize your infernal talent as author. Well, your revenge is complete, the finale perfect!”“I may have said I would write to Conti, but to do it was another matter,” cried Camille. “I am incapable of such baseness. But you are unhappy, and I forgive the suspicion.”“What will become of Calyste?” asked the marquise, with naive self-conceit.“Then Conti carries you off, does he?” Camille asked.“Ah! you think you triumph!” cried Beatrix, anger distorting her face. Camille tried to hide a satisfaction beneath false sympathy; Beatrix, versed in such deceptions, saw through it. Sitting under a strong light on the divan where the first three weeks had hosted comedies and where many secret tragedies began, they examined each other for the last time and felt forever parted by undying hatred.“Calyste remains to you,” said Beatrix; “but I am fixed in his heart, and no woman can ever drive me out of it.”Camille replied ironically with the famous line: “You reign, you love, and you depart!”Neither noticed Conti and Calyste’s absence. Conti had remained at table with his rival, begging him to keep him company finishing a bottle of champagne.“We have something to say,” Conti added, to prevent refusal. Placed as he was, the young Breton could not refuse.“My dear friend,” the composer said in his most caressing voice as Calyste listened after a couple of glasses, “we are both good fellows and can speak frankly. I have not come suspiciously. Beatrix loves me,”—with a gesture of self-conceit—“but the truth is, I have ceased to love her. I am not here to carry her off, but to break off our relations and leave her the honors of the rupture. You are young; you don’t yet know how useful it is to appear the victim when you are the executioner. Young men spit fire and leave a woman in fury, but wise men do as I do; they get themselves dismissed, feigning mortification, which leaves regret in the woman’s heart and a sense of her superiority. You don’t know how hampered men are by rash promises women accept when gallantry makes us swear eternal faithfulness; let time cure what we promise. I have seen that you love Beatrix. I leave her in a position where she loses nothing of her majesty; she will certainly coquet with you, if only to tease that angel Camille Maupin. Well, take her, love her—you’ll do me a service; I want her to turn against me. I fear her pride and virtue. Perhaps it will take time to effect this chase. On such occasions the wisest plan is to take no step at all. I did just now, walking on the lawn, attempt to let her see I knew all and was ready to congratulate her on her new happiness. Well, she was furious! At this moment I am desperately in love with a prima donna, Mademoiselle Falcon of the Grand Opera. I think of marrying her; yes, I have got as far as that. When you come to Paris you will see I have changed a marquise for a queen.”Calyste’s candid face revealed the satisfaction and love Conti wanted to discover. No man, however blasé, does not flare when his love is threatened by a rival. When lovers reach this extremity, both strive to act first. Questioned by the composer, Calyste related all that had happened during the last three weeks at Les Touches; Conti, concealing fury under charm, received it all.“Come, let us go upstairs,” Conti said. “Women are so distrustful; those two will wonder how we can sit here together without tearing each other’s hair out; they might come down to listen. I’ll serve you faithfully, my dear boy. You’ll see me rough and jealous with the marquise; I shall seem to suspect her; there’s no better way to drive a woman to betray you. You will be happy, and I shall be free. Seem to pity that angel for belonging to a man without delicacy; show her a tear—for you can weep, you are young. I, alas! can weep no more; and that’s a great disadvantage lost.”They went to Camille’s salon. At Calyste’s request Conti sang the famous “Pria che spunti l’aurora,” which had often been his triumph. Never was his singing more extraordinary than that night, when so many feelings contended in his breast. Calyste was in ecstasy. As Conti sang the first words of the cavatina, he looked intently at Beatrix, giving the words a cruel signification fully understood. Camille, who accompanied him, guessed the order thus conveyed, which bowed Beatrix’s head. She looked at Calyste and felt sure he had fallen into some trap despite her advice. This conviction became certainty when the evidently happy Breton came up to bid Beatrix good-night, kissing her hand with a little air of confident triumph.By dawn, as the song foretold, Conti was carrying Beatrix away with Camille’s horses to the first relay. The morning twilight allowed Madame de Rochefide to see Guerande, its towers whitened by the dawn against the dark sky. Melancholy thoughts possessed her; she was leaving one of the sweetest flowers of her life—a pure love such as a young girl dreams of; the only true love she had known or likely to know. The woman of the world obeyed the laws of the world; she sacrificed love to their demands just as many women sacrifice it to duty. Sometimes pride can appear as high as virtue. Read thus, this history is that of many women.The next day Calyste went to Les Touches about midday. From the window where, the day before, he had seen Beatrix watching, he saw Camille, who ran down and told the cruel truth in one word: “Gone!”“Beatrix?” asked Calyste, thunderstruck.“You have been duped by Conti; you told me nothing, and I could do nothing for you.”She led him to her little salon, where he flung himself on the divan and burst into tears. Felicite smoked her hookah and said nothing, knowing no words could arrest such anguish. Calyste, unable even to think, sat all day in torpor. Just before dinner, Camille tried to speak after begging him to listen.“Friend,” she said, “you caused me the bitterest suffering, and I had not, like you, a beautiful young life in which to heal myself. For me, life has no spring, nor my soul a love. To console myself I sought above. Here, in this room, the day before Beatrix came, I drew her portrait; I did not do her injustice, or you might have thought me jealous. I wanted you to know her as she is, for that would have kept you safe. Listen now to the full truth. Madame de Rochefide is unworthy of you. The scandal of her fall was unnecessary; she did it deliberately to play a part in society. She is one of those women who prefer the celebrity of scandal to tranquil happiness; they fly in the face of society to obtain the fatal alms of rebuke; they desire to be talked about at any cost. Beatrix was eaten up with vanity. Her fortune and wit had not given her the feminine royalty she craved; they had not enabled her to reign supreme over a salon. She then bethought herself of seeking the celebrity of the Duchesse de Langeais and the Vicomtesse de Beauseant. But the world gives its interest to real feelings only. Beatrix playing comedy was judged second-rate. There was no reason for her flight; the sword of Damocles was not suspended over her. She is neither sincere, loving, nor tender; if she were, would she have gone away with Conti this morning?”Camille spoke long and eloquently to open Calyste’s eyes, but her effort was useless; he displayed absolute belief in Beatrix. She forced him to come down into the dining-room and sit while she dined; he could not swallow. Such bodily contractions and nervous reactions belong to extreme youth; later organs harden. A man often resists violent grief because his organs are stronger.Mademoiselle des Touches was alarmed at Calyste’s calm resignation after his burst of tears. Before he left, he asked permission to go into Beatrix’s bedroom, where he had seen her on the night of her illness; there he laid his head on the pillow where hers had lain.“I am committing follies,” he said, grasping Camille’s hand, and bidding her good-night in deep dejection.He returned home to the usual company at mouche and spent the evening beside his mother. The rector, the Chevalier du Halga, and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel all knew of Madame de Rochefide’s departure and rejoiced. Calyste would now return to them; they watched him cautiously. No one imagined the result of a first love in a heart so simple and true.For several days Calyste went regularly to Les Touches. He paced the lawn where he had walked with Beatrix. He often went to Croisic to stand on that fateful rock or lie for hours in the box-bush; by finding footholds he had means of climbing. These solitary trips, his silence, his gravity, made his mother anxious. After about two weeks of this caged-animal conduct, the poor lover ceased to cross the bay; he had scarcely strength to drag himself along the road from Guerande to the spot where he had seen Beatrix watching. The family, delighted at the departure of “those Parisians,” saw nothing fatal or diseased in the lad. The two old maids and the rector, pursuing their scheme, kept Charlotte de Kergarouet, who nightly played coquetries on Calyste, obtaining in return nothing better than advice in playing mouche. During long evenings Calyste sat between his mother and the little Breton girl, observed by the rector and Charlotte’s aunt, who discussed his mood as they walked home. Their simple minds mistook lethargic indifference for submission to their plans.One evening when Calyste, wearied, went off to bed, the players dropped their cards and looked at each other; they had heard his steps with anxiety.“Something is the matter with Calyste,” said the baroness, wiping her eyes.“Nothing is the matter,” replied Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel; “but you should marry him at once.”“Do you think marriage would divert his mind?” asked the Chevalier.Charlotte, urged by the baron, arrived with unusual care. After a discourse on matrimony to which he could not answer, Calyste requested a few days’ grace. The old baron rubbed his hands with joy and whispered the “good news” to the baroness.Breakfast was gay; Charlotte sparkled. After the meal Calyste took Charlotte to the grotto. Their parents watched from the window.“They are watching us,” Charlotte said.“They cannot hear us,” he replied.“True; but they see us.”“Let us sit down, Charlotte,” he said, taking her hand.“Is it true your banner used to float from that twisted column?” she asked, already thinking of owning the house. “You will make changes, won’t you, Calyste?”“I shall not have time,” he said, taking her hands and kissing them. “I love someone you have seen and who loves me, and I cannot make any other woman happy; though from childhood you and I have been destined. But I shall wait.”“And I, too,” Charlotte said, tears in her eyes. “You cannot long love a married woman who, they say, has gone off with a singer—”“Marry, Charlotte,” Calyste interrupted. “With the fortune your aunt intends to give you, you could choose some better man than I. I have brought you here not to tell you what you know, but to entreat you, as a childhood friend, to take this rupture upon yourself and say you have rejected me. Say you do not wish to marry a man whose heart is not free; thus I shall be spared the sense that I have done you public wrong. You do not know how heavy life is now; I cannot bear the slightest struggle; I am like a man whose vital spark is gone. If it were not for the grief I should cause my mother, I would have flung myself into the sea. Do not speak of this to anyone. Good-bye, Charlotte.”He kissed her hair and left by the postern-gate to Les Touches, remaining near Camille till past midnight. Returning home at one, he found his mother waiting with her worsted-work. Softly he clasped her hand and asked, “Is Charlotte gone?”“She goes tomorrow, with her aunt, in despair,” answered the baroness. “Come to Ireland with me, my Calyste.”“Many a time I have thought of flying there—”“Ah!” cried the baroness.“With Beatrix,” he added.Some days after Charlotte’s departure, Calyste joined the Chevalier du Halga for his daily promenade. They sat on a bench with a view from Les Touches to the rocks of Croisic, against which waves dashed. Calyste was thin and pale; his strength diminished; he felt shudders denoting fever. His eyes, surrounded by dark circles, had the fixed-brightness of obsession. The chevalier was the only companion with whom he could exchange a few ideas; in him Calyste divined an apostle of his own religion and recognized vestiges of an eternal love.“Have you loved many women?” he asked.“Only one,” the chevalier replied.“Was she free?”“No,” exclaimed Du Halga. “Ah! how I suffered! She was the wife of my best friend—my protector—yet we loved each other so!”“Did she love you?” Calyste asked.“Passionately,” the chevalier replied fervently. “Until her death; she died at forty-nine during the emigration at St. Petersburg. I have thought of fetching her to Brittany and laying her near me! But she lies in my heart.”The chevalier brushed away his tears. Calyste took his hand and pressed it.“I care for this little dog more than for life,” the old man said, pointing to Thisbe. “The little darling is precisely like the one she held on her knees. I never look at Thisbe but I see Madame l’Amirale’s hands.”“Did you see Madame de Rochefide?” Calyste asked.“No,” replied the chevalier. “It is sixty-eight years since I have looked at any woman with attention—except your mother, who has some of Madame l’Amirale’s complexion.”Three days later the chevalier said, “My child, I have a hundred and forty louis laid by. When you know where Madame de Rochefide is, come and get them and follow her.”Calyste thanked him, envying his existence. But day by day he grew morose; he seemed to love no one; all things hurt him; he was gentle only to his mother. The baroness watched his decline with growing anxiety; by prayer and entreaty she made him swallow food. Toward the end of October the lad ceased to go to the mall; the chevalier came in vain to tempt him out.“We can talk of Madame de Rochefide,” the old man would say. “I’ll tell you my first adventure.”“Your son is ill,” he confided to the baroness when convinced efforts were useless.Calyste answered that he was well; but like all youths in melancholy, he took pleasure in thoughts of death. He no longer left the house but sat in the garden on a bench, warming himself in a pale sunshine, alone with his thought.Soon after Calyste ceased even to go to Les Touches, Felicite asked the rector to come. The assiduity with which Abbé Grimont called at Les Touches became the great topic of the town. The masters and servants were afflicted by Calyste’s increasing weakness though none imagined youth might die of love. Even the chevalier had no example of such a death among his memories. They attributed Calyste’s thinness to want of food. His mother implored him to eat. Calyste tried to conquer repugnance to comfort her; nourishment taken against his will only increased the slow fever consuming him.During the last days of October the cherished child could no longer mount the stairs; his bed was placed in the lower hall, surrounded by family. They finally sent for the Guerande physician, who broke the fever with quinine and reduced it in a few days, ordering Calyste to take exercise and find amusement. The baron, roused from apathy, recovered some strength; with Calyste, Gasselin, and two dogs he started for the forest, and for days they hunted. Calyste obeyed, visiting neighbors’ chateaus. But he had no spirit; nothing brought a smile. His livid, contracted features betrayed utter passivity. The baron, worn by exertion, returned home exhausted with Calyste almost as ill. For several days both were dangerously ill; the family sent, at the Guerande physician’s request, for two of the best doctors in Nantes.The baron had received a fatal shock on realizing Calyste’s change. With the lucidity of the dying he trembled that his line might perish. He prayed, and his face turned constantly toward his son’s bed. At the least motion Calyste made, a commotion stirred within him. The baroness no longer left the room where Zephirine sat knitting in horrible unease. Demands were made for wood and provisions; Zephirine, not nimble enough, gave her keys to Mariotte but insisted on knowing everything; she questioned Mariotte and her sister-in-law. One night, when father and son dozed, Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel told Zephirine she must resign herself to the baron’s death. The old woman dropped her knitting, groped, and drew from her pocket an old black chaplet, on which she prayed with a fervor that gave her withered face a new splendor; the other old woman imitated her, and all present, on a sign from the rector, joined in the spiritual uplift.“Alas! I prayed to God,” said the baroness, remembering her prayer after reading Calyste’s fatal letter, “and he did not hear me.”“Perhaps it would be well,” said the rector, “to beg Mademoiselle des Touches to come see Calyste.”“She!” cried Zephirine, “the author of all our misery! She turned him from his family, taught him impious books, an heretical language! Let her be accursed! She has destroyed the du Guenics!”“She may perhaps restore them,” the rector said gently. “Mademoiselle des Touches is a saintly woman; I am her surety. She has only good intentions toward Calyste.”“Let me know the day she sets foot in this house, that I may get out of it,” cried the old woman passionately. “She has killed both father and son. Do you not hear death in Calyste’s voice? He is so feeble he barely whispers.”At that moment the three doctors arrived. They examined Calyste and the baron; they were surprised the baron yet lived. The Guerande doctor calmly told the baroness that for Calyste it would probably be best to take him to Paris to consult the most experienced physicians; it would cost over a hundred louis.“People die of something, but not of love,” said Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel.“Alas! whatever be the cause, Calyste is dying,” said the baroness. “I see all the symptoms of consumption, that most horrible disease of my country, about him.”“Calyste dying!” said the baron, opening his eyes; two large tears rolled slowly down his wrinkled cheeks—the only tears he had probably ever shed. Suddenly he rose, walked to his son’s bedside, took his hand, and looked earnestly at him.“What is it you want, father?” said Calyste.“That you should live!” cried the baron.“I cannot live without Beatrix,” replied Calyste.The old man dropped into a chair.“Oh! where could we get a hundred louis to bring doctors from Paris? There is still time,” cried the baroness.“A hundred louis!” cried Zephirine; “will that save him?”Without waiting, the old maid ran her hands into her placket-holes, unfastened her petticoat, and let it fall, producing a heavy sound as coins tumbled. She knew where she had sewn her savings and ripped them out with the rapidity of magic. The gold pieces rang as they fell, one by one, into her lap. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel gazed in amazement.“But they’ll see you!” she whispered.“Thirty-seven,” answered Zephirine, counting.“Every one will know how much you have.”“Forty-two.”“Double louis! all new! How did you get them, you who can’t see clearly?”“I felt them. Here’s one hundred and four louis,” cried Zephirine. “Is that enough?”“What is all this?” asked the Chevalier du Halga, coming in, puzzled by the old blind friend holding a petticoat full of gold.Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel explained.“I knew it,” said the chevalier. “And I have come to bring a hundred and forty louis I have been holding at Calyste’s disposal, as he knows.”The chevalier produced rouleaux from his pocket. Mariotte, seeing such wealth, sent Gasselin to lock the doors.“Gold will not give him health,” said the baroness, weeping.“But it can take him to Paris, where he can find her. Come, Calyste.”“Yes,” cried Calyste, springing up, “I will go.”“He will live,” said the baron, in a shaking voice; “and I can die—send for the rector!”The words cast terror on all present. Calyste, seeing the mortal paleness on his father’s face, came to his side. The rector, after hearing the doctors, had gone to Mademoiselle des Touches intending to bring her back; in proportion as he had formerly detested her, he now admired and protected her as a shepherd protects the most precious of his flock.When news of the baron’s approaching end spread in Guerande, a crowd gathered; peasants, paludiers, and servants knelt in the courtyard while the rector administered last sacraments to the old Breton warrior. The whole town was agitated by the probable extinction of this old Breton race; it was felt as a public calamity.The solemn ceremony affected Calyste deeply. His filial sorrow silenced the anguish of his love for a moment. During the baron’s last hour he knelt beside him, watching death come. The old man died in his chair in presence of the assembled family.“I die faithful to God and his religion,” he said. “My God! as the reward of my efforts grant that Calyste may live!”“I shall live, father, and I will obey you,” said the young man.“If you wish to make my death as happy as Fanny has made my life, swear to me to marry.”“I promise it, father.”It was touching to see Calyste, or rather his shadow, leaning on the chevalier’s arm—a spectre leading a shade—follow the baron’s coffin as chief mourner. The church and little square were crowded with country people from thirty miles round.But the baroness and Zephirine soon saw that despite his promise Calyste fell back into fatal stupor. On the day the family donned mourning the baroness took her son to a garden bench and questioned him closely. Calyste answered gently and submissively, but his replies proved only the despair of his soul.“Mother,” he said, “there is no life in me. What I eat does not feed me; the air does not refresh me; the sun feels cold; it seems to you to light that front of the house, but to me it is a blur. If Beatrix were here it would be dazzling. There is but one thing left that keeps its shape to my eyes—this flower,” and he drew from his breast the withered bunch the marquise had given him at Croisic.The baroness dared not say more. His answer seemed more indicative of madness than his silence. She saw no hope or light in the darkness.The baron’s last hours had prevented the rector from bringing Mademoiselle des Touches to Calyste. But that day, while mother and son sat on the bench, Calyste quivered at seeing Felicite through the opposite windows. She reminded him of Beatrix, and his life revived. It was to Camille the poor mother owed the first motion of joy that lightened her mourning.“Well, Calyste,” said Mademoiselle des Touches, “I want you to go to Paris with me. We will find Beatrix,” she added in a low voice.The pale, thin face flushed red and a smile brightened him.“Let us go,” he said.“We shall save him,” said Mademoiselle des Touches to the mother, who pressed her hands and wept for joy.A week after the baron’s funeral, Mademoiselle des Touches, the Baronne du Guenic, and Calyste started for Paris, leaving the household in charge of old Zephirine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Félicité carefully planned to secure Calyste’s future by allying him with the ducal Grandlieu family, whose line would end in five daughters. She had informed the Duchesse de Grandlieu of Calyste’s past and her intentions. After selling her rue du Mont‑Blanc house for two million five hundred thousand francs, her agent bought a rue de Bourbon house for seven hundred thousand; she intended to use one million to recover the du Guenic estates and leave the remainder for Sabine de Grandlieu. Félicité knew the family’s arrangements: the youngest would marry the Vicomte de Grandlieu (the heir); Clotilde‑Frédérique would remain unmarried for Lucien de Rubempré’s memory; two sisters were already married; and Sabine, nearly twenty, was the eligible match. Félicité resolved that Sabine should cure Calyste of his passion for Béatrix.On the journey to Paris Mademoiselle des Touches revealed these plans to the baroness. The new rue de Bourbon house was being furnished as Sabine and Calyste’s future home should the scheme succeed. At the hôtel de Grandlieu the baroness received the honors due her rank. Mademoiselle des Touches encouraged Calyste to see Paris, investigated Béatrix’s whereabouts (she had gone abroad), and occupied him with diversions. As the ball season began, Calyste noticed a resemblance between Sabine and Madame de Rochefide, Paris’s most bewitching woman; this fancied likeness made him attentive to Sabine’s coquetry. Sabine liked Calyste, and the winter of 1837 restored his spirits. He listened without repugnance when his mother reminded him of his promise to his dying father and urged marriage with Sabine. Outwardly he agreed though inwardly indifferent—an indifference the baroness alone perceived and trusted time and a happy home would conquer.On the day the families gathered in the Grandlieu salon to sign the marriage contract, when the notary Léopold Hannequin began the preliminaries, Calyste, disquieted, suddenly and curtly refused Mademoiselle des Touches’s benefactions. Embarrassed, the company watched as Sabine entered and handed him a letter from Mademoiselle des Touches intended for this moment. Calyste read Camille Maupin’s letter.Camille’s letter was a renunciation and a last appeal. She wrote that before entering her convent cell she was allowed one last look at the world she abandoned, and that thought was of Calyste, who had been her whole world. Now betrothed to God, she implored him not to contest her chosen service. She acknowledged that if love is suffering she had indeed loved him; yet she wanted him to accept the service she could render: not a gift but a trust. She had thought more of his children and the old Breton house than of him when planning to devote her Parisian profits. She begged him to accept part of her fortune to restore the du Guenic name and sustain his lineage. Camille promised to pray for him and his family, describing her new innocence and serene devotion; her conversion was attributed to the Abbé Grimont, who soon became vicar‑general of the diocese.Reading the letter moved Calyste; he returned to the company and said, “Let us sign the contract.” The following week, after an early ceremony at Saint‑Thomas d’Aquin, Calyste and Sabine left amid tears and congratulations. The Duchesse de Grandlieu and Clotilde wept, fearing Sabine had been launched upon life with a husband who had not wholly married of his own free will.
