The baroness, in a preceding carriage, had been asked by the duchess to take maternal care of Sabine. A chasseur rode as courier; servants, postilions, and ritual accompaniments completed the convoy. The duchess hinted to Calyste that she understood him as they bade farewell.The narrative reflects on marriage’s nature: it is not mere pleasure but a test of temperamental compatibility, physical sympathies, and character harmonies. Women, especially mothers and marriageable daughters, know its risks; they weep at weddings because they grasp what is at stake, while men smile, thinking they risk little.Much of a honeymoon belongs to the bride. Sabine, trained in the faubourg Saint‑Germain’s manners and possessing virginal sincerity, proved as interesting as Mademoiselle de Chaulieu. Her letters to her mother during the honeymoon reveal her mind and temperament. In Guérande she wrote that Calyste had married with great sorrow; their difficulties were greater than her mother had anticipated. She confessed that she loved Calyste as if he were not her husband—so entirely and devotedly that she feared losing dignity but embraced devotion nevertheless. Alone in the carriage their initial awkwardness led to polite words; she later pressed him to recount the events that had led to his despair and the marriage he felt obliged to make. His confession about Madame de Rochefide produced an argument, but he ended with protestations of absolute forgetfulness and gestures of affection—kissing and clasping her hand—gestures that made her more devoted, proud at having cured a wound he had thought incurable. She acknowledged that her devotion risked reducing her dignity but believed that loving him absolutely might be worth that danger.After a few days of intimacy they abandoned formalities. Her mother‑in‑law, trying to take her mother’s place, was charming and attentive; she sensed Sabine’s heroism and hid her anxiety in precaution. At the sight of Guérande’s towers Sabine tested Calyste, asking if he had truly forgotten Béatrix; his delight at such confidence led Sabine to devote herself entirely to excising Madame de Rochefide from his memory. She admitted her devotion had made her his slave, though she resolved not to surrender all self‑respect.By May 15 Sabine declared marriage delightful. She described playing the châtelaine—riding through forests, visiting farms, dining on oaken tables with centenary linen, attending local festivities, and receiving affection from tenants and neighbors. Calyste had assumed the seigneurial role with ease; peasants greeted him as a “beautiful seigneur” and likened him to former Guenics. Félicité’s repurchase of the du Guenic estates had drawn the tenants’ admiration and proposals for fair leases. Calyste planned to quarter his arms with those of Des Touches and adopt a motto; the couple visited the convent with Abbé Grimont, who affirmed that Félicité was indeed saintly. Mademoiselle des Touches, thinner and paler, would not receive Calyste but welcomed Sabine, speaking candidly.She explained she had entered the order of Saint‑François de Sales to discipline the intellect rather than mortify the body; her “guilty” head had deceived her heart. She urged Sabine to preserve her happiness by exercising the “science of a woman’s life”: be capricious, coquettish—not by odious scheming but by an artful balance between usury and prodigality—so as to bind Calyste to her, bear him children, and secure his respect for their mother. Above all, Mademoiselle des Touches counseled that Sabine should try to prevent Calyste from ever meeting Béatrix again.The name Béatrix stunned them both. When Sabine asked whether she should return to Guérande, Mademoiselle des Touches answered yes but warned, “Never go to Les Touches. I did wrong to give him that property.” She called Les Touches “Bluebeard’s chamber,” a place that could awaken a sleeping passion and thus be dangerously compromising. Sabine summarized their conversation and confessed she could not fully follow the advice to be capricious; her nature made her give herself over to love. She feared losing the happiness she had so willingly seized, yet clung to her devotion like a mother to an infant, trembling at any threat.Note: When Béatrix was first published in 1839, the volume ended with a paragraph stating that Calyste, rich and married to the most beautiful woman in Paris, retained a sadness nothing dispelled—not even the birth of a son at Guérande—and that Béatrix still lived deep in his heart, leaving uncertain what disasters might follow should he meet Madame de Rochefide again. In 1842 that concluding paragraph was suppressed and the story continued as above.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Guerande, July, 1838.Sabine writes to her mother, the Duchesse de Grandlieu, in an outpouring of anguish and bewildered jealousy. Married only three months, she has learned the meaning of jealousy: a mingling of the deepest hatred and the deepest love. She feels betrayed, unloved, and relieved to have a mother who will listen. The source of her torment is Les Touches, a place her husband Calyste’s past is attached to and which everyone—mother-in-law, aunt Zéphirine, Mademoiselle des Touches, and the Chevalier du Halga—treats as a forbidding name. Their hints and warnings ignite Sabine’s curiosity and test her security: when forbidden, Les Touches becomes irresistible. She teases and questions, and like many women who tempt fate, she is drawn into the very peril she fears. Calyste’s reticence about Les Touches pricks her pride; he is silent or evasive when she mentions Béatrix de Rochefide, the woman connected with that place. When finally he agrees to go, Sabine allows herself to be led there, intent on discovering whether his old passion is truly dead.At Les Touches Sabine enjoys the morbid fascination of a place she had been told to avoid. She watches Calyste closely, seeking signs that his old emotion has faded. At first he seems pensive and she thinks she has conquered the past. Yet reminders of Béatrix—the bush where he once flung her into the sea, anecdotes of her improprieties—stir uneasy glimpses of jealousy in Sabine. A remark of Calyste’s—“cease to galvanize that passion”—both alarms and reassures her. The more Sabine seeks tests, the more she reveals her dependence on his affection; the more she clings to his smiles, the more afraid she becomes that her love is not returned as fully as she gives it.The visit convinces her that Les Touches has reawakened something in Calyste. She sees in him a revival of the stormy sentiments she feared, and she withdraws from Les Touches, swearing never to return. Though she hates Madame de Rochefide and even longs for her death, Sabine recognizes that her hatred proves the depth of her love for Calyste. She’s tormented by dreams of Béatrix and plans to investigate whether Madame de Rochefide is in Paris; if so, Sabine contemplates staying away, afraid that another meeting would tip the balance of her marriage. Her letters to her mother reveal a young woman slowly realizing that her ideal marriage—she had imagined a union of love—has become something different: Calyste, while kind and charming, behaves sometimes like a man who took her for his convenience or who remains emotionally attached elsewhere.By October 1838 the Guénic family return to Paris. The hôtel du Guénic is being restored, Grindot the architect supervising, Clotilde and the Grandlieu duchesse overseeing. Sabine settles in the rue de Bourbon to see how her marriage is judged by family and society, more anxious to be approved than to preside over a great household. Calyste, easy and somewhat indifferent, allows others to manage his social life; he has attained his place by name and marriage. Parisian pleasures, the duchess’s and Clotilde’s direction, and the distractions of high society renew some lightness in the household. Sabine’s anxiety and envy give way to pride in being admired; pregnancy and the arrival of a son in October 1839 seem to secure the marriage. Like many mothers, Sabine makes the natural but imprudent choice to nurse her child herself, believing herself inseparable from the baby.By August 1840 Sabine has nearly finished nursing; two years in Paris have changed Calyste. Association with the Duc Georges de Maufrigneuse, card-playing pleasures, clubs, and other habits typical of idle young men have altered him. He learns to enjoy the entertainments of the town and the company of men who find in leisure the attraction of liberty from domestic duty. A young wife’s maternal care can accommodate a husband’s dissipations; Sabine, who loves and believes in him, tolerates much. One October evening, to escape their infant’s cries, Calyste is persuaded by Sabine to go to the Variétés; he sits in a stall, agreeing as a courtesy. During the interlude his eye is struck by Madame de Rochefide in a proscenium box. Seeing Béatrix again after three years convulses him. Her presence—light, motion, seductive art—revives in him the powerful impressions of the past. Sabine, with her duty and steadiness, contrasts painfully with Béatrix’s vivacity; past passion and present comfort stand opposed.Calyste first intends to leave but, drawn, follows the half-open proscenium door and finds Béatrix between Canalis and Raoul Nathan. She has changed in three years—she has learned the arts of dress, coquetry, and cultivated a more poetic, studied allure. Béatrix whispers, teases, and in a tone that mixes complaint and triumph calls Sabine “a little fool.” Although married, she affects an air of wounded dignity and a desire to retain remnant attachments; abandoned by Conti she has refined seduction into an art. She tells Calyste she suffers, is exhausted, and claims to need a friend. He is wounded and charmed. She places him at once in a role both consoling and compromising. Canalis leaves them together; Béatrix’s perfume and presence disarm Calyste, who feels the old passion return. Her mixture of feminine resource and cultivated deprivation—an effect of deliberate artifice—transforms her poverty into a stage of dignity: a modest house in the rue de Chartres opposite the Parc Monceau, cheap staff, and a garden that retains traces of aristocratic taste. Calyste, intoxicated by memory and the possibilities of renewed intimacy, accepts her invitation to visit.At Béatrix’s house the air is charged: silk, velvet, antiques, a dagger used as a paper-cutter, watercolors of bedrooms—decor that suggests both charm and impertinence. She greets him in a toilette calculated to awaken desire. The fragrance she once wore at Les Touches returns; the effect is immediate. She confesses two principles: she will not deny herself foolishly to a man she truly loves, yet she will not belong to any man. She pretends to respect Calyste’s duties—his young wife, his baby—while simultaneously insinuating possibilities. She ridicules Sabine—brown, dry, stiff, the product of Grandlieu’s line—and suggests that Sabine is unworthy of a man like Calyste. Her talk is calculated to wound the sensibilities of love and pride. The conversation goes on for hours; Béatrix’s mockery of Sabine and her claim to an enduring attachment inflame Calyste.Béatrix manipulates Calyste with a mixture of cruelty and seduction. She taunts him about his marriage, urging him to dine with her and then accusing him of cowardice when he hesitates to deceive Sabine. When he suggests writing a note to excuse himself, she flaunts her power by demanding that he actually lie. Under her goading he composes a curt line—an unelaborated “I dine out; do not expect me”—which she sends. Calyste returns home in the small hours, exhausted and guilty. The note is laconic and on feminine scented paper; Sabine, naturally trusting yet anxious, explains it away at first but is unsettled. She is delighted in the morning to see her child well and to present him to Calyste. His distracted cordiality and his charm hide a deeper turmoil; Sabine does not yet understand the dimensions of his untruth.Calyste, inexperienced in deception, scrambles to manage witnesses. He begs the Vicomte de Portenduere to corroborate a tale, then hurries back to Béatrix to ensure further intimacy. He stays until late, returning with his horse drenched and terrified Sabine by his lateness and the scent that sticks to his clothes. On seeing him she is at once relieved and alarmed; the perfume and the note’s paper linger in her memory and body. Her reaction is violent: dread, faintness, and a sense of having been betrayed. The next step is a desperate, horribly vivid scene of inner collapse and outright hysteria.Sabine falls into convulsed prostration. She sends messages, rings for physician Dommanget, and, overwhelmed by a mix of indignation and despair, confides in her friend the Vicomtesse de Portenduere, Ursula, asking her to find out where Savinien and Calyste went. The fatal paper with a coronet on it—evidence of some titled woman—becomes in Sabine’s mind a mark of scandal. She imagines Calyste’s fidelity lost only three years into their marriage. Ursula, moved by the young wife’s danger, pledges secrecy and decides to intercede. She goes to the duchess, who, while shocked, recognizes the need for immediate deception to preserve Sabine’s life. The duchess counsels a practical lie: to prevent Sabine’s hysteria, they will invent explanations that will shield her temporarily from the reality. They are prepared to lie for the sake of a daughter’s health, admitting that such mendacity will require penance later.The duchess goes to Sabine and, with Dommanget and Ursula, organizes a credible fiction. She tells Sabine that Béatrix is not in Paris and that the coronet-paper is fashionable—it does not necessarily indicate a marquise. She also fabricates a convenient cause for Calyste’s lateness: gambling losses at the hands of the Duc de Trailles and the jeune noble Georges de Maufrigneuse. Dommanget, playing along, announces that Calyste lost about thirty thousand francs at cards, which will explain his odd mood and pecuniary anxieties. The duchess’s ploy is to take Calyste’s apparent guilt and recast it as a cause of domestic worry which will be more palatable to Sabine than the truth of a liaison. Her hope is to avert Sabine’s crisis by falsifying a narrative that Calyste might conceivably have—deplorably—fallen into without infidelity: the vice of cardrooms and masculine folly.Calyste, ashamed and cowardly, is trapped between his own conscience and the lie. He sits by Sabine’s bed, trying to put tenderness into his gaze. Dommanget, the duchess, and Ursula maintain the deception. Sabine takes the explanation with the terrible ambivalence of a woman caught between gratitude for her husband and the lingering sense of betrayal. She declares, with a flash of brave pride, that her husband’s losses mean nothing to her; she will not judge. Calyste kisses her, whispers that she is an angel, and two days later she is thought out of danger. The crisis has been smoothed by lies designed to save a life rather than to conserve innocence: the duchess’s moral reckoning is that to save Sabine she herself has told a falsehood, which will afterward need atonement.Meanwhile Calyste returns to Béatrix and tells her, almost boastfully, that he has sacrificed his wife to be with her; that Sabine nearly died from the shock, that her milk “went to her head,” and that the supposed erysipelas might leave marks on her. Béatrix listens with a face cold as ice. Far from being melted by remorse or flattered by the sacrifice, she reacts by savoring Sabine’s misfortune. Her icy sarcasms are savage: the younger, virtuous wife becomes to her a useful foil, the contrast serving Béatrix’s sense of triumph. Calyste’s attempt to arouse pity for his wife in Béatrix rebounds perversely: she uses the misfortune as an argument in her favor, insinuating that Sabine’s suffering will only make her more desirable to Calyste—a macabre and cruel way to claim victory.Béatrix’s behavior is explained by her nature and experience: abandoned by Conti and reduced to the role of an artful survivor, she has learned to weaponize vanity, melancholy, and performance. She is not merely a courtesan but an aristocratic woman who can still hide a courtesan’s mechanisms beneath the mask of a grande dame. She understands masculine weakness and the psychology of conquest: contempt, disdain, and the simulation of indifference become tools for securing the next triumph. To win Calyste back, or at least to keep him in her orbit, she begins a calculated campaign of disdain, mockery, and organized contempt for Sabine and for the faubourg Saint-Germain’s comforts. Béatrix’s deliberate uglification of Calyste—telling him he is ugly, coarse, and humiliating his physical sense—works to stimulate his desire by provoking the pride that wants to be conquered. That reverse psychology, the performance of disgust, is for her the surest way to rekindle passion.She also wages a subtler assault on the milieu that supports Sabine. She contrasts her own cultivated, if reduced, bohemian elegance and poetic homelessness with the hôtel du Guénic’s bourgeois comfort, implying that Sabine’s household is vulgar complacency and that Calyste’s tastes deserve a wilder, more aristocratic companion. Béatrix’s strategy is twofold: to make Sabine appear small and to render Calyste eager to reclaim the illusion of romantic struggle that first made him love Béatrix. She counts on the fact that deserted wives often abandon their former art; she plays off the natural jealousy that admiration for the courtesan produces among parlor-bred wives.The reconciliation between Calyste and Béatrix culminates in a scene staged as theatrical romance: a bower abundant with flowers and rare plants, in which Béatrix applies the “science of nothings,” her arts of triviality made seductive. Here she exacts conditions: she requires Calyste to swear hatred of his wife, to renounce the domestic idyll, and to dedicate himself to Béatrix in the fashion of their Guérande days. She has decided that the spectacle of Sabine’s fall is her pedestal—that public notoriety, achieved by the ruin of a beautiful young Grandlieu, will be the proof of her own continued power. Calyste, caught between shame and the intoxication of a renewed passion, submits to these demands. He has learned neither the subtle lies required to juggle a wife and a mistress nor the social arts of adultery; he is inexperienced in deception and too raw and eager in his affections. His bluntness—confessing Sabine’s existence to Béatrix and boasting of her collapse—makes him more vulnerable and less able to contain the consequences of his desires.Throughout these scenes Sabine is portrayed as the victim of her own innocence and of forces she neither comprehends nor can oppose: the cunning of a practiced coquette, the social permissiveness of Paris, the corrupting lure of masculine idleness, and the protective but manipulative devotion of her mother and friends. The duchess’s protective falsehoods are well-intended but complicate the moral landscape: they postpone the confrontation between husband and wife and substitute an expedient fiction for the truth that might heal or destroy. Calyste’s nature—enamored of the tumult of first passion, susceptible to flattery, and ill-equipped to deceive with finesse—dooms him to repeat his mistakes. Béatrix, educated in the arts of humiliation and seduction, knows how to convert every advantage into a weapon. Her refusal to pity or spare Sabine, her relish in the young wife’s humiliation, and her demand that Calyste renounce his domestic loyalty mark the cruelty of her ambition.The narrative closes with this uneasy victory: Béatrix triumphs by making Calyste her votary and by transforming Sabine’s domestic virtue into an instrument of notoriety. Calyste’s surrender—his oath to hate his wife and to embrace the romantic drama of the past—cements the new disorder. Sabine, meanwhile, remains at the center of the wrecked ideals: pure, loving, and heroic in her consternation, she is betrayed both by the husband she adored and by the brittle society that enables the machinations of a woman like Béatrix. The domestic idyll that once promised continuity and moral certainty has given way to a Paris where artful passion, vanity, and social theater can sunder marriage and produce suffering that the most prudent maternal intervention can only temporarily palliate.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After nursing her first child, Sabine returned to ordinary life looking enchanted and refreshed. Maternity had renewed her; she tried to recapture the charm of the honeymoon, only to find that her husband was no longer the Calyste she had adored. Instead of resting in happiness, she watched him with growing unease until she smelled the fatal perfume of another woman. This time she sought certainty rather than confiding in friends or the mother who had so kindly deceived her—certainty came quickly, as inexorable as the sun.About two weeks after the first crisis, Sabine received a letter:Guerande.To Madame la Baronne du Guenic:My dear Daughter,—Your aunt Zephirine and I are lost in conjectures about the dressing-table of which you tell us in your letter. I have written to Calyste about it, and I beg you to excuse our ignorance. You can never doubt our hearts, I am sure. We are piling up riches for you here. Thanks to the advice of Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel on the management of your property, you will find yourself within a few years in possession of a considerable capital without losing any of your income.Your letter, dear child as dearly loved as if I had borne you in my bosom and fed you with my milk, surprised me by its brevity, and above all by your silence about my dearest little Calyste. You told me nothing of the great Calyste either, but then, I know that he is happy, etc.Sabine wrote across it, “Noble Brittany does not always lie,” and laid the paper on Calyste’s desk. He found and read it, recognized her handwriting, then flung it into the fire, resolved to pretend it had never reached him. Sabine spent a week in an agony known to only the most solitary souls: Calyste’s silence terrified her. She reproached herself—had her virtue become hateful? Had she humiliated her idol? Those thoughts ploughed furrows in her heart.Further proof arrived. Beatrix, bolder now, wrote to Calyste at home; Madame du Guenic, recognizing the paper and perfume, handed the letter unopened to her husband, saying, “My friend, that letter is from the Jockey Club; I recognize both the paper and the perfume.” Calyste colored and pocketed it. Sabine, left alone with little Calyste, tried to hide her torment in maternal duties, but despair overwhelmed her. When servants told her Calyste would not dine at home, she sprang up, ordered a carriage, and went to the Opera, dressing brilliantly to seem smiling and triumphant. But when Otello’s music struck her, she fled; music was sometimes stronger than resolve.From then on luxury turned to torment. Riches that had once delighted her became nettles: jewelry and perfumes burned like vipers. Sabine entered the aristocratic mode of suffering where every indulgence reminded her of her pain. She watched her husband’s movements, learning to divine whether he was going to the rue de Chartres to Madame de Rochefide. She studied his forehead, hair, eyes, every little thing with the desperate attention of a hunted woman. These fatal investigations rotted the tender roots of trust.If he stayed at home, Sabine tried to be gay and humble. Once he complained that the rooms were cold and bare; she thought, “Madame de Rochefide likes flowers,” and filled the hotel du Guenic with blossoms none in Paris could surpass. When Calyste complained of draughts, she bought a glass screen to match what she imagined his mistress used; when he pecked at dinner and left plates untouched, she schemed with servants to learn Madame de Rochefide’s recipes and serve better dishes, yet he still found fault. Sabine bought English cruets and spiced sauces to imitate the condiments she believed his rival provided; but the rival’s inventions were endless.This struggle consumed months. It was agony but preferable, Sabine thought, to the deadness of indifference. Yet one evening, after dressing herself in a toilette intended to eclipse a rival, Calyste laughed, “In spite of all you can do, Sabine, you’ll never be anything but a handsome Andalusian.” She collapsed: “I may never make myself a blonde, but if this continues I shall soon be thirty-five years old.” She stayed home, tore flowers from her hair, trampled her gown, and went to bed, broken.Her suffering thinned her; grief consumed her, though she persisted in the role she had imposed: gentle, humble, suppressing the bitter words that pain suggested. Her health became apparent to her family. The duchess, an excellent mother, perceived a moral cause to her daughter’s weakness and tried to draw Sabine away from her martyrdom. Sabine at first kept silent, fearing interference between herself and Calyste, even declaring she was happy. Eventually, after months of caressing by her mother and sister Clotilde, she confessed her despair, cursed life, and spoke of death with delirious joy. She begged Clotilde, who vowed to remain unmarried, to be a mother to little Calyste should she die.One evening, with Athenais and Clotilde, Sabine gave voice to her bitterness and humiliation, warning her sister before marriage: falsely to adorn oneself is often necessary; virtue may wreck happiness. “I perish through my best qualities,” she cried. “All I knew—my virtues—were rocks on which my happiness is wrecked. I have ceased to please because I am not thirty-six years old. I laugh frankly, and that is wrong; to captivate I ought to play off hypocrisy. I am straightforward; but duplicity is more pleasing. I am loyally passionate, but I ought to be manoeuvring and hypocritical.” She imagined that, had she been worldly like her rival, she would be consoled, pitied, even adored. Instead, she had to learn “the wisdom of indifference” if she hoped to win her husband back.Clotilde whispered to be vengeful; Sabine answered she wished to die “irreproachable and without the mere semblance of doing wrong. A woman’s vengeance should be worthy of her love.” The duchess, calmer, assured her that love is a means to preserve the family. She had already discussed the delicate crisis with Sabine’s father, the Duc de Chaulieu, and d’Ajuda; she promised to find means to bring Calyste back. Clotilde smiled, noting that the Marquise de Rochefide never kept her adorers long, and vowed to help rescue Calyste if their mother’s conscience forbade drastic measures.Sabine then revealed another anguish: she believed she was pregnant again. This compounded the horror—if Madame de Rochefide bore a child, Calyste might favor that child over Sabine’s. “If Calyste loved the child of that woman more than mine—Oh! that’s the end of my patience and all my resignation,” she said, falling into a chair. Lifting the secret made her feel both exposed and relieved—the hidden sorrow had been a support that, once confessed, freed her to seek help.The duchess, anxious to act, summoned the Abbe Brossette, a cautious, austere vicar of about fifty, known for his gravity and faith. When he arrived, the duchess, with measured gravity, asked if she might undertake a “rather wicked intrigue” for a great good and whether it would hinder her salvation. Explaining that Calyste had left Sabine for Madame de Rochefide, she confessed she had thought of launching a small man—self-willed and odious—upon Madame de Rochefide to induce her to dismiss Calyste. The abbe, smiling, advised that while the duty of a Christian woman is to withdraw a sinner from evil rather than push her along it, for a woman so far advanced as Madame de Rochefide, only a thunderbolt might save her; from the world’s point of view the method would be decisive.The duchess proposed other plans—perhaps provoking a quarrel so that a duel would force the matter—but the abbe steered her toward a less odious scheme. Rather than driving away one nail with another, she might persuade Monsieur de Rochefide to take back his wife, thereby restoring Calyste to Sabine and perhaps saving a soul. The duchess had even resolved to spend money and had vowed a charitable donation if she succeeded. At first Monsieur de Grandlieu had laughed at her; the abbe’s prudence and spiritual nuance steadied the conversation. He suggested, more morally, giving Madame de Rochefide a husband instead of adding a fresh scandal to her life. That correction pleased the duchess; she thanked him fervently.The abbe warned of a risk: what if, after reconciliation, Madame de Rochefide kept Calyste? The duchess admitted that when one does not commonly intrigue, one may do it badly; she would employ professional intriguers. The abbe, seeing the aim—saving a daughter—reluctantly approved that she “save her child,” and promised to advise if conscience troubled her. Delighted, the duchess accompanied him to the salon door, her resolve firm to use whatever means necessary to restore her daughter’s home.Thus the scene ends with the duchess poised to embark on an intrigue—tempered by the abbe’s cautious endorsement—to recover Calyste, free Sabine from humiliation, and preserve the family, even if the means required moral compromises and the aid of those accustomed to such artifices.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A storm was gathering over Monsieur de Rochefide, a Parisian who enjoyed what many desired: being to Madame Schontz almost as much a husband as he had been to Béatrix. The contrast between Rochefide’s contentment and the ruin that often befalls deserted women illustrates how laws and morals favor men; what destroys a woman can bring a man comfort. This disparity might encourage some women, like Sabine du Guenic, to remain and practice chosen virtues rather than flee.After Béatrix left him, Arthur de Rochefide, now sole heir, inherited the hôtel de Rochefide and two hundred thousand francs a year from his father; combined with his marriage fortune, this amounted to roughly a thousand francs a day. For the sort of man Mademoiselle des Touches described, such wealth was nearly sufficient happiness. While his wife performed maternal duties, Rochefide enjoyed his fortune without extravagance or intellectual ambition. Vanity, handsome looks, performed wit on stage and in journals, and a jocular military air from service in the Royal Guard made him appear amusing to uncritical women. He was essentially an imitator—adopting fashions, being accused only of long toilettes and wearing a corset—pleasing by reflecting others’ ideas and never aging in public esteem.As a husband he was pitied; people blamed Béatrix for abandoning such a good fellow and mocked her. A member of every club and subscriber to every patriotic or partisan absurdity, this loyal, brave, very silly nobleman resembled many rich men. Seeking distinction, he cultivated a fashionable mania: he fancied himself sultan of a four-footed harem managed by an old English groom at great cost, his specialty being horse racing. He supported a hippic magazine while knowing little of horses, relying entirely on his groom. Thus this semi-bachelor had nothing truly his own—no original mind, taste, position, or eccentricity; his fortune itself came from his fathers. After marital displeasure he was so pleased to be again a bachelor that he joked he had been born with a caul.Living without the obligations marriage demanded of husbands, Rochefide scarcely inhabited his house, rarely dined there, and seldom slept: the reason was his new liaison. Bored with fashionable women and after various adventures, Rochefide had, in effect, taken as his mistress a certain Madame Schontz—celebrated in the circulating circles of Fanny Beaupré, Susanne du Val-Noble, Florine, Mariette, and Jenny Cadine. To portray that world faithfully, one must note the varied endings of such women—poverty, early or self-inflicted death, lucky marriages, or opulence.Madame Schontz, first called La Petite-Aurélie, belongs to a high class of women whose social utility is undeniable: without such Aspasias on Montmartre and similar quarters, far fewer houses would be built in Paris. These women pick speculative spots on architectural steppes where risk and reward hinge on exact geography. When Rochefide first saw Madame Schontz, she lived on the third floor of the last house in rue de Berlin—on the border between misery and comfort. Her real name was not Schontz or Aurélie; she concealed her father’s name, as many girls of such origin do. Madame Schontz had the Saint-Denis education—well-formed but, once the school’s protector (the Emperor) vanished, offering little real opening.Aurélie (Josephine Schiltz) was the daughter of Colonel Schiltz, an Alsatian who nearly saved the Emperor in 1814 and died ruined. Napoleon placed little Josephine at Saint-Denis; left without resources after her parents’ deaths, she stayed on as an under-mistress until 1827. Her patience failed; beauty and the example of other resourceful comrades tempted her toward a courtesan’s adventurous life. She adopted Aurélie as a name and St. Aurélie as patron saint.Lively, witty, and well-educated, she committed more faults than her duller companions; she knew various writers (clever but insolvent), tried rich but foolish men, and sacrificed solid prospects to one true love. One day of extreme misery—dancing at Valentino’s in borrowed garments—she attracted Rochefide, who had come to see the galop. Her cleverness captivated a man uncommitted to passion. Two years after Béatrix’s desertion, Rochefide, unblamed for consorting “in the thirteenth arrondissement,” effectively remarried.Their happiness unfolded in four phases, a typical pattern for such liaisons: initial caution and miserly provision, followed by display and social elevation, then deeper integration into household and finances, and finally habit and consolidation of power by the woman.At first Rochefide feared being “carotted” (duped) and behaved niggardly. He allowed Madame Schontz five hundred francs a month, furnished shabby rooms in rue Coquenard costing 1,200 francs a year, and studied Aurélie’s character while she, perceiving his intent, gave him a character to study. He was pleased to find in her a woman of noble bearing: she spoke English, German, and Italian, knew foreign literatures, held her own at the piano, had dabbled in painting to surprising effect, and had absorbed scientific amusements at Saint-Denis. For a while she allowed Arthur to take credit for traits she had cultivated.By year’s end she began to show practical eagerness—arriving in draggled gowns at times when Rochefide waited—and by ten months the second phase began. She persuaded him to give her a fine apartment in rue Neuve-Saint-Georges, splendid furniture, a complete service of plate, 1,200 francs a month, a hired carriage, and the trappings of respectability. Unsentimental, she knew Rochefide’s calculations and used them. Sick of restaurant life, he offered Aurélie forty francs a day for dinners; she accepted. Having exhausted moral credit, she asked for 500 francs more monthly for dress—to avoid shaming her gros papa whose circle was Jockey Club men—and framed it as a way to increase Arthur’s social standing among his peers.She assumed the role of competent housekeeper and manager: balancing two thousand five hundred francs a month without debt, serving dinners superior to Nucingen’s, with wines at twelve francs a bottle. Rochefide, amazed, declared her “a treasure.” He bought a third of an Opera box and began to consult her, taking credit for her sayings and thus enhancing his reputation. She refused a Russian prince offering five thousand francs a month, professing to love Arthur as a wife loves an honest husband—an avowal that, when repeated to club acquaintances, increased public complacency toward Rochefide and public neglect of the hôtel de Rochefide. Shifting his whole existence to Madame Schontz, Arthur prospered; after three years he had four hundred thousand francs to invest.The third phase deepened their bond: Aurélie became loving to Arthur’s son, fetched him from school, and dowered him with presents and pocket money. She guided Arthur’s investments—urging purchases in the Funds when low and thus securing a large gain for him—yet claimed no reward. Rochefide invested six hundred thousand in Bank of France stock, placing half in Josephine Schiltz’s name; he hired Grindot to decorate a little house on rue de La Bruyère. Rochefide then ceased to manage his affairs; Madame Schontz collected revenues and paid bills. Acting as both wife and businesswoman, she gratified Arthur’s tastes as Madame de Pompadour gratified Louis XV.She patronized a few young artists and men of letters—bright, hungry types eager for reputations. Her conduct, tactical and superior, soon made her the most agreeable woman on that social borderland. A dozen young men supplied witty sayings and social currency without questioning the mistress’s constancy; they proclaimed her intelligent and thereby advertised her attraction. Rivals spread petty calumnies, but Aurélie out-blagued them. She also directed Arthur away from racing, arguing that buying horses reduced the turf to gambling and suggesting his money be given to poets instead; persuaded, he economized sixty thousand francs and cut the expense. She taunted would-be suitors—Finot and others—declaring Rochefide had made her “half a wife and a lady,” something an upstart could not do; this reinforced the bonds.The fourth phase—habit—completed her victory: she could say of a man, “I hold him!” Arthur, having bought a small hôtel in Mademoiselle Josephine Schiltz’s name (eighty thousand francs), merged his tastes, defects, and pleasures into Madame Schontz and became incapable or unwilling to change. In five years she consolidated such power that introductions to her required proposal; she refused dull or smirched rich people, admitting only select aristocratic names. Ostensibly she managed the three hundred thousand francs Rochefide had given her, invested by a broker, Gobenheim—the only broker admitted into her house. Secretly she controlled another hidden fortune of two hundred thousand francs, built from savings and the constant rotation of the known sum; she augmented this hoard by wearing and then selling jewels and diamonds. When called rich, she replied that three hundred thousand at the Funds produced only twelve thousand a year and that she had spent heavily in hard times—an answer that made her appear prudent and modest while concealing real independence.Thus Rochefide, once a dilettante imitator, ended up wholly dependent on a clever, tactful woman who combined the roles of lover, housekeeper, financier, and social director. Madame Schontz’s ascent typified the strategic, stagewise conquest common to women of her class: turn cautious advantage into display, display into management, and management into intractable habit. She secured social prestige, financial autonomy (both open and secret), a patronage network, and the unshakeable devotion—if not passion—of a man who had transferred his ego and vanity into her keeping.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Such conduct implied a plan, and Madame Schontz had one. Jealous for two years of Madame du Bruel, she was consumed with the ambition to be married by church and mayor. Every social position has its forbidden fruit—some small thing magnified into the weightiest concern. Her scheme required a second Arthur; Rochefide’s secret rival remained undiscovered. Bixiou fancied Leon de Lora the favorite; the painter suspected Bixiou, who, past forty, ought to be making a fate. Suspicions also fell on Victor de Vernisset, a Canalis-school poet desperately in love; he accused Stidmann, a young sculptor, of rivalry. Claude Vignon, the young Comte de la Palferine, Gobenheim, Vermanton—all frequented the salon and were suspected, then proved innocent. No one fathomed Madame Schontz, certainly not Rochefide, who thought she preferred the young, witty La Palferine; she was virtuous from self-interest and wholly bent on making a good marriage.Only one man of equivocal reputation was ever seen in Madame Schontz’s salon: Couture, a stock-gambler who had once scandalized his brothers. Couture had been one of Madame Schontz’s earliest friends, and she alone remained faithful to him. The false alarm of 1840 swept away the last vestige of this speculator’s credit; Aurélie, seeing his ill-luck, made Rochefide play in another direction. Glad to find a place at Aurélie’s table, Couture—who sometimes received a thousand-franc note from Finot, the cleverest or luckiest of parvenus—was shrewd enough to offer his hand and name to Madame Schontz. She studied whether this bold speculator had political power and sufficient gratitude not to desert his wife. Couture, about forty-three, half worn-out, did not redeem the unpleasant sonority of his name by birth; he said little of the authors of his days.Madame Schontz was bemoaning the scarcity of eligible men when Couture presented a provincial, supplied with the two handles by which women take hold of such pitchers when they wish to keep them. To sketch this person is to paint a portion of the youth of the day.In 1838, Fabien du Ronceret, son of a chief-justice of the Royal court at Caen (recently deceased), left Alençon, resigning his judgeship—an office his father had compelled him to hold—and came to Paris to make a noise. A Norman ambition, difficult to realize on an income scarcely scraping eight thousand francs a year; his mother still held a life-interest in a valuable estate in Alençon. In earlier visits he had tried his rope like an acrobat and had perceived the great vice of the social replastering of 1830. He meant to turn it to profit, following the example of the longest heads of the bourgeoisie. This requires a glance at one effect of the new order.Modern equality, unduly pushed, has produced in private life—parallel to politics—three great divisions of the social I: pride, conceit, and vanity. Fools wish to pass for wits; wits want to be thought men of talent; men of talent want to be treated as genius. By pulling down recognized superiorities, a nation opens the sluice through which a torrent of secondary ambitions rushes, each meaner than the last. Democrats say there was an evil in the aristocracy, but they substitute a dozen armed, contending aristocracies—the worst of all. Proclaiming equality, they promulgate a declaration of the rights of Envy. We inherit the Revolution’s saturnalia transferred to mind, industry, and politics; reputations won by toil, service, and talent are treated like privileges taken from the masses. Distinction is sought at any price—by ridicule, affectation, and petty public causes—creating fictitious dignities and a profusion of presidents, vice-presidents, and secretaries for societies whose numbers exceed the questions they pretend to solve. Society on a grand scale has been demolished to make a million little ones in the image of the defunct. These parasitic organizations reveal decomposition: are they not maggots in a dead body? All these societies are daughters of one mother, Vanity. Catholic charity or true beneficence proceed differently; they study and cure wounds rather than perorate in public meetings for the pleasure of perorating.Fabien du Ronceret, without being superior, had divined—by the greedy common-sense peculiar to a Norman—the gain he could derive from this public vice. Every epoch has a character clever men exploit. Fabien’s mind, though not clever, was wholly bent on making himself talked about.“My dear fellow, a man must make himself talked about, if he wants to be anything,” he said on parting from the king of Alençon, a certain du Bousquier, a friend of his father. “In six months I shall be better known than you are!”So Fabien obeyed the spirit of his age. He made his debut in Bohemia, where he was known as “The Heir” because of premeditated prodigalities. Du Ronceret profited by Couture’s follies for the pretty Madame Cadine: during an ephemeral opulence he had arranged a delightful ground-floor apartment with garden in the rue Blanche. The Norman, who wanted luxury ready-made, bought Couture’s furniture and the improvements he was forced to leave—kiosk, rustic gallery, India mattings, potteries—though he called the result his den and never acknowledged the architect Grindot, the carvings of Stidmann, or the paintings of Leon de Lora—Fabien’s crowning vanity lied for the sake of magnifying himself.He crowned the magnificence with a greenhouse built along a southern wall—not through any love of flowers but to attack public notice through horticulture. He had nearly reached his end. Elected vice-president of a floral society presided over by the Duc de Vissembourg, he wore the Legion of Honor ribbon at an exhibition, delivered an opening speech bought from Lousteau for five hundred francs and boldly pronounced it his own, and made a stir by presenting a flower given him by old Blondet of Alençon as the product of his greenhouse.But this was nothing. The Heir, who wanted to be accepted as a wit, planned to consort with clever celebrities and reflect their fame—hard to do on eight thousand francs a year. He repeatedly asked Bixiou, Stidmann, and Leon de Lora to present him to Madame Schontz and allow him into her menagerie of lions. Failing these, he applied to Couture, for whose dinners he had so often paid that the late speculator felt obliged to recommend him to Madame Schontz, if only as an elegant unpaid footman whom mistresses employ to run errands when servants are lacking.In three evenings Madame Schontz read Fabien like a book and said to herself, “If Couture does not suit me, I am certain of saddling that one. My future can go on two legs now.” This queer fellow whom everybody laughed at was really the chosen one—chosen, however, with an intention that made such preference insulting. The improbability of the choice kept suspicion away. Madame Schontz intoxicated Fabien with secret smiles and little scenes on the threshold when she bade him good-night, if Rochefide stayed. She often made Fabien a third with Arthur in her opera-box, excusing it by saying he had done her some service. Men have a natural conceit common as to women—the belief of being loved exclusively. Of all flattering passions none is more prized than that of a Madame Schontz: the man she makes the object of a love she calls “from the heart,” as distinct from another sort. A woman like her, who plays the great lady and whose intrinsic value is real, would be an object of pride to Fabien, who fell in love so that he never presented himself before her eyes except in full dress, varnished boots, lemon-kid gloves, embroidered shirt and frill, variegated waistcoat—in short, with all the external symptoms of profound worship.A month before the duchess’s conference with her confessor, Madame Schontz confided the secret of her birth and real name to Fabien, who did not at all understand the motive. A fortnight later she was surprised at his want of intelligence and exclaimed to herself, “Heavens! how stupid I am! he expects me to love him for himself.” Accordingly she took the Heir in her calèche to the Bois—she now had two little carriages—to discuss her future and declared she wished to marry.“I have seven hundred thousand francs,” she said, “and if I could find a man full of ambition who understands my character, I would change my position; for do you know the dream of my life? To become a true bourgeoise, enter an honorable family, and make my husband and children truly happy.”The Norman wished to be “distinguished” by Madame Schontz, but marrying her seemed a folly to a bachelor of thirty-eight whom the revolution of July had made a judge. Seeing his hesitation, Madame Schontz made him the butt of her wit and disdain and turned to Couture. Within a week Couture, whom she put upon the scent of her fortune, had offered his hand, heart, and future—three things of about the same value.The manoeuvres of Madame Schontz had reached this stage when the Duchesse de Grandlieu began inquiries into the life of the Béatrix of the Place Saint-Georges.Following the Abbe Brossette’s advice, the duchess asked the Marquis d’Ajuda to bring the celebrated Comte Maxime de Trailles—the archduke of Bohemia, political cut-throat and youngest of young men though fully fifty. D’Ajuda dined with Maxime at the club in the rue de Beuane and proposed afterward to go play dummy whist with the Duc de Grandlieu, who was feigning gout.Maxime did not deceive himself about the invitation’s meaning; he felt certain the duke or duchess needed him. Club life—men playing cards with others they do not receive at home—is a telling sign of the age.After several games the duke went to bed, leaving his wife tête-à-tête with Maxime and d’Ajuda. The duchess, seconded by the marquis, revealed her project and asked Maxime’s assistance while ostensibly seeking only his advice. Maxime listened without committing, waiting until she asked point-blank.“Madame, I fully understand you,” he said, casting that shrewd, penetrating look by which great scamps compromise their interlocutors. “D’Ajuda will tell you that if any one in Paris can conduct that difficult negotiation it is I—of course without mixing you up in it; without it being even known that I have come here this evening. Only, before anything is done, we must settle preliminaries. How much are you willing to sacrifice?”“All that is necessary.”“Very well, then, Madame la duchesse. As the price of my efforts you must do me the honor to receive in your house and seriously protect Madame la Comtesse de Trailles.”“What! are you married?” cried d’Ajuda.“I shall be married within a fortnight to the heiress of a rich but extremely bourgeois family—a sacrifice to opinion! I imbibe the very spirit of my government and start upon a new career. Consequently, Madame la duchesse will understand how important it is to have my wife adopted by her and her family. I am certain of being made deputy by my father-in-law’s resignation, and I am promised a diplomatic post in keeping with my new fortune. I do not see why my wife should not be as well received as Madame de Portenduere in that society of young women. My wife is pretty, and I will undertake to uncotton-night-cap her. Will this suit you, Madame la duchesse? You are religious, and if you say yes, your promise, which I know to be sacred, will greatly aid my change of life. It will be one more good action to your account. Alas! I have long been the king of mauvais sujets, and I want to make an end of it. After all, we bear, azure, a wyvern or, darting fire... and we have been counts since Catherine de’ Medici.”“I will receive and protect your wife,” said the duchess solemnly, “and my family will not turn its back upon her; I give you my word.”“Ah! Madame la duchesse,” cried Maxime, visibly touched, “if Monsieur le duc would also deign to treat me with some kindness, I promise you to make your plan succeed without its costing you very much. But,” he paused, “you must take upon yourself to follow my instructions. This is the last intrigue of my bachelor life; it must be all the better managed because it concerns a good action.”“Follow your instructions!” the duchess replied. “Then I must appear in all this.”“Ah! madame, I will not compromise you,” Maxime protested. “I esteem you too much to demand guarantees. I merely mean that you must follow my advice. For example, it will be necessary that du Guenic be taken away by his wife for at least two years; she must show him Switzerland, Italy, Germany—in short, all possible countries.”“You confirm a fear of my director,” said the duchess naively, recalling the Abbe Brossette’s prudence.Maxime and d’Ajuda smiled at the idea of an agreement between heaven and hell.“To prevent Madame de Rochefide from ever seeing Calyste again,” the duchess continued, “we will all travel—Juste and his wife, Calyste, Sabine, and I. I will leave Clotilde with her father—”“It is too soon to sing victory, madame,” said Maxime. “I foresee enormous difficulties; though I shall no doubt vanquish them. Your esteem and protection are rewards which would make me commit the vilest actions, but these will be—”“The vilest actions!” cried the duchess, whose piety disclosed selfishness.“And you would share them, madame, inasmuch as I am only your agent. But are you ignorant of the degree of blindness to which Madame de Rochefide has brought your son-in-law? I know it from Canalis and Nathan, between whom she was hesitating when Calyste threw himself into the lioness’s jaws. Béatrix has contrived to persuade that serious Breton that she has never loved any one but him; that she is virtuous; that Conti was merely a sentimental head-love—in short, a musical love. As for Rochefide, that was duty. So, you understand, she is virgin!—a fact she proves by forgetting her son, whom for more than a year she has not tried to see. The little count will soon be twelve, and he finds in Madame Schontz a mother who is all the more a mother because maternity is, as you know, a passion with women of that sort. Du Guenic would let himself be cut in pieces and would chop up his wife for Béatrix; and you think it easy to drag a man from such credulity! Ah! madame, Shakespeare’s Iago would lose all his handkerchiefs. People mistake theatrical passion for true love. Love, Madame la duchesse, is not loving a noble woman; it is to say to oneself: ‘She whom I love is infamous; she deceives me; she is abandoned; she is hellish,’ yet we rush to her and find there the blue of heaven. That is how Molière loved, and how we, scamps that we are, love. As for me, I weep at the great scene of Arnolphe. Now, that is how your son-in-law loves Béatrix. I shall have trouble separating Rochefide from Madame Schontz; but Madame Schontz will no doubt lend herself to the plot; I shall study her interior. But as for Calyste and Béatrix, they will need blows of an axe, far deeper treachery, and so base an infamy that your virtuous imagination could never descend to it—unless indeed your director gave you a hand. You have asked the impossible; you shall be obeyed. But in spite of my settled intention to war with fire and sword, I cannot absolutely promise you success. I have known lovers who did not recoil before the most awful disillusions. You are too virtuous to know the full power of women who are not virtuous.”“Do not enter upon those infamous actions until I consult the Abbe Brossette to know how far I may be your accomplice,” cried the duchess, with a naiveté that revealed the selfishness in her piety.“You shall be ignorant of everything, my dear mother,” interposed d’Ajuda.On the portico, as the marquis’s carriage drew up, d’Ajuda said to Maxime: “You frightened that good duchess.”“But she has no idea of the difficulty of what she asks. Let us go to the Jockey Club; Rochefide must invite me to dine with Madame Schontz tomorrow, for to-night my plan will be made, and I shall have chosen the pawns on my chessboard to carry it out. In the days of her splendor Béatrix refused to receive me; I intend to pay off that score, and I will avenge your sister-in-law so cruelly that perhaps she will find herself too well revenged.”The next day Rochefide told Madame Schontz that Maxime de Trailles was coming to dinner. That meant notifying her to display all her luxury and prepare the choicest food for this connoisseur emeritus, whom all women of Madame Schontz’s type revered. Madame Schontz considered her toilette and her house with the same care she would give to presenting to such a personage.In Paris there are as many royalties as varieties of art, mental and moral specialties, sciences, and professions; the strongest and most capable of their practitioners has a majesty all his own and is respected by peers who know their art’s difficulties. Maxime was, in the eyes of rats and courtesans, an extremely powerful and capable man who had known how to make himself excessively loved. He was also admired by men who appreciate the difficulty of living in Paris on good terms with creditors; in short, he had no rival in elegance, deportment, and wit except the illustrious de Marsay, who often employed him on political missions. All this explains his interview with the duchess, his prestige with Madame Schontz, and the authority of his words in a conference he planned to hold on the boulevard des Italiens with a young man already known, though newly arrived, in the Bohemia of Paris.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Maxime de Trailles summoned Finot and arranged a Café Anglais breakfast with Couture and Lousteau to learn about Madame Schontz. Finot, deferential to Maxime, complied; at breakfast Couture, prompted by Finot and unwittingly by Lousteau, revealed what Maxime wished to know.Around one o’clock Maxime loitered on Tortoni’s portico with du Tillet, ostensibly waiting for the Comte de la Palferine. When the prince of Bohemia arrived, Maxime drew him aside and asked if La Palferine had debts—fifty or a hundred thousand? The count admitted only six thousand; Maxime shrugged that small sums can hamper as much as great ones, praised the young man’s spirit, and offered help: twenty thousand francs and a scheme to make a woman love him within ten days. La Palferine, flattered but feeling inferior, accepted. Maxime instructed him to meet that evening at Madame Schontz’s and to report nightly at one a.m. on the boulevard de la Madeleine.Maxime intended to exploit Madame d’Espard’s dislike of Beatrix; he called at the Hotel de Grandlieu and found the duchess, Madame du Guenic, and Clotilde upset: Calyste had not returned, and Beatrix was distraught. Maxime urged secrecy and proposed a plot requiring twenty thousand francs and perhaps offices—hinting at a receiver-generalship as inducement.Madame Schontz had invited a circle of talented or ambitious men—Claude Vignon, Couture, Fabien du Ronceret (the Heir), Bixiou, Leon de Lora, La Palferine, Nathan—nine guests whose aim was to be noticed. Madame Schontz, anxious to seem young and beautiful, arranged her dress and manner to charm. Maxime arrived, praised Rochefide’s good fortune through her, and bantered about art versus lourettes; Aurelie insisted she saw herself as an artist and a progenitrix of honest men.Conversation ranged over literary opinions and social gossip. Privately, Maxime handed La Palferine the twenty thousand-franc notes, told him not to be stingy, and directed Nathan to present him to Madame la Marquise de Rochefide two nights hence. Maxime urged a display of wit and a slightly dissolute air; La Palferine pledged to report nightly.Fabien du Ronceret, the Heir, attended in affectation; Maxime scorned him but learned of his ties with d’Esgrignon. The dinner was lavish. After the meal Maxime confided to Couture a scheme: if Couture wanted steadiness, Madame de Rochefide could secure a receiver-generalship for him—Aurelie’s fortune would make it possible—but Maxime wanted Couture’s vote in return. Couture promised allegiance. Maxime then drove La Palferine home.That night Madame Schontz and Fabien flirted; when Arthur Rochefide accidentally surprised them, Aurelie feigned wounded virtue and threatened marriage to Fabien, humiliating Arthur. Maxime advised Aurelie how to force the issue—rouse jealousy, make Arthur feel unloved, demand a dramatic parting—promising Fabien a chief-justiceship and the Legion of Honor soon. Arthur woke to Aurelie’s icy sarcasm; she accused him of schemes to reconcile with Madame de Rochefide. He protested. She threatened to leave him and refused his offers, preferring to cultivate Fabien.Rochefide, in despair, sought Maxime’s counsel at the club. Maxime suggested Arthur show less obvious devotion and perhaps turn toward another woman, Madame Antonia, to recover self-respect and win his wife back. Though Arthur wished to remain faithful, Maxime urged practicality. Rochefide hurried home but found Aurelie had contrived a compromising scene: Fabien’s hat had been left, and the maid’s clumsy removal suggested intimacy. Arthur, devastated, submitted.Within a week La Palferine transformed: he paid debts, adopted a carriage, joined clubs, and published a successful novelette. Nathan, who promoted him to Madame de Rochefide, told Beatrix that La Palferine adored her. Ten days after Maxime’s arrangement, the count made his debut at Beatrix’s house while Calyste was at a party. Nathan primed her curiosity with artful reticence and lauded La Palferine’s prowess, implying his skill as a lover, which heightened Beatrix’s interest. The contrary attraction Maxime exploited—where courtesans sometimes yearn for virtue and virtuous women secretly desire libertinism—worked on Beatrix; she began to doubt whether her upbringing had made her a dupe.The next day Calyste seemed to Beatrix a loyal but unimaginative gentleman; after the dazzling soirée his plainness failed to satisfy her appetite for brilliance. She feared a meeting between Calyste and La Palferine; Maxime’s maneuver soon forced a public humiliation. At the opera, where Beatrix sat in an obscure box, she exited and was recognized on the crowded staircase; whispers and stares left her isolated. La Palferine, graciously offering his arm, escorted her, sat with her in her carriage, and rode home beside her, to Calyste’s stupefaction. In the salon the count conversed brilliantly, ignoring Calyste, who seethed. La Palferine left on a witty note, ensuring the lovers’ quarrel.Maxime, watching from the Jockey Club, checked on Aurelie’s progress. Madame Schontz, pleased with her strategy, had managed to eject Arthur: she led Fabien to believe she would reform and marry him, and Fabien, intoxicated and ambitious, embraced the project. Maxime promised Fabien the offices he’d been promised. Two days later Charles-Edouard told Maxime, “It is done.” Aurelie had turned Arthur out; Maxime consoled the marquis and suggested reconciliation could be bought with three hundred thousand francs and a new match arranged.Meanwhile the duchess learned Calyste planned to leave for Italy; Sabine wanted to follow and even bought pistols. Maxime counselled d’Ajuda to remain silent, promising that Madame de Rochefide would not leave Paris but would soon separate from Calyste. He arranged for his own marriage to be accepted publicly and urged that the duchess travel to Italy soon.Beatrix, now alternately consumed by passion and disdain, demanded that Calyste abandon Sabine to prove his devotion. Entrenched, she thought him unlikely to break with the Grandlieus because Sabine’s fortune mattered. Yet Calyste, driven by despair, obtained a passport and funds from his mother; nine days after La Palferine’s opening success he resolved to force a reconciliation. He arrived at Beatrix’s house intending to oust La Palferine and depart with her. Antoine, the old footman, sought to shield her by telling La Palferine she was out, but when La Palferine appeared Calyste watched him depart in a carriage; Beatrix, desperate, sent La Palferine a long letter of devoted confession—an unprecedented signed avowal.When La Palferine called next, Beatrix, ensnared and overjoyed, embraced him. He set a condition: she must be publicly reconciled with Rochefide, restore her social honor, return to the Hôtel de Rochefide and play the grand lady in public; she must achieve this by political means and persistence. He had already arranged for the reconciliation; she had instructed him to insist on it. Calyste, repeatedly refused by Antoine and by La Palferine’s household, left letters and begged to be heard. Eventually La Palferine and Maxime confronted him and, with cold civility, showed him the six unopened letters he had sent: La Palferine had no wish to cause scandal. He disclosed that Beatrix had taken him up in her carriage after the opera and had insisted on bringing back Rochefide. Rochefide had been living with Madame Schontz, who had left Paris, and had returned with Aurelie to Nogent-sur-Marne while the Hôtel de Rochefide would be restored for the marquise’s triumphant return. La Palferine advised Calyste to resume life with Sabine after three months and not to regret Beatrix: she was vain, incomplete both for vice and virtue, and would have abandoned him without remorse.Calyste left stunned but relieved to have his illusions pierced. Three days later the Duchesse de Grandlieu found Calyste content at home with Sabine planning the layette; Sabine whispered to the duchess, “I am loved, mother, and forever!”ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy (name — other appearances):- Ajuda-Pinto, Marquis Miguel d’ — Father Goriot; A Daughter of Eve; Honorine; Cousin Betty; The Unconscious Humorists############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE################################################ ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################If the French dislike emigration as much as the English love travel, each attitude has its reasons. England’s merits are everywhere, but France’s unique charm—the life of the intellect, the gift for conversation, that subtle half-speech of Paris—rarely exists elsewhere. A Frenchman whose raillery finds no echo abroad would wither; thus French travellers rejoice when they find a little Paris overseas: not exact cookery or the newest pleasantries, but wines, a critical atmosphere, and that urban wit which defines Paris.In 1836, when the Sardinian Court was at Genoa, two Parisians felt almost at home while seated in a palazzo on the last fold of the Apennines near San Tomaso. The villa, one of Genoa’s splendid villas above the city, offered after a storm a clear, still evening—sea and sky matching, the quay and groves silent, marble heads spouting water, stars and Mediterranean waves—that Boccaccian hour known to southern shores: a perfumed air, voluptuous ease in one’s chair, a sorbet spoon, a town at your feet and a fair woman opposite.Around a table sat the Marquis di Negro and the Marquis Damaso Pareto, two Frenchmen posing as Genoese, the Consul-General with his wife and two sleeping children, the French Ambassador and his wife, a secretary who fancied himself crushed and mischievous, and the two Parisians who had come to bid farewell to the Consul’s wife at a splendid dinner. The central figure was Camille Maupin—Mademoiselle des Touches—the celebrated woman of letters who dominated this little festival.The two Parisians were the landscape painter Leon de Lora and the critic Claude Vignon. Camille had been to Florence on business and had brought Leon to see Italy, extending the trip to the Campagna; returning by the Cornice road to Marseilles, she stopped at Genoa for Leon’s sake. The Consul-General, eager to honor a woman of her talent, invited her; she, wary of display, at first refused, finally consenting only to a farewell day at the Consul’s villa. Leon argued that her presence would repay his hosts, and she sacrificed one rare private day to the social world that watched her.Etiquette fell away; curiosity buzzed among the women, eager to see whether Camille’s manly talents undermined her feminine charm. Conversation, animated by Leon’s sallies and good taste, rarely lingered on literature, but inevitably turned to woman’s sin—when a woman falls, is she to blame or her seducer? The respectable women present—the Ambassadress, the Consul’s wife Onorina, and Camille—were unsparing toward the fallen woman. The men argued some virtues could survive a fall. Leon, impatient, cut through the debate; the Consul rose to put his children to bed and to send a little black pocket-book by Gina—an act revealing the couple’s mutual tenderness.“I will tell you a story in which I had a part,” the Consul-General said, “and after that we can discuss it. Begin by dissecting a corpse.” All prepared to listen. He then began his tale.He was thirty-four, married about six years, bearing Lord Byron’s living portrait—poetic in looks and temperament. His handsome face and amiable nature had won Onorina Pedrotti, the only child of a banker without male heirs. Though daughters rarely inherited in Genoa, Onorina was the exception. The Consul had seemed reluctant to marry, but after two years, and with the Ambassador’s encouragement, the marriage was arranged; the town interpreted his reserve as passionate melancholy. Onorina gave her love as consolation; Signor Pedrotti had no cause to complain. Parisian protectors watched the young diplomat; the Ambassador secured him a title and honors. When Count Pedrotti died in January 1831, the Casa Pedrotti’s fortune passed to the young couple; Onorina’s dowry had been a million francs.Onorina typified the Michelangelesque Genoese beauty: a full, well-placed bosom, long hair, dark complexion, dreamy eyes, and an embroidered white dress. Picture Michelangelo’s Night re-dressed for modern life and you have Onorina, with a handsome boy of six and a little girl of four—the childhood sculptors love. Camille, in secret study, observed this beautiful family. She noted the Consul’s occasional absent-mindedness and wondered why a man of his intellect and income—about a hundred thousand francs a year—remained in Genoa. Yet she saw, through small details, the husband’s faithful devotion; his calm masked consummate diplomatic composure.The tale shifted to the Consul’s youth. At twenty-two, in 1824, having taken his law degree, his uncle Abbe Loraux, seventy-two and Cure of the White Friars, sought a protector for him. One morning the Abbe said dress quickly: the young man was to be introduced to a man willing to take him as secretary—Comte Octave (the narrator uses only his Christian name out of discretion). The Count, a high legal authority close to the Dauphiness and newly a State Minister, offered salary and rooms but would not admit the secretary to his table, fearing servants would intrude; the position promised learning, society, and future advancement. The Abbe urged discretion.The narrator described himself as raised under a virtuous headmaster at the College Saint-Louis, reared in his uncle’s house, pious and modest, with a thirst for literature and theatre. Entering Comte Octave’s service promised independence: a hundred louis a year, society, and perhaps a protectress to keep him from youthful danger. The Abbe’s maternal care had kept him from excess.Comte Octave lived in a vast house like the Hotel Carnavalet, with a grand hall and a marble staircase. The narrator saw grandeur ill-tended: grass on the cobbles, blackened walls, a vast library opening into a great garden. Labranche, the footman, announced them. Comte Octave, dressed plainly, rose from his desk, greeted the Abbe warmly, and welcomed the young man, offering a room and explaining that the job would be hard but rewarding.At first the narrator felt small beside the Count, but soon perceived a remarkable personality. The Count’s exterior was austere, hermit-like, yet beneath lay depth: a harsh but refined face, a large mouth hinting at irony and kindness, a broad forehead, small turquoise eyes keen and expressionless like Talleyrand’s; hair streaked black and white, and white, womanly hands. Though the Count’s public rank and fortune were substantial, certain details of office were withheld out of discretion.Living with the Count, the narrator found not peaceful retirement but youth frozen under sorrow and suppressed passion. The Count’s face sometimes looked forty when he smiled for the Abbe, but otherwise seemed transfigured by some blow. He was pious—attending early mass for workmen and servants—but his piety was private, almost secretive. The narrator sensed subterranean caverns of sorrow; the Count avoided inquiry like a hunted man. He often absented himself at odd hours, leaving the young man to manage tasks.The Count loved flowers and kept rare plants, often buying them half-withered—perhaps reflecting his own faded state. He threw himself into public duties with almost frantic zeal, as if to outpace another passion. He was not a cynic; he was religious, rigorous, a silent man who admired and pitied without display. The narrator perceived harrowing internal battles and suspected a love or an unworthy mistress might be the cause. He noticed oddities: the Count once conversed eagerly with an old woman, fueling suspicion; he never invested savings; he sometimes walked the garden rubbing his hands raw and shifted in expression from hardness to velvety softness. At times he became childlike—tears, excitement, long solitary stares at a goldfish basin as if soothed by its mechanical movement.Over months they studied each other. The Count, whom the narrator later learned to be thirty-seven, carried an exterior of dutiful propriety concealing volcanic passion constrained by will. He performed his duties and charities; his life was relentless labor interrupted by moments when sorrow broke through. Affection grew between them: the narrator felt for the Count like a son; curiosity, tempered by respect, made him watch for the cause of the Count’s unhappiness.The house had been mismanaged; despite an income around 160,000 francs, the Count’s spending on servants left disorder. The narrator set about reform: dismissed useless servants, secured honest ones, improved food and management. In two years the Count enjoyed modern comforts—good horses, competent cookery, reasonable housekeeping at about 30,000 francs a year. The Count joked about servants becoming rich restaurant owners. Their relationship deepened; the Count treated the young man as a son, confided practical matters, and introduced him into high circles, often sending his carriage and servants in his name.Once, after the narrator had lost two thousand francs gambling and refused to touch his savings, he asked the Count for help. The Count simply gave him six thousand francs and said they would split things henceforth: since the young man represented him, his self-respect must be preserved. This generosity strengthened their mutual trust. Yet the Count never revealed his secret sorrow, and the narrator never pressed.What did the Count do in evenings? The narrator wondered: vices, jealous attachment, or infirmity? The Count’s odd talk with an old woman and his failure to invest savings suggested secret expenditures. Sometimes he would sit for hours staring at the goldfish, the senator’s mask dissolving into helplessness.Thus the Consul described Comte Octave’s second life: a great mind bowed by private catastrophe, fulfilling public duties with martyr-like fidelity while hidden passions consumed him. The narrator had become devoted as to a father and had reorganized the house, bringing order and comfort. Yet beneath domestic improvement the mystery remained: long disquiet, tears, sudden sullenness, then gracious humility. The Count’s melancholy and hidden struggles had become an absorbing problem, and the narrator’s affection was matched by a desire to know and to help.This is as far as the Consul had told his tale when the evening’s pause came at the Genoese villa table.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“On a certain Monday,” he resumed, “M. le Président de Grandville and M. de Serizy (then Vice‑President of the Council of State) met at Comte Octave’s house. They formed a committee of three; I was the secretary. The Count had already secured my appointment as Auditor to the Council of State. All documents for their inquiry into the political matter privately submitted to them lay on a long table in the library. They had trusted the Count to examine the papers preliminarily. To avoid carrying all the papers to M. de Serizy as president, they decided to meet first in the Rue Payenne. The Cabinet at the Tuileries attached great importance to this work; its chief burden fell on me—and to it I owed my appointment that year as Master of Appeals.“Though the Comtes de Grandville and de Serizy, whose habits resembled my patron’s, never dined away, we were still discussing the matter late when the servant told me, ‘MM. the Cures of Saint‑Paul and of the White Friars have been waiting in the drawing‑room for two hours.’It was nine o’clock.“‘Well, gentlemen, you find yourselves compelled to dine with priests,’ said Comte Octave. ‘I do not know whether Grandville can overcome his horror of a priest’s gown—’“‘It depends on the priest.’“‘One of them is my uncle, and the other is the Abbé Gaudron,’ said I. ‘Do not be alarmed; the Abbé Fontanon is no longer second priest at Saint‑Paul—’“‘Well, let us dine,’ replied the Président de Grandville. ‘A bigot frightens me, but there is no one so cheerful as a truly pious man.’We went into the drawing‑room. The dinner was delightful. Men of real information—politicians whose business gives consummate experience and practice of speech—are admirable storytellers. With them there is no medium: they are either heavy or sublime. In this sport Prince Metternich equals Charles Nodier. A statesman's fun, faceted like a diamond, is sharp, sparkling, and full of sense. Confident the proprieties would be observed, my uncle let his wit play—refined, gentle, penetrating, elegant, like men used to concealing thoughts under a black robe. There was nothing vulgar nor idle in this light talk, which I compare, for its effect, to Rossini’s music.The Abbé Gaudron was, as M. de Grandville said, more Saint Peter than Saint Paul: a peasant full of faith, square on his feet and tall; his ignorance of the world and literature enlivened conversation with guileless amazement and unexpected questions. They began discussing a plague‑spot of social life—adultery. My uncle noted the contradiction the legislators of the Code, still shaken by the Revolution, had set between civil and religious law, which he said was at the root of the mischief.“‘In the eyes of the Church,’ said he, ‘adultery is a crime; in those of your tribunals it is a misdemeanor. Adultery drives to the police court in a carriage instead of standing at the bar to be tried. Napoleon’s Council of State, too tender toward erring women, was inefficient.
