Should they not have harmonized civil and religious law, and sent the guilty wife to a convent, as of old?’“‘To a convent!’ said M. de Serizy. ‘They must first have created convents, and in those days monasteries were being turned into barracks. Besides, think of what you say, M. l’Abbé—give to God what society would have none of?’“‘Oh!’ said the Comte de Grandville, ‘you do not know France. They were obliged to leave the husband free to take proceedings: well, there are not ten cases of adultery brought up in a year.’“‘M. l’Abbé preaches for his own saint, for it was Jesus Christ who invented adultery,’ said Comte Octave. ‘In the East, the cradle of the human race, woman was merely a luxury, regarded as a chattel; no virtues were demanded of her but obedience and beauty. By exalting the soul above the body, the modern family in Europe—a daughter of Christ—invented indissoluble marriage, and made it a sacrament.’“‘Ah! the Church saw the difficulties,’ exclaimed M. de Grandville.“‘This institution has given rise to a new world,’ the Count went on with a smile. ‘But the practices of that world will never be those of a climate where women are marriageable at seven years of age, and more than old at five‑and‑twenty. The Catholic Church overlooked the needs of half the globe.—So let us discuss Europe only.“‘Is woman our superior or our inferior? That is the real question so far as we are concerned. If woman is our inferior, by placing her on so high a level as the Church does, fearful punishments for adultery were needful. And formerly that was what was done. The cloister or death sums up early legislation. But since then practice has modified the law, as is always the case. The throne became a hotbed for adultery, and its increase marks the decline of Catholic dogma. Today, where the Church demands only sincere repentance, society marks rather than executes. The law still condemns but no longer terrifies. Thus two moral standards exist: the world and the Code. Where the Code is weak, as I admit with our dear Abbé, the world is audacious and satirical; few judges would not have committed the faults they thunder against. The world, which contradicts law in its rejoicings, habits, and pleasures, is severer than Code or Church: it punishes a blunder after encouraging hypocrisy. The whole marriage law needs reconstruction. French law would be perfect if it excluded daughters from inheriting.’“‘We three among us know the question very thoroughly,’ said the Comte de Grandville with a laugh. ‘I have a wife I cannot live with. Serizy has a wife who will not live with him. As for you, Octave, yours ran away from you. So we three represent every case of the conjugal conscience, and, no doubt, if ever divorce is brought in again, we shall form the committee.’Octave’s fork dropped on his glass, breaking it and his plate. He turned as pale as death and flashed a thunderous glare at M. de Grandville, hinting at my presence, which I caught.“‘Forgive me, my dear fellow. I did not see Maurice,’ the Président went on. ‘Serizy and I, after being the witnesses to your marriage, became your accomplices; I did not think I was committing an indiscretion in the presence of these two venerable priests.’M. de Serizy changed the subject, listing all he had done to please his wife without success. He concluded that human sympathies cannot be regulated; social law is best when nearest natural law. Nature disregards affinities of souls; her aim is propagation. Hence the Code was wise to leave latitude to chance. Preventing daughters from inheriting while male heirs existed was an excellent provision—whether to hinder degeneration, or to make households happier by abolishing scandalous unions and favoring moral qualities and beauty.“‘But then,’ he exclaimed, lifting his hand with a gesture of disgust, ‘how are we to perfect legislation in a country which insists on bringing together seven or eight hundred legislators!—After all, if I am sacrificed,’ he added, ‘I have a child to succeed me.’“‘Setting aside all the religious question,’ my uncle said, ‘I would remark to your Excellency that Nature only owes us life, and that it is society that owes us happiness. Are you a father?’ my uncle asked.“‘And I—have I any children?’ said Comte Octave in a hollow voice, and his tone made such an impression that there was no more talk of wives or marriage.After coffee, the two Counts and two priests slipped away, seeing poor Octave had fallen into melancholy and did not notice. My patron sat in an armchair by the fire, like a man crushed.“‘You now know the secret of my life,’ he said when we were alone. ‘After three years of marriage, one evening I found a letter in which the Countess announced her flight. The letter had dignity; women preserve virtues even when committing that sin. The story now is that she went abroad on a ship that wrecked; she is supposed dead. I have lived alone seven years. Enough for this evening, Maurice. We will speak of my situation when I have grown used to speaking of it. Chronic disease needs time to accustom to improvement—which often seems another aspect of the complaint.’I went to bed agitated; the mystery seemed more obscure. I foresaw some strange drama, for there could be no vulgar difference between the woman the Count could select and his character. The events driving the Countess from so noble, amiable, perfect, loving a man must have been singular. M. de Grandville’s remark was like a torch thrown into caverns I had long walked; though the flame dimly lit them, I perceived their extent. I could imagine the Count’s sufferings without knowing their depths. His sallow face, parched temples, overwhelming studies, moments of absentmindedness, the smallest details of this married bachelor’s life stood out in luminous relief during the hour of mental questioning—the twilight before sleep—into which I gave myself.Oh, how I loved my poor master! He seemed sublime. I read a melancholy poem; I saw perpetual activity in a heart I had accused of torpor. Must not supreme grief at last stagnate? Had this judge, with so much power, ever revenged himself? Was he feeding on her long agony? Is it not remarkable in Paris to keep anger seething for ten years? What had Octave done since this misfortune—the separation of husband and wife is a great misfortune in our day, when domestic life has become a social question unlike before?We allowed days to pass on watch, for great sorrows have a diffidence of their own; but at last, one evening the Count said in a grave voice:“‘Stay.’############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This, as nearly as may be, is his story.“My father had a ward, rich and lovely, who was sixteen when I came back from college to live in this old house. Honorine, brought up by my mother, was just awakening to life. Full of grace and childish ways, she dreamed of happiness as one dreams of jewels; religion itself was poetry to her ingenuous heart. She looked to the future as a perpetual fête. Innocent and pure, shame and grief had never touched her. She felt weak and destined to obedience, and awaited marriage without desiring it. Her smiling imagination knew nothing of the corruption of the world. The dear child had suffered so little that she had not developed courage. We lived together like two brothers.“After a year, by the basin in the garden, throwing crumbs to the fish, I asked her, ‘Would you like us to be married? With me you could do whatever you please, while another man would make you unhappy.’“‘Mamma,’ she said to my mother, ‘Octave and I have agreed to be married—’“‘What! at seventeen?’ my mother protested. ‘No, you must wait eighteen months; if eighteen months hence you like each other, well, your birth and fortunes are equal; you can have a suitable marriage and a love match.’“When I was twenty-six and Honorine nineteen, we were married. Respect for my parents—old folks of the Bourbon Court—kept us from making the house fashionable; we lived as children. I went into society; I initiated my wife into fashion; I regarded it as my duty to instruct her.“I recognized afterwards that marriages under such conditions carry a rock against which many affections are wrecked. The husband becomes a pedagogue and love perishes beneath the rod that sooner or later gives pain; a young, handsome wife, discreet and laughter-loving, will not accept any superiority beyond her natural grace. During the difficult beginnings of a household I perhaps assumed a magisterial tone; or I trusted too entirely to that artless nature. I kept no watch over the Countess, in whom revolt seemed impossible. Alas! neither in politics nor domestic life has it been settled whether empires and happiness are wrecked by too much confidence or too much severity. Perhaps I failed to perceive Honorine’s girlish dreams? Who can tell while happy days last what precepts he has neglected?“I remember only the broad outlines of my self-reproaches, made in good faith; my indulgence struck me as worthy of Jesus rescuing the woman taken in adultery.“It was eighteen months after my father’s death—my mother followed in a few months—when the fearful night came which surprised me with Honorine’s farewell letter. What delusion had seduced my wife? Was it sensuality, misfortune’s magnetism, genius? I could not know. The blow was so terrible that for a month I remained stunned. Afterwards reflection counselled me to continue in ignorance; Honorine’s misfortunes have since taught me too much. So far, Maurice, the story is commonplace enough; but one word will change it all: I love Honorine; I have never ceased to worship her. From the day she left me I have lived on memory; one by one I recall the pleasures for which Honorine no doubt had no taste.“Oh!” he cried, seeing my amazement, “do not make a hero of me; do not think me such a fool as the Colonel would say. After frightful struggles I tried to forget; money in hand, I stood on the threshold of infidelity, but the memory of Honorine rose before me like a white statue. I recalled the infinite delicacy of that exquisite skin, the ingenuous face, the heavenly fragrance, the light in her eyes, the prettiness of her movements, and I fled as one would from violating a tomb. At consultations, in Court, by night, I dream incessantly of Honorine; only by excessive strength of mind do I attend to business. This is the secret of my labors.“I felt no more anger with her than a father feels seeing his child in danger. I understood I had made a poem of my wife, a poem I delighted in so intoxicatedly that I fancied she shared the intoxication. Ah! indiscriminating passion in a husband is a mistake that may lead to any crime in a wife. I had left all the faculties of this child, loved as a child, unemployed; I had perhaps wearied her with my love before the hour of loving had struck for her. Too young to see that in the constancy of the wife lies the germ of the mother’s devotion, she mistook this first test of marriage for life itself, and the refractory child cursed life, unknown to me, out of modesty perhaps. In that cruel position she was defenseless against any man who stirred her deeply. I, so wise a judge with my mind absorbed, understood too late these unwritten laws of a woman’s code: I read them by the light of the fire that wrecked my roof. Then I constituted my heart a tribunal; by the law the husband is judge: I acquitted my wife and condemned myself. Love seized me as a mean, despotic passion which comes over some old men. To this day I love the absent Honorine as a man of sixty loves a woman he must possess, and yet I have the strength of a young man. Society laughs at such a desperate conjugal predicament. Where it pities a lover, it ridicules a husband who cannot keep his wife under the canopy of the Church and the Maire’s scarf. I had to keep silence.“Serizy is happy. His indulgence allows him to see his wife; he can protect and defend her; as he adores her, he knows all the joys of a benefactor. ‘I remain married only for my wife’s sake,’ he told me.“But I—I have nothing; I have not even to face ridicule, I who live solely on a love which is starving! I who can never find a word to say to a woman of the world! I who loathe prostitution! I who am faithful under a spell! But for my religious faith, I should have killed myself. I have thrown myself into hard work as into a gulf, and come out alive, fevered, sleepless!“I cannot repeat all his words; passion gave him an eloquence above the pleader’s, and I, like him, felt my cheeks wet with tears. After a pause, he finished with this revelation:“This is the drama of my soul, but it is not the living drama acted in Paris. The interior drama interests nobody. I know it; one day you will admit it. No one can burden his heart with another’s pain; the measure of suffering is in ourselves. You can imagine me calming despair by contemplating a miniature in which I see and kiss her brow and lips, breathe the whiteness of her skin; or leaping with hope and then writhing under despair, trampling Paris to quell irritation by fatigue. My life is a continual paroxysm of fear, joy, and dejection.“As to the drama—it is this. You think I am occupied with Council of State, Chamber, Courts, politics. Seven hours at night suffice for all that; my faculties are over-wrought. Honorine is my real concern. To recover my wife is my only study: to guard her in her cage without her suspecting she is in my power; to satisfy her needs, supply the little pleasures she allows herself, to be about her like a sylph without letting her know, for if she knew the future would be lost—that is my life. For seven years I have never gone to bed without seeing the light of her night-lamp, or her shadow on the curtains.“She left my house taking nothing but the dress she wore that day. The child carried magnanimity to folly. Eighteen months after her flight her lover deserted her, appalled by the cold, cruel aspect of poverty—the coward! The man doubtless had counted on the easy life abroad. Honorine had sixty thousand francs a year of her own. The wretch left the dear creature expecting an infant and without a penny. In November 1820 I found means to get the best accoucheur to pose as a humble suburban apothecary. I induced the parish priest to supply her needs as charity. To hide my wife, secure her against discovery, find a devoted housekeeper to be my confidante—this was a task worthy of Figaro! You may suppose that to find where my wife had taken refuge I only had to make up my mind.“After three months of desperation rather than despair, I resolved to devote myself to Honorine with God only in my secret. Love must have its daily food. Ought I not to protect this child, whose guilt flowed from my imprudence, against fresh disaster—to fulfil my part as guardian angel? At seven months the infant died, happily for her and me. For nine months more my wife lay between life and death, deserted when she most needed a manly arm; but this arm,” he said, holding out his, “was extended over her head. Honorine was nursed as she would have been at home. When, on recovery, she asked by whom she had been assisted, she was told ‘By the Sisters of Charity, by the Maternity Society, by the parish priest.’“This woman, whose pride is almost a virtue, has shown resistance in misfortune, which on some evenings I call obstinacy. Honorine was bent on earning her living. For five years I have lodged her in the Rue Saint-Maur in a charming little house where she makes artificial flowers and articles of fashion. She believes she sells them to a shop, paid so well that she makes twenty francs a day; in six years she suspected nothing. She pays about a third of true cost, so on six thousand francs a year she lives as if she had fifteen thousand. She is devoted to flowers, and pays a gardener a hundred crowns, who costs me twelve hundred in wages, and sends me a bill for two thousand francs every three months. I have promised him a market-garden on land I hold in the name of a clerk. The smallest indiscretion would ruin him. Honorine has a little house, a garden, and a splendid hothouse for five hundred francs a year. There she lives under the name of her housekeeper, Madame Gobain, an old woman of discretion I found, whose affection Honorine has won. But their zeal is kept hot by the promise of reward. The porter and his wife cost me dreadfully dear for the same reasons. However, for three years Honorine has been happy, believing she owes her comfort to her own toil.“Yes,” he cried, reading the question in my eyes, “I have made the attempt. My wife had been in the Faubourg Saint-Antoine. One day, when I thought of reconciliation, I wrote a letter, re-written twenty times! I went from the Rue Payenne to the Rue de Reuilly like a condemned man. It was dark and foggy; I met Madame Gobain who was to tell me of my wife. Honorine, on recognizing my writing, had thrown the letter into the fire without reading it. What a dagger-stroke to a man who delights in the trickery by which he gets fine Lyons velvet cheaply, a pheasant, a fish, fruit, for a tenth of their value, for a woman ignorant enough to think two hundred and fifty francs ample wages for a cook fit for a bishop.“You have sometimes found me rubbing my hands in a kind of happiness. Well, I had just succeeded in some ruse worthy of the stage—I had sent her an Indian shawl by a purchaser as the property of an actress who had hardly worn it. My life can be summed up in two words: I love and I wait! I have in Madame Gobain a faithful spy on the heart I worship. I go every evening to chat with the old woman to hear all Honorine has done; a single exclamation might betray the secrets of that soul which is wilfully deaf and dumb. Honorine is pious; she attends church and prays, but she has never been to confession or taken Communion; she foresees what a priest would tell her. She will not listen to advice or an injunction to return to me. This horror of me overwhelms me; I have never done her the smallest harm. I have been kind. Granting I may have been hasty in teaching her, that my man’s irony may have hurt her girlish pride, is that reason for persisting in a determination only implacable hatred could inspire? Honorine has never told Madame Gobain who she is; she keeps absolute silence as to her marriage, so the worthy woman can never speak a word in my favor. The others know nothing; they live in awe of the Prefect of Police and their respect for a Minister’s power. Hence it is impossible for me to penetrate that heart; the citadel is mine, but I cannot get in. An act of violence would ruin me for ever.“How can I argue against reasons I do not know? Should I write a letter and have it copied and laid before Honorine? That would risk a third removal. The last cost me fifty thousand francs. The purchase was made in the name of the secretary whom you succeeded. The unhappy man, who did not know how lightly I sleep, was detected by me opening a box with the private agreement; I coughed, and he panicked; next day I compelled him to sell the house to the man in whose name it now stands, and I turned him out.“If it were not that I feel my noblest faculties satisfied—if my part were not that of divine fatherhood; if I did not drink in delight by every pore—there are moments when I should believe I was a monomaniac. Sometimes at night I hear the jingling bells of madness. I dread the violent transitions from faint hope to complete despair. A few days since I was considering the horrible end of Lovelace and Clarissa Harlowe, and saying to myself, if Honorine were the mother of a child of mine, must she not necessarily return under her husband’s roof?“I have such faith in a happy future that ten months ago I bought and paid for one of the handsomest houses in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré. If I win her back I will not allow her to see that house again nor the room from which she fled. I mean to place my idol in a new temple. That house is being made a marvel of taste. I have been told of a poet who, mad with love for an actress, bought the handsomest bed without knowing how she would reward him. Well, one of the coldest of lawyers was stirred to the depths by that anecdote. The orator of the Legislative Chamber can understand the poet who feeds his ideal on material possibilities. I love as a poet—as an emperor!”“As I heard the last words, I believed Count Octave’s fears were realized; he rose and walked, gesticulating, then stopped as if shocked by his own vehemence. ‘I am very ridiculous,’ he added after a long pause, looking at me as if craving pity.“‘No, monsieur, you are very unhappy.’“‘Ah yes!’ he went on. ‘From my speech you must believe in the intensity of a physical passion which for nine years has absorbed me; but that is nothing compared with the worship I feel for the soul, mind, and heart in that woman. By retrospection I now see the graces of Honorine’s mind and heart, which I paid little heed to while happy. Day by day I appreciate the extent of my loss, discovering the exquisite gifts of that capricious and refractory young creature who has grown so strong and proud under poverty and the shock of cowardly desertion. And that heavenly blossom is fading in solitude and hiding! The law we spoke of,’ he went on with bitter irony, ‘is a squad of gendarmes—my wife seized and dragged away by force! Would that be to triumph over a corpse? Religion has no hold on her; she craves its poetry and prays, but she does not listen to the Church’s commandments. I have exhausted mercy, kindness, love; I am at my wits’ end. Only one chance of victory is left—the cunning and patience with which bird-catchers at last entrap the wariest birds. Hence, Maurice, when M. de Grandville’s indiscretion betrayed to you the secret of my life, I regarded that incident as a decree of fate. Have you enough affection for me to show me romantic devotion?’“‘I see what you are coming to, Monsieur le Comte,’ said I; ‘I guess your purpose. Your first secretary tried to open your deed box. I know the heart of your second—he might fall in love with your wife. Can you devote him to destruction by sending him into the fire? Can any one put his hand into a brazier without burning it?’“‘You are a foolish boy,’ replied the Count. ‘I will send you well gloved. It is no secretary of mine that will be lodged in the Rue Saint-Maur in the little garden-house I have at his disposal. It is my distant cousin, Baron de l’Hostal, a lawyer high in office...’“After a moment the gate bell rang, and a carriage came in. The footman announced Madame de Courteville and her daughter. The Count’s cousin, the widow of a judge, had a daughter and no fortune. What could a woman of nine-and-twenty be in comparison with a young girl of twenty as lovely as an ideal mistress?“‘Baron, Master of Appeals till you get something better, and this old house settled on her—would you not have enough reasons for not falling in love with the Countess?’ he whispered, taking my hand and introducing me to Madame de Courteville and her daughter.“I was dazzled, not so much by these advantages as by Amélie de Courteville’s beauty and her costuming designed to secure a marriage.“But I will not talk of myself,” the Count resumed.“Three weeks later I went to live in the gardener’s cottage, cleaned, repaired, and furnished with the celerity explained by three words: Paris; French workmen; money! I was as much in love as the Count could desire as a security. Would prudence in a young man of five-and-twenty be equal to the part I undertook, involving a friend’s happiness? I confess I counted on my uncle’s advice, for I had been authorized by the Count to take him into confidence if I deemed it necessary. I engaged a garden; I devoted myself to horticulture; I worked frantically, turning up the soil, appropriating the ground to flowers. Like maniacs of Holland or England, I gave out I was devoted to one flower and especially grew dahlias. You will understand that my conduct, even in small details, was laid down by the Count, whose intellect was directed to the trifling incidents of the tragi-comedy in Rue Saint-Maur. As soon as the Countess had gone to bed, at about eleven, Octave, Madame Gobain, and I sat in council. I heard the old woman’s report: everything the Countess had done. He inquired into her meals, occupations, frame of mind, the flowers she aimed to imitate. I understood what love in despair may be when it is the threefold passion of heart, mind, and senses. Octave lived only for that hour.“During two months, while my garden work lasted, I never set eyes on the little house where my fair neighbor dwelt. I had not inquired whether I had a neighbor, though the Countess’ garden was divided from mine by a paling, along which she had planted cypress trees already four feet high. One morning Madame Gobain announced as disastrous news the intention, expressed by an eccentric neighbor, to build a wall between the gardens at the end of the year. I will say nothing of the curiosity which consumed me to see the Countess! The wish almost extinguished my budding love for Amélie. A wall would cut away three-quarters of the hatchet-shaped plot and leave Honorine’s garden a narrow alley.“The Countess was in despair.“‘My good Gobain,’ she said, ‘what sort of man is this florist?’“‘I do not know whether it is possible to tame him. He seems to have a horror of women. He is the nephew of a Paris curé. I have seen the uncle once; a fine old man of sixty, very ugly but amiable. It is possible the priest encourages his nephew’s love of flowers, as they say in the neighborhood, that nothing worse may happen—’“‘Well?’ asked the Countess.“‘He is a little cracked,’ said Gobain, tapping her head.“A harmless lunatic is the only man no woman distrusts in matters of sentiment. You will see how wise the Count had been in choosing this disguise for me.“‘What ails him then?’ asked the Countess.“‘He has studied too hard,’ replied Gobain; ‘he has turned misanthropic. If you want to know all that is said about him—’“‘Madmen frighten me less than sane folks; I will speak to him myself! Tell him I beg him to come here. If I do not succeed, I will send for the curé.’“The day after, as I walked my graveled path, I caught sight of the half-open curtains on the Countess’ first-floor window and a woman’s face peeping out. Gobain called me. I glanced at the Countess’ house and, by a rude shrug, expressed, ‘What do I care for your mistress?’“‘Madame,’ said Gobain, ‘the madman bid me leave him in peace, saying that even a charcoal seller is master in his own premises, especially when he has no wife.’“‘He is right,’ said the Countess.“‘Yes, but he ended by saying, “I will go,” when I told him he would distress a lady living in retirement who found her solace in growing flowers.’“The next day a signal from Gobain informed me that I was expected. After the Countess’ breakfast, when she walked before her house, I broke out some palings and went towards her. I had dressed as a countryman in old gray flannel trousers, heavy wooden shoes, shabby shooting coat, a peaked cap, a ragged bandana, hands soiled with mould, and a dibble in my hand.“‘Madame,’ said the housekeeper, ‘this good man is your neighbor.’“The Countess was not alarmed. I saw at last the woman whose conduct and her husband’s confidences had made me so curious. It was early May. The air was pure, the spring verdure and fragrance a fitting setting for this creature of sorrow. As I saw Honorine I understood Octave’s passion.“Her pallor struck me by its peculiar tone of white—for there are as many tones of white as of red. Her skin showed the blood in blue veins; at the slightest emotion a rosy flush came. The sunshine through the acacias shed on Honorine a pale gold like that Raphael and Titian enwrap their Virgins in. Her brown eyes expressed tenderness and vivacity; their brightness seemed reflected through long lashes. By lifting her eyelids she could spellbind; there was feeling, dignity, terror, or contempt in her way of raising or dropping those veils of the soul. She could freeze or give life by a look. Her light-brown hair, carelessly knotted, outlined a poet’s brow, high, powerful, dreamy. The mouth was voluptuous. All the lines and forms of the head had a stamp of nobleness defying time.“Though slight, Honorine was not thin; her figure might revive love when it believed itself exhausted. She perfectly represented the word mignonne—pliant like a kitten. Her small feet made a light inherent sound that harmonized with the rustle of her dress, producing a feminine music stamped on the heart. Her gait bore the quarterings of her race with pride, so even a working man would make way in the street. Gay and tender, haughty and imposing, she remained a child. But a child who might be as strong as an angel; once hurt, she would be implacable.“Coldness on that face must be death to those she had smiled on. Inhaling the perfume of violets about her, I understood how the memory of this wife arrested the Count on the threshold of debauchery, and how impossible to forget a creature who was a flower to the touch, the eye, and the soul. Honorine inspired chivalrous devotion irrespective of reward. A man seeing her must say to himself: ‘Think, and I will divine your thought; speak, and I will obey. If my life sacrificed in torments can procure you one day’s happiness, take my life; I will smile like a martyr.’“Many women study expression and succeed in producing similar effects; in her it was all outcome of delightful nature. If I tell you this, it is because her soul, thoughts, and heart’s exquisiteness are what we are concerned with, and you would blame me if I had not sketched them.“I was near forgetting my part as a half-crazy lout, clumsy and hardly chivalrous.“‘I am told, madame, that you are fond of flowers?’“‘I am an artificial flower-maker,’ she said. ‘After growing flowers I imitate them, like a mother painting her children. That is enough to say I am poor and unable to pay for the concession I desire from you.’“‘But how,’ said I, grave as a judge, ‘can a lady of such rank ply so humble a calling? Have you, like me, good reasons for employing your hands so as to keep your brain from working?’“‘Let us stick to the question of the wall,’ she said with a smile.“‘We have begun at the foundations,’ I replied. ‘Must I not know which of us ought to yield to the other in behalf of our suffering, or, if you choose, our mania? Oh! what a charming clump of narcissus!’“She had made a perfect museum of flowers and shrubs, arranged as an artist. The retired garden breathed comforting scents and suggested sweet thoughts and graceful pictures. It showed the sign-manual of our true character stamped on all when nothing compels hypocrisy. I looked at the narcissus and at the Countess, pretending to be more in love with the flowers than with her to keep up my part.“‘So you are fond of flowers?’ she asked.“‘They are the only beings that never disappoint our care and affection.’ I delivered a diatribe comparing botany and the world, ending miles from the dividing wall. The Countess naturally brought me back to the point; women not in love have the cold blood of an experienced attorney.“‘If you insist on my leaving the paling,’ I said, ‘you will learn all the secrets of gardening I want to hide; I am seeking a blue dahlia, a blue rose; I am crazy for blue flowers. Is not blue the favorite color of superior souls? We are neither of us at home; we might as well make a door of open railings to unite our gardens. You too are fond of flowers; you will see mine, I shall see yours. If you receive no visitors, I have none but my uncle, the Curé of the White Friars.’“‘No,’ she said, ‘I will give you the right to come into my garden at any hour. You will always be admitted as a neighbor with whom I hope to keep good terms. But I like my solitude too well to burden it with loss of independence.’“‘As you please,’ said I, and leaped the paling.“‘Now, of what use would a door be?’ I cried from my domain, mocking her with a madman’s grimace.“For a fortnight I seemed not to notice my neighbor. Towards the end of May, one lovely evening we were both walking opposite sides of the paling and exchanged civil words. Finding me in deep dejection she spoke to me of hope in short, nurse-like sentences. I leaped the fence and found myself at her side. The Countess led me into the house, wishing to subdue my sadness. So I penetrated the sanctuary where everything harmonized with the woman I had tried to describe.“Exquisite simplicity reigned. The interior was a dainty box of eighteenth-century art: the dining-room frescoed with garlands, staircase in monochrome, a faded drawing-room hung with tapestry panels, a bath, and upstairs a single bedroom with dressing-room and a library she used as a workroom. The kitchen was in the basement. The balcony’s garlands hid the roof; only lead cornices were visible. In this retreat one was a hundred leagues from Paris.“But for a bitter smile that occasionally played on those red lips, one might have believed the violet hidden in her thicket of flowers happy. In a few days we reached intimacy from close neighborhood and the Countess’ conviction I was indifferent to women. A look would have spoilt all, and I never allowed thought of her to appear in my eyes. Honorine regarded me as an old friend; her manner was pity. Her looks, voice, and words showed she was far from coquetry. She soon gave me the right to enter the pretty workshop where she made flowers, a retreat full of books and curiosities, as smart as a boudoir where elegance emphasized the vulgarity of her tools. She had poetized a manufacture.“Perhaps of all the work a woman can do, making artificial flowers lets her display the most grace. Coloring prints requires stooping; embroidery brings consumption or curvature; engraving music is laborious; sewing earns little. But making flowers and light articles needs a variety of movements and ideas that keep a pretty woman in her sphere; she may chat, laugh, sing, or think.“There was feeling for art in how she arranged the myriad-colored petals on her long table. The saucers of color were clean and orderly; a little cabinet held tiny moulds; a fine Japanese bowl the paste; wire lay in a drawer. She had a passion for achievement, attempting difficult racemes and corollas. Her hands were swift as her thoughts; her fingers seemed fairy-like in the twisting, fitting, and pressure required. She adapted each motion with the lucidity of instinct.“I could not tire of admiring her shaping a flower, padding the wire, adjusting leaves. She displayed the genius of a painter, copying faded flowers and yellowing leaves, struggling even with wildflowers, artless yet elaborate.“‘This art,’ she said, ‘is in its infancy. If Parisian women had some of the genius the harem slavery draws out in Oriental women, they would lend a complete language of flowers to their wreaths. To please my taste I have made drooping flowers with leaves of Florentine bronze for a young woman whose life is a failure—poetical fitness. How many things a woman might express by her headdress! Are there not flowers for drunken Bacchantes, flowers for stern bigots, pensive flowers for bored women? Botany may express every sensation and thought, even the most subtle.’“She employed me to stamp leaves, cut material, and prepare wires. My affected desire for occupation made me skilful. We talked as we worked; when idle I read new books to her, keeping up my part as a man weary of life, gloomy and misanthropic. My person led to banter as to my physical resemblance—save his club-foot—to Lord Byron. It was tacitly admitted that her troubles outweighed mine, though my causes for misanthropy might have satisfied Job.“I will not speak of the shame which tormented me as I inflicted false wounds on my heart to excite her compassion. I soon appreciated my devotedness by estimating the baseness of a spy. Her sympathy would have comforted any great grief. This charming creature, weaned from the world and alone for years, offered kindnesses with childlike effusiveness and such compassion as would fill with bitterness any profligate who fell in love with her; for alas, it was all charity and pity. Her renunciation of love and dread of what is called happiness, she proclaimed with equal vehemence. These days proved to me that a woman’s friendship is far superior to her love.“I suffered my revelations being dragged from me with grimaces as a young lady allows before sitting at the piano. The Countess strengthened our intimacy; she found in me a counterpart of her antipathy to love, and I fancied she was content with being a Man Friday on her desert island. Solitude perhaps weighed on her. There was nothing coquette; she told me she had no heart except in the ideal world. I compared these two lives: his, activity and agitation; hers, inaction and quiescence. My misanthropy let me utter cynical sallies against men and women, hoping to bring her to confidence; she was not to be caught, and I began to understand the mulish obstinacy commoner among women than assumed.“‘The Orientals are right,’ I said one evening, ‘when they shut you up and regard you as playthings. Europe has been punished for admitting you to society and accepting you on an equal footing. Woman is the most dishonorable and cowardly being, and that is where her charm lies. Where would be pleasure in hunting a tame thing? Once a woman inspires a man’s passion, she is for him forever sacred. Men’s gratitude for past delights is eternal, though the woman may forget.’“‘God has, no doubt, reserved such perfect bliss for Paradise,’ she said. ‘But if your argument is witty, it is false. What can those women be who give themselves up to a succession of loves?’ she asked, looking at me like the Virgin in Ingres’ picture.“‘You are an actress in good faith,’ I said. ‘Still, lovely as you are, you have loved; ergo, you forget.’“‘I!’ she cried, evading. ‘I am not a woman. I am a nun, and seventy-two years old!’“‘Then how can you assert you feel more keenly than I? Sorrow has one form for women; the only misfortunes they regard are disappointments of the heart.’“She looked at me sweetly and persisted in her wilfulness.“‘I am a nun,’ she said, ‘and you talk to me of a world where I shall never again set foot.’“‘Not even in thought?’ I asked.“‘Is the world so desirable?’ she replied. ‘Oh! my mind wanders higher. The angel of perfection, the beautiful Gabriel, often sings in my heart. If I were rich I should work to stop myself soaring on many-tinted wings. There are meditations which ruin us women. I owe much peace of mind to my flowers, though sometimes they fail. On some days an idea possesses me that some great event is impending and life will change; I stare into darkness, dislike my work, and after many fatigues find everyday life again. Is this a warning from heaven? I ask myself—’“After three months of this struggle between two diplomats—youthful melancholy and a woman whose disgust of life made her invulnerable—I told the Count it was impossible to drag this tortoise out of her shell; it must be broken. The evening before, in our last friendly discussion, the Countess had exclaimed: ‘Lucretia’s dagger wrote in letters of blood the watchword of woman’s charter: Liberty!’“From that moment the Count left me free to act.“‘I have been paid a hundred francs for the flowers and caps I made this week!’ Honorine exclaimed gleefully one Saturday evening when I visited her in the ground-floor sitting-room which the unavowed proprietor had had regilt.“It was ten o’clock. July twilight and a glorious moon lent a misty light. Gusts of perfume soothed the soul; the Countess clinked five gold pieces given to her by a suppositious dealer in frippery, another of Octave’s accomplices, M. Popinot.“‘I earn my living by amusing myself,’ she said; ‘I am free, when men armed with laws have tried to make us slaves. I have transports of pride every Saturday! I like M. Gaudissart’s gold pieces as much as Lord Byron liked Mr. Murray’s.’“‘This is not becoming in a woman,’ I said.“‘Pooh! Am I a woman? I am a boy with a soft soul; a boy whom no woman can torture—’“‘Your life is the negation of your being,’ said I. ‘What? you, on whom God lavished His choicest treasures, do you never wish—’“‘For what?’ she asked, disturbed by a speech that for the first time had contradicted my assumed part.“‘For a pretty little child, with curling hair, running among the flowers, calling you mother!’“A prolonged silence revealed the effect of my words. Leaning on her sofa, the Countess did not faint but froze under a nervous attack she afterwards compared to an insidious poison. I called Madame Gobain, who laid her mistress on the bed, unlaced and undressed her, restoring not life but consciousness of dreadful suffering. I walked the path behind the house weeping and doubting. I wished to give up this bird-catcher’s part I had rashly assumed. Madame Gobain came down and found me with my face wet with tears, then hurried up to say:“‘Monsieur Maurice is crying like a child.’“Roused by the evil interpretation our mutual behavior might attract, the Countess summoned strength to put on a wrapper and come down to me.“‘You are not the cause of this attack,’ she said. ‘I am subject to these spasms, a sort of cramp of the heart—’“‘And will you not tell me of your troubles?’ I asked, wiping my tears. ‘Have you not told me you were a mother and unhappy at losing your child?’“‘Marie!’ she called. Gobain came in.“‘Bring lights and some tea,’ she ordered, with the calm decision of a Mylady in armor of pride.“When the housekeeper had lighted tapers and closed shutters, the Countess showed a mute countenance; pride and gravity reasserted themselves. She said:“‘Do you know why I like Lord Byron so much? It is because he suffered as animals do. Of what use are complaints when they are not elegy, mockery like Don Juan, or reverie like Childe Harold? Nothing shall be known of me. My heart is a poem I lay before God.’“‘If I chose—’ I began.“‘If?’ she repeated.“‘I have no interest in anything,’ I replied, ‘so I cannot be inquisitive; but if I chose, I could know all your secrets by to-morrow.’“‘I defy you!’ she exclaimed with ill-disguised uneasiness.“‘Seriously?’“‘Certainly,’ she said, tossing her head. ‘If such a crime is possible I ought to know it.’“‘First, madame,’ I went on, pointing to her hands, ‘those pretty fingers show they were not made for toil. Then you call yourself Madame Gobain, yet you said to Marie: “Here, this is for you?” Marie is the real Madame Gobain; you conceal your name behind your housekeeper’s. Fear nothing from me. You have in me the most devoted friend you will ever have: a friend I give the sacred and pathetic meaning to—the friend who will defend you against everything, and only wishes you to be as happy as such a woman ought to be. Who can tell whether the pain I caused was not voluntary?’“‘Yes,’ she replied with threatening audacity, ‘I insist on it. Be curious and tell me all you can find out about me; but,’ and she held up her finger, ‘you must also tell by what means you obtain your information. The preservation of the small happiness I enjoy here depends on the steps you take.’“‘That means you will fly—’“‘On wings!’ she cried, ‘to the New World—’“‘Where you will be at the mercy of brutal passions you would inspire,’ I interrupted. ‘Is it not the essence of genius and beauty to shine and excite desires? Paris is a desert with Bedouins; it is the only place to hide among those who work for a livelihood. What have you to complain of? Who am I? An additional servant—M. Gobain, that is all. If you have to fight a duel you may need a second.’“‘Never mind; find out who I am. I have already said I insist. Now I beg that you will,’ she added with the grace all ladies can command.“‘Well then, to-morrow, at the same hour, I will tell you what I may have discovered,’ I replied. ‘But do not therefore hate me! Will you behave like other women?’“‘What do other women do?’“‘They lay upon us immense sacrifices, and when we have made them they reproach us later as if for an injury.’“‘They are right if the thing required appears to be a sacrifice!’ she replied pointedly.“‘Instead of sacrifices say efforts and—’“‘It would be an impertinence,’ she said.“‘Forgive me,’ I replied. ‘I forget that woman and the Pope are infallible.’“‘Good heavens!’ she said after a long pause, ‘only two words would be enough to destroy the peace so dearly bought, which I enjoy like a fraud—’“She rose. ‘Where can I go? What is to become of me? Must I leave this quiet retreat that I arranged to end my days in?’“‘To end your days!’ I exclaimed. ‘Has it never struck you that a time would come when you could no longer work, when competition will lower the price of flowers and articles of fashion—?’“‘I have already saved a thousand crowns,’ she said.“‘Heavens! what privations such a sum must represent!’ I cried.“‘Leave me,’ she said, ‘till to-morrow. This evening I am not myself; I must be alone. Must I not save my strength in case of disaster? If you should learn anything, others would be informed, and then—Good-night,’ she added shortly, dismissing me with an imperious gesture.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“The battle is tomorrow,” I repeated to myself as I walked toward Honorine’s house, forcing indifference. Octave and she were both tormented—one hoping, the other dreading reunion. I arrived at ten and was shown into a white-and-blue bedroom where the Countess sat stunned by my respectful, grave manner.I told her plainly that her seclusion was the Count’s doing: Lenormand was a screen, her money and protection came from Octave, who had contrived her disappearance and watched over her. The Countess quivered. Her proud independence had become a prison; she wept from helplessness.“I will go,” she cried. “He forces me; I will go where no one will follow.”I urged her to explain to me and my uncle, a priest who could advise and mediate. I promised help: if she proved that she could not return to Octave, I would secure her refuge; otherwise I would support reconciliation. She insisted no man could enter the “scruples of my conscience,” and confessed that another man had once possessed all her love. She had given him an intense passion and could not divide herself. Though she esteemed and respected Octave—he had done her no wrong, had been tender and protective—she could never love him. To accept his conjugal forgiveness would be prostitution of her soul. Marriage, she said, would only perpetuate shame; she could not endure seeing his eyes measure her crime forever.I argued that religion, law, and social duty all pointed to reconciliation, that Octave’s seven years of devotion entitled him a hearing, and that he might accept her back without insisting on immediate intimacy. I offered my uncle’s presence as a way to test Octave’s sincerity. She suggested a convent as refuge, but I warned Octave’s influence could bar that option. I promised not to use power to force her: she would have independence, solitude, the protection of law and the Count’s care, and I would never intrude on her without consent.After our meeting, I found Octave pacing in Rue Saint-Maur, despairing. I read Honorine’s long, anguished letter to him and kept a copy. In it she explained that conscience, religion, law, and society all told her to return, to spare her husband sorrow and to become a mother; but she could not love Octave. She feared she would live as a woman haunted by her guilt, a wife who could never forget the other man. Marriage without love would be a degradation: she would feel like a chattel offering pleasure without virtue, and both would be tortured by comparisons and suspicions. She preferred solitude and expiation, convinced that only God could remake her; human forgiveness could not restore the passion she had once known. Yet she begged to be shown a place of solitude and peace if she must stay away.Octave, reading her letter, cried “She is mine!” and asked to be left alone. I reflected on love’s aspects—how the faithful, tragic woman can seem more noble than a pure, complacent girl—and determined to take a role in their drama that would not betray her: if Octave won her back, she must never know I had been his secretary. I would accept exile abroad.We arranged a test. With my uncle’s mediation, Honorine would read Octave’s reply in our presence, out of respect to herself and to the laws she held sacred. My uncle, a calm, venerable priest, arrived at ten. The Countess, struck by his presence, felt comforted. He handed her Octave’s letter, which was an appeal of humility and devotion. He admitted errors, confessed he had misunderstood marriage, and begged her mercy. He did not demand a wife but offered instead to be a guardian and friend, to create a new home where she could be “unapproachable, alone, free” yet legitimately protected. He promised not to force confession by Church or law and to accept her return only by her consent. If she persisted in solitude, he would respect it. The letter was full of tenderness, restraint, and a wish to make her life peaceful without humiliating passions.Honorine folded the letter to her heart and, after testing me with a question about my sincerity, accepted Octave’s terms: she would remain where she was for the present, under his permission and protection. My involuntary cry of relief showed my genuine feeling; she kissed my hand, promising we should meet again. I lied that I was leaving the next day, to preserve the fiction she preferred; my uncle continued the charade with a knowing look.Six days later I left for a consular post in Spain, as arranged: a graceful exile to protect my friendship and the Count’s honor. After I settled, Octave wrote to me describing his renewed life of longing and prudence. He told how he waited outside her lodge, how he offered her money and security, and how she accepted his gifts but not his assurances of love. When he pressed her to go abroad and then return openly to his house, she recoiled and preferred the existing cautious arrangement. He tried to win her with patience and letters. At last she consented to be his wife—on terms she dictated. She declared she could resign herself to duty and even love his soul, but she could not give the passionate love he wanted. She would be as an Eastern slave of devotion, perhaps to die in atonement; but she asked that he not call obstinacy what was worship of an Ideal. Octave, though hopeful, was wounded by the limits of her consent.Two months later the papers announced Comtesse Octave’s return; soon the birth of a son was formally declared. I kept the news long in my hand, divided between joy and foreboding. Pressured by friends and my uncle’s death, I eventually married. The Count and Countess continued their life. I received, months later, the final, devastating letter of Honorine, written during her last illness.She told me she was dying, perhaps because she was a mother. She had played the part of a perfect wife and deceived her husband into happiness; she had nursed my uncle in his last illness and had followed his direction. Yet she was consumed by memory and by a malady of recollection that refused to let her renew love for Octave. She described marriage without love as degrading the soul daily; she could only weep and dream when alone. The constant watchfulness and public duties left her no refuge; she locked up her grief until it ate into her life. She had even asked doctors to say she died of some plausible organic complaint, to spare her husband the truth that she was dying of an undying memory. She begged me to be guardian to her son and to keep her codicil secret unless necessary. She asked me to prevent the child’s future wife from cherishing the “mysterious flower of the Ideal,” lest she remain above ordinary domesticity as Honorine had been. She confessed she had appeared happy amid her flowers but had been dead for years if Saint Bernard’s dictum—“where there is no more love there is no more life”—were true.She died. The Consul closed the letters and the assembled guests—artists, diplomats, and friends—considered the tragedy. Octave, despairing, had left France for Italy; he suspected the truth and was consumed by remorse. The guests debated culpability and the nature of love and marriage. Mademoiselle des Touches defended Honorine’s rare, exalted temperament; others noted the unfairness of men who marry young women while preserving in their hearts an angelic image that true human companions cannot match. The consensus was that the two natures—noble, but incompatible—had been unable to reconcile; their union, founded on duty rather than mutual passion, ended in loss.Thus ended Honorine’s tale: a painful study of conscience, love, social duty, and the tragedy that results when an irredeemable memory meets an anguished generosity. The story closes among familiar figures from Balzac’s wider world, reminding us that this singular drama belongs to a larger web of human fortunes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################THE MIDDLE PART OF THE STORY — MIDDLE OF THE STORY repeated emphatically again, framed by separators.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“HULLO! There is that old Box-coat again!”A lawyer’s clerk—Simonnin, a gutter-jumper of thirteen or fourteen—threw a crumb like a bullet through the open window. It hit the hat of a stranger crossing the Rue Vivienne courtyard outside Maître Derville’s rooms. Boucard, the head clerk, scolded the boy: “Come, Simonnin, don’t play tricks… however poor a client may be, he is still a man!”Simonnin belonged to Boucard’s extended household: errand-runner, billet-doux carrier, street kid by habit, pettifogger by destiny—ruthless, impudent, greedy, idle, and often sharing a miserable lodging with an old mother. He sneered at the stranger’s “box-coat” when Boucard called him “old Box-coat.”Around the long table Godeschal, improvising mock eloquence while dictating an appeal, turned the arrival into sport. He parodied high rhetoric—“But, in his noble and beneficent wisdom, his Majesty, Louis the Eighteenth…”—and proposed tricking the visitor by saying the master saw clients only between two and three in the morning. The copying clerks laughed; Boucard muttered dates and figures. Their camaraderie—gossip, diction, and copying appeals—mingled with work.The office was one of those Parisian law dens—large, ill-lit, with a stove-pipe slanting to a bricked-up fireplace and a marble chimney-piece littered with bread, triangles of Brie, pork cutlets, glasses, bottles, and Boucard’s chocolate. Food, an overheated stove, and old papers combined with mud and snow tracked in by the clerks. A rotating desk, piles of broutilles, yellow seizure notices, and rows of dust-encrusted bundles tied with red tape gave the place its peculiar physiognomy. It was a schoolroom for youths, a passage for clients, and a laboratory for the chief; scrupulously neglected yet carefully handed down with relics of older practice. For clients an attorney’s office was one of Paris’s most repulsive marts; inside, absorbed in law’s drama, the surroundings mattered less.A knock interrupted their talk. Five clerks, bright-eyed and hungry, called “Come in!” Boucard kept his nose in the bills. The new-comer entered humbly, attempted to smile, but the clerks’ indifference made him lower his eyes. He addressed Simonnin: “Monsieur, is your master at home?” The messenger feigned deafness. Godeschal, with his mouth full of bread, and the others bantered as always.“This is the fifth time I have called,” the stranger said. “I wish to speak to M. Derville.”“M. Derville is in bed; he does no serious work till midnight,” Godeschal replied. “If you’ll lay your case before us, we could help you as well as he can—”The stranger refused to speak to anyone but the lawyer. He looked tired; there were no chairs—attorneys seldom provided many. The inferior client stands, grumbles, and wastes no time, “which is not allowed for when the bill is taxed,” an old lawyer had said.“I will wait till he is up,” the stranger said.Boucard, finishing the bill, rose, sniffed his chocolate, surveyed the old man from head to foot, and grimaced at his coat. Assuming no fee could be wrung from such a client, he brusquely advised him to return at one in the morning. The clerks resumed their meal, indifferent to the old man’s embarrassment.“I will come again to-night,” the stranger repeated, with the tenacity of the unfortunate who hopes to catch humanity at fault.When poverty confronts Justice and Benevolence with their failures, it pleads the harder. The clerks, however, mocked him as soon as he left. “He looks as if he had been buried and dug up again,” one said. They guessed his background—colonel, retired concierge, brewer, porter—trading insults like cards. Simonnin called out from the window, and, prodded by the clerks’ mirth, the old man returned.“Will you be kind enough to leave your name?” Boucard asked.“Chabert.”“The Colonel who was killed at Eylau?” Hure chimed in.“The same, monsieur,” the old man answered with antique simplicity, and turned to go.The clerks exploded—cat-calls, exclamations. Desroches was slapped for owing a theatre ticket. The jesting resumed with gusto: which theatre? the Opera? Madame Saqui? Godeschal, pedantic even in mockery, argued about what counts as a “play” and whether Curtius’ Waxworks could be paid for as such—applying legal habits to trivialities: define the fact, consider the point of law. The office’s speech was always legalistic, even in banter.Godeschal doubted whether the man might be making game of them—yet admitted, “On my honor, Colonel Chabert is really and truly dead. His wife is married again to Comte Ferraud, Councillor of State. Madame Ferraud is one of our clients.” Boucard closed the interruption: business must go on. The appeal had to be copied and filed before the Fourth Chamber met that day; judgment would be given. The clerks were ordered back to work; they grumbled that nothing got done amid such distractions.One clerk observed that, if the stranger had really been Colonel Chabert, Simonnin’s mock-deafness might have earned a boot in the right place—a more physical verdict than Godeschal’s legalistic reasoning. Eventually they agreed to go to the Français to see Talma in “Nero,” Simonnin to the pit; then they resumed dictation.“Given in June eighteen hundred and fourteen (in words),” Godeschal read, and the copying-clerks’ pens creaked over stamped paper like trapped insects. He tried several openings—“We hope that my lords on the Bench will not be less magnanimous than the august author of the decree…”—and then hesitated: “I must read my sentence through again. I do not understand it myself.” Practicalities intruded—figures, particulars, petty scorn—and the boys went on.The scene—the youthful camaraderie, petty malice, mixture of professional charlatanry and genuine craft, and grotesque cheerfulness amid small concerns—was one of those remembered “good times.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At about one in the morning a man calling himself Colonel Chabert knocked at Maître Derville’s door; Derville was an attorney at the Court of First Instance in the Department of the Seine. The porter said Derville had not returned, but the visitor insisted he had an appointment and was shown upstairs to the lawyer’s rooms, where the head clerk was arranging papers for the next day’s cases. The clerk explained that Derville reserved the night for studying his cases when silence favored thought; the young lawyer earned well by this disciplined, spare life.When Derville arrived in evening dress, both he and his clerk were struck by the man’s appearance. The Colonel was dry and lean; a wig partly hid his forehead, his eyes seemed veiled, and his pale, livid face gave a corpse-like impression. A scar ran from the nape across the skull above the right eye—an old wound that made his appearance shocking. “Monsieur,” said Derville, “to whom have I the honor of speaking?” “To Colonel Chabert.” “Which?” “He who was killed at Eylau,” answered the stranger.Taken for mad, the man nevertheless asked to confide his position to the lawyer. Derville, used to odd clients, dismissed his clerk and asked him to be brief. The Colonel then related his story with a mixture of lucidity and the hesitation of one long disbelieved.He commanded a cavalry regiment at Eylau and had been instrumental in Murat’s charge. In action he was struck by a Russian officer with a blow that split his skull; he fell from his horse and was trampled as regiments passed over him. Imperial surgeons reported him dead and a certificate of death was made out. Yet he had not perished. He supposed tetanus or catalepsy left him apparently lifeless when he was stripped and thrown into a common grave in the hurry of battle.Later, at Stuttgart, an old quartermaster named Boutin—whom the Colonel had once saved—explained how his horse had fallen over him, shielding him from trampling and bullets and leaving a space that preserved his head. When he came to, he found himself buried among the dead. By feeling about he found an arm that served as a lever and worked his way through the piled bodies until he saw daylight through the snow. His head was cut open but coagulated blood and skin may have plugged it enough to keep him alive. Fainting and reviving, he shouted until he was finally dug out by a woman and her husband, who took him to their hovel. He remained for six months between life and death, speechless or delirious, then was admitted to the hospital at Heilsberg.He lacked the documents that would prove his identity—the surgeon Sparchmann’s deposition and the notarial declaration he had made at Heilsberg—because war had separated him from them. Wandering afterward beggared and scorned, he had tried to obtain from German authorities the proofs that might restore him to France and to his rights, but had been laughed at, regarded as mad, even locked up at Stuttgart for two years. On his release with ten thalers and a key of the fields he set out to find his wife and his fortune.He knew Derville was lawyer to the Countess Ferraud, who was indeed his wife. Chabert said that after many fruitless attempts to interest lawyers he had come to Derville because he hoped to get the German documents. He described his misery: naked when he left the hospital, wandering, begging, enduring insults, living with the hope that Boutin might find his wife. Boutin had set out for Paris to inform Madame Chabert but never reached her; he was killed at Waterloo. The Colonel arrived in Paris without money, ill and naked; after a stay at the Hôtel-Dieu he sought his house in the Rue du Mont-Blanc, only to find that it had been sold and pulled down. An old lawyer who had held his affairs was dead; his successor laughed at him when he claimed to be Chabert. He watched his wife’s house, sometimes seeing her carriage flash by after a ball; she had married M. Ferraud, had two children, and enjoyed an income of thirty thousand francs, which he asserted belonged to him. He had written her letters—she had received some—yet she gave him nothing.Derville listened, sometimes interrupting. The Colonel’s narrative was plain and full of detail; the lawyer was struck by his candor and grateful when Derville told him he would begin inquiries and make advances. The old man, so long repudiated, was overwhelmed when the young lawyer offered to try to obtain the Heilsberg papers and to give him five francs a day meanwhile. Derville spoke with practical reserve: the case would be exceptional and difficult, might go before three tribunals, and success was not certain even if the German documents could be obtained. He added a letter to his notary authorizing fifty francs every ten days to the Colonel’s order, to be recorded as a loan rather than alms, and urged him not to accept charity.Chabert was deeply moved. After ten years repudiated by his wife and by society, he could hardly believe the small assistance was real. Gratitude choked him; he thanked Derville warmly, saying that after the Emperor the lawyer would be the man to whom he owed most. Derville accompanied him to the stairs with a light, and, once outside by a street lamp, the Colonel took the two gold pieces out of the letter and looked at them. “I may smoke cigars!” he said to himself.Before leaving, Derville confided to his head clerk Boucard that the tale might cost him twenty-five louis; if robbed, he would not regret the money, for he had seen “the most consummate actor of the day.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About three months after their interview, late one night in Derville’s room, Crottat, the young notary who had advanced half-pay to Derville’s eccentric client, urged him to reclaim the six hundred francs given the old soldier. Derville, however, noticed papers Boucard had spread out and a foreign letter stamped from Prussia, Austria, Bavaria, and France; he sent Boucard to have it translated. The Berlin notary replied that the requested documents would arrive in a few days, properly witnessed and stamped—most witnesses were at Eylau, and the woman who had saved Comte Chabert was near Heilsberg.“This looks like business,” Derville said, and turned to Crottat for the Roguin file. Crottat summarized the Chabert estate: Rose Chapotel, the widow, had remarried without a settlement so the property became common; the personalty had originally been about six hundred thousand francs. Chabert’s will had left a quarter to hospitals and another quarter to the State; after contest and settlement, the Emperor restored to the widow what would have gone to the treasury. Effectively, about three hundred thousand francs remained for any claimant after previous distributions.Derville went to Faubourg Saint-Marceau, Rue du Petit-Banquier, to the lodging named on the lawyer’s receipt: the house of Vergniaud, an old quartermaster turned dairyman. A rutted street brought him to a shabby yard and patched hovel with barnyard smells, rabbit hutches, a cowshed, poultry and a cart-horse; tin cans and drying rags testified to daily toil. Three little boys played; when asked about Chabert they at first shrank, then laughed at being mocked.Colonel Chabert appeared, smoking a short clay pipe, cap greasy yet bearing a military calm. He ordered the boys “Silence in the ranks!” and led Derville past a manure heap to his room: a straw bed, scraps of carpet, an earthen floor, damp walls, a reed mat, a box-coat on a nail and two pairs of boots. On the table lay reprints of the Bulletins de la Grande Armée. Despite the squalor, Chabert’s face expressed hope more than despair.“Does the smell of the pipe annoy you?” he asked, offering a stool. Derville, aghast at the discomfort and suspecting waste, heard Chabert explain simply that the family who lodged him had fed him gratis for a year; he could not leave them while he had any money left, and he was finishing teaching their children to read. If he recovered his fortune, he would do better; meanwhile “it does very well.”When Derville told him the woman who had dug him from the battlefield was still alive and that papers were coming, the Colonel flung his pipe to the ground, near-ecstatic in his frustration. As they walked, Derville laid out the legal landscape: Chabert’s identity and claims would have to be judicially proved; papers could be disputed; preliminary inquiries, appeals, and foreign commissions might delay matters. The Comtesse’s bigamy complicated things; the Code did not clearly settle such a case, and judges might treat it as a question of equity and favor existing family ties. Even if the law seemed to favor Chabert, litigation could be long, bitter and costly.Chabert thought the matter simple: presumed dead and alive again, he wanted his wife and fortune returned and reinstatement on the army list—perhaps a promotion, since he had been made Colonel of the Imperial Guard the day before Eylau. Derville warned him that the facts might support his claims but that the Court could be swayed by the Comtesse and public opinion; costs would be heavy. He explained the estate’s disposition: because a quarter had been given to hospitals and the remainder had been handled after Chabert’s supposed death, some money had already gone to hospitals and to the widow; the Emperor’s decree had returned to the widow what would have gone to the Exchequer, leaving roughly three hundred thousand francs for Chabert to claim, minus costs. Derville estimated preliminary inquiries at ten or twelve thousand francs—funds he could not front, being pressed by debts from buying his business. Chabert, moved to tears by the obstacles, put himself wholly in Derville’s hands.Derville promised to send a power of attorney and to help financially if possible. When he left, Vergniaud, who had watched from outside, told him Chabert occupied “the best room” and had often helped them with bills, wounding their pride; he asked Derville to lend a hundred crowns to furnish the room. Seeing honest poverty, Derville agreed but resolved to give the money directly to Chabert so the dairyman would not reap the charity.As he drove away Derville began to consider their adversary: the Countess Ferraud. He resolved to conceal his hand while discovering hers and to win by exploiting a woman’s chief fear. He surveyed the Ferraud position. Comte Ferraud was the only son of an émigré councillor who had lost his fortune; under the Consulate and by marrying Rose Chapotel—who had turned her portion of the old fortune to good account—he became wealthy. By the Restoration he had estates and preferment and now held a post he hoped would lead to a peerage. Ambition consumed him.To manage the fortune and further his rise, Ferraud attached Delbecq, a ruined attorney hungry for office, as secretary. Delbecq, tempted by the Countess, became her instrument; practical, shrewd and unscrupulous, he increased her capital and was promised a provincial judgeship for his services. Delbecq, greedy for place, trebled her capital. This partnership secured the family’s social ascent.By 1818, with the Restoration consolidated, Madame la Comtesse Ferraud had a place at Court and comfort; privately she endured a terror. Though she had known her first husband lived and ignored him, his sudden reappearance threatened to destroy the life she had built. She had resolved to make herself so rich and well-placed that a second marriage could not be dissolved. Chabert’s return therefore posed a peril she could not explain or confide to Delbecq or the police; many women conceal such anxieties and learn to live with them.Derville went to the Hotel Ferraud in Rue de Varennes and contrasted its luxury with Chabert’s squalor: silver, mother-of-pearl, rare plants in china jars. The Countess received him at breakfast, playful with a monkey on a pole, elegant and careless. Derville told her bluntly that Colonel Chabert was alive. She laughed it off as nonsense, claiming no letter had arrived and boasting she still drew the widow’s pension. Derville’s fixed gaze and insistence unnerved her; he suggested she had received a first letter because it contained securities, and pressed that she was “caught” in a snare laid by an attorney. She flared with anger and then went pale but could not fully recover.“I am as much your lawyer now as I am Colonel Chabert’s,” Derville said, warning that public opinion could turn against her: her fortune came from M. le Comte Chabert, whom she had cast off, and circumstances might make her appear heartless. She protested that even if Chabert were living the law would uphold her second marriage on account of the children and she would only have to restitute some two hundred and twenty-five thousand francs. Derville replied the Bench’s view was uncertain. More dangerous, he said, was a domestic shift: if Ferraud were told his marriage was void and his wife dishonored, ambition might lead him to accept annulment, receive a peerage and remarry for title.Derville hinted that a decorated general, restored and insisting on his rights, could be an alluring rival who might capture the Count’s wife. He had struck the Countess’s hidden wound. Pressed, she asked what to do. Derville advised compromise and suggested she might manipulate Chabert’s affection to secure terms rather than risk ruinous litigation. She conceded, offering to retain Derville as her lawyer. He left, saying he would await her orders and would either report proceedings or meet her in his office to agree terms.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A week after Derville’s two visits, on a fine June morning the estranged husband and wife, separated almost as if by a supernatural chance, set out from opposite ends of Paris to meet in the lawyer’s office engaged by both. The money Derville had advanced enabled Colonel Chabert to appear suitably: he arrived in a decent cab, wearing a wig, blue cloth and white linen, and the broad red ribbon of the higher grade of the Legion of Honor under his waistcoat. Resuming the habits of wealth restored his soldierly bearing; his grave, mysterious face reflected hope and seemed revived. He no longer resembled the Chabert of the old box-coat; a passerby would have recognized one of the noble wrecks of the old army.When the Countess stepped from a splendid brougham, she wore a simple dress set off to display her youth; a pink-lined bonnet framed and softened her face. The office was unusually lively: Simonnin breakfasted by the open window and joked with the clerks, who noticed the two visitors—Chabert in Derville’s private room, the Countess in the main office. Derville had seated her apart, uncertain whether she wished to meet the man who claimed to be her first husband.Derville read aloud a memorandum he had drawn up. Its preamble named Hyacinthe Chabert, Count, living in Paris, and Madame Rose Chapotel, wife of the said Comte Chabert. It proposed that she and Chabert acknowledge, before witnesses and notaries, that the person designated was her first husband; that Chabert would assert his rights only under certain circumstances—specifically, if she failed to carry out the conditions of a secret agreement; and that Chabert would accept a friendly suit to annul his certificate of death and dissolve the marriage. By a subsequent clause she pledged to secure Chabert an income of twenty-four thousand francs on government stock, to revert to her at his death.“Too dear!” the Countess exclaimed. Derville suggested compromise; she hesitated.“I want—I will not have a lawsuit. I want—” she began.“You want him to remain dead?” asked Derville.“Monsieur,” the Countess replied, “if twenty-four thousand francs are necessary, we will go to law—”“Yes, we will go to law,” the Colonel said in a deep voice, opening the door and standing before his wife, one hand in his waistcoat, the other hanging by his side—an attitude made ominous by his adventure.“It is he,” the Countess thought.“Too dear!” the old soldier exclaimed. “I have given you near on a million, and you are cheapening my misfortunes. Very well; now I will have you—you and your fortune. Our goods are in common, our marriage is not dissolved—”“But monsieur is not Colonel Chabert!” cried the Countess, feigning astonishment.“Indeed!” the old man said with intense irony. “Do you want proofs? I found you in the Palais-Royal—”The Countess turned pale; the remark pierced her. Chabert, touched at hurting the woman he had once loved, paused; her venomous glance made him continue abruptly: “You were with La—”“Allow me, Monsieur Derville,” the Countess said. “You must give me leave to retire. I did not come here to listen to such dreadful things.” She left quickly; Derville rushed after her but she had gone.Back in his private room the Colonel paced in towering rage. He reproached himself for past foolishness and trusted appearances. Derville warned him that any attempt at force would be madness—he might be arrested or worse, and the Countess might set a trap to have him confined in Charenton. He advised prudence and promised to notify her of their proceedings to protect him. The Colonel, stammering apologies, left.Descending the dark staircase, crushed by the most cruel blow—his wife's recognition of him without acknowledgment of identity—he heard a rustle on the lowest landing and she stood before him. With a familiar gesture she took his arm. Her action and recovered gracious tone allayed his wrath, and he allowed her to lead him to the carriage. As if by magic he found himself sitting beside her.They drove across Paris. “Monsieur,” the Countess said in a voice trembling with a rare emotion—this single word expressed reproach and pardon, hope and despair, a question and an answer—“I knew you at once.”“Rosine,” said the old soldier, “those words contain the only balm that can help me to forget my misfortunes.” He pressed her hands with tears.She explained why she had behaved as she did: his letters had reached her months late, opened and soiled; after obtaining Napoleon’s signature to her second marriage contract she had believed some swindler sought to make a fool of her. To avoid disturbing M. Ferraud and his family, she had taken precautions against a pretended Chabert. “Was I not right?” she asked.“Yes, you were right. It was I who was the idiot,” he admitted. She declared she would preserve dignity and avoid scandal in Paris; though she felt bound by right to him, she was no longer his in fact, and she loved M. Ferraud. She did not blush to confess it, and she claimed a right to happiness. The Colonel accepted this with dignity; the man of honor that he was could not demand mockery of love from a wife who no longer loved him.For a long stretch they rode without speaking. Chabert pictured their two children—he became softly paternal. The Countess sought to revive his love without awakening desire, and guided him toward the role of father and friend. She arranged for his stay at a delightful house she had prepared for him in a park near Groslay in the valley of Montmorency. Misfortune had made the Colonel more helpful and good; she displayed tender attentions, sweet melancholy, and a manner calculated to win his forgiveness and occupy his mind with memories rather than claims.She did not yet know exactly what to do with him but meant to annihilate him socially. On the third evening, uneasy at the results of her maneuvers, she finished a letter to Delbecq, instructing him to demand the deeds from Derville in her name, copy them, and come to Groslay.
