He left visibly moved; he even thought the rooms might suit him if the Marquis vacated them.The next morning Popinot went to the Palais de Justice to act on his written report. In the robing-room the usher told him the Court President wished to see him. Privately, the President informed Popinot that the Keeper of the Seals, having learned Popinot had been to tea with Madame d’Espard in whose case he had been employed, thought it improper for Popinot to sit on the case—“Caesar’s wife must not be suspected.” Though the President believed Popinot had acted independently, the Keeper’s remark made Popinot’s continuance risky before public opinion and the press. To avoid scandal, the President asked him to withdraw from the case as a favor; in compensation, the President would see that Popinot received the Cross of the Legion of Honor, long due him.Popinot bitterly accepted without denying the charge. M. Camusot, a pale, fair, ambitious judge newly arrived from a provincial Court, was named in his place. Popinot withdrew with a bow, scornful of the false suspicion. The episode closes with the date, Paris, February 1836.ADDENDUMSeveral personages here recur elsewhere in Balzac’s Human Comedy—Bianchon, Bordin, Camusot de Marville, Desroches (son), Espard (various family members), Godeschal, Grozier, Jeanrenaud, Mongenod, Négrepelisse, Nucingen (Baronne Delphine de), Popinot, and Rabourdin—linking this episode to Balzac’s larger social panorama. End of Project Gutenberg’s The Commission in Lunacy, by Honoré de Balzac.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY — central ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################ ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Whenever you have seriously looked at the exhibition of sculpture and painting since the revolution of 1830, have you not felt uneasiness and sadness at the sight of those long overcrowded galleries? Since 1830, the true Salon no longer exists. The Louvre has been taken again—this time by a populace of artists who have maintained themselves there.Formerly, when the Salon showed only choicest works, exhibiting conferred the highest honor. Among two hundred selected paintings the public could still choose: an invisible hand crowned the masterpiece. Eager discussions—abuse and praise alike—marked success. Now neither the crowd nor criticism grows impassioned about the products of that bazaar. Forced to select for itself what a jury once chose, the public's attention soon tires, the exhibition drags on, and the Salon closes. Before 1817 the admitted pictures never went beyond the first two columns of the long gallery of old masters; in that year they filled the whole space. Historical, high-art, genre, easel pictures, landscapes, flowers, animals, and water-colors could scarcely offer more than twenty pictures a year worthy of attention; the public cannot attend to more. The more artists increase, the more careful the jury of admission should be.The true character of the Salon was lost when its exhibitions spread along the galleries. It should have kept fixed, strict limits where each specialty could show its masterpieces. A decade's experience proved the excellence of the former institution. Now, instead of a tournament we have a mob; instead of a noble exhibition, a tumultuous bazaar; instead of selection, a chaotic mass. The result: a great artist is swamped. Decamps' "Turkish Cafe," "Children at a Fountain," "Joseph," and "The Torture" would have profited far more had four pictures been shown among the hundred good works of a year than his twenty among three thousand others jumbled in six galleries.By a strange contradiction, ever since the doors opened to everyone there has been talk of unknown and unrecognized genius. When, twelve years earlier, Ingres' "Courtesan," Sigalon's, Gericault's "Medusa," Delacroix's "Massacre of Scio," Eugene Deveria's "Baptism of Henri IV" were admitted despite jealousy, the world saw young vigorous palettes existed and made no complaint. Now, when the veriest dauber can send his work, all the talk is of genius neglected! Where judgment no longer exists, there is nothing judged. But artists will return in time to examination and selection, which present their works to the crowd for whom they work. Without selection by the Academy there will be no Salon, and without the Salon art may perish.Since the catalogue has grown into a book, many obscure names appear in it, attached to ten or a dozen pictures yet remaining in obscurity. Among these perhaps the most unknown to fame is Pierre Grassou, from Fougeres—called simply "Fougeres" among his brother-artists—who now holds a place, as they say, "in the sun," and whose case suggests the bitter reflections that introduce this sketch—reflections applicable to many other members of the tribe of artists.In 1832 Fougeres lived in the rue de Navarin, on the fourth floor of one of those tall narrow houses resembling an obelisk, with an alley, a dark stairway, three windows on each floor, and within, a courtyard, or rather a square pit. Above the three or four rooms he occupied was his studio, looking toward Montmartre. The studio was painted brick-color for a background; the floor tinted brown and well frothed; each chair had a bit of carpet bound round the edges; the simple sofa was as clean as in some worthy bourgeoise's bedroom. All these things showed the tidy ways of a small mind and thrift of a poor man. A bureau held studio implements, a table for breakfast, a sideboard, a secretary; in short, all the articles necessary to a painter, neatly arranged and very clean. The stove added to this Dutch tidiness, made more evident by the northern light flooding the large apartment. Being merely a genre painter, Fougeres did not need the vast machinery that ruins historical painters; he never recognized sufficient faculty to attempt high art and therefore clung to easel painting.At the beginning of December, a season when Parisian bourgeois fancy perpetuating their forms and figures already too bulky, Pierre Grassou rose early, prepared his palette, lighted his stove, ate a roll soaked in milk, and waited till the frost on his windows melted enough to let full light in. The weather was fine and dry. As he began to work in his tidy studio he heard and recognized the step of Elie Magus, a picture-dealer, a usurer in canvas. The next moment Elie entered."How are you, old rascal?" said the painter.Fougeres had the cross of the Legion of Honor, and Elie Magus bought his pictures at two and three hundred francs apiece, so he gave himself the airs of a fine artist."Business is very bad," replied Elie. "You artists have such pretensions! You talk of two hundred francs when you haven't put six sous worth of color on a canvas. However, you are a good fellow; I'll say that. You are steady; and I've come to put a good bit of business in your way.""Timeo Danaos et dona ferentes," said Fougeres."No," said Elie."Well, it means the Greeks never proposed good bits of business to the Trojans without getting their fair share. In the olden time they used to say, 'Take my horse.' Now we say, 'Take my bear.' Well, what do you want, Ulysses-Lagingeole-Elie Magus?"This shows the mildness and wit with which Fougeres employed studio fun."Well, I don't deny that you are to paint me two pictures for nothing.""Oh! oh!""I'll leave you to do it, or not; I don't ask it. But you're an honest man.""Come, out with it!""Well, I'm prepared to bring you a father, mother, and only daughter.""All for me?""Yes—they want their portraits taken. These bourgeois—they are crazy about art—have never dared to enter a studio. The girl has a dot of a hundred thousand francs. You can paint all three—perhaps they'll turn out family portraits."Elie's uncanny laugh frightened the painter; he fancied Mephistopheles talking marriage."Portraits bring five hundred francs apiece," went on Elie; "so you can very well afford to paint me three pictures.""True for you!" cried Fougeres, gleefully."And if you marry the girl, you won't forget me.""Marry! I?" cried Pierre Grassou—"I, who have a habit of sleeping alone; who get up at cock-crow, and all my life arranged—""One hundred thousand francs," said Magus. "And a quiet girl, full of golden tones, as you call 'em, like a Titian.""What class of people are they?""Retired merchants; just now in love with art; have a country-house at Ville d'Avray, and ten or twelve thousand francs a year.""What business did they do?""Bottles.""Now don't say that word; it makes me think of corks and sets my teeth on edge.""Am I to bring them?""Three portraits—I could put them in the Salon; I might go in for portrait-painting. Well, yes!"Old Elie descended the staircase to fetch the Vervelle family. To know how this proposition would affect the painter, and what effect the Sieur and Dame Vervelle, adorned by their only daughter, would produce, it is necessary to glance at the anterior life of Pierre Grassou of Fougeres.As a pupil, Fougeres had studied drawing with Servin, a great draughtsman in academic circles. After that he went to Schinner's to learn the secrets of the powerful, magnificent color that distinguished that master. Master and scholars were discreet; at any rate Pierre discovered none of their secrets. From there he went to Sommervieux' atelier to acquire composition, but composition was shy and distant to him. Then he tried to snatch from Decamps and Granet the mystery of their interior effects. The two masters were not robbed. Finally Fougeres ended his education with Duval-Lecamus. During these studies Fougeres' habits and ways of life were tranquil and moral to a degree that furnished matter of jesting to the various ateliers where he sojourned; but everywhere he disarmed his comrades by his modesty and by the patience and gentleness of a lamblike nature. The masters, however, had no sympathy for the good lad; masters prefer bright fellows, eccentric spirits, droll or fiery, or else gloomy and reflective, which argue future talent. Everything about Pierre Grassou smacked of mediocrity. His nickname "Fougeres" (that of the painter in the play of "The Eglantine") was the source of much teasing; but, by force of circumstances, he accepted the name of the town where he had first seen light.Grassou of Fougeres resembled his name. Plump and of medium height, he had a dull complexion, brown eyes, black hair, a turned-up nose, rather wide mouth, and long ears. His gentle, passive, resigned air gave a certain relief to these leading features of a physiognomy full of health but wanting in action. This young man, born to be a virtuous bourgeois, having left his native place and come to Paris to be a clerk with a color-merchant (formerly of Mayenne and a distant connection of the Orgemonts) made himself a painter simply by the obstinacy that constitutes the Breton character. What he suffered, the manner in which he lived during those years of study, God only knows. He suffered as much as great men suffer when hounded by poverty and hunted like wild beasts by the pack of commonplace minds and troops of vanities athirst for vengeance.As soon as he thought himself able to fly on his own wings, Fougeres took a studio in the upper rue des Martyrs, where he began to work. He made his first appearance in 1819. The first picture he presented to the jury at the Louvre represented a village wedding laboriously copied from Greuze. It was rejected. When Fougeres heard of the decision, he did not fall into one of those fierce fits of self-love to which strong natures give themselves up; he quietly fetched his canvas, wrapped it in a handkerchief, and brought it home, vowing he would still make himself a great painter. He placed his picture on the easel and went to one of his former masters, Schinner—whose triumph at that year's Salon was complete—and asked him to criticize the rejected work. The great painter left everything and went at once. When Fougeres placed the work before him, Schinner, after a glance, pressed his hand."You are a fine fellow," he said; "you've a heart of gold, and I must not deceive you. Listen; you are fulfilling the promises you made in the studios. When you find such things at the tip of your brush, my good Fougeres, you had better leave colors with Brullon, and not take the canvas of others. Go home early, put on your cotton night-cap, and be in bed by nine o'clock. The next morning go to some government office, ask for a place, and give up art.""My dear friend," said Fougeres, "my picture is already condemned; it is not a verdict I want of you, but the cause of that verdict.""Well—you paint gray and sombre; you see nature as a crape veil; your drawing is heavy, pasty; your composition is a medley of Greuze, who only redeemed his defects by qualities you lack."While detailing these faults Schinner saw on Fougeres' face so deep an expression of sadness that he carried him off to dinner and tried to console him. The next morning at seven Fougeres was at his easel, working over the rejected picture; he warmed the colors, made the corrections suggested by Schinner; he touched up his figures. Then, disgusted with such patching, he carried the picture to Elie Magus. Elie Magus, a sort of Dutch-Flemish-Belgian, had three reasons for being what he became—rich and avaricious. Coming last from Bordeaux, he had started in Paris selling old pictures and living on the boulevard Bonne-Nouvelle. Fougeres, who relied on his palette to go to the baker's, bravely ate bread and nuts, or bread and milk, or bread and cherries, or bread and cheese, or bread and cherries, according to the seasons. Elie Magus, to whom Pierre offered his first picture, eyed it for some time and then gave him fifteen francs."With fifteen francs a year coming in, and a thousand francs for expenses," said Fougeres, smiling, "a man will go fast and far."Elie Magus made a gesture; he bit his thumbs, thinking he might have had that picture for five francs.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Several days Pierre walked down from the rue des Martyrs and stood at the corner of the boulevard opposite Elie Magus's shop, watching his picture receive no notice. After a week the picture disappeared. Fougeres went slowly to the dealer's; the Jew was at his door."Well, I see you have sold my picture.""No, here it is," said Magus; "I've framed it, to show it to someone who fancies he knows about painting."Fougeres had not the heart to return to the boulevard. He set about another picture and spent two months on it, eating meagrely and working like a galley-slave. One evening he went to the boulevard; his picture was not to be seen."I've sold your picture," said Elie Magus."For how much?""I got back what I gave and a small interest. Make me some Flemish interiors, a lesson of anatomy, landscapes, and such like, and I'll buy them of you," said Elie.Touched to the core, Fougeres regarded Magus as a father. He went home joyful; the great painter Schinner had been mistaken—at least some hearts in Paris beat in unison with his. He was ingenuous and proud like a boy; another, more suspicious artist would have read the ironical twitch of Magus's moustache and the satisfaction of a dealer who had trapped a poor painter.He walked the boulevard in a glow of joy and met Joseph Bridau. They went to the Opera, but Fougeres saw nothing; he was full of pictures. He bought colors, canvases, fed a pot, hired models; Magus lent him stuffs, and he "dug" at his work. After two months' seclusion he finished four pictures. Schinner and Bridau saw in three a servile imitation of Dutch interiors à la Metsu, and the fourth a copy of Rembrandt's "Lesson of Anatomy.""Still imitating!" said Schinner. "Ah! Fougeres can't manage to be original."Bridau advised him to do something else than painting. Humiliated, Fougeres accepted advice, retouched his pictures, and took them to Elie Magus, who paid twenty-five francs apiece. At that price he earned nothing, and he barely broke even thanks to his frugal living. He made excursions to the boulevard to see his works and underwent a strange hallucination: his neat, clean paintings, hard and shiny as porcelain, looked as if covered with a mist, as if old daubs. Magus was out; Pierre suspected his eyes.Fougeres continued to paint. After seven years' toil he reached a passable level—about as good as any second-rate artist. Elie bought and sold all his paintings; the Breton earned laboriously about two thousand francs a year while he spent but twelve hundred. At the Exhibition of 1829, Leon de Lora, Schinner, and Bridau—leaders of the art movement—pitied his perseverance and had a picture of his admitted to the grand salon. The canvas, powerful in interest but derived in sentiment from Vigneron and in execution from Dubufe, represented a young man in prison having his hair cut at the nape; a priest, two weeping women, and a sheriff's clerk reading aloud formed the group; on a poor table a meal was untouched; light came through bars near the ceiling. Fougeres had been inspired by Gerard Dou: he turned the "Dropsical Woman" group toward the window, substituted a condemned man for the dying woman, replaced the Dutch doctor with a cold clerk, added an old woman to the young, and put on the group an executioner's simple, good-humored face. The plagiarism was cleverly disguised and unrecognized. The catalogue read:510. Grassou de Fougeres (Pierre), rue de Navarin, 2.Death-toilet of a Chouan, condemned to execution in 1809.Though essentially second-rate, the picture had immense success because it recalled the "chauffeurs" affair of Mortagne. A crowd gathered every day before the fashionable canvas; even Charles X paused. "Madame," told of the painter's patient life, became enthusiastic. The Duc d'Orleans asked the price. Clergymen declared the subject morally edifying. Madame bought the picture for a thousand francs; the Dauphin ordered another like it. Charles X conferred the cross of the Legion of Honor on this son of a peasant who had once fought for the royal cause in 1799. The minister of the Interior ordered two church pictures from Fougeres.This Salon of 1829 was Pierre Grassou's fortune, fame, future, and life. He learned that originality starved while imitation sold. This discovery—copy to live—was the gold mine that shaped his career; it is the fatal principle by which society elevates certain wretched mediocrities and excludes real talent. Yet Fougeres's modesty and genuine surprise disarmed envy. Pity and admiration for his perseverance did the rest: "Perseverance in the arts should be rewarded. Grassou hasn't stolen his successes; he has delved for ten years, the poor dear man!" Newspapers criticized, but the chevalier Fougeres accepted that like other counsel, with angelic patience.Having by this time fifteen thousand francs laboriously earned, he furnished an apartment and studio in the rue de Navarin, painted the Dauphin's picture and the church commissions, and delivered them punctually—much to the exchequer's discomfort. By thirty-seven Fougeres had produced for Elie Magus about two hundred pictures, all unknown, and had attained that manner and point of execution before which true artists shrank and the bourgeoisie worshiped. He was loved for rectitude, steadiness, kindness, and loyalty, though his palette earned no esteem. His comrades called his "vice" the misfortune of painting.Yet he gave sound counsel; unlike superficial critics, he felt beauty and judged justly. After the revolution of July, he sent about ten pictures a year to the Salon; the jury admitted four or five. He lived with rigid economy, mounted guard, went to reviews, visited friends, looked at works of art, took a few trips, and planned a journey to Switzerland for inspiration. An excellent citizen—he paid rent and bills—he had never had time to love. Poor and a bachelor, he had not sought to complicate his life.Incapable of increasing his fortune by other means, he took his savings every three months to his notary Cardot, who invested them in first mortgages; the interest he drew and added to his quarterly receipts. He hoped the day would come when these savings would yield an income of two thousand francs, allowing him otium cum dignitate to paint true finished pictures and to enter the Institute, sit beside Schinner and Leon de Lora, reach the Academy before Bridau, and wear a rosette. Such modest, bourgeois dreams were his.One day, hearing steps on the staircase, he tidied himself and admitted a family whose studios mockingly called "a melon on a pumpkin": Monsieur Vervelle, a bottle dealer wearing a three-thousand-franc diamond; his wife, plumply veneered and ostentatiously adorned; and their daughter Virginie, pale, with stringy hair and large innocent eyes, in a leghorn bonnet. A true painter might have dismissed such bourgeois clients, but Magus, who had brought them, stayed. Fougeres glanced at Magus and said, "There's fat in it!"—studio slang for a lucrative commission.Monsieur Vervelle, hearing that the painter was decorated, quipped that a decorated artist was more suitable. The family were dazzled by Fougeres and assumed his honesty and thrift. During the sitting they hovered about the easel, marvelling and flattering; Virginie's modest bows and lowered eyes charmed the painter. He gave them sketches and drawings, small gifts that impressed them. Old Vervelle boasted of his country-house gallery—Rubens, Gerard Dou, Mieris, Terburg, Rembrandt, Titian, Paul Potter—and Madame Vervelle declared he had over one hundred thousand francs' worth of pictures. "I love Art," he said.The family learned from the notary and the neighborhood that Fougeres had been prudent: Cardot, their notary as well, praised the painter—he had saved thirty-six thousand francs and now saved about ten thousand a year. This endorsement seemed decisive. As the Vervelles left, the father told Virginie to walk in front; she overheard a remark that would awaken her curiosity: "Decorated—thirty-seven years old—an artist who gets orders—puts his money with our notary. We'll consult Cardot. Madame de Fougeres! Not a bad name."While the Vervelles discussed Pierre, he thought of them. He could not rest; he walked the boulevard, looking at red-haired women and making odd reasonings: gold was the handsomest metal, tawny yellow suggested it, the Romans favoured red hair—he turned Roman. After two years of marriage what man would care about hair color? Beauty fades, money steadies life. By night he had found Virginie Vervelle charming; the prospect of security warmed him.On the second sitting the family returned. Fougeres had shaved, put on clean linen, arranged his hair, and chosen becoming garments. The mutual flatteries continued; Virginie's delicate bows and lowered eyes enchanted him. He praised the father as witty, sending the family into raptures; gifts of small drawings heightened their regard. Old Vervelle warned that pictures were money; yet Cardot's praise tipped the scale. Vervelle's friends now spoke only of the celebrated painter Fougeres, and marriage prospects were quietly under consideration.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Fougeres began Mademoiselle Virginie's portrait he was already virtually son‑in‑law to the Vervelle family. The three Vervelles had made his studio a second home, attracted by its neat, artistic air. Toward the end of the sitting the stairway shook and Joseph Bridau burst in like a whirlwind, hair flying and face haggard. He paced, fussed at Grassou’s coat and muttered about creditors. Then, seeing the portrait, he burst into blunt advice."Wood is dear," he said to Grassou. "The British are after me… Any shot in your locker? How much do you want?" He urged bolder color—“Put some vermilion on your palette, warm those cheeks; touch the little brown spots; butter it in”—taunting the painter for pretending to know more than Nature. Fougeres, embarrassed, sent Grassou to write a note while he obliged Bridau. The bottle‑dealer took the palette, accepted the IOU, and left without ceremony, plainly impatient with Virginie.The Vervelles were startled by Bridau’s intrusion; the mother humbly preferred the decorous Grassou to any confusing genius. Timidly flattered by their deference, Vervelle invited Fougeres to his country house the next Sunday, promising wines and a picture collection that would compensate an artist for dining with merchants. This flattery particularly touched Pierre Grassou’s innocent vanity. He was an atrocious mediocrity with a heart of gold—loyal, poor at draughtsmanship, unambitious yet proud of small honors. Decorated with the Legion of Honor, he accepted and travelled modestly to Ville d'Avray, imagining marriage to Virginie and possession of the villa.The Vervelles received him with enthusiastic bourgeois pomp. The day became a triumph: he was shown the park, led about the manicured grounds, feted with a magnificent dinner, and then taken to the theatrical climax—the picture gallery opened under carefully arranged lamps. Three neighbors, an old uncle, and other guests awaited his judgement of Pere Vervelle’s celebrated collection, which the bottle‑merchant loved to inflate.Framed and labeled in gilt were names to dazzle any novice: Rubens—Dance of fauns and nymphs; Rembrandt—Interior of a dissecting room… One hundred and fifty varnished pictures shone; some were even veiled by green baize that prudence forbade drawing in ladies’ company. Pierre stood aghast as he recognized half his own works among the displayed "masters." He saw Rubens, Rembrandt, Mieris, Metzu, Paul Potter, Gerard Douw—twenty great names, yet many paintings were his.Alarmed, a guest asked why his face had gone pale; he was led aside and told the host, ostentatiously, he should show him a Murillo. In whispers at the gallery, Pierre pressed Vervelle: had he bought these from Elie Magus? Yes, Vervelle said, all originals. Pierre asked what Magus had charged for some pieces; Vervelle named three thousand francs, though to the public he claimed forty thousand and boasted of a collection worth three hundred thousand. The painter then dropped the bombshell: he had painted those pictures and sold them to Elie Magus for a total under ten thousand francs. Magus, Pierre explained, had had them "aged" and retailed as old masters.Pere Vervelle, far from being shamed, was delighted—if true, he said he would double his daughter's dowry, and the artist, now acclaimed as Rubens, Rembrandt, Titian, Gerard Douw, became a local celebrity. The Vervelles called him "Monsieur de Fougeres" and, happy with his inflated status, accepted the portraits he had painted of the family; when finished he presented them to his future father‑in‑law, mother‑in‑law, and wife without charge.From then on, Pierre Grassou—who never missed the Salon—was regarded in bourgeois circles as a fine portraitist. He earned about twenty thousand francs a year while spoiling a thousand francs’ worth of canvas. His wife’s dowry had been six thousand francs; he lived with his father‑in‑law; the Vervelles and Grassous kept a carriage and were content. Grassou remained firmly within the bourgeois world: no family portrait between the barrier du Trone and the rue du Temple escaped him, none costing less than five hundred francs. The principal reason the bourgeoisie employed him was practical—he was prudent and "laid by twenty thousand francs a year with his notary."Grassou’s civic partisanship—he took a creditable part in the May 12th riots—earned him a promotion to officer of the Legion of Honor and a major’s post in the National Guard. The Museum of Versailles commissioned a battle scene from such an exemplary citizen, and he paraded the commission as royal favor among his friends. At home, Madame de Fougeres adored her husband, who had given her two children and was a good father and spouse. Yet Grassou could not silence a gnawing thought: real artists scorned his work, studios used his name as an epithet, and critics ignored him in the feuilletons. Ambition for the Academy remained alive in him.Bitter as that was, Grassou took a sly revenge: he bought works by struggling celebrated artists and substituted those true pieces for the daubs in the Ville d'Avray collection—an anonymous but pointed correction. Unlike many mediocre men who are aggressive and vindictive, Pierre remained beneficent, obliging and modestly malicious by stealth.ADDENDUMThe following characters recur elsewhere in Balzac’s Human Comedy:- Joseph Bridau: appears in The Purse; A Bachelor's Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Start in Life; Modeste Mignon; Another Study of Woman; Letters of Two Brides; Cousin Betty; The Member for Arcis.- Cardot (Parisian notary): The Muse of the Department; A Man of Business; Jealousies of a Country Town; The Middle Classes; Cousin Pons.- Grassou, Pierre: A Bachelor's Establishment; Cousin Betty; The Middle Classes; Cousin Pons.- Lora, Leon de: The Unconscious Humorists; A Bachelor's Establishment; A Start in Life; Honorine; Cousin Betty; Beatrix.- Magus, Elie: The Vendetta; A Marriage Settlement; A Bachelor's Establishment; Cousin Pons.- Schinner, Hippolyte: The Purse; A Bachelor's Establishment; A Start in Life; Albert Savarus; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; The Imaginary Mistress; The Unconscious Humorists.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY REPEATED THRICE, THEN CUT.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Some provincial houses wear a melancholy like cloisters or ruins; silence and barrenness prevail until a pale, motionless face appears at a window. Such a dwelling crowned the steep street toward the château in Saumur. This little-frequented, narrow, tortuous lane, echoing under its pebbles, is lined with three-century-old wooden houses, their carved ends blackened by time. Slate-covered beams trace bluish lines along frail walls; rotten shingles sag; blackened window-sills with faint sculpture hold clay pots. Doors studded with enormous nails bear domestic hieroglyphics—marks of Protestantism, Leaguer curses, or trades—the history of France written in wood.Next to a tottering artisan’s house rose a country gentleman’s mansion with battered armorial bearings. In this hilly street ground-floors are not shops but the simple ouvroirs of another age: low, deep, dark rooms without show-windows. Doors open in two parts; the upper half is fastened back while the lower, fitted with a spring-bell, swings continually. Light and air enter through the upper door or an open space between ceiling and low front wall, closed each evening by solid shutters and iron bars.That low wall serves as a counter displaying mere samples—tubs of cod and salt, bundles of sail-cloth, cordage, copper wire, iron hoops, a few cloth pieces. Inside a neat girl in a white kerchief calls her father or mother, who sells what you want, phlegmatic, civil, or arrogant. A cooper may seem to own little beyond boat-ribs and laths, yet his wood-yard below supplies the cooperage of Anjou; a hot season makes him rich, a rainy one ruins him. Here, as in Touraine, weather controls commerce: wine-growers, proprietors, wood-merchants, coopers, inn-keepers, mariners—all watch the sun and dread frost, rain, drought. The barometer smooths, saddens, or brightens their faces; from end to end of this street men call out its signs: “Here’s golden weather,” or “It rains louis,” knowing what sun or rain will bring.On Saturdays these traders, with vineyards and provisions arranged, close shop and spend two days in the country. Purchases and profits settled, they have hours for pleasure, observation, criticism, and spying. Neighbors remark on every partridge or pot; a girl peeking at a window is seen by idling groups. Consciences live in sunlight; the dark, silent houses hide no mysteries. Life is mostly at door thresholds where households breakfast, dine, quarrel, and examine every passer-by. Once strangers were bantered from door to door—origin of many stories and the nickname copieux for Angers folk famed for such urban sarcasm.The ancient mansions of Saumur’s old town crown this street; the melancholy dwelling at its top is one of those venerable relics. Follow the thoroughfare to the dark recess hiding the door of Monsieur Grandet’s house. To explain why the town calls it by his name, we must relate Monsieur Grandet’s biography.Grandet’s provincial reputation is best understood locally. In 1789, still called le Père Grandet, he was a master-cooper who could read, write, and cipher. That year he married the daughter of a rich wood-merchant; with about two thousand louis-d’or—his and his wife’s dowry—and help from two hundred double louis given by his father-in-law to the republican presiding over sales, Grandet bought cheaply one of the finest vineyards, an old abbey, and several farms. The inhabitants, not very revolutionary, admired his boldness and patriotism; in truth his thought was open only to vineyards. Appointed to administrate Saumur, his influence proved pacific and commercial: he protected former nobles when possible and supplied Republican armies with puncheons of white wine, taking payment in splendid fields then reserved for sale.Under the Consulate Grandet became mayor, governed wisely, and reaped better pickings. Under the Empire he was called Monsieur Grandet; Napoleon supplanted him in office but he left without regret. He had built roads to his properties; his house and lands, assessed advantageously, paid moderate taxes; and his vineyards, through his care, became the “head of the country,” the local term for the finest yielders. He might well have asked for the Legion of Honor.By 1806 Grandet was fifty-seven, his wife thirty-six, and their only daughter ten. That year he inherited three fortunes—those of Madame de la Gaudiniere (née de la Bertelliere), old Monsieur de la Bertelliere, and Madame Gentillet—a hoard amassed by avarice. Old Monsieur de la Bertelliere prized his sight of gold above all. The inhabitants computed his savings by “the revenues of the sun’s wealth,” as they said.Grandet thus obtained a modern nobility. He farmed a hundred acres of vineyard yielding seven or eight hundred hogsheads in good years; he owned thirteen farms, an old abbey whose windows he had walled to save expense, one hundred and twenty-seven acres of meadow with three thousand poplars planted in 1793, and the house where he lived. This was his visible estate; as to other property, only two men could hazard a guess at its value: Monsieur Cruchot, a notary who managed Grandet’s usurious investments, and Monsieur des Grassins, Saumur’s richest banker, in whose profits Grandet had a secret share.Cruchot and des Grassins, discreet though obsequious, prompted townspeople to estimate Grandet’s wealth by their deference. No one doubted he kept a private treasure, a nightly hoard of louis. Avaricious observers found proof in his eyes, a yellow-metal gleam. The look of a man used to drawing enormous interest becomes furtive, eager, mysterious—an unspoken language of passion. Grandet inspired respect as one who owed no man: skilful cooper and experienced wine-grower, he could tell whether to make a thousand puncheons or five hundred; he never failed in speculation and always had casks when they were dear. He stored vintages and sold them slowly; his famous 1811 brought over two hundred and forty thousand francs.Financially, Grandet was part tiger, part boa-constrictor: lying in wait, springing on prey, swallowing masses of louis, then resting like a snake digesting—impassible, methodical, cold. Many felt the rending of those polished claws. For Cruchot he procured money at eleven per cent; for others des Grassins discounted bills at frightful deductions. Market talk daily mentioned Grandet. To some his fortune was a patriotic boast. Innkeepers and merchants told strangers: “We have two or three millionaire establishments; but as for Monsieur Grandet, he does not himself know how much he is worth.”In 1816 Saumur reckoned his landed property near four millions; having made about a hundred thousand francs a year from 1793 to 1817, it was fair to presume he held cash nearly equal to his estate. After a game of boston or vine-talk, townspeople said: “Le Père Grandet? Le Père Grandet must have at least five or six millions.”“You are cleverer than I; I have never been able to find out the amount,” Cruchot or des Grassins would answer when overheard.If Parisians spoke of Rothschild or Lafitte, Saumur folk compared them to Grandet; incredulous if anyone said a Parisian was richer. Such a fortune explained his actions. Early oddities once ridiculed had long ceased. His slightest movements, like a blink, became laws to the countryside where men studied him like a naturalist studies instinct.“It will be a hard winter,” said one; “Père Grandet has put on his fur gloves.”“Père Grandet is buying quantities of staves; there will be plenty of wine this year.”Grandet never bought bread or meat: his farmers supplied capons, chickens, eggs, butter, and his tithe of wheat. He owned a mill; the tenant took grain as rent and returned flour and bran. La Grande Nanon, his only servant, baked every Saturday. He arranged with gardeners and tenants for vegetables; he sold most fruits at market. Firewood came from hedgerows and half-rotten sheds whose planks farmers carted to town, cut, and stacked in his wood-house, receiving his thanks. His known expenses were consecrated bread, clothing for his wife and daughter, hire of their chairs in church, La Grande Nanon’s wages, tinning saucepans, lights, taxes, repairs, and industrial costs. He had six hundred acres of woodland recently bought, put under a neighbor’s care for a promised indemnity; after acquiring this, he ate game for the first time.Grandet’s manners were simple: he spoke little in short sententious phrases in a soft voice. Early he had stuttered when speaking at length; this incoherence, attributed to defects of education, was assumed and will be explained later. Four brief sentences sufficed him: “I don’t know; I cannot; I will not; I will see about it.” He never said yes or no nor committed himself to writing. If people talked, he listened coldly, chin in his right hand, right elbow on the left, forming opinions he never changed. He reflected long before any business agreement; when opponents confidently revealed plans, Grandet answered: “I can decide nothing without consulting my wife.” His wife, reduced to helpless slavery, was a useful screen in business. He went nowhere among friends; he neither gave nor accepted dinners; he made no stir, economizing even in motion. He never disturbed others’ things out of respect for property, yet at home coarser habits surfaced where self-control relaxed.Physically Grandet was five feet high, thick-set, square-built, with knotted knees and broad shoulders; his round face, pitted by small-pox, a straight chin, white teeth, and eyes with the calm, devouring expression of a basilisk. His forehead showed transverse wrinkles and prominent bumps; his yellow-gray hair was mockingly called silver and gold. His thick-ended nose bore a veined wen the common people said was full of malice. His countenance showed dangerous cunning, an integrity without warmth, and the egoism of a man used to concentrate every feeling on avarice’s enjoyments and upon the only human being who mattered to him—his daughter and sole heiress, Eugénie. Attitude, manners, bearing, all testified to the self-belief habit of success gives.Though outwardly unctuous, Grandet’s nature was iron. His dress had not changed since 1791: stout shoes tied with thongs, thick woollen stockings, short coarse maroon breeches with silver buckles, a yellow-and-puce velvet waistcoat, a large maroon coat, a black cravat, and a quaker’s hat. His gloves, thick as a gendarme’s, lasted twenty months; to preserve them he always laid them on his hat in one particular spot. Saumur knew no more.Only six persons had the right of entrance to Grandet’s house. The most important of the first three was a nephew of Monsieur Cruchot. Since his appointment president of the Civil courts of Saumur this young man had added Bonfons to Cruchot, signing himself C. de Bonfons. Any litigant who called him Monsieur Cruchot would soon feel the folly in court. The magistrate, thirty-three, owned Bonfons worth seven thousand francs a year; he expected to inherit his uncle the notary’s and another uncle Abbé Cruchot’s property. These three Cruchots, backed by cousins and allies, formed a party like the Medici.Madame des Grassins, mother of a son of twenty-three, came assiduously to play cards with Madame Grandet, hoping to marry her Adolphe to Mademoiselle Eugénie. Monsieur des Grassins furthered his wife’s schemes by services constantly rendered to the old miser. The des Grassins had adherents and cousins. On the Cruchot side the abbé—the family Talleyrand—backed by his brother the notary, contested every inch with his female adversary and tried to obtain the heiress for his nephew the president.This secret warfare for Eugénie’s hand kept Saumur agitated. Would Mademoiselle Eugénie marry Monsieur le président or Monsieur Adolphe des Grassins? Some said Monsieur Grandet would give his daughter to neither: eaten with ambition, he was said to seek a peer of France to whom an income of three hundred thousand francs would make the Grandet casks acceptable. Others replied the des Grassins were nobles and rich; Adolphe was personable; unless the old man had a papal nephew at his beck, such an alliance should satisfy a man who had come from nothing—whom Saumur remembered with an adze in his hand and the bonnet rouge. Certain wise heads noted Cruchot de Bonfons had the right of entry at all times whereas his rival was received only on Sundays; others trusted Madame des Grassins’ intimacy with the women. “It is diamond cut diamond,” said a Saumur wit.The oldest inhabitants declared the Grandets knew better than to let the property go out of the family, and that Mademoiselle Eugénie Grandet of Saumur would marry the son of Monsieur Grandet of Paris, a wealthy wholesale wine-merchant. To this the Cruchotines and Grassinists replied: the two brothers had seen each other only twice in thirty years; Monsieur Grandet of Paris, mayor and deputy and colonel of the National Guard, disowned the Saumur Grandets and meant to ally with some ducal family favored by Napoleon. In short, nothing was not said of the heiress, talked of for fifty miles and even on conveyances from Angers to Blois.At the beginning of 1811 the Cruchotines won an advantage. The estate of Froidfond, noted for its park, mansion, farms, streams, ponds, and forests and worth about three millions, was put up for sale by the young Marquis de Froidfond, who had to liquidate. Maître Cruchot, the president, and the abbé, aided by adherents, prevented sale in small lots. The notary concluded a bargain for the whole property, payable in gold, persuading the marquis that suits against purchasers of small lots would delay collection; it was better to sell the whole to Monsieur Grandet, solvent and able to pay in ready money. The marquisate of Froidfond was accordingly conveyed into Monsieur Grandet’s hands, who, to Saumur’s astonishment, paid for it under proper discount and formalities.This affair echoed from Nantes to Orléans. Monsieur Grandet took advantage of a cart returning by Froidfond to see his chateau. After casting a master’s eye over the property, he returned to Saumur, satisfied he had invested at five per cent, and seized by the stupendous thought of extending the marquisate by concentrating his property there. Then, to fill his coffers, now nearly empty, he resolved to thin his woods and sell the poplars in the meadows.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One now understands what is meant by “the house of Monsieur Grandet”: a cold, silent, pallid dwelling above the town, sheltered by ruined ramparts. The porte-cochere’s two pillars and arch were of tufa, that soft Loire stone that hardly lasts two centuries; irregular holes, weather-eaten, made the arch and walls look like vermiculated work, a sort of jail gateway. Above the arch, a crumbling bas-relief representing the four seasons was blackened; a projecting plinth had taken root with random growths—pellitory, bindweed, nettles, plantain, even a little cherry-tree.The archway door was solid oak, brown, shrunken, and split, held by symmetrical iron bolts; a small square grating with rust-red bars filled the middle panel and served as the knocker’s aperture. Through that grating—originally for recognizing friends in civil war—one could see, at the far end of the dark slimy vault, a few broken steps leading to a garden shut in by thick damp walls that nourished sickly herbage. These walls were the ruined ramparts under which stretched the gardens of several neighboring houses.The principal ground-floor room opened directly from the porte-cochere vault. In small towns of Anjou, Touraine, and Berry the hall is antechamber, salon, office, boudoir, and dining-room all in one; it is the theatre of domestic life. There the barber came twice a year to cut Monsieur Grandet’s hair; farmers, the curé, the under-prefect, and the miller’s boy came on business. The room, with two street windows, was entirely of wood: gray panels with ancient mouldings covered the walls; ceiling beams painted gray showed between whitewashed spaces yellowed with age. An old brass clock inlaid with arabesques adorned the mantel of the ill-cut white-stone chimney-piece; above hung a greenish mirror whose bevel reflected a thread of light along a gothic damascened steel frame. Two copper-gilt candelabra decorated the hearth corners; by removing their side branches each main stem served as a candlestick. Antique chairs were covered with La Fontaine tapestry, faded and darned.Closets or buffets surmounted by dirty shelves stood at the hall’s corners. An old marquetry card-table, its top a chessboard, stood between the windows; above it hung an oval barometer with a black border enlivened by gilt bands where flies had damaged the gilding. Opposite the chimney-piece were two pastel portraits said to represent Madame Grandet’s grandfather, old Monsieur de la Bertelliere, as a lieutenant in the French guard, and the deceased Madame Gentillet in shepherdess guise. The windows were draped with red gros de Tours curtains held back by silken cords with ecclesiastical tassels—luxuries little in keeping with Monsieur Grandet’s habits but included in the house’s purchase along with the steel pier-glass, the tapestries, and the rosewood buffets.By the window nearest the door stood a straw chair on castors to lift Madame Grandet high enough to see passers-by; a stained cherry-wood work-table filled the embrasure, and Eugenie Grandet’s little armchair stood beside it. For fifteen years the mother and daughter had lived a peaceful round: from April to November daytime work; on November 1 they took their winter station by the chimney. Grandet permitted a fire only from November 1 to March 31, whatever the weather; a foot-warmer filled with embers—the la Grande Nanon contrivance—saved mother and daughter from chilly mornings and evenings. They managed family linen and domestic tasks themselves; if Eugenie wished to embroider a collar she had to steal time from sleep and deceive her father for light. For a long time the miser dispensed tallow candles to his daughter and la Grande Nanon as he rationed bread and other necessities.La Grande Nanon was perhaps the only person capable of willingly accepting her master’s despotism; the town envied the Grandets for possessing her. Called “la Grande” for her five-foot-eight height, she had lived with Monsieur Grandet thirty-five years. Though paid only sixty francs a year, she was reputed among Saumur’s serving-women to be one of the richest: those sixty francs, accumulated over thirty-five years, had enabled her to invest four thousand francs in an annuity with Maître Cruchot. That result, the fruit of long, patient economy and hard slavery, seemed gigantic to others who envied her without seeing what had been sacrificed.At twenty-two she had been unable to find a situation because her face repelled most; robust, ready for labor, and forced to leave a farm that had burned down, she came to Saumur. Le Père Grandet, then set on marriage and establishing his household, noticed the girl. A cooper by trade and a good judge of strength, he appreciated the work to be had from a woman shaped like a Hercules—firm on her feet, strong in the hips, square in the back, with hands like a cartman and honesty as sound as her unblemished virtue. Her warts, brick-tinted skin, and ragged garments did not dismay him. He fed, shod, and clothed her, paid wages, and set her to work without treating her too roughly. Nanon, grateful and attached, wept and bound herself sincerely to him. From that day he ruled and worked her with feudal authority.Nanon did everything: she cooked, made lye, washed linen in the Loire and carried it home on her shoulders; she rose early and slept late; she prepared vine-dressers’ food during harvest, watched market-people, protected her master’s property like a faithful dog, and obeyed his absurd exactions without murmur. In the famous year 1811, when the grapes were gathered with unheard-of difficulty, Grandet resolved to give Nanon his old watch—the first present in twenty years of service. Though he turned over old shoes as gifts in other years, the watch was notable only because anything like a token so rarely passed his hands. Necessity had made the poor girl as niggardly as her master, and he had grown to love her as one loves a dog; Nanon accepted his spiked collar of control whose spikes no longer pricked. If Grandet cut bread with parsimony, she made no complaint; she gladly shared the household’s severe regime in which no one was ever ill. She laughed when Grandet laughed, felt gloomy or chilly with him, warmed herself and toiled as he did. Never did he need to find fault with her for pilfering grapes, plums, or nectarines. “Come, fall-to, Nanon!” he would say in generous years.To the peasant who had earned nothing but harsh treatment, Grandet’s ambiguous laugh was like a sunbeam. Nanon’s simple heart and narrow head could hold only one feeling and one idea: gratitude. For thirty-five years she never ceased to see herself before Monsieur Grandet’s wood-yard, ragged and barefoot, and to hear him say, “What do you want, young one?” Her gratitude was ever new. Sometimes Grandet, reflecting that she had never heard a flattering word and might one day appear before God more chaste than the Virgin Mary, would say, looking at her, “Poor Nanon!” The exclamation, always followed by an undefinable look from the old servant, became a chain of friendship nothing would part. Such compassion, arising in the miser and accepted gratefully by the old spinster, had something inconceivably horrible about it: this cruel pity was to Nanon the sum total of happiness.Many households in Saumur treated servants better but received less satisfaction in return. People asked what the Grandets had done to make Nanon so attached. Her kitchen, whose barred windows looked into the court, was always clean, neat, and cold—a true miser’s kitchen where nothing went to waste. When Nanon had washed dishes, locked up dinner remains, and put out her fire, she left the kitchen and went to spin hemp beside her masters. One tallow candle sufficed the family for the evening. The servant slept at the end of the passage in a closet lit only by a fan-light; her robust health enabled her to live in this hole, one ear always open like a watchdog.Other parts of the dwelling will be described as events require, but this sketch of the hall, where the household’s whole luxury appears, suggests the nakedness of the upper floors.In 1819, at the beginning of a mid-November evening, La Grande Nanon lit the fire for the first time that season. The autumn had been fine. That day was a fête well known to the Cruchotines and the Grassinists: the six antagonists, armed at all points, prepared to meet at the Grandets and outdo each other in testimonials of friendship. That morning all Saumur had seen Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet, accompanied by Nanon, on their way to Mass; people remembered the day as Mademoiselle Eugenie’s birthday. Calculating when the family dinner would end, Maître Cruchot, the Abbé Cruchot, and Monsieur C. de Bonfons hastened to arrive before the des Grassins and be first to pay compliments. All three brought enormous bouquets gathered in their greenhouses; the president’s stalks were ingeniously wound with a white satin ribbon fringed with gold.In the morning Monsieur Grandet, following his custom on Eugenie’s birth and fête days, went to her bedside and solemnly presented his paternal gift—which for thirteen years had been a curious gold-piece. Madame Grandet gave her a winter or summer dress as needed. These two dresses and the gold pieces, supplemented by two more on New Year’s and his fête, gave Eugenie a little revenue of about a hundred crowns, which Grandet loved to see her amass: was it not putting money from one strongbox to another, and training his heiress’s parsimony? He sometimes demanded an account of her treasure, formerly increased by the Bertelliere gifts, saying, “It is to be your marriage dozen.”The “marriage dozen” is an old custom still practiced in parts of central France: when a young girl marries, her family, or that of the husband, gives her a purse containing, according to means, twelve pieces, or twelve dozen, or twelve hundred pieces of gold. The poorest shepherd-girl never marries without her dozen, even if only coppers. They still tell of a “dozen” presented to a rich heiress containing one hundred and forty-four portugaises d’or. The custom anchors the idea that immediate trinkets and ceremonial largesse belong to marriage rites.During dinner, pleased to see Eugenie in a new gown, Grandet exclaimed, “As it is Eugenie’s birthday let us have a fire; it will be a good omen.”“Mademoiselle will be married this year, that’s certain,” said la Grande Nanon, carrying away the remains of the goose.“I don’t see anyone suitable for her in Saumur,” said Madame Grandet, glancing timidly at her husband and revealing the conjugal slavery under which she languished.Grandet looked at his daughter and said gaily, “She is twenty-three years old to-day; we must soon begin to think of it.” Eugenie and her mother exchanged a look of intelligence.Madame Grandet was a dry, thin woman, yellow as a quince, awkward and slow—one born to be trodden upon. She had big bones, a big nose, a big forehead, big eyes; her features suggested mealy fruits without savor. Her teeth were black and few; her mouth wrinkled; her chin long and pointed. Yet she was excellent—angelic sweetness, resignation as to suffering, rare piety, a good heart, and unalterable equanimity that made her universally pitied and respected. Her husband never gave her more than six francs at a time for personal expenses. Ridiculous as it seems, this woman—who by fortune and inheritances had brought Père Grandet more than three hundred thousand francs—felt so profoundly humiliated by dependence, and her gentleness prevented revolt, that she had never asked for a penny or remarked on deeds Maître Cruchot brought for her signature. This secret pride, this nobility of soul perpetually misunderstood and wounded by Grandet, governed her conduct.Madame Grandet habitually wore a greenish levantine silk gown, making it last nearly a year; a large white cotton kerchief, a bonnet of plaited straw, and almost always a black-silk apron. She seldom left the house and never asked anything for herself. Grandet, seized with occasional remorse when he remembered how long since he had given her the last six francs, would stipulate “the wife’s pin-money” when he sold his yearly vintage. The four or five louis presented by a buyer were the chief visible signs of Madame Grandet’s annual revenues. But after receiving those, her husband would often say, as though their purse were common, “Can you lend me a few sous?” and the poor woman, glad to help the man her confessor upheld as her lord and master, would return several crowns in winter out of the “pin-money.” When Grandet drew from his pocket the five-franc piece he allowed monthly for Eugenie’s small expenses—thread, needles, and toilet—he never failed to ask as he buttoned his breeches’ pocket, “And you, mother, do you want anything?”“My friend,” Madame Grandet would answer, moved by maternal dignity, “we will see about that later.” Wasted dignity—Grandet thought himself generous to his wife. Philosophers who meet the like of Nanon, Madame Grandet, and Eugenie may say irony lies at the bottom of Providence.After dinner, when Nanon fetched a bottle of black-currant ratafia from Monsieur Grandet’s bed-chamber and nearly fell on the stairs, Grandet scolded, then—because it was Eugenie’s birthday and Nanon had been frightened—offered her a little glass. Seeing her pale, he promised to mend the step that had given way. He took candle and tools and went to patch the worm-eaten staircase, whistling songs of his youth while the three Cruchots knocked.“Is it you, Monsieur Cruchot?” asked Nanon at the grating; she opened the door and the hearth’s light enabled the Cruchots to find their way into the room. Grandet, recognizing them, excused his haste: “I’ll be with you in a moment. I’m not proud; I am patching up a step on my staircase.” The president, always solemn, approved the sentiment in a phrase: “A man’s house is his castle.”The president, the Abbé Cruchot, and Maître Cruchot arrived with bouquets. The president, profiting by the darkness, took Eugenie by the elbows and kissed each side of her neck with complacency that made her blush. The abbé kissed her hand. Maître Cruchot showed officious familiarity. Grandet, entering, declared: “As this is Eugenie’s birthday let us illuminate,” and with the parsimonious ritual of his household he removed the candelabra branches, set two new tallow candles with paper, and seated himself, looking alternately at friends, his daughter, and the two candles.The Cruchots were pleased but uneasy, aware of rivalries. The des Grassins had not come when the group first assembled, but soon arrived: Madame des Grassins, a lively plump woman who kept her youth by provincial calm and virtuous habits; her husband, a former quartermaster of the Imperial guard, retired and swaggering; and their son Adolphe, tall, blond, pale and slight, who had spent sums in Paris beyond his allowance. They brought gifts: the father presented a rare Cape heather in a little box; Adolphe offered a workbox with silver-gilt utensils, monogrammed E.G. As Eugenie opened it, she felt unexpected delight, blushed and trembled, and looked to her father for permission. Monsieur Grandet authorized her to accept, the tone making the scene most theatrical: “Take it, my daughter.”The three Cruchots felt crushed as they saw Adolphe’s success with Eugenie: such riches, even in small personal gifts, were unheard-of to her. Monsieur des Grassins offered Grandet snuff, adjusted the Legion of Honor ribbon in his buttonhole, and glanced with a certain insolence at the Cruchots. Madame des Grassins eyed the blue vases holding the Cruchot bouquets with satirical interest. The abbé, joining Grandet at the window embrasure, muttered in the miser’s ear that their rivals “throw money out of the windows.” Grandet shrugged: “What does that matter if it gets into my cellar?” The abbé suggested that if Grandet wanted to give gilt scissors to his daughter he had the means; Grandet replied he would give something better.“Don’t stand on ceremony,” Madame des Grassins advised; “we will join you at cards.” Grandet suggested loto for all, the two young people included, and Nanon set the tables. Madame des Grassins succeeded in seating her son beside Eugenie. The party played, provided with numbered pasteboard and blue-glass counters, while each man’s thoughts pivoted on Monsieur Grandet’s supposed millions. The old cooper, inwardly self-conceited, regarded his guests—including Madame des Grassins’ pink feathers, the banker’s martial head, Adolphe, the president, and the notary—as useful harpoons to fish with. The trivial gaiety in the old gray room dimly lit by two tallow candles, the whirr of Nanon’s spinning-wheel, the sincere smiles only on Eugenie’s and her mother’s lips, the mingling of small domestic pleasures with important interests—all made the scene a melancholy comedy.It is a drama of all times and places: a young girl, like certain birds valued by others, is lured by proofs of friendship she does not understand; three pure simple hearts—Nanon’s, Eugenie’s, and her mother’s—are inspired by tender sentiments, while the modern god, money, dominates through Grandet’s figure. Tender sentiments are secondary here; money’s power is manifest in the miser’s face. Eugenie and her mother knew nothing of Grandet’s real wealth; they estimated life’s things by the small glimmer of their pale ideas, used to doing without, ignorant and therefore capable of a certain contentment. Their feelings, bruised though they did not realize it, were the spring of their existence and made them exceptions among lives purely material. Frightful condition of the human race! There is no joy that does not come from some species of ignorance.At the moment Madame Grandet had won a loto of sixteen sous—the largest ever pooled in that house—and while la Grande Nanon laughed as Madame pocketed her small winnings, the knocker resounded on the house-door so loudly the women jumped in their chairs.“There is no man in Saumur who would knock like that,” said the notary.“How can they bang in that way!” exclaimed Nanon; “do they want to break in the door?”“Who the devil is it?” cried Grandet.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Nanon took a candle and opened the door; Grandet followed. Madame Grandet, alarmed, ran to the room crying “Grandet! Grandet!” The loto‑players exchanged looks.“Suppose we all go?” said Monsieur des Grassins; “that knock strikes me as ill‑omened.”A young man entered, accompanied by a coach‑office porter with two trunks and a carpet‑bag. Grandet, sharp, ordered his wife back to the loto: “Madame Grandet, leave me to speak with monsieur.” He shut the door; the players paused.Speculation ran: “From Saumur?” “A traveller.” “Paris, no doubt,” said the notary. Eugénie noticed Nanon’s absence. Adolphe whispered that the newcomer might be M. Grandet’s son—Monsieur Charles—whom he had seen at a ball. When Grandet returned with the traveller, he strutted before his guests.“Sit near the fire,” Grandet said. The stranger saluted; the men inclined their heads, the women bowed. Madame Grandet tried to offer refreshment, but Grandet brusquely cut her off. The stranger thanked Eugénie—“Thank you, my cousin, but I dined at Tours”—and declined refreshment.“From Paris?” Madame des Grassins asked. The young man was Monsieur Charles Grandet of Paris. With an eye‑glass he inspected the table and company and impertinently ogled Madame des Grassins. The loto numbers were called languidly as the guests watched Charles and Eugénie with curiosity.Charles, twenty‑two and handsome, contrasted sharply with the provincial regulars. He was a thorough Parisian dandy: a full hunting costume, the finest gun, multiple waistcoats, Buisson coats, fine linen, gold trinkets—“implements of a man of fashion”—from a little whip to chased pistols. Though his father had warned against ostentation, Charles had taken a private coupe, had his hair refreshed at Tours, put on a black satin cravat and round collar, showed crossed cashmere waistcoats and a gold‑chain watch, gray trousers with black seams, gloves, and a cane with a gold knob. Only a Parisian could wear such foppery with a dashing air.The Cruchots and Grassins, shabby, snuff‑taking provincials, stared as if at a giraffe. Monsieur des Grassins and his son, more versed in fashion, mocked him with satirical looks. Grandet, absorbed in a long letter and holding the only candle at the card table, paid no heed. Eugénie, who had mended her father’s clothes and never seen such elegance, found Charles almost seraphic: she inhaled the scent of his curls, envied his soft gloves and refined features, and watched him as if he were an engraving come to life. His manners, eye‑glass, and disdainful glance enchanted her; for the first time she dreamed of a phoenix cousin.The game ended slowly. La Grande Nanon entered to ask for monsieur’s bed sheets; Madame Grandet followed. Madame des Grassins suggested they stop and keep their stakes. The guests gathered near the fire.Moved by a girl’s instinct, Eugénie slipped away to prepare her cousin’s room. She arranged a warming‑pan, covered a table, insisted on fresh linens and a good fire, and decorated the chimneypiece with a lacquer tray, a crystal goblet, a gilt spoon, and an antique flask. She even offered a five‑franc piece meant for expenses to buy a wax candle and a little sugar. Madame Grandet protested—“What will your father say?”—but Eugénie coaxed Nanon with the plea that it was her cousin’s birthday. Nanon laughed and obeyed, though alert to the master’s scrutiny.Meanwhile Madame des Grassins made overtures to Charles, flattering him into conversation and advising that Saumur could be pleasant if he avoided being shut up with the miserly uncle, the pious aunt, and the uneducated cousin. Monsieur des Grassins laughed; the notary, the president, and the abbé made discreet remarks. The Abbé Cruchot slyly suggested that Madame des Grassins should show Saumur’s honors to the visitor. Adolphe introduced himself—“I had the honor of dancing vis‑à‑vis with you at Baron de Nucingen’s ball”—which pleased Charles.Charles, unbuttoning his coat and striking a theatrical pose, seemed to think he might succeed in Saumur. The des Grassins and the Cruchots traded witticisms and innuendo: he ogled the salon’s beauties, Madame des Grassins considered him promising marriage material, the abbé hinted at coquetries and social duties, and Adolphe might have a rival.All the while Père Grandet’s attention flickered over a single letter he read by candlelight. The notary and the president, sensing his preoccupation, watched him. The letter, from Victor‑Ange‑Guillaume Grandet in Paris to his brother, announced a catastrophe: Victor declared he could not survive the disgrace of bankruptcy. Failures of his broker and notary had consumed his resources, leaving debts near four million francs and assets perhaps a quarter of that. The wine stock had fallen in value; in three days Paris would shout his dishonor. Unable to endure the infamy, he bewailed the ruin he left his adored son and, foreseeing his end, begged his elder brother to be a father to Charles. He entreated that his son be treated gently and provided means to enter trade or the Indies. Victor confessed he had nothing to leave and begged his brother to obtain from Charles a renunciation of any claim on his estate; if the brother refused, remorse would follow; if he accepted, a prayer would bless him. The letter closed with a blessing and a dying man’s trust.Grandet folded the letter, slipped it into his waistcoat, and masked his feelings behind humble airs and hidden calculation. Recovering composure, he brusquely asked his nephew if he had warmed. “Thoroughly, my dear uncle,” Charles answered. Grandet, already thinking of practicalities, told him Nanon would show his room: “It isn’t a dandy’s room; but you will excuse a poor wine‑grower who never has a penny to spare. Taxes swallow up everything.”The des Grassins rose to leave—“We do not wish to intrude, Grandet; you may want to talk to your nephew”—and offered parting bows. The notary fetched his lantern to accompany them. Madame des Grassins, who had not expected the evening to end early because her servant was absent, accepted the abbé’s arm at first but preferred her son; the abbé, indignant but playful, ended up walking with her husband’s party.“He is a good‑looking young man,” the abbé whispered to Madame des Grassins. “Good‑bye to the grapes—Eugénie will belong to the dandy. Unless this cousin is enamoured of some Parisian woman, your son Adolphe will find another rival—”“Not at all,” she answered coolly; she thought Eugénie plain and expected no danger. “This young man cannot fail to see that Eugénie is a little fool.”“Always place yourself beside Eugénie, madame, and you need never speak ill of her; the young man will make his own comparisons.”Madame des Grassins had already invited Charles to dine with her in two days. As the Cruchots and Grassins dispersed, they analyzed the evening with provincial acumen, sensing altered alliances. Usually rivals, they perceived the need for a temporary truce to oppose a common threat: they must hinder Eugénie from falling for her cousin and prevent the cousin from being taken in by opportunistic comparisons. They contemplated launching “treacherous insinuations, soft‑spoken calumnies, slanders full of faint praise and artless denials” to mislead him—an alliance of petty strategies to preserve their positions in Saumur society.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the four relatives were left alone, Monsieur Grandet told his nephew it was too late to discuss business and that they would settle things the next day. He outlined the household routine—breakfast at eight, a light midday repast, dinner at five—and refused any invitation to accompany Charles into town, blaming work. He shrugged off talk of wealth, calling himself practically penniless and still working like an apprentice. Nanon brought a candle; Madame Grandet offered any help Charles might need. Charles, polite and amused, accepted a faded yellow Anjou candle from Nanon, and Grandet led him through the passage beside the kitchen rather than the hall door, stopping at an inner swing-door against the north wind. Nanon bolted the outer door and loosed the wolf-dog, whose muffled bark warned strangers.Ascending worm-eaten stairs, Charles’s hopes sank as he saw smoke-stained walls and creaking steps. He mistook his aunt’s and cousin’s smiles for friendliness. At the landing he noticed three Etruscan-red doors; one above the kitchen was effectively walled off and only reachable through Grandet’s bedchamber. That room served as his office and strong-room: an iron-barred window, scales for weighing louis, hidden places for deeds and cash, and the solitary key in the miser’s hand. Grandet kept his accounts there like an alchemist guarding treasures; none, not even his wife, might enter.Eugenie’s door faced the walled room; Madame Grandet’s opened next to hers. Grandet gave Charles lodging in the attic above his own room so he could overhear him if need be. After brief goodnights, Grandet showed Charles the attic. Nanon had made a warming-pan fire, which the miser deemed unnecessary and scolded her for. Charles, surprised by the shabby comforts, unpacked in a room with moth-eaten valances, a cold stone fireplace, yellow-wood chairs, a simple night-table, and a thin carpet. He chatted easily with Nanon—who, astonished by his silk dressing-gown, joked it would make a fine altar-cloth and daydreamed of embroidery and damask—while Eugenie dreamed of love.That night Grandet brooded. He resented that his brother had left him this nephew and fretted that he had not even fifty francs to give the young man. He pictured the nephew’s finery as a burden. Madame Grandet, practiced in reading her husband’s moods, feigned indifference and kept quiet.At dawn Eugenie awoke with unfinished prayers and a new awareness. For the first time she wished to appear attractive: she brushed and arranged her hair, smoothed her stockings, and put on a new gown that pleased her. Sitting by the window she studied the court and narrow garden—a well twined with vine, neatly stacked wood, lichened paving, disjointed steps, a rotten trellis, two stunted apple-trees, gravel walks, box-edged beds, and a terrace of old walls leading to lindens and raspberry-bushes, with a drooping walnut near the miser’s sanctum. Under the autumn sun the thawed frost and falling leaves stirred a tenderness in her; she feared she was too plain for Charles, though her modest beauty had an unassuming nobility she did not fully see.Flushed, Eugenie went to ask Nanon for cream and a galette for her cousin’s breakfast. Nanon laughed at the plan, fretting about supplies and Grandet’s stinginess, and playfully scolded her. Grandet came down amid the fuss and a discussion about provisions followed: he insisted that if flour and butter were used, all baking must be done in one oven to save wood. Nanon, in a small rebellion, persuaded him to spare enough for a galette by promising to manage the fires. She admired Charles’s fine boots and linen with wonder and envy; Grandet grumbled that some people put more money into boots than into the men who wore them.Grandet then took Eugenie for a morning walk to his fields by the Loire, accompanied by Maitre Cruchot, the notary, who knew Grandet’s eye for profit. On the riverbank, where thirty men were clearing poplars, Grandet explained his calculations—how removing poplars reclaimed hay and profit—and showed where to plant cheaper poplars on poor land to save nourishment. Cruchot marveled at his knack for turning trees into tens of thousands of francs.When Cruchot mentioned the talk in Saumur about the dandy nephew from Paris and the need for a marriage-contract, Grandet declared bluntly that he would rather fling his daughter into the Loire than give her to that cousin. The cold refusal struck Eugenie like a blow: her budding hopes, tied to Charles since the previous evening, withered. She returned trembling, the light drained from the street. At the house, Cruchot showed Grandet a newspaper: Monsieur Grandet of Paris had shot himself after being ruined by his broker and notary. The notary feared the Saumur Grandet had earlier refused aid; Grandet merely said he had known, and that the son—Charles—didn’t yet know. Cruchot departed, understanding the reserve.At breakfast, Madame Grandet and Eugenie sat knitting. Nanon reported Charles still asleep. Grandet, cold and harsh, announced the suicide: “His father has blown his brains out.” Eugenie wept at the thought of the poor young man; Nanon sympathized, but Grandet scoffed, saying the young man wasn’t worth a sou. He ordered that no one speak to Charles of the news until he returned at noon; he intended to talk with the nephew about his affairs then and warned Eugenie that Charles would be sent off to the Indies and she would never see him again.Alone, Eugenie flung herself upon her mother, gasping that she was suffocating. The two exchanged a brief, intuitive tenderness. Madame Grandet, aware of her daughter’s emotion, counseled restraint, asking whether Eugenie already loved him; Eugenie protested that liking and the desire to help were not wrong. The women silently conspired to make Charles’s breakfast special. Eugenie pleaded for cream and strong coffee; Nanon volunteered to run and buy coffee, fearful of being seen by Grandet. She arranged the table: grapes from the attic, a pyramid of pears, vine leaves for presentation, listening at Charles’s door to his sleeping breaths. Nanon returned with two fresh eggs she’d begged from a farmer; Eugenie embraced her in gratitude.Thus the household prepared in small furtive joys—Nanon’s schemes for paltry luxuries, the mother’s silent understanding, Eugenie’s innocent coquetry—while Grandet’s economy and dominant will cast a shadow. The possible marriage, the news from Paris, and Grandet’s firm determination to keep his daughter’s fortune from Charles intertwine, kindling inner turmoil: Eugenie’s tender awakening and anguish, Grandet’s miserly calculations and contempt, and the private locked world of the strong-room where gold and papers are jealously guarded. Each character’s habits and moods set the stage for conflicts to come, with love and pity on one side and avarice and pride on the other.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After two hours of anxious preparation—rising often to watch the coffee and listen for her cousin—Eugenie set a modest breakfast, a departure from their usual midday meal of bread, fruit, a little butter, and wine. She fretted that her father might return before Charles had eaten and checked the clock repeatedly. “Don’t be troubled, Eugenie; if your father comes in, I will take it all upon myself,” Madame Grandet soothed, and Eugenie wept with sudden gratitude and love for her mother.Charles arrived about eleven, dressed with Parisian elegance, smiling and graceful; his presence quickened Eugenie’s heart. He joked about the ruin of his Anjou estate and sank into an easy-chair while the women—provincial, habit-bound—talked of church walks and haymakers. Accustomed to luxury, Charles admired small comforts like fresh eggs, butter, and a pretty watch; the women were tender with him, and he returned their kindness with caressing looks that stirred Eugenie, who blushed when she handled his ring.Charles teased with vanity and good humor. He laughed at their boiled coffee in a plain pot and suggested a Chaptal coffee-pot, which Nanon dismissed as impractical when there were cows to feed. Eugenie offered to make it, earning a reproachful glance from her father even in his absence. As the three women grew silent with apprehension—each expecting Grandet’s return—the familiar knock came and Monsieur Grandet entered. His sharp eye took in the spread and the guest; with dry sarcasm he accused them of feasting.“Ha! ha! so you have been making a feast for your nephew; very good!” he said, then, taking a glass, cut bread, spread butter from his own knife, and ate standing. He noticed the sugar and demanded to know its source; Nanon said it was bought from Fessard. Charles, tasting bitter coffee, looked for sugar, but Grandet had already pocketed it.
