Believing his father’s affairs settled by his uncle, he imagined himself anchored in the Faubourg Saint‑Germain—reappearing as the Comte d’Aubrion, like the Dreux reappearing in Bréze. Dazzled by the Restoration’s prosperity and fascinated by aristocratic splendor, his intoxication, begun on the brig, increased after he reached Paris, and he resolved to follow the course his would‑be mother‑in‑law indicated. His cousin counted for no more than a speck in this brilliant prospect; still, he went to see Annette, who advised him to marry and promised her support in his ambitious projects. Annette was delighted to fasten an ugly, uninteresting girl on Charles, whose West Indies life had made him very attractive: bronzed complexion, bold, decisive manners like a man used to sharp decisions, to rule, and to succeed. Charles breathed easier in Paris, conscious of his part to play.Des Grassins, hearing of his return, approaching marriage, and fortune, came to see him and inquired about the three hundred thousand francs still required to settle his father’s debts. He found Grandet conferring with a goldsmith about jewels for Mademoiselle d’Aubrion’s corbeille. Charles had brought magnificent diamonds; the setting with plate and jewelry for the new establishment amounted to over two hundred thousand francs. When des Grassins, who had already called several times, presented himself, Charles treated him with the impertinence of a young man of fashion conscious of having killed four men in as many duels in the Indies. Coldly, and without fully grasping the plea, Charles replied:“My father’s affairs are not mine. I am much obliged, monsieur, for the trouble you have been good enough to take—by which, however, I really cannot profit. I have not earned two millions by the sweat of my brow to fling them at the head of my father’s creditors.”“But suppose that your father’s estate were within a few days to be declared bankrupt?”“Monsieur, in a few days I shall be called the Comte d’Aubrion; you will understand, therefore, that what you threaten is of no consequence to me. Besides, you know as well as I do that when a man has an income of a hundred thousand francs his father has never failed.” So saying, he edged Monsieur des Grassins to the door.At the beginning of August that year Eugénie sat on the little wooden bench where her cousin had sworn to love her eternally, where she usually breakfasted when the weather was fine. The poor girl was happy for the moment in the fresh summer air, letting memory recall the great and little events of her love and the catastrophes that followed. The sun had just reached the angle of the ruined wall full of chinks that no one, by the mistress’s caprice, was allowed to touch, though Cornoiller often told his wife it would fall and crush somebody one day. At that moment the postman knocked and gave a letter to Madame Cornoiller, who ran into the garden, crying:“Mademoiselle, a letter!” She gave it to Eugénie, adding, “Is it the one you expected?”The words rang in Eugénie’s heart. She turned pale and held the letter for a moment. She trembled and could not break the seal. La Grande Nanon stood before her, hands on her hips, her joy showing in the lines of her face.“Read it, mademoiselle!”“Ah, Nanon, why did he return to Paris? He went from Saumur.”“Read it, and you’ll find out.”Eugénie opened the letter with trembling fingers. A cheque on the house of “Madame des Grassins and Coret, of Saumur,” fluttered out. Nanon picked it up.The letter ran that Charles had met with success, returned rich, and had followed his uncle’s advice; the deaths of his aunt and uncle left room for his succession. He remembered their youthful vows—the little wooden seat, the passage, the attic chamber—and acknowledged that these recollections had sustained him. Yet, having travelled and matured, he declared that an alliance had been proposed which satisfied his idea of matrimony. Love in marriage was a delusion; society and its conventions must be obeyed. He admitted he did not love Mademoiselle d’Aubrion, but his present income—he named it eighty thousand francs—enabled him to marry into the Aubrion family, securing a title, a place as gentleman‑of‑the‑bedchamber, and a brilliant position. Such a match would give his children a social rank and prospects—an entailed estate of forty thousand francs a year would make any State position attainable. “We owe ourselves to our children,” he wrote.Charles professed to be frank: he would not deceive Eugénie. He offered her the choice; his conscience bound him to his word, yet if she, whom he called his friend, wished him to renounce social ambitions, he would willingly accept the pure and simple happiness she represented. The postscript enclosed a cheque on des Grassins’s bank for eight thousand francs, payable in gold, which included capital and interest of the sum she had lent him. He expected a case from Bordeaux containing a few things he begged her to allow him to offer as a mark of gratitude, and he told her she could send his dressing‑case by the diligence to the hôtel d’Aubrion.“By the diligence!” said Eugénie. “A thing for which I would have laid down my life!”Terrible and utter disaster! The ship that had borne all her hopes had gone down, leaving not a spar, not a plank, on a vast ocean of expectation. Some women, abandoned, will try to tear a lover from a rival, commit crimes, or rush to the ends of the earth. Others bow their heads and suffer in silence; they go their way dying, resigned, weeping, forgiving, praying, and recollecting until the last breath. This was Eugénie’s love after she read that dreadful letter. She raised her eyes to heaven, thinking of her dying mother’s last words, who, with the prescience of death, had looked into the future: Eugénie, remembering that prophetic life, measured her destiny. Nothing was left for her; she could only unfold her wings, stretch upward, and live in prayer until deliverance.“My mother was right,” she said, weeping. “Suffer—and die!”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Eugenie returned slowly from the garden, avoiding the corridor where her cousin’s presence still lingered in traces of his morning cup and Sèvres sugar-bowl. The day promised to be grave: Nanon reported that the parish curate, allied with the Cruchots and Monsieur de Bonfons, had come to speak of marriage. The old abbé had urged him to admonish Mademoiselle Grandet about the duty of marriage for a woman of her station. Eugenie, preoccupied with her own grief, thought he had come about the thousand francs she gave monthly to the poor and told Nanon to fetch the alms; the curate only smiled.He explained that his subject was a poor girl of Saumur who neglected her Christian duties through lack of charity to herself. Eugenie, overwhelmed, declared her intent to renounce the world and live for God in silence and seclusion. The curate counseled caution: marriage is life, the veil is death; to take the veil was to turn inward selfishly and to abandon social obligations. He reminded her that she supported many poor families, that her fortune was a trust to be preserved, and that a husband could help manage affairs and protect her property from lawsuits. She loved God too well not to find salvation within the world she adorned and owed example to.At that moment Madame des Grassins arrived, burning with spite and the news she could not resist. She revealed, by a letter from her husband, that Charles Grandet had returned from the Indies, had been in Paris a month, and was on the verge of marrying Mademoiselle d’Aubrion—until the Marquis d’Aubrion, learning of Guillaume Grandet’s liabilities, threatened to refuse his daughter unless the uncle’s creditors were paid. Des Grassins intended to declare Guillaume bankrupt and to explain his position to the creditors, though he professed respect for Eugenie and postponed action until she had spoken. Eugenie, stung, read enough, then coldly returned the letter.When pressed for fees owed by Charles, Madame des Grassins paid. Turning to the curate, Eugenie, with the composure of her father’s stiff manner, put a wrenching question: would it be a sin to remain a virgin after marriage? The curate evaded specifics and deferred to theological texts. She retreated to her father’s private room and spent the day alone, not appearing at dinner. In the evening she joined the assembled company, feigning calm under the weight of scandal; the town knew of Charles’s return and treachery. She smiled beneath her pain and met condolences with courtesy. As the game broke up late, she interrupted Monsieur de Bonfons’s departure.Against provincial convention, she made an astonishing proposal. To the astonishment of the assembly and with equal parts irony and resolve, Eugenie offered her hand to the president—Monsieur de Bonfons—on strict terms. She demanded that he swear to leave her free in life, to claim none of the usual marital rights over her, and to accept that in her heart she could only give friendship to a husband. He might have the use of her hand and fortune only on this condition: that he undertake, at once, to go to Paris, learn the names of her uncle’s creditors, convene them, and pay every claim in full with interest at five percent from the date of each debt to the present day, obtaining legal receipts before a notary. Only then should he present the discharged claims to her cousin Charles. In proof of her earnestness she produced a certificate of Bank of France shares worth fifteen hundred thousand francs as immediate funds. The president, moved, vowed to obey.When he left for Paris, Eugenie broke down and wept; with that act she severed her earlier attachment for Charles. The president reached Paris and, with des Grassins, summoned the creditors—none failed to appear. In a remarkable act of justice for Parisian commerce, he paid each claim in full with interest; the payments were legally registered and des Grassins received a fee for his services. Having secured the acquittances, the president sought out Charles at the hôtel d’Aubrion, where the young man had just quarrelled with his prospective father-in-law over marrying the Marquis’s daughter given the cloud on the family’s finances. The president handed Charles a letter from Eugenie.In it Eugenie told her cousin that she appreciated his judgment and that, though she could not give him the pleasures of the world or participate in its calculations, she could not marry him while his father’s affairs remained unsettled. She ended the letter with a dignified farewell, saying he would always have her faithful friendship but no more. Charles, surprised that she had been so generous and disciplined with her fortune, was informed by the president that Eugenie had recently lost two millions—she had been said to possess about nineteen millions four days earlier but now had seventeen millions—and that the couple would have combined income enough for a comfortable position: an income of seven hundred and fifty thousand francs at marriage.Charles tried to recover composure and speak of mutual enrichment; the president, smiling, hinted at the diminished but still enormous fortune. A dressing-case the president delivered to Charles might have contained a ring or trinket; Charles, enraged by the president’s apparent mockery, vowed to offend him. The president left, and three days later returned to Saumur to announce his marriage to Eugenie. In due course he rose rapidly: six months after the marriage he was appointed councillor in the Cour royale at Angers, then president of the superior courts, and he aimed for a seat in the Chamber of Deputies and even for a peerage. Provincials spoke sardonically of his kinship to royalty; la Grande Nanon remarked to Eugenie in awe that “the king will be his cousin.”Nevertheless, Monsieur de Bonfons’s ambitions were only partially realized. Though he finally dropped his patronymic Cruchot and signaled social ascent, he died eight days after being elected deputy of Saumur. The text suggests Providence punished him for the sordid calculations and legal cunning with which he had drawn his marriage contract: it provided that husband and wife, if childless, would give all their property to each other without inventory, thereby enabling a husband to inherit all. This clause, and the manner of the contract, explain the president’s deferential treatment of his wife; he had respected her passion and solitude because that restraint protected his claim. Women called him considerate and delicate; they pitied him, and some imputed to Eugenie a passionate grief that they discreetly criticized.Eugenie, possessing the intuitive acuity of an isolated soul formed by suffering and meditation, had understood that the president secretly desired her death to obtain the fortune he had calculated would accrue to the union through inheritances from his uncles. But she pitied him. Providence, the narrative suggests, punished his calculative selfishness by cutting him down just as he seemed to have achieved his ambitions; had he lived, his deference and the contractual clauses might have been used to his material advantage. To Eugenie, gold remained indifferent. She longed for heaven, lived piously and did good in secret, and never wearied of charity.Widowed at thirty-six, Eugenie retained her beauty tempered by solemnity: a white, placid face, gentle voice, and simple manners. She combined the saintliness of a woman untouched by worldly contamination with the rigid bearing of an old maid and the petty, provincial habits of her upbringing. Despite her vast means she continued to live simply; the house’s hearth was only lit by rules set in her youth, and she dressed modestly. Her household seemed parsimonious, yet she disarmed accusation through noble uses of her wealth: she supported pious and charitable institutions, endowed a hospital for old age, founded Christian schools, and endowed a public library. The churches of Saumur benefited greatly from her gifts. Though sometimes ironically called “mademoiselle,” Madame de Bonfons inspired a reverential respect. Her large-hearted benefactions redeemed the narrowness of her education and the petty habits of her early life, even as money’s cold calculations had taught her a wary distrust of emotion.“I have none but you to love me,” she confided to Nanon, revealing the loneliness beneath her public beneficence. She healed secret wounds in many families, and she departed for heaven attended by a train of benefactions. Eugenie remained, in the narrator’s words, in the world but not of it: created by nature to be supremely wife and mother, she had neither husband, children, nor family. Later, rumor briefly suggested she might marry again; Saumur whispered of the Marquis de Froidfond and of renewed social sieges like those once led by the Cruchots. Yet those rumors were unfounded. Nanon and Cornoiller, ignorant of worldly corruption, were incapable of the machinations and compromises that had touched Eugenie’s life.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:Chaulieu, Eleonore, Duchesse de — Letters of Two BridesGrandet, Victor-Ange-Guillaume — The Firm of NucingenGrandet, Charles — The Firm of NucingenKeller, Francois — Domestic Peace; Cesar Birotteau; The Government Clerks; The Member for ArcisLupeaulx, Clement Chardin des — The Muse of the Department; A Bachelor’s Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; The Government Clerks; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Ursule MirouetNathan, Madame Raoul — The Muse of the Department; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; The Government Clerks; The Member for ArcisRoguin — Cesar Birotteau; Eugenie Grandet; A Bachelor’s Establishment; The Vendetta############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Middle of Story — Continued############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY ################################################################################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################There is a particular hour between day and night that artists adore: twilight blurs edges, softens contrasts, and invites a dreamy mood in which light and shade converse. Silence deepens contemplation; the artist, stepping back from a nearly finished canvas, sees meanings flash before the inner eye. In that hour, material art seems to lose its matter—figures speak and walk, shadow is shadow, light is day; flesh breathes, eyes move, and fabrics change sheen. The imagination magnifies every realistic detail and finds beauty alone. Illusion rules, summoning a mind’s night filled with fancies that lift the soul away from the mundane.One such evening a young, gifted painter—who saw art as Art itself—worked on a large painting from a step-ladder, rapt with self-criticism and admiration. His reverie lasted until night had fallen. Whether he meant to step down or misjudged his footing, the ladder tipped; he fell, struck his head on a footstool, and lay unconscious.A sweet voice roused him. When he opened his eyes a bright light hurt them; through a mist he heard two women whisper and felt two timid hands supporting his head. He regained consciousness to an old Argand lamp’s light and saw the most charming face he had ever seen: a young woman epitomizing the ideal beauty his imagination had fashioned—delicate as Prudhon, poetically sentimental like Girodet, with fresh temples, regular brows, virginal purity. She was slight, graceful, simply dressed.Gratitude and admiration came fumbling to his lips. A handkerchief pressed his forehead; the ether used to revive him left a pungent odor. An older woman, bearing a marquise’s air, held the lamp and instructed the younger. The girl explained they had heard the crash and, alarmed by an ensuing silence, entered the studio through a latched door to find him lying motionless. The mother had fetched what was needed to bathe and revive him; he had cut his forehead but felt better. The older woman immediately advised leeches or bloodletting the next day—falls could be dangerous.The girl's glances at him and at the pictures were discreet, proper, almost absent-minded; her eyes showed the natural interest women take in suffering. The two strangers were more occupied with his safety than his work, and they left without pressing questions or brazenness, looking back with anxious kindness. Their noble, simple manners made a deep impression on him later when memory pulled at details.On the landing, the old lady gently chided her daughter for leaving the door open; the painter joked that she had left it “to hear me fall.” The girl blushed. They offered to accompany him downstairs because the stairs were dark; he declined, and they stayed by to light his way.To appreciate the scene’s effect on the painter, one must know his circumstances. A few days earlier Hippolyte Schinner had set up a studio in the attics of that very house, in a dim, muddy quarter near the Madeleine and his rooms on the Champs-Élysées. Fame had recently showered him with success: he had become one of the country’s most acclaimed artists and was beginning to feel free from want—“enjoying his last privations,” as he put it. No longer willing to waste time or endure long walks to cheaper studios, he had taken the attic.Hippolyte Schinner was a man who inspired fascination without revealing his secrets. He was the idol of a mother who had sacrificed all for him. Mademoiselle Schinner, an Alsatian farmer’s daughter, had never married. Once betrayed by a wealthy suitor who treated her without delicacy, she renounced the world, refused alms from him, and made a nobility of her shame. She devoted herself to maternal love, saved a treasure for her son, and her sacrifices were rewarded: at the last exhibition Hippolyte received the Legion of Honor; newspapers hailed a genius, artists acknowledged a master, and dealers festooned his canvases with gold.At twenty-five he understood his position. He lived to restore to his mother the pleasures she had renounced, hoping fame and fortune would make her happy and respected. He chose his friends among the honorable and distinguished, fastidious in selection because he wanted to elevate the status his talent had given him. Early solitude had left him with beliefs and an adolescent soul open to shy, poetic emotions that worldliness calls foolish but which were sincere in their singleness.Schinner’s manners were gentle and polite; his voice, from the heart, stirred noble sentiments and bespoke modesty. Those who met him felt a moral attraction they could not name. These traits explain why, when the porter’s wife returned with a coach, he did not press her for gossip about the two women who had tended him. He replied to her chatter but could not stanch her curiosity.She said they were likely Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, who had lived in the building four years. No one knew their means: in the mornings an old half-deaf woman came to help; in the evenings two or three elderly gentlemen with ribbon loops—visitors—stayed late. One man came in a carriage with servants and reportedly had sixty thousand francs a year. They lived quietly and economically, paying promptly. Their names did not match: the mother and daughter bore different surnames. The girl dressed smartly for walks in the Tuileries and attracted many suitors whom she turned away; yet gossip flourished.After the coach came and went, and after his mother tended his wound and forbade his studio return for a few days, Hippolyte’s idle hours drew his memory back to the fainting scene. Adelaide’s profile returned vividly, the mother’s faded features, the gentle touch—gestures, tones, and attitudes rose from deep waters. Once he could work again, he went early to the studio but truly pressed to visit his neighbors. Passion makes strange promises; he mounted to the fourth floor, heart quick, and stood before Mademoiselle Leseigneur’s humble brown door, imagining shared misfortunes of birth and feeling a kinship.No painter of manners has sufficiently portrayed certain Parisian privacies: elegant outward toilettes and brilliant women whose homes betray ambiguous comfort. The house belonged to a proprietor who hated repairs and decoration, regarding property purely as business. Chimneys might fall and he would forgo theatre nights for “repairs.” Hippolyte, having seen similar poverty in his mother’s home, was not shocked by dark greasy paint, oily stains, and other signs of neglect—these marks of scarcity held a kind of poetry for an artist.Mademoiselle Leseigneur opened the door, bowed with Parisian tact, and, with discreet pride, turned to shut a glass partition that screened a cluttered back room—linen over irons, an old camp bed, charcoal, a filter, crockery—domestic utensils. Muslin curtains hid the disorder; windows faced a neighboring yard. With a quick eye he took in uses, furniture, and the general condition. The front half of the main room, an ante-room and dining-room, wore old salmon-rose paper with a patched flock border. Prints of Alexander’s battles, gilding rubbed, adorned the walls. A heavy, timeworn mahogany table stood amid chairs whose carved mahogany and red morocco seats, gilt nails and all, were scarred like veterans.The room presented a museum of inconsistency: luxury and penury mingled. A finely finished telescope hung near discolored glass; a painted sideboard pretended to be mahogany. Shabby rugs and worn furniture had a hard cleanliness that accentuated their oldness. An indescribable odor mingled with stairwell smells despite a partly open window. Dust stirred through curtains carefully drawn to conceal a window bay where former tenants had left attempted ornaments—a domestic fresco of faded taste.Adelaide opened the inner room and announced the painter with evident pleasure. Hippolyte, who had known similar poverty at home, entered with sympathy. Madame Leseigneur rose from an antique armchair to offer him a seat. He thanked them for their care and especially the young girl who had heard him fall. Adelaide lit an Argand lamp and placed it on the chimney shelf, sitting slightly behind him to view him while appearing to adjust the lamp; the tactic embarrassed both.While speaking with Madame Leseigneur, Hippolyte scanned the room. Egyptian iron fire-dogs were scarcely visible under cinders before a sham log; an old Aubusson carpet, faded and mended, left cold tiles exposed. Walls imitated figured silk in reddish paper; opposite the windows a crack and the paper’s outline suggested double doors closing a recess where Madame Leseigneur likely slept, disguised by a sofa. Above a mahogany chest hung a dim portrait of an officer, painted abroad perhaps; window curtains of red silk matched the faded worsted work of chairs. On the chest’s marble sat a malachite tray with a dozen magnificently painted coffee cups—perhaps Sèvres. On the chimney shelf stood an Empire clock: a warrior in a quadriga whose wheel marked the hours. Tall candlesticks held smoke-yellowed tapers; porcelain vases crowned with dusty artificial flowers stood in corners.In the middle of the room a card-table with new packs posed a dilemma: either these women were impeccably honest, or they earned a living through intrigue and gambling. Yet Adelaide’s pure manner and noble bearing inclined Schinner to believe in their innocence, to attribute the furniture’s incoherence to legitimate, if strained, means.“Make a little fire and bring me my shawl,” the mother asked. Adelaide wrapped her in a cashmere shawl, faded and darned, and revived the fire with wood from the lumber-room.The subsequent conversation is hard to reproduce exactly. With tact born of early hardship, Hippolyte avoided indiscreet pity. The two women began by talking about painting; women readily dissolve first-call awkwardness by questioning an artist. Light talk gave Hippolyte room to speak, reveal his habits and mind. The mother’s face bore the traces of trouble—prominent features, an outline suggesting shrewdness softened by grace—an expression that might be artifice or delicacy. Female faces conceal mysteries: frankness can border on duplicity. The line between intrigue and heart is inscrutable; only intuition discerns.At Schinner’s age the heart’s first impulse is to believe in goodness. Studying Adelaide’s noble brow and soulful eyes, he breathed the modest fragrance of virtue. He seized the moment to discuss portraits and examine a faded pastel whose chalk had flaked away.“You are attached to that picture for the likeness,” he said. “The drawing is poor.”“It was done at Calcutta, in great haste,” the mother answered, her voice agitated. She stared at the formless sketch with the absorption of someone remembering past happiness; regret left its mark on her face. Schinner read in her gaze the trace of a once-happy life and sat beside her.“Madame,” he said, “the pastel’s colors will soon disappear; the portrait will only survive in your memory. Allow me to reproduce the likeness on canvas. It would be more permanent—and a pleasure to me. Artists sometimes welcome a simpler task as a respite from larger works.”The old lady flushed; Adelaide shot a glance of deep feeling. Schinner sought a tie with his neighbors that would allow him into their life. His offer appealed to affection and his pride as an artist, and seemed inoffensive. Madame Leseigneur accepted with composed dignity, aware of the delicate obligations such favors create.“You are right,” he added. “The uniform looks naval.”“Yes,” she replied. “That was the uniform of a captain in command—Monsieur de Rouville—my husband. He died at Batavia from a wound received while fighting an English ship off the Asiatic coast. He commanded a frigate of fifty-six guns; the Revenge carried ninety-six. The struggle was unequal, but he defended his ship bravely, held out till nightfall and escaped. When I returned to France, Bonaparte was not yet in power, and I was refused a pension. When I applied again, lately, I was told that if the Baron de Rouville had emigrated I should not have lost him; that by now he would have been a rear-admiral; his Excellency quoted degrees of forfeiture. I dislike holding out my hand as a beggar for a grief that silences a woman. I resent this monetary valuation of irreparable blood—”“Dear mother, this subject always harms you,” Adelaide murmured. The Baronne bowed and fell silent.At that moment Adelaide observed, with a touch of reproach and innocence, that she had supposed a painter’s work to be quiet—reminding Schinner of his earlier crash. A carriage arriving at the door interrupted them. Adelaide went to fetch tall gilt candlesticks with partly burnt candles, lit them, and without waiting for the bell opened the outer door, setting down the lamp. A kiss given and taken reached Hippolyte’s ear and touched him. He was impatient to see the man who had kissed Adelaide; conversation in undertones delayed the reveal.At last Mademoiselle de Rouville returned, followed by two men whose dress and countenances demanded description. The first, about sixty, wore a coat styled for Louis XVIII—neither purely civil nor military—with fleurs-de-lis embroidered on the lapels and gilt buttons. His powdered hair showed no hat’s pressure; he might be taken for fifty, robust, with a frank, loyal émigré expression tinged with libertine gallantry—the reckless air of a Musketeer. His gestures and manner betrayed no intention of renouncing royalism, religion, or love affairs; he suggested the unrepentant world of the émigré aristocrat.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A striking figure stood behind the portrait of “Louis XIV’s light infantry”—the Bonapartists’ nickname for those old Monarchy survivors. He should have been the picture’s principal subject, yet he was merely an accessory: a lean, dry man dressed like the first but seeming his reflection—an older coat, less white powder, dimmer fleurs-de-lis, feebler intellect, life nearer its end. Rivarol had called him “the moonlight” of the other. The painter found this speechless Chevalier mysterious. He spoke to no one and was spoken to by none. Friend, relation, servant, protector—his role was obscure. At the Baronne de Rouville’s he provoked curiosity without satisfying it. Who, after the Restoration, could recall an attachment that before the Revolution had bound him to the dead woman who had been his friend’s wife?The leader of the group, least dilapidated, gallantly kissed Madame de Rouville’s hand and sat by her. The Chevalier bowed and stood a couple of chairs away. Adélaïde stood behind the old gentleman’s armchair, leaning her elbows on the back. Though the old gentleman’s familiarity was fatherly, it seemed to annoy the girl. “What, are you sulky with me?” he asked, then shot Schinner a shrewd glance asking, “Is he one of us?”“This is our neighbor,” said the Baronne, indicating Hippolyte. “Monsieur is a celebrated painter; despite your indifference to the arts, you must have heard his name.”The old man saw the suppressed name and bowed. “Certainly. I heard much about his pictures at the last Salon. Talent has immense privileges,” he added, noting the artist’s red ribbon. “That distinction, which we must earn at the cost of blood and long service, you win in youth; but all glory is of the same kind,” he said, touching his Cross of Saint-Louis.Hippolyte murmured thanks and fell silent, content to admire Adélaïde. Her face gleamed in his mind; he forgot the misery of the room. He answered briefly, inwardly divided between what he heard and what he felt—hope pouring out wild visions of happiness. He refused to analyze the evening’s small incidents.After a while the Baroness and daughter began to play cards with the old gentleman. The Chevalier stood behind, watching the game and responding with nods and repeated gestures. The gentleman complained of bad luck; Adélaïde sweetly offered advice. At last the game ended. The old man flung two louis on the table in temper: “Forty francs—the exact sum. Deuce take it! It is eleven o’clock.”“It is eleven o’clock,” repeated the silent figure, looking at the painter.Hippolyte thought it time to go. He found a few commonplace remarks, took leave, and left wholly possessed by the first raptures of true love, without analyzing the evening.The next day he longed to see Adélaïde again. He waited until the afternoon. Blushing, he asked Mademoiselle Leseigneur for the Baron’s portrait. “But come in,” Adélaïde said; she had heard him. Tongue-tied, he felt happiness dull his wit. To see Adélaïde, to hear her skirt’s rustle after longing all morning, made trifles precious. Adélaïde brought a chair to reach the picture; finding she could not reach without stepping on a chest, she blushed and asked, “Will you get it down?”He read her modesty, returned a look of mutual intelligence—love’s sweetest language. Adélaïde cast down her eyes. Hippolyte, almost timid, took down the picture, examined it, and left saying only, “I will return it soon.” The brief contact set long ripples in both hearts.Back in his studio he prepared a fresh canvas and, with ardor, worked until dinner. That evening he returned, stayed from nine till eleven, and, a little bolder, chatted with Adélaïde. A week passed and their feelings slowly deepened into mutual understanding. Each day Adélaïde’s greeting grew more intimate; they laughed and talked with the innocence of newly made friends. Schinner learned piquet poorly and lost often. Without a word of love they knew they were everything to each other: Hippolyte enjoyed his power over his gentle friend; Adélaïde, timid and devoted, permitted many concessions. Familiarity with the old Count waned: Adélaïde perceived the painter’s melancholy when the old man unceremoniously kissed her hand or throat.Mademoiselle Leseigneur began to expect accounts of the painter’s visits and scolded him for absences; he ceased other friendships and lived chiefly in the small apartment that enchanted him. Adélaïde’s jealousy flared when she heard he still visited Paris salons at eleven; she declared such life bad for his health and argued that a man who spent charms on several women could not love warmly. Driven by passion and Adélaïde’s demands, the painter lived almost exclusively with them.Never was love purer. Both were trusting and delicate; their passion grew without sacrifice. Two months after meeting, their lives were nearly one: Adélaïde could tell by his footsteps that he was home; he always looked in when he returned to his mother and flew back at evening. He thought the ideal she dreamed of was realized.The old gentleman came less often; Hippolyte, once jealous, had taken his place at the green table and shared his constant ill-luck. Yet sometimes, in happiness, Madame de Rouville’s desperate poverty haunted him. “Strange—twenty francs every evening?” he wondered, and dared not vocalize his odious suspicions.He spent two months on Adélaïde’s portrait; when finished, varnished, and framed, he considered it one of his best. Madame de Rouville had not mentioned it again. Was it indifference or pride? He would not analyze her silence. Joyfully plotting with Adélaïde, he planned to hang it while the Baroness was out. One day, while her mother walked in the Tuileries, Adélaïde came to his studio to see the portrait in its light. She stood speechless in rapt admiration. He leaned forward uneasily; she held out her hand, two tears fell, and he kissed it. They stood mute, longing to confess love but not daring. Overcome, they went downstairs and hung the picture. Hippolyte dined with the Baroness, who, in tears, embraced him.That evening the old émigré, the Baron de Rouville’s comrade, visited to announce a promotion to vice-admiral; his land travels through Germany and Russia had been counted as naval campaigns. Seeing the portrait, he shook Hippolyte’s hand and exclaimed he would give five hundred pistoles to see himself as well depicted. The Baroness, grateful, looked at Hippolyte. He suspected the admiral meant to buy both portraits; his pride and jealousy were offended, and he replied sharply that if he were a portrait-painter he would not have done this one. The admiral bit his lip and sat down to cards.Hippolyte stayed near Adélaïde, who proposed a dozen hands of piquet. As he played he noticed an unexpected eagerness in Madame de Rouville to win and finger the old man’s gold. Evil suspicions troubled him: could the Baroness live by gambling? Was she losing to pay debt or rent? Why would so rich a man frequent a fourth-story room where he lost some forty francs each evening? Why had he been familiar with Adélaïde and then given up any claim?These reflections led him to watch the old man and the Baroness more closely. Their sidelong glances at Adélaïde displeased him. “Am I being duped?” became his last, horrible thought. He resolved to stay after the two old men left to confirm or dispel his fears. He drew out his purse to pay and, carried away by thought, laid it on the table and fell into reverie; then, ashamed, he rose, answered a commonplace question, and went close to the Baroness to look at her as she talked.He stepped out, racked by doubts. A few steps down, he turned back to fetch the forgotten purse. “I left my purse here!” he cried to Adélaïde.“No,” she answered, reddening.“I thought it was there,” he said, pointing to the card-table. Not finding it, shamed for Adélaïde and the Baroness, he felt in his waistcoat and said, “I must have made a mistake.”The purse held fifteen louis d’or and change. The theft seemed flagrant and was denied; Hippolyte no longer doubted his neighbors’ morals. He stood on the stairs, legs shaking, dizzy, unable to walk, struggling with his destroyed hopes. He recalled trifling observations that now confirmed his suspicions. Had they waited until the portrait was given them? Perhaps the Baroness had used gambling to hide her need. He even remembered Adélaïde’s curiosity about a worn silk netting—had she been watching his purse? He briefly fantasized other motives—maybe the admiral hoped to marry Adélaïde—but rejected them: if she intended to marry him, they would not have robbed Hippolyte.Clinging to illusion, he told himself the purse must have fallen, then searched his memory and saw it distinctly on the green cloth. Yet he excused Adélaïde, thinking misfortune might explain a degrading action; perhaps some secret misfortune forced them. He refused to accept the proud face as a lie.The next morning he had not slept. The loss of the future they might have shared made deep inroads. To lose a dreamed future is keener than losing real happiness. Thus Hippolyte wandered under the Tuileries’ shade, absorbed and forgetful.There he met François Souchet, a schoolmate about to go to Italy. “Why, Hippolyte, what ails you?” the sculptor asked.“I am most unhappy,” Hippolyte admitted.“Only a love affair can make you so,” Souchet replied. Led into confidences, Hippolyte told him of the Rue de Suresnes and the young girl on the fourth floor. Souchet laughed and recalled what others knew: the mother’s appearance gave away her station. Jokes and mockery followed; Hippolyte’s reticence made him suffer as his secret was handled lightly. He pretended contradiction, but the jesting continued. “Come at four and study their walk,” Souchet advised.Torn, Hippolyte left his friends, convinced Adélaïde must be superior to such accusations and filled with remorse for suspecting her. His love was such that, despite the theft, he still worshiped her. He thought of playing the rescuer of a woman deserted by others—romantic and grand to his sensitive soul.He sat in his studio, looking at the portrait without working, tears in his eyes. Night came; roused, he went downstairs, met the admiral, looked darkly, and fled. He had planned to see the ladies, but the admiral’s presence froze him. For the hundredth time he wondered what interested this prodigal, who had eighty thousand francs a year, in a fourth-story room where he lost forty francs nightly; motives multiplied in his mind.In the next days Hippolyte threw himself into work to distract himself; study consoled him though memories of tender hours persisted. One evening, leaving his studio, he saw the ladies’ door half open and Adélaïde standing in the window recess. The door and staircase made it impossible not to be seen. He bowed coldly with a look of supreme indifference; yet thinking of the pain this coldness would cause, he shuddered. The week of disdain would have been terrible; perhaps the purse had been found and she had been seeking him. This hope filled him with remorse: perhaps Adélaïde deserved an inquiry and at least justification. Ashamed of resisting his heart, he called that evening on Madame de Rouville.All his suspicions vanished at the sight of Adélaïde, pale and thin. “Good heavens! what is the matter?” he asked.Adélaïde gave him a look of deep melancholy that pained him. “You must have thought us indifferent?” said the Baroness.“I was wrong,” he replied. “There are forms of pain we know not how to confide.”“The sincerity and strength of friendship are not to be measured by time,” the Baroness observed sadly.Hippolyte, blushing with shame, ascribed the loss to an accident. The evening was delicious; Adélaïde could read his remorse, and though he could not confess his doubts, he came back more in love and seeking tacit forgiveness. Yet when the Baroness suggested a game—“Let us have our little game; my old friend Kergarouet will not let me off”—his fears revived. He sat at the table; Adélaïde said he needed a partner.Throughout the game the Baroness and her daughter exchanged looks that alarmed Hippolyte more because he was winning. At last a final hand left the lovers in the old lady’s debt. Reaching for money, he saw a purse Adélaïde had slipped before him without his noticing; the poor child held the old one in her hand and, to keep her countenance, was looking into it for the money to pay her mother. The sight made blood rush to his heart; he was near fainting.The substituted purse contained his fifteen gold louis. It was worked with gilt beads; rings and tassels showed Adélaïde’s taste and the small hoard she had spent on it. It was an impossible, delicate way to show him that his gift could only be repaid by proof of affection.Overcome, Hippolyte clasped Adélaïde, pressed her to his heart, and kissed her; then, with an artist’s simple heartiness, he exclaimed, “I ask for her for my wife!” looking to the Baroness.Adélaïde looked half-wrothful; Madame de Rouville, somewhat astonished, considered her reply when a bell rang. The old vice-admiral entered, followed by his shadow and Madame Schinner. Alarmed by calumnies against Adélaïde, Hippolyte’s mother had inquired and reported them to the admiral, who swore to punish the scoundrels.Prompted by his wrath, the admiral explained his intentional losing at cards as his method to help the Baroness. After Madame Schinner paid her respects, the Baroness looked at Comte de Kergarouet, the Chevalier du Halga, Hippolyte, and Adélaïde, and said warmly, “So we are a family party this evening.”PARIS, May 1832ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.- Bixiou, Jean-Jacques — A Bachelor’s Establishment; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; The Firm of Nucingen; The Muse of the Department; Cousin Betty; The Member for Arcis; Beatrix; A Man of Business; Gaudissart II.; And yet sorrows should be spoken of to a young girl idolized as you are, for the day may come when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them. It is difficult, Anna, to find in our manners and morals a subject worthy of your eyes; no choice has been left me; but perhaps you will feel how fortunate your fate is when you read the story sent to you by                                                    Your old friend,                                                         De Balzac.I. THE LORRAINSAt dawn one October day in 1827 a sixteen-year-old youth in humble clothes stood in a small square of Lower Provins. The river mills already whirred; the echo from the Upper Town made the place so still that even a distant diligence could be heard. Two long sides of the square, divided by an avenue of lindens, were built in a plain bourgeois style. Few trades opened onto it; fronts were shaded by vines or roses climbing to second-story windows. One end opened toward the main street and river gardens; the other led to a quieter parallel street.At that quieter end the youth recognized the house he sought: a white stone front grooved like courses, closed gray blinds, slender iron balconies with yellow-painted rosettes. Three dormer windows pierced a slate roof; a new weather-vane of a hunter shooting a hare topped the center. Three stone steps led to the door. A leaden pipe discharged sink-water into a gutter at one side, showing the kitchen; two shuttered windows with heart-shaped openings indicated the dining-room. Vent-holes to the cellar had painted iron shutters cut in open-work. Everything looked recently repaired, suggesting the small taste and self-satisfaction of a retired petty shopkeeper.The young man inspected these homely details with pleasure tinged by sadness; his eyes traveled from kitchen to roof. A calico curtain in one garret window caught the rosy light of the rising sun. He stepped back, leaned against a linden, and sang in the drawling tone of western France a Breton ditty often sung to newly married couples:“We’ve come to wish you happiness in marriage,  To m’sieur your husband  As well as to you:You have just been bound, madam’ la mariée,  With bonds of gold  That only death unbinds:You will go no more to balls or gay assemblies;  You must stay at home  While we shall go.Have you thought how you are pledged to be  True to your spouse,  And love him like yourself?Receive these flowers our hands do now present you;—  Alas! your fleeting honors  Will fade as they.”This tender, melancholy air would awaken a Breton maiden’s memories. As he finished the first couplet the singer, never taking his eyes from the attic curtain, saw no sign of life. While he sang the second couplet the curtain stirred. At “Receive these flowers,” a face appeared; a white hand cautiously opened the casement, and a girl made a sign with her head as he closed with the melancholy thought, “Alas! your fleeting honors will fade as they.”To her the young workman suddenly produced a yellow flower, common in Brittany—the furze, or broom.“Is it really you, Brigaut?” she said in a low voice.“Yes, Pierrette, yes. I am in Paris. I have started to make my way; but I’m ready to settle here, near you.”A first-floor window beneath Pierrette’s attic creaked. The girl showed terror and whispered: “Run away!”The lad leaped like a frightened frog to a bend in the street where a mill projected as the square opens into the main thoroughfare; but despite his agility his hob-nailed shoes echoed on the stones, easily distinguished from the mill’s music, and no doubt heard by the person who had opened the window.That person was an old maid. No man would have torn himself from a morning nap to listen to a minstrel in a jacket; only a maid wakes to songs of love. She opened her blinds with the furtive motion of a bat, looked about, saw nothing, and only heard the flying footfalls. In her neglected state—no false front or collarette, a little black silk bag covering a bony skull, pushed-aside night-cap—she had a harsh, angular face with deep wrinkles; the sight of her at a window was abrupt and forbidding. She owned the house with her brother, who slept so soundly that nothing disturbed him.The old maid stretched her neck from the window, raised cold pale-blue eyes toward the attic, tried to see Pierrette, and, finding it useless, drew back like a tortoise hiding its head. The blinds were then closed, and the street resumed its silence, broken only by peasants coming in or other early risers.When there is an old maid in a house, watch-dogs are unnecessary; not the slightest event escapes her observation or comment. This trifling incident was therefore destined to excite grave suppositions and to open the way for one of those obscure family dramas—none the less terrible because secret.Pierrette did not go back to bed. Brigaut’s arrival was an immense event to her. During the night—Eden of the wretched—she escaped the fault-findings that weighed on her by day. Like the hero of a ballad, her sleep seemed her only happy life; waking hours were a bad dream. She had her only pleasurable waking in three years. The memories of her childhood sang their melodious ditties in her soul. The first couplet had come in a dream; the second made her spring from bed; at the third she doubted her ears; but when the fourth was sung, standing in her night-gown with bare feet at the window, she recognized Brigaut, the companion of her childhood.Yes—there was the well-known square jacket with bobtails, the printed cotton waistcoat, the linen shirt fastened by a gold heart, the large collar, the earrings, stout shoes, and trousers of blue-gray drilling unevenly colored—the humble durable things of Breton peasantry. The big white-horn buttons that fastened the jacket made the girl’s heart beat. When she saw the bunch of broom her eyes filled with tears; then a dreadful fear drove back the budding happiness. She thought her cousin sleeping in the room below might have heard the noise she made in jumping to the window. The fear was just; the old maid had appeared, and Pierrette made Brigaut the terrified sign which he obeyed without understanding it. Such instinctive submission to a girl’s bidding reveals one of those innocent and absolute affections that blossom now and then in life. Whoever had seen the lad as he ran away would have loved the ingenuous chivalry of his feeling.Jacques Brigaut was worthy of Pierrette Lorrain, who was just fifteen. Two children! Pierrette could not help crying as she watched his flight in the terror her gesture had conveyed. Then she sat in a shabby armchair before a small table topped by a mirror. Resting her elbows, head in hands, she thought for an hour, calling to memory the Marais, the village of Pen-Hoel, perilous pond voyages in a boat untied from an old willow by little Jacques; the faces of her grandfather and grandmother, her mother’s sufferings, Major Brigaut’s handsome face—in short her careless childhood. It was a dream, a luminous joy on the gloomy present.Her beautiful chestnut hair escaped in disorder from her cap, rumpled in sleep—a cambric cap she had made. Little ringlets escaped from gray curl-papers at each side of her forehead. From the back hung a heavy braid half unplaited. Her whiteness betrayed the malady of girlhood, chlorosis, which deprives the body of color, destroys appetite, and disorders the organism. Waxy tones marked her flesh. The neck and shoulders betrayed wasted arms, flung forward and crossed upon the table. Her feet seemed enervated, the night-gown barely reached her knees, showing flaccid muscles, blue veins, and impoverished legs. The cold, which she heeded not, turned her lips violet; a sad smile drew up the corners of a sensitive mouth that showed small, ivory-white teeth. Her ears were dainty, and despite the roundness of her face she was lovely. All animation was in the eyes: the iris brown like Spanish tobacco with black flecks and golden reflections round brilliant pupils. Pierrette was made to be gay but was sad. Her lost gaiety still showed in the vivacious eye, the ingenuous grace of her brow, the smooth curve of her chin. The long eyelashes lay upon cheek-bones made prominent by suffering. Paleness rendered the lines and details purely distinct. The ear alone was a little masterpiece of modeling.Perhaps you would like to know her history, and this is it.Pierrette’s mother was a Demoiselle Auffray of Provins, half-sister by the father to Madame Rogron, mother of the present owners of the house.Monsieur Auffray had married at eighteen and again near sixty-nine. By his first marriage he had an only, plain daughter, who married at sixteen to innkeeper Rogron; by his second he had a charming daughter, so that Madame Rogron was fifty when the half-sister was born and already had two grown children.The youngest daughter married at eighteen a man of her choice, a Breton officer named Lorrain, captain in the Imperial Guard. The captain, anxious to rise to a colonelcy, exchanged into a line regiment. While he, then a major, and his wife enjoyed themselves in Paris on the allowance of Monsieur and Madame Auffray, or scoured Germany, old Auffray (formerly a grocer) died at eighty-eight without making a will. His property was administered by his daughter, Madame Rogron, and her husband so completely in their interests that nothing remained for the widow beyond the house she lived in on the little square and a few acres. This widow, the mother of Madame Lorrain, was thirty-eight at her husband’s death. Like many widows, she unwisely remarried. She sold the house and land to her step-daughter, Madame Rogron, and married a young physician named Neraud, who wasted her fortune. She died two years later of grief and misery.Thus the share of her father’s property that should have come to Madame Lorrain almost entirely disappeared, reduced to a small sum of eight thousand francs. Major Lorrain was killed at the battle of Montereau, leaving his wife, then twenty-one, with a little daughter of fourteen months and only a pension and an eventual inheritance from his parents, Monsieur and Madame Lorrain, retail shopkeepers at Pen-Hoel in the Marais. These Lorrains, Pierrette’s grandparents, sold wood, slates, tiles, pipes. Their business, from incapacity or ill-luck, did poorly and gave them scarcely enough. The failure of Collinet at Nantes, caused by 1814 events and a fall in colonial products, deprived them of twenty-four thousand francs they had deposited with that house.The arrival of their daughter-in-law was welcome. Her pension of eight hundred francs was a handsome income at Pen-Hoel. The eight thousand francs which the widow’s half-brother and sister Rogron sent her from her father’s estate were placed in the Lorrains’ business; they gave her a mortgage on a little house they owned in Nantes, let for three hundred francs and barely worth ten thousand.Madame Lorrain the younger, Pierrette’s mother, died in 1819. The child was small, delicate, and weakly; the damp climate of the Marais did not agree with her. But the husband’s family persuaded her, to keep her with them, that in no other place could she find a healthier region. She was so petted that her death brought nothing but honor to the old Lorrains.Some declared that Brigaut, an old Vendeen who had served under Charette and other leaders in the wars against the Republic, counted for much in the younger Madame Lorrain’s willingness to remain in the Marais. All Pen-Hoel saw him—called respectfully Major Brigaut—spending his days and evenings in the Lorrains’ parlor beside the window of the imperial major. Toward the last, the curate of Pen-Hoel urged old Madame Lorrain to have her daughter-in-law marry Brigaut, promising to have the major appointed justice of the peace through the Vicomte de Kergarouet’s influence. The young woman’s death ended the matter.Pierrette was left with her grandparents, who owed her four hundred francs a year, interest on the little property placed in their hands. This small sum supported her. Growing less fit, they soon faced an active competitor who ruined them. With his connivance, the Lorrains borrowed on notes they could not meet, driving them into bankruptcy. Pierrette’s claim on the house in Nantes was superseded by her grandmother’s legal rights, enforced to secure daily bread. The house sold for nine thousand five hundred francs, of which one thousand five hundred went for costs. The remaining eight thousand came to Madame Lorrain, who lived on their income in a humble place at Nantes called Saint-Jacques, where the two old people had bed and board for a small payment.As it was impossible to keep Pierrette with them, the old Lorrains wrote to her uncle and aunt Rogron in Provins. These Rogrons were dead. The letter might easily have been lost, but the post persistently traced it to Monsieur Rogron, son of the deceased, a mercer in the rue Saint-Denis in Paris; curiosity about a possible inheritance ensured its transmission, and the Treasury its fee.These Rogrons, toward whom the old Lorrains, though dreading to part with their dear granddaughter, stretched their supplicating hands, became unexpectedly the masters of Pierrette’s destiny. It is therefore indispensable to explain both their antecedents and their character.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Pere Rogron, the innkeeper of Provins who had married old Auffray’s daughter by his first wife, was a coarse, gaudy figure: inflamed face, veiny nose, cheeks stained by Bacchic redness, short, fat, pot-bellied, with stout legs and thick hands. He had the shrewdness of a provincial innkeeper and liked good living and being waited on by pretty girls. Neither he nor his wife was handsome; both suited one another. Rogron belonged to the blunt, egotistical sort whose vices were displayed without concealment. Grasping, selfish, indecent, and given to gratifying whims, he squandered earnings until his teeth failed; what remained was a miserly selfishness. In old age he sold his inn, gathered what he could of his late father-in-law Auffray’s property, and moved into the shabby house on the square of Provins bought cheaply from the widow of old Auffray, Pierrette’s grandmother.The Rogrons’ income included about two thousand francs a year from twenty-seven local lots and twenty thousand francs from selling the inn. The Auffray house, though dilapidated, became their residence. Rogron turned to horticulture, expanding a garden to the river’s edge, building a stone embankment and cultivating aquatic flora.Early in their marriage the Rogrons had a son and a daughter, both unattractive—victims, like many, of a coarse upbringing. Sent cheaply to nurses who worked in the fields, the children returned from a rough provincial infancy with coarsened features and harsh voices. Their mother’s vanity was mortified, her sternness producing no effect; the father’s severity made that sternness seem mild by comparison. The children were often left to run loose in stables, courtyards, or streets, sometimes whipped, sometimes sent to their dour grandfather Auffray. The Rogrons defended their appropriation of most of Auffray’s property by accusing him of injustice to his grandchildren, yet they themselves had been harsh.Rogron ensured the children had basic training: he sent his son to school, bought a cartman to save him from conscription, and apprenticed his daughter Sylvie in Paris at thirteen with six hundred francs for board. Two years later their son Jérôme-Denis followed. When asked what he meant to do with them, Rogron brashly declared his intention to kick them into independence once they understood life, boasting that his father had kicked him three times whereas he would give only one kick and more money—an avowal of cutting parental responsibility to its financial minimum.Sylvie, apprenticed in the rue Saint-Denis, rose quickly. By nineteen she was earning four hundred francs; at twenty she was second demoiselle at the Maison Julliard, wholesale silk dealers; by twenty-one she was forewoman at a thousand francs. Jérôme began in the Maison Guepin; at eighteen he was head-clerk earning twelve hundred francs. The siblings socialized cheaply on Sundays and holidays. Around the end of 1815 they pooled savings of about twenty thousand francs to buy the property and good-will of the “Family Sister,” a large retail establishment in the quarter. Sylvie kept the books and correspondence, Jérôme managed the shop. By 1821 fierce competition had consumed much of their capital; it cost them everything to maintain the business and its reputation.Though scarcely forty, Sylvie’s natural ugliness, relentless work, and a severe expression aged her. Jérôme, looking silliest to customers, had a retreating forehead, close-cut grizzled hair, a dull, lifeless expression, and bluish eyes lacking spark. Short and fat like his father, he retained a flabby pallor typical of those confined to back-shops and counting-rooms, forever tying bundles, making change, snarling at clerks, and repeating phrases to customers.Their limited intellect had been entirely absorbed in keeping their business going and learning the trade: thread, needles, ribbons, pins, buttons, and haberdashery took up everything. Outside the shop they knew nothing of Paris beyond the Rue Saint-Denis; the shop was their universe. They were adept at scolding clerks and young women and assigning blame. Their pleasure was the hum of a busy counter; when Parisians strolled unconcerned with buying, the day was “bad weather for sales.”Rogron’s mechanical skill at tying parcels and keeping an eye on the shop made him admired by apprentices; he could fold and tie and spot everything that passed. Yet without his sister he would have been helpless. Sylvie possessed common sense and a trade genius; she advised purchases, dispatched him to remote French towns to save a trifle, and drove the business’s speculation. Necessity to support the business animated their relentless activity. Rogron understood only his clerkly tasks; he had no grasp of the business as a whole and often recoiled when Sylvie ordered goods sold below cost to clear worn fashions. He neither reasoned well nor ill, simply accepted his sister’s authority—partly because she was older—and the isolation of a life devoted to mere needs had left him clownish and vacuous. Sylvie had discouraged his marrying, preferring to keep him dependent and avoid the expense and disturbance a wife might bring.Silliness in such men is talkative. Rogron chattered constantly to clerks with trivial shop jokes and had developed a meaningless shop phraseology, explaining manufacturing details to customers who knew nothing, attaining a petty superiority through this babble. The Rogrons, baptized by mistake into shopkeeping, lacked heart-feelings; their natures were shriveled and harsh from toil, privation, and long apprenticeships. They were not implacable but impractical and unsympathetic toward misfortune. To them, virtue, honor, and loyalty reduced to the payment of bills. Irritable, stingy, and given to petty economies, they bore a dreadful reputation among rue Saint-Denis merchants. Their connection to Provins—returning three or four times a year and occasionally closing the shop—kept them afloat by attracting apprentices from the provinces, whom Rogron sent, praising his son’s success, to families hoping to start children in Paris. When clerks or young women escaped, rejoicing at freedom increased the Rogrons’ bad name; new victims were supplied yearly by the indefatigable father.From fifteen, Sylvie had two faces: the amiable, wheedling seller who smiled and charmed customers and the sour spinster peering from half-open blinds whose real expression was stern and forbidding. It was while they were in mourning for their father that the Lorrains’ letter reached them. Their father’s death left them the house in Provins—acquired virtually by their father from Pierrette’s grandmother—and other lands and moneys gained through usurious loans and mortgages on peasantry. The yearly taking of stock was over; the “Family Sister” had been paid for. The Rogrons owned about sixty thousand francs of merchandise, forty thousand in bank or cash, and the goodwill and value of their business. Sitting behind their private counter they debated what to do next.Like many retail shopkeepers, they aspired to become bourgeois. Selling the good-will could yield over a hundred and fifty thousand francs; placing remaining assets in public funds would bring each about four thousand francs a year, and proceeds could repair and improve their Provins house. They could then live comfortably in Provins. They eyed their forewoman—the daughter of a rich farmer at Donnemarie who had lost seven of nine children over five years—as a possible successor; Rogron had tried to make her his wife but failed. Sylvie opposed such a match but intended to make the woman their successor in the shop.The internal life of such shopkeepers is opaque: they live on hope—dreams of municipal honors, country-houses with plaster statues and trick fountains, distinctions in the National Guard. Provins nourished such fanatical longings in the Rogrons. Provins, described with affectionate exaggeration, is a sudden town appearing on a hill after a gray wheat plain, watered by two rivers, with a charming valley, ramparts, mills, inns, shops, retired merchants, diligences, and a sentiment of rooted attachment among those who leave to make fortunes. The Rogrons’ imaginations were captivated. As Jérôme piled buttons or rolled ribbons he pictured the upper town, ravines, rivers, and the little square below—the house he would renovate with new front, bedrooms, salon, billiard-room, dining-room, kitchen garden turned English pleasure-ground with lawns, grottos, fountains, and statuary. He coveted upholsterers’ windows and draperies for his imagined Provins house. Though their Paris rooms were sparsely furnished, in fancy they already owned better furniture. Sylvie, pen in hand, envisioned receiving the bourgeoisie of Provins, admiring herself in salon mirrors, and selecting caps. The rue Saint-Denis atmosphere and market mud made them long for Provins roses; the promised valley became a monomania, intensified by the grind of mercer’s business.It was amid these meditations that the Lorrains’ letter came. The Rogrons knew little of their cousin Pierrette Lorrain; their father had acquired the Auffray property after they left home and had spoken little about the family. A brief genealogical discussion revealed Aunt Lorrain to be the younger sister of their own mother by their grandfather Auffray’s second marriage—a second marriage the Rogrons believed had wronged them by dividing Auffray’s property. With this suspicion, they considered the Lorrains’ request to take in the orphan Pierrette through a self-interested lens. Would taking charge of a cousin commit them to obligations? Might she become a legal heir if she remained unmarried? Could they send her away if displeased? Would they be forced to marry her into their family? If Jérôme found a wife among Provins heiresses, property should be preserved for his children; Sylvie’s ideal “yoke-mate” was a stupid, rich, ugly girl who could be governed. They decided to refuse taking Pierrette in. Sylvie volunteered to write the answer but delayed, busy with urgent business; when the forewoman offered to buy their stock and good-will—a sale the Rogrons accepted—the matter dropped from the old maid’s mind.Sylvie and her brother left for Provins four years before Brigaut’s arrival that would touch Pierrette’s life; their subsequent doings there, born of the same commercial, narrow antecedents, are essential to the story. Provins, with its charm and the Rogrons’ bourgeois ambitions, proved destined to be no less fateful for Pierrette than the Rogrons’ Parisian commercial upbringing had prepared them to be.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the petty shopkeeper returns from Paris to the provinces, he brings back echoes of the capital that soon fade, leaving only superficial marks where Paris briefly touched the town. The Rogrons, brother and sister, were seized by such an impulse: having saved, they spent on remodeling their old house into something they imagined charming.Their fixed idea, endlessly discussed with the contractor, produced the façade and interior Brigaut examined: elaborate plans, handsome furnishings, and a parade of choicer objects meant to rival Provins’ notables. “If Monsieur Garceland has it, put it in,” Mademoiselle Rogron pronounced; they fretted over details, balked at extra costs yet surrendered to comparisons—“The Lesourds had theirs painted last year”—aiming to match the town’s leading families.For a year they watched workmen, answered townspeople’s questions, and tried to insinuate themselves into Provins society. In Paris they had known no one; in Provins their former masters had returned to prominence: Monsieur and Madame Julliard of the “Chinese Worm,” the Guepin clan now keeping the “Three Distaffs,” and Madame Guenee, from whom the Rogrons had bought the “Family Sister.” These families intermarried and dominated municipal life—Garceland the mayor a Guepin son-in-law; Abbé Peroux, the curate, brother to Madame Julliard; Monsieur Tiphaine junior, a judge, brother to Madame Guenee.The queen of Provins was the beautiful, clever, ambitious Madame Mélanie Tiphaine junior, who, removed from a convent to marry, had become the social manager of the town. She hosted twice-weekly salons, cultivated every vanity to forward her husband’s career, and aimed to have him elected deputy and later a judge in Paris. Her circle—joined by Madame Guenee, the Julliards, and others—formed the town’s ruling court. Julliard junior, secretly enamored of her, launched the “Bee-hive,” a journal of family contributions and local notices. The few aristocrats held a separate salon at the Comtesse de Breautey’s.The Rogrons were received, after careful study, first by Madame Julliard the elder and Madame Garceland, then—after difficulty—by Madame Tiphaine. Society winnows by wealth, education, manners, accomplishments, and character; the Guepins, Guenees, and Julliards stood higher than the Rogrons, whose father had been held in contempt over the Auffray property—a fact familiar to notary Auffray.At Madame Tiphaine’s salon everyone understood one another; the Rogrons hoped admission by the mayor would suffice. Sylvie practiced boston; Rogron, unable to play, twirled his thumbs and spoke only of his new house. He choked on words and made a comic show of embarrassment. Sylvie betrayed herself at cards—sharp, irritable, whining when she lost, insolent when she won; nagging and quarrelsome, she quickly became a social scourge. Yet they had a silly, open ambition: having spent thirty thousand francs on their restoration and possessing at least ten thousand francs a year, they believed themselves wealthy and sought to impose on Provins, which promptly measured their vulgarity, ignorance, and envy.Madame Tiphaine had already noticed them and told Julliard junior she would not admit such people to her select salon. “I wish my society to be select; and it certainly will not be if I admit those Rogrons,” she said. Monsieur Tiphaine sneered at their shopkeeper origins; Julliard called them impudent, wanting to crush other houses though “they have hardly enough to live on.” They judged the sister’s harsh voice and thick hands intolerable. Madame Tiphaine’s remark—“my salon is not an inn”—was much admired and circulated. A month passed before she returned Sylvie’s visit; provincial insolence and measured neglect stung.Sylvie had an awkward evening at Madame Tiphaine’s, provoking a scene with old Madame Julliard over boston. Afterwards the card-tables were set before Sylvie arrived so she would be shunted to an onlooker’s role; at Madame Julliard senior’s they played whist, which Sylvie did not know. Invitations stopped. Satirical townspeople teased the Rogrons about the eggs in their cornice and their wine cellar; the pair tried to parry by giving sumptuous dinners to return civilities and display luxury.Their first dinner for town leaders became a test. Madame Tiphaine, who had introduced the Parisian custom of leaving after coffee, was anxious to go; the Rogrons accompanied her to the door in provincial form, but when she departed without staying for the full evening, the rest quickly imitated her, leaving the Rogrons disconcerted. They had hoped to astonish guests with their “palace,” but Madame Tiphaine later described the house to her salon in terms that ridiculed their taste: a long corridor and portico, a painted statue, a black-marble painted staircase, white calico curtains with red borders fastened by brass lion-paws, lamps like restaurant lights—novelties everywhere. The dining-room had five cheap engraved heroics in gilt frames; the salon was all red—curtains, furniture, mantelpiece—heavy common candelabra, a clock on Roman standards, a chandelier wrapped in green muslin, walls papered in red flock to imitate velvet, chromo-lithographs in stucco frames, uniform cashmere and elm furniture; an Aubusson carpet in a vulgar pattern completed a room that looked as if no one lived there: no books, knick-knacks, or flowers. Madame Tiphaine preferred Madame Julliard’s pastels and old provincial silver; “The provinces are provinces; they are only ridiculous when they mimic Paris,” she concluded. Her sketch revealed the absurdities a clever builder had foisted on the Rogrons—expensive novelties that gratified bourgeois taste but exposed their vulgarity.Their plans failed. Invitations were refused—“already engaged” to Madame Garceland and others. The Rogrons had supposed a few dinners would secure position; only young men who would mock them and habitués who dined everywhere accepted. Frightened by the loss of forty thousand francs on her “dear house,” Sylvie economized: she dismissed the cook, hired a country girl, and took to cooking “for pleasure.”Fourteen months after returning, they had fallen into solitude and idleness. Banished from society, Sylvie developed a strong hatred for the Tiphaines, Julliards, and the rest of the “clique” and sought a rival circle. But the lesser bourgeoisie comprised only small shopkeepers free only on Sundays and men like Vinet and the Bonapartist Baron Gouraud, whom Rogron carelessly befriended despite warnings.The siblings sat by the stove, recalling former customers and old resentments, weighed down by ennui. Days lengthened into lazy habits: lingering in bed, shaving as an event, quarreling about hot water, wandering to inspect garden shrubs or a sick hen, tapping the barometer like a friend. They read the “Bee-hive” and the Constitutionnel, the cost shared by Vinet and Gouraud; Rogron delivered the paper to Gouraud and enjoyed long yarns and puzzling warnings about the clique—warnings he foolishly repeated. His walks yielded scraps of gossip—archaeological projects, talks about Provins’ catacombs and springs—that became topics at home. Evenings were desert-like; they creaked at one another like rusty wheels. Rogron spoke of marrying, which frightened him; Sylvie sought a third person and remembered a distant cousin, Pierrette Lorrain, scarcely inquired about and perhaps presumed dead.Sylvie, too careful to lose opportunity, pretended to have found a Lorrain letter to raise the prospect of Pierrette’s arrival. She answered Madame Lorrain’s letter half-affectionately, half commercially, blaming delay on the change of abode and hinting Pierrette might inherit twelve thousand francs a year if Jérôme did not marry. The Rogrons awaited the cousin like caged beasts. Sylvie even went to Madame Tiphaine to mention Pierrette, deploring the girl’s misfortune and posing as eager to assist—only to be met by sarcasm. During cards, Madame Garceland recalled the Rogrons’ shameful conduct over the Auffray property; the notary explained events. Monsieur Tiphaine asked where the little girl was.“In Brittany,” said Rogron.“Brittany is a large place,” Monsieur Lesourd remarked.Sylvie clumsily answered questions about when they had replied and where the girl might be sent. Rogron blundered, claiming to have written to the Institution of Saint-Jacques, only to be told that was an almshouse for the elderly. Desfondrilles recalled a Nantes house sold under court order swallowing up Mademoiselle Lorrain’s claim, leaving the girl unprovided for. The Rogrons retreated, blaming malicious gossip. Sylvie saw that her ostensible benevolence had made no impression; she had lost ground. Thereafter the Rogrons no longer concealed their hatred of that class.Into this resentment stepped two disreputable allies. Colonel Gouraud, a retired officer—short, fat, decorated, with a red, battle-scarred face and the Legion of Honor—lived modestly on a small pension and jolly lawlessness; he loved opportunity and plunder. The lawyer Vinet, tall, thin, pale, and spiteful, nursed liberal opinions in place of talent. Disfavour with the court, a marriage that had enraged a family, and poverty made him eager for opposition triumphs to elevate him. His quick, rasping speech and readiness to exploit legal avenues made him dangerous.Both watched the Rogrons with curiosity. Vinet aimed to found a newspaper and, seeing the brother’s money and house, concluded Rogron could be made useful. When Gouraud told him the Rogrons had quarreled with the Upper-town bourgeois aristocracy, Vinet proposed a plan.“One woman or another—handsome or ugly—you don’t care; marry Mademoiselle Rogron and we can organize something at once,” he said. Gouraud had thought of opportunistically exploiting Pierrette Lorrain; Vinet suggested securing wills in their favor. “You’d get a very comfortable house,” he said. As for the little girl—Gouraud leered, promising to assess how far he could profit.Thus the Rogrons, rejected by the elite they sought to join, became the center of a new, unscrupulous circle: a veteran with a taste for plunder and a lawyer eager to exploit both politics and private advantage. Their vanity and hatred of the clique, paired with the colonel’s coarse designs and Vinet’s legal opportunism, set in motion schemes that would determine Pierrette’s fate and the Rogrons’ future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After her grandparents were taken to the hospital where they were expected to die, young Pierrette—proud and unused to charity—felt a private relief on learning she had well‑to‑do relations. Brigaut, the son of her mother’s friend and her childhood companion, then an apprentice cabinet‑maker in Nantes, offered the sixty francs of his apprentice tips to send her to Paris; she accepted with the indifference of true affection. Brigaut spent Sundays consoling Pierrette at Saint‑Jacques, delighting in protecting the girl he loved and promising to make a fortune while she waited. In October 1824, just after her eleventh birthday, the sorrowful grandparents and Brigaut entrusted her to the Nantes–Paris diligence conductor, begging him to see her safely onto the Provins coach.
