Brigaut ran after the coach until out of breath; both cried as she disappeared.Unfamiliar with city life and effectively penniless, Pierrette arrived in Paris dependent on the conductor’s kindness. Told of her “rich cousins,” he paid hotel expenses and insisted the Provins conductor care for the child and obtain payment from the family. Four days later, about nine on a Monday night, a kindly conductor of the Messageries‑royales led Pierrette—with only a small bundle of two dresses, two chemises, and two pairs of stockings—to Mademoiselle Rogron’s, announced, “I’ve brought you a cousin,” and demanded forty‑seven francs for expenses. The Rogrons, astonished, paid. The conductor refused thanks, saying they had cared for the child as if she were their own.Sylvie, Rogron, and their maid Adèle received Pierrette with stolid surprise; she was thrown to them like a parcel, with no soft transition from the wretched chamber at Saint‑Jacques to their dining room, which seemed a palace to her. Shy and speechless in her coarse Breton clothes—blue petticoat, cambric apron, thick shoes, blue stockings, white kerchief, red worsted mittens, and a washed Breton cap haloing her rosy face—Pierrette might have charmed others. But the Rogrons viewed her through the lens of cost and decorum. They inspected her bundle, decided she must sleep, and tucked her into a modest room with threadbare furnishings. Pierrette, amazed at cotton sheets and the warmth of the house, quietly praised it while Sylvie and Rogron fretted about future expenses. “You won’t get off under three hundred francs,” Rogron declared; Sylvie lay awake shocked.Reared on affection and freedom at Pen‑Hoel and petted by everyone, Pierrette had known hardship at Saint‑Jacques but had lived on her grandparents’ love. Now the cold greeting of her cousins baffled and wounded a heart delicate as a fawn’s. Still tired, she slept.Next morning she rose early, explored the staircase and garden, marvelled at the kiosk and river, and was punished at breakfast for noise and dirt. “You must be very good and quiet,” Sylvie scolded; Rogron called her dirty and ignorant; Adèle sniffed at her coarse chemises. Their remarks hurt Pierrette though she scarcely understood why. The Rogrons, proud of their house and furniture, had turned care for possessions into a worship. Sylvie kept a closet of dusting tools and took petty delight in maintaining household goods; her ardor for cleanliness hardened into principle and pleasure.Determined to remake Pierrette to Provins standards, they set about sewing and fittings the first week: underclothes, boots, a corset, a blue reps dress, a silk‑lined cape, and a blue velvet bonnet—fashionable trappings Sylvie hoped would stand up to local scrutiny. She engaged Mademoiselle Borain, the town’s best seamstress, scolding the child for ignorance when Pierrette confessed she had only played in Brittany and been loved by her grandparents. Pierrette preferred the warm, watching company of the sewing women to her relations’ pedantry; the seamstresses liked her, while Sylvie fretted the expense—“That child will make us pay through the nose!”—yet pushed on, buying quality clothes to rival other mothers.Transformed, Pierrette became Provins’s most enchanting child; Sunday after Sunday, the ladies embraced her. Their admiration inflated Sylvie’s self‑regard—she believed the praise reflected her benevolence—yet she grew resentful when invitations for Pierrette did not bring callers to her salon. The child’s success, which might have delighted a maternal heart, only enraged the Rogrons’ selfishness.Fashion, however, exposed fragility: used to running and rough play, Pierrette wore out shoes and tore delicate garments. A mother might have corrected gently; the Rogrons calculated loss. Constant admonitions about propriety, economy, and decency all flowed from one theme—“Pierrette will ruin us.” In fault‑finding lectures they reverted to petty, shopkeeping habits they'd ostensibly left behind; deprived of larger spheres to govern, they exercised despotism on the helpless child. Narrow natures expand by persecuting: for them self‑interest fused with a thirst for control and vindication.Pierrette became central to their lives: first as an expense, then as a presence to be governed and displayed. Sylvie’s initial softness hardened into the cold “Pierrette.” Reprimands sharpened; the cousins sank into habitual severity they convinced themselves was beneficial. Too sensitive and straightforward for such hardness, the child dreaded scoldings; tears rose at the slightest reproof. She learned to repress her natural sprightliness, showing it only in friends’ homes. Within a month she had acquired passive compliance; when Rogron asked if she were ill, she fled to the garden and wept by the river.One day, despite careful measures, she tore her best reps frock at Madame Tiphaine’s, where she’d been happy. Terrified of home punishment, she confided in friends; Tiphaine happened to have matching material and discreetly repaired the frock. When Sylvie discovered this “trick,” she forbade Pierrette further visits to Tiphaine’s, cutting off one of the few joyful outlets she had. This refusal, and the beginning of formal education, marked the end of the first three months in Provins—the only period that had held genuine pleasure.Meanwhile the colonel and Vinet watched the Rogrons with foxlike caution, making calls and infiltrating their confidence with polite reserve. On the very night Sylvie forbade Tiphaine’s visits, the colonel and a lawyer friend—using gossip and partisan spite to inflame Sylvie—suggested the Rogrons form an opposition salon against Provins’s “ministerialists.” Vinet exploited slander to rouse Sylvie; the lawyer painted Tiphaine’s speech as an insult, provoking the Rogrons’ vanity. Vinet then introduced his mild, well‑bred wife to Sylvie, whose meek deference pleased and flattered Sylvie’s self‑importance. These new allies encouraged the Rogrons’ snug social ambitions and further isolated Pierrette.Under their watchful, acquisitive eyes the child’s life narrowed. Shut indoors and permitted out only under strict supervision, she lived beneath an incessant “Don’t touch that, child, let that alone!” She was drilled in carriage and behavior; Sylvie, rigid and proud, ordered her erect and sometimes enforced compliance with slaps. The free, joyous Breton girl learned, little by little, to curb every liveliness and to become, as best she could, an automaton—quiet, obedient, and constrained to match the Rogrons’ appetite for domination and display.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening early in Pierrette’s second stay with her cousins, the unseen child came to kiss the guests good-night. Sylvie turned away coldly and Pierrette burst into tears. Vinet and Sylvie mocked her; Sylvie contrasted the grandmother who took Pierrette’s money with a cousin who would leave her hers. “I would rather be robbed and loved,” Pierrette said and was threatened with being sent back. Humiliated, she went to Rogron, who, with Sylvie, accused her of mischief—opening a watch, breaking a mainspring—and of heedlessness. Ashamed, she left the room.Rogron resolved to subdue the child. Madame Vinet suggested school; Vinet and the colonel had other plans. Vinet proposed using the managing editor of an opposition journal as Pierrette’s teacher if Rogron would back the paper financially. Sylvie called Pierrette a “rough diamond” needing polishing, and the plan to hire a master and launch a paper advanced while Pierrette dissolved in tears. Blaming her cousin, she resolved to be faultless; unaware she became the subject of further schemes.Pierrette did get a writing-master to teach reading, writing, and arithmetic—an arrival greeted at the Rogrons’ as a harbinger of disorder: ink-spots, scattered sand, torn books. She was told to earn her keep and not be a burden; to weep was treated as an affront to their generosity. Rogron adopted the tone he used with clerks: scolding her, summoning her from play to drill lessons, growing almost savage. Sylvie, teaching what little she knew of women’s work, showed no tenderness. Their narrow pleasure in worrying the child hardened into severity, justified by alleged “self-will.” Unable to adapt instruction to Pierrette’s slow learning, they called her stupid and clumsy for trifles. Constantly abused by words and looks, she took on a sheep-like submission, sheltering her mind in passive obedience. Her bright colors faded; when she complained of feeling ill “everywhere,” they dismissed it as nonsense or feigned attention-seeking.Only Adèle, the fat servant, softened toward the child—warming her bed secretly after Sylvie’s rebukes. But the Rogrons treated Pierrette’s affection as dissimulation; any gentle feeling met suspicion and accusations of ulterior motives. Colonel Gouraud, anxious to please Sylvie, approved her actions; Vinet attributed the child’s behavior to Breton obstinacy and encouraged the Rogrons. Flattered, Rogron agreed to be security for the Courrier de Provins; Sylvie invested five thousand francs.The colonel and Vinet then organized a wider financial and political campaign. They sold shares in the paper to farmers, independents, and buyers of national-domain land, exploited local fears, and divided judicial advertisements between the existing Bee-hive and the new Courrier. The paper’s first number contained a pompous eulogy of Rogron, likening him to Laffitte and arousing expectations of contested elections. The Courrier attacked local figures like the Tiphaine family; Madame Tiphaine feared losing social position.As the Courrier spread, Vinet adopted liberal dress and airs; his wife took a servant and comported herself like the wife of a prominent man. Vinet and his ally Cournant, a liberal notary, worked to increase the Rogrons’ rentals: pursuing suits over planting and trees, collecting fines, and ejecting peasants who had defaulted on debts secured by the elder Rogron. Using the Rogrons’ savings—who had been setting aside a large sum annually—they invested in improved lands, dividing holdings into small farms rented to safe tenants. By 1826 these investments yielded about five thousand francs a year, with tenants bearing many repair costs.By the end of Pierrette’s second stay, life under the Rogrons had hardened into cold indifference, petty fault-finding, and the absence of affection; she felt a grave chill closing in. She contemplated running back to her grandparents in Brittany, but two events prevented escape: old Lorrain died, and Rogron was appointed her guardian. Had the grandmother died first, Rogron—advised by Vinet—would have claimed Pierrette’s eight thousand francs and left the old man destitute. Prompted by Vinet’s remark that one never knew who might live or die, Rogron forced Madame Lorrain to secure for Pierrette the reversion of the eight thousand francs at her death.Preparing for her first communion, Pierrette abandoned thoughts of flight and began to change in another way. Sylvie insisted that the local vicar, Monsieur Habert—a zealous, austere priest thought to belong to a Congregation—should instruct her. Habert and his sister, a thin, unmarried schoolmistress, took an interest in the child. Rooted in Breton Catholic practice and naturally inclined toward devotion, Pierrette responded fully to the priest’s teaching. Suffering predisposed her to mysticism; the priest taught her to see Jesus as a heavenly bridegroom and to interpret her trials as part of divine providence. Faith gave meaning to her suffering; she abandoned escape and took refuge in prayer, hope, and charity. Surprised, Sylvie also adopted more piety under the priest’s influence, while Rogron—bound to Colonel Gouraud, Vinet, and liberal ambitions—remained unmoved.Mademoiselle Rogron befriended Mademoiselle Habert; the sister offered to take Pierrette to her school to spare Sylvie the trouble, but the Rogrons decided the house would be too lonely without the child. Their attachment seemed excessive to the rival camp.Gouraud and Vinet, however, feared the Haberts’ influence: they suspected the priest aimed to marry his sister advantageously, as the colonel hoped to marry Sylvie. They saw the priest and his sister as rivals as formidable as themselves. Both pairs—the liberals led by Vinet and the Haberts with their clerical circle—visited the Rogrons frequently, turning the salon into a battlefield where each faction sought the household’s allegiance.The liberals struck back. Vinet brought Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf and her mother from Troyes—Bathilde de Chargeboeuf, noble yet impoverished—persuaded that by combining her means with Vinet’s she could live in Provins and marry the gullible Rogron. Their arrival strengthened the liberal party and scandalized Provins’ aristocrats. Bathilde, at twenty-five, was dazzling: fair, well-formed, with the carriage of a high-bred woman. Her simple but careful dress and light courtship overwhelmed Rogron, who masked excitement as indifference. Sylvie and Céleste Habert were deceived by his manner; Vinet was not.The colonel, courting Sylvie, took pains each evening to appear the party leader: clean shirt, velvet, a new overcoat, a rosette, foregoing smoking. Vinet and the colonel also spread insinuations that Monsieur Habert was sympathetic to liberal ideas; the bishop, hearing this, summoned Habert and told him to stop visiting the Rogrons—but his sister persisted. The Rogron salon thus became firmly established as a liberal rendezvous.Politics and matrimonial schemes mingled: the Villele ministry fell in 1826; Vinet, who had borrowed to buy land to qualify as a candidate, nearly beat Tiphaine by two votes. The Rogrons’ salon served as Liberal headquarters, frequented by notary Cournant and Doctor Neraud. The Rogrons themselves scarcely understood the extent of their triumph.Bathilde, instructed by Vinet to view Pierrette as an enemy, treated the girl with disdain. Madame Vinet, constrained by her husband’s will and later estranged by the Chargeboeufs’ arrival, could do little for Pierrette. The child often clung to Madame Vinet, begging to be taught embroidery; gently treated, Pierrette could learn and shine. But with the Chargeboeufs installed and Sylvie contemplating marriage, Pierrette was increasingly seen as an obstacle.Nearly fourteen, pallid from neglected illness, Pierrette’s existence struck Sylvie as a burden. The salon habitués advised turning her into a servant: dismiss Adèle and have Pierrette keep house and cook. Pierrette, generous and self-sacrificing, offered willingly to learn the bitter bread of service. Adèle was sent away, depriving Pierrette of her only protector.Reduced to the status of a servant, she was scolded for the smallest speck of dust. Objects she once admired became odious; no matter how she strove, her cousins found cause to reproach her. In two years she received no praise or kindness; the best she could hope for was simply not being scolded. She bore the morose ill-humor of two celibates—people unfamiliar with tenderness—with angelic patience, daily reminded of her dependence.Such pressure wore on her body as well as her spirit. Secret internal pains undermined her strength and halted her development. Slowly, through hidden sufferings and exhaustion, the child declined toward the fragile, silent state in which her childhood companion later found her—the pale figure beneath the morning song.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Before recounting the domestic drama Jacques Brigaut’s arrival provoked in the Rogron household, it helps to explain how he came to Provins; he plays a quiet but pivotal role.When Brigaut fled the house he had not merely been frightened by Pierrette’s gesture; he was appalled by the change in her. The voice, eyes, and gestures he had known—lively and tender—had been replaced by something he scarcely recognized. From a distance his legs trembled; he felt a chill down his back. He had seen only Pierrette’s shadow. Climbing to the Upper town, he found a vantage where he could see the square and her house; he stared at it mournfully and discovered the depth of his attachment to his adopted sister.Passion between children is rare, yet Pierrette and Brigaut had often protected each other. He had given her money for a journey once and nearly killed himself chasing the departing diligence; she knew nothing of this. For him such memories warmed his hard life. For her sake he had struggled to improve himself: he learned a trade, went to Paris to make his fortune, and after a fortnight could not resist returning to Provins, walking from Saturday night to Monday morning. He had meant to go back to Paris, but seeing Pierrette kept him; an unconscious magnetism held him. Tears from his eyes and from hers showed him how deep his feeling was. To her he was a Breton boy of childhood; to him she was life itself.At sixteen Brigaut could not yet do fine joinery—he had not learned to draw or model a cornice—but during his apprenticeship he earned four or five francs a day by piece-work, enough to live in Provins near Pierrette. He chose the best cabinet-maker in town, intending to learn by work while keeping watch over her. His mind was made up: he returned to Paris for certificate, tools, and baggage, and three days later became a journeyman in Monsieur Frappier’s establishment, the best cabinet-maker in Provins. Active, steady workmen were prized; by the end of the month he was foreman and was fed and lodged by Frappier, who taught him arithmetic and line drawing. The shop and house were in the Grand’Rue, not a hundred feet from Pierrette’s square.Brigaut suppressed his passion and behaved prudently. He made Madame Frappier tell him what she knew about the Rogrons, including how their father had grabbed old Auffray’s property. He collected other family details. He met Pierrette at market with her cousin and shuddered to see the heavy basket she carried; on Sundays he went to church in his best clothes to look for her. There, for the first time, he learned Pierrette was Mademoiselle Lorrain. Pierrette saw him and discreetly signaled him to keep away. That tiny gesture contained a world for him, as had the earlier signal from her window that told him to run away. Oh, what fortune he must make to marry her—he was told she would inherit the Rogron house, a hundred acres, and twelve thousand francs a year, besides savings.The persevering Breton therefore resolved to educate himself thoroughly in his trade in Provins rather than in Paris, to remain near Pierrette and later explain his plans and the protection he could offer. He wanted to know why she looked pale and why debility had dulled her eyes; he wanted to discover what made her seem on the brink of death. The two signs she had given—gestures admitting friendship but pleading caution—alarmed him. She evidently asked him to wait, for otherwise there was danger. As she left the church she gave him a tearful look; but he could no more have guessed the events inside the Rogron house during his fortnight in town than he could have squared the circle.Pierrette herself had reason to be fearful the morning after Brigaut invaded her dreams. She thought Sylvie must have heard the song; she did not know the powerful reasons the old maid had to be alert. For the last eight days strange events and bitter feelings had agitated the Rogron salon’s principal figures. These hidden matters, carefully concealed, were destined to fall like an avalanche on Pierrette. Such secret schemes and calculations underlie many revolutions, whether political, social, or domestic; their subtle significance must be traced to understand what followed.Mademoiselle Habert (Céleste) and Mademoiselle Sylvie Rogron both desired to marry Rogron; Céleste was ten years older and hoped her children would inherit all the Rogron property. Sylvie, at forty-two, faced a risky age for marriage. Céleste, instigated by her vindictive brother the priest, magnified Sylvie’s fears. Sylvie trembled; celibates are haunted by fear of death, and with a change of ministry the Liberals had come to power in town. The colonel would likely be made mayor; the thought appealed to Sylvie. She resolved to consult a doctor, though the step would expose her.Consulting Monsieur Neraud, the Liberal physician and rival of Martener, would be a political mistake; so Céleste proposed to hide Sylvie in her dressing-room while she herself consulted Monsieur Martener about the delicate matter. Whether Martener was Céleste’s accomplice is unclear; at any rate he told Céleste that even at thirty the danger, though slight, existed. “With your constitution you need fear nothing,” he added. But pressed about a woman over forty—a middle-aged unmarried woman—Martener grew clinical: elasticity declines with age, especially in sedentary women; childbirth after forty is rare and risky for an unmarried woman. His grim prognosis: an unmarried woman would make a great mistake marrying after forty; marriage then would be more a union of interests than real conjugal vitality.Sylvie left the doctor's more frightened than before. Céleste urged patience: “wait,” she counseled, Jesuitically aware that time might resolve her rival’s suit. Sylvie’s renewed devotion to the church made her conscience troublesome; the priest explained the Church sees marriage primarily for propagation and rebukes second marriages and passion without social purpose. This moral peril gave extraordinary force to her passion; the very prohibition added attraction. Mademoiselle Rogron’s perturbation did not escape the lawyer Vinet.One evening after a game, Vinet drew Sylvie aside. He perceived she was troubled and, by letting others leave, elicited the truth. A shrewd man of the world and the editor of the Courrier, he privately counseled Sylvie to delay marriage for ten years. Inwardly he wondered how to secure the Rogron fortune for his own family—he planned it should go to Bathilde—and he immediately schemed to drive the colonel from the field. The priest’s influence and Vinet’s financial cunning balanced each other; Rogron himself had little piety, and the black-robed pair were fairly matched in manipulating him.When Céleste’s victory over Sylvie seemed assured, Vinet saw his chance to arrange Rogron’s marriage to Bathilde; he knew Jérôme (Rogron) was already inwardly susceptible to Bathilde and guessed that once alone they would be married. Vinet, who had learned of Sylvie’s secret love, realized how such an obsession in an old maid gripped by religion could be exploited: it could be used to ruin Pierrette and the colonel alike, each becoming the cause of the other’s downfall.The next day Vinet met the colonel while Rogron and the colonel strolled their daily round. Whenever the three men were seen together the town gossiped; the triumvirate symbolized local Liberal pride: Vinet the editor, the colonel the arm, Rogron the purse. Vinet stopped the colonel and confided: Pierrette is charming; you can do better than marry Sylvie; if you play it well you can marry Pierrette in two years. He then explained the Jesuit’s maneuver and its effect on Sylvie. The colonel, who had long thought about Pierrette but concealed his feelings, was stirred. His roughness to the child was a mask; though he often treated her harshly in public, when alone he would be affectionate. After Vinet’s talk he began to seek opportunities to find Pierrette alone, and his behavior softened; he praised her father and recounted his bravery.A few days before Brigaut’s coming, Sylvie had suddenly seen Gouraud and Pierrette conversing and jealousy seized her with monastic violence. Jealousy, credulous and suspicious, enlarges fancy without adding intelligence; it filled her with fantastic ideas. When she heard Brigaut’s ditty a few mornings later, she leaped to a conclusion: the man who had sung “Madam’ le mariée,” addressing Pierrette, must be the colonel. She had noticed a change in the colonel’s manners for a week; he was the only man who had paid her attention in her solitary life. So she watched him with all her mind and eyes; by hope and despair she magnified everything into a mirage. By dint of staring she saw nothing real. Alternately she repelled, admitted, and conquered the thought of rivalry. She compared herself—forty-two with gray hair—with Pierrette’s delicate fairness and thought of men of fifty who might love such girls. Before the colonel came to the house, she had heard odd stories at the Tiphaines’ salon about his life. Old maids hold exaggerated Platonic sentiments and fixed doctrines. The mere thought of being jilted by the colonel was torture; she lay awake rehearsing desires, Pierrette’s conduct, and the strange song that had awakened her with the word “marriage.” Foolishly she opened her window—without reflecting that Pierrette would hear her. If she had spied instead, she would have seen Brigaut and the fatal scene might not have begun.Pierrette, fragile, unwell, had her duties: she unbarred and opened kitchen shutters and the garden door, fetched brooms and utensils with meticulous care, and labored to please her cold-eyed cousin. She hated reproof and sought calm; happiness was seeing her cousin’s glance be calm rather than satisfied. Her skin was damp with labor when she returned to put the kitchen in order and light the stove to carry hot water to her two cousins and to prepare the dining-room fire. For this she fetched wood from the cellar, moving from warm rooms into damp air—sudden transitions that worsened her condition. She did not know she was ill; strange cravings came, she ate raw vegetables secretly, and she was unaware how serious her state was. If Neraud, the Rogrons’ physician, had warned her before Brigaut’s arrival she would only have smiled; life was so bitter she could smile at death. But Brigaut’s song and presence revived her like rain on parched soil; unconsciously she wanted to live and thought perhaps she did not suffer after all.That morning Pierrette slipped into her cousin’s bedroom to make the fire, left hot water, roused Rogron, then went to take in milk, bread, and provisions. She lingered on the doorstep hoping Brigaut would come; he was by then already on his way to Paris. Finishing the dining-room, she worked in the kitchen when she heard Sylvie coming down. Mademoiselle Rogron appeared in a brown silk dressing-gown with her cap awry and a night-gown showing; she came straight to Pierrette.“Ha! here you are, lovesick young lady!” Sylvie mocked.“What is it, cousin?” Pierrette asked.“You came into my room like a sly cat and crept out the same way, though you knew I had something to say to you.”“To me?”“You had a serenade this morning, as if you were a princess.”“A serenade!” Pierrette repeated, bewildered.“A serenade,” Sylvie mocked. “And you’ve a lover, too.”“What is a lover, cousin?” Pierrette inquired honestly.Sylvie evaded the definition. “Do you dare tell me a man did not come under your window and talk to you of marriage?”Pierrette, schooled by persecution, answered cautiously: “I don’t know what you mean—”“Who means?—your dog?” said Sylvie sharply.“I should have said ‘cousin,’” Pierrette corrected humbly.“And didn’t you get up and go in your bare feet to the window?—which will give you an illness; and perhaps you didn’t talk to your lover, either?”“No, cousin.”“You have many faults, but I did not think you told lies. You will have to explain this to your cousin and to me, or I shall take severe measures.”Sylvie, exasperated and curious, meant to frighten the girl. Pierrette, who endured more than she had strength for, kept silence. Silence is sometimes a slave’s weapon; it baffles envy’s accusations. Sylvie watched her, saw the girl’s cheeks color in burning patches—signs a mother would have noticed and soothed, but an old maid without maternal feeling drew only suspicion from them.“She blushes; she is guilty!” thought Sylvie.Pierrette’s silence was turned against her. “Pierrette,” Sylvie continued, “before your cousin comes down we must talk together. Come,” she softened, “shut the street door; if anyone comes they will ring and we shall hear them.”Despite the mist rising from the river, Sylvie took Pierrette across the lawn to the rock terrace—an ornamental quay with iris and water plants. She changed tactics, thinking firmness might catch Pierrette tripping; the hyena became a cat.“Pierrette,” she said, “you are no longer a child; you are nearly fifteen; it’s not surprising you should have a lover.”“But, cousin,” said Pierrette, raising angelic eyes, “what is a lover?”Sylvie could not define it decently; instead she answered bluntly, “A lover is a man who loves us and wishes to marry us.”“In Brittany we call the young man a suitor,” Pierrette answered simply.“Well, remember that in owning your feelings for a man you do no wrong; the wrong is in hiding them. Have you pleased some of the men who visit here?”“No.”“Do you love any of them?”“No.”“Certain?”“Quite certain.”“Look at me, Pierrette.”The girl looked.“A man called to you this morning in the square.”Pierrette lowered her eyes and said nothing.“You went to your window, you opened it, and you spoke to him.”“No cousin, I went to look out and I saw a peasant.”“Pierrette, you have much improved since your first communion; you have become pious and obedient; I am satisfied with you. I don’t say this to puff you up with pride.”In the old maid’s mind Pierrette’s meekness and silence had been misread for hypocrisy. Pierrette raised grateful eyes at the praise, but the praise shifted to threat.“But if it is all hypocrisy, if I find you a serpent that I have warmed in my bosom, you will be a wicked girl, an infamous creature!”“I think I have nothing to reproach myself with,” Pierrette answered, painfully shocked.“You know that to lie is a mortal sin?”“Yes, cousin.”“Well, you are now under the eye of God,” said Sylvie, pointing upward; “swear to me that you did not know that peasant.”“I will not swear,” said Pierrette.“Ha! he was no peasant, you little viper.”Pierrette rushed away like a frightened fawn. Sylvie called her in a dreadful voice.“The bell is ringing,” Pierrette answered.“Artful wretch!” thought Sylvie. “She is depraved in mind; now I am certain the little adder has wound herself round the colonel. She has heard us say he was a baron. To be a baroness! little fool! Ah! I’ll get rid of her; I’ll apprentice her out, and soon too!”Sylvie was so lost in thought she did not notice her brother Rogron coming down the path lamenting frost on his dahlias. “Sylvie! what are you thinking about? I thought you were looking at the fish; sometimes they jump,” he said. “How did you sleep? Don’t you think my skin is getting tabid?” Rogron had been uneasy about his health since his failed suit with Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf.Pierrette called that breakfast was ready. At sight of her Sylvie’s face went green and yellow; bile in commotion, she looked at the corridor floor and ordered Pierrette to rub it.“I will rub it now if you wish,” Pierrette said, unaware the additional labor might harm her.Through breakfast Sylvie pretended she wanted this and that just to make Pierrette rise repeatedly. Pierrette, painfully conscious, brought milk mixed with cream in a large silver goblet after heating it in a bain-marie. The cousins poured coffee. When Sylvie prepared hers she saw an atom of coffee-grounds floating on the surface. There the storm broke.“What is the matter?” Rogron asked.“The matter is that mademoiselle has put dust in my milk. Do you suppose I am going to drink coffee with ashes in it? Well, I am not surprised; no one can do two things at once. She wasn’t thinking of the milk! a blackbird might have flown through the kitchen today and she wouldn’t have seen it! and then it was my coffee, ha! that didn’t signify!”She lifted aside the grounds that had passed through the filter.“But, cousin, that is coffee,” Pierrette said.“Oh! then it is I who tell lies, is it?” Sylvie cried, blasting the girl with a fearful look.Emotional strain often sharpens clarity; Sylvie’s anger had the cold clearness she often used in her shop to inspire fear. “You had better dare to give me the lie!” she continued. “You deserve to be sent from the table to eat by yourself in the kitchen.”“What’s the matter with you two?” asked Rogron. “You are as cross as bears this morning.”“Mademoiselle knows what I have against her,” said Sylvie. “I leave her to make up her mind before speaking to you; for I mean to show her more kindness than she deserves.”Pierrette looked out the window to avoid Sylvie’s frightening eyes. “Look at her! she pays no attention to what I am saying than if I were that sugar-basin! And yet mademoiselle has a sharp ear; she can hear and answer from the top of the house when someone talks to her from below. She is perversity itself,—perversity, I say; and you needn’t expect any good of her; do you hear me, Jérôme?”“What has she done wrong?” Rogron asked.“At her age, too! to begin so young!” screamed Sylvie.Pierrette rose to clear the table and give herself something to do; the scene was nearly unbearable. Though such language was not new, she had never grown used to it. Her cousin’s rage seemed to accuse her of crime. She imagined what fury would be if they knew about Brigaut; perhaps they would send him away and she would lose him. All a slave’s rapid thoughts came; she resolved to keep absolute silence about an incident her conscience told her was innocent. But the cruel words and wounding suspicion so shocked her that as she reached the kitchen she was seized with a convulsion of the stomach and felt deadly sick. She dared not complain; she was not sure anyone would help her. Returning to the dining-room she was white as a sheet, said she was unwell, and went to bed, dragging herself by the baluster and thinking she would die. “Poor Brigaut!” she thought.“The girl is ill,” Rogron observed.“She ill! That’s only shamming,” Sylvie replied loudly so Pierrette might hear. “She was well enough this morning, I can tell you.”This last blow prostrated Pierrette; she went to bed weeping and prayed to God to take her from this world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For a month Rogron had stopped bringing the “Constitutionnel” to Gouraud; nevertheless the colonel came punctually to fetch his paper, to gossip, and, weather permitting, to take Rogron for a walk. Sylvie dressed with as much coquetry as she could—hoping to see the colonel and question him. She fancied herself handsome in a green gown, yellow shawl with red border, and a white bonnet with gray feathers. About the hour the colonel usually arrived she took her place in the salon with her brother, keeping him at home in dressing-gown and slippers.When Gouraud entered, Rogron begged him to wait, leaving Sylvie alone with the colonel. He noted a certain solemnity on the pock-marked face of the old maid and paid her compliments. Sylvie said she had wanted to go out, but Pierrette, her little cousin, was ill. Gouraud’s distrust of Vinet was constant: Vinet controlled the paper, took the revenues, and reigned as master while the colonel, the responsible editor, earned little. Vinet and his associate Cournant had done the Rogrons many services; Gouraud was only a powerless half-pay officer. Vinet would be the deputy consulted by the liberals. The colonel also knew Rogron harbored an ardent, concealed passion for Bathilde de Chargeboeuf; when Gouraud had hinted he might propose to Bathilde, Rogron had turned cold and hostile.Thus Vinet reigned in the Rogron household; the colonel was marginalized, holding only the precarious tie of a tender, if somewhat deceitful, affection for Sylvie. A lawyer’s suggestion that Gouraud break with Sylvie and marry Pierrette had flattered the colonel’s vanity; yet he suspected Vinet intended to separate him from Sylvie and, by exploiting her fears, throw the Rogron property into Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf’s hands.Left alone with Sylvie, the colonel detected her uneasiness; she had planned to see him privately. Suspecting Vinet, he went on guard. He never believed a woman’s word; when Sylvie said Pierrette had gone to bed before midday, he suspected secrecy. He tried to sound casual.“You are dressed divinely,” Gouraud said, and Sylvie shyly accepted the compliment. He suggested sending Pierrette to Paris to be placed in a milliner’s shop; Sylvie, uneasy, saw in this the mark of Vinet’s meddling. Alarmed, Gouraud deliberately spoke disparagingly of Bathilde and of beauty as a poor foundation for marriage; at forty-eight he preferred comfort and a companion, not children or a young wife whose admirers might humiliate him. When Sylvie asked bluntly, “Then you don’t love Pierrette?” he laughed and denied it.Reassured, Sylvie pretended shrewdness, attributing the marriage plan to her brother. The colonel, seeing an opening, declared his real wish: he had long chosen Sylvie for her good qualities and desired no wife but her. He took her hand and kissed it. Sylvie tried one more test: “But if I want you to marry Pierrette? If I leave her my fortune?” The colonel refused, fearing a younger wife’s competition, preferring to avoid future humiliations. Sylvie smiled but remained grim inside.When Rogron returned, the colonel bowed to Sylvie in a lover-like way and, on their walk, told Rogron he had been joking about Bathilde: he confessed his wishes, declaring he longed to be Rogron’s brother-in-law. “If nothing is wanting but my consent you have it,” cried Rogron, delighted to be rid of Bathilde as a rival. Sylvie then spent the morning imagining household arrangements—an upper floor for her brother and the rest furnished for her and her husband—and resolved, true to old-maid suspicion, to test the colonel and prevent any private intercourse between him and Pierrette.Pierrette came down before dinner to lay the table. Sylvie, forced to cook, seized any pretext to show suspicion: she swore at Pierrette for a grease spot on her gown—proof, she declared, that Pierrette hadn’t done her work herself. “You are like the dog of the marshal who woke up as soon as the saucepans rattled,” Sylvie snapped, accusing the girl of feigning illness and lying generally.After dinner Sylvie sent Pierrette to dress in her best clothes; suspicion made Sylvie invent reasons to keep Pierrette close, to judge the colonel’s truthfulness.That evening the Chargeboeuf ladies arrived first. On Vinet’s advice Bathilde dressed elaborately—blue velveteen, transparent fichu, garnet pendants, ringlets—displaying queenly manners and girlish coquetry aimed at Rogron. Her mother carried an aristocratic insolence. Bathilde had intelligence; during a two-month stay at Vinet’s house he had discovered and fed her mind, inflaming her contempt for a world that prized money over beauty. Vinet told her marriage could be used to gain position and influence: marry Rogron, make him deputy, and through political maneuvering secure promotion—then, if fortunes changed, marry up later. He spoke of women as instruments to advance men like post-horses.Vinet, who visited nightly with Madame and Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf, had transformed himself into a man of consequence—gold spectacles, silk waistcoat, white cravat—shedding former bitterness for a sly, agreeable perfidy. Madame de Chargeboeuf bewitched the salon with condescension; Bathilde staged coquettish displays for Rogron, stepping before him, adjusting her bonnet, placing her foot on the fender for his admiration. She teased him, and he, pacified, murmured, “You are too beautiful... too dangerous.”Pierrette’s meek civility contrasted painfully with Bathilde’s disdain: Bathilde pushed past her; Pierrette took her hat as if she were a servant. Vinet feigned admiration, fixating on Pierrette to insult her indirectly. “Fine!” the poor child answered, saying the compliment was for Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf. The scene made Pierrette painfully aware of her dependence and smallness.Céleste Habert entered with the colonel. Mademoiselle Habert, now the Rogrons’ enemy, resembled a stern schoolmistress—caps, grim mouth, coarse hairs on a mole; she took snuff and sat with a posture of habitual stiffness. Her presence completed the company, and everyone showed civility despite undermining one another.They took to a game of boston. Mademoiselle Habert sat opposite Sylvie; the colonel beside her; Bathilde near her mother and Rogron; Pierrette floated between Sylvie and the colonel. Sylvie watched the colonel closely and plotted. When she undertook a grande misere in hearts, the pool rose high. Pierrette, keeping her embroidery, watched the colonel. At a moment when the colonel’s play seemed to follow a hint, Sylvie cried that Pierrette had seen her hand and then accused the girl outright. Céleste and others insisted the colonel had simply played his game; Desfondrilles, the deputy-judge, swore Pierrette had seen only the colonel’s hand; notary Cournant echoed the verdict.But Gouraud flattered the idea that “little girls know how to slide their eyes into everything,” and persisted that Pierrette had peeked. Under pressure, Sylvie cried, “You are a story-teller and a little fool,” and the truthful Breton denied it, then fled in fear without a light, ran to her room, and struck her head on the open passage door. Sylvie, hearing the blow, cried, “Good! I’m glad of it.” Desfondrilles and Mademoiselle Habert observed the injury; Sylvie justified it by blaming Pierrette for disobedience. Madame de Chargeboeuf, laughing, suggested Sylvie pay her misere first, hinting at Sylvie’s notorious bad faith at cards. Sylvie sat and forgot Pierrette; her indifference surprised no one. When the game ended around half past nine, she flung herself into an easy chair by the fire and did not rise as guests departed. The colonel’s behavior tormented her; she did not know what to think. “Men are so false!” she cried as she went to bed.Pierrette had struck a blow on the head just above the ear; the next day there was a swelling. At breakfast Sylvie said, “God has punished you. You disobeyed me; you treated me with disrespect... you got what you deserved.” Rogron, more sensible, suggested she ought to put on a compress of salt and water. Pierrette, meek and dependent, accepted even such remarks as proofs of kindness.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The week continued in torment. Sylvie increased her cruelty; Pierrette, proud and stubborn, complained not, bearing vague and sharp pains while thinking constantly of Brigaut — fearing for him if discovered, comforted that he was nearby in Provins. The single glimpse she had of him lingered like an exile’s look at his country.Brigaut, moved by the same hunger to communicate, devised a simple plan: Pierrette should lower letters on a string from her window at a set hour. One market morning he slipped a note to her while pretending to buy fruit; she hid it in her apron, cheeks aflame. These two children, scarcely aware of the depth of their feelings, carried more emotion between them than many adult lovers. Sylvie, who did not notice Brigaut’s Breton accent, paid him no heed.That night Pierrette read Brigaut’s letter. He begged her to let down a string at midnight so they could exchange notes, called her the victim of “monsters,” invoked their Breton past, vowed to earn enough to take her home, said he had 150 francs and could send money by the string, and begged her to pray with him in church. The letter uplifted Pierrette; it was balm in her misery.Risking discovery, she stole pen, paper, and ink and wrote back: a simple outpouring naming Brigaut and her grandmother as the only people she loved; describing brutal treatment — forced to scrub pots, do rough work that marred her hands, be scolded and used like a dog — and begging rescue back to Pen-Hoël, to memories of sliding on the pond. She confessed pains in head and body, hid her tears, and asked if he would love her always. She weighted the note with a crust of bread and lowered it at midnight. Brigaut broke the string to take it and lingered beneath her window; they signaled to one another by candlelight for over an hour before parting.Next day Pierrette rose transformed — light, joyful — a change Sylvie noticed with the sharp jealousy of an old maid. At dinner Sylvie accused her of feigning illness or having a lover, ordered her to sit in the dining-room with a candle, and watched her closely. Pierrette endured; Brigaut’s promised nightly visits kept her alive. One midnight he left a new note explaining an owl-cry signal his father had taught him and said he would return in a few days with good news, hinting at measures to liberate her. He also wrote secretly to Madame Lorrain in Nantes, begging her to come for her granddaughter and to justify herself against rumours that she had taken Pierrette’s money; he feared the grandmother might be dead.Pierrette clung to Brigaut’s assurances as a desert traveler clings to a palm-tree. But Sylvie had grown suspicious. She had seen a figure under the window and then, alarmed, discovered Pierrette’s cord and began spying as relentlessly as any customs officer. Pierrette, foreseeing danger, sewed the letters into her corset and covered them. That seemed to end the lovers’ nightly meetings.Sylvie spent three nights awake, then sent Pierrette to confession and searched the garret, finding nothing; her resentment hardened into cruel watchfulness. Pierrette’s pains worsened; she developed a regular fever. One Sunday evening, amid a salon game, Pierrette fainted. The colonel caught and carried her to a sofa. Sylvie sneered it was a trick, and in front of guests forced Pierrette to unfasten her gown; when Sylvie felt papers in the corset she pretended outrage, calling the girl a hypocrite. Afterwards she detained Vinet, a lawyer and ally, and revealed through him her decision never to marry the colonel. Vinet, pragmatic and selfish, advised Sylvie to secure power by other means: find a rich wife for Rogron, or forge bargains that would leave Pierrette with nothing. He hinted the colonel was a gambler whose recklessness would ruin him unless he married a fortune. This planted schemes of revenge and deprivation in Sylvie’s mind.That night three owl-like cries echoed in the square. Pierrette, feverish, opened her window at the signal and lowered a ball of silk. Sylvie, awake and frantic, heard the noise, raced upstairs, and surprised Pierrette unfastening the cord. A violent struggle followed. Pierrette clenched the letter in her hand; Sylvie grabbed and twisted the child’s arm, dug her nails in, and — in frenzy — even bit the wrist, trying to force the hand open. Pierrette screamed, “Help! help! they are murdering me!” The grotesque contest between stoic innocence and savage jealousy left Pierrette exhausted and bleeding.At that instant loud knocks sounded at the door. Rogron opened; Brigaut rushed in, followed by a tall gray woman — Pierrette’s grandmother — whose sudden appearance changed everything. Seeing the torn corset and the bloody hand, the old woman carried Pierrette away in her arms to Frappier’s cabinet-maker’s house nearby, where Brigaut and the Frappiers improvised a bed. Pierrette fainted; the letter fell from her loosened fingers.Madame Lorrain’s presence cowed Sylvie. She had come from Pen-Hoël after receiving Brigaut’s urgent letter. The old Breton woman — solemn, spare-faced with glittering eyes — grasped in an instant what Pierrette had endured. She swore, knelt upon the bricks, and demanded Brigaut fetch the best doctor in Paris. Producing banknotes — forty-two thousand francs recently given her by a repentant creditor, Monsieur Collinet, to reimburse past losses — she offered funds for immediate care. Brigaut rode off by diligence for Bianchon in Paris, as Martener, the local doctor, advised.Martener examined Pierrette and found an abscess above the left ear; the bruised hand could not have been self-inflicted. He urged full disclosure of the maltreatment and awaited the Paris physician’s arrival while prescribing a quieting draught: Pierrette needed sleep. Left to her grandmother, the child told everything — the endless scolding, the heavy labor, the insults, the scalded and injured hands, the hunger for the Breton home — details that would later serve in a judicial inquiry. The grandmother’s knowledge of Pierrette’s suffering and her promises of security soothed the girl.Meanwhile Sylvie, stunned by the grandmother’s sudden might and by Brigaut’s letters she had read after ripping open the corset, slept in uneasy bewilderment, not foreseeing where her spite would lead. The colonel, intercepted by Vinet’s schemes and threatened by political and marital entanglements, found himself at odds with those around him. Vinet and Sylvie plotted to secure property and influence; the colonel, warned and enraged, demanded a rich wife before elections or threatened consequences.At Frappier’s Pierrette lay half-dead, her fingers unbent and bloodied, while those who loved her — Brigaut, Madame Lorrain, the Frappiers — stood silent. The grandmother, who had fetched the money that made rescue possible, clasped her hands and resolved to complain to the authorities and secure proper care. Martener, convinced this was no mere youthful pretence, prepared to consult with the best surgeon available. Brigaut had already left for Paris to fetch Bianchon.Thus the little drama — love, spying, cruelty, and rescue — reached a decisive moment. Pierrette, exhausted by the physical consequences of her courage, sank into a sleep made safe by her grandmother’s presence and a soothing draught, while the others, stunned and ashamed, anticipated the legal and moral reckonings to come.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At nine that morning Monsieur Martener told Monsieur Tiphaine about Pierrette’s condition: the quarrel with Sylvie, the physical and moral abuses by the Rogrons, and two serious illnesses those cruelties had produced. Tiphaine summoned Auffray, a notary and Pierrette’s maternal relative. The affair ignited a factional feud: Rogron supporters had already spread slanders about Madame Tiphaine’s mother and du Tillet, and Tiphaine saw Pierrette’s case as a chance to humiliate the Rogrons, who hosted a salon tied to anti‑monarchist activity and an opposition journal. The public prosecutor was alerted; Auffray was chosen as guardian so he could call a Family Council and, backed by testimony from three doctors, demand Pierrette’s release. Lesourd, the procureur, would steer the matter toward a criminal inquiry.By midday Provins was aflutter. Pierrette’s faint cries from the previous night became public horror when a crowd gathered at Frappier’s shop; Frappier reported her arrival with broken fingers and a bloody hand. Doctor Bianchon’s post‑chaise arrived about one; Martener and the hospital surgeon were summoned. The Rogrons were accused of deliberate ill‑treatment that had nearly killed their cousin.At the law courts Vinet hurried to the Rogrons. Under questioning Sylvie at first evaded, then admitted jealousy and, reluctantly, that her suspicions were baseless. Vinet, exploiting their fear, insisted on a plausible public motive for his defense. They contrived Rogron’s immediate marriage to Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf—announced that day and to have banns posted Sunday—and Sylvie agreed to appear in the marriage contract as assigning her capital while retaining income. Vinet urged antedating the contract to bind the Chargeboeufs publicly and preserve their visits, promising to defend them if they signed. By noon Vinet had authority to draw the contract and represent the Rogrons.At twelve a petition accused Brigaut and the widow Lorrain of abducting the minor Pierrette from her guardian’s house—turning the defenders into complainants. The hearing was postponed until four; Tiphaine wanted Auffray present with the doctors’ report. The marriage announcement and Sylvie’s concessions alienated two supporters, Mademoiselle Habert and Colonel Gouraud, who publicly stayed with the Rogrons but intended to harm them. Martener and others recounted that Pierrette had earlier struck herself the evening she was forced to leave the salon, citing Mademoiselle Rogron’s heartless remarks. Vinet, having secured the Chargeboeuf alliance, planned to use it to dominate Provins socially and politically.From midday to four the Tiphaine circle watched over Pierrette. Unaware of the tumult, she found consolation with her grandmother and Brigaut. Bianchon protested the barbarity and insisted all town physicians examine the case; Dr. Neraud, a Rogron friend, attended. The unanimous medical report explained Pierrette’s malady—a sadly common but horrifying cranial condition—and attributed it to ill‑treatment.At four President Tiphaine presided. Madame Lorrain, Auffray, Brigaut and others entered the court; Vinet alone represented the Rogrons. He claimed two strangers had forced entry into the Rogron house and abducted the minor. Auffray, as surrogate guardian, presented the physicians’ report—signed by a Paris authority and all Provins doctors—asserting that Pierrette’s near‑fatal condition resulted from ill‑treatment by Rogron and his sister. Auffray asked that Pierrette not be returned to the guardian’s household but remain with a family member and proposed convening a Family Council to consider deposing the guardian.Vinet objected that he had not seen the report and could not rebut it. The judge, noting the report might already have been submitted to the procureur du roi, heard the case anyway. Tiphaine forbade returning Pierrette to her guardian until the Family Council met, commissioned the attending physician and the hospital surgeon to inspect her, and stipulated that if her injuries were alarming the criminal courts would intervene, without prejudice to Auffray’s civil suit.Vinet scoffed, calling it fuss over a girl supposedly involved with an apprentice, and left to rally his faction. He framed the Tiphaines’ actions as partisan persecution and Sylvie’s intervention as natural for a jealous sister seizing letters.Frappier’s house was soon judged unsuitable; Pierrette was moved to Auffray’s with conspicuous care. Laid on a stretcher and accompanied by a Gray Sister and her grandmother, she passed under many windows, her pallor strengthening Auffray’s case. At Auffray’s she was installed on the second floor with Martener and the hospital surgeon attending; she was tenderly cared for.That evening the dispute polarized public opinion. The Rogron salon filled as Vinet rallied Liberals; the Chargeboeuf ladies dined there to sign the contract. Vinet minimized the Pierrette episode, confident royal courts would vindicate them; some accepted the Chargeboeuf alliance as wiping the Rogrons’ slate clean and depicting Pierrette as depraved. In Madame Tiphaine’s salon, vengeance for past slanders was turned on the Rogrons, now cast as monsters. Journalists offered opposing accounts: one claimed no case existed; the Rogron paper advised patience for the law.The Family Council, chosen by the juge de paix, included Rogron and the two Auffrays as nearest relatives, Monsieur Ciprey (a maternal nephew), Mademoiselle Habert, Pierrette’s confessor, and Colonel Gouraud. The judge’s choice seemed impartial—Habert and Gouraud had been seen as Rogron allies. Rogron requested Vinet for the council, postponing it to late December when Tiphaine would be in Paris—a delay tactic by Vinet.Vinet spoke for three hours before the council, trying to prove an intrigue between Pierrette and Brigaut to justify Sylvie’s severity and portraying the guardian’s delegation of duties as natural. Despite his efforts, the council unanimously removed Rogron’s guardianship; Auffray became guardian and Ciprey surrogate. Testimony included Adele the servant against her former masters, Mademoiselle Habert recounting Mademoiselle Rogron’s cruel remarks, and Brigaut’s letter from Pierrette describing her ill‑treatment. The council concluded Pierrette’s illness resulted from neglect by the guardian, and the case would proceed publicly. Rogron, advised by Vinet, opposed the council’s report being accepted by the court. Authorities intervened as Pierrette’s condition worsened; the trial, placed on the docket, was postponed until March 1828.Meanwhile Rogron married Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf; Sylvie moved to the second floor with her, and Madame Rogron supplanted Madame Tiphaine socially. Tiphaine, supported by Nucingen and du Tillet, advanced to civil judge and moved to Paris; his son inherited and sold the Provins house—evidence, in Vinet’s telling, of Tiphaine’s contempt for the town. The promotion yielded little for Vinet’s faction, but Vinet exploited the events politically. Thus Pierrette’s brutal martyrdom—culminating in trepanning performed under Bianchon’s and Martener’s advice—was mired in legal delay while she languished in pain.Martener and the Auffrays grew fond of Pierrette and her grandmother. Martener, who had begun his career in Paris but returned to provincial practice, devoted himself to curing her, treating her with reverent care. The Auffrays’ household fell quiet in her presence; their house, on a terrace beneath chateau ruins, became her refuge, and her room gave her rare views of a landscape she had seldom seen.Brigaut, unable to work, visited three times daily, faithful and sorrowful. The grandmother, concealing her despair to comfort Pierrette, made her a Breton cap recalling better times. Pierrette’s pale, serious features embodied melancholy; all served her devotedly. Madame Martener sent a piano that transfixed her; her calm resignation impressed clergy and lay visitors alike.Martener corresponded with Bianchon and sought to drain the abscess; periodic improvements cheered them, but the cranial disease remained grave. Bianchon brought the celebrated Desplein from Paris, who warned only a miracle could save the child: caries of the bone had begun and, at her age, bones were fragile. The operation was performed in early March 1828 by Desplein, who cautioned the odds. Martener had proposed an even bolder procedure—rejected by Desplein. Returning from Paris, Martener announced that science could do no more; recovery was left to God. They responded with masses and vows.While Pierrette lay dying, Vinet continued to malign her in court. The judge accepted the Family Council’s report; Vinet appealed. The procureur du roi required further evidence, including testimony from Pierrette. When Desfondrilles came to take it, Pierrette was dying with her confessor near for extreme unction. She begged forgiveness for her cousins, saying judgment belonged to God; she told her grandmother to leave all to Brigaut and to give Adele a thousand francs, insisting that had Adele stayed with the Rogrons she would not be dying.On a bright Tuesday of Easter week at three in the afternoon, Pierrette died. Her grandmother kept vigil and sewed her shroud. Brigaut went to Frappier’s to make the coffin; Frappier watched him plane ribbons of wood wet with tears and lined the coffin with lead. By chance the lead cost exactly what Pierrette had given Brigaut for her journey—a coincidence that overwhelmed him. He burned his tools, settled accounts, and bid farewell, his grief part of the awful finale of the Rogrons’ tyranny.At the Auffray house the room where Pierrette lay was crowded. Rogron’s emissaries stood near the body, and a surgeon in a dissecting apron prepared instruments; Madame Lorrain cried out, fearing they intended to open the body. Brigaut, who had just brought the coffin, seized a chisel, threatened the men, and swore he would kill anyone who touched her. Auffray and the curate restrained him; the lawyers and doctors left. The plumber soldered the lead to make the coffin harder to open. With Pierrette dead, the guardianship suit collapsed; Rogron did not dare attend her funeral.Brigaut left Provins, went to Paris, joined the Royal Guard, and afterward the Algerian expedition, rising to major and later an officer; he inherited forty‑six thousand francs from Madame Lorrain, who died in Paris in 1829. Vinet prospered politically—elected deputy in 1830 and later procureur‑general—while Rogron became receiver‑general and Tiphaine, after aligning with the July dynasty, became president of the Royal court. Madame Tiphaine and Madame Rogron lived amicably; Vinet remained close to Tiphaine. Rogron declined in health and sense; Sylvie managed his property and lived frugally. Desfondrilles, aided by Vinet, became president of the Provins court.Locals gradually softened the scandal into gossip about weak constitutions or imprudent liaisons; many involved felt no remorse. Only two men remembered Pierrette daily—Doctor Martener and Major Brigaut; they alone knew the hideous truth. To grasp the full scale of that truth one thinks of Beatrice Cenci—another young woman ruined by intrigues—reminding us that legality often serves scoundrelism unless one believes in a higher justice.ADDENDUM: Many characters recur in Balzac’s Human Comedy—Bianchon, Brigaut, Desplein, General Gouraud, Matifat, Nucingen, du Tillet, Madame Tiphaine, Vinet, and others appear in works like Father Goriot, Lost Illusions, César Birotteau, The Chouans, Cousin Pons, The Vendetta, The Member for Arcis, and The Firm of Nucingen.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# ############################################################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Early in the autumn of 1826 the Abbé Birotteau, the story’s central figure, was caught in a downpour returning from an evening with friends. Small, apoplectic and near sixty, he hurried across the deserted Cloister behind Saint-Gatien at Tours, fearing damp shoes. He was nonetheless in good spirits: he had just won three francs ten sous at rubber with Madame de Listomère, wrapped himself in a canon’s fur cape, and felt buoyed by talk that he might be named canon — a post he long coveted.On the Grand’Rue side of the Cloister a Close had become the narrow Rue de la Psalette. Opposite, a single house, overshadowed by Saint-Gatien’s buttresses, had long housed abbés and belonged to an old maid, Mademoiselle Gamard. Bought by her father during the Terror and accepted by the restored Church, the dim, cramped dwelling suited only those of absolute nullity or uncommon fortitude. Devout and economical, Mademoiselle Gamard boarded priests and ran the house with punctilious thrift.Birotteau had lived two years in that house and prized its rooms as highly as the hoped-for canonry. His two ambitions — being Mademoiselle Gamard’s boarder and becoming a canon — reflected a provincial priest’s craving for decent lodging, good food, clean clothes and the dignity of a title. His passion for the apartment had begun in admiration of his friend Chapeloud’s rooms and grown into obsession.Chapeloud, who had become canon when worship was restored under Napoleon, had long occupied the south-facing apartment, improving it with modest legacies and gifts: an oak bookcase from a demolished château, waxed floors, painted woodwork, a long ebony table, Boulle cabinets, folios and Fathers of the Church. He transformed shabby rooms into a dignified oratory and a salon hung with red damask and mahogany. Birotteau, poor and a peasant’s son like Chapeloud, admired these changes and secretly yearned for them. When Chapeloud fell ill, Birotteau’s thought ran, “If Chapeloud dies I can have this apartment.” He never contemplated influencing his friend’s will.Chapeloud, indulgent and aware of his friend’s weakness, forgave the covetousness and, on his deathbed while Birotteau read the Quotidienne, said, “This time you will certainly get the apartment.” He bequeathed his library and furniture to Birotteau, who, though saddened, found consolation in the inheritance and in Mademoiselle Gamard’s cheerful agreement to take him as lodger. He moved into the apartment and enjoyed comforts he had long imagined: meticulous linen, steady fires, well-selected wood, fragrant orris-root and the small attentions for which Chapeloud praised the spinster. Mademoiselle Gamard’s household was celebrated for its perfect order and attentive service.Yet having obtained his long-desired comforts, Birotteau soon replaced the old obsession with a new one: the canonry. Encouraged by Madame de Listomère’s talk, he believed promotion was near. Absorbed in these hopes, he did not at once notice, on returning one evening, that his umbrella was gone and that small domestic attentions were missing. He found the outer door locked, Marianne the servant answering awkwardly that Mademoiselle had thought he was out; his candlestick had been moved to an antechamber; the fire in his room had not been burning; his slippers were not placed where they usually were. These trivial changes he read as deliberate signs of displeasure. By a sort of clerical logic he concluded that Mademoiselle Gamard intended to chill and humiliate him. The small humiliations mortified him deeply.Birotteau was kindly but simple-minded and ignorant of worldly calculations; his goodness verged on stupidity. His learning was shallow, his life centered on mass and confession; he lacked frank self-knowledge about what he had done to provoke dislike. He could not perceive how unconscious selfishness and provincial narrowness — common failings — made him difficult. He was a clumsy egoist, unlike Chapeloud, who was a shrewd one.Chapeloud had understood Mademoiselle Gamard: an old maid of about thirty-eight whose pretensions amounted to a strong sense of self. He judged she needed constant, precise attentions if one would live comfortably under her roof. So he kept limited contact, avoided breakfast and supper, and came to dinner a few minutes early to pay polite compliments about the food and service. By these attentions, and by never creating points of contact beyond politeness, Chapeloud secured peaceful coexistence and gained praise for being easy to live with.Troubert, the other lodger, was Mademoiselle Gamard’s creature: obedient, patient and entirely under her rule, often suspected of coveting her property because of their intimate domestic life. When Chapeloud died the landlady at first considered offering the rooms to Troubert, but seeing Birotteau’s attachment she accepted him as lodger for her own advantage and even improved the rooms: replacing brick with wooden floors and rebuilding the chimney.But Birotteau, dazzled by his new condition, did not adopt Chapeloud’s circumspection. He fell into Mademoiselle Gamard’s life with the timidity of a man who fears seeming rude and lingers too long in conversation. He began to spend his evenings there; the spinster, whose real wish had long been to make her salon a weekly center of company, welcomed the chance to display more prominent visitors. When the vicar soon deserted her salon for Madame de Listomère’s and Mademoiselle Salomon’s — preferring aristocratic circles to which the landlady did not belong — his withdrawal gravely wounded Mademoiselle Gamard; she had hoped to use him to attract society. Her disappointment, joined to an old-maid’s craving for return on social investment and wounded pride, turned into resentment. The vicar, however, never understood this. He perceived, belatedly and clumsily, signs of slights and concluded that ill will was directed at him though he could not see the reasons.Mademoiselle Gamard, like many who refuse to adapt their habits to changing lives, indulged a petty despotism over small things: the basket of counters, the arrangement of cards, the order of household routine. Such trifles she magnified into tests of obedience. The vicar, unlike Chapeloud, could not accept being persistently cinched by such crochets; he withdrew rather than submitting, irritating her more by his absence than by any direct challenge.Her friends, defending her, called Chapeloud amiable and blamed Birotteau for turning away from the old maid; society often sided with those who escaped the Cloister’s nightly round. Mademoiselle Gamard enjoyed the condolence she received, and Troubert explained the departure by saying the abbé sought fashion, luxury and witty talk — flattering her with the implication he preferred higher company.In truth, the vicar’s abandonment was a clumsy desertion, and his naïveté and lack of social tact gradually made him intolerable to his landlady. Eighteen months after she had taken him in, when he mistook her silence for contentment, she had in fact begun a deliberate, underhand persecution. The locked door, the forgotten slippers, the unlit fire and the moved candlestick were its first signs. This prologue, recounting the small domestic developments that precede the greater drama, insists on particulars: the petty meannesses, jealousies and ambitions of bourgeois life inflict injuries as keen as those produced by grander interests, and their slow, minute growths require this account to explain the coming catastrophe.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning Birotteau woke so absorbed in thoughts of the prospective canonry that he had already forgotten the four ominous signs of the previous night. He did not rise without a fire; he rang for Marianne and lay half-asleep in his usual reveries. When she had not come after twenty minutes he was about to ring again, but heard footsteps on the stairs. After a discreet knock, the Abbe Troubert, who visited him about once a month, entered. Seeing there was no fire, Birotteau sharply called for Marianne, opened the window and told her to come at once; then, quite naively, he told Troubert the steps he had taken and even the names Madame de Listomere was using in his favor—unaware that Troubert bore a grudge against that lady for twice having snubbed him when he was proposed as vicar-general by the bishop.Troubert and Birotteau presented striking contrasts. Troubert, tall, lean, yellow-faced and bilious, had long, deeply wrinkled features that often expressed sarcasm or contempt; he spoke little, seemed grave, and was suspected of ambition. His imposing presence, cautious conduct, saintly life and failing health removed fears about his alleged ability; his chronic malady made it likely he would soon be vicar-general. Birotteau was plump, ruddy, kindly, talkative and simple—qualities that marked him permanently as a vicar and nothing higher. His robust appearance, triple chin and even his gout seemed to others guarantees of long life and offered no hope of elevation.The Abbe Chapeloud, a sensible, amiable man popular in Tours, had quietly opposed Troubert’s rise by keeping him out of the best salons. Troubert treated Chapeloud with deference while Chapeloud remained unconvinced: “Distrust that lean stick of a Troubert,—Sixtus the Fifth reduced to the limits of a bishopric!” he had said to Birotteau.Troubert had come that morning, the day after Mademoiselle Gamard—Birotteau’s landlady and the old maid who had seemingly declared war on the vicar—had behaved harshly toward him, simply to ask to borrow Birotteau’s Register of Bishops. Birotteau, pleased, sent him to the library while he dressed. Presently the breakfast bell rang; Birotteau, reflecting that without Troubert he would have had no fire, thought, “He’s a kind man.”The two priests carried huge folios to the dining-room table. Mademoiselle Gamard greeted them sharply. “I hope you are not going to litter up my dining-room with your old books!” she snapped at Birotteau. Troubert explained he had borrowed them from the vicar. Mademoiselle Gamard remarked contemptuously that Birotteau did not often read such large books.Birotteau answered with his usual mellifluous politeness. She replied that he had woken her the night before out of her first sleep and had kept her awake. Sitting down, she added, “Gentlemen, the milk is getting cold.” Stupefied at the ill-natured reception and alarmed at the prospect of a dispute, the timid vicar fell silent. He struggled inwardly between feeling he should not submit to discourtesy and his instinct to avoid quarrel.To distract himself he examined the green lines painted on the oilcloth she habitually left on the breakfast table. They sat opposite each other; she dominated the head of the table from a high, cushioned chair near the stove. When Mademoiselle Gamard handed him his coffee, the vicar felt chilled by the silence and dared not meet Troubert’s dried features or the landlady’s threatening face. He turned to the fat pug on a cushion by the stove and addressed it, as he often did, to keep up conversation on his theory that speech favored digestion. The dog, once important in the household, merely closed his eyes peevishly.Mademoiselle Gamard believed in health maxims and in talking at meals; Birotteau had for some mornings strained to find topics to loosen her tongue. Their conversations, if recorded, would have illustrated provincial ignorance and credulous gossip—absurdities about Napoleon’s death, the Dauphin, taxes, and other political and medical superstitions. But that morning Birotteau felt tongue-tied; after a long pause he risked, “This coffee is excellent,” and later, “It will be finer to-day than yesterday.” At the second remark she cast her most gracious look toward Troubert, then turned on Birotteau with terrible severity.Mademoiselle Sophie Gamard was the quintessential old maid. To understand her is to see how society condemns the unproductive consumer, and how celibacy—whether from lack of charm, deliberate independence, or mischance—isolates and degrades a woman’s standing. Deprived of activities through which womanly qualities develop and are exercised, such women are starved of purpose. Bitterness, jealousy and wounded pride follow; they blame others for their condition, then themselves, and often turn to revenge. Their failure shows in an oblique, ashamed glance, a hardness of manner, and a certain selfishness: used to being useless, they prefer what is convenient for themselves to what would please others. Habits of self-centeredness produce peevishness and lack of refinement; deprived of love’s practice, their faculties wither.All these truths could be read in Mademoiselle Gamard’s dull gray eyes, the dark circles under them, the straight lines of her wrinkles. Her sparse chin hair, thin lips barely covering long teeth, dark complexion, and the gaps where she badly adjusted a false front revealed a carelessness born of inward conflict. Her thin brown gowns, limp collar and thick, ribbed neck matched the hard, angular movements of a woman who had risen from rural origins—the daughter of a wood-merchant—and who carried in her face and gait the despotism of character and narrowness of mind. Her abrupt gestures, loud nasal blows on her handkerchief, and erect, blocklike stride betrayed a lack of grace. When in good humor she would recount missed chances of marriage and men’s lack of means, proof, to her, that worldly prudence had prevailed over the heart.Her dining-room showed her taste: varnished paper with Turkish landscapes, two pier tables, a barometer, little faded worsted cushions before each abbé’s chair. Her salon, “the yellow salon,” was yellow in curtains, furniture and walls; the mantelpiece with a gilt mirror, crystal candlesticks and clock seemed sharp and precise. Her private apartment was never shown; conjecture thought it full of odds, worn-out furniture, and things dear to an old maid.Unable to expend womanly activity naturally, she had converted it into petty intrigues, provincial cabals and self-seeking schemes—the occupation of many old maids. Birotteau had developed in her the only strong feeling she could muster: hatred. This hatred, previously latent beneath provincial calm, became intense because expended on petty things within a narrow sphere. Birotteau’s timidity and incapacity to see the world’s malice made him especially helpless before her. He was the sort of man doomed to suffer because he could not avoid the harms that beset him.After breakfast, unnerved by the silence, Birotteau left with the coffee heavy in his stomach and strolled among the narrow, box-edged paths of the little garden, laid out in a star. Turning after a first round, he saw Mademoiselle Gamard and Troubert standing motionless on the threshold; he felt as if they were watching and counting his steps. Nothing is so distressing to a timid person as being closely examined, more so when those eyes express hatred. Embarrassed, he fancied he was in their way and, moved by fear and kindness, left for Saint-Gatien, where funerals, a marriage and baptisms occupied him and eased his pain.When his watch told him dinner should be ready he hurried back, mindful of Mademoiselle Gamard’s punctuality. Passing the kitchen, he saw the first course had been removed. In the dining-room she said sourly, with evident pleasure at finding ground for reproach, “It is half-past four, Monsieur Birotteau. You know we are not to wait for you.” The vicar looked at the clock and noticed, by the moved gauze, that the hands had been set ahead of the cathedral clock. He said nothing; any remark would have provoked one of her fierce expositions, in which women of her class could be eloquently censorious. Mademoiselle Gamard instinctively divined a thousand petty annoyances that might provoke a timid man like Birotteau, and delighted in plotting against his domestic comfort—always so contrived as to leave her apparently never in the wrong. Her methods showed a profoundly malignant ingenuity: she contrived to torment him while keeping the appearance of justice and propriety.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Eight days after the events that began this story, changes in Abbé Birotteau’s household and in Mademoiselle Gamard’s behavior convinced him a long-simmering plot had matured. At first the annoyances—petty thefts, a tampered candlestick and clock—were trifling, but enough to make him feel watched. Gamard, pleased with her long-planned revenge, discharged it with the meticulous malice of a woman who channels life into petty observances.These torments wounded the timorous, self-pitying vicar, who could not bring himself to confide in friends for fear of seeming ridiculous. The secrecy undermined his health and pride; finally he resolved to ask Abbé Troubert for official intervention. In Troubert’s cold, austere room the vicar, ashamed to intrude, described his plight.
