Troubert listened with some amusement and attributed the difficulty partly to Birotteau’s distraction by elevated thoughts; he offered to arbitrate but insisted on hearing both sides. He advised patience, suggesting Gamard’s temper shifts might stem from illness, and implied that if Birotteau stayed longer he might come to esteem her better.Birotteau left baffled and, unable to consult anyone, decided to escape the tension by spending a week at Madame de Listomère’s country house, the Alouette—ironically the exact action Gamard wanted. Ten days into his stay, Gamard’s lawyer, Monsieur Caron, arrived in Tours to ask how long Birotteau intended to be away; his prolonged absence might provoke dispute. Dazed, the vicar asked for time to take advice.At the Alouette the company rallied to his aid. Madame de Listomère suspected Troubert wanted the canonry; Monsieur de Listomère urged resistance; Monsieur de Bourbonne suggested Gamard might be scheming to turn him out. Then Mademoiselle Salomon de Villenoix arrived with decisive news: the vicar-general was gravely ill and had temporarily delegated his powers to Abbé Troubert, making the canonry effectively dependent on him. Abbé Poirel had recently discussed Birotteau’s supposed annoyances to Gamard in a way that cast blame on the vicar. Madame de Listomère bluntly asked whether Birotteau preferred becoming a canon or staying with Gamard; all advised securing the canonry. Acting on this counsel, a servant fetched Caron back.Emotion overcame caution. In a brief conference Caron urged Birotteau to sign a paper relinquishing domicile. Confused, exhausted and pressured by respected friends, the vicar mechanically signed: he declared he had left Gamard’s house of his own will and had been properly lodged and fed. Caron asked where to send his belongings; Birotteau named Madame de Listomère. De Bourbonne, skeptical, examined the paper and noted it implied prior agreements between Birotteau and Gamard. The deed, he was told, lay in Birotteau’s library.Encouraged by Madame de Listomère and Mademoiselle Salomon, who soothed him like mothers, Birotteau briefly forgot his misery at the prospect of living with them. But that night reality returned: moving meant scattering books and furniture, ending the habits that composed his life. He could not accept trading a year of peace in Gamard’s house for the canonry. He clung to Mademoiselle Salomon’s friendship but distrusted new attachments.Mademoiselle Salomon’s character explains her intense sympathy: having once tended a mad lover for years, she had since devoted herself to the feeble and helpless. Early the next morning she returned Birotteau to Tours and left him at the cathedral quay to see to the Cloister. At his house he found his old apartment occupied; Marianne informed him Troubert was installed in his rooms. Troubert, comfortably seated in Chapeloud’s library, confirmed the depth of the conspiracy with a sardonic calm that left the abbé dumbstruck.Troubert told him Gamard had notified him of Birotteau’s departure and that his installation had been necessary; if the furniture were Birotteau’s, Gamard’s scrupulous honesty would ensure its return; if Birotteau wished to return, Troubert would yield the apartment. Devastated, Birotteau sought Gamard and met her on the staircase. When he protested she should have allowed him to pack, she reminded him of the clause in their agreement: because there was an eight-hundred-franc yearly difference between the board paid by the late Abbé Chapeloud and Gamard’s charges—and because Gamard had lent sums to make up Chapeloud’s legacy duties—Birotteau had engaged, in consideration of those loans, to leave all his household property to Gamard at death or to transfer it should he voluntarily give up the apartment. With Poirel’s recent nomination as canon, Gamard exulted. Overwhelmed, Birotteau fled.At Madame de Listomère’s he found his linen and papers already packed in a trunk. Poirel’s nomination meant he had no home, no means, and no furniture. Mademoiselle Salomon, thinking his distress sprang from the shock of the appointment, had him taken back to the Alouette; friends tended him until he recounted the morning’s events. De Bourbonne examined the deed and cursed Gamard for being “armed with claws.” Birotteau had never suspected anything would separate him from the house where he had hoped to live and die; in his eagerness to enter Gamard’s house he had signed without looking closely.Madame de Listomère insisted she had made him sign and must restore him to happiness. De Bourbonne suggested the deed might be fraudulent; the Baron and others urged litigation. Privately, de Bourbonne warned that many friends would soon desert him and hinted that true peace might come if Birotteau resigned the vicariate and left Tours for a distant parish where Troubert could not pursue him—a thought that horrified the vicar, for he equated leaving Tours with death. Celibate attachments had been replaced by habits—walks, salons and small social pleasures—that sustained him.Word spread that Madame la Baronne de Listomère had invited Birotteau to stay; the baroness’s reception implicitly censured Gamard and wounded her deeply. To understand Gamard’s resources one must note her network around Troubert: she made evening rounds with him to houses where circles of old maids, gossip-prone men and busybodies met. These women, ever ready to retail scandal, functioned as a gossip-police that transmitted household secrets to Troubert and shaped local opinion. The quarrel became the absorbing passion of these salons—small-town politics amplifying a private dispute.Madame de Listomère took Birotteau to her lawyer, who found the case difficult. Friends who had earlier assumed justice would be easily had delayed action; Gamard’s allies had taken the initiative and were shaping public opinion. The lawyer, practiced with the devout, warned against litigation: the deed gave Gamard the law, and in equity judges and respectable laymen might view Birotteau as ungrateful. Gamard had lent him money to pay Chapeloud’s legacy duties without a formal receipt; Birotteau, inexperienced and unsuspicious, had lived under Gamard’s roof for only two years while others had been there far longer; suing could look like ingratitude. The lawyer refused to take the case and urged Madame de Listomère not to embroil herself.De Bourbonne counseled caution; the Baron demanded action. A radical lawyer was finally engaged, a choice that polarized opinion and attracted political and anti-clerical energies. An expert from the Museum appraised the paintings and Gothic furniture at substantial sums: the Virgin of Valentin and the Christ of Lebrun at 11,000 francs, the bookshelves and furniture at about 12,000, and the total appraisal exceeding 36,000 francs. On this basis Birotteau could not plausibly have intended to give away such wealth; legally he had equitable grounds to challenge the agreement, and if refused, Gamard’s conduct might amount to intentional fraud. The radical lawyer’s writ was skillfully drafted and so aggressive in its denunciation of the old maid that copies were maliciously distributed through town, inflaming the controversy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few days after his quarrel with the old maid began, the Baron de Listomere—expecting promotion to corvette captain—received a letter warning he might be put on the retired list. Astonished, he went to Paris and to the minister, who laughed off his fears; yet the next day a secretary indiscreetly showed him a document confirming the news, awaiting only the director’s signature.Hurrying to an uncle who was a deputy, the baron learned the true reason: the minister had first accused him of Radical sympathies, but the real issue was ecclesiastical. The baron had spoken slightingly of Abbe Troubert, the vicar-general and an influential Jesuit proconsul in the province. His uncle warned him to make peace at once; Troubert might become a bishop and could even harm the uncle’s prospects.This explained to the baron the secretive activity that Birotteau had long wondered about: Troubert “sits up all night.” Troubert’s position, skill, and female network in Tours made him powerful; archbishop, general, prefect—all yielded to his covert influence. The Baron de Listomere resolved to avoid another attack.Three days later he told his aunt the family must abandon support of “that fool of a Birotteau.” He detained Monsieur de Bourbonne for counsel. Bourbonne, clear-sighted and already aware of Troubert’s strength, advised a graceful retreat: bow to Troubert but not so low as to be trampled; feign an intention to leave the service; send Birotteau away to placate Mademoiselle Gamard; invite Troubert to the salon—women could win priests; make a slight menace to retain bargaining power. “If some clever Radical gets that empty head, he may cause trouble,” he warned, urging immediate dismissal of Birotteau lest a court verdict favor him and alarm Troubert.The next morning Madame de Listomere, embarrassed, asked Birotteau to withdraw his suit against Mademoiselle Gamard and to leave her house. She explained plainly the wider stakes—the web of revenge and Troubert’s power, whose hatred of Chapeloud gave motive for seizing Chapeloud’s property and persecuting his friend. Birotteau, pale and shaken, listened as one at a precipice. The baroness promised he would lack nothing and that Bourbonne would arrange matters to save appearances. “Grant me the right to abandon you,” she begged.The bewildered abbe, admitting Chapeloud had been right about Troubert’s malice, yielded: “I trust myself to you—I am but the stubble of the streets.” He asked only for Chapeloud’s portrait; the baroness promised to consult Mademoiselle Gamard and the archbishop.Birotteau left dismayed; Troubert now seemed a pyramid whose hands were in Paris and elbows in the cloister. The Listomeres’ explanation to the town—that the nephew meant to marry and leave the navy, seeking the vicar’s apartment—masked their submission to the Congregation’s occult power. The lawsuit still awaited judgment; their opponents had yielded while menacing.Mademoiselle Gamard then caught cold leaving the cathedral and grew dangerously ill. The town’s women bewailed her: “Mademoiselle Gamard’s sensitive nature could not bear the scandal; in spite of the justice of her cause she may die of grief—Birotteau has killed his benefactress.” Such talk spread through Tours.Madame de Listomere went to pay her promised visit, but the old maid was too ill. She asked to see the vicar-general. Troubert, gratified to receive a woman who had hitherto ignored him, kept her waiting and then received her with consummate dissimulation. Bourbonne had coached the baroness to be a mediator, not an interested party. Their conversation became a duel of implied meanings. Madame de Listomere expressed regret at the lawsuit and offered a deed of relinquishment for the dying woman; she hoped to end the matter and show compassion to Birotteau, who had asked for Chapeloud’s portrait as the price of renunciation.Troubert, with a calculating air, asked Mademoiselle Gamard and returned with her reply: she would not part with Chapeloud’s portrait—her feelings for her late friend were too faithful. Troubert himself stated he would not yield it if it were his: his loyalty justified his right. The baroness tried to smooth things—proposing a copy of the picture and inviting him to whist—but Troubert, stroked by vanity, promised to oblige for her sake.The baroness left, hoping the archbishop would complete the peace. But Mademoiselle Gamard died the next day. Her will left everything to Abbe Troubert; the fortune was appraised at three hundred thousand francs. He promptly sent the Listomeres invitations to the services and funeral; the baroness insisted on attendance. Bourbonne added that the baron must go to the cemetery; the young lieutenant, unluckily, had not yet left Tours.The funeral services displayed unusual ecclesiastical magnificence. Only Birotteau wept, kneeling in a side chapel; he believed himself guilty and prayed for the deceased, lamenting that he had not gained her forgiveness. Troubert followed the body to the grave and delivered an eloquent discourse that enlarged the old maid’s life to monumental proportions; in the peroration he declared her innocent and, revealing more joy than grief, sprinkled the coffin with holy water.The next morning Mademoiselle Salomon told Madame de Listomere that “our poor Abbe Birotteau has just received a frightful blow: he is appointed curate of Saint-Symphorien.” Saint-Symphorien lay beyond the bridge, a three-mile suburb—an exile in sight of the town. The parsonage was cold and damp, the parish too poor; the removal seemed an infamous plot.To end the history: five months later Troubert was made Bishop of Troyes; Madame de Listomere died, leaving an annuity of fifteen hundred francs to Abbe Birotteau. When the baroness’s will was read, Monseigneur Hyacinthe, Bishop of Troyes, delayed his departure from Tours on receiving the news and, furious at being foiled by the baroness—whom he had lately received while she had secretly aided a man he counted an enemy—Troubert again threatened the baron’s career and jeopardized his uncle’s peerage. He made one of those soft, vengeful priestly speeches in the archbishop’s salon. The Baron de Listomere then submitted; his later conduct showed entire subservience to the Jesuit’s will.The new bishop deeded Mademoiselle Gamard’s house to the cathedral chapter, gave Chapeloud’s books and bookcases to the seminary, and presented the two disputed pictures to the Chapel of the Virgin—but he kept Chapeloud’s portrait. No one could explain this near-total renunciation of Gamard’s bequest; Bourbonne supposed the bishop had secretly kept invested monies to support his rank in Paris. Only the night before Troubert’s departure did Bourbonne unearth the hidden reason: the baron had contested Madame de Listomere’s legacy to Birotteau on grounds of undue influence. The deathblow to the feeblest victim was complete.A few days after the case was brought the baron was promoted to captain. Ecclesiastical discipline followed: the curate of Saint-Symphorien—Birotteau—was suspended; his superiors judged him guilty. The murderer of Sophie Gamard was also a swindler; had Troubert kept the property openly, it would have been harder to censure Birotteau.When Monseigneur Hyacinthe drove along the quay by Saint-Symphorien in a post-chaise on his way to Paris, poor Birotteau sat in an armchair in the sun on a terrace above the road. Pale and haggard, smitten by the archbishop, he was the skeleton of the man who less than a year earlier had rolled contentedly along the cloister. The bishop cast one look of pity and contempt, then went his way.There is little doubt Troubert might once have been a Hildebrand or an Alexander VI. In our era the Church is no longer the political power it once was; celibacy concentrates a man’s faculties on a single passion—egotism—which makes celibates either useless or mischievous. We live in an age when governments tend to make Man for Society rather than Society for Man. There is a perpetual struggle between the Individual and a social system that uses him while he tries to use it. In earlier times man served first father, then clan or city; later caste or religion; today his life attaches to a vast country and perhaps someday to the whole universe.Will such moral cosmopolitanism—Christian Rome’s hope of Brotherhood—prove only a sublime error? It is natural to believe in such a noble vision, but human nature lacks divine proportions. Souls vast enough to hold a world of feeling are rare. Those who bear a science, a nation, or a code may seem egotistical, but that passionate concentration creates peoples and ideas; to do so they unite the tenderness of woman and the force of God. The histories of Innocent III, Peter the Great, and other great leaders show this same vast thought of which Troubert was but a provincial representative.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.     Birotteau, Abbe Francois       The Lily of the Valley       Cesar Birotteau     Bourbonne, De       Madame Firmiani     Listomere, Baronne de       Cesar Birotteau       The Muse of the Department     Troubert, Abbe Hyacinthe       The Member for Arcis     Villenoix, Pauline Salomon de       Louis Lambert       A Seaside Tragedy############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################ ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################To Monsieur Charles Nodier, member of the French Academy.My dear Nodier, I offer you a book of domestic deeds hidden from courts, where the finger of God, often called chance, supplants human justice, and morals—though sometimes mocked—remain striking and instructive.These stories teach much about Family and Maternity. Perhaps too late, we may learn the consequences of the weakening of paternal authority. That authority once endured until a father’s death and served as the domestic tribunal for private crimes; kings sometimes enforced it. However tender a mother, she cannot fulfill the patriarchal function any more than a woman can occupy a king’s throne. In this work I show plainly the necessity of the indissoluble marriage bond, the fatal effects of feminine weakness, and the danger of unrestrained selfish interests. A society that idolizes wealth should shudder at the impotence of law when success is deified and every means of attaining it forgiven. May it return to the Catholic faith for moral purification and an education broader than that of a lay university.In the “Scenes from Military Life” many noble self-devotions will appear; let me point out how the necessities of war can corrupt minds, which then behave at home as if still on campaign.Your sagacious view of our age and your bitter reflections have perceived the havoc wrought by four distinct political regimes. I dedicate this history under your protection; your name may shield it from the criticisms that will follow—for where is the patient who stays silent when the surgeon lifts the dressing from his wound?With pleasure in dedicating this Scene to you and pride in your friendship, I remainYour sincere admirer,  De Balzac  Paris, November, 1842.THE TWO BROTHERSCHAPTER IIn 1792 the town of Issoudun had Doctor Rouget, a physician regarded as malign in temper. People said he made his wife miserable, though she was the neighborhood beauty. Whether the wife was foolish or merely weak in health—rumored to be why the doctor married her—friends and enemies never wholly exposed the household. Rouget was of the “not pleasant to deal with” sort; townsmen treated him civilly. His wife, a demoiselle Descoings, bore a son and, ten years later, a surprise daughter, Agathe.These facts matter: without them Rouget might read as a monster, yet he only exemplified the evil sheltered under the axiom that “men should have strength of character,” a maxim that has caused much female misery.The elder Descoings, Agathe’s maternal grandparents, were wool commission merchants who grew rich and stingy. Their son Descoings disliked Issoudun, moved to Paris, established as a grocer in the rue Saint-Honoré, and, with love, patience, and some support from his parents, married his predecessor’s widow, Madame Bixiou.In 1792 Descoings seemed prosperous. When the elder Descoings retired he bought forfeited estates. Doctor Rouget, perhaps foreseeing his wife’s death, sent Agathe to Paris to his brother-in-law to let her see the capital and to win the affection of childless relatives. Agathe, the handsomest maiden of Issoudun, did not resemble her parents. Her birth had caused a breach between Rouget and his friend Monsieur Lousteau, and Madame Rouget spent much time with Madame Hochon, Lousteau’s sister and Agathe’s godmother, to whom she confided her sorrows; Madame Hochon later told most of Issoudun what it knew of Madame Rouget.When Rouget told his wife he would send Agathe to Paris, the first words from the mother were, “I shall never see my daughter again.” Madame Hochon thought she was right. The poor mother grew yellow with grief amid the foolish neglect of her son Jean-Jacques, who added to her misery. Jean-Jacques resembled his father in the worst ways.Agathe’s arrival did not bring happiness to her uncle. About the same time Descoings was imprisoned after an accusation by citoyenne Duplay, Robespierre’s housekeeper, who took offense at his remarks about famine and denounced him for “accaparation.” His wife’s ill-advised pleas and appeals to officials, though earnest, only worsened matters. Bridau, secretary to Roland, intervened on the family’s behalf and was struck by Agathe’s calm, innocent beauty; he married her with her father’s consent. Doctor Rouget, eager to secure his purposes, hurried to Paris to draw the marriage contract to his advantage. Bridau’s ardent and disinterested love gave carte blanche to the perfidious doctor, who exploited his son-in-law’s blindness—a practice that this story will expose.Madame Rouget, and then the elder Descoings, died; Rouget acquired vineyards, farms, and ironworks. Jean-Jacques proved incapable and was allowed by his father to become an idle proprietor. After his wife’s death in 1799, Rouget led a debauched life within his house and died in 1805. Jean-Jacques remained a bachelor, for reasons partly his father’s fault.Agathe, whom her father scarcely recognized as his child, bore a likeness to Rouget’s mother—an odd skipping of resemblance that vexes heredity. In turn, Agathe’s eldest son, who resembled her physically, inherited the moral qualities of his grandfather.Agathe’s beauty was pure and placid—an oval face, clear complexion, golden hair, a composed mouth, long lashes, and tender dark-blue eyes. Bridau’s portrait of her hangs in his atelier. She was the ideal provincial housekeeper, pious though not sanctimonious, educated by the Church. She was an accomplished wife but ignorant of life, which made her vulnerable: the Roman epitaph “She did needlework and kept the house” fitted her.Under the Consulate Bridau adored Napoleon. In 1804 he became head of a department at the ministry of the interior with a salary of twelve thousand francs and handsome emoluments. From 1804 to 1808 the Bridaus lived in a handsome suite on the Quai Voltaire near the ministry and the Tuileries, keeping only a cook and footman. Agathe rose early to market with her cook, prepared breakfast, saw Bridau off, cleared the table, cared for the children, and sat knitting by his writing table when he worked late. They attended the theatre occasionally and used a ministerial box; Bridau received friends on Thursdays and gave an annual Shrove-Tuesday ball.Their conjugal life contained three notable events: the birth of two sons three years apart and Bridau’s death in 1808, killed by overwork just as Napoleon planned to appoint him director-general, count, and councillor of state. An inflammatory fever from exertion took him before the Emperor could reward him. Napoleon, moved by Bridau’s devotion, inquired into his widow’s circumstances: both sons received scholarships at the Imperial Lyceum from the Emperor’s privy purse, and Madame Bridau was given a pension of four thousand francs.From marriage to her husband’s death Agathe had no communication with Issoudun. Her mother died as she was about to bear her youngest son; when her father died, the coronation occupied Bridau and Agathe chose not to leave him. Her brother Jean-Jacques did not write. Once a year she received a letter from her godmother Madame Hochon and replied with commonplaces, paying little heed to her pious advice.Some time before Doctor Rouget’s death Madame Hochon warned Agathe she would get nothing from her father’s estate unless she gave a power of attorney to Monsieur Hochon. Agathe, reluctant to harass her brother, let matters lie. Whether because Bridau thought the transfers legal or shared his wife’s magnanimity, he refused Roguin, his notary’s, advice to use his position to contest the deeds by which her father had deprived her. Thus husband and wife tacitly sanctioned the transfer. Roguin, however, forced Bridau to reflect that if he died first Agathe would be left without property. Reviewing their finances, Bridau found that between 1793 and 1805 they had used nearly thirty thousand of the fifty thousand francs that old Rouget had given Agathe at her marriage. He invested the remaining twenty thousand in public funds, yielding about two thousand francs a year. As a widow, Agathe could live suitably on an income of six thousand francs; with prudence she planned to move, dismiss the footman, and keep only a cook. But her intimate friend Madame Descoings—insisting on being considered an aunt—sold her establishment and came to live with Agathe, taking Bridau’s study as her bedroom.The two widows pooled their revenues, jointly possessing about twelve thousand francs a year. Such seemingly “natural” measures deserve attention, for fools excuse wrongs as natural. Here, the serpent lies amid flowers.In 1809 Madame Descoings, never revealing her age, was sixty-five. Once a beauty, she had kept her looks by constitution—medium height, plump, fresh, with fine shoulders and a rosy complexion; her blond hair showed no gray despite her husband’s fate. She loved good cheer, the theatre, and secretly gambled in lottery shares. She dressed well, was the pleasantest of companions, and never opposed anyone. If she did not marry again, it was likely due to the times: during the Empire men found young women too easily.Madame Descoings cheered Madame Bridau with theatre and drives, prepared dinners, and tried to marry her to her own son by her first husband, Bixiou. To this end she revealed a family secret: her son Bixiou, thirty-five, a widowed major who later perished at Lützen, had a son—a grandson she secretly saw and claimed as the child of her first wife. That boy, educated with Agathe’s sons at the Imperial Lyceum on a half-scholarship, proved clever, witty, and gifted as a draughtsman and designer.Agathe, devoted to her children, declined to remarry out of fidelity to her husband and common sense. But being a good mother is harder than being a good wife: a widow must both love and command her children, and few women are firm enough for both. Thus Agathe, despite many virtues, became the innocent cause of grave unhappiness. Through lack of worldly intelligence and blind confidence, she fell victim to Madame Descoings, who gambled with the household money, nursing a hope for a winning combination that would enrich her grandson, Agathe, and the Bridaus.When debts reached ten thousand francs Madame Descoings increased her stakes; failing to win, the debt rolled up. At twenty thousand francs she lost her head but still hoped. She attempted to mortgage property, but Roguin showed that Doctor Rouget had secured her only a life-interest, disinheriting Madame Descoings in favor of his son Jean-Jacques; no lender would advance twenty thousand francs to a sixty-six-year-old on an annuity of about four thousand. One morning Madame Descoings fell at Agathe’s feet and confessed the state of things. Agathe reproached her not; she dismissed the footman and cook, sold all but necessities, converted most of the government funds to pay the debts, and bade farewell to her appartement.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One of the worst corners in Paris was the stretch of the rue Mazarin between the rue Guenegard and the rue de Seine, behind the Institute: high gray walls, chill shadows, little sun and a constant north wind. The poor widow Madame Bridau took a third-floor lodging in a shallow house on that damp, dark corner, opposite the Institute’s studios where art pupils—rapins—lived in melancholy, barred cages. From her windows she saw the dome of the Institute and a street traversed only by hackney-coaches. She had chosen the lodging because it cost only four hundred francs a year on a nine-year lease, because it was near her sons’ school, and because she knew the quarter.The interior matched the exterior. The dining-room, hung with yellow paper patterned with small green flowers and floored with unglazed tiles, contained only a table, two sideboards, and six chairs brought from the other apartment. The salon had an Aubusson carpet given when the ministry was refurnished and a common mahogany sofa covered in green silk. Over it hung an amateur pastel of Bridau—his noble brow and frank smile suggesting a man of duty, the Emperor’s “Justum et tenacem.” Opposite was a bright Vernet of the Emperor on horseback. Agathe, after her husband’s death, had indulged herself in two large birdcages for canaries and Java sparrows; the lodging soon looked littered—cats on the sofa, canaries loose at times, millet and chickweed scattered, tidbits in broken saucers, garments everywhere. Everything of Bridau’s was scrupulously preserved: his desk implements wrapped and sealed, his last pen carefully kept, the cup from which he had drunk his last draught left on the mantel.Having lost the only man she had known and loved, Agathe renounced coquetry and ordinary self-care and lived only for her children. A melancholy took her face after the move. She placed small hopes in the Emperor, who already gave three hundred francs a year to each child from his privy purse, besides scholarships. The brilliant Madame Descoings lived on the second floor; she had advanced Madame Bridau three thousand francs out of her annuity through Roguin the notary, a loan that would take years to restore. Reduced to an income of twelve hundred francs, the Descoingses lived modestly with her niece, keeping a woman-for-mornings only; Madame Descoings liked to cook. In the evenings a few old friends—ministry men who owed their places to Bridau—came for cards with the two widows. Madame Descoings, still hoping one stroke of luck would repay her loan, was dear to the Bridaus partly from a sense of past obligation and partly because her niece never reproached her under privation.Philippe and Joseph were cosseted. The old gambler of the Imperial Lottery cooked them good dinners and later could be persuaded to spare a few francs for pencils, shoes, or marbles. Madame Descoings’s passion for gambling forced her to be content with fifty francs a month for household expenses so she could risk the rest; Madame Bridau, by maternal love, kept her expenses to the same sum. As a penance for former overconfidence she cut off little enjoyments and glorified thrift as virtue. She mistrusted notary, aunt, and government solemn promises: Roguin’s assurance that Descoings’s assignment would restore the sold sum in seven years did not reassure her. She trusted only herself and practiced privation. She fancied that by laying aside three thousand francs every year from her pension she would have thirty thousand in ten years, yielding fifteen hundred a year for her children—if she lived long enough to see it. Thus two widows descended from hollow opulence to voluntary poverty—one through vice, the other through the purest virtue.These petty domestic details matter: the view into student dens below, the tumult of the rapins, the need to look up at the sky to escape the damp street, the portrait full of soul, the sight of rich old colors in the calm home, the mother’s preference for her elder child and opposition to the younger’s tastes—these facts prefaced the story and help explain Joseph Bridau, who would become one of the greatest painters of the modern French school.Philippe, the elder, strikingly resembled his mother: blond, blue-eyed, and with a daring look mistaken for intrepidity. Old Claparon, a ministry colleague and whist partner of Bridau, tapped Philippe’s cheek and prophesied he’d “stand to his guns.” Stimulated, the boy adopted a resolute manner, excelled at bodily exercises, and fought at the Lyceum, acquiring endurance and contempt for pain—the groundwork of military valor. He developed a distaste for study; public education failed to form both mind and body.Agathe believed physical resemblance implied moral likeness and expected delicacy of feeling combined with manly vigor. Philippe’s winning ways confirmed her hopes. Joseph, three years younger, resembled his father only in defective ways: his thick black hair was always disorderly; he could not keep his clothes clean; new clothes looked old on him. Philippe’s vanity made him tend to his belongings; unconsciously Agathe scolded Joseph and held up his brother as example. Fetching the boys from school she worried especially about Joseph. Such trifles bred maternal preference.Among the ordinary circle—Du Bruel, Claparon, Desroches, even the Abbé Loraux—no one noticed Joseph’s faculty for observation. Absorbed in his tastes, the future colorist paid little attention to himself; the torpor alarmed his father. His face, later to gain originality, wore a sullen expression, pinched and contracted by close attention to surroundings. Philippe flattered his mother with clever sayings; Joseph was taciturn, a dreamer. Agathe hoped great things of Philippe and expected nothing of Joseph.Joseph’s leaning toward art began simply. During Easter 1812, returning from a walk in the Tuileries with Philippe and Madame Descoings, he saw a pupil drawing a caricature of a professor on the Institute wall and stopped in admiration. The next day he watched pupils enter by the rue Mazarin door, slipped into the long courtyard with statues, busts, half-finished marbles, plasters, and baked clays, and gazed feverishly—his instinct awakened. He entered a ground-floor room where young men drew from a statue; they mocked his ugliness and scattered crumbs at him. For a quarter hour Joseph bore the jeers in Chaudet’s atelier, but his persistency and expression struck them. When asked what he wanted, Joseph said he wished to learn to draw. They encouraged him; when he mentioned he was Madame Bridau’s son, they exclaimed that he would be a great man and tested him. One rough trial required him to hold out his arms motionless; at thirteen he stood five minutes and then ten, sweating but immovable. Then Chaudet, bald, pale, and sickly, entered. He lowered Joseph’s arms, patted him, asked his purpose, and, recalling Bridau’s service, welcomed the boy. He gave him board, paper, chalks, a pupil fetched cakes and sugar-plums, and Chaudet petted the child. The scene—rough, warm-hearted, and real—impressed Joseph. Chaudet, whose own prospects had once been opened by the Emperor and then cut short, appeared like a vision.Joseph told his mother nothing but spent two hours every Sunday and Thursday in Chaudet’s atelier. Madame Descoings humored his fancies, supplying pencils, red chalks, prints, and paper. At school he sketched masters, drew comrades, and showed surprising assiduity in drawing class. Lemire, his drawing-master, reported Joseph’s faculty to Madame Bridau. Alarmed, she wept.“What is to become of me!” she cried to Madame Descoings. “Joseph, whom I meant to make a government clerk—protected by his father’s memory at the ministry—wants to be a painter, a vagabond! I always knew that child would give me nothing but trouble.”Madame Descoings confessed she had encouraged him, taking him to the Salon where his interest in pictures was extraordinary.“If he understands painting at thirteen,” she said, “your Joseph will be a man of genius.”“Yes; and see what genius did for his father—killed him with overwork at forty!” Agathe answered.At fourteen, Agathe, despite Madame Descoings’s entreaties, went to Chaudet and demanded he cease to “debauch” her son. She found the sculptor in a blue smock, modeling with spasmodic jerks born of passion. Initially rough, Chaudet was moved by Bridau’s memory and, furious at the plea to turn the child from his vocation, declared that a real calling must be obeyed: an artist was a king in his realm, happier and independent; to force Joseph into a clerk’s life would be murder. He dismissed his work for the day and refused to give up the boy.Agathe, shocked by the sight of a nude in the studio, left horrified. Chaudet forbade his pupils to admit the little Bridau; they jeered as she shut the door. When students learned she opposed the Institute, they plotted to get Joseph among them. He often slipped into Regnauld the painter’s studio and was encouraged to paint. When the widow complained, Chaudet’s pupils insisted they were not Chaudet and composed a long mocking song about her. That evening Agathe refused cards and sat drowned in tears. “She thinks her boy will have to beg his bread because he has the bump of painting,” Madame Descoings explained. The men offered usual advice: let the boy follow his bent; if not a genius, he could later be placed in an office. Their unanimity only increased Agathe’s anxieties. She feared the atelier’s naked women; Madame Descoings joked about “good fires.”Soon after came the disasters of the retreat from Moscow and Napoleon’s return to organize troops. Philippe, tired of school, wanted to serve; he saw a review at the Tuileries—the last Napoleon ever held—and was swept by soldierly splendor. Without his mother’s knowledge he petitioned the Emperor and, within twenty-four hours, was sent to the Imperial Lyceum at Saint-Cyr. Six months later, in November 1813, he was appointed sub-lieutenant in a cavalry regiment. He spent the winter in cantonments, learned to ride, and was dispatched to the front. During the campaign in France he was made lieutenant after an outpost affair where his bravery saved his colonel’s life. The Emperor named him captain at La Fère-Champenoise and took him on his staff. Philippe won the cross at Montereau, witnessed Napoleon’s farewell at Fontainebleau, refused to serve the Bourbons, and returned in July 1814 to find his mother ruined.Joseph’s scholarship was withdrawn after the holidays; Madame Bridau vainly entreated that it be recorded on the ministry rolls. Joseph, more devoted than ever, begged to go to Regnauld and promised to earn his living, saying he was an advanced second-class student who could do without rhetoric. Philippe, now captain at nineteen and decorated, flattered the mother immensely. Coarse and blustering, without merit beyond cavalry bravery, he was to her a man of genius; Joseph, puny, sickly, unkempt, and dreaming an artist’s glory, would only worry her.The winter of 1814–1815 favored Joseph. Secretly encouraged by Madame Descoings and Bixiou, a pupil of Gros, he worked in Gros’s celebrated atelier, forming intimacy with Schinner. The return from Elba came; Captain Bridau joined Napoleon at Lyons, accompanied him to the Tuileries, and commanded a squadron in the Guard’s dragoons. After Waterloo—where he was slightly wounded and received the officer’s Legion of Honor—he was near Marshal Davoust at Saint-Denis and, through Davoust’s intercession, retained his cross and rank though placed on half-pay.Joseph, anxious about his future, studied with ardor through turbulent times, sometimes to the point of illness. “It is the smell of the paints,” Agathe told Madame Descoings. “He ought to give up a business so injurious to his health.” Agathe’s anxieties remained chiefly for her lieutenant-colonel son. When he returned in 1816 reduced from a nine-thousand-franc commander’s salary to half-pay, she fitted attic rooms for him and spent her savings. Philippe belonged to the faithful Bonapartes of café Lemblin; he acquired and exaggerated the airs of a half-pay officer, spurned the Bourbons, and refused infantry opportunities. To his mother he seemed noble. “The father himself could have done no more,” she said.Philippe’s half-pay sufficed; he cost little at home, whereas Joseph’s expenses were paid by the widows. From then Agathe’s preference for Philippe was openly shown. Up to that time it had been secret; the colonel’s persecution for the Emperor and his wound drew forth maternal tenderness. “He is unfortunate” explained everything to her. Joseph, simple in the artist’s way and brought up to admire his big brother, was not hurt by maternal preference and shared his worship of the hero who had carried Napoleon’s orders. How could he doubt the superiority of the brother he had seen in green and gold, commanding on the Champ de Mars?Agathe, notwithstanding preference, was an excellent mother. She loved Joseph, though she could not understand him. Joseph adored her; Philippe let her adore him. Toward her the dragoon softened his military roughness yet never concealed contempt for Joseph—expressed in a patronizing, almost friendly way. He nicknamed the weak, shrunken seventeen-year-old “Cub.” Philippe’s patronizing manners would have wounded anyone less indifferent than Joseph, who believed soldiers hid goodness beneath brutal exteriors. Joseph did not yet know that soldiers of genius can be gentle; all genius is alike.“Poor boy!” Philippe said to his mother. “We mustn’t plague him; let him do as he likes.” To Agathe, the colonel’s contempt seemed fraternal affection. “Philippe will always love and protect his brother,” she thought.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1816 Joseph obtained his mother’s permission to convert the garret next to his attic into an atelier; Madame Descoings contributed some money. The two widows treated painting as a trade, but Joseph arranged the humble studio himself: Madame Descoings persuaded the owner to add a skylight; Joseph painted the hall chocolate and hung sketches. Agathe reluctantly installed an iron stove so her son could work at home without abandoning Gros’s or Schinner’s studios.The constitutional party, backed by half-pay officers and Bonapartists, stirred emeutes around the Chamber of Deputies; conspiracies were rumored. Philippe, flirting with these affairs, was arrested and released for lack of proof; the minister of war restored him to the active list, ending his half-pay. France seemed unsafe; Agathe feared provocateurs. Philippe spent time in disaffected cafés, drinking and acquiring bad habits, while Agathe lived in mortal dread. The Grecian sages still visited Madame Bridau nightly; Claparon, retained at the ministry though cautious, whispered Moniteur news. Desroches, retired after old Du Bruel, still fought for his pension. Their friends urged Agathe to send Philippe abroad.“They talk of conspiracies, and your son will be certain to fall a victim,” they warned.“Philippe is cut from the wood the Emperor made into marshals,” Du Bruel replied; “he mustn’t give up his profession. Let him serve in the East, in India—”“Think of his health,” Agathe answered.“Why doesn’t he get some place, some business?” Desroches asked. “There are private offices to be had.” Agathe insisted Philippe was a soldier and wouldn’t want anything else. Du Bruel suggested royal favor could make him lieutenant-colonel, though Madame Descoings doubted his prospects without noble birth.To please his mother, Philippe took the notion of joining General Lallemand in the United States and helping found the Champ d’Asile, one of the most disastrous swindles carried off under the name of a national subscription. Agathe gave ten thousand francs and saw him off at Havre. By the end of 1817 she lived on the six hundred francs left from her funds and, by prudent investment, secured seven percent on some savings. Joseph emulated her frugality: dressed plainly, denied himself comforts, and lived on bread, milk, and Brie; only Madame Descoings and Bixiou showed sympathy.Joseph later recalled how the coming summer saved him the cost of charcoal. Now a colorist as capable as Gros, he consulted his master only occasionally. He was already planning a revolt against classical traditions, aiming to free art to represent Nature — a struggle that began with his first Salon exhibition and never ceased.1818 was terrible for the family. Roguin, Madame Descoings’s notary, absconded with seven years’ withheld annuity funds. Three days later a bill of exchange for a thousand francs arrived from New York: Philippe had lost everything in the Champ d’Asile. The letter told of debts contracted and comrades who had indorsed for him. Agathe, blaming herself, wept. The two women faced selling silver, linen, and furniture. Joseph, alarmed, told Gérard, who secured an order for two royal copies of Louis XVIII’s portrait at five hundred francs each and supplied Joseph with materials. Joseph painted four panels in ten days, sold them, and brought his mother the thousand francs to meet Philippe’s draft. Then came a letter announcing Philippe’s imminent return on a packet whose captain had trusted him for passage; he would need a thousand francs at Havre.Joseph promised his mother the money after finishing the copies. Agathe, ecstatically grateful, kissed him and called him “his father all over.” Madame Bridau rushed to Havre and watched beyond the round tower daily for the packet. The sight of home softened Philippe, but his misfortunes in Texas and New York had developed his worst traits: he had grown brutal, selfish, rude, drank and smoked excessively, and imagined perpetual persecution. Such men either live by honest virtues or by treating every want as a necessity; Philippe had drifted into the latter, becoming cunning and self-centered while keeping an appearance of frank soldierly simplicity. His stature had grown somewhat rotund, his face bronzed, his speech terse and decisive; exposed to hardship, he had become the kind of man who makes himself feared. To his mother he remained a hero; in truth he had become, bluntly, a blackguard.At Havre Agathe bought him clothes and could not, at first, restrain his indulgence. The scheme of conquering Texas with imperial remnants had failed less for lack of idea than for treachery and poor management; the Liberals had used the enterprise selfishly, abandoning the devoted heroes. Agathe, a household manager with shrewd common sense, saw through the political chicane and recognized the subscription’s failure; she could not make her son see he had been duped rather than persecuted. During the costly week at Havre she could not persuade him to make terms with the royal government. She brought him back to Paris before her funds gave out.On arrival, those awaiting them noted how much Agathe had aged. Philippe greeted Madame Descoings coolly. Joseph, now twenty-one and confident, was hurt by his brother’s contempt. At a family dinner the friends — Du Bruel, Claparon, Desroches, and the Abbé Loraux — observed the exile’s roughened manners and coarse voice. While Joseph prepared the card tables, the intimate friends asked Agathe what she intended for Philippe. She could not get him to serve the Bourbons; Du Bruel suggested other roles like writing plays, as his son had once done. Philippe, Bixiou, and young Desroches played écarté. Madame Descoings, eager to celebrate the prodigal’s return, provided funds for play; Joseph secretly slipped twenty francs to Philippe.The evening grew lively. Philippe, a poor player, ended owing fifty francs. He showed bad temper and quarreled with young Desroches and then with Desroches senior, who forbade further play though his son was right. Madame Descoings silenced her grandson’s barbed witticisms for fear of provoking the colonel. Philippe's temper and gambling continued into the night.The next morning the two widows, counting resources, resolved to economize: Madame Descoings proposed selling furniture and giving up apartments to save seven hundred francs a year and pay Philippe fifty francs monthly until he found something. They pooled incomes — about five thousand three hundred francs, four thousand from Madame Descoings as a life annuity — and had consumed their savings. Philippe promised to find a situation and asked only for board and lodging. Agathe kissed him; Madame Descoings slipped him a hundred francs to cover his losses.Within ten days the furniture was sold and the household rearranged. Philippe’s habits hardened: he left after breakfast, returned at dinner, and spent evenings in cafés drinking brandy, reading newspapers, playing billiards at the Café Minerve, smoking many cigars, and gambling at night. He boasted of a regular life and, while often winning, returned home talking of smashing the ultras and the Bourbon bodyguard.He dined often with army comrades who spoke freely of hopes based on plots and inventions like a submarine to restore the Emperor. He particularly liked an old dragoons’ captain, Giroudeau, whose company led Philippe into further dissipation and into seeing women as resources. Giroudeau introduced Philippe to the theatre and to a young dancer, Florentine, and to another, Marie Godeschal — Mariette — who was beautiful, cold, and ambitious. Mariette, orphaned and poor, had a devoted brother who clerked in Derville’s law office; she resolved to use her beauty and talent to help him. Poorly dressed yet striking, she provoked Philippe’s passion. To Mariette, Philippe was a Guard staff-officer, and a liaison with him would elevate her above rivals.Giroudeau promised to speak to his nephew Finot at a theatrical journal and to get Philippe a place on a newspaper. Philippe revealed his poverty, but Giroudeau reassured him: his nephew could put him on a paper or help him get patronage. Mariette, thinking Philippe would write for a newspaper and help her career, accepted him.At Finot’s attic, the nephew — cool and sardonic — eyed Philippe. Finot mocked the Texan affair, then gave a plan: exploit his position as a victim of the Liberals and demand an accounting of the Champ d’Asile subscription. A new Opposition journal was starting; Finot could make Philippe its cashier with a salary of three thousand francs if Philippe found someone to go security for twenty thousand francs. “Threaten loudly,” Finot advised, “and we’ll silence you with a place.” Philippe was grateful and impressed by Finot’s worldly cleverness.That night at cafés Philippe fulminated against the Liberals, declaring he would demand an accounting of the moneys collected. He went to Mariette to tell her of the expected post on a newspaper with ten thousand subscribers and how he would advance her claims.Amid the political alarm following the assassination of the Duc de Berry, Agathe and Madame Descoings waited anxiously. Philippe returned with good news: he had been promised the cashiership at three thousand francs on condition they could go security for twenty thousand francs by depositing their shares in the Funds as guarantee while drawing the interest. The friends urged Agathe to go security; she and Madame Descoings, overjoyed at the prospect of providing for Philippe, transferred her shares — representing thirteen hundred francs in interest — to secure him. Philippe promised to pay one hundred francs monthly for board and lodging and was acclaimed the best of sons. Those who had doubted him congratulated Agathe and admitted they had been unjust. Joseph, not to be outdone, resolved to pay for his own support and succeeded.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Three months later the colonel, who ate and drank enough for four, began to find fault with the household provisions and, appealing to his pay, insisted the poor widows spend more on their table though he had not yet paid a penny. His mother and Madame Descoings, out of delicacy, would not press him for the promise. The year passed without a single one of Leon Gozlan’s so-called “tigers with five claws” ever finding its way from Philippe’s pocket to the family purse. Philippe quieted his conscience by seldom dining at home.“Well, he is happy,” said his mother; “he is easy in mind; he has a place.”Through Vernou’s feuilleton, who was intimate with Bixiou, Finot, and Giroudeau, Mariette was announced not at the Panorama-Dramatique but at the Porte-Saint-Martin, where she triumphed alongside the famous Begrand. Among the theatre’s directors was a rich general who had become an impresario for an actress he adored; he knew Philippe and Giroudeau. Finot’s journal and Philippe himself heralded Mariette’s first appearance, arranged promptly by the three men—a little conspiracy of vanity and folly.Bixiou, always mischievous, revealed to his grandmother and to the devout Agathe that Philippe, the heroic cashier, was in love with Mariette, the celebrated ballet-dancer. The news struck the widows like a clap of thunder. Agathe, whose religious scruples saw actresses as “brands in the burning,” and Madame Descoings, imagining women on the stage living off gold and pearls, were aghast.“Do you suppose my brother is such a fool as to spend his money on Mariette?” Joseph asked his mother. Bixiou soothed them, predicting Mariette’s swift move to the Opera and hinting an ambassador was infatuated with her—thus implying Philippe’s danger might be brief. He also related the miserly funeral of old Claparon, arranged cheaply by his banker son, and wove comic moralities from it.Philippe, driven partly by mercenary motives, proposed marriage to Mariette; she refused, expecting engagement at the Grand Opera and preferring to keep her independence. For the rest of the year Philippe was rarely home—either at his office, the theatre, or with Mariette. His household knew nothing; his friends Giroudeau, Finot, Bixiou, Vernou, and Lousteau saw him living for pleasure. He shared the amusements of singers and dancers, and after four o’clock until midnight he was typically at some dinner, card party, or supper. Philippe relished this carnival life.When Mariette appeared at the Opera in January 1821, she captured one of the highest dukes in Louis XVIII’s court. Philippe, who tried to oppose the noble, was driven by passion and ran into gambling losses. By April he had begun dipping into the funds entrusted to him as cashier, and by May he had taken eleven hundred francs. In that fatal May Mariette went to London to seek further patronage while the new opera house was being prepared. Philippe, consumed by love though despised by Mariette as a dull grasping soldier, found himself left behind; she had never meant to remain faithful to him, using men as rungs on a ladder. Yet she bore a peculiar gratitude to the first man who had eased her way.Unable to follow Mariette, Philippe returned to his attic room in his mother’s apartment and brooded. The pleasures that had become a habit—dinners, suppers, enlivening social evenings, the fever of wits and journalists—were now necessities to him. The idea of life without Mariette’s glamorous atmosphere preyed on him; he even contemplated suicide, not because of the deficit but because he could not live without continual enjoyments.Full of gloom he went to his brother’s painting-room, where Joseph was copying a picture for a dealer and spoke of his modest earnings as an artist. Philippe asked Joseph how long it would take him to paint his portrait; Joseph said three or four days—too long for Philippe, who claimed he had but one day and declared, with forced gaiety, that he was going never to return. Alarmed, Joseph pressed him and learned, to his and their mother’s horror, that Philippe had taken eleven hundred francs from the funds he administered and must render his accounts the next day. He had lost half the family security and said he could not live under dishonor. Joseph swore secrecy but pleaded that the money could be repaid; Philippe, declaring he would shoot himself rather than live dishonored, fled.Joseph told their mother and Madame Descoings. The revelation stunned them: the son of Bridau to steal the money entrusted to him! The old women alternated between terror and consoling rationalizations. Madame Descoings, who saw similar lapses every day and judged them not entirely fatal, tried to comfort Agathe, who could only picture her son dead. They sought to lessen the sin; Agathe could not accept her son living under dishonor and begged him to return. The day was spent in cruel alternations of hope and dread, every sound at the window prompting fresh conjectures. Meanwhile Philippe, quietly audacious, prepared his defense at the office.He presented accounts claiming he had withdrawn eleven hundred francs for safekeeping at home. Then, at five, he took five hundred francs more from the desk and went—against all propriety for an officer and cashier—to the gambling-house he had avoided since he took the cashier post. At first he won, gaining as much as six thousand francs. Thinking to erase his deficit in one stroke, he risked everything; fortune seemed to favour him when the wheel turned in his favour, but eventually intermittent “divining” senses deserted him and he lost all. Fatigued by the strain, he returned home oblivious to suicide, dishonour, or his family’s anguish. Seeing him pale and woe-begone, his mother knelt, kissed his hands, and entreated him not to kill himself. His heart touched by their tears, Philippe embraced them, said their pity gave him life, and took the indulgent rescue as a reprieve.Madame Descoings and Agathe arranged an excellent dinner and indulgences such as cigars, making sacrifices to soothe him. The household forgave him; Agathe did not yet know all the consequences. At daybreak Agathe, having weathered a sleepless night, confronted the stark financial reality: she was reduced to six hundred francs a year. Madame Descoings, whose heaviness suggested a short future and with four thousand francs at stake in her life, was the other pillar of family security. Agathe resolved she would nurse rather than be supported by her children. Yet the two widows were practical: Madame Descoings proposed that Desroches, an old friend and shrewd clerk, spin a story to the press and authorities that Philippe had been victimized and was unfit to continue as cashier—thus persuading them to accept his resignation and avoid scandal. This lie, the two hoped, would preserve honor.Agathe embraced the plan, and Desroches carried it out with the cool efficiency of a clerk loyal to Bridau’s family. He dined with them, urged Agathe to go the next day to the Treasury to transfer funds and secure her pension, and obliged Philippe to sign a petition to the minister of war asking reinstatement in active service. Desroches promised to follow up and to exploit the duke’s triumph over Philippe for influence. He predicted Philippe would be made lieutenant-colonel in the Duc de Maufrigneuse’s regiment within three months. The press ceased its scrutiny in two months, as Finot had foreseen. Publicly, Philippe’s crime had no consequences; but Agathe’s faith in her son was mortally wounded and she lived thereafter in fear mingled with occasional satisfactions as the worst had not been realized.When men like Philippe—brave in body but cowardly in honor—observe that life goes on after near catastrophe and that family kindness buys impunity, they grow bold. They learn how to bend the social rules to their benefit. A fortnight after the crisis, Philippe resumed the café life: drams, billiards with punch, nightly gambling where he risked modest stakes, which he won enough to cover his dissipations. To deceive his mother and Madame Descoings, he cultivated an appearance of thrift: a greasy, rubbed hat, patched boots, a shabby overcoat with a discoloured ribbon, worn gloves, and a ragged neckcloth. Mariette remained the sole object of his affection; her treachery had hardened his heart. When flush, he followed slum women with contempt; otherwise he breakfasted and dined at home and returned about one o’clock—three months of this life restored Agathe’s confidence.Joseph, meanwhile, worked in his atelier on a promising picture he hoped would make his reputation. Madame Descoings, believing Bixiou’s prediction of Joseph’s future fame, tended him like a mother—bringing breakfast, running errands, blacking his boots. Joseph was absent until dinner and spent evenings at the Cenacle among friends, giving himself the deep self-education of a man of talent. Agathe, seeing little of him and feeling no anxiety, concentrated on Philippe, whose alternations of fear and calm consumed her maternal attention. Desroches, who visited weekly, brought hopes that the Duc de Maufrigneuse had asked for Philippe, the minister of war had ordered an inquiry, and reinstatement would come early in the year.To this end Desroches used all his influence. At the prefecture of police he learned of Philippe’s gambling and advised Madame Descoings, discreetly, to watch him; one indiscretion might imperil the entire plan. Agathe sat in the chimney-corner reading prayers; Madame Descoings, less pious, consulted the cards, interpreted dreams, and applied cabalistic rules to her lottery obsession. She pursued a “trey” of three numbers she believed long overdue—one of its numbers having, she fancied, been absent from wheels for twenty-one years. Convinced it would finally pay, she hoarded savings sewn into her mattress to pile on a last, enormous stake. She saw the lottery as a social opium for the poor, a five-sous chance at five days of happiness that allowed hope where nothing else could. Joseph was indulgent toward this folly; he listened to her dreams without scolding and soothed her.Madame Descoings’s passion for the trey and Joseph’s absorption in art became a domestic pattern: each member loved something—Philippe tobacco and drink, Agathe God, Desroches the law, Joseph the “beau idéal.” Joseph, who had once used Madame Descoings as a model, was painting a large canvas—an old woman leading a young courtesan to a Doge of Venice—which promised great success when completed. This masterpiece, later mistaken by Gros for a Titian, would secure recognition for Joseph at the Salon of 1823. But for the present he had to work for dealers, copying masters and learning their methods; the discipline improved his brush. Practical and frugal about his earnings, Joseph concealed the small savings he had begun to accumulate, knowing both his mother and Madame Descoings had their own roads to ruin—one through Philippe’s dissipations, the other through the lottery.To hide his savings, Joseph bought an old chest with carved bas-reliefs and used a skull in a compartment as a receptacle for his monthly reserve. Since Philippe’s return, he noticed a steady discrepancy between his recorded spending and the small sums lying in the skull. At first he blamed carelessness; the discrepancy persisted, then grew. He confided his suspicions to Madame Descoings, whom he trusted with a tenderness he had never received from his own mother. Together they watched. The next day they discovered the thief: Philippe had been entering the studio when Joseph was absent and taking the little sums he needed. The poor painter, torn between brotherly affection and outrage, resolved to catch him.Madame Descoings wrapped the matter in maternal pity—“Poor boy! he wants tobacco; he’s accustomed to it”—but Joseph, more outraged, took a sterner view. He had long considered Philippe a dead weight, a man who lived the life of a Sardanapalus while his and their mother’s fortunes were imperilled; he predicted Philippe would end by bringing the family to beggary and suggested emigration to divert him. Bixiou’s censure—“He is a downright rogue, that brother of yours”—echoed Joseph’s private scorn. For Joseph the peril was not only financial but moral: Philippe’s conduct threatened the family’s honor repeatedly and without learning. Yet Joseph’s passion for art, his deep study, and his determination to succeed sustained him. He observed his companions and noted how each human passion found its outlet: his brother’s stupidity and vice, Madame Descoings’s gambling, his mother’s piety,—all were human foibles he could turn into motives and material for his genius.Thus the household lived in uneasy equilibrium: Agathe’s maternal devotion and shrinking income; Madame Descoings’s lottery hopes and domestic benevolence; Joseph’s quiet artistry and growing suspicion; Philippe’s reckless pleasure-seeking and occasional remorse. Desroches, for his part, continued to smooth ways within administration and society to shield the family name, while the press and acquaintances arranged the narrative that made Philippe a victim rather than a criminal. The result was temporary peace and a reconstitution of the family’s external respectability—the very mercy that encouraged Philippe’s relapse into vice, and the domestic arrangements that allowed him to pilfer from the only brother who worked, even as Joseph prepared the picture destined to bring him fame and, perhaps, to save them all from ruin.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Despite Philippe’s outward coolness at his nightly gambling, he was often “cleaned out.” To get his ten-franc stake each evening he robbed the household—his brother’s pocket, whatever coins Madame Descoings or Agathe left about. After a dreadful dream in which he stole every coin from her gown, Madame Descoings could no longer deny the truth. Agathe, pretending to sleep, wept through the night. Madame Descoings’s old remark, “One wrong act is not a vice,” repeated enough times, had ceased to comfort: Agathe saw that her son lacked delicacy and honor.The morning after the vision Agathe entreated Philippe to ask her for whatever he needed. His requests became so frequent that in two weeks Agathe’s savings were gone. Penniless and shrunken by worry, she and her aunt discussed taking low-paid needlework. Madame Descoings watched Agathe decline; the young woman’s face lost its freshness, her eyes grew dim, sleep fled her. A consuming inward fire ate her; she listened each night for Philippe’s step, learning the nuances of his walk, voice, even the sound of his cane—the clues that told her whether he had won or lost. When drunk and victorious his walk dragged and his voice gruff; when he had lost, his step was sharp and his manner angry. Debauchery coarsened him: his features grew hard, eyelashes fell out, his breath and boots smelled of taverns.At the end of December Madame Descoings suggested Philippe get new clothes; he answered bitterly that his mother had no money and he lived on a mortgaged pension. Joseph defended the need for decency; Agathe begged Joseph to be indulgent. Philippe dismissed political loyalty as no excuse for slovenliness and spoke of a “debt of honor” to Giroudeau who had lent him money by way of Florentine. He excused shabby boots by invoking Napoleon selling his plate at Saint Helena—an absurd romanticism to cover shame.One day Philippe learned, or guessed, that Madame Descoings had hidden twenty napoleons in her mattress as payment for a heavy lottery stake—the famed Paris “trey.” Obsessed with the sixteen-page dream of easy wealth that the trey promised, Madame Descoings kept the money tucked away. Philippe watched and schemed. When the household went out at midday and left the door locked, he feigned forgetting something and had a locksmith let him in. He searched the bed, ripped open the ticking, took twenty napoleons, and remade the bed so no one would suspect.Intent on multiplying this theft, Philippe adopted the gambler’s regimen of short, measured sessions. But gambling’s science—its bank’s apparent impassibility, the quick, mechanical operation of the tables, the cunning of croupiers—preys on the best self-control. Philippe, however, had the cold nerve of a veteran commander; he could stare into the abyss with no vertigo. With four hundred francs he went to the Palais-Royal gambling-house, and in half an hour walked out with seven thousand. He paid those he owed and returned. By six he had twenty-five thousand; by ten he had seventy-five thousand. Drunk with success, he broke his rule at midnight and played longer; by three in the morning the one hundred and fifty thousand he'd briefly amassed had vanished back to the bank. Exhausted and drunk, he was carried away by a waiter to a lodging-house and left there to sleep off a nervous fever. Thus, in a single night, he had brought mourning, poverty, and ruin into his home.The night before, as dinner waited, Agathe and Madame Descoings expected Philippe. Alice—Agathe—always went to bed early for midnight mass; Madame Descoings and Joseph lingered with dreams of the trey and plans for doubles and singles. When Joseph innocently asked where the four hundred francs to “pay up” the stake were kept, the old woman led him and Agathe to her bed-chamber and opened the mattress to show him the savings. She found only the torn ticking. Overcome, she fainted; Joseph and Agathe revived her with ordinary remedies. She had felt the coins when she made the bed that morning and knew they were gone. “HE has taken them, the monster!” she cried.Agathe, distraught, questioned the concierge and learned of Philippe’s return and the locksmith. The old woman, white and moving like a spectre, repeated her conviction. She begged Agathe to sell her silverware if need be; Agathe produced the box of plate only to find a pawnbroker’s ticket where the spoons had been. The falsehood—that she had pawned them secretly to replenish the mattress—collapsed; the box was empty. Silence and mutual accusation filled the room until Joseph, moved, ran to his studio and returned with three hundred francs—the fruit of his frugal life—placing it in Madame Descoings’s hands. She refused, torn between hope in the trey and abhorrence of taking such help. Still, Joseph had acted: affection triumphing over vice in an understated domestic heroism. At midnight Joseph dashed out to find a lottery office open to pay the stakes; he failed, and the tickets under the Palais-Royal portico were already sold paid-up.Agathe and her aunt went to mass; Joseph went to bed dejected. At dawn they rose late: had Madame Descoings paid, she could have checked the results by half-past nine. But she had not. Old Desroches brought the printed winning numbers and announced that her trey had come up—three millions. The news struck the old woman like a thunderbolt. She collapsed; a doctor was sent for. The thin hope that had been her life—twenty years of sacrifice and waiting—was realized only to be thwarted by the robbery. The shock threw her into apoplexy; she lay unconscious, murmuring “Three millions!” when she returned to consciousness late that afternoon. As she lay there, the house was silent and exhausted.Philippe returned that evening, disordered and feverish. Agathe and Joseph were waiting, their grief raw. Philippe arrived staggering, flushed and raving. When confronted, he defied them: if his mother had taken twenty thousand francs from him, he felt he had a right to take twenty napoleons from her mattress; he called his nightly theft repayment. He ridiculed their melodrama and claimed he was “the glory of the family,” while they called him “the disgrace.” In fury Joseph struck him down; a struggle ensued. Bixiou and Doctor Haudry entered; Joseph had overpowered his brother. The doctor, seeing Philippe’s state—chilled, teeth chattering, with legs swollen—ordered him undressed and put to bed. When Agathe cut off his boots, ten pieces of gold rolled out onto the floor—coins Philippe had forgotten he had hid in his footwear. He muttered that had he remembered them earlier he might have won the trey; then fell into a raging delirium.Philippe’s fever was violent. They removed him to his room, and the doctor, fearing self-harm, borrowed a strait-waistcoat from the hospital. At nine o’clock his madness quieted somewhat. Abbé Loraux and Desroches tried to comfort Agathe, who would not be consoled. Only Joseph and Madame Descoings knew the depth of her final inward wound.On her deathbed, Madame Descoings obtained from Joseph a solemn promise: he would not let Philippe ruin Agathe and would convert her property into a life annuity for her niece. She died on December 31, five days after the seizure that began when she discovered the loss. The five hundred francs left in the house barely sufficed for the funeral; some small silver and furniture were sold. Reduced to an eight-hundred-franc annuity and stripped of the capital, Agathe sold what she could, gave up the third-floor apartment, and prepared to work.Philippe’s convalescence was slow. When he seemed better, Agathe told him kindly that the costs of his illness had consumed their ready money and that she would have to work; she urged him, gently, to re-enter the army and support himself. He sneered, said she and Joseph did not love him, and declared he preferred life alone. When Agathe implored him to make himself worthy of their affection, he interrupted with scorn, pulled on his shabby hat, and left. She gave him a hundred francs wrapped in paper; he took it, embraced her without warmth, and said he would visit Florentine, Giroudeau’s mistress, because “they are real friends.” Agathe, trembling and bereft, fell to her knees and prayed that God would take her unnatural child into His keeping—her maternal love broken and abdicated.Thus ended the household’s quiet tragedy: Philippe’s gambling and theft shattered the hopes and health of those who loved him, Madame Descoings’s long-awaited fortune slipped through their fingers, and a mother’s last charity replaced where judgment could not. The family was left impoverished and morally exhausted, the wounded ones carrying the scars of betrayal and sacrifice.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By February 1822 Madame Bridau had taken the attic room over the kitchen of her old apartment; Joseph’s studio and bedroom faced it across the stair. Seeing his mother reduced, Joseph refurnished the garret with an artist’s neatness: a rug, a simple bed, glazed cotton on the walls, a double door with a portière, and a blind to soften the light. Though Agathe’s life in Paris was now penniless and plain, Joseph tried to make it decent.To spare her frugal housekeeping, Joseph took Agathe daily to dine at a table d’hôte on the rue de Beaune frequented by well-bred women, deputies, and the titled; dinners cost ninety francs a month. Agathe paid only for breakfast and enjoyed the remnant habits of her married life. Alarmed when she learned the dinner’s cost and ignorant of how her son earned money (“painting naked women” never occurred to her), she obtained through her confessor, the Abbé Loraux, an office in the Comtesse de Bauvan’s lottery—an appointment worth seven hundred francs a year. Lottery offices often sufficed to support a manager’s family. The Comtesse promised the manager’s place when it vacated; meanwhile Agathe took an assistant’s post at six hundred francs, working from ten until midnight. For two years Joseph fetched her every night; friends saw him come straight from the opera or a salon to be punctual at the rue Vivienne office.Agathe settled into the monotonous regularity that civil misfortune brings. Mornings she tidied her room—no cats or birds now—prepared breakfast for Joseph, arranged his bedroom, tended the fire, and sat sewing by the iron stove, retiring when a comrade or model entered. She understood nothing of art and made no pretence; amazed at the fuss about color and composition, she later found no reason for painters’ disputes. She made Joseph’s shirts, mended stockings, cleaned his palette, supplied rags, and kept the studio in order. Joseph noticed her care over trivial things and lavished tenderness on her; their relationship was affectionate rather than intellectual.One morning, while Joseph sketched a large picture, Agathe asked about Philippe as if casually: Had he suffered? Had poverty changed him? Joseph denied it, saying Philippe kept carnival in New York as in Paris, and that if he were suffering he’d gladly give him money but did not want to see him—“he killed our poor Descoings.” Agathe hoped Joseph would paint Philippe’s portrait; Joseph consented for her sake, calling Philippe “your passion.”That afternoon Joseph found Philippe in the rue du Sentier. Philippe had taken Giroudeau’s place as cashier at a weekly journal run by Finot’s nephew; his salary was cut and now amounted to six hundred francs plus five hundred from his cross—enough for a warm office day and theatre passes at night. He lived with journalists and actresses, oddly well behaved at the office. He agreed to sit for a portrait in his uniform, saying two Sundays would suffice.When Joseph told Agathe, she trembled with joy and decorated the studio with flowers. On the first Sunday Philippe sent his uniform and breakfast was prepared by Agathe, who wept silently. He posed for several hours, then dressed ordinary and proposed dining at the Palais-Royal, jingling gold. Joseph, uneasy at seeing him flaunt money, hesitated, but Agathe insisted. Philippe gave a splendid dinner costing a hundred francs and later took them to the theatre; Agathe feigned illness and refused to go, returning to rue Mazarin in gloom.At the next sitting Agathe was visibly present, interrogating Philippe about his lodgings—he lodged with journalists and actresses, ate kirsch before posing, boasted of future greatness but was reticent about means. Joseph, saying the Salon was near and he needed money for frames and to finish a copy of a Rubens ordered by dealer Elie Magus, postponed the sitting till the next Sunday. That evening Joseph prepared a stratagem: he varnished his finished copy of the Rubens, placed it where the original had been, and set the original on his easel. Pierre Grassou, a fellow painter, was taken in and delighted. Magus did not come as agreed; Joseph left the key with the concierge and dined with Grassou.An hour later Philippe arrived, obtained the key, went upstairs, took the copy thinking it the original, returned the key claiming he’d forgotten something, and rushed off to sell the Rubens for three thousand francs. He had sent Magus a note asking him not to call till the next day. When Joseph returned that night with his mother, the concierge told him Philippe’s trick. Joseph feared ruin and ran to the studio. Relieved to find the original still there, he cursed Philippe as a “vile scoundrel.” Agathe, bewildered, wept when told. Joseph insisted they warn the concierge and keep the keys themselves; he vowed to paint his brother’s blackguard face from memory. Agathe, heart-broken, asked him to leave the portrait as it was—she could not bear to look at it.Philippe knew the money from the Rubens would have been used to prevent his brother’s ruin, yet he still took the copy. After this crime Agathe never mentioned him; her face grew cold and bitter.
