One thought possessed her: “Some day we shall hear of a Bridau in the police courts.”Two months later Giroudeau, an old dragoon and supposed friend of Philippe’s, appeared on urgent business. He was a debauched figure—fishy gray eyes, greasy hair, a faded coat with a Legion of Honor ribbon, gold rings in his ears. He told them Philippe could no longer work at the office; lodged with Mademoiselle Florentine in a miserable attic, he was dying and needed a doctor and medicines or he would be taken to the hospital. He had pledged his pension and had nothing. Giroudeau and Florentine would pay half the cost if Agathe paid the rest—three hundred francs for a nurse and two months’ care. Agathe thanked him but said the son was banished from her heart and she had no money—she worked in the lottery office to avoid burdening Joseph.Giroudeau pressed Joseph; Joseph declared Giroudeau a swindler and refused. Giroudeau left mortified. Three months later, in July, as Agathe crossed the Pont Neuf she saw a man along the Quai de l’Ecole who looked like Philippe in the last stages of poverty: ragged neckcloth, scurfy hat, patched boots, threadbare coat, dirty collar, black hands, a waistcoat in place of a shirt. His nearly bald head and sunken face showed he had just left the Hôpital du Midi. His blue overcoat still bore the ribbon of his cross; passersby noticed the rosette with curiosity and pity.Agathe trembled. Forgetting past crimes—stealing, gambling, debauchery—she saw only a man recovering from illness and likely to die of starvation. Impulsively she thrust her purse into Philippe’s hand and fled as if guilty. For two days she ate nothing, haunted by the image of her son dying in Paris; at forty-seven she came to look seventy. Her eyes were dimmed with tears and prayers. But worse followed: a conspiracy among officers was discovered and the Moniteur printed lists of arrests. Agathe heard Philippe’s name in her lottery office and fainted; the manager, understanding, gave her two weeks’ leave.Joseph consulted Desroches about Philippe. While Joseph spoke to the younger Desroches—now reputed a clever lawyer—Giroudeau called on the widow Desroches and persuaded Agathe that twelve thousand francs would secure Philippe’s release by buying the silence of two witnesses. Desperate, Agathe promised to get the money without knowing how. She wrote to her godmother, Madame Hochon, begging her to ask Jean-Jacques Rouget to send or lend the sum and promising to repay in two years. Madame Hochon’s reply, returned by courier the next day, refused. She explained that her brother Hochon, with an income of at least forty thousand francs and savings estimated at over six hundred thousand, would give nothing to nephews he had never seen; she could not act without her husband’s signature and Rouget would refuse. She hinted Agathe’s presence in Issoudun might neutralize Rouget’s concubine’s influence and suggested Agathe come stay with her; she promised to try to make her husband consent and hinted at using a will to coerce him if necessary. She urged Agathe to employ a good lawyer and to come to Issoudun at once.The letter impressed Agathe, who showed it to Joseph after telling him of Giroudeau’s demand. Joseph advised telling everything to Desroches. The next morning at six they found Desroches in the rue de Bussy. The lawyer—cold, stern, with a cracked voice, rough skin, implacable eyes and a foxlike visage—bounded like a tiger on hearing Giroudeau’s proposal and scornfully warned Agathe not to be duped. He urged her not to give a farthing; he’d answer for Philippe and would rather see him brought before the Court of Peers if that would save his future. He advised securing the property at Issoudun and getting evidence of undue influence if Jean-Jacques had made a will favoring a woman; he offered to go to Issoudun himself in the holidays if needed. His briskness alarmed Joseph; Desroches winked and told Joseph bluntly that Philippe was a “great scoundrel” who might have caused the conspiracy’s discovery—either a fool or a traitor—and would be under police surveillance.“Go to Issoudun with your mother,” Desroches urged. “Try to save the property.” Joseph, having sold two pictures to fund the trip, told his mother they would start for Berry. After writing to Madame Hochon, Agathe and Joseph left that evening for Issoudun, leaving Philippe to his fate. On the road, Agathe, reflecting on the Luxembourg prison to which Philippe had been transferred, said, “If it were not for the Allies he would never be there!” Joseph gallantly embraced his mother and praised her maternal devotion: “You are a mother just as Raphael was a painter. And you will always be a fool of a mother!”Distracted by the journey, Agathe re-read Madame Hochon’s letter, seized by the words “concubine” and “slut” used by a pious septuagenarian, and by the suggestion that her presence might save the inheritance. Joseph, ignorant of the Code, admitted his helplessness; he recalled Desroches’s counsel to gather evidence of undue influence if Jean-Jacques had made a will favoring the woman. “Don’t trouble your heads uselessly,” Agathe said; “when we get to Issoudun my godmother will tell us what to do.” Their naivety in the task Desroches set them was plain as they entered the Sologne.Returning to Issoudun after thirty years’ absence, Agathe was about to find changes in its manners that require a portrait of the town. Without it the reader would scarcely understand Madame Hochon’s heroism in helping her goddaughter, or the strange position of Jean-Jacques Rouget. That a brother should for thirty years give no sign of life to a sister was extraordinary and suggested peculiar circumstances; any other sister and nephew would long since have inquired. There is, moreover, a connection between Issoudun’s condition and the Bridau family’s interests, which will appear as the story goes on.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Issoudun, without disparaging Paris, ranks among France’s most ancient towns. Tradition makes Probus the Noah of the Gauls; Caesar praised the Champ-Fort wine (“de Campo Forti”), still one of Issoudun’s best. Rigord noted the medieval population and trade, yet Armand Peremet’s excavations revealed older strata: beneath the famed tower lay a fifth‑century basilica—probably unique in France—whose stones came from a Roman temple once on the site, preserving traces of an earlier civilization.Peremet contends that place‑names ending in “‑dun” point to autochthonous, Druidic, or Celtic origins. Beneath the Gaulish dun likely stood a Roman temple to Isis, hence Chaumon’s name Issous‑Dun—“Is” for Isis. Richard Coeur‑de‑Lion probably built the tower—where he coined money—over the fifth‑century basilica and enclosed the church within feudal fortifications. Issoudun later briefly sheltered the routiers and cottereaux Henry II sent against his son Richard during the Poitou rebellion.Evidence of Roman ingenuity survives in the Tournemine’s conversion into a canal—the “Rivière forcée”—a stream raised above the Theols that surrounds the town, doubtless Roman work. A northern suburb crossed by a rue de Rome is inhabited by people who call themselves Romans: vine‑growers with distinct features and customs, perhaps descendants of those who exterminated Cottereaux and Routiers on the plain of Charost in the twelfth century.After the 1830 insurrection, national turmoil hid a violent local uprising of vine‑growers. The bourgeois resisted military entry, invoking ancient municipal rights. Backed by seven or eight thousand vine‑growers, rioters burned archives and excise offices and dragged a customs officer through the streets, crying at each lantern, “Let us hang him here!” The National Guard saved him by carrying him to prison on a trumped‑up indictment. The general entered only after compromise; yet a faubourg man once swung a volant (a huge pruning‑hook) round his neck, shouting “No more clerks, or there’s an end to compromise!” A leader promised suppression of excisemen and ultimately intervened to save the general.In the fourteenth century Issoudun numbered sixteen or seventeen thousand—remnants of a once larger medieval population. Charles VII owned a mansion, known into the eighteenth century as the Maison du Roi. Once a center of the woollen trade, the town supplied much of Europe with wool for blankets, hats, and Chevreautin gloves. Under Louis XIV., Issoudun, birthplace of Baron and Bourdaloue, was famed for elegance and correct speech; the curate Poupard praised its subtlety and wit. Today that wit and splendor have faded. The town now counts barely twelve thousand inhabitants, including vine‑dressers of four large suburbs—Saint‑Paterne, Vilatte, Rome, and Alouette—that are almost separate villages. The bourgeoisie are scattered through the streets. Issoudun still holds Berry’s fleeces market, a commerce endangered by gradual improvements in stock‑breeding spreading through the region.The vineyards produce wine drunk across two departments and, if tended differently, could rival Burgundy or Gascony. Yet conservative habits persist: growers leave the marc in the fermenting juice, making the wine detestable though it might be a source of wealth. They claim the marc’s bitterness lessens with age, so a vintage will keep a century—an excuse as old as Guillaume le Breton.Issoudun’s decline stems from torpor and shortsighted parochialism. When a Paris–Toulouse highroad was planned, the natural line from Vierzon to Châteauroux would have passed through Issoudun, shorter than the present route via Vatan. Leading citizens and the town council insisted the road detour by Vatan, arguing a highway through Issoudun would raise prices and force them to pay thirty sous for a chicken—a narrow, protective folly like that once forcing a Sardinian highway to detour eight miles.Falling wine prices please bourgeois consumers seeking cheap provisions but threaten vine‑growers as costs and taxes rise—just as the woollen trade suffers from poor sheep‑breeding. Country folk often prefer poverty to the social stigma of adopting new habits; many bourgeois express this with inward pride.Social stagnation has set in. Families live apart; society lacks the class distinctions that give manners character. Nobility has vanished—cottereaux, routiers, the Jacquerie, religious wars, and the Revolution swept them away, a loss Issoudun applauds. The town repeatedly refused a garrison, claiming cheap provisions, thereby losing intercourse with the age and the benefits troops bring; before 1756 it had been a delightful garrison town. A judicial quarrel between a lieutenant‑general and the Marquis de Chapt deprived Issoudun of its garrison, and the forty‑fourth demi‑brigade’s temporary sojourn during the civil war did not reconcile inhabitants to soldiers.Bourges, likewise declining, shows the same malady. Vitality drains from such communities. The government is partly to blame: administration should find and heal wounds by sending energetic men with power to change things, but instead it approves fatal tranquillity. Who would accept being buried in arrondissements where useful work lacks glory? Ambitious outsiders soon sink into local inertia; Issoudun would have benumbed Napoleon.In 1822 the arrondissement’s administrators were nearly all locals, turning administration into nullity or farce. The procureur du roi, Monsieur Mouilleron, was cousin to much of the community; his substitute likewise from a town family. The court judge, notorious for a provincial saying, ended a summing‑up by telling the accused, “My poor Pierre! the thing is as plain as day; your head will be cut off. Let this be a lesson to you.” The commissary of police, in office since the Restoration, had relations throughout the arrondissement. Religion’s influence was weak; even the curate was held in little esteem. This radical, ignorant bourgeoisie—fond of annoying others—spread tales and gossiped, though Sunday‑schools continued, taxes were paid (the one thing Paris extracts from the provinces), and the mayor passed routine resolutions. But lax government reflected the moral and intellectual state of the governed. Such provincial conditions risk spreading if bourgeois rule continues to dominate policy.Topography: Issoudun stretches north–south along a hillside curving toward the Châteauroux road. At the hill’s foot the canal called the “Rivière forcée,” fed from the Theols, was built in the town’s flourishing days—to supply manufactories or flood rampart moats. This artificial arm of the Tournemine joins other streams beyond the Rome suburb. Threads of water irrigate wide meadows, enclosed by pale terraces dotted with dark specks—the vineyards’ appearance for many months. Vine‑growers annually cut plants to ugly stumps, leaving them unsupported and sheltered by barrels. A traveller from Vierzon, Vatan, or Châteauroux, weary of monotony, is pleasantly surprised by Issoudun’s verdant meadows—an oasis supplying vegetables within thirty miles. Below the Rome suburb lie vast kitchen‑gardens, upper and lower Baltan. A long poplar avenue leads from town across the meadows to the ancient convent of Frapesle, whose English gardens, grandly named Tivoli, draw Sunday lovers.Traces of ancient grandeur appear in town divisions. The château, once almost a town with walls and moats, remains a distinct quarter entered by ancient gateways via three bridges across two river arms, resembling an old city. Ramparts show formidable foundations on which houses have sprung. Above the château stands the famous tower, once the citadel. To conquer the city, which lay around these two fortified points, still left the tower and castle to be taken.The Saint‑Paterne suburb, a palette‑shaped extension beyond the tower encroaching on meadows, was likely part of the ancient city. In 1822 this seemed certain because the charming Romanesque church of Saint‑Paterne still stood—recently pulled down by the heir of its buyer from the Nation. That splendid portal perished without a single drawing; the lone voice raised to save it found no echo in town or department. Though the château quarter resembles an old town of narrow streets and mansions, the main city, repeatedly captured and burned—especially during the Fronde—has a modern air. Spacious streets and well‑built houses contrast with the citadel’s look, earning Issoudun the epithet “pretty” in some geographies.In a town lacking industry, art, or learned occupations, many young men after the Restoration (1816) found no career until marriage or inheritance. Bored and craving distraction, they “shed their cuticle” by sowing wild oats at the town’s expense. To avoid daytime recognition and police courts, they took their pranks by night. Thus, amid traces of lost civilizations, a last flare of drollery burst forth.These young men, imitating courtly and military camaraderie, banded together as the “Knights of Idleness.” By day they played saints, sleeping late after nights of mischief. Beginning with commonplace pranks—changing signs, ringing bells, tipping casks into cellars—they escalated after 1817 when Maxence Gilet, called Max, became Grand Master and until 1823 led a campaign of mischief that spread a sort of terror in Issoudun and kept artisans and bourgeois perpetually uneasy.Maxence’s background, energy, and bearing fitted him for the role. Town rumor claimed he was the natural son of the sub‑delegate Lousteau; others said Doctor Rouget. In truth his father was a dragoon officer; his mother, wife of a poor sabot‑maker in the Rome suburb, was strikingly beautiful. Madame Hochon paid for the lad’s schooling; Doctor Rouget subsidized his seminary board until 1805, leaving paternity ambiguous. In 1806, at seventeen, Max committed an involuntary homicide—stealing fruit, he startled a pregnant woman whose death was blamed on him. Threatened, he fled to Bourges and enlisted in a regiment bound for Egypt.In three campaigns Max distinguished himself and rose to captain. In Portugal (1809) he was left for dead in an English battery, taken prisoner and sent to the Spanish hulks at Cabrera from 1810 to 1814—an appalling prison. To resist corruption there he killed seven bullies in duels, establishing order on his hulk by strength and skill. Returning in 1814, innocent of worse guilt, he found his parents dead and destitute. He sought rank and decoration at Paris; a marshal put him in the Guard as captain and promised the Emperor’s recognition. At Fleurus he earned mention; Napoleon later put him on the decoration list.After Waterloo Max retreated. Marshal Feltre refused to acknowledge his grade or cross; Max resigned in disgust, losing half‑pay. The town roused to help: the mayor found him a six‑hundred‑franc post, which Max soon left. He became Grand Master of the Knights of Idleness and lived in ways that lost bourgeois favour—though none openly contradicted him, for he was feared. Issoudun harbored more Bonapartists than most towns; these men became nearly all Liberals.Around Max a dozen officers rallied—many veterans who had refused Bourbon service. They admired him except Captain Carpentier and Major Mignonnet. Carpentier, a cavalry officer risen from the ranks who had married into the best local family, and Mignonnet, an École Polytechnique man, judged Max dishonourable. The Imperial army had divided types: many soldiers despised civilians, while others—artillerymen, engineers—kept republican habits. Thus Potel and Renard befriended Max; Mignonnet and Carpentier sided with the bourgeoisie.Max showed no ostentatious complaint at exclusion from polite society. Handsome, well dressed, keeping a horse—a rarity—he had pride and expected revenge. Though the bourgeois disapproved, the common people admired his courage and daring; many degradations were unknown to them. He became a local champion of Bonapartism and Opposition, counted on in great occasions.One episode displayed his heroism. In 1819 a battalion of royalist officers, fresh from Maison Rouge, lingered at the café Militaire—the Bonapartist rendezvous—and insulted the patrons by demanding royalist papers. Max, with Major Potel and Captain Renard and thirty young men, entered; a captain tore and spat on a paper. Max slapped an officer and fought; in a street fight at Frapesle he killed his man, Potel wounded another who later died, and the battalion left that night. The affair spread through Berry and made Max a local hero.The Knights, nearly all under twenty‑five, trained like pupils of Amoros—bold, agile, clever and strong. They practised roof‑climbing, scaling houses, noiseless walking, mixing mortar, walling doors, and amassed ropes, ladders, tools, and disguises. They perfected pranks with a Panurge‑like genius that provoked laughter while covering victims with ridicule and silencing complaints. Being sons of good families, they held household secrets that facilitated outrages.They lurked in doorways at night, calling “What’s the matter?” in tones of terror to rouse citizens into the streets in shirts and caps. They dressed as devils and invaded a poor bookbinder’s basement, forcing him into his cutting‑press and leaving him shrieking about Lucifer. They rebuilt a tax collector’s chimney so the flue sent smoke into the house for two months until he rebuilt it. They stuffed hay and oiled paper into a pious woman’s chimney; when she lit her fire the next morning it seemed a volcano—fire‑engines came, the Knights among the firemen deluged her house, frightening her into illness. They wrote anonymous letters threatening robbery, then prowled softly under windows, whistling. One prank sent letters to eighty heirs of a miserly woman announcing her death; they arrived in deep mourning only to find a scene of confusion between servants and notaries.The sub‑prefect finally awoke to the intolerable state, made worse by his own pretensions. He boasted of his egg‑boiling skill—breakfasting on two fresh eggs precisely timed—and for a month each night his eggs were replaced by hard‑boiled ones, ruining his reputation as the “sous‑préfet à l’oeuf.” Max greased his stoves with fetid oil nightly; his wife once found her shawl glued inside and had to go without it. The sub‑prefect requested transfer. His cowardly submissiveness helped establish the Knights’ comic authority.Beyond rue des Minimes and place Misère lay a quarter enclosed by an arm of the Rivière forcée and ramparts, from place d’Armes to the pottery market. This square of narrow streets and hovels, long a haunt of the poor and disreputables, housed low trades and a kind of “cour des Miracles.” One thoroughfare is still called “the street of the Executioner,” and a red door marks where the executioner, or his assistant from Châteauroux, lives; vine‑dressers keep intercourse with him, for he inherits the craft of curing wounds. Second‑hand sellers and old‑clothes dealers made the air fetid—typical of such quarters where two or three Jews sometimes gain ascendancy.At a corner in the livelier half of this quarter stood, from 1815 to 1823, a public house kept by Mère Cognette. The house, of white stone, one storey with an attic, bore a huge pine branch over the door. A blue poster above the words “Good Beer of Mars” showed a soldier pouring foam toward a very décolletée woman. The ground floor was an immense hall; a winding staircase led to the upper storey. A low, long room lit from a narrow, sunken court served as the Knights’ secret assembly chamber—hidden, like a provincial chimney flue.Père Cognet, formerly a groom, and La Cognette, formerly a cook, had bought the house by uniting savings. La Cognette, about forty, lively and shrewd, was chosen by Max as the Order’s Leonarde for her character and cookery; Père Cognet, thick‑set and under his wife’s rule, kept absolute silence about nightly gatherings. From 1816 to 1823 neither revealed what went on. Their devotion was absolute, secured by self‑interest: when the Knights knocked a certain way Père Cognet would light fires and candles, open the door, fetch a particular wine laid in for the Order, and La Cognette prepared excellent suppers for the expeditions planned that night or the next.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While Joseph and Madame Bridau were traveling from Orléans to Issoudun, the Knights of Idleness played one of their best jokes. Early one morning an old Spaniard, a former prisoner of war who sold grain near Issoudun, left his empty cart at the foot of the town tower. Maxence, who had come for a Knights’ rendezvous, whispered, “Here’s Père Fario’s cart. I nearly cracked my shins over it. Let us get it up on the embankment of the tower first, and we’ll decide afterwards.” The Issoudun tower, raised by Richard Cœur-de-Lion on ancient foundations, sat atop a steep cone of monuments; toward the town its pedestal rose eighty feet. In an hour the Knights took the cart off its wheels and hoisted it piece by piece to the top of the embankment at the tower’s base—a feat like soldiers carrying artillery over a mountain pass. They remounted the cart and, hungry and thirsty, returned to Mère Cognette’s to laugh at how Père Fario would fume when he came for his barrow in the morning.The Knights did not prank nightly; three clever fellows could not invent mischief every day. When not meeting at night they hunted, harvested, and skated. Among the twenty youths some were closer to Maxence and made him their idol. Two grandsons of Madame Hochon—François Hochon and Baruch Borniche—were his henchmen. Accepting family gossip about Lousteau’s left-handed parentage, they regarded Max as kin. Max lent them money, took them hunting, and exercised more influence than their grandfather; both were orphans under Monsieur Hochon’s guardianship.That night François and Baruch sat one on each side of Max at the middle of a table lit by four tallow candles. A dozen or fifteen bottles had been drained; only eleven Knights were present. Baruch, whose name reflected Issoudun’s Calvinist past, said as the wine loosened tongues, “You are threatened in your stronghold.” Max asked what he meant. Baruch explained that Madame Hochon had had a letter from Madame Bridau—her goddaughter—saying she and her son were coming to Issoudun; his grandmother was readying two rooms. The suggestion alarmed the others: Madame Bridau was sister to Rouget, and if she took steps when Rouget died she might secure the property that Max counted on.Max listened with an officer’s gravity. He was thirty-four, with a soldierly face, ardent eyes, black hair bristling in five “points,” about five feet six, well proportioned and broad-shouldered—attractive, commanding, and used to concealment after hard experience. When François and Goddet voiced the town’s fears that Flore Brazier—the “Rabouilleuse,” as respectable folk mockingly called her—might lose influence to legitimate relatives, Max cut in. He resented the contemptuous nickname for a woman he was known to favor. In the conversation he admitted, more plainly than before, his attachment to Mademoiselle Flore Brazier: people thought she provided his money and that he intended to marry her when Père Rouget died. He acknowledged that Flore aimed at the old man’s property, and faced the Knights’ crude gossip about her.Max declared he had already silenced those who meddled with his affairs, and asked the Knights if they would help get rid of the Bridaus “within the limits we have marked out for our fooleries.” He denied being an idiot—he would not poison anyone—and said he would be satisfied if Flore, reduced to her own standing, still sufficed for him; he loved her enough to prefer her to Mademoiselle Fichet, the richest heiress in Issoudun. The Knights cheered him: “We’ll stand by you!” Eleven acclamations, toasts, and relaxed morals reflected their mood. Max’s leadership among these youths—coming from the best families and giving them favors—had created a following useful in his designs to recover position. He waved his glass and made arrangements: “Be in the market-place early, all of you, and let me know when the old fellow goes for his cart.” Goddet added, “Don’t forget Fario’s cart!”—a joke some wanted to keep.At half-past three in the morning the Knights slipped away. Max returned slowly to the quarter of the rich bourgeoisie, having concealed his fears though the news had struck home. His time on the hulks at Cabrera had taught him dissimulation. Above all, the forty thousand francs a year from Rouget’s landed property underpinned Max’s interest in Flore; her influence over Rouget had given him hope of a secure financial future. Yet the arrival of legitimate heirs disturbed that faith. Rouget’s savings—seven hundred and fifty thousand francs placed with different notaries at Bourges, Vierzon, and Châteauroux—could not be quietly transferred into Flore’s name without discovery. “The important thing is to get rid of these relations; once they’re driven away we must hurry to secure the property,” thought Max. He entered Rouget’s house with a pass-key and went to bed, promising himself clearer thoughts the next day.The narrative then explains why Flore became known as the “Rabouilleuse” and how she came to be quasi-mistress of Jean-Jacques Rouget’s home. As old Doctor Rouget aged he perceived his son’s nonentity and treated him harshly; coming one day from his rounds he encountered a bewitching little girl at the edge of some fields along the avenue de Tivoli. The child sprang up from the brook, half naked and barefoot, wearing a forlorn petticoat of coarse strips and a rough paper hat; despite rags she had a charming head and figure. She and her uncle were catching crabs. “Rabouiller” is a Berrichon word meaning to trouble the water to bring crabs to the surface; Flore Brazier held her rabouilloir with childlike grace. The doctor learned she was from Vatan, that her father was in an asylum, and that she earned five sous a day catching crabs and gleaning in harvest, spinning in winter. She was about twelve.Rouget proposed she come to his house: “You shall be well fed and well dressed, and have some pretty shoes.” Uncle Brazier, a sun-rotted peasant, agreed—more readily when Rouget offered wages and proposed to pay in advance. The doctor pronounced her “worthy of the name” and ordered a room prepared. That evening all Issoudun talked of Rouget’s sudden adoption of the little rabouilleuse; in that satirical region the nickname stuck to Flore before, during, and after her good fortune.Rouget’s house, inherited from the Descoings family, impressed the peasant child: a panelled hall, a marble chimney-piece with a gilded mirror, old masters and sacred pictures, Boulle clock, silver candlesticks, carved armchairs—luxuries little valued in the town but unmistakable signs of wealth. Rouget treated her to good living, Fanchette the cook showing both Rouget’s despotism and domestic bustle. The doctor’s ostensible motives varied in town gossip: some thought he desired a youthful companion; others declared him past such things and merely calculating. From 1800 to 1805 Rouget provided Flore a life of comfort. He dressed her, gave jewels and a gold watch, and provided a master to teach her to read, write, and cipher; but Flore disliked the bit of learning and Rouget stopped at the point she would not take to studies.Provincial gossip multiplied explanations: was the old man courting her, planning a marriage, or merely indulging a fancy? The town’s minds insisted on sensational motives, and few sympathized with Rouget’s restraint or the complexity of his feelings. The doctor’s intentions—whether paternal, selfish, or lecherous—were debated and colored by small-town malice. Some suggested he might marry Flore and father an active boy like Max; others laughed at the improbability of old men’s virility. Brazier, when drunk, was said to boast of having cheated Rouget; rumor thrived.Flore grew up between the saturnalias of 1793 and 1798 and the slow restoration of worship under the Concordat; revolutionary impulses had loosened moral restraints, and marriage often became a civil contract. By 1802, religious practice was still weak in many parishes; moral checks were slack, and Flore—born in 1787—had been formed in this environment. She was envied by peasant girls and deemed “wide awake,” a local phrase implying a shrewdness born of practical life. The doctor treated her kindly but remained, at heart, calculating; in his last years he displayed less warmth, and town gossip read that as confirming his indifference.As Rouget aged and his end approached, the notary urged him to make provision for the young woman. His answer revealed his nature: “So I do,” he said cynically; “my death sets her at liberty.” That phrase—bitter, calculating, and clothed in a certain witty cruelty—registered in the notary’s mind as the concentrated hatred of a man whose desires had been frustrated by Nature. When urged again to provide for Flore, the doctor replied, “Her beauty will make her rich enough!”—a remark confirming that he intended to leave her nothing, relying on the world’s appetite for a pretty woman to secure her future. Thus the town’s suspicions about the doctor’s motives and the precariousness of Flore’s position were confirmed by his whitely cruel remarks, and by the prevailing gossip that shaped how she was viewed thereafter.Throughout, Max’s personal stakes are clear: Flore’s connection to Rouget and the prospect of Rouget’s fortune shaped his fortune-seeking plans. He both loved and depended upon her influence. The arrival of Madame Bridau and her son threatened that arrangement, and Max—practical, deceitful, and socially adept—turned to his followers among the Knights of Idleness to execute petty and perhaps decisive measures, confident in the small-town setting where rumor, pranks, and private schemes could alter fortunes as surely as law or inheritance.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Jean-Jacques Rouget did not mourn his father, though Flore Brazier did. The old doctor had made his son unhappy since Jean-Jacques came of age in 1791, but he had given the little peasant girl the comforts country folk prize. After the funeral, when Fanchette asked Flore, “Well, what is to become of you, now that monsieur is dead?” Jean-Jacques’s dull face suddenly showed feeling.Seventeen and still delicate, with the embonpoint of a life without toil, Flore retained the distinction that had attracted the doctor. When Jean-Jacques, trembling, asked if she felt at home and whether she was honest, his timidity made him clumsy; he could only take her hand and, unable to say more, suggest she stay and be mistress of the house. Flore, touched and practical, agreed; the explanation of his character would come slowly.At his father’s death Jean-Jacques—then thirty-seven—remained as submissive and timid as a child. His timidity sprang from dulled faculties and nervous weakness; he had passion without intellect, enough sense for ordinary affairs but no resource when desire froze his speech. Small, awkward, with a vulgar flattened face and pale skin, he was socially awkward and sought solitude. The doctor, who wanted his son married but feared surrendering control of the estate, bred avarice and mechanical habits into him, keeping the lands in order while sparing management burdens. This failed to conceal Jean-Jacques’s passionate love for Flore. She was the light of his home; he watched her secretly and rejoiced that his father had trained her into easy obedience. Passion steadied his mind as much as intellect could have.For three weeks after the funeral the strange situation persisted: Flore treated him indulgently, and Jean-Jacques hovered between confession and silence. One night she found him crouched like a dog on the landing and thought, “He loves me.” Eventually, during another dessert, Flore told him the truth about his father’s temper; Rouget, taking her hand, asked if she would stay with him. She answered she would if he wished it. Ten months later, after long hesitation and need of certainty, Jean-Jacques at last relaxed: his face cleared, he breathed happiness, and Flore—as mistress—insisted he take better care of himself. The town noticed the transformation and gossiped: some thought Flore would make him marry, others praised her devotion. Thus she obtained management of the Rouget household—from father to son, as young Goddet had said.Fanchette, the cook, alone thought it wrong that Flore should preside. She protested injured virtue but was humiliated by a child mistress. Owning three hundred francs a year and an annuity from the doctor, she could live without working and left the house nine months after the funeral, on April 15, 1806—a date the narrator hints may mark when Flore ceased to be an “honest girl.”Foreseeing Fanchette’s departure, Flore resolved to manage without a servant. For six months she studied the cook’s arts. The doctor, trained in Paris under Rouelle, had applied chemistry to cooking and improved provincial technique—subtleties of omelettes, the use of a cagnard, how to avoid browned-butter tartness. Flore, a born fryer and roaster, soon surpassed Fanchette and became a cordon-bleu, caring finely for Jean-Jacques’s comfort. She rubbed furniture, kept everything clean, managed the wash and mended linen with thrift. She learned the family business, consulted the notary Monsieur Heron, and advised Jean-Jacques. Having lost both her own parents—the uncle Brazier, who left a wine-shop, dying shortly before the doctor—she served Rouget with an orphan’s gratitude.This period was paradise for poor Jean-Jacques. He was trained into monastic regularity: Flore woke him with breakfast, he took a morning walk, read papers at three, lingered at dinner, and went to bed early to save fire and candles—provincial economies that lull the mind. For nine years their life was regular. Jean-Jacques lent at five percent on first mortgages, releasing the wife’s rights if married, never more than a third of a property’s value, and charged notes for an extra two and a half percent—his father’s rules. Rouget’s seven and a half percent levy, while a burden on peasant ambition, seemed reasonable and allowed him choice; notaries gained commissions.During these nine years Flore obtained absolute control. At first she treated him familiarly; then, surpassing him in intelligence and force, she became in fact his superior and he the servant of his servant. He accepted this by clinging to her care as a son to a mother. Flore kept the house and managed all business; Jean-Jacques left everything to her so completely that life without her seemed impossible. She indulged his fancies, understood him thoroughly, and her brightness was his chief pleasure. Secure in her power, she did not think to make him marry her.By the end of 1815 Flore, now twenty-seven, had reached full beauty: plump, fresh, white-skinned, the ideal buxom housewife, likened to a handsome barmaid of a higher type. In 1816 she saw Maxence Gilet and fell in love at first sight. Maxence, a penniless officer, saw more than a passing fancy in Flore once he learned of her position and Jean-Jacques’s weakness. He readily took up abode at the Rougets’, recognizing how little the old bachelor would resist.Flore’s passion changed household affairs: she grew sullen and gave Jean-Jacques flashes of temper like a wife suspecting infidelity. She reproached him bitterly: sixteen years she had spent her youth in his house; he hoarded wealth—“six hundred and fifty-nine thousand well invested, and sixty thousand francs’ income”—yet refused to offer the worthy and impoverished Captain Maxence a home on the empty second floor. “You’d rather rats and mice run riot than put a human being there,” she cried, calling him a fratricide for his inaction. Jean-Jacques, terrified at losing her, promised to invite Maxence.He did so politely and publicly; a reconciliation took place, but Jean-Jacques noticed that Flore’s affections had wholly shifted. For weeks she complained to tradespeople about Rouget’s alleged stinginess in welcoming his natural brother. No one was deceived: they saw Flore as clever and artful. Old Rouget, however, found comfort after Max’s arrival; he gained a companion who paid him attention without servility. Maxence talked, discussed politics, walked with Rouget—he was lively company.After Max’s arrival Flore stopped cooking, saying it spoiled her hands. Mere Cognette, grand master of idleness, produced a relative: an old maid who had lost a curate master and promised devotion—an excellent cook named Vedie, pitted by smallpox and ugly, who took over the kitchen in exchange for an annuity of three hundred francs after ten years. Flore now adopted the title Madame Brazier and upgraded her dress: corsets, fine wool and silk, neckerchiefs, embroidered caps, collars, lace ruffles and boots. She renewed her youth through richness and elegance, polishing a rough diamond into worth, and wished to honor Maxence. In 1817 she brought an English horse from Bourges for the weary cavalry captain. Max also found in Issoudun an old lancer of the Imperial Guard, a Pole named Kouski, who, now poor, quartered himself at Rouget’s as the captain’s servant and became idolized by Maxence after a duel with three royalists.Thus, from 1817, the household numbered five people, three of whom were masters, and expenses rose to about eight thousand francs a year. Flore had come from peasant origins to absolute control of a comfortable fortune, and Jean-Jacques, passive and devoted, remained fenced in by the very domestic regularities and thriftiness that had once consoled him—now the instruments of his subordination to a woman who had learned how to rule.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Madame Bridau returned to Issoudun to claim, Maitre Desroches said, an inheritance at stake, Jean‑Jacques Rouget had fallen into a semi‑vegetative state. After Max’s instalment, Flore imposed strict order in the household. Rouget ate better under Vedie’s cooking but did not regain health; he grew weaker, his eyes circled with dark rings. Neighbors, thinking him mentally deficient, did not note his failing faculties. He lived only for Flore, obeying her slightest look and watching her like a dog. At fifty‑seven he seemed, in Madame Hochon’s phrase, older than Monsieur Hochon, an octogenarian.People assumed Max’s apartment suited his charm. In six years the captain had improved and adorned it for himself and Flore—colored tiles, passable wallpapers, mahogany furniture, gilt mirrors, muslin curtains, a canopy bed—comfort that was luxury in Issoudun though common in Paris. One small oddity caused comment: rush‑matting on the stairs to muffle footsteps. Max came in at daybreak without waking anyone; Rouget, as always, suspected nothing of his guest’s nocturnal activities with the Knights of Idleness.One morning Flore, in a striped cotton dressing‑gown, lace cap, and furred slippers, softly opened Max’s door and found him asleep. He woke instantly, like a soldier. Flore feared mischief; Max told her something serious had happened and sent her for coffee, bidding her feign ignorance and return at nine. A quarter‑hour later Baruch burst in, exclaiming that Fario was hunting for his barrow.Max dressed and strolled to the tower embankment, where a crowd had gathered. Fario was a withered little Spaniard—called “goodman Fario”—with piercing eyes and an air of old‑world gentility concealing a peasant stubbornness. He swore his cart had been left at the foot of the tower; the horse stood harnessed thirty feet away. Max gravely examined the scene and suggested the cart might have been drawn somewhere. Fario insisted the cart was light but now sat inexplicably atop the embankment.Pointing to the top of the embankment, Max revealed the barrow perched there. The market erupted. Goddet joked the devil made common cause with inn‑keepers; laughter rose and Fario lost his temper. Max directed Francois to clear the crowd below, then, taking advantage of Fario’s stupefaction, he and Baruch and others climbed the steep embankment. There was no trace of how the cart had been carried up.At the top, Max, with a theatrical air, lifted the barrow by the shafts and flung it down, warning those below. The cart smashed picturesquely; the crowd, kept at a distance, cheered. Fario raged, first accusing brigands, then suspecting Max, and finally, mestizo and vindictive, threatened to demand a new cart. Max japed that he would give a magnificent cart if Fario lent him two hundred and fifty francs, cooling the Spaniard with a mocking bargain. Though furious, Fario left muttering to procure a replacement. The spectacle—one shaft standing upright like a tree—kept Issoudun talking for days; the tale spread through Berry and reached other towns. Because of Fario’s vindictive remark, Max and the Rabouilleuse were whispered about and discussed in Bourges, Vatan, Vierzon, and Chateauroux. Max enceinte knew how venomous such talk would be.“We can’t stop their tongues,” he said. “Ah! I did a foolish thing!”“Max!” Francois warned. “They are coming tonight.”“Who?”“The Bridaus. My grandmother has had a letter from her goddaughter.”Max, always calculating, counseled caution. He thought neither he nor Flore ought to seem opposed to the Bridaus; if the heirs were to be disposed of, let the Hochons drive them out. He advised judging the Parisians at Mere Cognette’s the next day and then planning how to set old Hochon against them. Max saw social manipulation as the proper approach.While Max plotted, Flore, despite advice to the contrary, erupted at the poor bachelor she controlled. Rouget’s small attentions and vulgar fondlings—his only joys—were suppressed. Flore cast him into disgrace: no tender epithets, no help with his toilette, no anticipation of his wants. A cold, sharp, ironic “you” cut through his heart; the “you” was a declaration of war. If he called, she answered from below the stairs with impatience. Her blows of contempt and feigned neglect wounded the miserable old man, whose existence depended on her favor.Vedie, the servant, intervened tactfully, urging Rouget to cherish Flore, reminding him the woman was superior and that making her unhappy would be his ruin. Rouget, bewildered and humiliated, was stricken; these alternations of tenderness and severity affected him like climate changes attack the body. Emotion set off his coughing fits. Flore’s cruelty was a moral pleurisy that wore him down; she could make only him foolish. She berated him for nothing and then, when she pleased, relented with artful tenderness.That morning she told him vaguely of his sister Agathe’s coming from Paris with her sons, much to his astonishment. Rouget protested he had never sent for them and that his father had made him promise to leave nothing to Agathe, who, he said, had no claim on him. Flore threatened to tear his will and drive them off, declaring she and Max his true family. Max intervened, arguing Rouget should receive his sister and nephews to avoid scandal and the appearance of manipulation. He counseled keeping the property arrangements unchanged while entertaining the Bridaus to preserve appearances in Issoudun. His motive was practical: rumor could ruin him.Flore, now mollified, helped set the table. Rouget, full of admiration for Max, took him by both hands and declared his love for him as if he had been a son. Max urged Rouget to receive his kin but not to alter his will. After breakfast, Max carried Flore back to his rooms and discussed finances. He feared a secret will had been made; they must be cordial to the Bridaus while converting Rouget’s mortgages into cash. The Funds were rising; by investing Rouget’s seven hundred and fifty thousand francs in the Funds at eighty‑nine, they could secure a profit, make him borrow a guaranteed sum for two years, and keep control. Flore delighted in these schemes and praised Max’s cleverness. He planned also to maneuver the Hochons into disposing of the Parisians.The narrative then turns to the Hochon household across from Rouget’s in the Grand and Petite Narette at the Place Saint‑Jean. The two houses, likely by the same architect, faced each other so occupants could observe goings‑on when curtains and doors were open. Monsieur Hochon, formerly tax‑collector at Selles and born at Issoudun, had returned to marry Lousteau’s sister and had exchanged offices. Retiring before 1787, he escaped the Revolution’s perils yet adhered to its triumphs like many “honest men.” He had a reputation for miserliness, illustrated by a story: at his daughter’s wedding, when the cook asked for string to truss a turkey, he gave a used bit and demanded it be returned.As he aged, Hochon became petty; at eighty‑five he was the sort to pick up a pin mid‑talk and call it “the wife’s stipend.” He enforced strict, abstemious rules at home, weighing and measuring food for his household: Madame Hochon née Lousteau, his grandson Borniche and his sister Adolphine (heirs of old Borniche), and his other grandson Francois. His eldest son, swallowed by the 1813 draft, had died at Hanau; his widow left debts Hochon refused to pay, citing that “women are minors.”The house was large but sparsely furnished. Two second‑floor rooms, barely fit for Madame Bridau and Joseph, contained old armchairs, a walnut table with a pitcher, and heaps of winter fruit on straw where rats ran—an odor of fruit and vermin. Madame Hochon, ever provident, directed cleaning, patched wallpaper, pieced curtains from old cloth, borrowed night‑tables, and hired chests of drawers to make these rooms presentable. She even risked using two candlesticks carved by her father in his turner phase: relics from 1770–80 ornamented with root circlets. These sacrifices increased Hochon’s anxiety; he had not previously believed the Bridaus’ arrival would be real.After breakfast the day of Fario’s trick, Madame Hochon reminded her husband to receive her goddaughter, Madame Bridau, properly, and privately warned him she would make provision in her will for Agathe if he slighted her. Hochon replied he knew forms of civility at his age. She pressed him, denouncing Maxence Gilet as the man getting Rouget’s money—“you’ve warmed a viper in your bosom,” he muttered—and warning of the risk of living with the Rouget connection. He fretted about the Parisians’ influence and the possibility that, once established, they would “extricate” Rouget from the Hochons and their circle.Madame Hochon, spare and erect with powdered hair and a taffeta petticoat, placed her snuff‑box down and insisted she loved Agathe and would see justice done. Hochon left bewildered by her firmness. She took up her prayer‑book; old age prevented daily attendance at church, but she added petitions for Agathe’s success to her services, begged God to open Rouget’s eyes, and had the curate say a mass during a novena. Concealing her plans from her grandchildren, whom she called “parpaillots,” she set Adolphine, her granddaughter, to make the novena in church by proxy.Adolphine, eighteen and raised at her grandmother’s side in a household of monotony, sewed for seven years, and now took an interest in Joseph Bridau, hoping to sway him away from the “monstrosities” grandfather Hochon attributed to the young Parisian. The town’s older bourgeoisie approved Madame Hochon’s conduct in receiving her goddaughter; they secretly despised Maxence Gilet’s methods yet wished the Bridaus well. Thus Rouget’s sister and nephew’s impending arrival split Issoudun into two parties: the respectable, older bourgeoisie who offered good wishes but little help, and the Knights of Idleness with Max’s partisans, who might resort to high‑handed measures against the Parisians.Throughout these events the author underscores a social truth: Flore Brazier’s coarse behavior is the same species of feminine dominance as a duchess’s pouting or a wealthy bourgeoise’s ironies—only education and circumstance differ. The same tactics of insult, contempt, and feigned tenderness operate at all social levels when women move from obedience to power. Max, meanwhile, is the strategist, using public scandal, private cajolery, and financial manipulation to secure Rouget’s fortune and his own advantage. The Bridaus’ arrival threatens these plans, and the town’s factions ready to back either the newcomers or the entrenched clique prepare the stage for the next maneuvers.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Agathe and Joseph arrived at the Messageries-Royales office in the Place Misère at three in the afternoon. Madame Bridau, though fatigued, felt revived by the sight of her native town; each step stirred memories of girlhood. News of the Parisians spread at once through Issoudun; within minutes everyone knew they had come. Madame Hochon greeted her godchild at the door with affectionate, almost maternal warmth; after seventy-two barren years and the graves of three unhappy children, she welcomed this tie to the past and immediately took an interest in Agathe. Agathe was led into the salon where Monsieur Hochon sat, dour and sardonic.Introductions were made: Baruch Borniche, François Hochon, and little Adolphine, a shy, blushing girl who avoided looking at Joseph Bridau. The miserly Monsieur Hochon sniffed at flattery; the two young men and the old man studied Joseph’s gaunt, brigand-like face and wild expression with curiosity and guarded mistrust. Agathe presented Joseph warmly as “my good Joseph,” betraying a mother’s pride and recollections of her own son in the Luxembourg prison. Joseph admitted bluntly he was “like my father, and very ugly at that.” Madame Hochon squeezed Agathe’s hand as if to say she now understood her preferences.The household bustled: trunks were carried to attic rooms, town gossip flared, and Baruch rushed off to announce Joseph’s arrival and to describe his haggard appearance. By evening Joseph was the talk of Issoudun; gossip made him seem monstrous, potentially dangerous, and the very thing everyone speculated about. Some joked of having their portraits painted while he was in town.Inspecting the Hochon house underscored the fall from Paris to provincial life: bare, damp rooms, shabby stairs and furniture. At dinner, Hochon’s miserliness governed everything. Joseph found the meal meager, each course served with ceremonious stinginess—watered soup, thin bouilli reduced to shoe-thin slices, vegetables parceled out as if separate parts of the meal, custard flavored ungenerously, and a ritualistic handing out of bread from a great loaf. There was a country vintage wine, Adolphine’s table-flowers, and the household’s slow, habit-bound economy. Joseph felt they might better have stayed at an inn. When he asked for more bread, Hochon unlocked a cupboard and broke off a section with the resigned sanctity of someone performing a ritual; requesting more was evidently forbidden.Yet the surroundings revealed small mercies. When Gritte, the aged servant, brought dessert—Touraine-Berry cheese with its mold, nuts and old-fashioned biscuits—and claimed there was no fruit, Joseph laughed; parsimony had become second nature. Hochon, prodded, produced peaches, pears, plums and grapes. The young men’s healthy appearances suggested they managed to eat well despite the household’s prudence; Baruch smiled, hinting that family visits to Mére Cognette supplied their regular feasts.Madame Hochon’s kindness showed in a private flask of ratafia she brewed from a convent recipe. She counseled patience: life in Issoudun was always a kind of Lent, nourishing enough to keep existence going and no more. She told Agathe that Hochon would soon go to his club to read the papers, leaving them some privacy. In the salon—frozen in provincial stasis as if Monarchy, Revolution, Empire and Restoration had left no mark—she and Agathe spoke of life’s trials and resignation. Adolphine, destined for the cloister, listened for tales of local characters: Maxence Gilet, the Rabouilleuse, and Jean-Jacques Rouget. Madame Hochon promised numerous curious visitors that night and recounted local intrigues—Maxence’s influence over Rouget; the Rabouilleuse’s exploits—hinting the house and its furniture had been recently restored after being abandoned by a usurper.Joseph, meanwhile, observed less of these social dynamics than the faces in the salon: from five till half-past nine he sat and studied the guests, taking in their features and expressions like a painter’s practice. His sarcastic looks and sinister visage left the company uneasy. After the household retired, Madame Hochon kept Agathe speaking with her until midnight; Agathe glimpsed the waste of a noble unrecognized soul in Joseph and acknowledged that despite Parisian small joys, she was not the most unhappy. The old woman framed hardship as preparation for a final reckoning.At midnight the town’s Knights of Idleness gathered under the boulevard Baron trees. François thought Maxence meant to give supper; Baruch fretted about feeding two extra mouths. Maxence arrived and called for a raid on Mére Cognette’s—an all-out cry to the town. At her modest tavern supper, some twenty-two members of his Order ate well. After the feast Maxence rose and spoke: his honor had been attacked that morning by a peddler—“a Spaniard, Cabrera”—and he intended a revenge that would remain within their rules. His scheme was perversely ingenious: each member was to procure clandestinely twenty rats—male or female—within three days, starve them until ravenous, and release the four hundred beasts in the old Capuchin church where Fario stored grain. The rats would eat a “not insignificant quantity,” causing Fario to suffer, and thus the Spaniard’s store would be diminished.Maxence framed the plan as philanthropic, protecting “persecuted” animals like Brahmins; he credited the idea to others while comparing it to Samson’s foxes, though he claimed higher motives. Mademoiselle Flore Brazier set mousetraps; Kouski sought field-mice; Goddet offered to find large animals; Beaussier would commandeer pigeons; the committee even discussed a monkey or squirrels to attract more rats. Max appointed commissariat and emphasized secrecy: turn shoe-soles backward, make the watchman drunk. He offered prizes—his shotgun or horse—to anyone devising a trick causing the Parisians trouble with the Hochons or driving them away without harming the ancestors of his two friends. Thus a townful of small plots set to work, collectively scheming against Agathe and Joseph.The next morning, before the Hochons’ second breakfast at ten, Agathe and Joseph saw Jean-Jacques Rouget across the street in a state of dishabille and debility that shocked Agathe. He was gaunt and altered, forced into odd postures by the influence of local characters—chief among them Flore Brazier. Flore stood behind him, made up and fashionable, one arm around him in a staged scene: snowy shoulders, a dazzling décolletage, a gauze neckerchief trimmed with lace, a grenadine dress with leg-of-mutton sleeves and a glittering gold chain. The tableau was theatrical, and Joseph, unrestrained as a man of the atelier, burst into praise: Flore’s beauty and form were like a Titian’s Venus, a “magnificent caryatid,” a model fit for masterful art. Adolphine and Madame Hochon took his talk for studio jargon; Agathe signed behind his back that she was used to such talk. But Agathe feared this woman’s power over her brother, noting that if Jean-Jacques were not thereby deprived of reason and prospects, he would have married and had children, likely dazzling consequences for Agathe’s claims to the family property.Mid-breakfast, Kouski presented a letter from Jean-Jacques to his sister. Monsieur Hochon read it aloud: Rouget, constrained by ill health, regretted he could not pay the first visit but invited Joseph to dine the next day, asking Baruch Borniche and François Hochon to accompany him. Hochon’s slyness surfaced: he muttered that Jean-Jacques could as well have written the note as he could have given away twenty-five louis, implying a hint of shared manipulation and a soldierly hand in his correspondence. Madame Hochon, fearing that Agathe might be left with a legacy that could fall into Hochon’s hands, was eager to settle the matter quickly: an enormous fortune was at stake, and the situation roused Monsieur Hochon’s dormant political acumen.While Gritte returned with the town’s gossip: the Rabouilleuse had had the salon and Rouget’s portion of the house in a pitiable state but had staged a grand turnaround; Kouski had fetched provisions; everything suggested a dinner “fit for the archbishop of Bourges.” Flore Brazier had dressed to dazzle—diamonds in her ears, costly lace, a silk dress, black satin slippers—so conspicuous that the town whispered of her ornaments and the expense of her attire. Joseph, amused and pleased, quipped they would have to dress up for the occasion.As they drafted Agathe’s reply to Rouget’s letter, the Hochons arranged political strategy. Agathe’s reply thanked her brother for the invitation, explained that her long neglect of correspondence owed to the trials of Paris—her husband’s disgrace and their son Philippe’s imprisonment for devotion to the Emperor—and justified her coming to seek consolation among her birthfolk. She explained Joseph’s profession required long study and sacrifice; while it might bring honor, it promised little immediate fortune. She accepted Jean-Jacques’s invitation on Joseph’s behalf, asserting a sister’s right to come to her brother’s house and promising Joseph would attend the dinner. The letter emphasized family ties, the hardships she had borne, and the distinct fates of her two nephews—one in prison, the other pursuing art—hoping to touch Jean-Jacques’s sense of family duty.Monsieur Hochon, who had been ruminating over the possibilities, finally spoke his counsel: Agathe must see Jean-Jacques and press him plainly about his nephews. He distrusted Maxence and Flore as practised scoundrels and feared Joseph lacked the force to struggle with them; he warned Joseph that with his romantic notions he would risk folly. Still, Hochon urged Joseph to go, to observe and to try to get a private moment with his uncle: if he succeeded, he must “pull the wool from his eyes” about the situation Jean-Jacques had let himself into and plead for his mother’s cause; if he failed, that failure would shed light on the strength of Maxence and Flore’s hold. Joseph, who admitted he lacked domestic statesmanship, was told to report everything he saw and heard that night—Hochon’s interest had been unexpectedly aroused by the prospect of a great fortune and by the path through which it might be secured.Thus the Parisians, newly arrived and already under the town’s curious microscope, prepared to meet Rouget. Around them, provincial manners—stingy dinners, ritualized hospitality, theatrical female influence—and secretive malice—Maxence’s rat conspiracy—set the scene for the coming contest over property, reputation and family ties. The Hochons positioned themselves as Agathe’s allies, shrewd and fearful of losing any advantage; Maxence and his Order schemed to harry the newcomers; Flore Brazier’s influence over Jean-Jacques loomed as a dangerous obstacle. Joseph, an unrecognized artist with a dramatic, unsettling face, would be both a subject of gossip and the crucial interlocutor in the effort to restore Agathe’s claims.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At four o’clock Joseph crossed the avenue of weak lindens from the Rouget house to the Hochon house. Kouski announced him. The table was set in the large hall; Joseph, who at once recognized his uncle, went up, kissed him, and bowed to Flore and Max.“We have not seen each other since I came into the world, my dear uncle,” the painter gayly announced.“You are very welcome, my friend,” said the old man, looking at his nephew in a dull way.“Madame,” Joseph said to Flore with an artist’s vivacity, “this morning I was envying my uncle the pleasure of admiring you every day.”“Isn’t she beautiful?” the old man murmured; his dim eyes brightened.“Beautiful enough to be the model of a great painter.”“Nephew,” said Rouget, nudged by Flore, “this is Monsieur Maxence Gilet; he served the Emperor, like your brother, in the Imperial Guard.”Joseph rose and bowed.“Your brother was in the dragoons, I believe,” Maxence said. “I was only a dust-trotter.”“On foot or on horseback,” said Flore, “you both risked your skins.”Joseph and Max inspected one another. Max, a dandy with an eager face and his father’s gray eyes, presented to the artist’s eye a show of handsome compensation in Flore and the promise of tedious company; he called it a triangular household.At that instant Baruch and François entered.“Have you seen the tower of Issoudun?” Flore asked Joseph. “No? Then, if you’d like a walk before dinner in an hour, we’ll show you the great curiosity of the town.”“Gladly,” said the artist, incapable of seeing any impropriety.While Flore went to put on her bonnet, gloves, and shawl, Joseph jumped up to look at the pictures.“Ah! you have pictures, indeed, uncle!” he cried at one that struck him.“They came to us from the Descoings, who bought them during the Revolution, when the convents and churches in Berry were dismantled,” the old man answered.Joseph was lost in admiration.“Magnificent! Oh, what painting! That fellow didn’t spare his canvas. Better and better, as at Nicolet’s—”“There are seven or eight very large ones up in the garret, kept on account of the frames,” said Gilet.“Let me see them!” cried the artist; Max took him upstairs.Joseph returned wildly enthusiastic. Max whispered to the Rabouilleuse, who drew the old man to a window embrasure while Joseph overheard her say, “Your nephew is a painter; you don’t care for those pictures; be kind, and give them to him.”“It seems,” said Jean-Jacques, leaning on Flore’s arm to reach Joseph by an Albano, “—it seems that you are a painter—”“Only a ‘rapin,’” said Joseph.“What may that be?” asked Flore.“A beginner,” replied Joseph.“Well,” continued Jean-Jacques, “if these pictures can be of any use to you in your business, I give them to you,—but without the frames. Oh! the frames are gilt, and besides, they are very funny; I will put—”“Well done, uncle!” cried Joseph, enchanted; “I’ll make you copies of the same dimensions, which you can put into the frames.”“But that will take your time, and you will want canvas and colors,” said Flore. “You will have to spend money. Come, Père Rouget, offer your nephew a hundred francs for each copy; here are twenty-seven pictures, and I think there are eleven very big ones in the garret which ought to cost double,—call the whole four thousand francs. Oh, yes,” she added, turning to Joseph, “your uncle can well afford to pay you four thousand francs for the copies, since he keeps the frames—but bless me! you’ll want frames; and they say frames cost more than pictures; there’s more gold on them. Answer, monsieur.” She shook the old man’s arm. “Hein? It isn’t dear; your nephew will take four thousand francs for new pictures in place of the old ones.”“Well, nephew, I will pay you four thousand francs for the copies—”“No, no!” said the honest Joseph; “four thousand francs and the pictures, that’s too much; the pictures, don’t you see, are valuable—”“Accept, simpleton!” said Flore; “he is your uncle, you know.”“Very good, I accept,” said Joseph, bewildered; for he had recognized a Perugino.The artist beamed as he went out with the Rabouilleuse on his arm, which suited Maxence’s plans. Neither Flore, nor Rouget, nor Max, nor anyone in Issoudun knew the true value of the pictures, and Max reckoned he had secured Flore’s triumph cheaply as she paraded the town, leaning on the master’s nephew. People flocked to their doors to see the crab-girl’s triumph. This sensation helped Max; when they returned at five, everyone talked of the cordial understanding between Max, Flore, and the nephew. The incident of the pictures and the four thousand francs circulated. The dinner, with Lousteau, one of the court judges, and the Mayor, was splendid and lasted five hours. By nine o’clock, at dessert, Joseph, seated opposite his uncle and between Flore and Max, had fraternized with the soldier and thought him a good fellow. Joseph returned home at eleven somewhat tipsy. Rouget was carried to bed dead-drunk; he had soaked up the wine like sand.“Well,” Max said to Flore when alone, “isn’t this better than making faces at them? The Bridaus are well received, given presents, smothered with attentions; they will sing our praises and leave us in peace. Tomorrow you and I and Kouski will take down the pictures and send them over to the painter; we’ll put the frames in the garret and cover the walls with one of those varnished papers representing scenes from Telemachus.”“Oh, that will be much prettier!” said Flore.Next morning Joseph did not wake till midday. From bed he saw the pictures, brought in while he slept, leaning against the opposite wall. While he examined them anew, recognizing each masterpiece and searching for signatures, his mother had gone to see and thank her brother, urged by old Hochon, who, having heard of the painter’s follies the night before, despaired of the Bridau cause.“Your adversaries have the cunning of foxes,” he said to Agathe. “In all my days I never saw a man carry things with such a high hand as that soldier; they say war educates young men! Joseph has let himself be fooled. They shut his mouth with wine, those miserable pictures, and four thousand francs! Your artist hasn’t cost Maxence much!”The long-headed old man instructed Madame Bridau about a line of conduct: enter into Maxence’s ideas, cajole Flore, set up intimacy, and thus obtain a few moments’ interview with Jean-Jacques alone. Madame Bridau was warmly received by her brother, to whom Flore had taught her lesson. The old man was ill from his excesses. Agathe, under the circumstances, could scarcely at once speak of family matters; Max thought it proper and magnanimous to leave brother and sister alone. The calculation worked: poor Agathe found her brother so ill she would not deprive him of Madame Brazier’s care.“Besides,” she said to the old bachelor, “I wish to know a person to whom I am grateful for the happiness of my brother.”These words pleased the old man, who rang for Madame Flore. Flore, not far off, showed servile attentions and tenderness to her master, fussing over him like a new bride, and the poor creature received it with feeling.“We owe you much gratitude, mademoiselle,” said Agathe, “for the attachment you have long shown my brother, and for the way you watch over his happiness.”“That is true, my dear Agathe,” said the old man; “she has taught me what happiness is; she is a woman of excellent qualities.”“And therefore, my dear brother, you ought to have recompensed Mademoiselle by making her your wife. Yes! I am too sincere in my religion not to wish to see you obey the precepts of the church. You would each be more tranquil in mind if you were not at variance with morality and the laws. I have come here, dear brother, to ask for help in my affliction; but do not suppose that we wish to make any remonstrance as to the manner in which you may dispose of your property—”“Madame,” said Flore, “we know how unjust your father was to you. Monsieur here can tell you,” she went on, looking at Jean-Jacques, “that the only quarrels we have ever had were about you. I have always told him that he owes you part of the fortune he received from his father, and your father, my benefactor,—for he was my benefactor.” She added tears: “I shall ever remember him! But your brother, madame, has listened to reason.”“Yes,” said the old man, “when I make my will you shall not be forgotten.”“Don’t talk of these things, my dear brother; you do not yet know my nature.”After such a beginning, it is easy to imagine how the visit went on. Rouget invited his sister to dinner on the next day but one.During these three days the Knights of Idleness captured an immense quantity of rats and mice, kept them half-famished, then let four hundred and thirty-six into a grain storage one night, including some breeding mothers. They also put a dozen pigeons into the old church roof. These animals revelled while the watchman, enticed away by drink, took no care of his master’s property.Madame Bridau believed, contrary to old Hochon, that her brother had as yet made no will; she intended to ask about Mademoiselle Brazier as soon as she could take a walk with him alone—a hope Flore and Maxence constantly dangled and disappointed.Meantime the Knights searched for a way to put the Parisians to flight, finding none practicable.At the end of a week—half the time the Parisians were to stay—the Bridaus had made no progress. “Your lawyer does not understand the provinces,” old Hochon told Madame Bridau. “What you have come to do can’t be done in two weeks, nor in two years; you ought never to leave your brother, but live here and try to give him some ideas of religion. You cannot countermine the fortifications of Flore and Maxence without getting a priest to sap them. That is my advice, and it is high time to set about it.”“You certainly have very singular ideas about the clergy,” said Madame Hochon to her husband.“Bah!” the old man exclaimed, “that’s just like you pious women.”“God would never bless an enterprise undertaken in a sacrilegious spirit,” said Madame Bridau. “Use religion for such a purpose! Why, we should be more criminal than Flore.”This conversation took place at breakfast with François and Baruch listening.“Sacrilege!” exclaimed old Hochon. “If some good abbé, keen as I have known many, knew what a dilemma you are in, he would not think it sacrilege to bring your brother’s lost soul back to God, force him to send away the woman who causes the scandal (with a proper provision, of course), and show him how to set his conscience at rest by giving a few thousand francs a year to the seminary and leaving his property to the rightful heirs.”The passive obedience the old miser exacted from his children and grandchildren prevented Baruch and François from showing surprise, but they exchanged glances about how dangerous such a scheme would be to Max’s interest.“The fact is, madame,” said Baruch, “that if you want to secure your brother’s property, the only sure way will be to stay in Issoudun for the necessary length of time—”“Mother,” said Joseph hastily, “you had better write to Desroches about all this. As for me, I ask nothing more than what my uncle has already given me.”After recognizing the great value of his thirty-nine pictures, Joseph had carefully unnailed the canvases, covered them with paper, gummed the edges, and laid them one above another in an enormous wooden box, which he sent to Desroches by the carrier’s wagon, proposing to write him a letter by post. The precious freight had been sent off the night before.“You are satisfied with a pretty poor bargain,” said Monsieur Hochon.“I can easily get a hundred and fifty thousand francs for those pictures,” replied Joseph.“Painter’s nonsense!” exclaimed old Hochon.“Mother,” said Joseph, “I am going to write to Desroches and explain the state of things here. If he advises you to remain, you had better do so. As for your situation, we can always find another like it.”“My dear Joseph,” said Madame Hochon, following him from the table, “I don’t know anything about your uncle’s pictures, but they ought to be good, judging by the places from which they came. If they are worth only forty thousand francs—a thousand apiece—tell no one. Though my grandsons are discreet, they might, without intending harm, speak of this windfall; it would be known all over Issoudun; and it is very important that our adversaries should not suspect it. You behave like a child!”In fact, before evening many persons in Issoudun, including Max, had heard this estimate, which caused a search for old paintings and the appearance of many execrable daubs. Max repented having driven Rouget into giving away the pictures, and his rage against the heirs, after hearing old Hochon’s ecclesiastical scheme, increased. The influence of religion on such a feeble creature as Rouget was the one fear. The news decided Maxence to turn Rouget’s investments into money and to borrow on his landed property to buy into the Funds as soon as possible; but he considered it even more important to get rid of the Parisians at once.Flore, by Max’s advice, pretended Monsieur was too feeble to take walks and ought to have a carriage. This pretext avoided inquiry when they went to Bourges, Vierzon, Châteauroux, Vatan, and other places where withdrawing investments obliged Max and Flore to be with Rouget. At the close of the week, all Issoudun was amazed that the old man had gone to Bourges to buy a carriage—a step the Knights regarded as favorable to the Rabouilleuse. Flore and Max selected a hideous “berlingot,” twenty-two years old, a sort of demi-fortune carriage. It only cost four hundred and fifty francs; Max bought a stout mare and repainted the carriage dark brown, buying a tolerable harness at a bargain. The town awaited Pere Rouget’s equipage; at its first appearance every household was on its doorstep.The second time the old bachelor went out he drove to Bourges, went before a notary, and signed a power of attorney in favor of Maxence Gilet to make all the transfers enumerated. Flore reserved to herself the business of making Monsieur sell investments in Issoudun and its neighborhood. The principal notary in Bourges was requested by Rouget to get him a loan of one hundred and forty thousand francs on his landed estate. Nothing was known at Issoudun of these secret proceedings. Maxence, a good rider, went with his horse to Bourges and back between five in the morning and five in the afternoon. Flore never left the old bachelor. Rouget consented without objection to the actions Flore dictated; but he insisted that the investment in the Funds producing fifty thousand francs a year should stand in Flore’s name as holding a life-interest only, and in his as owner of the principal. His tenacity in this domestic dispute worried Max; he thought it the result of reflections inspired by seeing the natural heirs.Amid these movements, which Max concealed, he forgot the Spaniard and his granary. Fario returned to Issoudun to deliver his corn after various trips to raise the price of cereals. The morning after his arrival he noticed the church roof black with pigeons. He hurried to the storehouse, where he found half his grain devoured. Thousands of mice and rat marks showed further ruin. The church was a Noah’s ark. But anger turned the Spaniard white when he found the grain at the bottom sprouting from water introduced by tin tubes into the pile. The pigeons and rats could be instinct, but the wetting showed human hand.Fario sat on the chapel steps with his head between his hands. After half an hour he spied the squirrel Goddet had sent him playing on a cross-beam. He rose, turned to his watchman with a calm face, made no complaint, but hired laborers to gather sound grain and spread the moist to dry, saving as much as possible; after estimating his losses at about three fifths, he attended to filling his orders. But his previous manipulations had raised the price of cereals, and he lost on the three fifths he had to buy to fill orders; his losses were really over half. The Spaniard, with no enemies, at once attributed this revenge to Gilet. He was convinced Maxence and others were the authors of the nocturnal mischief and intended to ruin him. It was a matter of over three thousand francs—nearly his whole capital since the peace. Driven by the desire for vengeance, the man displayed the persistence of a detective. Hiding at night in different parts of Issoudun, he soon acquired proof of the Knights’ proceedings: he saw them, counted them, watched their rendezvous, and knew of their suppers at Mère Cognette’s; he lay in wait, witnessed their deeds, and thus became well informed of their nocturnal habits.In spite of Max’s journeys and preoccupations, he did not neglect his nightly employments—first, because he did not wish his comrades to suspect his operations with Père Rouget’s property; second, to keep the Knights in hand. They were convened for a prank to be talked of for years: poisoned meat was to be thrown to every watchdog in town and environs. Fario overheard them congratulating each other after a supper on the probable success of this massacre and laughing over the general mourning it would cause.“It will make people forget Fario’s cart,” said Goddet.Fario needed no further proof; his mind was made up.After three weeks in Issoudun, Agathe and Madame Hochon were convinced of the old miser’s observation that it would take years to destroy Max and Flore’s influence. She had made no progress in Jean-Jacques’s confidence and was never left alone with him.
