On the other hand, Mademoiselle Brazier triumphed openly by taking Agathe to drive in the caleche, sitting beside her on the back seat while Monsieur Rouget and his nephew occupied the front. Mother and son impatiently awaited an answer to the confidential letter they had written to Desroches. The day before the night on which the dogs were to be poisoned, Joseph, nearly bored to death in Issoudun, received two letters: one from the great painter Schinner and the second from Desroches.Schinner’s letter, from Beaumont-sur-Oise, said Joseph was recommended for the chateau de Presles for the Comte de Serizy; Schinner was off to Italy; Joseph should take his place at the Salon if need be. Desroches’s letter gave practical advice:My dear Joseph,—Your Monsieur Hochon strikes me as an old man full of common-sense, and you give me a high idea of his methods; he is perfectly right. My advice, since you ask it, is that your mother should remain at Issoudun with Madame Hochon, paying a small board—say four hundred francs a year—to reimburse her hosts. Madame Bridau ought, in my opinion, to follow Monsieur Hochon’s advice in everything; for your excellent mother will have many scruples in dealing with persons who have no scruple at all, and whose behavior to her is a masterstroke of policy. That Maxence, you are right enough, is dangerous. He is another Philippe, but of a different calibre. The scoundrel makes his vices serve his fortunes, and gets his amusement gratis; whereas your brother’s follies are never useful to him. All that you say alarms me, but I could do no good by going to Issoudun. Monsieur Hochon, acting behind your mother, will be more useful to you than I. As for you, you had better come back here; you are good for nothing in a matter which requires continual attention, careful observation, servile civilities, discretion in speech, and a dissimulation of manner and gesture wholly against the grain of artists.If they have told you no will has been made, you may be sure they have one. But wills can be revoked, and as long as your uncle lives he is susceptible of being worked upon by remorse and religion. Your inheritance will be the result of a combat between the Church and the Rabouilleuse. There will come a time when that woman will lose her grip and religion will be all-powerful. So long as your uncle makes no gift of the property during his lifetime, and does not change his estate, all may come right when religion gets the upper hand. For this reason you must beg Monsieur Hochon to keep an eye on the condition of your uncle’s property. It is necessary to know if the real estate is mortgaged, and if so, where and in whose name the proceeds are invested. It is so easy to terrify an old man with fears about his life, in case you find him despoiling his own property, that almost any heir with a little adroitness could stop the spoliation at its outset. But how should your mother, with ignorance of the world and religious ideas, manage such an affair? I cannot throw more light. All you have done probably gave the alarm, and your adversaries may already have secured themselves—“That is what I call an opinion in good shape,” exclaimed Monsieur Hochon, proud of being appreciated by a Parisian lawyer.“Oh! Desroches is a famous fellow,” answered Joseph.“It would be well to read that letter to the two women,” said the old man.“There it is,” said Joseph, giving it to him; “as to me, I want to be off to-morrow; and I am now going to say good-by to my uncle.”“Ah!” said Monsieur Hochon, “I see that Monsieur Desroches tells you in a postscript to burn the letter.”“You can burn it after showing it to my mother,” said the painter.Joseph dressed, crossed the little square, and called on his uncle, who was finishing breakfast. Max and Flore were at table.“Don’t disturb yourself, my dear uncle; I have only come to say good-by.”“You are going?” said Max, exchanging glances with Flore.“Yes; I have work at the chateau of Monsieur de Serizy, and I am all the more glad because his arm is long enough to do a service to my poor brother in the Chamber of Peers.”“Well, well, go and work,” said old Rouget, with a silly air. Joseph thought him extraordinarily changed within a few days. “Men must work—I am sorry you are going.”“Oh! my mother will be here some time longer,” remarked Joseph.Max made a movement with his lips which Flore observed, meaning: “They are going to try the plan Baruch warned me of.”“I am very glad I came,” said Joseph, “for I have had the pleasure of making your acquaintance and you have enriched my studio—”“Yes,” said Flore, “instead of enlightening your uncle on the value of his pictures, which is now estimated at over one hundred thousand francs, you have packed them off in a hurry to Paris. Poor dear man! he is no better than a baby! We have just been told of a little treasure at Bourges,—what did they call it? a Poussin,—which was in the choir of the cathedral before the Revolution and is now worth, all by itself, thirty thousand francs.”“That was not right of you, my nephew,” said Jean-Jacques, at a sign from Max, which Joseph could not see.“Come now, frankly,” said the soldier, laughing, “on your honor, what should you say those pictures were worth? You’ve made an easy haul out of your uncle! and right enough, too—uncles are made to be pillaged. Nature deprived me of uncles, but damn it, if I’d had any I should have shown them no mercy.”“Did you know, monsieur,” said Flore to Rouget, “what your pictures were worth? How much did you say, Monsieur Joseph?”“Well,” answered the painter, red as a beetroot,—“the pictures are certainly worth something.”“They say you estimated them to Monsieur Hochon at one hundred and fifty thousand francs,” said Flore; “is that true?”“Yes,” said the painter, with childlike honesty.“And did you intend,” said Flore to the old man, “to give a hundred and fifty thousand francs to your nephew?”“Never, never!” cried Jean-Jacques, on whom Flore had fixed her eye.“There is one way to settle all this,” said the painter, “and that is to return them to you, uncle.”“No, no, keep them,” said the old man.“I shall send them back to you,” said Joseph, wounded by Max and Flore’s offensive silence. “There is something in my brushes which will make my fortune, without owing anything to any one, even an uncle. My respects to you, mademoiselle; good-day, monsieur—”Joseph crossed the square in a state of irritation artists can imagine. The entire Hochon family were in the salon. Seeing Joseph gesticulate and talking to himself, they asked him what was the matter. The painter, as open as the day, related before Baruch and François the scene; two hours later it was the town’s talk, embroidered with various circumstances. Some persons insisted the painter was maltreated by Max; others that he had misbehaved to Flore and been turned out.“What a child your son is!” said Hochon to Madame Bridau; “the booby is the dupe of a scene they have been keeping for the last day of his visit. Max and the Rabouilleuse have known the value of those pictures for the last two weeks—ever since he foolishly told it before my grandsons, who blurted it out to all the world. Your artist had better have taken himself off without taking leave.”“My son has done right to return the pictures if they are really so valuable,” said Agathe.“If they are worth, as he says, two hundred thousand francs,” said old Hochon, “it was folly to put himself in a way to be obliged to return them. You might have had that, at least, out of the property; whereas, as things are going now, you won’t get anything. And this scene with Joseph is almost a reason why your brother should refuse to see you again.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between midnight and the small hours the local idlers made their rounds feeding the town’s dogs; their errand over, they supped and slipped home before dawn. As Max turned into the Grande rue, Fario, lurking in a recess, stabbed him, wiped the blade on his handkerchief, and fled toward Vilatte. The Spaniard washed the handkerchief in the Riviere forcée and returned to his lodgings at Saint-Paterne; later a watchman found him asleep.Max cried out as he fell. Lousteau-Prangin, the judge’s son and a relation of the sub-delegate, and young Goddet ran up, shouting that Max was being killed; the town, used to false alarms, mostly dozed on. They fetched Monsieur Goddet, the surgeon, and carried Max home, while the Rabouilleuse, Jean-Jacques Rouget, Kouski, and the Vedie clustered about in alarm. Max, reviving and finding the wound not mortal, seized the moment to inflame suspicion: he whispered that he believed he had recognized “that cursed painter.”The servant at Monsieur Hochon’s, seen sweeping the steps, reported Joseph Bridau had risen at half-past four and had walked his room all night. This confirmed what artists guessed: Joseph had been restless with worry over the scandal at Issoudun and the charge that he had cheated his uncle. He had risen at dawn and gone to the poplar avenue to steady himself. Seeing him pacing, neighbors made him the obvious suspect.Monsieur Goddet probed Max’s wound and, like many country surgeons who relish the gravity of their work, declared the blow serious and left a dramatic verdict—“he might die”—to excite the town. By dawn nearly two hundred people crowded around Place Saint-Jean and the two Narettes. Max, playing his part, whispered to Flore that he would not be long in bed and urged them to exploit the assault: pretend he might die; accuse Joseph Bridau to have him jailed briefly so his mother would hurry back to Paris and the Parisians would quit the town—thus ridding Issoudun of the hated city folk and any priestly meddling they feared.Flore descended in tears and, sobbing, accused “the painter” — she pointed to the very face that townsfolk distrusted — of having been angry with Max over pictures he had “wormed out” of Pere Rouget. The Rabouilleuse and the Knights of Idleness took the cue: they interpreted the assault as proof the Parisian painter had schemed to secure his uncle’s fortune by removing Max. Rumors spread that the accused had left that morning, and fury mounted.A Knight of Idleness ran to Monsieur Mouilleron, the local commissary, and the crowd swelled, raw with provincial prejudice against Parisians. Lousteau-Prangin, Monsieur Mouilleron, the lieutenant of gendarmes and two men pushed through the throng, though the shouting already prejudiced them against Joseph. After conferring with Max, Mouilleron sent officers to inspect the scene. The Hochon house was promptly guarded by gendarmes. Gritte, the Hochon servant, rushed to wake her master with the cry that the town was in revolt and that Joseph was accused of murdering Max.Hochon, alarmed for his gold, bolted his house. Madame Hochon, pale and weeping, accepted the rumor of Joseph’s guilt only to worry for Agathe’s fragile nerves; Agathe, roused and half-dressed, fainted at the sight of the magistrates and gendarmes arriving. Lousteau-Prangin noted the crime was committed near Hochon’s, and, when pressed, Gritte confirmed she had seen Joseph leave that morning. Lousteau ordered warrants issued; the magistrates fretted that the exasperated crowd—said to be reinforced by twelve hundred more from the faubourg de Rome—might take the law into their own hands.While officials debated, a clamor from another quarter announced Joseph’s capture. He had been seen at the place Misere and, returning quietly past the mill at Landrole to breakfast, had been seized by townspeople ready to execute justice. Two gendarmes snatched him from the hands of those who had already pinioned him and threatened him with death. The men escorting him declared they meant to protect him; whether guilty or innocent, they argued, the crowd adored Max Gilet and would not be restrained.Joseph, pale but composed, was dragged amid jeers, insults and stone-throwing from Place Misere to Place Saint-Jean. The gendarmes formed a ring, one was wounded, and Joseph bore blows on his legs and shoulders. The lieutenant told the magistrates the only way to prevent a massacre was to take Joseph to the Palais under armed escort; a show of legal custody might disperse the mob’s thirst for summary vengeance. Mouilleron agreed, and Hochon, trembling for his household, consented. Joseph himself, calm and dignified, agreed to accompany them if they would allow him to write to his mother.At the Palais, in Lousteau-Prangin’s private office, Joseph gave a composed account of his movements: he had been out past five, stood at half-past five before Saint-Cyr’s facade, talked with the sexton, passed the vegetable market where women had assembled, gone to the mill of Landrole and watched the ducks for several minutes — miller’s men would have seen him — and then walked along the avenue to Tivoli where he talked with the gardener. He asked the magistrates to verify these facts, requested not to be imprisoned and offered to remain in their office until cleared. His clear, sensible statements impressed them; Mouilleron promised to summon witnesses but warned an arrest might be inevitable to pacify the crowd. Joseph requested to write to his mother and did so; the letter calmed Madame Bridau, who had been in a nervous collapse. When Monsieur Goddet later said Max’s wound was not mortal, people began to breathe easier.Earlier that night someone had slipped Max a typed note advising him that the assailant promised not to strike again if Max had Joseph Bridau released. Max had burned the note but sent a message later to Mouilleron asserting he had recognized the attacker and that a small pocketbook had deflected the blade, making the wound less fatal; Goddet added the attacker wore list shoes whereas Joseph left in boots. When Max wrote to Mouilleron reporting the list-shoe detail and asked for Joseph’s release, Lousteau-Prangin had already verified Joseph’s alibi by questioning the bell-ringer, market-women, washerwomen and the miller’s men. Max’s note clinched matters: Joseph was innocent. Mouilleron himself escorted him back to the Hochons’. His mother embraced him with a tenderness she had not shown before; the wronged son found in misfortune a human warmth.Mouilleron, self-satisfied, declared he had read Joseph’s innocence in his face, but that the only way to protect him from the mob had been the ostensible arrest. Everyone urged Joseph to leave the town: the procureur du roi counseled taking the postmaster’s carriage that night and returning to Paris by the diligence from Bourges; Hochon, burning to see them go, agreed. Agathe, distraught and affectionate, kissed Madame Hochon’s hand and pleaded to be gone. The old woman, pious and melodramatic, answered that perhaps they would never meet again below — she had suffered enough.Monsieur Rouget arrived with a farewell visit; he offered his caleche for the drive to Bourges and accepted responsibility for the pictures only half convinced of their value. Baruch, at his grandfather’s hint, saw the postmaster. At eleven that night the two Parisians, Joseph and Agathe, left Issoudun in a wicker cabriolet drawn by one horse and postilion. Adolphine and Madame Hochon wept as they parted; they alone of Hochon’s household regretted the Bridaus’ departure.Back at Max’s bedroom Francois Hochon and the Rabouilleuse checked Max, who, tired and feverish, confessed the plan had succeeded. He had told Mouilleron that his assassin might try to strike again and had begged the magistrates to conduct an ostensible active search but in truth to let the attacker go, unless they preferred to stand accused of his death. Flore rejoiced that at least for a while they were rid of the Parisians. The next day Issoudun celebrated the Parisians’ departure as a provincial victory over the City; townspeople mocked the Bridaus as schemers who came to fleece the countryside and were outwitted by their betters. A few voices, the Hochons’ and the sober inhabitants’, disapproved of the triumphalism, but the prevailing mood was triumphal and xenophobic: “That’s Paris for you!” they cried. The Rabouilleuse, the Rabouilleuse’s friends and Max’s partisans were exultant.Joseph and Agathe, on the road, found some consolation: Joseph recovered spirits lost by the insult and imprisonment; his mother, however, remained in low spirits, anxious about the Court of Peers’ trial of Philippe and the military conspirators, and the cloud of suspicion surrounding her son. To get away from the trial’s news and protect his nerves, Joseph, after briefing Desroches fully on Issoudun, took a commission with the Comte de Serizy to decorate the chateau at Presles.Philippe’s own fate was briefly noted by contemporary history: whether his conduct in the trial was treacherous or merely part of political maneuvering, the Court of Peers sentenced him to five years’ police surveillance and ordered him to leave Paris that day for Autun. The punishment was essentially a parole, a town as prison. Hearing that the Comte de Serizy, one of the peers on the court-martial, had engaged Joseph for frescoes, Desroches sought out the count and persuaded him of the brothers’ need. He urged the minister of war to have the director-general of police move Philippe from Autun to Issoudun and to grant the former lieutenant-colonel a small monthly allowance of sixty francs. The count promised to obtain what Desroches asked.Three days later Desroches, armed with this authority, fetched Philippe from the Court of Peers’ prison to his house in the rue de Bethizy and delivered a stinging, practical lecture. He rebuked Philippe for careless dissipation, for nearly wrecking his mother by his folly, and for the consequences of his past conduct. Then, bluntly, the lawyer explained the case in terms a client could understand: the brothers’ financial ruin could be repaired only by prudence; revenge was irrelevant. Desroches insisted Philippe could recover his uncle’s inheritance if he behaved with policy and prudence. He had already, without telling Joseph, sent the disputed pictures to Monsieur Hochon with instructions that they should be surrendered only to Philippe. Desroches praised Maxence Gilet’s courage, reminding Philippe that he would need a brave and constant rival in the local struggle.Philippe, hardened and shrunken by imprisonment, brightened at the news that his residence would be Issoudun rather than Autun. He was told he would receive a quarterly stipend to clothe him decently, and Desroches promised to put him on the diligence for the evening. Philippe, who owned little beyond the threadbare blue overcoat on his back, asked for his sabre, sword and pistols; Desroches, aghast at the man’s appearance, said more than weapons would be needed. Godeschal, Desroches’s head-clerk and the brother of Mariette, appeared; Mariette — the actress Philippe cared for — was preparing for the opening of a new theatre. They dined frugally; then the two lawyers put the political convict on the diligence and wished him luck. Thus Philippe, thinned by disgrace yet scheming, was sent toward Issoudun to pursue, with instruction and a modicum of means, the recovery of an inheritance complicated by Max’s machinations and the local animosities fanned by the wound in the night.The episode closed with the town’s triumphal exultation at having chased away the Parisians, the temporary victory of provincial prejudice, and the movement of the central characters: Joseph and Agathe removed and soothed for the moment, Max convalescent and triumphant that his stratagem had paled the city folk, and Philippe dispatched back toward the scene with a new plan and the legal backing of Desroches.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On November second, All-Souls’ day, Philippe Bridau registered at the Issoudun police and, on the official’s advice, took rooms in rue l’Avenier. Word spread that an officer, banished after a recent military conspiracy, had arrived — more excitement because he was the brother of the painter who had been wrongly accused. Maxence Gilet, recovered from his wound, had turned all Père Rouget’s mortgages into cash and placed the proceeds in the “grand-livre.” The loan of 140,000 francs on Rouget’s real estate caused scandal; in the provinces such news leaks swiftly. Monsieur Hochon, anxious for the Bridaus and still worried by the earlier terror over his house, asked old Monsieur Heron, the Bourges notary, about the matter.Heron explained that, but for him, Rouget would have kept the 50,000-franc income in Maxence’s name; he had advised Mademoiselle Brazier to rely on the will and had urged Maxence and his mistress to let the sudden change pass unnoticed. Hochon, uneasy, pressed for protection of the Bridaus. Maxence and Flore, feeling secure, welcomed Philippe’s arrival; they believed they could get Rouget to sign a power of attorney transferring funds to Max or Flore if danger threatened. Even if the will naming Flore principal were revoked, the 50,000-franc income and the newly obtained 140,000-franc mortgage would be consolation.The day after arriving, Philippe called on his uncle about ten, dressed shabbily. Flore recoiled at his repulsive appearance; Gilet felt the chill of a hidden enemy. Philippe’s ruined blue greatcoat was buttoned to the throat; his trousers and boots were ragged, his hat greasy, his malacca cane dull. His face had the exhausted look of a gambler — sunken cheeks, red-rimmed lids, a menacing brow, a fringe of hair, and cold steel-blue eyes. He introduced himself hoarsely as Philippe Bridau, a lieutenant-colonel ill-treated by the Bourbons, complaining that by police order he must live on a pay of sixty francs a month, and he made a point of disdain for relying on his uncle.He offered his hand and declared he would sooner cut off his hand than beg from his uncle after the treatment of his mother and brother in Issoudun. Flore invited him to breakfast; he declined, asserting he had eaten. He hinted at safeguarding the Bridaus’ honor, spoke curtly about pictures at Monsieur Hochon’s for identification, and left, slamming the door so violently that it shocked Flore and Maxence more than his appearance had.They watched him cross to the Hochons’. Though furious, Gilet kept his temper; he knew political prudence was needed. At the Hochons’, curiosity and unease were intense — Madame Hochon shuddered at the man she associated with several misfortunes. Philippe, however, was polite and composed. He explained he had come to introduce himself and to manage living on sixty francs, admitted past faults, promised reformation, and praised Lousteau the journalist, which gained Madame Hochon’s favor. She advised him gently; he said prison had taught him much and he intended another life.Privately to Monsieur Hochon, Philippe warned that town gossip could ruin them and asked that advice be given only discreetly. Desroches had told Philippe to use Hochon’s counsel quietly; they agreed to “meet accidentally” in open places when needed. Philippe asked for names of veterans in Issoudun not favorable to Maxence. Hochon named Mignonnet, an artillery captain of the Guard, and Captain Carpentier, a cavalry officer who had replaced Gilet as mayoral clerk and befriended Mignonnet. Hochon suggested a job at a mutual insurance agency paying about fifty francs a month.Within a week Philippe had bought on credit a decent suit, boots, gloves, and a hat. Giroudeau sent linen, weapons, and a letter to Carpentier, who welcomed Philippe and introduced him to Mignonnet, recommending him as a man of merit. Philippe narrated parts of the conspiracy to them in a way that won admiration: he portrayed the recent plot as the continuation of older army efforts against the Bourbons — a northern operation with a Paris diversion — betrayed and reduced to a smaller barrack affair. His ruined costume, he implied, had helped minimize the conspiracy’s apparent size to the authorities. He presented himself as a man who had played both sides, preserving favor with influential figures while waiting to join whichever party served his interests.Carpentier and Mignonnet, impressed, brought him into their circle and recommended him for the insurance office Hochon had mentioned; the job required only three hours a day. Philippe frequented the officers’ club; his manners and their praise earned him respect. Determined to appear sober and respectable and to lull Gilet’s suspicions, he affected simplicity while quietly observing his uncle’s property and plotting. Hochon had given him details of Rouget’s wealth, which inflamed his covetousness. He and Hochon agreed not to alarm Maxence: if Rouget were spirited off to Bourges, the game would be lost.Philippe dined regularly with the officers and Hochon and soon had engagements most nights. He never spoke of his uncle, brother, or mother except in passing. Among Maxence’s friends he seemed a “sabreur,” an ostentatious soldier of the old army; some thought him either a dupe or a spy. Philippe fenced with Carpentier and practiced pistols with them — ostensibly sport, really to persuade Maxence that he preferred pistols for a duel. When he met Gilet he returned a bow with an officer’s condescension; Gilet showed no impatience and never spoke of Bridau at the Cognettes’.Philippe’s contempt and Gilet’s restrained bearing became the talk of Issoudun. Philippe investigated the circumstances of his brother’s arrest and of Gilet and the Rabouilleuse’s past, and he came into contact with Fario the Spaniard. Finding Fario reliable, Philippe made him a confidant and promised indemnity for past losses; Fario, a willing henchman, put his knowledge of the Knights of Idleness and his services at Philippe’s disposal. The town, abuzz, expected a deadly clash between the two men who intensely despised one another.Toward the end of November Philippe told Hochon he had discovered Hochon’s grandsons, Baruch and François, were intimate with Maxence and had spied in his house, reporting what was said there. He claimed to have overheard them and learned from Fario of their nocturnal visits to drinking-shops with Maxence; they owed Max over three thousand francs. Philippe suspected that Maxence and the Rabouilleuse intended to secure the 50,000-franc income and then run away to marry. They must learn what was happening under Rouget’s roof, Philippe insisted; he did not yet know how to proceed. Hochon promised to consider it.Meanwhile Jean-Jacques Rouget suffered as never before since Philippe’s first visit. Flore, fearing harm to Maxence, planned to leave Issoudun and marry Max in Paris after getting the funds transferred. Rouget refused to transfer, insisting Flore be sole heir — he feared she would abandon him if made rich. She tried cajolery then coldness; Rouget was found weeping and, for a week, breakfasted alone.The day after Philippe’s talk with Hochon, he returned to Rouget’s. Flore feigned tenderness; Gilet stayed away. Philippe, sensing opportunity, forced Flore to look him in the face and asked whether Rouget was free to walk with him. Flore stammered that he was; Philippe insisted Rouget accompany him on foot, implying refusal would confirm that Rouget was under Flore’s control. Seeing Rouget hesitate, Philippe pretended to go; he caught Flore making a menacing gesture at her uncle and told Rouget to meet him at the door in ten minutes or he would take other measures. He left for the Hochons’.At nine that night Hochon, Madame Hochon, Heron the notary, and the grandsons assembled. Hochon denounced Baruch and François for their nocturnal life with Maxence and for betraying secrets for money, even facilitating past crimes. He read the guardianship accounts: the Borniche children’s net fortune was 70,000 francs (part of their mother’s dowry), and Baruch’s portion was twenty thousand. Hochon declared he would release their property but no longer tolerate the grandsons’ conduct; he discharged his guardianship and conditioned their futures on strict behavior. Baruch was to be sent to Paris to study banking, François to Poitiers to study law with severely limited means. Having settled the legal business and obtained receipts, Heron prepared to leave when Philippe arrived.Philippe and Hochon spoke briefly: Heron had shown that the security for the 50,000-franc income could not be sold without the owner’s signature or a power of attorney; Rouget had given no such power since his arrival. If Rouget tried to give one in Issoudun they would know immediately; if he left town to sign, registration would reveal it. Therefore, if Rouget left, they would follow him and learn his movements. Philippe reported seeing his uncle cross the street and said Fario and a soldier named Benjamin Bourdet were watching; they would follow Rouget secretly. “If they let him walk with you,” Hochon mused, “Maxence must believe he has found some means to win the game.” Philippe said Maxence was afraid of him but could not evade him; Fario had already stirred public censure against Maxence and set gossip in motion.When Philippe left, Flore hurried to Maxence with a full account of his boldness. Max resolved that before resorting to fighting him they would play a daring card. He told Flore to send at once for Père Cognette’s horse and a char-à-banc; pack their things and go to Vatan pretending to stay, taking Vedie and the twenty thousand francs in Rouget’s drawer. If Philippe brought Rouget to Vatan, Flore must refuse to return unless Rouget signed a power of attorney. As soon as they obtained it, Max planned to slip off to Paris while she came back to Issoudun; when Jean-Jacques found her gone, he would be desperate and likely follow, and Max intended to use that moment to secure what he wanted.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While that plot unfolded, Philippe walked arm in arm with his uncle along the boulevard. Adopting a Parisian air, he taunted Rouget with the truth everyone suspected: Flore adored Max and treated Rouget like a servant; she wanted Max and aimed to remove Rouget from the way. The old man answered that he loved her still. Philippe replied that he could make “your Rabouilleuse supple as my glove,” and boasted he would kill Max like a dog—on one condition. He urged Rouget not to sign the power of attorney they sought before the third of December; delay it. They only needed it to sell the fifty thousand francs a year Rouget received in the Funds to pay for a wedding out of his millions.Rouget, bewildered and fearful of Flore’s influence, was told to promise to sign but not actually to do so, and to inform Philippe the night before he meant to sign. Philippe’s plan was stark: Max would not be Rouget’s proxy unless he killed Philippe first; if Philippe killed Max, Rouget was to accept Philippe in Max’s place and Philippe pledged to make Flore love him—or, failing that, to thrash her. Rouget protested he could never allow violence against Flore; Philippe, scolding him for weakness, insisted it was the only way to govern women and horses.Mignonnet and Carpentier joined them. Philippe reduced the rescue of Rouget to one outcome: either Bridau must kill Gilet or Gilet must kill Bridau. He asked them to arrange the coronation banquet seating so he would sit opposite Gilet and to act as seconds in the duel. They agreed. Philippe assured Rouget that postponing the signature until after December third would secure the uncle’s fortune and Flore’s affection.They walked nearly two hours; Philippe repeated his advice and mocked women as inferior. At half-past two Rouget reached home to Kouski’s tears—on Max’s orders—announcing, dramatically, that “Madame has gone away, and taken Vedie with her!” Rouget searched the house, fell into a chair and wept; he knew nothing of Max’s whereabouts. Gilet, aware it was politic to be seen, left Rouget alone to suffer. Flore’s departure made the old man frantic.Maxence Gilet found himself shunned by many who had once greeted him; the Knights of Idleness scandal and Bridau’s arrest had disgraced him. Captain Potel berated him for bringing shame on the Guard and urged him to appear at the coronation banquet; Max resolved to be there if possible. Determined to secure Rouget’s money before any duel, he muttered that he would have the funds before he fought; if he died, they should not go to Philippe. He planned to put property in Baruch’s name by a power of attorney and transfer it by his order. Calm outwardly, Max displayed the cool resolve of a general diverted to petty local ambition.He entered Rouget’s salon, feigned surprise at Flore’s flight, and questioned Kouski. The Pole reported that Flore had taken twenty thousand francs in gold from Rouget’s drawer and declared she would not return, weeping that Rouget had no affection for her and his nephew treated her like the lowest of the low. Rouget begged Max to find Flore; Max pretended reluctance to oppose Bridau but agreed to make inquiries and have dinner ready. Kouski announced that Flore had driven toward Vatan—“Madame is going back to her own people,” he said. Max suggested they ride to Vatan that night: he would go on ahead if Rouget saddled his horse.Monsieur Hochon had already told Philippe of Flore’s departure. Philippe guessed Max’s aim: if Rouget and Max drove to Vatan, Max would promise to reconcile them and likely secure the power of attorney. Philippe sent Fario and Benjamin to keep watch and hurried to Mignonnet’s to recruit Carpentier. A lad then brought Baruch a letter from Max asking his friend to receive a power of attorney from Rouget at Vatan the next morning and promising to send Rouget to Paris. Baruch, with most to lose, counseled surrender and amendment to save their prospects. Hochon rejoiced at the apparent salvation of Rouget’s property and urged Baruch to go to Vatan and accept the power, then await instructions.Soon Francois spied wheels—Rouget and Bridau returning together to the Grande-Narette, Benjamin and Carpentier following on horseback. Hochon went to Rouget and found the old man writing, at Philippe’s dictation, a letter to Flore threatening to revoke her inheritance if she did not return at once. Philippe had intercepted Rouget before he fell into Max’s trap. “I have made my uncle see he was running into a noose,” Philippe told Hochon. He believed the letter would bring Flore back that very night.Carpentier and Rouget read Maxence’s letter and jeered at his infamy. Philippe insisted on keeping Flore’s interests in hand; Rouget feared she loved Maxence too much to stay. Philippe planned for Carpentier to ride to Vatan and hand Rouget’s letter to Flore, hoping it would recall her. Carpentier drove off in the old calèche with Benjamin following, while the Pole—who had ridden ahead to warn Max and Flore—was to switch places with Benjamin later. When Philippe learned Kouski had fled to rejoin Max at the hotel de la Poste, he named Benjamin to replace the Pole and told Hochon the banquet would be jovial; he would sleep in the room next to Gilet’s if Rouget consented. Hochon agreed to stay and help keep Rouget from signing anything rash.At half-past eleven wheels returned to the Grande-Narette. Rouget, from agony to joy, saw two women emerge from the carriage. Philippe brusquely informed Flore she was no longer Rouget’s servant and must leave that night; Benjamin would replace Kouski. Seizing her hand, he led Flore to the place Saint-Jean and, with threats and assurances, told her bluntly that in three days Maxence might be dead or Rouget off guard. If Bridau died, Flore would be mistress of Rouget’s fortune; if Bridau lived, she would have to treat Rouget well. Philippe warned there were ways to get rid of a woman as well as a man, and reminded her he had younger Parisian alternatives waiting.Flore trembled at the thought of Max’s death and asked about killing him. Philippe cut her off and sent her back to Rouget. The old man took Flore home and shut the door; Kouski departed to rejoin Max, who had taken up at the hotel de la Poste.Next day Issoudun buzzed with speculation about what would happen at the banquet between Max and Bridau. Philippe made Vedie a brief proposal—an annuity or dismissal—which kept her neutral. Flore, fearing for Max’s life, adopted an affectionate manner toward Rouget; self-interested devotion sometimes passes for love, and it seemed to work: she came down to breakfast on Rouget’s arm, tears in her eyes when she saw Philippe sitting where Max had sat. Philippe coolly declared he had no desire to kill Gilet; he suggested Max might do better to go to America and make a fortune rather than scandalize Issoudun.Max, routed before the town and too proud to run from Philippe, refused to flee. He planned instead to kill the colonel. Though admitting they had committed a monstrous folly and that he ought to have gone to Paris, he would not retreat: his sense of honor required him to recover what he had lost by killing Bridau. Flore counseled flight to America and promised to find a way to get rid of Bridau, but Max insisted on fighting. The point of honor prevailed over sensible policy. Flore returned home and shut herself up to cry.Gossip swelled in Issoudun. Mignonnet told Hochon people were uneasy because Gilet knew all weapons. But Hochon praised Philippe’s skill; the colonel had managed well from the start. Some defended Max’s nocturnal amusements; others recalled his betrayal of Joseph Bridau and sided with the colonel. Above the personal duel, the town fretted over the fate of Rouget’s property—fifty thousand francs a year that Max seemed ready to seize. Every household in town prayed for the honor of Colonel Bridau.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Towards four o’clock the next day the old army officers at Issoudun gathered in the place du Marché, before Lacroix’s eating-house, to await Colonel Philippe Bridau; the coronation banquet was set for five. They discussed Max’s disgrace and dismissal from old Rouget’s house; the officers would dine at Lacroix’s, the common soldiers at a wine-shop. Only Potel and Renard defended Max. Renard asked why they must mind the affairs of an old man’s heirs; Potel blamed Max’s weakness with women. Predictions of sabres and crime circulated.Max joined the old soldiers and was received in heavy silence, supported only by Potel and Renard. Philippe arrived in full dress; his assured manner contrasting with Max’s constrained gratitude toward his few supporters. Bridau was warmly greeted by several officers, which dissolved Max’s last scruples born of Flore’s entreaties.“We shall fight,” Max told Renard; “to the death. Don’t talk to me—let me play my part well.” The Bonapartists returned to the officers’ group. Max bowed to Bridau; their glance was frigid. Renard toasted “the Little Corporal” and, at Philippe’s request, the adversaries sat opposite each other. Town youths lingered outside, expecting a duel.Throughout the meal both men masked vigilance with apparent joviality. At dessert Philippe toasted “To the Grand Army!” and Mignonnet followed with “Let us drink to those who attempted to restore his son!” All but Maxence Gilet bowed to Philippe. Max rose and bowed ironically to Philippe, then delivered a cold phrase—“May we all meet again at this time next year”—that raised tension. Plans for a duel were calmly fixed: sabres at ten that night, and at eight the next morning behind the Capuchins’ church; Goddet, the former army surgeon, would be present. The seconds set the combat to last ten minutes.That night Monsieur and Madame Hochon surprised Philippe in his rooms. Madame Hochon, tearful, implored him to say his prayers and presented a relic—a tooth of Sainte Solange on black velvet—urging him to wear it. Philippe joked, but Benjamin was paid thirty francs to sew the relic into his trousers. The old lady departed confident.On the cold morning of December third, Max, his seconds, and the Pole arrived at the meadow by the Capuchins’ apse. Philippe and his seconds waited, Benjamin among them. Potel and Mignonnet measured twenty-four feet and attendants drew lines: no man could retreat beyond his line under pain of dishonor. Each was to stand at his line and advance only on the seconds’ word.They stripped to shirts without trembling. As the signal was given Max spotted Fario’s sinister face peering through a hole in the church roof—eyes full of hatred that unsettled him. Philippe adopted an inside guard of a seasoned duellist; Max felt inferior. He tried a moulinet to bewilder Philippe, but the colonel’s wrist was iron and spring-like. Philippe’s parries were cool and precise; victory depended on calculation or a single false movement. At a poorly executed parry Max’s sabre was spun from his hand.“Pick it up,” Philippe said; “I am not the man to kill a disarmed enemy.” The magnanimous words stunned onlookers. When Max resumed position he had lost composure. Determined to redeem his shame, he rushed furiously with both hands on his sabre. Philippe received a cut slashing his forehead and part of his face, but with a terrible oblique moulinet he cleft Max’s head. These two savage blows ended the combat in the ninth minute. Fario came down to gloat over Max’s convulsions; Philippe was carried back to his uncle’s house.Maxence Bridau’s death closed the exploits of the Order of Idleness and relieved Issoudun. He had courage, self-possession, and political sagacity, but lacked the nobility of conduct needed for greatness. Because of the perfidy that brought him into disrepute, he was not much regretted. Reports praised both combatants’ bravery; “they had better both have been killed,” Monsieur Mouilleron remarked, meaning it would have been a good riddance for the Government.Flore Brazier’s situation, which might have embarrassed Philippe, was saved by her collapse after Max’s death. A brain fever set in, with dangerous inflammation from her escapade to Vatan; she hovered between life and death for three months, attended by Goddet, who also cared for Philippe.As soon as Philippe could write he sent letters. To Monsieur Desroches he reported he had “killed the most venomous of the two reptiles,” though his own head had been split. He implied the other “viper” remained and that he must come to terms with her because his uncle clung to her. He said illness had kept Flore in bed, a condition he hoped Providence would use to call her soul if she repented. He claimed Goddet and others had been won to his side; Goddet preached resignation to Flore and had promised to keep her in bed for three months. He had paid off the cook, who spread tales suggesting Max would have ill-treated Flore. “All goes well so far,” he wrote; “my uncle, by old Hochon’s advice, has destroyed his will.”To Monsieur Giroudeau, care of Mademoiselle Florentine, he requested a girl—Cesarine, if available—or some ballet-girl who could appear as the respectable daughter of a brave soldier and be ready at his call; if she answered his purpose he would give her fifty thousand francs on his uncle’s death. He described the duel and hinted at lively times ahead.Madame Bridau’s sister Agathe, informed by Madame Hochon, rushed to Issoudun and occupied Philippe’s former room. Town praise for Philippe revived her maternal tenderness. Madame Hochon excused Philippe’s conduct as youthful fling and extolled him as a future comfort if kept from Paris temptations. Philippe played the saint to his mother because he needed her. He did not want to bring in an actress unless he remained an object of horror to Mademoiselle Brazier; recognizing Flore’s hold on his uncle, he conceived the idea of marrying her to Rouget to legitimize and keep her—and the property—within reach. He asked his mother to visit Flore and treat her sisterly.Philippe presented the plan affectingly before Monsieur and Madame Hochon: Rouget’s way of life was not becoming, but by marrying Flore he could make her respected and stop scandal. The next day Agathe and Madame Hochon repeated this to Flore at her bedside. In Issoudun the colonel was praised for supposed nobility toward Flore. For a month she heard Goddet, Madame Hochon, Agathe, and Rouget urge a marriage. The idea of becoming Madame Rouget enticed her, and it was made plain she would be unwelcome in the Rouget family if she intended to turn Philippe out.“Besides,” the doctor whispered, “you really owe him this good fortune. Max would never have allowed you to marry old Rouget. And, if you have children, you can revenge Max, for that will disinherit the Bridaus.” Thus the notion of revenge through offspring was planted.Two months after the duel, in February 1823, urged by those around her and entreated by Rouget, Flore consented to receive Philippe; his scars moved her to tears, and his softened manner calmed her. By his wish they were left alone. He then began to play his hand: claiming to have advised the marriage from the start, he promised to ensure Flore’s treatment in the marriage contract and urged her that wealth and respect outweighed past losses. He hinted again at revenge through children, saying her offspring would disinherit the Bridaus. His manipulative phrasing masked calculation as sentiment; self-interest did the work of persuasion.At the beginning of April 1823 Rouget’s hall hosted a splendid dinner celebrating the signing of the marriage contract between Flore Brazier and the old bachelor. Guests included Monsieur Heron, the witnesses, Mignonnet, Carpentier, Hochon, Goddet, the mayor, the curate, Agathe Bridau, Madame Hochon and Madame Borniche. The bride, protected by Philippe and the two ladies, shone; the curate had instructed her for a fortnight and she was to take first communion the next day. Local journals hailed the marriage as a revival of religion and moral correction; the “Journal du Cher” and the “Journal de l’Indre” lauded Philippe’s supposed disinterestedness.By the contract Rouget secured to Flore a dower of one hundred thousand francs and a life annuity of thirty thousand. After the wedding Agathe returned to Paris triumphant and told Joseph and Desroches the “good news.” Desroches predicted Philippe would still keep his paw on the inheritance; Madame Bridau defended her son as statesmanlike. Desroches replied that Philippe would manage to appear honest while pursuing his ends.The morning after the marriage Philippe took Madame Rouget aside and laid out his plan with cold frankness. He revealed a letter from Mariette and friends describing a plot to provide an actress to entrap Rouget and secure the property for Philippe; the letter terrified Madame Rouget, showing the danger she had escaped. “I trust you,” he said, “but I expect some return. I have made you my aunt intending to marry you some day. You are worth more to me than Esther in managing my uncle. In a year from now we must be in Paris; you will amuse yourself much better there than here; I shall return to the army and become a general, and you will be a great lady. There’s our future; now work for it. But I must have a pledge to bind this agreement.”He demanded a power of attorney from Rouget within a month, under pretense of relieving the old man of business fatigues, and a month later a special power to transfer the income in the Funds to his name. When that stood in his name, he said, he and Flore would have equal interest in marrying each other—he could marry his aunt after a year’s widowhood but not “a disgraced girl.” He left without waiting for an answer. Vedie, clearing the table, found her mistress pale and sweating. Flore felt as if she had fallen to the bottom of a precipice; fear and a sense of guilty desertion overwhelmed her, yet an inner voice told her she deserved such subjection.Flore had once had a room in Rouget’s house; now Madame Rouget belonged to her husband and was deprived of freedom. She briefly held hope of bearing a child, but Philippe’s constant watch made that impossible. Benjamin served as Philippe’s innocent spy; Vedie trembled under his scrutiny. Flore felt deserted; without knowing how, she was certain that if he suspected pregnancy her doom would be sealed. Philippe’s politeness cloaked brutality; his gambler’s eye and the slightest movement suggested threat. The power of attorney he demanded he obtained as soon as he wanted it; Flore fell under his dominion as France had under Napoleon.Rouget rapidly declined into decay; in his decline the nephew remained cold and impassible, like a diplomat in convulsed times, neutral to the old man’s degradation.Meanwhile Philippe, now skeptical of Napoleon II., wrote to the minister of war via intermediaries to offer his services to the King. He declared Napoleon gone and offered to serve the monarch, professing submission and fidelity and asking for consideration and reinstatement. He enclosed requests for permission to go to Paris on urgent family business and endorsements from Issoudun’s mayor, sub-prefect, and commissary of police praising his conduct and citing newspaper articles about Rouget’s marriage.Two weeks later Philippe received permission and a letter informing him that by order of the King he was, as a preliminary favor, reinstated as lieutenant-colonel in the royal army.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lieutenant-Colonel Philippe Bridau returned to Paris with his aunt and the frail old Rouget; three days later he escorted them to the Treasury where Jean-Jacques Rouget’s income was assigned to Philippe. Exhausted and vulnerable, the old man was drawn by his nephew into the dissipations of the theatrical and journalistic world—actresses, artists, and questionable women among whom Philippe had wasted his youth. Induced by Giroudeau, Lolotte, one of the most beautiful ballet-girls of the Opera, befriended the old man and became his willing destroyer. Rouget died after a magnificent supper at Florentine’s; Lolotte blamed a slice of pâté de foie gras, and the Strasburg gourmand’s infamy was quietly put down to indigestion.Madame Rouget adapted to the loose society well enough, but Philippe placed her under the care of Mariette, who prevented actual ruinous excesses. In October 1823 Philippe returned to Issoudun with a power of attorney from his aunt to liquidate his uncle’s estate. By March 1824 he was back in Paris with sixteen hundred thousand francs—the net proceeds of Rouget’s property, not including the pictures, which remained with Monsieur Hochon. On Hochon’s recommendation he deposited the whole with Mongenod and Sons at six percent, with three months’ notice for withdrawal.Philippe invited his mother, Agathe Bridau, to witness his marriage contract; the bargain arranged by him ensured that the widow Rouget’s million-franc portion would pass to him should she die childless. He lodged his wife in a rue Saint-Georges apartment bought furnished from Lolotte, a dwelling she found charming but which he rarely visited. Secretly he bought a splendid hotel in the rue de Clichy for 250,000 francs—150,000 paid down—and lavished immense sums restoring and furnishing it, even using money meant to represent two years’ income. The restored paintings from Issoudun, estimated at three hundred thousand francs, embellished this show.With Charles X’s accession, the Duc de Chaulieu’s family regained court favor. Philippe, who had likely revealed conspiracies of 1820 and 1822, was made lieutenant-colonel in the Duc de Maufrigneuse’s regiment; the duke felt obliged to protect the man from whom he had taken Mariette. The appointment, and an effort to add a liberal tint to the Dauphin’s circle, led to Philippe’s presentation to the Dauphin and Dauphine. He played the part of rough soldier well, and parlayed this into an aide-de-camp position to a marshal well-placed at court.Promoted and intent on social advancement, Philippe solicited ennoblement. Colonel Bridau had bought the estate of Brambourg and asked to entail it as a county; he became Comte de Brambourg. He adopted the external trappings of a great lord—horses, carriages, liveries—and began frequenting the house of the Comte de Soulanges, a lieutenant-general of artillery. Insatiable for influence and supported by mistresses of powerful men, Philippe aimed to be one of the Dauphin’s aides-de-camp and even to marry Mademoiselle Amélie de Soulanges. He had the audacity to plead with the Dauphin that a veteran soldier might be useful “on occasion,” and employed a full range of sycophantic tones to ingratiate himself in high society. Having copied the respectability that Mignonnet had once affected, he now fit the part required by court life: fine establishment, sumptuous entertainments, complete social posing—but he excluded old friends whose past could compromise him.He was unrelenting toward companions of former debaucheries. When Bixiou sought his influence for Giroudeau, who wished to re-enter the army after Florentine’s desertion, Philippe brusquely refused: “The man has neither manners nor morals.” Yet he kept his own ruthless ambitions in view. He secured orders and titles—the Commanderships of the Legion of Honor and of Saint-Louis—and flaunted his status. Once, as Agathe and Joseph Bridau walked home in the rain, Philippe, bedizened in uniform and decorations, rode by and splashed them with mud as he waved a condescending greeting. Agathe, always ready to rationalize, excused his neglect as the price of great station, while Joseph bitterly noted his brother’s hauteur.To further his plans, Philippe painted himself as a victim of an unsuitable marriage. To the Duc de Maufrigneuse he declared in theatrical tones that his wife was a cook with base tastes, that she had taken to drinking, and that her imminent death would free a million-franc legacy he had been saving to invest. In truth Philippe had engineered the collapse of domestic virtue to make him appear a respectable suitor for Amélie de Soulanges: he cultivated libertine society around his wife and then withdrew support until she sank into dependence and vice. He counted on the legacy, his entail, and a prospective marshal’s baton for Soulanges to elevate him to general and peerhood.Meanwhile Agathe’s fortunes shifted. After the 1823 Salon, one of Joseph Bridau’s admirers awarded his mother the management of a lottery-office. She later exchanged it for an office near her son’s atelier in the rue de Seine, thereby supporting herself and freeing Joseph from obligation. Yet Agathe’s attachment shifted with appearance and success: Philippe’s glittering uniforms and courtly presence impressed her more than Joseph’s struggling genius. Joseph, a painter of talent but of uneven success, was thwarted by classical critics and an ungrateful bourgeois clientele. Though supported by friends like d’Arthez and Mademoiselle des Touches, he produced too much variety and too few commissions; he owed money and did not fulfill the material expectations borne by his circle. Agathe, unable to appreciate his artistic subtleties, yearned instead for the public honors that Philippe displayed—medals, parades, and courtly favor—so she took pride in her prosperous son and neglected Joseph’s worth.Yet practical tenderness endured: Agathe bore the household running on the lottery-office proceeds and secretly supported Joseph, paying his bills while hoping Philippe would do more. For three years she had expected Philippe’s visits; convinced he could easily relieve Joseph’s debts, she wrote him a pleading letter, reminding him that he had forgotten his mother for five years and beseeching him to help Joseph financially. The reply she received shattered her: a single savage note in which Philippe brusquely alluded to the social magnitude of the man he intended to marry and the gulf between a count’s wife and a cook. The curtness and callousness of “Your son, Philippe Bridau” on the envelope struck her down. Agathe fell half-fainting; Joseph nursed her, sent for Bianchon, and secured funds for pressing debts without revealing the truth to his mother. She lingered in a death agony for three weeks.On her deathbed Agathe summoned Abbé Loraux, her long-time confessor, and poured out her perplexed sorrow—“What have I done to displease God?” The priest gently indicted her life’s misplacements. He declared that her punishment was God’s proof to bring her to repentance: she had misjudged her children and lavished love upon an “unnatural son” who had pillaged her while overlooking the son who loved her without equal return. The priest’s rebuke opened her eyes. She wept, repented, and turned her heart to Joseph, whom she pressed to forgive. He, though stunned by the ostracism and his brother’s cruelty, embraced her and forgave her in the fulness of that last tenderness. For two weeks each expression seemed the sum of a lifetime; Agathe, soothed by Joseph’s devotion, died reconciled. In delirium she murmured of Philippe’s conspicuous sacrifices—“Whom does Philippe take after?”—and Joseph followed her alone to the grave. Philippe, who had been invited to business at Orléans on the day of the funeral, had actually fled Paris after receiving Joseph’s reproachful note: “Monster! my poor mother has died of the shock your letter caused her. Wear mourning, but pretend illness; I will not suffer her assassin to stand at my side before her coffin.”Joseph, too shattered to work, was kept company by devoted friends: d’Arthez, Michel Chrestien, Fulgence Ridal, Pierre Grassou, and Bianchon. Two weeks after the funeral they received a curious letter from a shabby old woman, requesting assistance for Comtesse Flore de Brambourg. Written with clutching pathos and accusing Philippe of desiring his wife’s death, the letter alleged that Flore, abandoned and penniless, faced eviction and was considering suicide. Joseph read the name: “Comtesse Flore de Brambourg” — his sister-in-law. He shuddered, suspecting deeper villainy.The woman who delivered the letter, Madame Gruget, presented as a miserable, worn nurse. She recounted her pitiable charges: a young protégée who had loved imprudently and drowned herself, and Flore—beautiful once, now hideously wasted, penniless and ill. Bianchon, Desroches, Bixiou, and Joseph went to see Flore. Bixiou, with savage wit, supplied a summary of Philippe’s methods: paying off dissolute companions to corrupt his wife, ceasing to support her until vice and poverty did their work, and then abandoning her to languish. They found Flore in a garret cell under the roof, green and hollow-eyed, head bound, a mere skeleton of the former woman. She wept at Joseph’s compassion. Flore declared Philippe guilty—“Philippe killed Max, who advised dreadful things, and now he has killed me. God uses him as a scourge!”—and implored for priestly consolation she could not secure.Bianchon judged her disease possibly curable with surgical aid, and Desroches arranged transfer to Dr. Dubois’s private hospital. There, with the energetic intervention of Bianchon and Desroches and the sympathy of Joseph’s friends, Flore underwent bold surgery reported later in the Hospital Gazette. Still, she was too far gone: misery and starvation and the ravages of vice had exhausted her. She died three weeks later; the operation could not overcome her dereliction.While this drama unfolded, Bixiou took revenge in his own way. Knowing how Philippe courted the Soulanges family for marriage with their daughter, Bixiou, with characteristic malice and theatricality, disguised himself as a secular priest and recounted to the Comte de Soulanges—in a mock-hushed and portentous voice—stories of Philippe’s past: the suspicious deaths, the thefts, the moral lapses. He presented gossip, and the Comte, prudent toward his daughter’s prospects, made inquiries. The Soulanges housewoman later found reasons to hesitate about marriage with a parvenu whose youthful ledger contained such disreputable entries. Bixiou’s stratagem had its effect.Philippe, meanwhile, remained a figure who could be at once flamboyant and cold. He gave supper to du Tillet, Nucingen, Eugène de Rastignac, Maxime de Trailles, and Henri de Marsay, boasting of manifold millions—“three millions, excluding house and estate”—and of a future fortune. The bankers du Tillet and Nucingen traded on Philippe’s disdain for “civilians” and his confidence in the aristocratic Restoration. In July 1830 they used his credulity to ensnare him in speculation: they pretended to share his conviction of a Restoration triumph, encouraged him to risk, and then played against him as revolution loomed. Philippe fought for what he believed to be the cause; when orders called him with the Duc de Maufrigneuse to Saint-Cloud on July 25th he was spared the immediate danger of presiding over a charge upon the boulevards—an order that likely saved his life that day. But within a month his immense fortune had been swept away—only his house, furniture, Brambourg estates, and the pictures from Issoudun remained. He had trusted the wrong men and misjudged political probabilities.Broke and desperate to recover prestige, Philippe re-entered the army. In 1835 he obtained a regiment command in Algeria—three perilous years that offered a chance of attaining the general’s epaulets he craved. But his severity and brutality alienated his men, and the rivalry with Giroudeau—once a companion—became malignant. Philippe’s harshness verged on cruelty; his troops hated him despite sometimes consummate bravery. In early 1839, during a risky retreat before superior Arab forces, he rashly headed in with only a single company to break through, and there, isolated, he was surrounded. His cries—“Your colonel! to me! a colonel of the Empire!”—went unheeded. The conflict was ferocious and personal. Philippe Bridau met a savage end: hacked to pieces by scimitars, his head cut off.Joseph Bridau’s fortunes were, ironically, reversed by these tragedies. Around this time he married through the intercession of the Comte de Serizy a farmer’s daughter endowed with means; his father-in-law’s thrift and industry provided steady financial support. As a result of the entail on Brambourg, Joseph inherited his brother’s Paris house and the estate, plus the cherished Issoudun pictures—the very collection Philippe had once used as social capital. He thus acquired an income of sixty thousand francs and continued assistance from his father-in-law. Joseph’s art flourished: he painted magnificent works and did important service to the artistic community, though he had not yet been elected to the Institute. A clause in the entail also conferred the title Comte de Brambourg upon him—a distinction that caused him to laugh among his atelier friends, for it married noble name to the life of a bohemian painter.Throughout this narrative various figures from Balzac’s Human Comedy orbit appear: Bianchon, the faithful physician; Bixiou, the satirical society wit; Giroudeau, the military rival; Bianchon’s medical interventions; and a host of others—Madame Raoul Nathan, the Duc de Maufrigneuse, the Soulanges family, Nucingen and du Tillet the bankers, and more—each playing parts in the shifting fortunes of the Bridau family. The story traces Philippe’s cynical social climbing and cold calculations, his moral degradation in pursuit of rank and marriage, the collateral tragedies of his coldness—Agathe’s death from shock, Flore’s abandonment and death—and his final, violent fall in Algeria. Opposed to him stands Joseph, the struggling artist who, consistently generous and devoted, finally inherits what his brother had burned through: modest happiness, the pictures he loves, and an ironic title that crowns an improbable victory of virtue and art over cynical ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The commercial traveller, unknown to antiquity, is one of the striking figures of our age. He marks a transition from an epoch of individual creative power to one of universal, levelling influence—equalizing products, spreading them among the masses, and governed by the principle of unity, the final expression of modern social organization. We seem to pass from the saturnalia of popular thought to a dead level in which civilizations’ treasures are concentrated and redistributed.He is to ideas what stage-coaches are to people and goods: their vehicle. From the luminous centre he takes a handful of light and scatters it among drowsier regions. This human pyrotechnic is a scholar without learning, a juggler deceived by his own tricks, an unbelieving priest who expounds mysteries better for his lack of faith. Curious being: he has seen everything, claims all the ways of the world, soaked in Parisian vices yet affecting provincial familiarity. He links village and capital—neither Parisian nor provincial, but a traveller. He sees nothing to the core; he knows men and places by name, surveys things at the surface, measures with a little tape-line. His glance skims all and penetrates none. Busy with much, he is occupied by nothing truly profound.A jester and bon vivant, he courts every political opinion and professes patriotism to the marrow. A master mimic, he can switch smiles of persuasion, satisfaction, and good-humour and drop them to reveal his ordinary face. In his trade he must be a certain observer: probing men with a glance to guess habits, wants, and, above all, solvency. To economize time he makes quick decisions about his chances—practice that turns him into a shrewd judge; on that basis he sets up as critic of theatres, discoursing of both Parisian and provincial stages.He knows all the haunts of France “de actu et visu.” He can pilot you equally to vice or virtue. With an eloquence like a hot-water spigot, he can open or check a stream of phrases kept on tap, producing upon his victims a moral shower-bath. Loquacious as a cricket, he smokes, drinks, wears trinkets, overawes the common people, passes for a lord in villages, and never allows himself to be “stumped”—a bit of his own slang. He knows how to slap his pockets at the right moment and make money jingle if he fears suspicious servants think him a thief. Activity is among his most astonishing qualities: no hawk, stag, or hound can rival his rapid dart for a commission, his agility to trip up rivals, or his keenness in scenting a customer and where his wares will sell.What great qualities such a man must possess! In all countries one finds these low diplomats: consummate negotiators selling calico, jewels, frippery, wines, often showing truer diplomacy than ambassadors who know mostly its forms. No one in France doubts the powers of the commercial traveller: that intrepid soul who boldly brings the genius of civilization and Parisian inventions to reckon with the plain common sense of remote villages. Who can forget the manoeuvres by which he worms himself into the minds of the populace, bringing a volume of words to bear upon the refractory, like a slow file eating into porphyry? Would you know the force of language, the pressure a phrase can exert against rebellious lucre and the miserly country proprietor—listen to one of these ambassadors of Parisian industry revolve, work, and suck like an intelligent piston of the steam-engine called Speculation.“Monsieur,” said a shrewd political economist, director-cashier-manager and secretary-general of a celebrated fire-insurance company, “out of every five hundred thousand francs of policies to be renewed in the provinces, not more than fifty thousand are paid up voluntarily. The other four hundred and fifty thousand are got in by the activity of our agents, who go among those in arrears and worry them with stories of horrible incendiaries until they are driven to sign the new policies. Thus you see that eloquence, the labial flux, is nine tenths of the ways and means of our business.”To talk and make people listen—that is seduction itself. A nation with two Chambers, a woman who lends both ears, are soon lost. Eve and her serpent are the everlasting myth of an hourly fact that began, and may end, with the world itself. “A conversation of two hours ought to capture your man,” said a retired lawyer.Walk round the commercial traveller and look at him well: olive-green overcoat, cloak with morocco collar, striped blue cotton shirt. In this original figure—so distinct it cannot be rubbed out—how many personalities we meet! What an acrobat, circus, battery all at once! Intrepid mariner, he plunges armed with a few phrases to catch five or six thousand francs in the frozen seas of France’s interior, among the red Indians of the countryside. Provincial fish will not rise to harpoons; they are taken with seines, nets, and gentle persuasions. The traveller’s business is to extract gold from country caches by a purely intellectual operation, pleasantly and without pain. Think of the flood of phrases which, renewed each dawn, leaps over the length and breadth of sunny France.Now take an individual example.In Paris there lived an incomparable commercial traveller, the paragon of his race, possessing the necessary qualifications to success in the highest degree. His speech was both vitriol and glue—glue to catch and entangle, vitriol to dissolve hard heads, close fists, and close calculations. His line was once the hat; his skill at snaring the wariest provincial made him a celebrity, so that all vendors of “article Paris” sought him and begged him to take their commissions.When he returned to Paris between provincial triumphs, he lived in perpetual festivity: weddings and suppers. In smaller towns correspondents made much of him; in Paris the great houses feted him. Welcomed, flattered, and fed wherever he went, to breakfast or dine alone became novelty. He lived like a sovereign—or a journalist; in fact he was the perambulating “feuilleton” of Parisian commerce.His name was Gaudissart; flatteries won him the surname Illustrious. Wherever he went—behind a counter or before a bar, into a salon or atop a stage-coach, up to a garret or dining with a banker—everyone said, “Ah! here comes the illustrious Gaudissart!” Similia similibus—he believed in homoeopathy. Puns, horse-laugh, monkish face, friar’s skin—clothing, body, mind, and features—combined to give devil-may-care jollity to every inch of him. Free-handed and easy-going, he was the grisettes’ favourite: he jumped to the top of stage-coaches, helped timid ladies down, joked with postillions and sold them caps, smiled at maids and caught them by the waist or heart; gurgled at dinner like a bottle of wine, pretended to draw a cork by a brisk blow on his cheek, played tunes on champagne glasses, dared the company to copy him; chaffed the timid traveller, contradicted the knowing one, lorded it at table and managed to get the titbits. Strong as he was, he could throw aside nonsense and mean business when he flung away a cigar stump and said, with a glance at some town, “I’ll go and see what those people have got in their stomachs.”Buckled down to work he became the slyest and cleverest diplomat. All things to all men, he could accost a banker like a capitalist, a magistrate like a functionary, a royalist with pious sentiments, a bourgeois as one of themselves. Where he went he was what he ought to be; he left Gaudissart at the door when he entered, and picked him up when he came out.Until 1830 the illustrious Gaudissart was faithful to the article Paris. Close contact with humanity’s caprices taught him the windings of the human heart. He learned persuasive eloquence, the knack of loosening the tightest purse-strings, the art of rousing desire in husbands, wives, children, and servants—and how to satisfy it. No one better inveigled a merchant by the charms of a bargain and vanished at the crisis of desire. Grateful to the hat trade, he always declared that capping heads had enabled him to understand interiors; he had “capped and crowned” so many people that he felt at liberty to fling himself at their heads. His jokes about hats and heads were irrepressible.Yet after August and October 1830 he abandoned the hat trade and the article Paris, tearing himself from visible wares to mount into the higher spheres of Parisian speculation. “He forsook,” in his words, “matter for mind; manufactured products for the infinitely purer elaborations of human intelligence.” This needs explanation.The upheaval of 1830 brought old ideas recomposed by clever speculators. After 1830 ideas became property. A writer, too wise to publish, once said, “more ideas are stolen than pocket-handkerchiefs.” Perhaps we may one day have an Exchange for thought; even now ideas, good or bad, have their consols, are bought, imported, exported, sold, and quoted like stocks. If ideas are not on hand, speculators pass off words in their stead and live on them as a bird lives on millet. Do not laugh: a word is worth as much as an idea in a land where the ticket on a sack is more important than its contents. We have seen libraries working off the word “picturesque” when literature would have killed “fantastic.” Fiscal genius has guessed the proper tax on intellect; it has estimated the profits of advertising and registered a prospectus of thought’s quantity and worth—intellectual property weighed at the Stamp Office in the Rue de la Paix.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Intellect and its products, once turned into articles of commerce, must submit to the same laws as manufactured goods. Ideas conceived by idle Parisians—who zealously fight moral battles over champagne and pheasants—are handed at birth to commercial travellers charged with spreading them “urbi et orbi,” seasoned with advertisement and prospectus. These agents trap the departmental rodent called subscriber—sometimes stockholder, occasionally corresponding member or patron, but invariably fool. Many a poor provincial proprietor, seduced by the prospect of being the first to launch a new idea, has cried “I am a fool!” after sinking a thousand or twelve hundred francs into a void. Speculators sneer that subscribers should know that to succeed in the intellectual world they must start with more money than for a tour of Europe.Since 1830 honors and emoluments have been lavishly distributed across France to stimulate what are called the “progressive and intelligent masses”: titles, medals, diplomas, a sort of legion of honor for martyrs follow one another. Speculators in manufactured intellect have their own spice—premiums, forestalled dividends, and the conscription of famous names without the writers’ consent, who find themselves associated with more enterprises than days in the year; the law does not consider theft of a patronymic. Worse still is the rape of ideas: these caterers for the public mind, like slave-merchants, tear unborn notions from the parental brain and parade them half-clothed before the terrible public, which, if not amused, will cut off their heads by curtailing ingots and emptying pockets.This commercial madness reached the illustrious Gaudissart. Noted for his irresistible eloquence, he was offered an unprecedented commission by a life-insurance company, which trained him thoroughly—the secretary-general schooling him in the business, its dialect, the gullible public, impromptu replies, and unanswerable arguments—sharpening the file of a tongue about to operate upon the life of France. Gaudissart rewarded their pains by bringing in subscribers; the insurers boasted of him so loudly that managers of two newspapers—the Globe (Saint-Simonian) and the Movement (republican)—conceived employing him to obtain subscribers. Each proposed to pay ten francs per head if he brought in a thousand, but only five francs if he fetched five hundred. The bargain did not interfere with his life-insurance work; he even claimed indemnity from the Saint-Simonians for eight days spent studying their doctrines, asserting prodigious memory and intellect were necessary to grasp them, though he asked nothing from the republicans.Gaudissart inclined to republican ideas, had dabbled in conspiracies, had once been arrested and released for lack of proof; lately he had grown a mustache and believed a hat of certain shape and a pair of spurs would make him a fit representative of the Republic. For a week he went each morning to be Saint-Simonized at the Globe and every afternoon to the life-insurance company. His aptitude and memory were prodigious; by April 15, the spring campaign’s opening, he was ready. Two large commercial houses, alarmed at declining business, persuaded him not to abandon the commodity “Paris” and tempted him with large offers. The king of travellers yielded; enormous premiums and old friendships swayed him.Gaudissart delighted in letting himself be tyrannized by Jenny, a pretty florist he kept in a fourth-floor house in the Rue d’Artois, reputedly privately married to. She required innumerable attentions and ordered him to write from every town, rendering minute accounts of his proceedings. He delighted in fantasizing that subscriptions would refurnish her room: “I consecrate to the adornment of your room all the ‘Children’ I shall get in the provinces,” he told her—“Children” being his shorthand for subscriptions to a proposed newspaper for children. He imagined that five sous per subscriber would make her rich, and himself wealthy: instead of earning eight thousand francs a year, he would bring back twenty thousand at least from each trip. He planned to become a shareholder in newspapers like his friend Finot, son of a hatter, who had amassed thirty thousand francs income and sought a peerage. Why not Gaudissart as Baron, peer of France, ambassador in London, nonplussing the English? For the present, though, he must give himself wholly to capitalists: the Globe, the Movement, the Children’s Journal, life-insurance, and some of his old Article Paris.He rehearsed the grandiloquent political rhetoric he would adopt in the Chamber—declaring the Press an institution to be judged politically, debating whether it should be taxed, free, or encouraged—all in a Napoleonic pose that amused Jenny and himself. He was thirty-eight, of medium height, stout from stage-coach life, with a round, ruddy face epitomizing Abundance, Law, Force, Commerce. After a glorious breakfast with Jenny, Gaudissart departed on horseback to canvass the chief towns in his district for his multiple enterprises.After forty-five days between Paris and Blois, he stayed two weeks at Blois to write correspondence and make short visits. The night before leaving for Tours he wrote Jenny a gloating letter boasting of lodging two millions between Paris and Blois, selling Ternaux shawls at Orléans, lamenting that the Movement did not move republicans, recounting making a hundred Globes, and securing two thousand Children subscriptions.
