He bragged of cajoling a dynastic diner into subscribing to the Children’s Journal to avoid quarrel and promised to take Amboise in two days and “topple” Tours.From Tours he went to Vouvray, a rich district where the public mind seemed susceptible. Trotting along an embankment, admiring the landscape, he little dreamed his commercial infallibility would perish there. A note on the Tourangian mind: stamped by Rabelais’ spirit, Touraine’s people are polished, witty, poetic, voluptuous; their impulse subsides quickly. Atmosphere, ease, and jovial manners smother sentiment of art and enervate will. At home Tourangians sit at ease, employ wit to mock neighbors, and make merry—Rabelais’ Abbaye of Theleme is their emblem.In Vouvray Gaudissart met one of its native wit-masters: Monsieur Vernier, a former dyer, a social leader among small proprietors, and a merry-maker. The Tourangians especially hated Saint-Simonian doctrines and treated them with superb disdain. After arriving and consulting landlord Mitouflet, Gaudissart betook himself to Vernier to solicit help. He began his patter about bankers, respectability, guarantees—drawing his string of phrases. Vernier let him talk, apparently interested, but plotted a prank: to bring before him the town’s supposed banker—Margaritis, a half-insane man—and have Gaudissart sell to him.Margaritis lived at the head of the Valley Coquette with his devoted wife. Of Italian origin and half-mad, he carried two long-bladed knives, sometimes threatened his wife, kept a sack of flour and two puncheons of wine in the cellar, and for seven years read the same Indre-et-Loire newspaper without perceiving the repetition. He moved mechanically to say, “Go away!” yet had lucid intervals when he managed wine sales and gave useful advice. He stole delicacies and once flew into fury when told he did not hear. The evening before Gaudissart’s arrival Madame Margaritis had struggled to deceive her husband about the wine; Vernier had arranged to use Benoîte Fontanieu as witness to the joke.Vernier escorted Gaudissart to La Fuye, Margaritis’s modest house. Margaritis sat in a yellow Utrecht-velvet arm-chair, somewhat inert. Vernier introduced the traveller, telling him to “put away your mathematics and talk.” Margaritis rose and invited Gaudissart to sit, and the show began: Gaudissart discoursed on life-insurance, time as the most precious social wealth, and capitalizing future prospects—“cashing” talents by discounting time. He argued that the company would recognize a man’s intellectual capital; by paying a small premium the insured would secure his family and be freed from anxieties that distract from success. Insurance, he insisted, was the exchequer of beneficence, mutual insurance against poverty, enabling men to borrow on their paper at the Bourse and among bankers; civilization denies bread to those who provide luxury, and insurance bridges the gulf. The company also offered annuities superior to tontines. These arguments, delivered with the traveller’s articulate confidence, aimed to show the practical benefits of insuring nascent talent.Margaritis reacted unpredictably. He cried, “That is usury!” then was mollified when Gaudissart offered wine; he poured Vouvray of 1819 and dubbed it Head of Vouvray—a pun with caput, caputal—and even offered to sell his puncheons. He adopted local touches and humorously bargained: he would not pay eighty francs for the Globe but offered seven francs for the Children’s Journal and consented to push the Globe if Gaudissart took his wine. The two men strolled among the vines; the ladies and Vernier watched, laughing at the strange spectacle of the urbane traveller and the lunatic engaged in earnest commercial negotiation. At length Gaudissart, who required reciprocal agreements, wrote an order for delivery of the two casks and demanded Margaritis sign a bond to deliver them. Margaritis did so and counted out seven francs for the Children’s Journal; Gaudissart left humming triumphantly.The next day, however, the joke was revealed. At the Soleil d’Or Gaudissart heard from Mitouflet that Margaritis might be a lunatic. Furious at having been duped, he marched to Vernier’s salon where Vernier was laughing with friends. “Monsieur,” he began, calling him a scoundrel and blackguard for sending him to a lunatic and threatening to make him give satisfaction—reflecting the provincial code of honor. Vernier, delighted at the stir, defended his prank as justifiable mockery of a man trying to feather his nest by promoting a paper that preached a religion denying inheritance. Gaudissart, enraged, lunged to slap Vernier but was restrained; his wig fell on Mademoiselle Clara Vernier’s head. He declared he would stay at the Soleil d’Or until morning, ready to fight; Vernier answered he would “fight in Vouvray” and make Gaudissart stay longer than he thought.The town became agitated over the duel. Madame Fontanieu and the assistant-mayor implored Mitouflet to avert a violent death; he took charge. In the evening Mitouflet brought pens and paper to the traveller, advising him to settle business affairs if a duel loomed. He asked if Gaudissart was a good swordsman and offered foils; his experience won him sixty-two passes in practice. He advised pistols instead, loaded to fire wide so both might retire with honor. Gaudissart fretted about possibly killing the man; Mitouflet calmed him.The adversaries met by the bridge of La Cise next morning. Vernier nearly shot a cow in the scrambling; they fired in the air and embraced—recognizing that a rough joke had passed into a good one. Back at the inn, Gaudissart wanted to summon Margaritis for delivery of the casks. Vernier confessed he had none. Gaudissart demanded indemnity and refused to be outwitted without pursuing his claim. Madame Margaritis, alarmed at the fracas, brought thirty francs to compensate the traveller; Gaudissart accepted, seeing himself indemnified, and left Vouvray content with the settlement—though convinced that the region resisted new ideas.On his return from the southern departments, Gaudissart rode in a diligence and explained life’s enigmas to a young man like an infant. Passing Vouvray the youth admired the fine site; Gaudissart patronizingly replied, “Yes, Monsieur, but not habitable on account of the people. You get into duels every day. Why, it is not three months since I fought one just there,” pointing to the bridge, “with a damned dyer; but I made an end of him—he bit the dust!” The boast blended bravado with self-promotion.Throughout, the tale satirizes the commercialization of ideas, the manipulative machinery of advertisement and prospectus, the easy enlistment of famous names, the hasty plundering of nascent thought, and the provincial theater where urban pretensions meet local wit. Gaudissart, a consummate salesman—eloquent, ambitious, and vain—serves as both perpetrator and victim of this marketplace: he peddles life-insurance and newspapers, vows political ambition and social ascent, wins and loses subscriptions, is pranked by provincial satire, provokes a duel, and leaves paid off yet unconvinced of the provincial capacity for intellect. The story exposes a social economy where intellect becomes a commodity and the traveller an instrument of its distribution, often to comedic and tragic effect.Addendum: Several personages in this story recur across Balzac’s Human Comedy: Finot, Andoche (appearing in Caesar Birotteau; A Bachelor’s Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; The Government Clerks; A Start in Life; The Firm of Nucingen); Gaudissart, Félix (also in Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Cousin Pons; César Birotteau; Honorine); Popinot, Anselme (César Birotteau; Cousin Pons; Cousin Betty).############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Repeated 'MIDDLE OF THE STORY.' fragments interspersed with dense hash-block separators (################################################), creating rhythmic interruptions and emphasis.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyTHE MUSE OF THE DEPARTMENTBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by James WaringDEDICATIONTo Monsieur le Comte Ferdinand de Gramont.MY DEAR FERDINAND,—If fortune allows these lines to endure, they will repay a little for the trouble you have taken—you, the Hozier, the Cherin, the King-at-Arms of these Studies of Life; you to whom the Navarreins, Cadignans, Langeais, Blamont-Chauvrys, Chaulieus, Arthez, Esgrignons, Mortsaufs, Valois—the great names of the “Human Comedy”—owe their mottoes and bearings. The Armorial of the Etudes, devised by Ferdinand de Gramont, is a complete manual of French Heraldry, forgetting nothing, even the arms of the Empire, and I shall preserve it as a monument of friendship and patience. What knowledge of the feudal spirit is shown in the Beauseants’ Pulchre sedens, melius agens; the Espards’ Des partem leonis; the Vandenesses’ Ne se vend. Your poetic touch brings elegance and accuracy to countless learned details.Your old friend,DE BALZAC.THE MUSE OF THE DEPARTMENTOn the skirts of Le Berry stands Sancerre, a Loire town that crowns the highest hill of the Nivernais range. Its slopes descend to fertile yellow alluvium and, where floods have lain sand, to flats. Because the hill dominates the river, the little port of Saint-Thibault handles Sancerre’s commerce in wine, oak staves, and produce. By the time this story opens, suspension bridges at Cosne and Saint-Thibault have replaced ferries, signaling the post-1830 improvements favored by the House of Orléans.The town’s streets are steep and pebble-paved, girded by slopes called the Great Ramparts, and surrounded by vineyards. Wine is the chief industry: aromatic vintages close to Burgundy in character that must reach the Paris market because many won’t keep beyond seven or eight years. Below Sancerre lie small villages—Fontenoy and Saint-Satur—almost suburbs, their smiling vineyards recalling Neuchâtel.Sancerre retains narrow streets, ancient houses, and a citadel—a feudal relic that survived one of the most terrible sieges of the religious wars when French Calvinists showed ferocity surpassing even Scott’s Cameronians. Once richer and strategically important, the town has lost martial consequence and faces decline as trade favors the Loire’s right bank despite two bridges to Cosne.On the left bank, Sancerre prides itself on its view, pure air, fine vegetation, and citizens friendly and in tune with nature; though two-thirds are Calvinists, they are not Puritans. Small-town intrusions into private life coexist with a local patriotism grounded in home feeling.With at most 3,500 inhabitants compared to Cosne’s over 6,000, Sancerre’s fortunes have waned. It boasts notable sons—Horace Bianchon, a glory in modern medicine, and Étienne Lousteau, an influential journalist—but political power lies with seven or eight large landowners who control elections. A small conspiracy to throw off this electoral yoke failed because jealousy among its leaders split them. The proposed remedy—uniting behind a single champion at the next election, ideally one of the two distinguished men from Paris—originated with the borough’s Superior Woman, whose personal stakes and past require a brief account of her antecedent career.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Sancerre, about 1836, could boast a Superior Woman, long misprized but now locally celebrated. This was also the era when two Sancerrois in Paris achieved distinction: Etienne Lousteau wrote reviews for a widely read paper, and Bianchon, already chief physician to a hospital, Officer of the Legion of Honor, and member of the Academy of Sciences, had just been made a professor.If George Sand did not always imply blame, one might say she created “Sandism,” a sentimental school whose excesses could be charming but also revealed many superior women. Sand’s celebrity showed that France contained far more such women than had been acknowledged while the Maréchal de Saxe’s granddaughter held the field alone.The Superior Woman of Sancerre lived at La Baudraye, a town- and country-house within ten minutes of Sancerre, in the suburb of Saint-Satur. Present La Baudrayes, like many newer namesakes, had supplanted the older family whose fame dated to the Crusades and Le Berry’s history. The story of La Baudraye must be told.In Louis XIV’s time a sheriff named Milaud, whose ancestors had been fervent Calvinists, converted at the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes. To encourage conversions in a Calvinist stronghold the King gave him an appointment in the “Waters and Forests,” arms, the title Sire de la Baudraye, and the fief. Yet later descendants of the famous Captain la Baudraye fell victim to new anti-heretic decrees and were hanged—an unworthy deed of the great King.Under Louis XV, a Milaud rose from squire to Chevalier and used influence to obtain a cornet’s commission in the Musketeers for his son, who perished at Fontenoy, leaving a child. Louis XVI later granted this child, in memory of his father, the privileges of a farmer-general. This financier, elegant in society and fond of charades and verses for Chloris, lived among the nobility in exile but kept his money, aiding several highborn families. Tired of hoping and perhaps lending, he returned to Sancerre in 1800 and bought back La Baudraye out of vanity and pride—a decision intelligible in a sheriff’s grandson though the Consulate made prospects slender. He doubted his heir would perpetuate the la Baudraye line.Jean Athanase Polydore Milaud de la Baudraye, the only son, was delicate from birth, plainly the child of an exhausted constitution brought on by excess and late marriage—a sign of degeneracy in high society. Madame de la Baudraye, a Casteran de la Tour, reared this sallow, sickly boy with devoted maternal care. Her death partly prompted Monsieur de la Baudraye’s return to France. On his death the Lucullus of the Milauds left his son the fief—stripped of feudal dues but adorned with weathercocks bearing his arms—plus a thousand louis-d’or in 1802 and certain receipts for claims on distinguished émigrés, enclosed in a pocketbook full of verses wrapped with Vanitas vanitatum et omnia vanitas.Young La Baudraye did not die. He lived through monastic habits, the economy of action that Fontenelle preached as a religion for invalids, and above all the healthful air and elevation of Sancerre, which overlooks the Loire valley for leagues. Between 1802 and 1815 he added vineyard plots and devoted himself to viticulture. The Restoration initially seemed insecure; he dared not go to Paris to claim his debts. After Napoleon’s death he tried to convert his father’s autographs into money, failing to grasp the odd mixture of IOUs and verses. The winegrower wasted time trying to impress his identity on the Duke of Nivernais “and others,” returning to his vintage having won nothing but offers of service.The Restoration’s elevation of nobility made La Baudraye desire an heir. At forty-three in 1823—years no doctor would have promised—he hoped to reap the reward of sober living. His choice, however, seemed imprudent given his frail constitution; townsfolk suspected some deeper calculation.At that time the Archbishop of Bourges had converted a young woman, daughter of a citizen family that had upheld Calvinism and had escaped persecution under Louis XIV. The Piedefers—whose name suggested Reformation champions—had become respectable cloth merchants. In Louis XVI’s reign Abraham Piedefer fell into difficulties and left his children poor in 1786. One son, Tobie, went to the Indies; Moise Piedefer bought nationalized land during the Revolution, demolished abbeys with ancestral zeal, married a Catholic daughter of a Convention member who had been executed, and died in 1819, leaving a remarkably beautiful little girl named Dinah, raised in the Calvinist faith.Mademoiselle Dinah Piedefer was sent to the Demoiselles Chamarolles’ school in Bourges and soon distinguished herself for mind and beauty, though snubbed by girls of birth intended for higher worlds. Determined to match them, Dinah resolved to renounce Calvinism in hopes the Cardinal would favor his proselyte. Her conversion at seventeen was an ambition-driven act.The Archbishop, convinced Dinah would adorn society, sought her a husband. But families recoiled from a woman whose charms and education promised more expense than a husband could bear, even if she stood to inherit a modest share from La Hautoy. When these facts reached Polydore de la Baudraye, he went to Bourges; Madame Piedefer, devout and anxious for security, was ready to accept the first well-off suitor. The Cardinal welcomed Monsieur de la Baudraye, and La Baudraye, delighted to receive a wife from the Cardinal’s hands, only asked a formal promise that His Eminence would support his claims with the President of the Council to help recover debts from the Duc de Nivernais “and others.” The minister in the Pavillon Marsan found the plan too sharp, promising to attend to the business in good time rather than immediately assisting him.The announcement of La Baudraye’s imprudent marriage excited the Sancerre district. “It is quite intelligible,” said President Boirouge; the little man had been startled hearing handsome young Milaud, the Attorney-General’s deputy at Nevers, claim La Baudraye’s turrets for himself. Little La Baudraye, a changeling, would hate such a colossal rival. In Nevers, a plebeian branch of the Milauds had grown rich in cutlery and placed a representative in the civil service.To explain La Baudraye’s negotiations in Paris—and certain mysterious political phenomena that hampered the Ministry during the Restoration—it helps to recount his meeting with a Ministry emissary. The ministers’ promises being illusory, La Baudraye went to Paris when the Cardinal was called to the Chamber.Lodging at the Hotel de Mayence on Rue Saint-Honoré, the provincial was visited one morning by a confidential agent of the Ministry, an expert in “winding up” business. The elegant visitor, shown to a small room, observed La Baudraye cooking coffee over a bedroom fire and noted his shabby dressing-gown and tin saucepan. The Parisian did not mince words.“I will lay a wager, monsieur,” he said, “that you dine for forty sous at Hurbain’s in the Palais-Royal... All the princes’ creditors dine there. You recover scarcely ten per cent on debts from these fine gentlemen.” La Baudraye, thinking the man had come to buy his bills, was corrected: the visitor was Monsieur des Lupeaulx, Master of Appeals and Secretary-General to the Ministry, proposing an arrangement.Des Lupeaulx explained the political arithmetic: nobles in the King’s favor had no money but must show well; the Duc de Navarreins, a first gentleman of the Bedchamber, could not repudiate his debt but could not pay. La Baudraye, a clever noble, could take payment in favors—royal or ministerial. The collectorship at Sancerre was vacant; La Baudraye could be given the place, hold it briefly, resign, and sell it to Monsieur Gravier for twenty thousand francs; the Order of the Legion of Honor would also be conferred. In return he would restore all claims on the Duc de Navarreins.Tempted by money and a red ribbon, La Baudraye accepted. He returned to Sancerre as Collector of Taxes; six months later he was superseded by Monsieur Gravier, an agreeable financier of the Empire, whom La Baudraye introduced to his wife.Thus, through vanity, ambition, ministerial bargaining, and the Restoration’s political maneuvers, the modest vineyard-owner of La Baudraye married into a promising match, sought to recover émigré claims, and was ultimately placated with a short-lived office and honors in place of actual payment. The episode illustrates how personal pride, social aspiration, and statecraft intersected in provincial France under the restored monarchy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When released from his functions, Monsieur de la Baudraye returned to Paris to settle accounts with his debtors. He became Referendary under the Great Seal, a Baron, and an Officer of the Legion of Honor. After selling the referendary appointment and collecting remaining debts, he reappeared at Sancerre as Master of Appeals and secured a sinecure as Royal Commissioner to a commercial association in the Nivernais with a salary of six thousand francs. What seemed a financial blunder proved a shrewd match in marriage.By thrift and an indemnity from his father’s estate—nationalized and sold in 1793—he achieved his ambition by 1827. After six years of strict economy he paid four hundred thousand francs down and committed to further instalments to buy the estate of Anzy on the Loire, two leagues above Sancerre, and its celebrated castle by Philibert de l’Orme, long the Uxelles family seat. An entail created by letters patent in December 1820 included Anzy, La Baudraye, and La Hautoy—though this did not erase Dinah’s long years of unacknowledged parsimony until 1835.This financial story explains the first Baron de la Baudraye’s character. Those who know rural manias will recognize in him a land‑hunger that becomes a consuming passion—an outward avarice that often leads to ruin when mortgage interest outstrips agricultural yield. From 1802 to 1827, the ridiculed little man who trotted to Saint‑Thibault and tended his vineyards surprised his mockers when he seized the prize after the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse’s extravagance forced a sale.Madame Piedefer came to live with her daughter; she accepted an annuity of twelve hundred francs on La Hautoy and handed it to her son‑in‑law, so the combined acknowledged income of Monsieur de la Baudraye and his mother‑in‑law reached about fifteen thousand francs.Early in her married life Dinah transformed La Baudraye. She converted a forecourt into a formal garden, removed wine‑stores and shabby outhouses, and laid out a second garden behind the turreted manor with shrubs, flower‑beds and lawns screened from the vineyards by a creeper‑clad wall. She made the interiors as comfortable as circumstances allowed. To avoid being ruined by so superior a young woman, Monsieur de la Baudraye prudently kept silent about his Parisian recoveries; that secrecy lent him mystery and dignity in his wife’s eyes.The changes at La Baudraye made everyone eager to see the mistress. When, one spring morning in 1825, she appeared on the Mall in a blue velvet dress with her mother in black, Sancerre buzzed. Visitors admired fine furnishings—shawl‑pattern carpets, a carved gilt writing‑table, brocade curtains, a Japanese bowl of flowers among recent books—and hearing Dinah play the piano without demur confirmed her reputation. To avoid carelessness or bad taste she kept abreast of fashions and luxury through active correspondence with Anna Grossetete, a schoolfriend who had married well; thus Dinah often led, and distanced, local fashion.These trifles bred malignant envy among Sancerre’s ladies, but Dinah’s conversation and wit provoked absolute aversion. She tolerated no vacuous small talk, no old‑fashioned compliments, no petty slander. She would not endure tittle‑tattle; she preferred hearing of discoveries in science and art, the latest theatre pieces and poems, and by airing current cant she gave the impression of thinking deeply.Several men found her company congenial. The Abbé Duret, an old worldly cleric fond of cards, welcomed her taste; the sous‑préfet, Vicomte de Chargeboeuf, found in her drawing‑room an oasis from provincial life. Monsieur de Clagny, the public prosecutor, so admired Dinah that he refused promotion and became a devoted worshipper—tall, lean, with a minatory countenance and terrible eyes beneath enormous eyebrows, eloquent though not amorous. Monsieur Gravier, a little, round man who had been a charming ballad‑singer in the Empire and later became Paymaster‑General, secured the Receivership at Sancerre by payment and affected conspicuous fashion—buff waistcoat, gray trousers, snug coats, a silk tie slipped through a diamond ring. He fancied himself superior to Clagny.These four men extolled Dinah’s cultivation, taste and refinement, calling her a woman of superior mind. The women, however, whispered that “Madame de la Baudraye must laugh at us behind our back.” Branded a pedant for speaking grammatically, nicknamed the Sappho of Saint‑Satur, Dinah found herself ostracized: in a community of hunched backs a straight figure is monstrous. When she asked Clagny why neighborhood women made so few calls, he replied, “You are too superior a woman to be liked by other women.” Gravier, pressed, admitted that Dinah was not content with mere charm: she was clever, well educated, read every new book, loved poetry, played music and spoke delightfully—too much for jealous women to forgive.Men told Monsieur de la Baudraye he was fortunate to have such a wife, and Madame Piedefer boasted likewise in society. Those who know the world, especially Paris, know how such reputations are made.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Two years later, by the end of 1825, Dinah de la Baudraye was accused of preferring only men as visitors; soon it was said she did not care for women—and that became a crime. Not a trifling action escaped criticism or misrepresentation. After making every sacrifice a well-bred woman could make and placing herself entirely in the right, Madame de la Baudraye was so rash as to say to a false friend who condoled with her isolation: “I would rather have my bowl empty than with anything in it!” The remark produced a terrible effect in Sancerre and was cruelly retorted on the Sappho of Saint-Satur when, being childless after five years of marriage, little de la Baudraye became a byword for laughter.To explain the provincial witticism, recall the Bailli de Ferrette—some may have known him—of whom it was said he was the bravest man in Europe for daring to walk on his legs and who was accused of putting lead in his shoes to save himself from being blown away. Monsieur de la Baudraye, a sallow, almost diaphanous creature, would have been engaged by the Bailli de Ferrette as first gentleman-in-waiting if that diplomatist had been the Grand Duke of Baden rather than merely his envoy.Monsieur de la Baudraye’s legs were so thin that, for decency, he wore false calves; his thighs were like the arms of an average man, his body not unlike that of a cockchafer, and he would have made an advantageous foil to the Bailli de Ferrette. As he walked his leg-pads often twisted onto his shins; he thanked anyone who warned him. He wore knee-breeches, black silk stockings, and a white waistcoat until 1824. After the marriage he adopted blue trousers and boots with heels, which made Sancerre declare that he had added two inches to his stature so as to come up to his wife’s chin. For ten years he was always seen in the same little bottle-green coat with large white-metal buttons and a black stock that accentuated his cold, stingy face, lighted by gray-blue eyes as keen and passionless as a cat’s. Gentle, as men with fixed habits are, he seemed to please his wife by never contradicting her; he allowed her to do the talking and moved with the deliberate tenacity of an insect.Dinah, adored for her unrivaled beauty and admired for her cleverness by the most gentlemanly men of the place, encouraged their admiration by conversations it was later said she prepared beforehand. Finding her listeners rapt, she began to listen to herself, enjoyed haranguing them, and at last regarded her friends as the chorus in a tragedy, there only to give her cues. In fact, she had a fine collection of phrases and ideas, derived either from books or by assimilating the opinions of her companions, and thus became a sort of mechanical instrument, going off on a round of phrases as soon as some chance remark released the spring. To her credit, Dinah was full of knowledge and read everything—medical books, statistics, science, jurisprudence—because she did not know how to spend her days once she had reviewed her flower-beds and given orders to the gardener. Gifted with an excellent memory and the knack some women have for hitting the right word, she could talk on any subject with the lucidity of a studied style. Men came from Cosne, La Charité, Nevers, and from neighbouring towns to be introduced to Madame de la Baudraye, as in Switzerland they were introduced to Madame de Staël. Those who heard the tunes emitted by this musical snuff-box went away amazed and told such wonders of Dinah that all the women ten leagues round grew jealous.There is an indescribable headiness in the admiration we inspire, or in the effect of playing a part, which keeps criticism from reaching the idol. An atmosphere, perhaps from unceasing nervous tension, forms a halo through which the world below is seen. How otherwise account for the perennial good faith that leads to repeated presentments of the same effects, and the constant ignoring of warnings given by children, so dreaded by parents, or by husbands familiar with the peacock airs of their wives? Monsieur de la Baudraye had the frankness of a man who opens an umbrella at the first drop of rain. When his wife started on Negro emancipation or prison reform, he would take up his little blue cap and vanish without a sound, certain he could get to Saint-Thibault to see off a cargo of puncheons and return an hour later to find the discussion near its close. Or, if he had no business, he would walk on the Mall, which commanded the Loire valley panorama, and take a draught of fresh air while his wife performed a sonata in words or a dialectical duet.Once established as a Superior Woman, Dinah was eager to prove her devotion to remarkable creations of art. She threw herself into propaganda for the romantic school, extending Art to poetry, painting, literature, sculpture, furniture, and opera. She became a medievalist and prized Renaissance treasures, employing her allies as commission agents. Soon after marriage she acquired the Rougets’ furniture, sold at Issoudun in early 1824. She purchased good things in Nivernais and the Haute-Loire. At New Year and on her birthday her friends never failed to give curiosities. These fancies pleased Monsieur de la Baudraye; they made him seem to sacrifice a few crowns to his wife’s taste. In fact his land mania allowed him to think of nothing but the estate of Anzy.These “antiquities” then cost much less than modern furniture. In five or six years the anteroom, dining-room, two drawing-rooms, and boudoir Dinah arranged on La Baudraye’s ground floor—every spot, even the staircase—were crammed with masterpieces collected in the four adjacent departments. Called “queer” by neighbors, they suited Dinah. All these marvels, soon to be the rage, struck strangers introduced to her; they came expecting something unusual and found their expectations surpassed when, behind a bower of flowers, they saw catacombs full of old things, piled as Sommerard used to pile them—that “Old Mortality” of furniture. These finds served as springs which, turned by a question, played off essays on Jean Goujon, Michel Colombe, Germain Pilon, Boulle, Van Huysum, and Boucher; on Clodion, Venetian mirrors, Brustolone’s boxwood carving; on the thirteenth through seventeenth centuries; on Bernard de Palissy’s glazes, Petitot’s enamels, Dürer’s engravings—whom she called “Dur”—on illuminations on vellum, and on Gothic architecture, early-decorated, flamboyant and pure—enough to rouse both old and young.Possessed with the idea of waking up Sancerre, Madame de la Baudraye tried to form a so-called literary circle. The Presiding Judge, Monsieur Boirouge, who had a house and garden as part of the Popinot-Chandier property, favored the notion. The wily Judge discussed rules with Madame de la Baudraye; he proposed to figure as one of the founders and to let the house for fifteen years to the literary club. Within a year the members were playing dominoes, billiards, and bouillotte, and drinking mulled wine, punch, and liqueurs. A few elegant suppers were given and some masked balls during Carnival. As to literature—there were the newspapers. Politics and business were discussed. Monsieur de la Baudraye was constantly there—on his wife’s account, as she joked.This result deeply grieved the Superior Woman, who despaired of Sancerre, and therefore collected the wit of the neighborhood in her own drawing-room. Nevertheless, despite the efforts of Messieurs de Chargeboeuf, Gravier, and de Clagny, of the Abbé Duret and the two chief magistrates, of a young doctor, and a young Assistant Judge—all blind admirers of Dinah—there were occasions when, weary of discussion, they allowed excursions into agreeable frivolity, the common basis of worldly conversation. Monsieur Gravier called this “from grave to gay.” The Abbé Duret’s rubber made another pleasing variety on the oracle’s monologues. The three rivals, tired of keeping their minds at the “high range of discussion,” but not daring to confess it, would sometimes turn with ingratiating hints to the old priest.“Monsieur le Curé is dying for his game,” they would say.The wily priest lent himself readily to the little trick. He protested.“We should lose too much by ceasing to listen to our inspired hostess!” and so he would incite Dinah’s magnanimity to take pity at last on her dear Abbé.This bold manoeuvre, a device of the Sous-préfet’s, was repeated with such skill that Dinah never suspected her slaves of escaping to the prison yard, so to speak, of the cardtable; they would leave her one of the younger functionaries to harry.One young landowner, the dandy of Sancerre, fell from Dinah’s favor after some rash demonstrations. Having solicited admission to this circle, where he flattered himself he could snatch the blossom from the constituted authorities who guarded it, he was unfortunate enough to yawn in the middle of an explanation Dinah was favoring him with—for the fourth time, it is true—on the philosophy of Kant. Monsieur de la Thaumassière, grandson of the historian of Le Berry, was thenceforth regarded as a man entirely bereft of soul and brains.The three devotees en titre each submitted to these exorbitant demands on their mind and attention in hope of a crowning triumph when Dinah should become human; for none dared imagine she would give up her innocence as a wife till she had lost all illusions. In 1826, when she was surrounded by adorers, Dinah completed her twentieth year, and the Abbé Duret kept her in a sort of fervid Catholicism; so her worshipers had to be content to overwhelm her with little attentions and small services, only too happy to be taken for the carpet-knights of this sovereign lady by strangers admitted to spend an evening or two at La Baudraye.“Madame de la Baudraye is a fruit that must be left to ripen.” This was Monsieur Gravier’s opinion, who was waiting.As to the lawyer, he wrote four-page letters, to which Dinah replied in soothing words as she walked, leaning on his arm, round and round the lawn after dinner.Madame de la Baudraye, thus guarded by three passions and always under the eye of her pious mother, escaped the malignity of slander. It was evident to all Sancerre that no two of these three men would ever leave the third alone with Madame de la Baudraye; their jealousy was a comedy to lookers-on.To reach Saint-Thibault from Caesar’s Gate there is a way much shorter than that by the ramparts, down what in mountainous districts is called a coursière, at Sancerre le Casse-cou, or Break-neck Alley. The name fits a steep, stony path shut in by high banks of vineyards. By the Break-neck the distance from Sancerre to La Baudraye is much abridged. The ladies of the place, jealous of the Sappho of Saint-Satur, were wont to walk on the Mall, looking down this Longchamp of the bigwigs, whom they would stop and engage in conversation—sometimes the Sous-préfet and sometimes the Public Prosecutor—who would listen with every sign of impatience or uncivil absence of mind. As the turrets of La Baudraye are visible from the Mall, many a younger man came to contemplate Dinah’s abode while envying the ten or twelve privileged persons who might spend their afternoons with the Queen of the neighborhood.Monsieur de la Baudraye was not slow to discover the advantage he, as Dinah’s husband, held over his wife’s adorers, and he used them openly, obtaining remission of taxes and winning two lawsuits. In every litigation he used the Public Prosecutor’s name so effectively that matters were carried no further; like many undersized men, he was contentious and litigious in business, though in the gentlest manner.At the same time, the more plainly guiltless she appeared, the less conceivable Madame de la Baudraye’s position seemed to prying women. Frequently, at the Présidente de Boirouge’s house, ladies of a certain age would spend whole evenings discussing the La Baudraye household among themselves. They all suspected a mystery, a secret such as always interests women experienced in life. And, in fact, at La Baudraye one of those slow, monotonous conjugal tragedies was being played out which would have remained forever unknown if the merciless scalpel of the nineteenth century, driven by the demand for novelty, had not dissected the darkest corners of the heart, or at least those which the decency of past centuries left unopened. That domestic drama sufficiently accounts for Dinah’s immaculate virtue during her early married life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A young woman whose early triumphs sprang from pride and whose first scheme had succeeded was not likely to rest. Frail as Monsieur de la Baudraye seemed, he proved an unexpectedly good match for Mademoiselle Dinah Piedefer. Her first thoughts were practical: why was this country landowner of forty-four marrying a girl of seventeen, and what would this bargain mean for her?The little man never behaved as Dinah expected. He let her have the five acres around La Baudraye for pleasure grounds and at first provided seven or eight thousand francs for house improvements so she could buy furniture and redecorate in various styles. For a year and a half she fancied either he was not miserly or that she had great influence. After his second trip to Paris she discovered the provincial miser beneath his gentle mask. On her first request for supplies he fixed strict sums: two hundred francs a month for her personal expenses, twelve hundred francs a year charged on La Hautoy—two hundred more than the marriage settlement—and nothing explicit for housekeeping. He reminded her that, as a bachelor, he had lived on fifteen hundred francs a year and now spent six thousand including rates and repairs; that a winegrower’s returns could be ruined by weather, bankrupt clients, or speculation; that small owners must keep a year’s income in hand and expect at best two-thirds of returns.Resistance only spurred Dinah. She flung herself against his iron will masked with gentleness, tried to make him jealous or alarmed, but his confidence was imperturbable. He left her, when he went to Paris, with Medor’s conviction in Angélique’s fidelity. When she affected cold disdain to nettle him, Monsieur de la Baudraye watched her with a fixed, catlike stare. His mute indifference unnerved the young wife; she could not understand the selfish quiescence of a man who regulated his life with clocklike regularity. He evaded; she struck out—ten feet above his measured existence.Dinah’s fury at being condemned to La Baudraye and Sancerre is easy to imagine. She had dreamed of handling a fortune and managing the dwarf whom she at first humored. Hoping to appear on a greater stage, she accepted the vulgar incense of admirers in the hope her husband’s name might be drawn from the electoral urn; she supposed him ambitious after seeing him return thrice from Paris, each time with a higher social tone. But striking at his heart was like tapping marble. The man who had held offices—Receiver-General, Referendary, Master of Appeals, Officer of the Legion of Honor, Royal Commissioner—was in reality a mole throwing up little hills round a vineyard. Lamentations to officials increased their devotion to this supposed victim; like many women, Dinah never spoke of her speculative schemes, and finding them vain she ceased to scheme.In the autumn of 1827 news came of the Baron de la Baudraye’s purchase of Anzy. The little man showed a pride and glee that altered Dinah’s ideas for months; she fancied hidden greatness when she found him applying for a patent of entail. In triumph he cried, “Dinah, you shall be a countess yet!” There followed a patched-up reunion between husband and wife, humiliating to a woman whose apparent superiority was unreal while her unseen superiority was genuine. Dinah, ridiculous through the perversity of her cleverness, had real qualities of soul that provincial life never brought to light; Monsieur de la Baudraye, devoid of soul, strength, and wit, seemed a man of character only because he pursued a fixed plan he lacked power to change.Their lives entered a six-year phase during which Dinah became utterly provincial. Paris has many kinds of women—the duchess, the financier’s wife, the ambassadress, the minister’s wife—but in the country there is but one: the provincial woman. This is a sore of modern society. Nineteenth-century France divides into Paris and the provinces. The provinces are jealous of Paris; Paris barely thinks of the provinces except to demand money. Once Paris was capital of the provinces and subject to court influence; now all Paris is the Court, and the country is the town.However lofty or clever a girl from any department may be, if she marries in the country and remains there she inevitably becomes provincial. Second-rate ideas, indifference to dress, and the culture of vulgar people swamp the sublimer essence hidden in youth; everything decays. How should it be otherwise? Country girls see only provincials; their choice lies among mediocrities; provincial fathers marry daughters to provincial sons; crossing races is not considered, and mind degenerates. Men of talent, artists, and superior minds fly to Paris. The provincial woman is thus inferior in herself and twice so through her husband. How can she be happy under this twofold consciousness?A third element deepens the aridity. One flattering balm for a woman is the assurance of being something in the life of a superior man she chose. But in the country husbands are often inferior, and bachelors no better. When a provincial wife commits her “little sin,” she falls for some local dandy who is at best merely well dressed; she knows her fancy is chasing the commonplace. Dinah was preserved from this danger by pride and the conviction of her own superiority. Even without her mother’s presence, her pride and sense of destiny would have protected her. Flattered by admirers, she saw no lover among them. No man realized the poetic ideal she and Anna Grossetete had sketched.Stirred by homage she asked herself who she would choose. She preferred Monsieur de Chargeboeuf, a gentleman of good family, but his cold nature, selfishness, and narrow ambitions repelled her; he deserted a woman he once loved when his family feared scandal. Monsieur de Clagny, whose mind appealed to hers, was in some respects odious. When condemned to six years at Sancerre she nearly accepted Chargeboeuf’s devotion, but he was appointed to a prefecture and left. Clagny’s chief satisfaction was that the new Sous-préfet was a married man whose wife befriended Dinah. The lawyer’s only rival was Monsieur Gravier—the typical forty-year-old pursued by women who laugh at and encourage him. In six years among men introduced from twenty leagues round, none stirred Dinah’s anticipation of love or superior soul.Thus her choicest faculties failed to develop; she swallowed many insults to her pride, constantly bruised by a husband who calmly played supernumerary in the drama of her life. Forced to bury her wealth of love, she showed only surface to the world. Now and then she tried to rouse herself with manly resolutions; but she was kept in leading strings by money. Slowly, despite ambitious protests and grievous inward recriminations, she underwent the provincial metamorphosis. Each day took a fragment of her spirited determination. Rules for her person were abandoned. Though at first she kept up with fashion, she had to limit purchases to her allowance: instead of six hats, caps, or gowns she resigned herself to one gown each season; an admired bonnet served two seasons. Often her artistic sense led her to sacrifice personal needs to obtain some Gothic furniture. By the seventh year her morning dresses were made at home by the best local needlewoman; her mother, husband, and friends pronounced her charming in these inexpensive costumes which credited her taste. With no standard of comparison she fell into provincial snares. A Parisian might make a defect an adornment; the provincial woman accepted the worst. Dinah, extremely slim, showed it to excess; reduced by dull weariness she began to look like a skeleton in clothes; those who saw her daily did not notice the gradual change.This is a natural result of provincial life. In spite of marriage a young woman preserves her beauty some time; the town is proud of her, but everyone sees her every day and perception dulled. If she loses color it is scarcely remarked; a little neglect is thought charming. When Dinah ceased to have a new dress each season she seemed merely to have yielded to the place’s philosophy.Speech, choice of words and ideas, rust if not rubbed up in Paris; but provincialism most stamps gesture, gait, and movement. The provincial walks as if there were nothing to avoid; being known and always in her place, everyone makes way and she loses the charm of the unforeseen. Life in common induces mimicry: gestures, tone, manner, attitudes, and countenance are copied. In six years Dinah sank to the pitch of her society. As she took on Monsieur de Clagny’s ideas she assumed his tone and unconsciously adopted masculine manners from seeing none but men; she thought laughing at their ridiculous traits kept her safe, but some hue of what she laughed at remained. A Parisian woman, seeing many examples of good taste, learns to seize the moment to appear advantageously; Madame de la Baudraye, accustomed to the stage, acquired an indefinable theatrical and domineering manner—the air of a prima donna—ironical smiles that would soon have been cured in the capital.After she had adopted these absurdities, deceived by worshipers who imagined them graces, a moment of terrible awakening came like an avalanche. In a single day she was crushed by a frightful comparison.In 1829, after Chargeboeuf’s departure, she expected the Baronne de Fontaine. Anna’s husband, now Director-General under the Minister of Finance, took advantage of leave after his father’s death to take his wife to Italy. Anna came to Sancerre; the meeting was disastrous. Anna, less handsome than Dinah at school, was now a thousand times handsomer despite fatigue and travel dress. She stepped from an elegant chaise loaded with Paris milliners’ boxes and had a lady’s maid whose airs frightened Dinah. All the difference between a Paris woman and a provincial was evident: Anna spent six thousand francs a year on herself—the same sum that kept the household at La Baudraye.In twenty-four hours the friends exchanged confidences; the Parisian, seeing herself so far superior to the phoenix of Mademoiselle Chamarolles’ school, showed kindness and attentions that stabbed Dinah. She understood Anna’s advantages lay on the surface while her own were buried. When Anna left, Madame de la Baudraye, now twenty-two, fell into despair.“What ails you?” Monsieur de Clagny asked, seeing her dejected.“Anna,” she answered, “has learned to live, while I have been learning to endure.”A tragi-comedy enacted in the house, in harmony with money quarrels and successive transformations—a drama the Abbé Duret and Monsieur de Clagny alone partly understood—when Dinah, in idleness or vanity, revealed the secret of her anonymous fame.Though mixing verse and prose is odd in French literature, to give a fair idea of her unconfessed struggle it is necessary to analyze a poem born of her despair. Her patience broken by Chargeboeuf’s departure, Dinah took the Abbé’s advice to exhale her thoughts in verse—“You will find relief as those who write epitaphs or elegies,” he said. The strange production stirred the Allier, the Nièvre, and the Cher, proud of a poet to rival Paris. Paquita la Sévillane, published in the Echo du Morvan, was at once admired and suspected; some in Moulins thought Jan Diaz mocked a new school of eccentric verse imitating foreign mannerisms.The poem opened with a ballad praising Spain’s fragrant plains and Sevillian dancers, contrasting them with Northern squalor. Dinah then wrote a magnificent if affected description of Rouen—where she had never been—contrasting a manufacturing town’s life with Spain’s careless poetry versus sordid money-making. Jan Diaz made Paquita a Seville native sovereign of her town at thirteen, loved by three toreadors, whose soldier-lover carried her away to Normandy only to leave for Russia. Parting and vows were painted with Byron-like feeling. Much of the poem describes Paquita’s sufferings waiting alone in Rouen; she lives on narcotics and dreams, almost dies, but remains faithful; the soldier returns pale and frozen by Russia, and she, true, is still beautiful. The poem then takes a bitter turn: Paquita, failing to find suitable luxury in Rouen, falls into vice, ending in a house of ill-fame described with stark realism. Finally she is faded and old, but it was she who once sang of Spain.The six-hundred-line poem, with seguidillas framing it, had a gloomy vigor that alarmed Dinah, who found herself admired in three departments under the cloak of anonymity. While she enjoyed success, she feared provincial malignity: if the secret leaked, other women would trace likenesses between writer and Paquita. Reflection came too late; she shuddered at having “copied” some of her woes. “Write no more,” said the Abbé. “You will cease to be a woman; you will be a poet.” Moulins, Nevers, Bourges were searched for Jan Diaz; Dinah remained impenetrable.To divert suspicion she wrote a charming two-canto poem on the Mass-Oak, a Nivernais legend about foes from Nevers and Saint-Saulge who, about to battle in the forest of Faye, are stayed by a priest who, by saying Mass under an oak, makes them friends. This poem, purer and far superior to Paquita, was less admired.After these attempts Madame de la Baudraye, feeling herself a poet, had a light on her brow and a flash in her eyes that made her handsomer than ever. She cast longing looks at Paris, aspiring to fame—and fell back into her den at La Baudraye, daily quarrels with her husband, and a little circle whose characters had become a bore. Though literature gave relief—poetry echoed in her empty life and provided an outlet—it only increased her hatred of the gray, ponderous provincial atmosphere.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When George Sand’s revival after the Revolution of 1830 renewed interest in Le Berry, La Châtre took pride in producing a local rival to Madame de Staël and Camille Maupin. Provincial towns, eager to acclaim any feminine talent, fostered a wave of would-be Muses—women and girls drawn from quiet lives by the lure of fame. French common sense persisted, but women were increasingly allowed to voice opinions they would not have owned a few years earlier.Monsieur de Clagny exploited this freedom by collecting poems attributed to Jan Diaz in a small volume printed at Moulins, accompanied by a jaunty notice amusing to initiates. Practical jokes flourish while the author is anonymous; their force wanes once identity appears. The memoir of Jan Diaz—said to be born at Bourges in 1807, the son of a Spanish prisoner—might have misled a future biographer: it named Bourges professors and schoolmates like Lousteau and Bianchon, included early elegies and sonnets, local descriptions, and the tale Carola, and ended with the poet’s lonely death near Château-Chinon—details calculated to move regional feeling.Two hundred cheap copies were printed and most sold locally, showing provincial appetite for letters. After Clagny distributed copies, Dinah (Madame de la Baudraye) still had several wrapped in newspapers. Copies sent to Paris vanished in editors’ piles, and countrymen like Nathan praised the author as if dead. Lousteau, alerted by schoolfellows who remembered no Jan Diaz, investigated and discovered that Jan Diaz was a pseudonym for a woman. The revelation made Madame de la Baudraye the region’s heroine; Sancerre and Bourges hailed her as a potential rival to George Sand. A poem that might have been ridiculed as anonymous was lauded: provincial feeling tended to extremes.The Abbé who had counseled Madame de la Baudraye was dead; otherwise he might have restrained her public ambitions. Three years of unrecognized labor had weighed on Dinah’s soul; she welcomed fame as compensation for disappointed hopes. Poetry and dreams of celebrity, which had sustained her since meeting Anna Grossetête, no longer sufficed, and the Abbé Duret—who had once urged brave suffering—was gone to check her impulses.The priest had tried to reveal her husband’s nature, warning Dinah that weak natures can conceal dark capacities. Finding her husband incapable of love, she denied his capacity to hate. “Do not confound hatred and vengeance,” the Abbé had said: hatred is petty, vengeance more calculating. He warned her to beware offending Monsieur de la Baudraye; he would forgive an infidelity he could exploit but would be implacable if wounded in his pride.As the countryside praised Madame de la Baudraye as Jan Diaz, “little La Baudraye” felt his glory mortally struck—he alone knew the origin of Paquita la Sevillane. When that poem was mentioned, everyone pitied Dinah, and women rushed to condole with the one they had long felt oppressed by; she appeared elevated locally. Outwardly the shriveled husband showed no sign, but Dinah sometimes perceived an icy venom beneath his increased politeness. When she demanded an explanation, his cold impassibility prevailed. She raged, reproached him for eleven wasted years, staged a dramatic scene—and, ashamed of being bested, vowed never to write again. She kept that vow, and desolation spread through the Sancerrois: “Why did Madame de la Baudraye compose no more verses?” people asked.In social life, Dinah had no enemies; visits multiplied, and the presiding judge’s wife and other bourgeoises sought favor by paying court. Dinah’s nine years leading local opposition made her the object of lavish praise under the new epithet “Superior Woman,” replacing older local nicknames.Weary from brambles of disappointment and dark longings, Dinah fell into another trap. Observing Monsieur de Clagny’s self-sacrifice—his refusal of a Paris post favored by his family—she convinced herself he loved her and overcame repulsion. A Sancerre coalition formed to secure Clagny’s election as deputy; Dinah dreamed of following him to Paris. But the hoped-for hundred and fifty votes dwindled to fifty: jealousy and opposition, particularly from President de Boirouge and Monsieur Gravier, who suspected Clagny’s ascendancy in Dinah’s heart, and the machinations of a young sous-préfet who wanted promotion, defeated them. The sous-préfet, rewarded with a préfet’s post elsewhere, lamented he had not pleased Madame de la Baudraye.Despite outward calm—two mismatched, resigned people maintaining propriety—a yoke clung to Dinah. Why did she long to throw off the mask worn for twelve years? Many conjure illusions of mastering a husband or being loved; Dinah, having little impulse to break duty, did not even enough care for Clagny to forgive his defeat. The move to the Château d’Anzy and arranging her treasures in Philibert de Lorme’s splendid surroundings occupied her for months and gave her leisure to plan a decisive action, later explained by gossip.Struck by Lousteau’s reputation with women, Dinah set out to know him. She read his works and, more for his conquests than his talent, desired to attract him. She proposed Sancerre should elect one of Paris’s great men; Gatien Boirouge wrote to Bianchon, claiming kinship, and an old friend of Madame Lousteau was enlisted to stir the journalist’s ambition by suggesting Sancerre intended to elect a distinguished Parisian. Dinah wanted to meet illustrious men and give her fall the lustre of fame.Lousteau and Bianchon did not respond at once—Bianchon could not leave his chair—until September, at vintage time, when both arrived during the harvest; no public demonstration could be held. “We have fallen flat,” said Lousteau. By 1836 Lousteau, worn by Paris struggle, looked older than his years: bald, adopting a Byronic air to mask decay, misanthropic yet with eyes still flashing. He dressed fashionably to hide lost youth. Horace Bianchon, decorated with the Legion of Honor, was fat, burly, patriarchal, a hard-working philosopher with calm air. Their prosaic reception at the inn lasted all morning; only by chance Monsieur de Clagny learned of their arrival. Madame de la Baudraye, desperate, sent Gatien—who had no vineyards—to beg them to spend a few days at Château d’Anzy. Monsieur Gravier, the public prosecutor, the presiding judge, and Gatien organized a banquet for the town’s literati.Learning the beautiful Madame de la Baudraye was Jan Diaz, the Parisians went to Anzy in Gatien’s wagonette. Gatien praised his heroine as the handsomest local woman and a Superior Woman who might charm Paris. Bianchon and the mischievous journalist were secretly surprised when they found Dinah on the château steps in thin black cashmere framed by curls; the costume masked romantic affectations, and her figure and eyes suggested weariness. In Le Berry this artistic dress concealed the Superior Woman’s romantic pretensions.Putting on solemn faces, the Parisians listened to Dinah’s set speech of thanks for relieving her monotony. Walking them round the flowered lawn, she explained provincial life’s aridity: a choice between despair and dull resignation, producing etiolated flowers of stagnant minds. She described provincial women’s occupations—jam-making, washing, piano drudgery, embroidering, dances with the same partners, solemn walks, polite calls, and endless talk of gowns—and warned that what Paris initially mocks, it often courts; a country woman’s attachment can become an all-consuming fate. Country-bred women, little observed, nurture secret passions that can culminate in sudden catastrophe; Parisian coquetry is unknown there.Lousteau agreed that provincial women harbor surprises and that poets should not vilify them. Bianchon asked the cause of provincial woes; Dinah blamed Paris’s centralizing influence, which creates a dead uniformity of manners. Lousteau suggested a federation to recover individuality; Bianchon called it impractical. Dinah praised England for preserving country life—while still faulting its hypocrisies—and argued the English aristocracy’s residence in the country preserved individual charm.Madame Piedefer, a tall, red-faced chatterbox, arrived and was gently mocked by Lousteau; Dinah curbed her loquacity. Etienne tried to recover footing, but Dinah’s advances shifted to Bianchon; later she relented to Lousteau again. Great meanness can be done for small ends: proud Dinah suddenly remembered her album of autographs, an oblong book mostly blank yet already prized—its pages filled by the Baronne de Fontaine with Rossini, Meyerbeer, Victor Hugo, Lamartine, Béranger, George Sand, Scribe, Berlioz; Monsieur de Clagny had added a song by Lacenaire and a note from Napoleon; Monsieur Gravier had secured Mademoiselle Mars. Dinah, the only person for ten leagues with such a book, begged her guests to enrich it.Bianchon wrote an aphorism about the populace offering absolution for every crime and signed J. B. DE CLAGNY; he added a reflection distinguishing Napoleon and a water-carrier and invoking Nature, signing H. BIANCHON. Lousteau, amused, declared he would second any man brave enough to plead for the Monarchy; then, deeming his name insignificant, asked time to improvise something biting. Dinah, hopeful, handed him the book.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At five the next morning everyone at the Château d’Anzy was astir. Little La Baudraye had organized a day’s shooting for his Parisians—less as sport than to display twelve hundred acres he meant to reclaim, a costly project promising a large annual return.“Do you know why the Public Prosecutor has not come out with us?” Gatien Boirouge asked Monsieur Gravier. Gravier said Lebas had to sit for minor cases. Gatien scoffed: his father told him Lebas would not join early because Monsieur de Clagny had begged him as deputy. Gravier looked startled; Bianchon and Lousteau urged not to meddle, but Gatien fretted. He could not imagine Madame de la Baudraye unguarded with de Clagny. He feared Clagny’s eloquence and ambition might seduce Dinah, who disliked Sancerre and dreamed of Paris. Lousteau teased and recommended they not trouble themselves, yet proposed a plot: after dinner tell lurid stories of murderous wives to watch Dinah’s and de Clagny’s faces.At a copse Gravier went off with the others; when they reached a clearing, Bianchon suggested waiting for him. Gatien, certain Gravier would return to the château within twenty minutes, checked his watch and fretted that Dinah might be alone with de Clagny. The men argued playfully about ambition, virtue, and Paris; Lousteau mocked provincial prudery and promised to test Dinah’s innocence by provoking reaction with dramatic tales.That evening Lousteau returned an album to Dinah with an elegy—“Spleen”—in which he posed as a blighted poet. Dinah looked pitying; de Clagny scowled at what he thought sham melancholy. He sat to play backgammon with the cure; Gravier placed a lamp to light Dinah’s embroidery. The conspirators sat near. Dinah told Lousteau the basket she worked was for Monsieur de la Baudraye. Conversation turned to literature and morality: Lousteau mocked Parisian disbelief in women’s virtue; de Clagny complained of Parisian literature’s preoccupation with adultery.Etienne (Lousteau) launched an extravagant defense: ban every book referring to illicit love and you must burn Homer, Dante, Rousseau—most literature would go. He argued judges always represent established interests; they might condemn the novel or the miracle—Pilate, Anytus, modern prosecutors all act to enforce order. In their view Socrates and Jesus were criminals. To call religions or revolutions impostures is to think like a public prosecutor. De Clagny called this blasphemy; Etienne allowed the charge but not malicious intent, admitting many like him would make governance impossible. Madame Piedefer made faces; Bianchon warned Lousteau not to unsettle the company.To the promised revenge tales. Lousteau recited a story told by Charles Nodier about a Chevalier de Beauvoir: after the 18th Brumaire a Maille envoy, arrested on landing, was confined in the Castle of l’Escarpe. Treated kindly by the commandant, he won the governor’s wife’s fancy. Whether by imprudence or fate he was fettered in a dungeon, fed on black bread. A turnkey, bribed, supplied him a file and knotted rope; Beauvoir filed the bars, pretended escape, then, when the guard returned, killed him with the iron bar he’d filed, donned his clothes, mimicked his gait, and walked out among unsuspecting warders. The tale chilled some listeners but left de Clagny and Dinah unmoved.Bianchon then told “La Grande Bretèche,” the Spanish grandee walled in a cupboard, finishing to dramatic effect; Madame de la Baudraye admired a woman’s courage to risk all for love. Gravier offered a Spanish anecdote: once in Madrid a surgeon-major was blindfolded, taken to deliver a woman in secret; though the infant was dead he saved the mother. Later the distraught lover carried off the body; the surgeon, haunted, was ultimately shown an amputated arm with a telltale mole and was afterward stabbed to death by the lady’s husband. Gravier added that under Ferdinand VII he’d known a grandee with a motionless arm and a lovely wife—Spain bred strange passions.De Clagny followed with a French horror: a Tours woman, once devout, whose husband vanished amid the Loire army’s disbandment, confessed to bleeding him to death, salting and packing the body into casks, and throwing pieces into the Loire; the pickled head was discovered by the examining judge. She was condemned and executed; earlier repentance had softened public feeling. The company reflected on the hidden tragedies of private life and the difficulty of judging crimes between spouses.Later, the bachelors gathered in a passage by candlelight. Gravier revealed a scheme: they would seal Madame de la Baudraye’s and the Public Prosecutor’s doors to detect any nocturnal meeting. Like thieves, barefoot and cautious, Bianchon, Lousteau, Gravier, and Gatien stuck hairs across the two doors with wax—a common country-house trick—and agreed to inspect them at five. In the morning the hairs on both doors remained intact. Lousteau, surprised and delighted, declared this changed everything: if Dinah were virtuous, she was worth pursuing. He resolved to press his suit.In the garden Lousteau found Madame de la Baudraye and engaged her. She invited him to La Baudraye to see provincial life and called provincials simple but sincere. Etienne confessed he’d only teased Gatien into revealing his adoration; her silence at dinner had looked like indiscretion, but he assured her innocence was proven. “If you fancied the magistrate,” he said, “you’d have lost my respect. I love perfection; I cannot believe the Public Prosecutor matches you.” His frankness and portrait of Paris—its daring axioms, decadence, and witticisms—intrigued Dinah. He spoke of having sought pleasure but never love; perhaps he had defects that made him less lovable. Dinah warmed to him, intoxicated when he disparaged a celebrated woman of Le Berry and implied Dinah could rival her. She spent one of her happiest days with him and Bianchon.Their warmth spread: townsfolk exaggerated her reception of Etienne and word reached Sancerre. That evening sixteen people came to Anzy—family coaches, wagonettes, hired horses. Dinah, expecting triumph, lit the huge drawing‑room and displayed the furniture; the Parisians watched guests’ manners. Visitors flaunted ancestral laces and faded honors; the Présidente Boirouge complained of chest pains and Bianchon prescribed “tea after dinner” as an English remedy. Conversation turned to Bianchon’s fame and a proposed candidacy; Lousteau teased the idea of Bianchon in politics.A footman brought letters and proofs; Dinah found a printers’ wrapper and a folded serial romance proof—“Olympia, or Roman Revenge.” Lousteau read a fragment—brigands, vanished heroes, a Duchess forgetting a glove—and mocked the lack of “local color”; Bianchon defended censored writers. The provincial company listened, half scandalized, half entertained.Thus the evening passed at Anzy—stories, parodies, flirtations and a mingling of Parisian wit with provincial simplicities, while beneath the gaiety the bachelors’ scheme, jealous suspicions, and Lousteau’s courtship quietly set the season’s intrigues in motion.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The Public Prosecutor’s wife, erect and martial, nudged a smile at her neighbor as if to show she understood the scene. The Mayoress urged Lousteau to continue reading, and he forced a smile, called “Attention!” and resumed the torn proof. The fragment opened with Cardinal Borborigano confronting the Duchess and urging her to flee, while she retorted that the Duke of Bracciano would reappear when she wished to prove he was alive. The name “Cardinal Borborigano” delighted Bianchon; Dinah, pitying the eighteen faces watching Lousteau, glimpsed the whole melodrama: Rome, a presumed murder, a brazen wife sleeping on a volcano of crime.Lousteau read on, elaborating the romance’s tropes: a French Adolphe embraced by the Duchess, Rinaldo the brigand as a melodramatic conspirator, and the whole theatrical parade of Victor Hugo–style excess. The company urged him on. He paused to explain printers’ proofs—waste sheets printed one side only, made-up sheets, folio marks and tailpieces—and from these clues deduced the physical make-up of the book (four duodecimo volumes, second edition) and that the action was edging toward a climax despite missing pages.He translated a jumble of directions and quotes: a key swapped by Rinaldo, terror over a lost key leading to death, a tailpiece by Normand cut by Duplat. Skipping torn leaves, he jumped to a later full sheet marked IV.; J. There Rinaldo steals into the Baths of Vespasian, finds and uses a concealed door, then discovers he is trapped—what he thought a cellar of family treasures becomes a prison. Lousteau mocked the blunt Empire-era prose—panic expressed as a “hollow roar like that of a hyaena”—and contrasted it with modern writing, which elaborates inner life.Bianchon defended contemporary criticism and taste: readers now demand invention, style, thought and feeling; critics attack obvious defects because they envy the creators. Lousteau, heedless of cautions to be concise, pushed on. A groan answered Rinaldo’s cry; a voice called “Santa Maria!” and claimed to be the Duke of Bracciano. In pitch darkness the Duke guided Rinaldo until iron bars confirmed a cage. The Duke had been confined thirty months—kept upright by a counterpoise beneath the Duchess’s bedroom so she could taunt him when she embraced her lover. Tricked in a duel of wits, he had his poison shown but withheld, and was forced to watch his wife with her lover.The Duke begged Rinaldo for alliance. Rinaldo, the brigand, revealed files and pincers he had used to file through one bar; he lent a dagger. The spring clattered, the Duke sprang up, and Rinaldo—astonished at the Duke’s haste to leave—mused that he had sworn never to harm a woman. Lousteau added more printerly notes—another tailpiece, a Cupid on a snail, a blank page 230—and reached the fragmentary “Conclusion.” This portion was brief and voluptuous: the Duchess, bathed and goddess-like, finds Adolphe reclining; they profess love, and she presses the springs that open the hidden passage. Lousteau lamented the foreman’s tearing off of the rest of the proof but pronounced the author full of promise.Reactions varied. Gatien Boirouge and Monsieur Gravier admitted confusion; Lousteau defended the piece as typical of Empire romance. Monsieur Gravier thought the brigand’s courtly talk betrayed an author ignorant of Italy; Madame Gorju scorned the fiction and praised Bianchon’s prescriptions. Bianchon, glancing at Mademoiselle Euphemie Gorju, counseled leniency: twenty pages out of a thousand proved little. Lousteau and Bianchon mused on authorship—perhaps a woman, like “Madame Hadot,” given women’s reputed extravagance (Frankenstein, Leone Leoni, Ann Radcliffe were cited)—and Dinah glowered when Monsieur de Clagny used those examples, feeling slighted.Little La Baudraye, practical about revenge, observed that social punishment often suffices: a cuckolded woman may be reduced to penury and ostracism rather than slain; loss of finery and respect constitutes true misery. Madame de la Baudraye added that a woman might still find happiness if she had a lover. Monsieur de la Baudraye’s deadpan reply and the exchange between Bianchon and Lousteau produced wry asides. Dinah’s small laugh and the way her eyes met Louise’s remarks revealed to Bianchon intimations of Madame de la Baudraye’s inner life—wrinkles, a knowing smile, a reserve of experience—things the provincials missed.Bianchon saw in a single glance what might otherwise take years to unfold: Dinah’s luminous look at Lousteau signified a readiness to be moved. Lousteau, who had long been a Parisian poet, journalist and libertine, displayed that tinsel of intelligence and freedom that attracted women who wished to feel chosen and remembered. He noticed Dinah’s superiority to the provincial visitors—her dress, grace, complexion by candlelight against shy, ill-dressed faces—and found himself drawn to her personality as much as to the château’s antique beauty. Madame de la Baudraye’s audacious prophecy—that Lousteau would be the happy man of Dinah’s choice—suddenly seemed plausible.That night, as the party thinned and the Sancerre visitors prepared for their hour’s drive, only Monsieur de Clagny, Monsieur Lebas, Gatien and Monsieur Gravier remained to sleep at Anzy. Lousteau and Bianchon shared a room; Bianchon told him plainly, “You will be the happy man of this woman’s choice,” explaining how alluring a reputed lover can be: women like to feel they will be remembered longer than others, to appeal to experience, and to have a lover’s reputation confirm his value. Bianchon predicted Dinah’s beauty would be enhanced by love and pragmatically noted that a child would secure her fortune. Lousteau, half in jest, half in design, called making love to her perhaps an act of virtue and let Bianchon hint at plans for the next day.Next morning, Madame de la Baudraye planned a trip: she would take Bianchon as far as Cosne, drop her mother at La Baudraye, go on with the Parisians to Cosne and return alone with Etienne. She was elegantly dressed; Lousteau wore his studied Parisian “war-paint.” Monsieur de Clagny and Monsieur Gravier watched the Parisians climb into the carriage feeling out of place. Gatien returned prudently from the stables and joined the chaise as escort; Dinah sighed that she had not had three hours’ liberty in thirteen years. Bianchon hatched a simple ruse—ask Gatien to fetch lecture notes at Anzy so Dinah could be rid of him—which delighted Madame de la Baudraye. The drive between Anzy and Sancerre, with views of the Loire, proved agreeable.On the road Dinah and Lousteau discussed love in theory. He spoke in a tone of refined corruption: love obeys no law; it varies with character and circumstance; any woman, however firm, might be swept away in a few hours by an internal whirlwind known to God alone. He declared this idea the key to the adventures they’d been discussing for days.Throughout the reading and conversation Lousteau’s literary asides punctuated the evening. He mocked careless printers for lacking correctors, contrasted terse Empire outlines—Lubin loves Toinette, Toinette refuses, Lubin kills and is guillotined—with modern writers who reveal inner life, and teased contemporary quarrels between critics and creators. Bianchon agreed: readers now demand the five literary senses and punish works lacking them; critics, lacking inventiveness, denounce the incomplete. The Sancerre guests took offense at these put-downs, and Madame Piedefer urged pacification; provincials quickly anger when they suspect ridicule.By the time the visitors left, amusement had shifted to attraction: Lousteau’s interest in Dinah appeared natural. The evening closed with gossip, literary theory, and an implicit movement of flirtation. The reading had unveiled a melodramatic tale of entrapment and revenge—the Duke caged and mocked, the brigand’s reluctant aid—while the company displayed small-town temper, pretension, and social mechanics of flirtation. As they prepared to travel the next day, Lousteau’s claim that love is the key to all such adventures left both women and men reflecting on the thin line between plotting in fiction and plotting in life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For three days Dinah’s imagination brimmed with perilous possibilities fed by Parisian talk; Lousteau watched, ready to seize any moment her hidden readiness overcame irresolution.She planned to show La Baudraye to her two visitors. Gatien rode off on errands; Madame Piedefer went shopping; Dinah went to Cosne with Lousteau and Bianchon. Lousteau sat beside her while Bianchon rode behind; both men pitied a noble nature trapped in vulgar surroundings. Bianchon mocked the local magistrates and Gatien, bolstering Lousteau’s case by scorning the provincials.Crossing the Loire, Bianchon told Dinah she had been drawn to Etienne; he loved her but was frivolous, poor, and bound to Paris while she was rooted in Sancerre. He counseled prudence: make Lousteau a friend, accept limited visits, and secure beauty, happiness, and fortune. He warned bluntly that without love she would grow old and hideous, an argument that unhinged her defences. Though she protested she would not be a passerby’s mistress, Etienne’s arrival interrupted her dissembling; he seemed to study Cosne as he drew near.Bianchon prepared to leave, offering Dinah a final, almost paternal blessing and allowing Lousteau to take her hand. The touch thrilled Dinah; Lousteau felt triumphant, reading small gestures—adjusting her frock, fretting over muslin—as signs of choice. He shook Bianchon’s hand with studied indifference as the older man departed.Back at La Baudraye Lousteau abruptly declared his love. Dinah trembled and insisted she would not entrust the best of herself to a man who would see her only as transient pleasure. Gatien’s return forced the moment: Lousteau demanded Gatien go back to the inn, then deliberately messed Dinah’s muslin so she would not be presentable—an outrageous “trick” he called a way to spare her reputation. Baffled and hurt, Gatien was sent away; humiliated, Dinah fled in tears. Lousteau followed, threw himself at her feet, and vowed devotion.Madame Piedefer arrived; Lousteau suggested the mother find another dress. Hearing Gatien’s return, Dinah and her mother withdrew to change into something warmer, as Bianchon had advised. Gatien, bewildered by conflicting explanations—that the muslin was torn on a nail or trampled by Lousteau—felt mocked. Dinah reappeared in velvet, forced to hide coldness behind Lousteau’s presence, and was escorted to the carriage with him. Lousteau professed to renounce Paris and linger for her—rhetoric that, whether sincere or not, struck Dinah and made her laugh in contrition.That evening the town whispered: she had gone out in muslin and returned in velvet.
