The story became scandal; the Presidente Boirouge thundered that Dinah’s poetry proved she must live what she wrote; others muttered that virtue lasts until happiness arrives, then Calumny follows. Monsieur de Clagny defended her, arguing she and Lousteau pursued some great project, but was mocked.October’s beauty aided Dinah: as Bianchon predicted, genuine passion bloomed. Within a month she changed—alive, discovering dormant qualities, feeling Lousteau as an angel and delighting in being loved for herself. Lousteau, who prized her artlessness, played the consummate seducer, supplying every practiced flourish of passion and striving to imprint himself on her memory. Their long walks and evenings deepened mutual feeling; when lovers exhaust every step of the duet yet remain enamored, the love becomes real.Lousteau’s time was limited—he planned to leave Anzy in early November. The day before his departure the master showed them the château’s front bearing the arms of Uxelles; he wanted to replace them with his own coat of arms, a sign of pride. Lousteau teased, and the old man’s remark that Dinah was still young—there was time—passed with a smile Lousteau did not fully grasp.They postponed farewells; Dinah accompanied Lousteau as far as Cosne and secured many solemn promises. Back at La Baudraye she told Clagny, Gatien, and Gravier—each in turn—that Lousteau had taught her she had not been loved for herself, and she delivered a noble speech on man and base passions. She accepted Clagny’s steadfast devotion as confidant, lavishing on him the marks of friendship a woman can give to a suitor willing to be an adoring, submissive companion.Thus Dinah’s brief, passionate conversion—engineered and complicated by Lousteau’s artful audacity and Bianchon’s counsel—transformed her social standing and inner life, provoking scandal in Sancerre even as it led her to embrace a new identity shaped by affection and theatrical devotion.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In Paris again, Lousteau soon lost the afterglow of his happy time at the Château d’Anzy. The reason was simple: he lived by his pen.After the bourgeois triumph, to live by the pen was almost penal servitude—daily production, or the appearance of it, with imitation as costly as originality. Etienne’s weekly newspaper piece came each Monday like Sisyphus’s stone; besides that he wrote for literary magazines, two novels a year for weeklies, and miscellaneous articles. Facility and carelessness made him little more than a scrivener—respectable enough socially but cut off from claims to a higher literary position. In Paris, a man who writes but will not press for greater aims becomes merely a journalist, a hack.His life nevertheless had pleasures. Blue-stockings, actresses, publishers, and authors made much of his ready pen. Lousteau lived cheaply in the outward sense—no ostentation beyond paying rent—but he kept theatre boxes and a social calendar full of dinners, invitations, visits, and lounging. The sale of reviewed books paid for his gloves; vanity paid in drawings and gifts. His serial, novels, and articles were the tax for this easy life, yet it had been ten years in coming.Known and liked for mischief and good humor, Lousteau let himself float with the stream and never planned for the future. He led a small circle of newcomers, preserved fifteen-year habits with old companions, and indulged his wit. He earned seven to eight hundred francs a month, which he found insufficient for a prodigality peculiar to the impecunious; he was as hard up as when he first came to Paris and thought, “If I had but five hundred francs a month, I should be rich!”The cause was simple: handsome ground-floor rooms with a garden in the Rue des Martyrs, splendidly furnished. He had secured them in 1833 by a deal with an upholsterer, but they were let for twelve hundred francs, and the quarterly rent cleaned him out. He took hackney cabs freely, spent extravagant sums on breakfasts, smoked costly cigars, and never refused a mistress a dinner or a dress. He lived on future earnings and could no more find a hundred francs on his chimney-piece now than when he first earned two hundred.Sometimes tired of such vicissitudes and bored by perpetual amusement, Lousteau would sit in his small garden—no larger than a dinner-table—and ask intimates like Nathan or Bixiou what would be the end of them; white hairs were giving them respectful hints. Nathan joked about marriage as drama; Bixiou teased about each man having his own transient “Florine.” Etienne’s Florine was Madame Schontz.Madame Schontz, a Loretta near Notre-Dame de Lorette, was pretty and took pride in teasing friends by boasting of a wit for her lover; she claimed a real interest in Lousteau. Such bohemian penury mingled with Parisian luxury inevitably affected Dinah’s life—the provincial girl who wrote to him from Sancerre. Lousteau could laugh about his Baroness with friends and even with Madame Schontz; to those who see such behavior as mean, explanation is futile.When Bixiou asked what he had done in Sancerre, Etienne offered a witty, half-ironical account. Ten days later a letter from Sancerre arrived. He admired the phrasing, then threw the ten sheets into the fire: “That woman was born to reel off copy!” He was not much afraid of Madame Schontz, who loved him for himself; he had supplanted a friend in the heart of a marquise who sometimes dropped in at his rooms veiled and rifled his drawers.Another packet came—a rapturous eight leaves—but thinking he heard the Marquise, he tossed the proofs of affection unread into the fire. Madame Schontz, entering to find hampers from Sancerre, praised the Tenth Muse’s domestic talents. Lousteau wrote a clever, insidious letter to Dinah—one suited to third-party readers rather than transparent feeling—and by December had ceased to read her letters; they lay in a heap in an unlocked drawer beneath his shirts, their scent lingering.Chance then came in a form such bohemians should have seized. In mid-December Madame Schontz, who took a real interest in Etienne, begged him to call one morning on business. She told him bluntly: “My dear fellow, you have a chance of marrying.” He joked, but she explained. A young lady had “got into trouble” without her mother’s knowledge; her father, Maître Cardot, an honest notary, had kept the scandal quiet. He wanted to marry her off within a fortnight. He would give her a fortune of one hundred and fifty thousand francs and add a hundred thousand “under the rose” to cover damages. The family were established citizens of the Rue des Lombards.Etienne objected: why did the lover not marry her? “Dead,” Madame Schontz replied—the man had died of pleurisy after leaving the theatre. He’d been a penniless head-clerk who had entrapped the daughter to marry into the business. Cardot sought a man of at least thirty and would be proud to have a famous son-in-law—someone like Etienne. If he married, he would pay his debts, have twelve thousand francs a year, and a fatherless child without trouble. There was fifty thousand francs of income in the house and the value of the connection; in time Etienne might expect fifteen thousand more. Cardot was Camusot’s brother-in-law, and such connections promised prospects: a sinecure as a librarian or an appointment through Camusot’s influence, and even a stake in a newspaper to yield income—ten thousand francs a year was floated as possible from combined investments and patronage. Madame Schontz asked bluntly, “Can you do better for yourself?”She acknowledged the downside: a formidable mother-in-law. Madame Cardot was a bigot with lips like faded pink ribbons, prudish enough to destroy her daughter figuratively if she learned the truth. Cardot planned a respectable front—offering a house in the Rue Saint-Lazare bought at the July overthrow; selling it would give Etienne a place to come and go, see the daughter, and appear to possess property in Madame Cardot’s eyes. By Camusot’s influence Etienne might secure a public appointment; if he backed a newspaper, he could profit. Madame Schontz argued that marrying a “consolable widow”—a polite term for a woman with a past—was expedient and fashionable. She urged Lousteau to dine with Malaga the next day, where he would meet Cardot; Malaga had let the secret out and thus the notary would be easier.The plain, blunt language appealed to Etienne. He agreed to think it over and then, when pressed, accepted. That evening he wrote a long letter to the Marquise explaining reasons for marriage: constant poverty, torpor of imagination, white hairs, moral and physical exhaustion—four pages of argument. “As to Dinah, I will send her a circular announcing the marriage,” he thought. Fearful the marriage might fail, he became eager; he was civil to the notary and assumed an impressive demeanor. “I knew monsieur your father,” he told Cardot, “at Florentine’s… My follies are past. To get on now a man must be free from debt, have a good income, a wife, and a family. If I pay taxes enough to qualify me, I may be a deputy yet.”Cardot appreciated this profession of reform. Lousteau’s familiarity with the family past and apparent reformation put the notary at ease; soon he was presented as purchaser of the Rue Saint-Lazare house and dined with the family three days later.Cardot’s house near the Place du Châtelet betrayed economical virtue: gilding under green gauze, holland-covered furniture—wealth disguised by thrift and boredom. Lousteau noticed that Cardot’s real life was elsewhere. He saw a tall, fair girl with blue eyes, shy and languishing—Félicie Cardot. The elder brother, a clerk destined to succeed his father, took a fancy to him; there was also a twelve-year-old sister. Lousteau, adopting a Jesuitical air, played Monarchist and Churchman for the mother’s benefit—smooth, deliberate, complimentary.Within three weeks, at his fourth dinner, Félicie, who had watched him shyly, brought him a cup of coffee while he stood in the window recess and whispered through tears: “I will devote my whole life, monsieur, to thanking you for your sacrifice in favor of a poor girl—” Lousteau was touched; her look, accent, and attitude had feeling. He pressed her hand and thought, “She would make a good man happy.”Madame Cardot regarded her future son-in-law as a man of prospects and was delighted by his moral tone. Etienne, prompted by the wily notary, had pledged that he had no natural children or ties that could endanger Félicie. The mother, prudish and cautious, warned that a woman who sinned against duty clung to her sin; such a fall made her ruinous to others—an admonition Lousteau treated lightly and later joked about to Malaga.By now Lousteau had assumed airs of importance. His life had purpose at last: a house in the Rue Saint-Lazare, marriage to a charming woman, twelve thousand francs a year, respectable connections, and room for ambition. The young lady loved him; he would be linked to several families and could turn social ease into social position. He had seized a chance to exit penury, secure a roof and income, and convert bohemian ease into establishment—though not without costs: concealments, compromises, and the need to placate a rigid, suspicious mother-in-law.The narrative leaves Etienne in the confident flush of impending fortune, already assuming the roles of husband and gentleman that his friends and new relations expect of him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Cardot had borrowed an illustrated Gil Blas as a pretext to inspect Lousteau’s bachelor rooms and judge him as a suitor for her niece Félicie. Others, like Madame Camusot, had spoken ill of the journalist; Lousteau, who mocked the bourgeoisie, treated the visit as ordinary.One January day Madame Cardot and Félicie went to Rue des Martyrs to return the volumes and see Lousteau’s lodging. The porter was out; his daughter took the gold piece Madame Cardot slipped her and handed over the key. Lousteau, returning from breakfast, fancied he saw Dinah, the Baroness de la Baudraye, arrive by hired coach with trunks and a child. She announced she had left her country house to be with him, calling him “my love,” and claiming to be his wife and the child’s mother. Taken aback but practical, Lousteau tried to soothe her and conceal the situation as guests appeared: quick-witted Pamela, fourteen, and then Madame Cardot and Félicie, who slipped into the bedroom.Madame Cardot made a scene, denouncing Lousteau as unfit for her niece and vowing Félicie would never marry him. Dinah wept and clung to Lousteau, who whispered comforts that reassured the provincial woman. Soon Lousteau conceived a stratagem: if Didine believed he intended to marry Félicie, she might renounce her claim for his prospects. He even wrote to his friend Bixiou to play a severe elder and frighten the Muse of Sancerre into yielding.Meanwhile Lousteau installed Dinah, charmed her with attentions so she felt adored, and set Pamela to be her waiting-maid; Pamela ran to fetch dinner and borrow plates from Madame Schontz, who hurried to warn Cardot. The rooms’ superficially elegant air hid Lousteau’s poverty, but Dinah, intoxicated by romance, dreamed of domestic happiness.Bixiou arrived mocking and contemptuous, painting the practical ruin of Lousteau’s course: no steady income or savings, destroyed prospects for social, political, and financial advancement, risk of scandal over an affair and a child. Lousteau, stung, still insisted he loved Dinah and preferred her and fatherhood to fortune.The argument closed when Madame Schontz returned with news: Cardot had preserved Félicie’s prospects by placing her with the late head-clerk’s deputy, so Lousteau’s chance at a wealthy marriage was gone. Furious at losing fortune and a respectable match, and with rivals neutralized, Lousteau devoted himself entirely to Dinah, keeping her dependent by amusements and tender attentions. Thus began the familiar Parisian pattern: his selfish, calculated passion and her naive devotion produced an intoxicating intimacy in which passion drowned prudence and prophecy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This winter in Paris meant to Madame de la Baudraye what October had been at Sancerre. Etienne Lousteau, eager to make “his wife” a Parisienne, paired their honeymoon with theatre evenings where Dinah sat in the stage box. Initially clinging to provincial modesty, she soon yielded to Lousteau’s vanity and instruction in fashion and society. Her Sancerre air vanished; his friends praised his conquest. Etienne, writing little and running into debt, affected indifference; Dinah, proud, bought clothes from her savings and fancied she had cost him nothing. After three months she loved the opera, knew the plays and actors, and adapted to the sharp, witty Parisian world where cleverness reigns.One morning, reading Lousteau’s papers, Dinah noticed a line that recalled Sancerre: “Monsieur le Baron de Clagny... appointed Deputy Public Prosecutor.” A journalist sneered that the lawyer loved her; she only said, “Poor man! He is following me.” At that dazzling stage of passion, familiarity and freshness coexisted; enchantment reigned without collision. Dinah spoke love’s hyperbole and retained modesty and ambition in intimacy; Lousteau involuntarily held her in high esteem. “There is in Dinah,” he told Bixiou, “the stuff to make both a Ninon and a De Staël.”When Dinah’s pregnancy began to show, she planned to withdraw but insisted on attending a Nathan first night—the literary ceremonial. Dressed perfectly, she was abandoned by Lousteau during the evening and found herself exposed in the box. Monsieur de Clagny entered. He greeted her warmly but warned that a woman living openly with a man of letters invites curiosity and loss of respect; perhaps she would be happier at home with her mother. Dinah replied she had renounced society for love and was content. Clagny, moved, wondered whether Lousteau appreciated such sacrifice. Dinah invited him to see for himself; he left to avoid scandal when Lousteau returned, who boasted, “You are a greater hit than the piece.” The brief triumph pleased Dinah, but three days later reality arrived: they were penniless, with two quarters’ rent unpaid and distraint imminent. Dinah repented tempting Etienne into extravagance.Unaccustomed to owing, she learned through Migeon she could pawn jewels; her “uncle” advanced nine hundred francs. She kept three hundred for the baby and gave Lousteau the rest so he could complete a novel for a periodical. “Finish without sacrificing to necessity,” she urged. “Polish the style; work up the subject. I will be housewife and attend to business.” For months Etienne had taken Dinah daily to the Café Riche; she was dismayed to find five hundred francs owing for the last fortnight. He shrugged: “It makes little difference whether we are robbed at a restaurant or by a cook.” She secured a kitchen and two servants’ rooms and wrote to her mother for linen and a loan. Her mother sent two trunks of linen, some plate, and two thousand francs.Ten days after the theatre, Clagny called and found Dinah making a cap; the proud image of a woman at needlework moved him. He saw she intended to be Lousteau’s guardian spirit, to lead him to a nobler road. Dinah recognized her husband’s troubles sprang from indolence and lack of will; she strove to make him happy by economy and method. “His happiness will be my absolution,” she told Clagny.But Clagny, deceived at first by Lousteau’s ingratiating manner, gradually saw the truth. Between April and July, while Dinah expected confinement, she discovered why Lousteau had not triumphed over poverty: he was idle and weak-willed. Genius requires will; talent without it remains unproductive. Men who lead their age deny themselves and keep their faculties ready. Lousteau’s cigarette habit dulled him; criticism as a profession—praising or tearing down for convenience—stultified him. He could not bridge the gulf between dexterity and art.Dinah’s intellect quickened and she measured her idol. She saw him scamping work, writing at the last minute. She excused him—“He is a poet!”—and tried to help. Loving him, she sought through her mother to negotiate an allowance from her father without Etienne’s knowledge. A few days before July’s end she crumpled a letter from her mother conveying Monsieur de la Baudraye’s ultimatum: Dinah could live in perfect luxury at Anzy and therefore might return. Lousteau read the letter and, with ominous gallantry, vowed vengeance—words flattering to a woman whose antipathies were flattered.Five days later Bianchon and Duriau were engaged at Lousteau’s; he made a display over the infant’s arrival. Monsieur de Clagny and Madame Piedefer were to be godparents. Dinah gave birth to a son of remarkable beauty. Bianchon and Clagny registered the child as the son of Monsieur and Madame de la Baudraye, unknown to Etienne. Lousteau rushed to a printer and had fifty circulars set up proclaiming: “Madame la Baronne de la Baudraye is happily delivered of a son. Monsieur Etienne Lousteau has the pleasure of informing you.” Clagny found a list of Sancerre names Lousteau planned to notify, confiscated proofs and remaining circulars, and had corrected proofs printed naming Monsieur le Baron de la Baudraye as informing party. Clagny destroyed the originals and intercepted sent copies, recovering thirty and leaving only one of the original notes—sent to Nathan. He implored Nathan to yield the one copy; at first Nathan laughed, but, convinced the notice could damage a woman and child ignorant of their financial and social peril, he surrendered.When the jest was counteracted, Clagny reprimanded Lousteau in Madame Piedefer’s presence and found Etienne fuming. “I did it with a purpose!” Etienne protested. “Monsieur de la Baudraye has sixty thousand francs a year and refuses an allowance; I wished to make him feel the child is in my power.” Clagny coolly replied he had agreed to be godfather to prevent such schemes; if the child were registered as the Baudraye heir, it could inherit entailed estates. Lousteau retorted, “And is the mother to die of hunger?” Clagny promised to transact with Monsieur de la Baudraye. He left chilled; Dinah, his idol, seemed loved for her money; he pitied her.Infant care and Madame Piedefer’s presence hindered writing; Lousteau moved to first-floor rooms. Obliged to attend first nights without Dinah and living apart, freedom proved alluring. He was dragged to jollifications and friends’ mistresses’ houses, seeing brilliantly dressed young women in whom economy seemed treason. Dinah, after nursing for three months, could not compete with such perishable charms.Home life nonetheless had its charms. In three months the mother, Didine the Sancerre cook, and little Pamela transformed the apartment. Lousteau found breakfasts and dinners served with domestic luxury; Dinah anticipated his wishes; he felt king at home. Didine’s affection showed in every trifle. The deceptions of his unreal passion continued.Dinah knew Lousteau’s life threatened ruin to love and household. After ten months she weaned her baby, installed her mother upstairs, and restored family intimacy. Daily domestic intercourse gives a woman power; men left too often to themselves become susceptible to release. Dinah intended to be indispensable and to infuse energy into Lousteau, whose weakness she mistook for security. She suggested subjects, sketched treatments, sometimes wrote whole chapters. Her collaboration revived dying talent; she produced two successful books while keeping her role secret from Madame Piedefer.This galvanism improved pay, and they lived comfortably through 1838. Lousteau grew used to seeing Dinah do his work and paid her nothing—“monkey money.” The more she gave, the more she loved him; she felt she could never give him up.But another child came, and that year proved a trial. Despite precautions, Etienne contracted debts and worked nearly to death to pay them while Dinah was ill; she thought him heroic. Appalled at supporting two women, children, and two maids, he could not continue sustaining them by his pen after failing even to maintain himself. So he let things take their chance and exaggerated home-made love to secure liberty abroad.Dinah endured stoically without adequate support. Belief in his love gave her strength. She worked hard, sacrificing dress—dying gowns black—and risking beauty. Malaga mocked the stench of black; thrift irked Lousteau, who reproached her for dowdiness while secretly benefiting from her sacrifices.She tried to steer this weak man; in doing so she lost some moral lustre; suspicion and reproach led to quarrels and bred disrespect. Lousteau entertained friends—Nathan, Bixiou, Blondet, Finot—whose manners and talk were depraving. They mocked her principles as provincial prudery and preached woman’s superiority. During Carnival 1840 she disguised herself to spy on Etienne at balls and suppers. The morning after Mid-Lent she returned from a fancy-dress ball intending to go to bed; she had gone to watch Lousteau, who, believing her ill, had engaged that evening to Fanny Beaupré. Warned by a friend, he behaved so as to deceive her, and she, eager to be deceived, was duped.Stepping from the hired cab she met Monsieur de la Baudraye; the porter pointed her out. The old man took his wife’s arm and, with cold conjugal authority, confronted Dinah: “So this is you, madame!” To escape Etienne’s eye she had chosen a dress he would not detect; using her mask she slipped away, changed, and went to her mother’s rooms, where she found her husband waiting. Despite assumed dignity she blushed.“What do you want of me, monsieur? Are we not separated forever?” she asked.“Actually, yes. Legally, no,” he answered. Madame Piedefer signalled; Dinah understood his visit was prompted by interest. “Nothing could have brought you here but your own interests,” she said bitterly. “Our interests,” he replied coldly, “for we have two children. Your Uncle Silas Piedefer is dead in New York; after making and losing fortunes, he left some seven or eight hundred thousand francs—some say a million. I am chief in our common interests and act for you.”“In everything that relates to business I trust Monsieur de Clagny,” she retorted. “He knows the law; come to terms with him.”“I have no occasion for Monsieur de Clagny to take my children from you,” Monsieur de la Baudraye said.“Your children!” Dinah laughed. “Your children to whom you have sent no sou!” The outburst froze under his coolness. “I do not intend to part from them. I shall take them to Anzy, if only to save them from seeing their mother disguised like a—”“Silence!” Dinah cried. “What do you want of me?”“A power of attorney to receive our Uncle Silas’ property.” Dinah wrote two lines to Clagny and asked her husband to return in the afternoon.At five o’clock Clagny—now Attorney-General—explained her position and undertook to bargain with the old man. Monsieur de la Baudraye, needing her power of attorney to deal as he wished, bought it by concessions: he agreed to allow her ten thousand francs a year so long as she resided in Paris; the children were to be placed in his custody at six; and Clagny extracted payment in advance.Little La Baudraye came jaunty to say good-bye in a white india-rubber overcoat. He resembled the La Baudraye of 1836 so that Dinah despaired. From the garden, where he smoked, Lousteau watched him cross the forecourt. This short scene altered the writer’s private calculus. Their life together had cost both sides; in moral accounting Lousteau considered himself the creditor. It was a favourable moment to desert her. Tired after three years of a comedy that could not be habit, he hid his weariness and forced smiles like a debtor. The advantages he had expected in the future—an allowance, secured possibilities—had sustained him; when Monsieur de la Baudraye embarked for America, Lousteau ceased to believe in that future.Entering the drawing-room where Dinah had just left her husband, he found her grave. “Etienne,” she said, “do you know my lord proposed that if I wish to return to Anzy during his absence, he hopes my mother’s advice will weigh with me and that I shall go back with my children.” “It is very good advice,” Lousteau replied dryly. His tone struck Dinah, who lived only in love; two tears slipped unnoticed as she wiped them. She confessed she felt she had sold her children by selling the chance to remain, that he would have them at six and she could only see them in Sancerre—torture.Lousteau knelt and kissed her hands, coaxing. “You do not understand me,” he said. “I blame myself; I am not worth such sacrifices. I am second-rate in literature. If I can no longer cut a figure at the bottom of the newspaper, the editors will throw me aside. We tight-rope dancers have no retiring pension! I am forty-two, idle as a marmot. My love can only be fatal to you. At twenty-two I lived on Florine; what was excusable in youth is a disgrace to a man of forty. Out of devotion you wear black and that does me no credit.” Dinah shrugged magnanimously. His prudence, measured like Clagny’s, gratified and staunched her tears. “He loves me for myself alone!” she thought, smiling.After four years their intimacy combined every shade modern society creates: heart and head, passion, caprice, and taste. Lousteau could magnetize Dinah—one look or touch reduced her to obedience. Fascinated by his wit and magnetized by his airs, his vices seemed trivial; she even loved the cigar-smoke drifting into her room. She hated editors who refused Lousteau money and deluded herself that he was writing a remunerative novel rather than merely spinning out debts.This, she argued, is true love: having placed herself outside the pale, why should he not sacrifice some of a man’s honour for her? “We live outside social convention,” she said. “Nathan can accept homage from Florine; why not from me? We will square accounts when we part; only death can part us. We shall be married some day—time enough to part when little La Baudraye is safe back again.” “I never thought of that,” Lousteau said simply, adding to himself, “Time enough to part when little La Baudraye is safe back again.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################From that day Etienne Lousteau lived in luxury; Dinah, at first nights, could hold her own with the best-dressed women in Paris. Lousteau affectingly adopted the air of a man crushed by fate, ruined by Madame de la Baudraye, and pretended despair to elicit pity. He took precautions because of Dinah’s jealousy when accepting invitations, yet remained shamelessly unfaithful. Monsieur de Clagny, in despair at seeing Dinah disgraced though she might have been rich and in the position she had sought, came to warn her — “You are betrayed” — and she only answered, “I know it.”Reduced to silence for a moment, Clagny could still find the words to speak. He was interrupted by Madame de la Baudraye, who asked Lousteau plainly whether he still loved her. “I would lose my soul for you!” he cried, leaping up. The man’s eyes flashed; he trembled like a leaf at being accepted as his idol’s avenger; he felt rapture in the poor joy of being given that role. Dinah explained that such passion was how she loved Lousteau; the lawyer wept; even the stern judge who had once condemned a man to death had tears in his eyes.Yet Lousteau’s satiety — the odious, slow dwindling of illicit passion — betrayed itself in countless little humiliations that Dinah had not perceived until they grew into insurmountable grievances. Finally Madame de la Baudraye understood Lousteau’s character: a poet, defenseless against misfortune, mean from laziness, rather inclined to pleasure, a great cat who could not be hated. She told her mother she would be a mother to him. That resolution showed love turning into burden and toil; the part she assumed became a painful sacrifice rather than joy.Lousteau made that assumption still harder. When he wanted to go out after dinner he played tender comedies, addressing Dinah in words full of devotion; he would take her by the chain and, when he had bruised her, ask, “Did I wound you?” These mock caresses degraded Dinah, who hoped yet for a revival of his love. The mother yielded to the mistress with shameful readiness; she felt herself a mere plaything and at last confessed: “I will be his plaything!” — and found joy in it, a rapture of damnation.A woman of sterner spirit might have preferred solitude, but Dinah feared the emptiness it implied. She preferred the miseries of fierce intimacy to the absence of the bittersweet joys that spring from remorse and struggle. Every night, she awaited Lousteau’s return as a thirsty traveler in a desert longing for the bitter pool; she was not fully alive until she heard his boots and the bell. She tried to restrain him by giving pleasure, hoped to rival his other lovers, and rehearsed partings as if in a drama. A single artless word or kiss would plunge her back into the depths of love. She sometimes contemplated suicide while pacing the small town garden. Her devotion, vast and inexhaustible, remained intact.Adolphe was her Bible; she refused to be an Ellenore. She forbade herself the tactics of tears and reproach celebrated in the critic’s commentary she admired. She resolved not to dog his footsteps nor use silence as reproach; she held out the hope that if, for one day, he should recognize her merits and say, “That victim never uttered a cry!” that would suffice. And she consoled herself that others had him for an hour, while she had him for life.While Dinah endured these humiliations, her husband, Monsieur de la Baudraye, acted with the cold, calculating spirit of a man devoted to his estate. He had been punishing his wife privately and, meanwhile, devoted himself to reclaiming three thousand acres of moorland — a project to which he had been committed since 1836. Living like a mouse, he manipulated the property of Silas Piedefer so ingeniously that he reduced the apparent value to eight hundred thousand francs while pocketing twelve hundred thousand. He did not announce his return to Paris; while his wife endured unspeakable suffering, he was building farms, digging trenches, ploughing rough ground, displaying a courage that made him one of the most remarkable provincial agriculturists.The four hundred thousand francs he had taken from his wife were spent in three years on these improvements at Anzy, expected to return seventy-two thousand francs a year net after taxes. The eight hundred thousand he had invested at four and a half percent, bought during the financial panic of March, would yield forty-eight thousand francs for her expenses. In his mind he had restored her income sufficiently to discharge his obligation. He fancied himself on the road to being the richest proprietor in France, calculating an eventual income of a hundred and forty thousand francs, paying his taxes, working expenses, his wife’s allowance, and a small pension to his mother-in-law — and thus to be fit to petition for the title of Count. He boasted that his outlay amounted to a respectable sum and contemplated going to Paris to seek promotion to the peerage. He said these things with such composure that no one dared laugh; only the presiding judge remarked that a man like him should be happier if he had a daughter.By early 1842 Madame de la Baudraye, feeling she was to Lousteau no more than a reserve, had again sacrificed herself completely to secure his comfort. She resumed wearing black as mourning; pleasure began to turn into remorse. Her mother watched for exhaustion, as their priest advised, believing that the collapse would surely come and that then the children could be reclaimed. Madame Piedefer urged separation from Lousteau, not asking her daughter to give him up entirely but to withdraw. In violent real-life situations, resolutions rarely close as in novels; they end in disgust, the blighting of the soul, habit, or another passion that robs a wife of interest. As sense, propriety, family interest, the fatigue of constant sacrifice, and some insult too cruel to describe combined to awaken Dinah’s revulsion, her mother prepared to intervene.Clagny, who had taken on Dinah’s cause, assured her that if she gave up Etienne her husband would allow her to keep the children, live in Paris, and restore her command of her fortune. “With care and judgment, and the support of pious persons, you may have a salon and conquer a position. Paris is not Sancerre,” he said. Dinah left it to Clagny to negotiate reconciliation with her husband.La Baudraye had been selling produce and timber and, without informing his wife, had come to Paris to invest two hundred thousand francs in a residence in the Rue de l’Arcade. He had been on the Departmental Council since 1826, and paying ten thousand francs in taxes now made him eligible, under new laws, for a peerage. Some time before the 1842 elections he offered himself as a candidate unless called to the Upper House; he asked for the title of Count and for promotion in the Legion of Honor. Monsieur de Clagny supported him, arguing to the authorities that promoting this enterprising agriculturist to the peerage would guarantee his undertakings. The thought of being a Count appealed to the Baron: he wanted to enjoy life.He wrote to his wife, with a letter dictated by Clagny, asking her to live under his roof and furnish the house, giving play to her taste as shown at the Chateau d’Anzy. He requested that Clagny place sixty thousand francs at Madame la Comtesse’s disposal for decoration, even suggesting a marble tablet with the inscription Hotel de la Baudraye. He accounted for Silas Piedefer’s money, told her about the four and a half percent investment, and allowed her that income for expenses, including the children’s education. He would be compelled to stay in Paris during the sessions of the House of Peers and asked her to reserve for him a suite in an entresol over the kitchens.Comparing this future grandeur with her present humiliation was unbearable to Dinah. Only the day before she had been shunned by Anna de Fontaine at Chamarolles’ school. The prospect of becoming a Countess — to have the peer’s blue hammer-cloth on her carriage, leaders of the literary world in her drawing-room, and to look with quiet triumph at those who had scorned her — thrilled her. The thought of rehabilitation, of restored rank and social victory, weighed heavily as she prepared to accept her destiny.In May 1842 Madame de la Baudraye paid small household debts, left a modest sum after settling accounts, sent her mother and the children ahead, and prepared to confront Lousteau. At dinner, she told Etienne she had upset the pot and requested his company at the Rocher de Cancale. She bewildered him with the light, easy manners of a woman who had been his slave but had spent months acting the part of a mistress. He joked about her being “figged out,” but she demanded respect: “Do not forget the respect you owe to Madame de la Baudraye.” He put his arm round her: she replied that there was no longer any Didine; he had killed her. “I am taking you to the first performance of Madame la Comtesse de la Baudraye,” she said.“It is true, then, that our insect is a peer of France?” he sneered.“The nomination is to be gazetted tonight,” she answered. The revelation of his title and the changes in their circumstances concentrated Dinah’s sense of what she had lost and what she might now recover socially. She tried to control trembling and spoke gravely: they were parting forever. She had dismissed the servants, put the house in order, and wanted a calm, decent separation. “I shall always feel a mother’s affection for you, but in secret,” she said.Lousteau, in his later anger, gave her a dark lecture about her reading of Benjamin Constant and her failure to understand men. He argued that she had read Adolphe with “a woman’s eyes,” seeing only Ellenore while failing to see Adolphe’s forfeited future — how a man sacrifices his prospects for love and can never become what he might have been. Men who sacrifice themselves for women lose their privileges and are often deserted. He accused Dinah of having spoiled his life and wrecked his prospects; she had made him give up ambition for a love that the woman herself might later abandon. Dinah, astonished, promised to restore to him all he had lost by her, to transform him if God gave him life and he renounced his courtesans and actresses.Their bitterness rose; Lousteau suggested secrecy, that they hide their love and see each other in private. Dinah cried “Never!” — a definitive refusal that she uttered in fear of being swayed by him. “Never” she insisted when he taunted her with a suggestion she had earlier made. For Lousteau, that “Never” was a death-warrant of his power: Dinah remained insensible to his scorn. He could not suppress a tear at the loss of sincere affection; he had found in Dinah a gentle La Valliere and a delightful Pompadour, and like a boy who has killed a cockchafer by tormenting it, he wept. Dinah fled, paid the bill, and went home, calling herself a brute in her recollection.Dinah, who had made her house a model of comfort, now remade herself to fit her new position — a transformation that cost thirty thousand francs more than her husband had anticipated. A fatal accident that removed the Orleans heir in 1842 forced the Chambers to meet in August; La Baudraye presented his titles to the Upper House sooner than expected and then saw what his wife had achieved. He was so delighted he paid the thirty thousand francs without complaint, as he had once paid eight thousand to decorate La Baudraye. After being presented in the Luxembourg by two peers — the Baron de Nucingen and the Marquis de Montriveau — he met the old Duc de Chaulieu walking; the contrast between himself and the great noble filled him with intoxicated pride.Madame de la Baudraye, though consumed by melancholy she concealed by determined dignity, was charming, witty, and seemed young again in her court mourning. The Count lauded her youth to Monsieur de Nucingen: “You might declare the Countess was not yet thirty.” Her companions joked about her vanity and curiosities. La Baudraye’s pride was small but genuine; he admired the silver covers on the table and joked about the family’s fleeces.After the Regency vote passed, the Count returned to Sancerre for the vintage and resumed his old habits. That winter the Countess, supported by the Attorney-General to the Court of Appeals, tried to form a small circle in Paris. She contrived an “at home” day, selected men of mark, went twice weekly to the Opera with her mother and Madame de Clagny, and, despite cleverness and social success, was never really happy but with her children, on whom she lavished disappointed affection. Clagny, who had championed her, recruited pious women as much as fashionable ones and tried to introduce famous foreign celebrities on Wednesdays, praising her as exceptional even to those she barely addressed. Her silence and attention impressed them; she conquered by charm and occasional smart sayings, the woman who had once resurrected Lousteau’s writing.To Clagny, this phase seemed like an Indian summer after a sunless year. He affected a senior dignity and kept a respectful distance; he attempted a discreet devotion that he hoped would be returned. Dinah made fun of him to his face; yet he thought his lionizing might have been noticed. Once, on the way from the theatre, after seeing Leon Gozlan’s La Main Droite et la Main Gauche, Clagny noticed that Dinah was pensive. He asked what she was thinking about; she demurred, then confided that she had seen Lousteau and wondered whether he had money or a cigar. Clagny, unable to keep a secret any longer, told her the truth: Lousteau was living as a husband with Fanny Beaupre. He had hesitated to tell her for fear she might suspect an ungenerous motive.Dinah seized his hand. “Your husband is one of the rarest souls!” she began to say, and then shrank into silence, reflecting that Lousteau had not matched Clagny’s generosity. The knowledge that Lousteau had degenerated into domestic respectability with Fanny cured Dinah’s melancholy; she threw herself into fashion and social triumphs. She achieved a certain success, though introductions among women were difficult. In March, through the influence of priests and Clagny, she was appointed receiver of subscriptions for Madame de Carcado’s charity to gather Royal Family donations for Guadeloupe earthquake relief. The Marquise d’Espard later called the maneuvers for her rehabilitation the finest she had seen in the world.By early spring 1843 Paris smiled and the Champs-Elysées turned green. Lousteau’s attendant in Fanny Beaupre’s household had seen Madame de la Baudraye in a carriage, well dressed with dreamy eyes and her two boys at the windows, and was stung. He had been warring with shameful poverty, yet had maintained a point of honor to avoid public humiliation. Lousteau dined and smoked as if rich, indulged in expensive cigars, walked in patent-leather boots — but he feared execution on his goods; Fanny had pawned everything and advances from publishers had dried up. The journalist had borrowed a hundred francs from the poor Bixiou and faced humiliation: a yellow poster announced the sale of a handsome suite of furniture. He wandered the streets, searching for ideas, and heard Bixiou’s warning that persistent drunkenness would one day wake him mad.On the Friday before the sale he felt doomed. He might once have answered, “The furniture is old; I will buy new,” but he could no longer perform such literary legerdemain. The markets were unsympathetic and publishers paid poorly. Walking with a cigar and an affected smile, he saw Madame de la Baudraye pass in a carriage; he had nothing left. At seven in the evening he arrived in a hackney at her door and begged the porter to send a note: would Madame la Comtesse receive him for a moment? He used the familiar seal they had once shared — the carnelian on which she had “Parce que” engraved. The sight of that seal made Dinah pale. She had just been dressing for the Opera, yet she resolved to see him.Dinah received Lousteau in a boudoir with open doors, exquisitely dressed in pale blue brocade and pearls. He was the picture of a neat dandy. He told her he was drowning; his affairs were going to the devil. He listed his needs — to save his furniture from the auction he needed eighteen hundred francs, to repay friends as much again, to cover rent and to appease his ‘uncle.’ “And you — to live on?” she asked. He answered that he had his pen. “It is heavier to lift than people think,” she smiled, and promised to help. She did not have the entire sum on hand, but told him to come at eight the next morning: the bailiff would wait until nine, especially if Lousteau brought him away to be paid.He thanked her, calling her a drop of water in the desert, sent by an angel. The tenderness between them revived: he pressed her hand to his heart, their eyes kindled with the old flame, they embraced, cheek to cheek. Dinah felt the old attraction; she tried to control it, but passion flared briefly. In a decisive moment she called to her mother to fetch money from the banker, and hurried her away in order to hide the scene in the boudoir from detection. Madame Piedefer, persuaded to fetch funds, later consulted the parish cure and lamented her daughter’s condition. The priest replied that any moral regeneration not grounded in strong religious sentiment and the Church is built on sand. He urged the mother to persuade Dinah to perform her duties if they hoped to save her.Within ten days the Hotel de la Baudraye was shut. The Countess, the children, her mother, and household, including a tutor, had gone to Sancerre for the summer. Dinah intended to be everything that was “nice” to her husband; tongues said she had been compelled to return, and that the Count would soon have his wish for a little girl. Gatien and Monsieur Gravier lavished attention on the Countess; the tutor was supposed to have a chance at favor; Madame Piedefer urged devotion and religion.In June 1844, as the Comte walked in Sancerre with his two fine boys, he met the Public Prosecutor Monsieur Milaud and proudly introduced them, saying, “These are my children, cousin.” The lawyer twinkled and replied with the familiar provincial banter: “Ah ha! so these are our children!”Thus the Muse of the Department ends with the return of Dinah to family life, her brief re-entry to Parisian society, the ruin and humiliation of Lousteau, and the Count’s modest triumph as a provincial man made peer. Dinah’s moral position remains ambiguous: she sacrifices for love, seeks at once social rehabilitation and maternal devotion, and oscillates between tenderness, pride, and penitence. Lousteau, a charming, lazy poet and journalist, derives benefits from her tenderness, squanders them in libertinage, and finally collapses into poverty and dependence. Clagny, the good lawyer, is the faithful friend who counsels and aids Dinah, while Madame Piedefer and the local priest represent the moral and religious pressure that ultimately directs Dinah back to the domestic sphere.ADDENDUMSeveral personages in the tale figure in other stories of Balzac’s Human Comedy, linking this narrative to a larger social panorama: Fanny Beaupre (appears in A Start in Life; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life), Madame Berthier (Felicie Cardot) in Cousin Pons, Horace Bianchon in many works including Father Goriot and Lost Illusions, Bixiou in The Purse and many other tales, Camusot in A Distinguished Provincial at Paris, and other recurring characters such as Cardot, Falcon, the Grosstete brothers, Marshal Hulot, La Baudraye’s other appearances, the Laizomère, Lousteau himself, Lupeaulx, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, Milaud, Raoul Nathan and his wife, the Duc de Navarreins, the Baron Nucingen, and numerous other figures who populate Balzac’s interlinked universe — characters who traverse novels like Cesar Birotteau, The Firm of Nucingen, The Member for Arcis, A Daughter of Eve, The Secrets of a Princess, and many more, thus binding the moral and social fates depicted in The Muse of the Department to the sweep of Balzac’s grand design.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Story's middle: center stage now############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Middle of story; middle of story; middle of the story repeated here.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Monsieur Eugene-Auguste-Georges-Louis Midy de la Greneraye Surville, Royal Engineer of the Ponts et Chausses. As a testimony to the affection of his brother-in-law, De BalzacAN OLD MAIDCHAPTER I. ONE OF MANY CHEVALIERS DE VALOISFrance boasted many Chevaliers de Valois; one resided in Alençon, scarcely knowing his namesakes elsewhere. He claimed descent from old Valois lines, though the male branches had mostly vanished. The Alençon chevalier matched the stereotype: an elderly, tall, thin, nearly penniless gentleman who had spent his youth in Paris, reached thirty amid conquests before the Revolution, and was accepted provincially as a genuine Valois. His standing rested on manner, not wealth: he dined out, played cards nightly, and told polished stories of Louis XV’s court and revolutionary days. He never repeated his bon mots, avoided discussing love affairs, neglected mass yet remained staunchly royalist—an attitude forgiven because of his charm.He cultivated the little theatricalities of the ancien régime: an old gold snuff-box bearing the Princess Goritza’s portrait, a taste for refined apparel, and other fading graces. Though fifty-eight, he insisted he was fifty; careful dress and hair gave him a preserved air. A single enormous nose dominated his pale face—one side red, the other white—suggesting a “hot liver” that physicians jokingly linked to a prodigal heart; his gallantries confirmed the diagnosis. Pleasant laugh-lines and a courtly bearing testified to a life of amusement.His neat but slightly worn linen, maroon coat with gilt buttons, close-crimped cravat, square-buckled shoes, and twin watch-chains created an effect of elegant thrift. He wore cotton in his ears and two tiny diamond ear-rings shaped as little blackamoors’ heads—oddities tolerated because they fitted his role. He played the old-marquis part with skill, an art rarer since the loss of figures like Mole.Private details remained mysterious. He lodged modestly on Madame Lardot’s rue du Cours, two rooms for a hundred francs a year, returning there only to sleep and breakfast lightly. He made almost no fire, walked between eleven and four, read, and paid quarterly visits to his man of business. He professed a small annuity—six hundred francs—though a banker paid him one hundred and fifty francs quarterly, remitted by Monsieur Bordin of Paris, at the chevalier’s insistence on secrecy.Welcomed in Alençon’s best houses, he enlivened salons with card skill, witty stories, and a kindly approving grimace. Young women coveted his compliments; his old-fashioned pet names—“my heart,” “my jewel”—were still charming. At cards he praised opponents, tolerated poor players, and in a small gesture or snuff-box movement could quiet a room. To working girls, he was paternal and flattering, giving trifles—chocolates, ribbons—that bought their secret loyalty and discretion. Despite small winnings, he remained the town’s darling.But his means were larger than his declared annuity. After the July days, townsfolk learned his card winnings averaged about one hundred and fifty francs every quarter, and that he had the pluck to have his annuity paid to himself quietly. Over time he had amassed about ten thousand francs. The Bourbon Restoration brought the Marquis de Pombreton repaying twelve hundred pistoles, enabling investments on the Grand Livre. Through influential acquaintances he obtained a three-hundred-franc pension from the king’s privy purse and the cross of Saint-Louis, allowing him to style himself a retired colonel for services in the Catholic armies of the West. Thus, besides his theatrical poverty, he enjoyed an honest income of roughly one thousand francs. He changed little: whist, boston, backgammon, piquet, graceful snuffing, measured walks. Yet he harbored a steady ambition: to marry the wealthy old maid Mademoiselle Cormon, using her fortune to reclaim a place at court. This hope explained his royal bearing and decision to live in Alençon.CHAPTER II. SUSANNAH AND THE ELDERSOne mid-spring Wednesday the chevalier, dressing in a green-flowered damask gown, heard the light step of a young wash-girl. Three taps and she slipped into his room.“Ah! is it you, Suzanne?” he said, stropping his razor. “What have you come for, my dear little jewel of mischief?”“I have come to tell you something which may perhaps give you as much pleasure as pain,” she answered.Suzanne worked for Madame Lardot; the wash-rooms served the town’s best linen. The chevalier, self-appointed judge of love as revealed by linen, was discreet and generous with trifles. He frequented the work-room, bestowing candies and small attentions on his “little ducks,” who adored his gallant protection—an echo of seigneurial ways. He no longer ruined himself for mistresses; he gave toffees and compliments and enjoyed their gossip as a household feuilleton.Suzanne, clever, ambitious, handsome—reminiscent of Sophie Arnould in a Norman, somewhat rubenesque form—was a favorite. She confided to him her plan: to go to Paris and secure a protector, preferably an old bachelor who’d finance her trip. The chevalier listened with paternal indulgence, draping her before him in a gesture of faded gallantry, then advised: go to Monsieur du Bousquier. “Don’t take linen to anyone else,” he warned; du Bousquier, an old bachelor, was rich and ambitious, fearful of scandal and thus easily managed. He urged her to twist the man round her finger, but warned not to involve him—he had no money to help her.Du Bousquier was of an old Alençon family who made his fortune as commissary of provisions to the armies from 1793 to 1799. He lived magnificently, mingled with Barras and Fouché, and had once supported hopes of ministerial rank, but miscalculation at Marengo and Napoleon’s disfavour ruined him. Returning to Alençon with only twelve hundred francs a year on the Grand Livre, he was received among royalists but distrusted by the nobility. He had once courted Mademoiselle Armande and been refused. Middle-aged, plump, broad-chested, with a voice like cutting moist wood, he favored the liberal faction, manipulating elections and nursing patient resentment against the Restoration.The chevalier had recommended Suzanne to du Bousquier because both he and du Bousquier secretly hoped to marry Mademoiselle Cormon, the wealthy old maid. The chevalier never asked her; du Bousquier had and been refused. Their rivalry was courteous in one and less restrained in the other, but both frequented the same evening circles.Suzanne sped to du Bousquier’s rue du Cygne house, a once-stately grey Jura-stone dwelling now neglectfully splendid. He lived with a single Breton servant, René, a simple, greedy fellow trained to polish boots and floors and to be a confidant.René grunted that there was no linen to wash; Suzanne went upstairs and found du Bousquier in bed, swinging curtain-rings. She spoke gravely: she could be useful to him; she offered her hand and proposed that if he attached himself to her he might profit—Paris was changing, governments remade, misfortune could prove opportunity. She asked whether he now worked for anybody; his answer was “for myself,” blunt and proud.She reproached him for never becoming a father; he had long toyed with the idea of marrying the respectable and wealthy Mademoiselle Cormon to advance socially and politically. Suzanne’s offer therefore presented a different choice: a bold, quick-minded wife who might help him in the present. He hesitated, flattered and suspicious, priding himself on never being duped by women.Suzanne added urgency: she could not stay in Alençon—her mother would beat her, Madame Lardot would dismiss her. She needed money to reach Paris and for what Paris required. Du Bousquier, pressed by debts—payments on his house, decorators’ bills—offered three hundred francs. She feigned to go; alarmed and unwilling to be shown up, he softened. When she mentioned seeking help from Madame Granson of the Maternity Society, he yielded further, giving her a key and telling her to take from his secretary a bag containing about six hundred francs—“it is all I possess.” He warned her against deception.Suzanne received the sack with impertinence: had he not given more if she had only asked? He kissed her, she cursed his stinginess on leaving, and tucked the bag into an osier gamebag. Already swift in mischief, she planned to ask Madame Granson and Mademoiselle Cormon’s Maternity Society to complete the thousand francs needed. In revenge for du Bousquier’s parsimony, she even contemplated using his complaisance to embroil him in a scandal. Thus, beneath playful dialogue and provincial charm, desires, rivalries, and schemes for social ascension intersected—an old chevalier plotting marriage to regain court favour, a ruined contractor nursing political grievances, and a clever wash-girl turning both men into instruments for her Parisian ambitions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Granson, widow of a lieutenant-colonel killed at Jena, lived on a meagre pension of nine hundred francs plus three hundred from a small property; her savings had been spent on educating a son. Their lodging in the rue du Bercail was a humble ground‑floor apartment in a provincial town: a street door above three steep steps, a passage to an interior courtyard, a gallery staircase, a dining‑room and kitchen on one side, and a multifunctional salon that served as the widow’s bedroom on the other. A portrait of the lieutenant‑colonel presided over the room.Athanase Granson, twenty‑three, slept in an attic and added six hundred francs to the household from a post in the mayor’s office, obtained through his relation Mademoiselle Cormon; he was keeper of the archives. Athanase was thin, pale, of medium height, with a hollow face and dark eyes that suggested repressed talent. Irregular features and a melancholy cast revealed an imprisoned spirit: contemplative, timid, discouraged by provincial stagnation. Poverty bred in him a fierce pride; he was more thinker than actor, liable to sudden resolves that others might call madness. His library work went unnoticed; lacking encouragement, his abilities languished.Beneath this lay a secret passion for his distant cousin, Mademoiselle Cormon, also pursued by the Chevalier de Valois and du Bousquier. His love began as calculation—Cormon was thought wealthy—and he hoped marriage would provide the stability to free him for fame and secure his mother’s comfort. That practical motive initially made him ashamed of his feeling, and he feared ridicule for a young man loving an “old maid” of forty. Yet in a provincial setting where manners are fixed and opportunities few, marriage for material security is common; Athanase’s prudence gradually yielded to real passion as habit and constant attention made him see only her perfections. Isolated, she became for him the single figure of desire, and his secret feeling deepened while his timidity grew.Madame Granson, plain and composed beneath the portrait, suspected her son’s attachment and calculated candidly. She saw advantages: a talented husband would honor the family and neighborhood. She weighed obstacles—his poverty and Cormon’s age—but believed patience could overcome them. Like other provincials, she watched for favorable circumstances and plotted quietly. Distrusting du Bousquier, she concealed her tactics from Athanase.Suzanne’s lie—confessed to Madame Granson—became a powerful weapon in the widow’s hands. As treasurer of the Maternity Society, Madame Granson could spread scandal under the guise of charity, rallying support for “the chaste Suzanne” and using sympathy to wound du Bousquier. The Chevalier de Valois anticipated possible outcomes but could not foresee the full storm.At breakfast—coffee, bread and butter, radishes—mother and son kept their simple routine. Athanase, leaning on his elbow, twirled his spoon and took in the poor room—red brick floor, straw chairs, a painted buffet, checked curtains. His pallid face, framed by black hair and lit by the window, was the first thing Suzanne saw when she entered. Suzanne, a grisette familiar with hardship, felt an instantaneous electric attraction—an inexplicable conviction: “It is he!” The flash dispelled her usual cynicism. What had seemed a jest the night before now felt like a grave misdeed; she recoiled at her own readiness to deceive. Practical considerations—the young man’s poverty, her hopes of betterment, perhaps destiny—checked her scruples.She asked for a private interview with Madame Granson and was led to the bedchamber. When Suzanne rejoined Athanase, tears came to her eyes. Madame Granson, radiant, had at last a terrible weapon against du Bousquier and promised the seduced girl the active support of charitable ladies, foreseeing a scandal over the guilty man’s head.“My dear child,” Madame Granson told her son, “we dine to‑night with Mademoiselle Cormon; do take pains with your appearance. Put on that handsome frilled shirt and your green coat of Elbeuf cloth. I have my reasons.” She urged him to study the Chevalier’s easy manners and to soften his stiff air. Athanase listened, surprised but obedient; he rose, took his cap, and went to the mayor’s office, wondering whether his mother suspected his secret. Passing the rue du Val‑Noble—Cormon’s street—he indulged the common daydream of the devoted provincial: how little she guessed that a young man loved her, would be faithful, let her manage her fortune, yet could not speak.Suzanne returned to her mother’s thinking of Athanase, and like many who long to help an adored man beyond their means, contemplated using her body as a stepping‑stone for his advancement. Her falsehood had served its purpose: a few days later she left town with money and fine clothes, including a green reps dress and a green bonnet lined with pink given by Monsieur de Valois—a gift she preferred even to money—and set off for Paris. Alençon lamented her misfortunes as the ladies of the Charity and Maternity Societies showed lively sympathy. Though Suzanne belonged to the Parisian‑bound class of gallantry, she remained in a fairly decent stratum; the Chevalier’s patronage and a distinguished Restoration journalist’s protection offered her prospects, perhaps even marriage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In nearly all the smaller French prefectures there exists a salon that gathers the respectable, well‑considered people of the community who are not at the very top of society. The master and mistress of such an establishment are among the town’s leaders, received wherever they choose; no public fête or formal dinner occurs without them. But the château people—the heads of great estates, the highest personages—do not go to their houses; social intercourse between them and the salon is kept to cards and the occasional reciprocal dinner.This salon, where the lesser nobility, clergy, and magistracy meet, exercises great influence. Judgment and mind of the region reside in that solid, unostentatious society, where each man knows his neighbor’s resources and where indifference to luxury and dress prevails—trifles beside concerns like securing ten or twelve acres of pasture. Immoveable in its prejudices, this circle follows a beaten track, accepting nothing from Paris without long trial; it rejects cashmeres and speculative investments alike, scoffs at fashions and novelties, reads little, and is indifferent to science, literature, or industrial inventions. It insists on removing an incompatible prefect; if administration resists, it isolates him, like bees walling up a snail in wax. Gossip in this society often becomes a solemn verdict. Young women seldom appear there; when they do it is to seek approbation of their conduct. The supremacy of one house wounds other natives, who console themselves by calculating the profit from its entertainments. If no fortune large enough exists to keep an open house, the leading people choose a neutral meeting‑place, as at Alençon, where the public salon convenes in the residence of an inoffensive old maid whose settled life offends no one.For years the upper classes of Alençon met at the house of Mademoiselle Rose‑Marie‑Victoire Cormon. Her fortune, unknown to her, was coveted by Madame Granson, her second cousin, and by two old bachelors: the Chevalier de Valois and the Chevalier du Bousquier. Rose lived with her maternal uncle, the venerable Abbé de Sponde, once her guardian and now her heir. The Cormon family had been among the province’s most considerable bourgeois families, allied with nobility: stewards to the Duc d’Alençon, magistrates, and bishops among their members. Her maternal grandfather, Monsieur de Sponde, had been elected by the Nobility to the States‑General; her father, Monsieur Cormon, by the Tiers‑État, though neither accepted the mission. For a century daughters of the family had married provincial nobles, and the Cormons appeared in many genealogies; no bourgeois family seemed so near nobility.The house where Mademoiselle Cormon lived had belonged to the family since Henry IV’s time when Pierre Cormon, steward to the last Duc d’Alençon, built it. Though it produced little revenue and was costly to maintain, such an aristocratic private dwelling in a provincial town attracted envy and temptation. The solid, ancient mansion in rue du Val‑Noble—granite with diamond‑cut blocks, one clear story, steep roof and projecting oak‑framed windows—conveyed an appearance almost peerly to visitors who thought the mayor ought to live there. Stables, coach‑house, kitchens, and laundry flanked the courtyard.A side of the porte‑cochère opened to a modest courtyard garden—roses, pinks, lilies, Spanish broom, potted pomegranate and myrtle in season—kept with a scrupulous, almost Dutch neatness that betrayed the life of an old maid who, lacking other occupation, introduced meticulous order into a region proud of professing indifference to comfort. A glass door from the portico led into an antechamber—a tiled, wainscoted gallery housing family portraits, cloaks, clogs, overshoes, umbrellas, and pelisses of guests; benches lined the walls for servants and a stove countered the north wind. The house was divided into two halves. Toward the courtyard were the staircase well, a large dining‑room overlooking the garden, and a pantry communicating with the kitchen. On the other side was the salon with four windows and two smaller rooms—a boudoir looking on the garden, and an office looking on the courtyard. The upper floor contained a family apartment and a suite where the Abbé lived. Garrets housed rats and mice whose nocturnal restlessness Mademoiselle Cormon lamented with comic surprise. The garden, about half an acre, bordered the shallow Brillante river, near dyehouses and trades; trimmed linden hedges and pottery vases completed the modest, tranquil setting: nothing pretentious, but nothing transitory.The ground floor, wholly devoted to reception, embodied the provincial spirit. The great square salon had four windows modestly cased in gray woodwork. An oblong mirror above the fireplace and draped green damask curtains were among the few ornaments. Furniture covered with tapestry, painted woodwork of an earlier century, chairs with La Fontaine scenes, some mended tapestries—everything showed preservation rather than novelty. A huge beam divided the ceiling; an old rock‑crystal chandelier hung swathed in green gauze. On the mantel were Sèvres‑blue vases, old girandoles, and a clock whose bronze‑gilt group illustrated Sedaine’s Le Déserteur: soldiers bringing a deserter and a fainting young woman, a moralized decoration typical of provincial taste. Portraits by Rigaud and pastels by Latour hung on the walls. Four card tables, a backgammon board, and a piquet table occupied the room—the only ceiled chamber in the house.The dining‑room, paved in black‑and‑white stone with painted beams, contained a large marble‑topped sideboard necessary for the provincial war on the stomach. Frescoed walls, varnished cane seats, and plain wood doors gave it a patriarchal air. The provincial genius preserves everything: nothing is new or decrepit; a cold precision pervades the house. This Cormon house typified burgher houses in Normandy, Brittany, Maine, and Anjou and helps explain manners and customs. Life within was calm and monotonously regular. A library below river level housed well‑bound, dusty books chosen from regional presses; transport costs limited purchases to desirable, well‑selected works.Mademoiselle Cormon’s society numbered about one hundred and fifty persons. Some visited country estates or traveled, but many came every night unless elsewhere invited; most were mature, few had traveled, and several had taken part in the chouannerie and were beginning to speak openly of that war now that rewards were being distributed to its defenders. Monsieur de Valois, a mover in the last uprising, had recently related stratagems used against Hulot, an old republican commander stationed at Alençon.Women in this society took little trouble with dress except on Wednesdays, when Mademoiselle Cormon gave a dinner and guests returned the “visit of digestion.” Wednesdays were gala days; the assembly was numerous; visitors appeared in ribbons; some women brought sewing or knitting; young girls made patterns for Alençon point lace to pay their expenses. Certain husbands attended out of policy; young men were few; whispers were impossible without attracting attention, so clandestine love‑making was rare.Every evening at six the antechamber filled. Each habitué brought cane, cloak, and lantern. Habits were patriarchal: if the Abbé lay down, or Mademoiselle retired, servants did not interrupt; the first comer received the next, and whist, piquet, or boston began when company sufficed, without awaiting the hosts. If dark, servants lit candles at the first bell; seeing the salon lighted, the abbé would slowly descend. Backgammon and piquet, three boston tables, and the whist table filled—twenty‑five or thirty persons; occasionally forty appeared. Between eight and nine servants accompanied their masters home; short of a revolution no one remained after ten. Guests departed in groups, discoursing on the game or continuing conversations on land, wills, quarrels among heirs, or aristocratic haughtiness—much like Parisians leaving a theatre. Du Bousquier, the society’s born statistician, estimated the salon’s habitués commanded 131 votes in the electoral college and mustered about eighteen thousand francs a year from nearby landed estates.Alençon’s society was not entirely represented by this salon; higher aristocracy had its own circles, and the receiver‑general’s house acted like a government inn where society danced, plotted, flirted, and supped. These salons communicated through mixed individuals with Mademoiselle Cormon’s house, which nevertheless sat in judgment on the others, criticizing their dinners’ luxury, their ices at balls, and the women’s behavior and fashions.As sovereign of this coterie, Mademoiselle Cormon naturally attracted suitors. To marry her was to reign in Alençon: election as deputy for a bourgeois, or official advancement for a noble. Two bachelors had long hopes: the Chevalier de Valois and the Chevalier du Bousquier. Athanase Granson, another suitor, loved her for herself as well as for her fortune. But the drama of their rivalries circled a woman who never guessed they existed, despite her eager desire to be married.Why, despite fortune and suitors, did she remain unmarried? Several reasons combined. She always wished to marry a nobleman; from 1788 to 1798 political circumstances were hostile and despite the Consulate and Empire the numbers of suitable bachelors were altered. She feared the Revolutionary tribunal and consequently hesitated. She refused old men and would not marry very young ones; provincial families commonly married sons early to avoid conscription, and she obstinately refused soldiers—she would not give a man up to the Emperor; she wanted him for herself. Thus, from 1804 to 1815 she found no suitable match among young men. Besides preferring nobility, she had a strong desire to be loved for herself. She set tests for suitors so subtle that unlucky aspirants failed without realizing it: an unguarded word, a joke, or a tone might condemn a man—one had no heart, another told lies, a third was irreligious, a fourth sought money. She demanded moral and social perfection, wanting to be married for imaginary defects that she intended to redeem by revealing virtues. Nobles met common souls who prioritized interest and lacked her finer calculations.As she approached a “second youth” her distrust grew. She presented herself unfavorably and behaved so that last wooers hesitated to bind themselves to a woman whose virtuous, scrutinizing nature required penetration men—who wanted virtue ready‑made—did not possess. Fear of marrying for her money made her suspicious beyond reason. After each disappointment she grew to despise mankind, adopting a secret misanthropy which tinged her conversation with bitterness and her eyes with severity. Celibacy hardened her manners; she strove to sanctify herself in despair: noble vengeance, she cut for God the rough diamond rejected by man. Public opinion judged her accordingly; society assumed her rejections had secret causes—deformity or hidden faults—but she was as pure as a saint, healthy, and full of kindness; Nature had intended her for motherhood.Mademoiselle Cormon’s person was not classically beautiful but had the rustic, buxom freshness called la beauté du diable. Broad feet, sinewy legs, a stout waist, and plump arms gave a Norman outline. Undecided eyes gave a sheepish simplicity; an aquiline nose contrasted with a narrow forehead; thick red lips showed kindliness while her forehead suggested a lack of ideas. Chestnut hair of extraordinary length added vigor. In a ball‑dress she might have seemed handsome; officers admired her robust figure. But as years passed plumpness filled her; no corset could restore youthful proportions. Her bosom’s amplitude overhung when she stooped; her chin doubled, shortening her neck and hindering carriage. No wrinkles but folds of flesh; jests said she powdered her skin like an infant’s.This ample person attracted Athanase. Many indebted elegants would have welcomed making Mademoiselle Cormon happy. But at forty she had fortified virtue by stern religious practices. For three years a confessor directed maceration and advised scourging—much that modern medicine would find absurd. These practices cast a monastic tint over Rose, who took on a yellow pallor; a down darkened her upper lip and her temples grew shiny. Rumors said she suffered rushes of blood to the head. She confided her ills to the Chevalier de Valois, who would pull out his snuff‑box and say: “The right composing draught, my dear lady, is a good and kind husband.”The violence of hope made Rose afraid to look a man in the face lest he perceive her feelings. By will—perhaps from tests—she treated those she felt attracted to ungraciously, fearful of seeming foolish. Society took her behavior for revenge for a refusal and made fun of her abortive marriages. By 1815 she was forty‑two; desire for marriage bordered on monomania because she saw chances of children slipping away. She loved wholesomely and simply—Catholic, chaste, incapable of coquetry.The Abbé de Sponde could not aid matrimonial manoeuvres. Now seventy, he attributed the Revolution’s disasters to Providence punishing a dissolute Church and adopted an ascetic life, hiding charity and penances. Detached from earthly interests, his mind turned to sufferers and the Church’s needs; he left property management to his niece, who gave him its income while he spent surpluses in secret alms. The abbé loved his niece as a daughter but was abstracted and could not conceive the flesh’s agitations; accustomed to reverence him, Rose dared not tell him of her desires. His silence was affable and benevolent; tall, spare, grave, solemn, his presence carried sacred authority. He was fond of the Voltairean chevalier; relics of nobility and clergy recognized each other by generous traits.Some might imagine Mademoiselle Cormon employed arts of coquetry to secure a husband—but she did not. She was immovable in her high‑necked chemisette; her gowns were made by Alençon’s two hump‑backed sisters, who had taste. She refused airy deceits of elegance, choosing substance in all things. Piety allowed no subtleties: she trod the path of salvation, preferring prolonged virginity’s sorrows to trickery. In a woman armed with a scourge, virtue will not compromise; consequently love and self‑interest must seek her resolutely. Devotion can produce moral myopia: pious persons turn so to celestial things they become blind to many earthly matters. The Chevalier de Valois found it hard to decide whether stupidity makes one pious or piety makes one stupid. But whether stupidity or sanctity, their sorrows deserve pity like those of genius.Thus Rose was judged virginally ignorant. She failed to read Athanase Granson’s eyes—shy herself, she never suspected shyness in others; she did not recognize quavering tones as unuttered sentiment. It is unsurprising that a devoted soul may be blind to a noble one: heart qualities differ from intellectual ones as genius differs from nobility of soul. This good creature, who grieved at making yearly preserves for only her uncle and herself, was becoming almost ridiculous. Sympathy and mockery mingled: some pitied her goodness, others derided her defects. People speculated on who would inherit her property and her uncle’s savings. For a while she was suspected of being an “original”—dangerous in the provinces, where originality, meaning ideas not understood by others, is not permissible; provinces demand equality of mind and manner.After 1804 the phrase “married like Mademoiselle Cormon” became proverbial in Alençon for ridiculous failures.
