Parisian sarcasm found it odd that so excellent a woman should excite laughter. She received the whole society at her house, was charitable, pious, incapable of malice, and embodied the spirit and habits of the place; she had never left them and loved their prejudices and interests.Despite an income of eighteen thousand francs from land—a considerable provincial fortune—she lived simply. At her country place at Prébaudet she rode in an old wicker carriolle hung on two white leather straps, drawn by a wheezy mare and hardly protected by rusty leather curtains. Jacquelin cared for it as if it were the finest coupé in Paris. Mademoiselle valued it, having used it for twelve years—a fact she noted with triumphant thrift. Most inhabitants were grateful she did not humiliate them by ostentation; had she imported a calèche from Paris they would have gossiped more about it than her matrimonial failures. In the provinces people look to results and care little about the elegance of means so long as they function.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################To complete the picture of the household’s internal habits, the circle around Mademoiselle Cormon and the Abbé de Sponde must include Jacquelin, Josette, and Mariette, who provided for the uncle and niece’s comfort.Jacquelin, about forty, short, fat, ruddy, with a Breton-sailor face, had served them twenty-two years. He waited at table, groomed the mare, tended the garden, blacked the abbé’s boots, ran errands, chopped wood, drove the carriole, fetched oats and hay from Prébaudet, and dozed in the antechamber in the evenings. He loved Josette, a thirty-year-old, short, plump woman whose face—“like a dirty apricot”—betrayed sense and character; town gossip held that she managed her mistress. Mademoiselle would have dismissed Josette if she had married Jacquelin, so the two resigned themselves to saving their wages and loving in silence, awaiting some distant change. Mariette, cook of fifteen years, knew how to prepare Alençon’s most esteemed dishes.The old Norman mare Pénélope, drawn to Prébaudet by Mademoiselle, had served the family eighteen years and was tended with as much devotion as any human servant. Mademoiselle and Jacquelin hoped to use her for ten more years. The household—four dependents and a beast—moved each day with mechanical regularity; as they said, “they had eaten their white bread first.” Mademoiselle Cormon, driven by a fixed idea, became hard to please, petty, and nagging—less by temperament than to occupy her active mind. Without husband or children, she busied herself with minutiae: napkins marked “Z” before the “O’s,” whether Pénélope had her oats at two o’clock, a neglected dust-layer, a poorly made toast, Josette’s delay in closing blinds that faded furniture. Small things became grounds for serious quarrels. Once Josette handed her the wrong devotional text and Mademoiselle left church in embarrassment; the town heard of the calamity. She would admonish Josette softly—“Such a thing must never happen again”—and the little quarrels served as catharses, secretly steadying her. Josette and Jacquelin accepted her moods like weather: “It is fine to-day,” or “It rains,” without blaming the heavens. Each morning they wondered in what humor she would rise, as farmers wonder at dawn mists.She had narrowed, quite naturally, to the infinite pettinesses of life: herself and God, her confessor and the weekly wash, her preserves and church services, and her uncle to care for, occupied her feeble intellect. Her perfect health magnified the slightest digestive trouble; she submitted to quarterly medicinal doses strong enough, it was said, to have killed Pénélope but seeming to rejuvenate her mistress. A small pimple could occasion inquiries for a week until Josette triumphantly blamed a hare or Mariette’s seasoning: “No doubt, no doubt, it was the hare!”Four times a year she spent days at her estate, Prébaudet. In mid-May she liked to see whether her apple-trees had “snowed” (fallen blossoms promising an abundant cider harvest), supervise winter repairs, and order flower-beds and the vegetable garden that supplied her table. Every season had business. Before departing she always gave a farewell dinner to intimate friends, though she expected to see them again within weeks. Her departure was town news: shopkeepers repeated it from door to door—“So Mademoiselle Cormon is going to Prébaudet!”—accompanied by provincial commentary.The Wednesday Suzanne chose to reveal her scandal was such a farewell Wednesday, when Mademoiselle was driving and Josette distractedly packed. Madame Granson had spent the morning visiting houses, and the malicious Chevalier de Valois was playing piquet with Mademoiselle Armande, who governed aristocratic salons. The chevalier and Madame Granson wanted to see how Mademoiselle Cormon, as both marriageable woman and president of the Maternity Society, would receive the news. Du Bousquier, the innocent suitor, walked on the promenade, beginning to suspect Suzanne had tricked him.On gala days Mademoiselle Cormon’s table was laid about half-past three; the fashionable dined at four. One of her chief pleasures was seeing herself dressed as mistress of the house to receive guests. When equipped, a ray of hope would glide into her heart; she felt that nature had not provided in vain and that some brave man might present himself. She reveled in inspecting the dining-room—an oblong table set with a snow-white cloth, twenty covers, a squadron of bottles with honorable labels, and little paper name cards in the trembling hand of the abbé—the only duty he assumed. After these preliminaries she would go in her finery to her uncle, who took his walk on the terrace overlooking the Brillante and the coppice. In waiting she never joined the Abbé de Sponde without asking some ridiculous question to rouse him from his meditative silence; she regarded it as her duty to talk and make herself agreeable, a practice advised by her spiritual director, the Abbé Couturier. She perspired in her corset thinking up ideas; her efforts produced absurdities such as: “No one can be in two places at once—unless it is a little bird,” which somehow provoked a debate on the apostles’ ubiquity. Such talk earned her the polite label “that good Mademoiselle Cormon”—a way of saying she was rather a fool; many peers, however, took her sincerity at face value and praised her.When conversation flagged the Chevalier de Valois, who loved to assist women, would transform her sayings into wit by sustaining them paradoxically; once when she could not distinguish an ox from a bull, he claimed the only difference was like that between sheep and lamb. He often covered her retreats so deftly that she seemed never to have said anything silly. She took his help as evidence of her adequacy and grew complacent in her ignorance, acquiring a self-possession that gave her speeches a solemnity akin to patriotic absurdities.One day she tried to draw her uncle into a matrimonial discussion. Leaning on his arm she reasoned: if everything below happens according to God’s will, then if she remains unmarried God wills it. But if she could marry tomorrow, does not her will destroy God’s? The Abbé de Sponde, a former prior of the Sorbonne, gently showed the childishness of the logic, pointing out she had put in an “if.” When she argued that God must have meant women to marry or not uniformly, the abbé replied that she blamed the Church, which declared celibacy better. “You have too much mind, Rose; you don’t need so much to be happy,” he said, a remark that pleased and confirmed her self-opinion.Guests arrived and the familiarities between servants and company began: Mariette telling Monsieur du Ronceret she had cooked cauliflowers au gratin “expressly for you,” Josette informing Madame Granson that “Mademoiselle has thought of you, and there’s fish for dinner,” the chevalier in his easy condescending tone asking Mariette if there was some dainty he should reserve. Du Bousquier was not invited. Mademoiselle Cormon, faithful to her ambivalence, treated the fifty-year-old suitor ill though she felt inexplicable sentiments toward him. She had refused him yet sometimes repented; a presentiment haunted her that she might yet marry him, and terror prevented her desiring it. Madame Granson and the chevalier, observing her naive glances toward du Bousquier, resolved to ruin his hopes.Two tardy guests—Monsieur du Coudrai, recorder of mortgages, and Monsieur Choisnel, the notary—kept dinner waiting. As they arrived, Jacquelin announced “They are all in the garden.” The company strolled the terrace above the river, admiring water-plants, mosaics of currents, and houses across the Sarthe with old wooden galleries and little gardens where clothes dried—a charming provincial landscape.The chevalier scanned faces; he had introduced his firebrand into the chief houses. The scandal about Suzanne and du Bousquier had not yet been openly discussed: provincials distill gossip, waiting the right moment. Whispers circulated: “You have heard?” “Yes.” “Du Bousquier?” “And that handsome Suzanne.” “Does Mademoiselle Cormon know of it?” “No.” “Ha!”The chevalier, noting Madame Granson’s triumphant look, suspected not only her joy but self-interest. He watched Athanase and saw, in a furtive look at Mademoiselle Cormon, a flash that set his plans awry. He offered his arm to Mademoiselle Cormon, whom he revered. She, secretly wishing for alliance, admired the name de Valois as a social object, though his seeming purity and indifference worried her; she wanted a husband a little dissipated—someone with experience to purify. Observers have remarked pious women’s leaning toward scamps; Mademoiselle’s wish showed this: she would have liked a man to combine the chevalier’s nobility with du Bousquier’s vigor.Two matters agitated the town and linked du Bousquier to civic tensions. One concerned the rector of Alençon who had once taken the constitutional oath but was winning Catholics by virtue; middle-class supporters rallied to him through du Bousquier, while Mademoiselle Cormon and the Abbé de Sponde, of the uncompromising “little Church,” refused to receive him. The second was du Bousquier’s idea of building a theatre in Alençon; Athanase Granson and other young clerks championed the project. The chevalier urged Mademoiselle Cormon to save Athanase from this influence. “You have so much sense and judgment in social proprieties and influence with his mother; ought you not to use it?” he said, implying that lycée-bred lads marched about in uniform and that Athanase’s shyness and pale looks foretold a sad end; he warned that such associations might cost him his place in the mayor’s office. Madame Granson, who overheard part of the talk, followed Mademoiselle hoping to learn the outcome. Jacquelin announced dinner, and the recorder cut off the chevalier, allowing him instead to address Madame Granson directly; she gratefully promised to enlighten her son. The chevalier had long seen Athanase’s unyielding republican convictions—rooted in artistic sorrows and a bitter social view—and knew he would persist. To remain faithful to the Left in Alençon was to earn Mademoiselle Cormon’s aversion.Thus this outwardly peaceful society was internally agitated by craft and passion like any diplomatic circle. The guests ate with healthy provincial appetites. Toward the end of the first course Mademoiselle Cormon made the celebrated remark that would be repeated for two years: in the debate against du Bousquier—whom the chevalier and others depicted as a pernicious intriguer—she had innocently said, “As for me, I thought that Monsieur du Bousquier cared chiefly for childish things.” The phrase had enormous success: it undermined the Princess Goritza’s dignity and astonished the chevalier into silent admiration; he applauded discreetly, as at the Opera.Prompted by the chevalier, du Bousquier was caricatured as a monster, a Pere Gigogne who had supplied the Foundling Hospital for fifteen years; his immoral habits were made to explain Parisian revels. The chevalier speculated that a du Bousquier might marry a “Suzette,” invoking Madame du Barry and the Emperor Joseph; Mademoiselle corrected him with naïve historicism. The Abbé de Sponde, touched with charitable solemnity, urged succor for the poor girl now unlikely to marry. The judge, Madame Granson, and others volleyed puns and jests until the abbé’s grave “Nor can he be a foster-father” quieted them.Usually a man is ignorant of town rumors; du Bousquier remained superbly unaware of Suzanne’s revelations and was jaunty at coffee. Mademoiselle Cormon, embarrassed, lectured Athanase with platitudes about royalist politics and morality. Athanase listened with a stupid air, gazing at her enormous bust and swelling in his love, which transformed her shrill voice into a soft murmur and her flat remarks into witticisms. Love, the narrator notes, is a maker of false coin, turning copper into gold.“Will you promise me?” she asked Athanase, and the words pierced his absorbed mind. Had he spoken then, he might have revealed his passion; but his shy absorption ruined him. When his mother demanded what he had been saying to Mademoiselle, he replied “Nothing.” Madame Granson, who believed du Bousquier forever lost in Mademoiselle’s esteem after that evening, thought little more of the remark.The salon later filled: tables for piquet and backgammon, groups by the fire in evening finery, each repeating their farewell: “So you are going tomorrow to Prébaudet?” “Yes, I really must.” Mademoiselle Cormon appeared preoccupied; Madame Granson noticed she was thinking. “What are you thinking of, cousin?” she asked. “I am thinking,” Mademoiselle replied, “of that poor girl. As president of the Maternity Society, I will give you fifty francs for her.” “Fifty francs!” cried Madame Granson in astonishment. Mademoiselle never gave so much, but her sudden generosity showed du Bousquier had risen in her estimation.“Du Bousquier is not only a monster, he is a villain,” said Madame Granson. “When a man has done a wrong like that, he ought to pay the indemnity. Isn’t it his place rather than ours to look after the girl?—who, to tell you the truth, seems rather questionable.” Mademoiselle, who could not quite grasp how hearts work, said innocently that perhaps one could be calumniated for loving Athanase, yet be loved truly. Madame Granson tried to explain the physical side: “It is impossible for Suzanne to love du Bousquier. And if the heart counts for nothing—” “But, cousin, what do people love with if not their hearts?” inquired the old maid, widening her eyes in honest puzzlement.Madame Granson advised a show of displeasure: not receiving du Bousquier in her house until he married would manifest exemplary morals. Mademoiselle promised to consult her uncle and the Abbé Couturier upon her return from Prébaudet. The lights, the women in best clothes, and the solemn assembly made Mademoiselle proud: to many it was the best of Parisian society. Du Bousquier, whistling a pastoral air, was the centre of curiosity: some admired him, others saw danger. The chevalier, going home, planned to spread a rumor of his marriage to Mademoiselle Cormon to secure attention and advantage—hoping to use connections to become mayor, then deputy, dreaming of peerage and power, and imagining how a marriage would serve his ambitions.When Madame Granson reached home she fiercely argued with her son Athanase, who could not see the link between his political opinions and his love—a quarrel the household had never known before.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning Mademoiselle Cormon, packed into the old carriole with Josette and a pyramid of parcels, set off for Prebaudet; fate was about to thrust a sudden chance at marriage on her.The day after her arrival, while she breakfasted and heard reports from her keeper and gardener, Jacquelin burst in breathless. “Monsieur l’abbé sends an express—the son of Mère Grosmort—with a letter. The lad left Alençon before daylight. Can’t I give him a glass of wine?” Alarmed, she read that Monsieur de Troisville, grandson of one of her uncle’s old friends and lately in the Russian service during the Emigration, intended to retire to Alençon and had asked the Abbe de Sponde for hospitality. The absent-minded abbé begged his niece to return at once to help receive this guest; the viscount’s delayed letter suggested he might arrive that very night.Her reaction was instantaneous. She ordered Penelope harnessed, demanded leave-taking in half an hour, and despite rain and warnings about the mare’s oats drove back to town at a gallop, pushing Jacquelin and Josette to hurry. Absorbed in visions of a trousseau and wedding-day schemes, she chattered about fitting Prebaudet for a vicomte and resolved, with her uncle’s help, not to let him leave as he entered.Her sudden return—Penelope lathered and at full gallop, parcels half-packed, the early hour, and her visible agitation—stirred Alençon. The great green gate opening and closing, the sight of a frantic old maid, and gossip sent ripples through the town. Josette guessed the expected man: between forty and forty-two, a bachelor of middling fortune. Mademoiselle already called herself Vicomtesse de Troisville.She scolded the abbé in absentia for his neglect and set the household into feverish motion. Mariette was sent to market for the best provisions with orders to spare no expense; Moreau the upholsterer was summoned to contrive a bed in Mademoiselle’s boudoir so the viscount would not be lodged too near her uncle’s bare room. She fretted about her appearance, the house’s furnishings, and whether the viscount’s smile would reflect her years. Everything comfortable now seemed rusty and absurd to the ardent spinster willing to spend half her savings to refit Prebaudet overnight.Josette, Jacquelin, Mariette, Moreau and their agents set to work like ants—scrubbing, polishing, bringing out ceremonial china and damask, preparing the best liqueurs, arranging clothes, and dressing themselves in full. The garden was raked, the table laid, and Mademoiselle rehearsed her role as hostess of a brilliant society.At about four the postilion’s click-clack announced arrival. A single post-chaise drew up; boys and men followed it to the gate. The abbé greeted the unknown gentleman; the chaise was emptied and rolled into the coach-house. The vicomte alighted with the carriage of a man who had seen Europe: blue eyes, black hair, olive skin, dignified, nearer forty-six than forty, with a manner that spoke diplomacy and travel.Mademoiselle, trembling with hope, presented herself when Josette opened the boudoir. The abbé introduced them: “Niece, this is Monsieur le Vicomte de Troisville; Monsieur, my niece, Mademoiselle Cormon.” She thought her uncle had done wonders. The abbé and his niece left the vicomte to arrange his lodgings while they walked by the river; she was already imagining matrimonial inevitabilities.Back inside, she declared the viscount’s manners, voice, and looks proved him suitable. The abbé, absent-minded as ever, observed only that the vicomte had written he wanted to buy a house; if he were married he would not have come alone, he added, not thinking that his niece might interpret differently. Mademoiselle, for whom forty seemed youth to a septuagenarian priest, continued to flatter hope. The vicomte admired the Brillante, the house’s harmony; she praised her trees, nightingales, and ancestral continuity, adding that she would change nothing unless she married—when everything must suit the husband. The vicomte complimented her principles and household arrangements with courtly ease.Dinner gave Mademoiselle a chance to shine. The vicomte spoke lightly of interiors and land; she took every look as encouragement, poured every cup with trembling eagerness, and assumed the bearing of a queen of Alençon. At dessert the sound of expected friends arriving heightened her excitement; she ordered coffee and Madame Amphoux’s rare liqueurs brought into the salon—a conspicuous sign, noted by all and destined to fuel gossip.Rumors spread fast. The Abbe de Sponde, asked about the newcomer, had said a vicomte who had served in Russia was expected. Between two and five a labial telegraphy ran: Mademoiselle Cormon had found a husband by letter and would marry the Vicomte de Troisville. Speculation varied—was he a poor younger son or a wealthy Russian?—and all her acquaintances determined to visit that evening.When the company assembled, du Bousquier muttered that the liqueurs’ presence meant something planned, while the chief-justice surmised that the marriage had been arranged long ago with letters from Odessa. The Chevalier de Valois, usually witty and composed, paled at the left cheek and sought to hide his agitation by commenting on the cold. Mademoiselle, flushed and triumphant from her morning’s emotion, presented the viscount to everyone with pride.Monsieur de Troisville and the Chevalier, both aristocrats by nature, recognized each other and struck an immediate accord, forming a circle by the fire. Conversation turned to houses and lodging. Then, in a moment that unmade all Mademoiselle’s hopes, the Chevalier asked whether the vicomte was married.“Yes,” Monsieur de Troisville replied. “For the last sixteen years, to a daughter of the Princess Scherbellof.”Mademoiselle Cormon fainted. Du Bousquier sprang forward and supported her to her bedroom; Josette cut her corset bindings, and du Bousquier splashed water to revive her. When she opened her eyes she saw du Bousquier and gave a modest cry—an intimate, involuntary sight of her disarray now observed by a man. He retired, and six women, led by Madame Granson, tended her.The company, amused and scandalized, discussed the comedy. The abbé had known the vicomte’s errand but, absent-minded, had not supposed his niece could be interested; the vicomte, preoccupied with house-hunting and indifferent to talk of his wife, had never announced she existed and naturally assumed Mademoiselle understood from his coming that he was single. Jacquelin, who had dreamed of his mistress’s match, felt the sting: Madame Amphoux’s liqueurs destined for a bachelor would not be served to a married man.Next morning, bled and recovered, Mademoiselle Cormon could have shown dignity and Christian resignation; instead she offered, with admirable composure, to lend her house temporarily to the viscount until he found a place. He told her lightly he had two sons and two daughters and would inconvenience her; the abbé reminded her that he had already offered the same in a previous letter she had not received. Shocked, she realized then that her uncle had known all along.Left alone and conscious that prompt marriage was the only cure for scandal, she considered her options. Josette urged her to accept du Bousquier. Two bachelors—du Bousquier and the Chevalier de Valois—sensed opportunity; each resolved to press his suit that morning. Du Bousquier, coarse but sincere, arrived first. The Chevalier, polishing his look with rouge for once, delayed and so lost his chance.Du Bousquier entered with rough earnestness. Mademoiselle, remembering how he had borne her when she fainted, saw his devotion in a new light. He declared he had never changed his feelings, that her first refusal had not discouraged him, and that in spite of her severity he loved her. He praised her beauty with a simplicity that struck her. Touched, she asked him to promise to live a Christian life with her, to respect her religious practices and confessors. He agreed. Then, with tender pragmatism, she asked one more thing: she begged him to declare—a lie she felt monstrous—that their marriage had been agreed upon for six months, so as to silence gossip. Du Bousquier, ardent and eager, accepted the sacrifice gladly.At that moment the Chevalier arrived. He found the pair, and du Bousquier, in triumph, confessed to him—ever eager to wound—that he had secretly kept an engagement and intended to marry Mademoiselle in a few days; the house in the rue du Cygne was therefore for sale, and the abbé had taken the vicomte to view it. The Chevalier believed the falsehood; after a cutting compliment about their happiness he noticed du Bousquier’s false front hanging askew and was provoked to amusement by the sight of a man affecting dignity with his wig awry. Mademoiselle blushed; du Bousquier was mortified; the old grudge between aristocrat and purveyor simmered.Seeing everything decided, Mademoiselle, anxious to keep the peace, proposed a simple game of piquet. Du Bousquier fetched the table, and the Chevalier, stunned by his loss, allowed himself to be led to the game. The abbé and the vicomte returned from viewing houses; the vicomte’s interest in Mademoiselle’s house confirmed the plausibility of du Bousquier’s fabrications. Mademoiselle took her uncle aside and begged that he mention to the viscount the rue du Cygne house was for sale—a plausible explanation for the visit—and concealed the lie from the absent-minded priest.The town took the story to heart. By evening the rumor of a marriage spread and entertained Alençon as much as the anxieties of 1814–1815 once had. Some laughed; others accepted it as fact. The middle classes rejoiced at seeing the proud Cormons humbled into marriage with a purveyor. The Chevalier offered his usual biting commentary among friends: “The Cormons end as they began; there’s only a hand’s breadth between a steward and a purveyor.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The news that Mademoiselle Cormon had married struck Athanase Granson like a stab to the heart, though he betrayed nothing outwardly. He learned it at the du Roncerets’ while his mother played boston; she thought his pallor was merely nerves, unaware that rumor had been eroding his hopes all day. Mademoiselle Cormon had been the one card on which Athanase had staked his life; her marriage felt to him like catastrophe. Hardened by a fatalistic education under the Empire, his imagination, already fed on dread, magnified the blow until hope and self-belief gave way. He sat beside his mother, eyes fixed on the cards in a stupor she mistook for indifference—his coldness explained his earlier refusal to make customary sacrifices for the match, whose “liberal” political overtones complicated private affairs.After that evening, Athanase fled to the Sarthe countryside—windmills, river meadows, lonely poplars—seeking the solitude he loved. Provincials lacked sensitivity for such views; he wandered vacant under a poplar while neighbors told his mother, “There is something the matter with your son.” She assumed he brooded on some great work. He abandoned political activity, sometimes adopting a satirical gaiety that was really personal scorn; he had few friends and no woman to console him. Had Suzanne appeared earlier, his miseries might have been exchanged for love; as it was, she crossed his path only later.Suzanne’s ambitions were stoked by tales of the Marquis de Montauran’s adventure with Marie de Verneuil at the More tavern. Enamored of beauty and seduction’s romance, she wanted to make life into a stage for exerting power over men. Months after leaving Alençon in haste, she returned with an artist to see Fougères and the scene of the Marquis’s story, imagining a disguised arrival and hoping to send money delicately to her mother and perhaps to Athanase.For a month the town argued over Mademoiselle Cormon’s marriage. Some denied it, some affirmed it; du Bousquier’s sale of his house to the Marquis de Troisville seemed to contradict the story until the twentieth day, when Monsieur Lepressoir, the liberals’ notary, went to her house and the contract was signed. The marriage was the first of many sacrifices Mademoiselle Cormon would make to her husband. Du Bousquier bore a long antipathy to Choisnel, the usual notary; Mademoiselle Cormon received anonymous letters and, to her surprise, learned from them that Suzanne had never been a heroine of any such exploit—the seducer with a false toupet was fiction. Suzanne confessed the trick, sent a thousand francs to a maternity society, and tried to punish the old purveyor; the society thereafter resolved to assist only real misfortunes.Despite gossip, the banns were published. The bride retired to Prébaudet, visited daily by du Bousquier with lavish bouquets. On a dull rainy June morning, Mademoiselle Cormon and Sieur du Bousquier were married at noon in the parish church before the whole town. They left the mayor’s office for the church in an ostentatious open calèche from Paris—an affront to provincials wary of Parisian luxury, rising rents, and foreign furniture. The loss of the old carriole, the new horses, and carriage display prompted gossip about outsiders taking local money. Pépéno had died six weeks before; Madame du Bousquier entered the church with her left foot—an omen, because “Left” had begun to carry political meaning—and the priest, by chance, opened his book at the De Profundis. No wedding party followed; the pair departed immediately for Prébaudet. People murmured that Parisian customs would conquer provincial ways. Jacquelin and Josette’s later gay wedding offered one cheerful refutation of dire predictions.Du Bousquier used proceeds from his former house to modernize the hôtel Cormon, decided to remain two seasons at Prébaudet, and took the Abbé de Sponde with them. The town feared “comfort.” Soon the bridegroom drove into town in a fine tilbury, deposited his wife’s savings at the Grand-Livre, and—by manipulating for a rise—had made himself a personal fortune almost as large as his wife’s.A fate worse than modern furniture was linked to the marriage. On the evening of the ceremony, Athanase sat with his mother by a small fire. Madame Granson proposed a visit to the du Roncerets’; Athanase, in a childish voice, asked her to stay longer, calling her “my dear mamma.” She consented, preferring to listen to his plans rather than play boston. He clung to her with desperate tenderness, kissed her eyes and hair, and, while trying to conceal a deadly resolve, told her he wished to leave Alençon and suffer away from her. She refused to be parted; he persisted. She offered money hidden in her petticoat for his outfit.After a brief outing to du Ronceret’s, Athanase went to the Sarthe, settled beneath his chosen poplar on two stones, reviewed a future he no longer expected, and in a deliberate, nearly soundless act slipped into the deep pool where he had long intended to end his life. Madame Granson returned at half-past nine to find a short note: “My good mother, I have departed; don’t be angry with me.” She thought he had gone off cleverly and went to bed in peace.The Sarthe had risen that morning and brought eels; a fisherman drew his net where Athanase had thrown himself and recovered the body at dawn. Friends prepared Madame Granson for the remains. The suicide shocked Alençon: the vain young man of genius had died with no protector the night before, and a thousand voices cried, “I would have helped him.” The Chevalier de Valois explained the suicide as an act of revenge for love, revealing Athanase’s sincere devotion to Mademoiselle Cormon. Guided by the chevalier’s rendering, Madame Granson recalled details that confirmed him; the story became touching and many women wept. Her grief was silent, concentrated, and not fully understood. The narrative distinguishes two sorts of maternal mourning: the public grief for a son admired in society, and the deeper private mourning of a mother who shared a child’s inner life.Before the new Madame du Bousquier returned to town, Madame du Ronceret drove to Prébaudet to fling Athanase’s corpse upon the roses of the bride’s joy and sober her bridal month. On the road back to Alençon, Madame du Bousquier met Madame Granson at the corner of rue Val-Noble; the mother’s dying look—filled with malediction and reproach—struck the bride to the heart.The evening after the catastrophe, Madame Granson sought out the town’s rector, though she had been hostile to him. After she shrouded her son with her own hands, she found the modest priest putting away flax and yarn to help the poor. He took her into his plain dining room; she noticed his frugality. She implored him; he professed to know why she had come and to trust her and Madame du Bousquier’s relation to pacify the bishop at Sées. He agreed to pray and say masses for the unhappy child, but insisted that scandal be avoided. He alone, without other clergy, would bury the body at night to prevent scandal.Toward midnight a coffin was secretly borne to the parish church by four young men, Athanase’s comrades. A few friends, veiled women, and half a dozen intimates attended a discreet mortuary mass said by the rector and a choirboy. The body was carried to a corner of the cemetery and marked by a plain black cross without inscription. Athanase lived and died in shadow. Neither the rector nor the bishop was blamed; the mother’s piety redeemed the impiety of her son’s final act.Months later, half beside herself with grief, Madame Granson went to the pool where her son had drowned, perhaps hoping to recover thoughts of him beneath that poplar or to see what his last eyes had seen. There she met Suzanne, who had come to Alençon the same morning she had heard the tragedy and who had planned—had Athanase lived—to send him several thousand francs by a false pretext. Seeing Madame Granson, Suzanne passed her weeping and whispered, “I loved him!” Before leaving town, Suzanne changed the bride’s orange blossoms to rue and declared that Madame du Bousquier would always be Mademoiselle Cormon, revenging Athanase and the chevalier with one cutting tongue.Alençon also witnessed a slower suicide: the Chevalier de Valois died in life over fourteen years. Three months after the du Bousquier marriage, his linen was frayed, hair unkempt; the elegance that had masked grief collapsed. The cavalier who had sustained appearances now showed the soul’s power over the body. Hope deserted him and he aged overnight—pallid, gaunt, his nose’s delicate grace lost under tobacco stains; his clever sayings grew rarer though he still took snuff and alarming dinners. His intercourse with the Princess Goritza lessened. One day he appeared at Mademoiselle Armande’s salon with his calf of leg on his shin-bone—an image that horrified the town. The chevalier, who for ten years had awaited Mademoiselle Cormon and missed his game, frightened society by this decay. A mortified grisette revealed his mornings’ details; he now passed for a libertine and bore blames the liberals sometimes ascribed to du Bousquier. The faubourg Saint-Germain, pitying him, drew him closer and restored a few smiles; but enmity against du Bousquier hardened. Eleven persons deserted the Cormon salon for the d’Esgrignons.The old maid’s marriage divided Alençon into parties. The salon d’Esgrignon represented the old aristocracy (the returning Troisvilles attached themselves to it); the Cormon salon under du Bousquier represented a class of opinion neither truly liberal nor resolutely royalist, the class that had cast votes like the famous 221. The salon du Ronceret, secretly allied to Cormon, was boldly liberal.The Abbé de Sponde, after returning from Prébaudet, felt inward pain and told no word to his niece; to Mademoiselle Armande he admitted he would have preferred the chevalier over Monsieur du Bousquier. The abbé mourned the loss of his little grove and lindens—symbols of home and prayer—cut down to make an English garden. Du Bousquier, with Abbé Couterier’s authorization, made alterations to please his wife that destroyed the venerable dignity of the old house: mahogany and blue satin, stucco columns and Greek moldings produced a mélange of out-of-season magnificence. At first the town gabbled, but months later took pride in the luxury. Manufacturers restored houses, modern furniture arrived, and astral lamps appeared. Yet the Abbé de Sponde perceived private unhappiness: noble simplicity had governed their lives; that character was lost the first winter when du Bousquier gave two balls every month—the abbé prayed on his knees during the revels. Political tone in the salon was perverted. The abbé saw du Bousquier’s imperiousness and his niece losing control of her property—left only the linen and table to manage—while Jacquelin became coachman, René groom, and a Parisian chef took charge. He saw in du Bousquier not merely ambition but a willingness to reconcile and seduce the clergy to consolidate power: du Bousquier arranged a reconciliation between the abbé’s friend, the minister of Saint-Léonard, and the parish rector. The abbé considered this an apostasy; he died as orthodoxy seemed to expire in the diocese.In 1819 the abbé’s bequest increased Madame du Bousquier’s income to twenty-five thousand francs, not counting Prébaudet or Val-Noble. Du Bousquier returned her capital and used it to buy lands contiguous to Prébaudet, enlarging the domain. He passed for one of the department’s richest men, a frequent liberal candidate who narrowly missed victory and then ostentatiously repudiated them by seeking royalist office. This rancorous republican, mad with ambition, resolved to rival and ruin Alençon’s aristocracy when it regained power. He strengthened himself with the Church by a feint of piety—accompanying his wife to mass, giving to convents, aiding congregations—secretly supported by liberals and protected by clerical approval. Calling himself a constitutional royalist, he kept beside the aristocracy to undermine it, and he succeeded.He subsidized point d’Alençon manufacture, renewed the linen trade, helped found a factory; Alençon gained industrial life. He posed as a banker and promoter of enterprises—insurance, public conveyances, petitions for roads and bridges—troubling the government until it yielded to community interest. Du Bousquier embittered provincial nobility against court nobility and the peerage, bringing constitutional royalists to side with the “Journal des Débats” and M. de Chateaubriand against the throne—a harbinger of journalism’s triumph in 1830.After 1830 he became receiver-general, relying on relations with the Duc d’Orléans and Monsieur de Folmon, receiving about eighty thousand francs a year. In public he appeared respectable, steady and obliging. Alençon owed him its industrial ties and the sudden proliferation of private carriages. Du Ronceret’s remark, “Du Bousquier is a very strong man,” became the countryside’s verdict.But to his wife the saying had a double meaning. The public politician was a domestic despot. This great citizen, generous to the world, was cold and without conjugal affection at home: astute, hypocritical, sly—a Cromwell in velvet. His wife bore no children; Suzanne’s and the chevalier’s insinuations seemed vindicated. The liberal bourgeoisie and the church party blamed Madame du Bousquier, saying she was too old at marriage and that pregnancy would have been dangerous. Friends praised du Bousquier, saying she was lucky to have him. Misjudged by society, the pious woman displayed her virtues at home. She lived in tears but presented a placid face; she told herself she had loved the chevalier yet had married du Bousquier, and Athanase’s death pursed her like remorse. Her uncle’s death added to her sorrow; grief soaked into her life.The chevalier contrived to expose du Bousquier’s baseness to her by eliciting, in the intimacy of the d’Esgrignon salon, the secrets of her house—the inability to have children and the deceptions of her marriage—thereby rendering du Bousquier for a time ridiculous and odious. Ridicule faded, gossip subsided, but the incident intensified du Bousquier’s hatred of Esgrignon. He took public revenge by inserting a spiteful notice in his own Courrier de l’Orne, deriding the existence of some Monsieur de Pombreton.Though her marriage was essentially negative, Madame du Bousquier found advantages: a remarkable man about her was preferable to solitude. Du Bousquier was preferable to a dog. He showed for his wife a sentiment more real and less selfish than that felt by servants or hopeful heirs. Later she regarded him as an instrument of divine chastisement: she had brought sorrow on Madame Granson and hastened her uncle’s death; obedient to religion, she kissed the rod and publicly praised him while weeping in prayer.Bruised and immolated, compelled to make her husband happy and attached to him by habit and duty, her life became perpetual contradiction. She had married a man she hated but whom she nevertheless tended. Often she walked in spiritual consolation while he ate her preserves with satisfaction. She watched to satisfy his slightest wish and read proofs of his dutiful oblivion in small attentions. When he kissed her forehead and called her “a good woman,” tears came to her eyes. He likely made concessions enough to earn her respect; Catholic virtue did not demand total dissimulation. She withdrew into severe religious practices and ceased thinking of vanity. She presented to the world a union of Christian virtue; du Bousquier was one of the luckiest men in France.The narrative notes the coincidence of the Chevalier de Valois’s death with Suzanne’s mother’s death: the chevalier died, broken by the fall of the monarchy, in August 1830. He had joined Charles X’s cortege and returned weakened, carrying money he intended for the king’s disposal; he died the day Charles X set foot on foreign soil. At the chevalier’s sale, Suzanne bought a snuff-box portrait for a thousand francs; later it passed to a collector. If the dead could see after them, the chevalier would have blushed for many things.The story closes with two reflective remarks. First, if it prompts possessors of relics to bequeath them to loved hands, it will please the romantic. Second, it contains a higher moral: the need for a new kind of education—anthropology chairs—because modern myths, like ancient ones, are misunderstood. Had Mademoiselle Cormon been more cultivated, had Alençon had a professor of anthropology, or had she read Ariosto, much of the conjugal tragedy might have been averted; she might have understood why mythic figures choose one lover over another. The narrator attributes this opinion to a pupil of M. Ballanche without claiming full responsibility.No information is given about the fate of the negroes’ heads in diamonds. Madame du Val-Noble may be seen at the Opéra; thanks to the chevalier’s guidance she appears nearly well-bred. Madame du Bousquier still lives, and after sixty confided to Madame du Coudrai that she could not endure the thought of dying an old maid.ADDENDUMThe following personages from this sketch appear elsewhere in the Human Comedy: Bordin; Du Bousquier (Du Croisier or Du Bourguier); Madame du Bousquier (Mlle. Cormon); De Casteran; Choisnel; Du Coudrai; Charles-Marie-Victor-Ange-Carol, Marquis d’Esgrignon; Marie-Armande-Claire d’Esgrignon; Madame Théodore Gaillard (Suzanne); Athanase Granson; Duc de Lenoncourt; Duc de Navarreins; Marquis de Pombreton; Du Ronceret; Madame du Ronceret; Admiral de Simeuse; Vicomte de Troisville; Chevalier de Valois; Duc de Verneuil.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Story midpoint############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################To Baron Von Hammer-Purgstall, Member of the Aulic Council, Author of the History of the Ottoman Empire.Dear Baron,—Your warm interest in my long, vast “History of French Manners in the Nineteenth Century,” and the encouragement you have given me to persevere, have made me feel entitled to associate your name with a portion of it. Are you not one of the most conscientious, studious representatives of Germany? Will not your approval secure for me the approval of others, and shield this attempt of mine? I am proud to have gained your good opinion, and have striven to deserve it by continuing my labors with the unflagging courage characteristic of your method of study and of that exhaustive documentary research by which you gave your monumental work to letters. Your sympathy with such labor as you yourself have bestowed upon the most brilliant civilization of the East has often sustained my ardor through nights of toil on the details of our modern civilization. You, whose naive kindliness can be compared only with our own La Fontaine’s, will be glad to know of this.May this token of my respect for you and your work find you at Dobling, dear Baron, and remind you and yours of one of your most sincere admirers and friends.DE BALZAC.There stands a house at a corner of a street, in the middle of a town in one of the less important prefectures in France. The names of the street and town must be suppressed by convention; if a writer attempts to chronicle his own age he must touch many sore subjects, and discretion is due. The house was called the Hotel d’Esgrignon; let d’Esgrignon be a conventional name, as much a fiction as Belval or Floricour. Though the principal names will be disguised, truth will peep through contradictions and improbabilities, like shoots from a root you have thought uprooted.The “Hotel d’Esgrignon” was simply the house of the old Marquis—Charles Marie Victor Ange Carol, Marquis d’Esgrignon. It was an ordinary house, but townspeople first joked about the name and, after twenty years, used it seriously.The name Carol (or Karawl, as some would spell it) was glorious among Northmen chieftains who conquered Gaul and established feudalism. Their descent had been consistent: Carol never bowed before King, Commune, Church, or Finance. Charged once with guarding a French March, the marquisate in their family was a real office, not an empty title. Their fief had always been their domain. They were provincial nobles in every sense, with an unbroken line; neglected by court for two centuries, they were lords paramount locally, regarded with something like awe. The house of d’Esgrignon, buried in its remote border country, was preserved as one preserves charred piles of an ancient bridge—intact by reverence. For thirteen centuries daughters of the house married without dowries or took the veil; younger sons contented themselves with dower shares and went into the Church or the army; a cadet once became an admiral, duke, and peer of France, and died without issue. The Marquis of the elder branch would never accept a ducal title.“I hold my marquisate as His Majesty holds the realm of France, and on the same conditions,” he told the Constable de Luynes—who seemed paltry to him then.The d’Esgrignons lost heads on the scaffold during the troubles. The old blood showed proud even in 1789. The Marquis of that day refused to emigrate; he was answerable for his March. The countryside’s reverence saved his head, though sans-culottes’ hatred compelled him to pretend to flee, and for a while he lived in hiding. In the name of the Sovereign People, the d’Esgrignon lands were dishonored by the District, forests sold by the Nation despite the Marquis’s protest; he was forty then. Mlle. d’Esgrignon, his half-sister, saved some of the fief thanks to Chesnel, the young steward, who claimed for her the partage de présuccession—the right of a relative to a portion of an émigré’s lands. Thus the Republic made over the castle itself and some farms to Mlle. d’Esgrignon. Chesnel, faithful steward, bought in his own name the church, parsonage, castle gardens, and places dear to his patron—the Marquis advancing the money. Chesnel later became a notary.The Terror’s slow, swift years passed, and the Marquis, esteemed in the countryside, decided he and his sister should return to the castle to improve the property Chesnel had saved from wreck. But the plundered, dismantled castle was too vast for a lord shorn of rights; too large for an estate whose woods had been sold piecemeal, yielding scarcely nine thousand francs’ income from residual estates.

Augustine Guillaume's cousin's tale is worth mentioning. Élodie-Rose, the widow of Maelien, lived a very miserable life. She was born in the same city, Paris, in the same year as Augustine, and grew up happily alongside her, until she got married. She married very early at 16, to a skinny man exactly twice her age with the same hair color and height,  and moved to Albi with him immediately afterward. The last time Augustine and Élodie-Rose saw each other was at Élodie-Rose’s wedding. Élodie-Rose became single again very shortly after and had to continue her life in a town far from her family. She endured a life full of hardship and pain. The six feet tall lady aged very quickly—her dark black hair turned gray, she developed stage 4 glioblastoma, the worst type of brain cancer, and her beautiful hazel eyes grew sad. She lived in Albi for two decades, most of it alone,  and died there a lonely death.


In October 1800 Chesnel brought the Marquis back to the old feudal castle. The sight moved Chesnel deeply: his patron stood in an empty courtyard gazing at a filled moat and towers razed to roof level. The descendant of the Franks searched for missing Gothic turrets and weather vanes, his eyes turned to the sky as if to ask heaven why social upheaval had occurred. Only Chesnel could understand the deep anguish of the great d’Esgrignon, now called Citizen Carol. The Marquis stood long in silence, drinking in the place—his forefathers’ ancient home, the air about it—then flung out a melancholy exclamation.“Chesnel,” he said, “we will come back again some day when the troubles are over; I could not bring myself to live here until the edict of pacification has been published; _they_ will not allow me to set my escutcheon on the wall.”He waved to the castle, mounted his horse, and rode back beside his sister, who had driven in the notary’s shabby basket-chaise.The Hotel d’Esgrignon in town had been demolished; factories now stood where the aristocrat’s house had been. So Chesnel spent the Marquis’s last louis on the old-fashioned building in the square—with its gables, weather-vane, turret, and dovecote. It had been the old courthouse and presidial; it had belonged to the d’Esgrignons for generations; and for five hundred louis it was made over in the title given by the Nation to its rightful lord. Half in jest, half seriously, the old house was christened the Hotel d’Esgrignon.In 1800 little difficulty was made over removing names from the fatal list, and a few émigrés returned. Among the first were the Baron de Nouastre and his daughter, ruined. M. d’Esgrignon generously offered them shelter; two months later the Baron died of grief in his house. The Nouastres were of the best provincial blood; Mlle. de Nouastre was twenty-two; the Marquis married her to continue his line. She died in childbirth, through an unskilful physician, leaving a son to bear the d’Esgrignon name. The old Marquis—only fifty-three but aged by adversity—saw the loveliest of women die, a noble figure in whom the charm of sixteenth-century femininity seemed to live. With her death joy left his old age. He stood beside her bed, kissed her forehead, drew out his watch, broke the mainspring, and hung it by the hearth. It was eleven in the morning.“Mlle. d’Esgrignon,” he said, “let us pray God that this hour may not prove fatal yet again to our house. My uncle the archbishop was murdered at this hour; at this hour also my father died—”He knelt and buried his face in the coverlet; his sister did likewise. Mlle. d’Esgrignon wept; the old Marquis’s eyes were dry. To stubborn Frankness he united Christian fortitude.These events occurred in the second year of the nineteenth century. Mlle. d’Esgrignon was then twenty-seven, a beautiful woman. An ex-contractor for army forage with an income of six thousand francs, a district man, persuaded Chesnel to carry a marriage proposal to the lady. The Marquis and his sister were indignant at such presumption; Chesnel was almost heartbroken—he could not forgive himself for yielding. The Marquis’s manner toward his old servant changed; never again was their affection the same. From then on the Marquis’s gratitude wounded Chesnel; to some exalted natures gratitude seems an excessive payment—what they prefer is the equality that springs from mutual choice. Chesnel had known high friendship; the Marquis had raised him to his level. The Marquis saw his old servitor as more than a servant, less than a child—a voluntary liege man bound by affection. There were no balanced obligations; sincere affection made them unnecessary.To the Marquis, Chesnel’s official dignity was nothing—he remained the notary in disguise. Chesnel, who felt part of the family, was furious with the presumed suitor du Croisier. He set others against Sieur du Croisier. Du Croisier, on the other hand, bore a grudge and nursed vengeance. He hated Chesnel and the d’Esgrignon family with the smothered, all-absorbing hate common in a country town. The rebuff ruined his standing among malicious provincials; in despair he courted a wealthy old maid and was refused again. He lost his hope of marrying Mlle. d’Esgrignon—access to the Faubourg Saint-Germain of the province— and his credit fell. He then married the old maid who had refused him and thereby worsened feuds.In 1805 M. de la Roche-Guyon, an old family allied to the d’Esgrignons, proposed formally through Chesnel for Mlle. Marie Armande Clair d’Esgrignon. She declined, saying she was a mother—she had just put her nephew, little Victurnien, to bed.The old Marquis rose, went up to his sister, returned from the cradle, kissed her hand reverently, and sat down again to say, “My sister, you are a d’Esgrignon.”A quiver ran through the noble girl; tears stood in her eyes. M. d’Esgrignon, the father of the present Marquis, had married secondly the daughter of a tax farmer ennobled by Louis XIV—a shocking mesalliance to the family, though of no importance since a daughter was the sole child. Armande knew this; the brother, kind as he had been, still regarded her as a stranger. His words recognized her as one of the family. Her answer crowned eleven years of noble devotion: “I shall die Mlle. d’Esgrignon,” she said simply to Chesnel.“For you there could be no fairer title,” returned Chesnel, intending a compliment. Poor Mlle. d’Esgrignon reddened.“You have blundered, Chesnel,” the Marquis said, flattered but vexed that his sister was hurt. He discoursed on the family heraldry—“A d’Esgrignon may marry a Montmorency; their descent is not so pure as ours. The d’Esgrignons bear or, two bends, gules”—and spoke of their device, Cil est nostre, taken at a tournament in Philip Augustus’s reign, with supporters: a knight or on the right and a lion gules on the left.“I do not remember that any woman I have ever met struck my imagination as Mlle. d’Esgrignon did,” Emile Blondet later wrote—who provides much of this history. He confesses his boyhood admiration perhaps colored by youth. If playing on the Parade and she walked by with her nephew Victurnien, seeing her thrilled him like galvanism. As a child he felt revived at sight of her. She had tawny-gold hair; a delicate down on her cheek with a silver gleam he loved to catch. Her dreamy emerald eyes flashed fire when they met his. He pretended to roll on the grass in games to admire her little feet up close; her soft whiteness, delicate features, clear forehead, and slender figure fascinated him without his fully understanding why. When she asked, in a musical voice, “What are you doing little one? Why do you look at me?” he would wriggle, bite his nails, red, and answer, “I do not know.” If she stroked his hair and asked his age, he would run and call, “Eleven!”Every princess and fairy of his readings looked and walked like Mlle. d’Esgrignon; later when copying heads from the antique, he noticed their hair braided like hers. When fancies vanished, Mlle. Armande remained a type—he would see her as again Agnes Sorel, Marie Touchet, Gabrielle. He lent her all the love she never expressed. The angelic glimpses of her haunt him even as an adult.Keep this portrait: it is faithful and instructive. Mlle. d’Esgrignon shows how pure goodness, without intelligence, may cause mischief.Two-thirds of émigrés returned to France in 1804–1805; many exiles from the Marquis’s province returned. Some defected, entering Napoleon’s service or marrying upstart families; those who sided with the Empire regained fortunes and properties and mostly went to Paris. But eight or nine families remained true to the proscribed noblesse and to Bourbon monarchy: La Roche-Guyons, Nouastres, Verneuils, Casterans, Troisvilles, and others. Rich or poor, for them pedigree mattered above money. The Marquis d’Esgrignon was their acknowledged head. His house became their cenacle. There His Majesty, Emperor and King, was still “M. de Bonaparte”; “the King” meant Louis XVIII at Mittau; the Department was the Province; the prefecture the intendance.The Marquis was honored for admirable behavior, noble loyalty, and undaunted courage; and respected in town for misfortunes, fortitude, and adherence to convictions. Such a man assumed the majestic dignity of ruined greatness. His chivalrous fair-mindedness was well known; litigants sometimes referred disputes to him. Even gentle Imperialists and authorities indulged his prejudices; but a rising section of new society—the Liberal party—laughed at an aristocratic oasis that one could not enter without proving irreproachable descent. Their animosity was all the keener because many town worthies believed the best society was to be found in the Marquis’s salon. The prefect’s wife, a Grandlieu, was sent as ambassadress.Those excluded nicknamed the salon “The Collection of Antiquities,” and called the Marquis “M. Carol.” The receiver of taxes addressed his applications to “M. Carol (ci-devant des Grignons),” mockingly adopting an obsolete spelling.“If I try to recall childhood,” Emile Blondet said, “the nickname always made me laugh despite my respect—my love—for Mlle. d’Esgrignon. The Hotel stood at the angle of two busy thoroughfares, not five hundred paces from the market. Two drawing-room windows looked on the street and two on the square; the room was like a glass cage: anyone passing could see through from side to side. Even at twelve I thought the salon a rare curiosity, on the border between reality and dream.”The room, the ancient Hall of Audience, stood above cellars with grated air-holes—once prison cells of the old courthouse, now used as a kitchen. Blondet remembered the magnificent lofty hearth, covered in carved tracery like a melon. Above it an equestrian relief of Henri III., who reclaimed the ancient duchy for the crown, in a carved gilt frame. The ceiling between chestnut cross-beams bore scroll-work; remnants of gilding remained. The walls were hung with Flemish tapestry—six scenes from the Judgment of Solomon—framed with garlands, satyrs, and cupids. The parquet was laid by the present Marquis; Chesnel had acquired furniture at sale: Louis-Quatorze consoles, tables, clock-cases, andirons, sconces, and tapestry-covered chairs, which completed a stately room, large for the house. An ante-chamber, the old Salle des Pas Perdus, communicated with the magistrate’s deliberating chamber, used as dining-room.Beneath old panelling, amid threadbare glories, eight or ten dowagers were arrayed in state in a quavering line—some palsied, others dark and shriveled, some erect, some bent—all tricked out in more or less fantastic outdated costumes with ribboned caps and faded lace. No painter or caricaturist ever caught the haunting fascination of those aged women; they come back in dreams. Whether misfortune taught me the secrets of irremediable disaster, or I grew to understand human feelings and the thoughts of Old Age and Regret, I know nowhere else have I seen the wan gray eyes or the dreadful brightness of others that I remember.Neither Hoffmann nor Maturin ever gave Blondet such a thrill of terror as the automaton movements of those bodies sheathed in whalebone. Actors’ painted faces do not shock because the rouge is visible; but years had leveled those women’s faces and furrowed them into wrinkles, making them like carved German nutcrackers. Peering through the windows, Blondet noted battered bodies, ill-jointed limbs, lantern jaws, protuberant bones, abnormal hips; their movements as they came and went seemed as extraordinary as their sepulchral stillness at the card-tables.The men looked gray and faded like the tapestries on the walls. Their dress conformed to the day, but their white hair, waxen faces, devastated foreheads, and pale eyes revealed kinship to the women, neutralizing any reality the costume might suggest.The certainty of finding them at the same tables daily gave them in Blondet’s eyes a theatrical, unearthly interest. When he and schoolboys proposed to look at curiosities in the glass cage for fun, but seeing Mlle. Armande made him tremble. There was jealousy in his admiration for little Victurnien—he who belonged, they felt instinctively, to a different, higher order. It seemed strange that a fresh young creature should be in that awakened cemetery before its time. They could not explain it, but felt bourgeois and insignificant in presence of that proud court.The disasters of 1813–1814, bringing Napoleon’s downfall, gave new life and hope to the Collection of Antiquities; but the events of 1815, the occupation, and government vacillation delayed fulfillment of their expectations. This story only begins to shape itself in 1822.By 1822 the Marquis’s fortunes had not much improved despite Restoration changes for émigrés. Of all nobles hit by Revolutionary legislation, his case was hardest. Before 1789 the d’Esgrignons derived most income from manorial dues paid by their tenants; they had reduced holdings to swell quit-rents and heriots. Families in this position were hopelessly ruined. They were not helped by Louis XVIII’s ordinance returning unsold lands, and later indemnity laws could not restore suppressed rights; what revived was a land tax called domaines, with revenues going to the State.The Marquis belonged to the small Royalist section that would tolerate no compromise with those they called, not Revolutionaries, but revolted subjects—or in parliamentary terms, Liberals or Constitutionnels. These Royalists, nicknamed Ultras by opposition, looked to Right-wing orators like M. de Polignac to protest the charter Louis XVIII had granted—regarded as an ill-advised, temporary concession, to be annulled when convenient. Thus the Marquis d’Esgrignon stood aloof, upholding the strictest Right until his vast fortune should be restored. He even scorned the indemnity advocated by the Villele ministry, intended to strengthen the Crown by ending lingering ownership distinctions.The miracles of the Restoration in 1814, Napoleon’s return in 1815, their undoings, and reinstatements took the Marquis by surprise at sixty-seven. At that age many high-spirited men were worn out by long struggle; their activity in provincial retreats had hardened into immovable conviction, and they lived an enervating routine. Could anything be worse for a party than representation by old men when its ideas are already considered antiquated?When Louis seemed firmly seated again in 1818, the Marquis asked what a man of seventy could do at court; content he was with the triumph of Monarchy and Religion while waiting for results of that indecisive victory. He continued as before, lord-paramount of his salon, the Collection of Antiquities.But when victors of 1793 became vanquished, the nickname first given in jest was used in bitter earnest. The town, like other country towns, was full of party hatreds; du Croisier, unexpectedly, married the old maid who had refused him at first; this marriage produced a provincial war and hastened separation of great and small noblesse, of aristocratic and bourgeois elements which had briefly united under Napoleonic pressure. After the pressure lifted, class divisions revived with damage.Vanity is the most national of French sentiments. Wounded vanity induced a thirst for Equality, although as the most ardent innovator will discover, Equality is impossible. Royalists pricked Liberals where they were most sensitive; this was sharpest in the provinces, where both sides accused the other of atrocities and employed the blackest deeds to secure public opinion. Individuals became identified with political opinions; opponents became private enemies. In a country town, theoretical disputes of Paris become personal combats—two duelists on watch for a chance to thrust steel. Each seeks to unbalance the other; political hatred becomes all-consuming. Epigram and slander are used to bring a party into discredit.In such warfare, the nobles had the advantage of wit and delicate irony; du Croisier’s faction resorted to poisoned weapons. The Chevalier, who chose the aristocratic Mons Sacer and turned his back on mixed society, directed his jests at du Croisier’s salon, stoking fires he could not foresee. Only purists and loyal gentlemen and women entered the Hotel d’Esgrignon; they committed no indiscretions. If Liberals sought to ridicule the nobles, they fastened on political actions; meanwhile the intermediate class—officials and others who paid court to higher powers—kept nobles informed of Liberal doings, much of it laughable. Du Croisier’s adherents smarted under inferiority, which increased revenge thirst.In 1822 du Croisier headed the province’s manufacturing interest while the Marquis headed the noblesse. Each represented a party. But du Croisier, rather than pose frankly as extreme Left, adopted positions later associated with the 221 deputies, thus keeping touch with magistrates, officials, and capitalists. His salon rivaled the d’Esgrignon one in power, larger, younger, and more energetic, felt across the countryside; the Collection of Antiquities remained inert, a passive appendage, often embarrassing central authority; Conservative pressure sometimes led the Government to fatal blunders.The Liberals had never successfully carried a candidate. The department declined to obey them knowing du Croisier would, if elected, sit on the Left-Centre benches and lean Left. He corresponded with the Keller brothers, bankers linked to the nineteen deputies of the Left; M. Keller was related by marriage to Comte de Gondreville, a Constitutional peer still in favor. Thus the Constitutional Opposition was prepared to vote last-moment, not for its professed candidate but for du Croisier if necessary; yet du Croisier was routinely defeated by Royalists. The party leaders, taking tone from the Marquis, had thoroughly gauged him; each defeat made du Croisier more bitter. Nothing so stirs up strife as the failure of an elaborate snare.By 1822 hostilities seemed to lull—both salons measured strengths and waited for opportunity. Ordinary folk were content with surface quiet that deceived the Government; those who knew du Croisier knew his implacable passion for revenge, especially in political ambition. Du Croisier, who used to turn white and red at the bare mention of d’Esgrignon or the Chevalier, chose an impassive countenance masking savage feeling. He smiled on enemies while hating them the more, watching them hourly. One partisan aiding him was M. du Ronceret, President of the Tribunal, a small squire who’d vainly tried to enter the Antiquities.The d’Esgrignons’ small fortune, managed carefully by Chesnel, barely sufficed; though they lived without ostentation, they lived like nobles. The governor found an Oratorian tutor for the heir, the young Comte Victurnien d’Esgrignon, who lived with the family and drew a salary. The wages of a cook, a waiting-woman for Mlle. Armande, an old valet for the Marquis, and a few other servants, daily household expenses, and the cost of a thorough education—spared in no way—absorbed the family income despite Mlle. Armande’s economies and Chesnel’s careful management, and the servants’ affection. Chesnel had not yet begun repairs on the ruined castle; he waited for leases to fall in so he might raise farm rents—rents had been rising due to improved agriculture and currency depreciation, benefits the landlord would realize at lease expirations of 1809.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The Marquis knew nothing of his household’s management or finances. Near the end of his life he and his circle clung to the illusion that the neglected d’Esgrignon house would be revived by his handsome heir, Victurnien, who would go to Court, enter royal service, and marry into an illustrious family. Their intimates—the Troisvilles, La Roche-Guyon, the Casterans, and the Duc de Verneuil—encouraged this belief. But forty years of history could not be erased: the restored monarchy and public opinion now demanded not only noble birth but money, political relevance, and continuous prestige. Old renown could not substitute for wealth and influence. Even the Troisvilles’ daughter’s marriage to General Montcornet, far from flattering antiquated pride, nearly split social circles—evidence that times had changed.Only Chesnel, the family’s old notary and former steward, saw the situation clearly. Devoted yet practical, he had witnessed the Revolution’s violence and the triumph of new social ideas; he understood that confiscations and deaths were binding facts and that the new order would not be overturned. His attachment to the d’Esgrignons was therefore affectionate and lucid. He tried tactfully to warn the Marquis, who met caution with a prophet’s smile and the conviction that history would repeat in their favor. Chesnel feared their superstitions and self-delusions would ruin Victurnien’s character.Idolized by his father and aunt, Victurnien was thoroughly spoiled but in some respects justified their hopes. Mlle. Armande loved him as a mother might but lacked maternal severity and foresight; she could not foresee danger. Chesnel’s pragmatic vigilance resembled a mother’s instinct more than the aunt’s blind adoration, but the household generally paid little heed to his counsels—he had even fallen into disgrace after proposing an imprudent marriage for the family.From infancy Victurnien was taught to assume superiority: peers among great nobles, the King his only superior, and the rest of mankind beneath him. With beauty, tact, a quick mind, and a thorough education—average height, graceful, an excellent horseman and marksman—he had every advantage to be a great man. But indulged and unopposed, he became insolent, audacious, and self-willed. The Chevalier, a charming old roue, misled the boy by glorifying the reckless manners and witty immorality of an earlier era; his jocular maxims mingled with the family’s rigid emphasis on honor. Thus good and bad counsel shaped Victurnien.At eighteen he entered society and quickly misread appearances. Accustomed to deference at home and to unguarded talk before an elderly father, he failed to perceive the hypocrisies and practical cautions of the larger world. His reckless behavior—shooting over neighbors’ lands, amorous imprudences requiring settlements and legal cover-ups—led to lawsuits and scandals quietly hushed by Chesnel, who paid to avert public shame. Encouraged by adroit enemies—particularly Du Croisier, who plotted revenge against the family—Victurnien’s faults were fanned. President du Ronceret and other officials, while outwardly lenient, fostered liberal clamor and public suspicion to discredit nobles; Du Croisier hoped to drive the d’Esgrignons into ruin through the young man’s vices. A young instigator, Fabien du Ronceret, befriended Victurnien to lead him on.Between eighteen and twenty-one Victurnien ran up considerable debts—nearly eighty thousand francs paid out by Chesnel, who hid this from Mlle. Armande and the Marquis. The family’s mode of life left little margin: the Marquis’s income barely covered the household. Victurnien’s tailor bills, tastes for English saddles and carriages, livery, and fashionable accessories swallowed his allowance. Chesnel, drawing on private savings of about twelve thousand livres yearly, became the young man’s secret benefactor, paying off lawsuits and bailing him out repeatedly. He hoped a sensible marriage would settle Victurnien, but the young man’s temperament—capable of lofty thought yet habitually surrendering to impulse—made him likely to repeat mistakes.Chesnel’s patience and warnings met with shrugging complacency from the Chevalier and indulgence from the family. The Chevalier dismissed debt as par for a gentleman’s course, recalling aristocratic generosity and minimizing danger. Chesnel foresaw ruin. His fear and affection prompted a decisive plan: to have Victurnien sent to Paris and presented at court, hoping the change would sober the boy and provide opportunities befitting his rank.One autumn evening in 1822, after cards and salon talk, the household confronted the truth about Victurnien’s debts. The Chevalier forced disclosure to the Marquis: Chesnel had lent an enormous sum—“something like a hundred thousand livres”—to the Count. The Marquis was mortified that his devoted steward would be the family’s creditor; pride and old notions of dignity made it painful to ask Chesnel for help plainly. Nevertheless, they resolved to send Victurnien to Paris to present himself to the King and secure an appointment; they accepted Chesnel’s loan rather than humiliate the family by other means.Chesnel explained that Victurnien’s companions and enemies in town—the president, Du Croisier and his circle—were seeking vengeance by ensnaring the young man, provoking scandals to ruin the family. He had, accordingly, borrowed money himself, mortgaging Le Jard, and had made arrangements in confidence so the loan would not appear as a direct family shame. Mlle. Armande and the Marquis were moved; Chesnel kissed her hand and, with paternal tenderness, urged that Paris provide the boy with more amusement and diversion than provincial society could, while also placing him where discretion and better company might discipline him.Preparations were hurried. The Marquis and Mlle. Armande instructed Victurnien to go to court, serve His Majesty, and there seek a career—army, navy, diplomacy, or royal household. They promised a modest provision and sent Josephin, Victurnien’s trusted valet, as companion. Chesnel wrote to an old notary friend in Paris, Maître Sorbier, begging vigilant oversight and intimating funds would be placed at Sorbier’s disposal. His letter revealed a deep emotional tie: he felt he had reared and protected the d’Esgrignons through revolution and decline, and that the young Count’s fate was intimately his concern.Victurnien accepted a monthly allowance of two thousand francs and left amid blessings. He felt no genuine regret; provincial life had been gentle but narrow, and Paris promised to amplify his vanity and gratify his tastes. In the capital he discovered that noble names no longer guaranteed employment; money and new social forces mattered most. Undeterred, he took to luxury and fashion as necessary to command respect and assumed a posture of independence that concealed need.He took rooms, stables, and equerries at great expense. His family’s reputation and the Faubourg Saint‑Germain’s curiosity opened many doors: old names still served as social passports. He found a circle of fashionable young men—de Marsay, Rastignac, de Ronquerolles, Maxime de Trailles—who initiated him into pleasures. The Vidame de Pamiers, a discreet source of gossip and introductions, welcomed Victurnien and took him to salons and dinners where literature and wit mixed with vanity and intrigue. There he met the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, an ethereal, calculating beauty whose pose of innocuous purity masked self-regard and power.Thus Victurnien entered fashionable life at its center. His name, looks, and Faubourg patronage made him desirable; his vanity made him determined to sustain an image of wealth and consequence. Chesnel’s precautions were undermined by Parisian finance and notaries eager for patronage; the young Count’s expenses—lodgings, dress, horses, entertainments—quickly devoured the funds set aside. Despite Chesnel’s hopes that Paris would rescue him from provincial follies, the city offered new temptations, alliances, and risks that promised either brilliant ascent or irreversible ruin.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By the end of 1823 the Kellers had advanced Victurnien two hundred thousand francs, a fact neither Chesnel nor Mlle. Armande suspected. Victurnien had also taken two thousand crowns from Chesnel in small sums to conceal his sources, while sending deceiving letters to his unsuspecting father and aunt. Parisian life was quietly preparing a dreadful catastrophe for the d’Esgrignon family; only du Croisier knew, and he intended not merely to ruin but to dishonor them. He had been certain of this since discovering the dangerous growth of Victurnien’s debts.Du Croisier’s first move was to corner his most hated enemy, the kindly notary Chesnel. The old man, living modestly in the Rue du Bercail and devoted to preserving the d’Esgrignon estates for posterity, was unprepared when Du Croisier arrived one evening and produced a bill for two hundred and twenty-seven thousand francs—principal and interest—for sums advanced to Victurnien in bills of exchange drawn upon, and honored by, du Croisier. He demanded immediate payment and threatened legal proceedings against the heir-presumptive. Chesnel, distraught, surrendered the unlucky letters and begged for secrecy; du Croisier agreed to hold his peace if paid within forty-eight hours.Pressed for money, Chesnel had already helped manufacturers and used the usual financial stratagems; he was near tears. The only way to raise such a sum would be to mortgage his lands fully. Sensing Chesnel’s incapacity, du Croisier abruptly offered to buy the notary’s property. The sale was arranged within two days. Chesnel, horrified at the thought of the d’Esgrignon heir undergoing imprisonment for debt, accepted; he was left with his practice, the sums due to him, and the house where he lived. Stripped of his lands, he paced his oak-paneled office and ceased thinking of Le Jard, thinking only of Victurnien.“What will become of him? He ought to come back; they must marry him to some rich heiress,” he muttered, burdened by foreboding. He dreaded breaking the news to Mlle.
