Armande; this man who had paid the family’s debts quaked at the thought. He went to the Hotel d’Esgrignon fearing the ruin of illusions.At the hotel Mlle. Armande had just received a charming, mendacious letter from her nephew, the happiest man alive, describing baths and travels in Italy with Mme. de Maufrigneuse—Venice, Milan, Florence—painting happiness everywhere. The poor aunt was enthralled and dreamt herself in Victurnien’s gondola, excusing a married woman’s love for her nephew. She was not an ascetic but a serene, tender figure; at that moment she allowed herself to be carried away.Chesnel’s heavy step on the garden walk recalled her to reality. “What is it?” she cried. “All is lost!” Chesnel answered, holding out the bills and telling briefly of his recent agony. “He is deceiving us! The miserable boy!” she cried. Chesnel accepted some responsibility: “We have allowed him his own way; he needed stern guidance—he had no mother.” The Marquis, having been absorbed in his son’s bright letter, recounted ancestral glories and remained unconcerned. The two companions in distress sat together.“You and M. le Marquis own the estate conjointly; the larger part is yours. You can raise money on it without saying a word to him,” Chesnel said. Mlle. Armande decided she must go to Paris to snatch her nephew from perdition; if anyone could carry him off, it was the woman whose maternal heart yearned over him. She meant to see the Duchess and tell her everything. For appearances she allowed it to be thought she suffered from a complaint requiring consultation with famous Parisian physicians—an excuse for the journey. She took Chesnel’s last bag of louis, her capuchine and mittens without comment. “Generous girl!” Chesnel said, delivering her to the carriage with her maid.Du Croisier had planned his revenge with provincial meticulousness: measured, remorseless vengeance. He had concluded Victurnien’s debts growing perilous and contrived to ruin the family’s credit. He wrote to Victurnien informing him that the Kellers had been instructed to advance no more money; the letter was timed to arrive when the Duchess and the Count faced artful poverty. In that missive he left a wide blank between the formal text and his signature. It was a simple trick: one could tear off the upper part and convert the signed lower part into a bill of exchange for any amount. The sheet’s reverse was blank and its envelope concealed the manipulation.When the letter arrived, Victurnien was already at the lowest ebb. After two years of luxurious, thoughtless living he was confronted by the absolute impossibility of procuring money. With the Duchess’ help he had extorted sums from bankers, but these loans were about to produce overdue bills and stern summonses from the Bank of France and the commercial court. For five years his purse had always been replenished; now, after Chesnel came du Croisier, and he had expected another gold-mine to pour out its wealth. He had been winning at play; fortune had spared him unpleasant necessities before. Now life became a chaotic nightmare—disorder, confusion, a frantic struggle to shut out debts and duels. He alone lived through this infernal prospect, seeing visions of a friendless outcast reading the last words on the last page—THE END.He already imagined acquaintances enjoying his downfall. Even those who had played high could not spare a banknote for a friend. There seemed no help—Chesnel would be ruined; he had been consumed by Victurnien’s extravagance.Sitting with the Duchess in their box at the Italiens, envied by the house but inwardly despairing, Victurnien reached such depths that he had even taken out pistols and thought of suicide. After the Opera he and the Duchess had retreated to their secret lodging; appearances were cleverly saved—their love-nest seemed a garret to outsiders but was luxuriously arranged within. Now, on the brink of ruin, he longed to flee. An evil thought crossed him: he would run away with the Duchess to some remote nook in America only if he could carry a fortune, leaving creditors to confront their bills. To carry out this plan he had only to cut off the lower portion of du Croisier’s letter bearing the signature and fill in figures to turn it into a bill, presenting it to the Kellers. There was a dreadful struggle with temptation; tears were shed; but the honor of the family triumphed—subject to one condition. Victurnien would do nothing unless he was sure of his beautiful Diane; he wanted her consent to their flight.So he went to the Duchess in the Rue Faubourg Saint-Honoré. Mme. de Maufrigneuse was pensive but resolute. She met cares gallantly; underneath graceful helplessness lay strength, insight, rapid decision and a cold resolve. She could either rise to the highest sentiments or show selfish insensibility. Pondering her fall, she set love aside to consider safety: refuge abroad, confessing to the King, captivating a du Tillet or Nucingen, or gambling on the stock exchange—all were calmly reviewed. She could stand apart and watch a crash as a spectator rather than be buried beneath it. Such dispassionate power was formidable.While dressing for a drive she had collected her thoughts and faced the possibilities of terrible downfall. She plucked love out of her heart to weigh necessities and was ready to reinstate passion once her position was safe. She resolved.Victurnien entered, terror in every line. He dared not touch the cornerstone that upheld his life—Diane. He spoke, with aristocratic disdain for sordid detail, of his hopeless situation. “What is the matter?” Diane asked. “I am hopelessly in debt. I have come to the end.” “Pshaw! it is nothing,” she replied with a smile. “Money can always be arranged; nothing is irretrievable except disasters in love.” Relieved by her composure, he unrolled the bright web of his life’s recent past—the seamy side—detailing his needs.She listened, intent and grave with love. When Victurnien proposed flight, she cried, “You are an angel!” The Duchess made no immediate answer; she weighed plans. “Good, very good,” she said at last. “But that is not the question, dear. Let us think of your affairs. Yes, we will go, and the sooner the better. Arrange it; I will follow. It is glorious to leave Paris and the world.” Her promise settled him; he would not go until it was sealed. He counted on his aunt, father, and Chesnel to hush up the affair; perhaps Chesnel might seek some compromise. Besides, obtaining money by this “business” was the only way to raise funds on the family estate. With three hundred thousand francs he and Diane would lead a hidden life in Venice.The next day Victurnien drew a bill for three hundred thousand francs and took it to the Kellers. They advanced the money—du Croisier happened then to have a balance—but cautioned that he must not draw again without notice. Du Croisier, much astonished, asked for a statement of accounts. It was sent. Everything was explained. The day of his vengeance had arrived.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Victurnien withdrew the money, he took it to Mme. de Maufrigneuse, who locked the banknotes in her desk and proposed one last night at the Opera. Distracted by fear that his place in the Duchess’ box would cost him dearly, Victurnien thought it wiser, with the three hundred thousand francs safe, to hurry to Chesnel and confess. The Duchess, unwilling to leave her beloved nest, looked at him in a way that kept him sleepless.The next afternoon Victurnien returned to learn her plan for that night’s escape. She scoffed at going into hiding—other women had vanished, she insisted, and they would brave the storm, certain of success. Victurnien, unnerved, hesitated. Diane reproached him with coldness; stung, he could not yield. She accused him of ingratitude after eighteen months’ tenderness and turned away. He protested, wept, implored; she toyed with her scarf, launched cruel epigrams at his rivals (Vandenesse, de Marsay, Montriveau), and kept him in suspense until, exasperated, he fled in a fury.Near the Quai d’Orsay his horse bolted; Josephin stopped him with news: he could not go home—the authorities had a warrant for his arrest. In his agitation Victurnien imagined a criminal arrest, having forgotten the bills-of-exchange affair that had been “stirred up” into orders to pay and legal proceedings. Told to flee to the Hotel du Bon la Fontaine in the Rue de Grenelle, he seized the refuge of his aunt, Mlle. Armande. At the inn he collapsed into her arms; she wept, half believing herself complicit in his ruin. That night they set out for Brest—Victurnien obsessed with his forgery, his aunt worrying over debts and bills. He suggested hiding in Chesnel’s house; she soothed him like a child.Chance betrayed them. That evening Chesnel had been negotiating to sell his practice to M. Lepressoir’s clerk for a hundred thousand francs. His successor saw Mlle. Armande arrive at an odd hour and watched. When Chesnel opened his door and saw Victurnien, he understood at once. He ushered the Count into a room beyond his private office and, thunderstruck but devoted, wept. Refusing to admit that a forger’s scandal should reach the old man’s father, Chesnel raged and proposed desperate remedies—softening du Croisier, buying back the draft from the Kellers, hiding Victurnien in a cellar—and then set off for Paris himself. He packed, took money, left the Count locked in the room, and told his housekeeper to bar visitors.Chesnel reached Paris in three days. At the Kellers he learned the draft had already returned to du Croisier; the document belonged to du Croisier, who could keep or return it. In despair, Chesnel went to the Duchess. By a mix of persuasion and insolence he gained admittance, told her bluntly the hundred thousand crowns she held had been gained by a forgery committed for love of her. Struck by shame and fear of implication, she handed over the notes and pledged to use her influence to save Victurnien, promising to save him “or die.” Triumphant, Chesnel left for the Rue du Bercail—but arrived an hour too late. The gendarmes had already come and taken Victurnien away.Wild with grief, Chesnel begged the officers for mercy. Seeing the Count led off, he whispered that if Victurnien could not hush the matter he must kill himself before the indictment. Stupefied, Victurnien could not comprehend. Chesnel then dashed to the Hotel du Croisier, where du Croisier sat that night surrounded by intimates—M. du Ronceret, President of the Tribunal; M. Sauvager, deputy Public Prosecutor; M. du Coudrai; the examining magistrate M. Camusot; among others. The gathering was half political, half social; they discussed the d’Esgrignon affair as a test of social and legal principle.Sauvager assumed the complacent tone of a zealous official. Camusot, who had authorized the warrant, was perturbed the arrest had been so prompt. They spoke of counsel from Paris—Berryer for the defense—and of the sensation this case could cause in Landernau, aware that high influence might intervene. Chesnel sought the examining magistrate in the street and implored him: support the house of d’Esgrignon and the Duchess, he hinted, and dukes and ministers would interest themselves in Camusot. He even threatened to denounce suspected corruption at du Croisier’s if the magistrate were hostile. Camusot, already sensitive to his wife’s influence and the wider stakes, listened.Chesnel then tried to soften du Croisier. He begged, on bended knee, offering lands, service, anything if du Croisier would withdraw the charge. But du Croisier reveled in his power. He saw in prosecuting the Count a chance to assert bourgeois dignity against the aristocracy and to put nobles in their place. He delighted in the idea of exciting the Court and the public by bringing down a proud house. He demanded more than money: political advantage, social recognition, and even matrimonial leverage tying property as surety—schemes that Chesnel and Mme. du Croisier found odious.Du Croisier framed the affair as larger than personal revenge: it was “France,” “the people,” a lesson that the bourgeoisie would not be trampled upon. He imagined victory in the Assize Court and a marriage that would cement his political aims. Desperate, Chesnel produced the hundred thousand crowns and implored Mme. du Croisier to accept them to spare the d’Esgrignons. Mme. du Croisier hesitated, torn between her husband’s will and pity. Chesnel, pressing every religious and familial plea, persuaded her to take the money and promise to swear before magistrates that she had received it on a date prior to the contested bill’s drafting—an explicit falsehood he urged as a “venial sin” to save lives and honor.To secure her resolve, Chesnel planned to involve the Abbé Couturier and the bishop, knowing clerical counsel would strengthen Mme. du Croisier’s willingness to perjure herself. He roused Mlle. Armande to rally ecclesiastical support and went home exhausted, having mapped out the men he must influence and the political tactics required.The narrative then widens to the provincial administration. Magistrates often begin careers in the provinces and split into two classes: the settled, torpid men resigned to local life, and the younger, ambitious men who seek promotion to Paris and look to shine at politically charged trials. In this town the bench was divided: conservative elders like President du Ronceret and old Blondet represented the resigned section; younger zealots like Camusot and his deputy formed the ambitious faction eager to make reputations by zealous prosecutions. The provincial administration leaned bourgeois and often displayed an overzealous, politicized public zeal that could overstep discretion.President du Ronceret appears as a petty, resentful man—nominated where he had once expected more—vaunting independence but practicing inconsistency, maintaining social pretensions while nursing private rivalries. Old Blondet, by contrast, is a veteran jurist, prudent and respected though sidelined. Chesnel’s understanding of these factions—their ambitions, petty rancors, and hopes of patronage—shapes his strategy. He sees that many men, still bound to hopes of advancement, may be swayed if they think powerful patrons in Paris support them. Thus he plans to use the Duchess’s influence and court connections as leverage.The crisis deepens: Victurnien has been arrested; Chesnel has bribed, pleaded, and schemed; the Duchess has returned the notes and promised aid; du Croisier delights in his leverage and demands political recompense; Mme. du Croisier, torn, agrees—after Chesnel’s urging and the money in hand—to give false testimony; and the local magistrates, split between ambition and caution, loom as the battleground. Chesnel lies awake mapping out the men to influence, promising to set great powers in motion through Mme. de Maufrigneuse, while the provincial world of judges, clerks, and aspiring officials gathers round the coming trial with all the pettiness and consequence of a local theater of justice.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Blondet possessed legal talent but despised his profession, devoting his ambitions, leisure, and energy to gardening. He corresponded with celebrated amateurs and sought botanical discoveries; his greenhouse, where pelargoniums reigned, was his refuge. Court business mattered little beside the inner world of fancies and emotions he cultivated among flowers. Had he been less absorbed, he might have become a deputy under the Empire and prominent in the Corps Législatif.His marriage deepened that retreat. At forty he rashly wed an eighteen-year-old, who bore him Joseph. Three years later Mme. Blondet, then the town’s prettiest woman, inspired a lasting passion in the prefect, who became the father of her second son, Emile. The affair was public knowledge, including to old Blondet. Rather than fueling ambition, the marriage encouraged his botanical tastes; neither husband nor wife wished to change places. Unequal to contesting youth and beauty, Blondet sought consolation in his greenhouse and kept a pretty servant. Mme. Blondet spent his substance on dress; only Emile’s education interested her. She loved Emile, Blondet loved Joseph, each blinded by paternal partiality.For twelve years Blondet bore these circumstances with eighteenth‑century grand seigneur composure, though he profoundly disliked his younger son. When his wife died in 1818 he turned Emile out, sending him to Paris on a scant allowance; Emile would have perished without help from his real father. Blondet’s house, opposite the prefecture, had a neat court and admired flowerbeds; climbing plants covered walls, honeysuckle hid piers, and cactuses perched in vases. Through glass doors one saw the garden that extended into two acres. Blondet’s income was about four thousand livres from land plus the house. He intended to leave his property and the garden bench to Joseph, making a will favoring him and setting aside money so Joseph could repurchase portions legally reserved to Emile.Emile, turned adrift, found distinction in Paris as a man of letters and in society, aided by friendships like that of Mlle. de Troisville (later Mme. de Montcornet). Mme. Blondet had tried to link him to the Troisvilles, who later favored him in Paris. Emile’s precocious literary reputation and good looks strengthened his bond with the Countess de Montcornet; by the time adventuring pressed upon him, their attachment proved unshakeable. Old Blondet never knew of Emile’s success; convinced his son was cursed, he remained ignorant of Emile’s greatness.Blondet’s integrity matched his passion for flowers. He knew law and botany alone, met litigants courteously, accepted seeds as gifts, and on the bench was impartial; litigants trusted his clear procedure. His steadiness made him indispensable to President du Ronceret, who, apart from matrimonial schemes, would have resisted forced retirement in favor of a son. Blondet could not foresee that Emile might have fulfilled such hopes.Their household was frugal to parsimony: fruit saved until it spoiled, no new bread, strict fast days. The maid, Mlle. Cadot, kept keys and watched expenses; she shared Blondet’s predilection for Joseph. Blondet tried to mold Joseph into a lawyer to satisfy Mlle. Blandureau’s parents, who insisted he be an assistant judge to marry their daughter. Joseph spent evenings at the Blandureaus’, where Fabien du Ronceret also frequented. Everything was measured; the garden, however, escaped thrift. The gardener kept paths and borders immaculate, and an amphitheatre of benches held thousands of pelargoniums that drew visitors from distant departments; Empress Marie Louise once visited and Blondet received the Legion of Honor. Blondet shunned society and was unaware of the President’s machinations.Michu, a young judge with twelve thousand livres income, cared more for pleasing high society than for routine. He did enough at the Tribunal to be useful and, with Parisian training as procuring counsel, could quickly solve legal questions that baffled others. He was socially and professionally indispensable, able to mediate delicate matters.Crisis arrived while the public prosecutor was in Paris. The President and du Croisier seized his absence to engineer a charge of forgery against Victurnien d’Esgrignon. Normally such a case might be hushed to avoid scandal, but the President contrived to have M. Sauvager, a young Royalist deputy public prosecutor who owed his place to subservience to the Ministry, undertake the charge without consulting his absent chief. The President and du Croisier persuaded Sauvager by promising advantageous matches and future fortunes if he showed devotion, urging him to act quickly to preempt Parisian intervention. Sauvager agreed.Camusot’s unexpected defection terrified the plotters. The examining magistrate Camusot had risen by backstairs influence: son of a silk mercer, married to Amélie Thirion (daughter of an usher of the Royal cabinet), he advanced through connections. Mme. Camusot, with Parisian instincts and impatience for provincial life, meddled in her husband’s career; she saw the d’Esgrignon affair as an opportunity to ingratiate the family with powerful circles, including the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, and secure promotion and a Paris appointment. Perceiving the President’s underhand maneuver, she advised Camusot to side with the d’Esgrignons, foreseeing advantage.Chesnel, who knew the Tribunal’s circle, counted on the public prosecutor, Camusot, and Michu to support the d’Esgrignons; he also believed Blondet, if persuaded, would favor what he saw as justice. Before dawn he was roused by a startling visitor: the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, disguised in man’s attire, had driven from Paris to save Victurnien. She declared she had brought a hundred thousand francs from the King’s private purse to buy Victurnien’s innocence if necessary; if bribery failed, she had a vial of poison to prevent an indictment. She had sent word to the public prosecutor, who was following her.Chesnel embraced the Duchess’s aid. They went to see Camusot early; the Duchess intended to charm Mme. Camusot and secure Camusot’s cooperation. Playing her role, she understood provincial manners and boasted of Thirion’s influence at court. She and Chesnel planned that the Duchess would speak to Mme. Camusot, and Chesnel would persuade Camusot that rescuing the d’Esgrignon was in the kingdom’s interest. The Duchess insisted on secrecy.Mme. Camusot, quick to grasp the stakes, accompanied the Duchess to Blondet’s greenhouse, presenting the Duchess as a distinguished horticulturist. She warned Blondet of the President’s duplicity: the President aimed to thwart Blondet’s hopes for Joseph’s marriage to Mlle. Blandureau, and Fabien du Ronceret had received clandestine proposals for Mlle. Claire’s hand. Blondet, a man of flowers, was shocked. The Duchess promised that if Blondet resigned properly and awaited confirmation from the public prosecutor, his son’s appointment could be secured within a week; she urged him to consult Camusot and Michu to hush up the affair quickly, ensuring Joseph’s prospects. Blondet, overwhelmed and suspicious, hurried to seek confirmation.At nine the three judges—Blondet, Camusot, and Michu—met secretly. Blondet, furious at the President’s trickery, insisted the matter was a trifle and argued it lacked the intent required for forgery: accounts had been squared and money allegedly deposited to meet the bill. Michu proposed removing the Count from peril by hastening the examination of du Croisier and his wife and issuing a decision the next morning. They agreed to act swiftly.Du Croisier was summoned and, under searching interrogation by Camusot, confirmed prior dealings with the Count and that Chesnel had often remitted sums. Camusot asked whether du Croisier knew money destined to meet the bill had been deposited with him beforehand; this unnerved him. Chesnel, summoned, confirmed he had deposited the money and produced letters to prove it; Mme. du Croisier corroborated, declaring Chesnel had entrusted sums to her in her husband’s absence. With these facts on record, the Count’s innocence emerged.Du Croisier returned home enraged at his wife’s deposition; she showed him bundles of banknotes she had kept concealed, arguing she acted for the family’s good. The town buzzed with the arrest, yet by ten the court dismissed the charge, allowing the Count to sue for libel. Chesnel paraded the exposure of du Croisier’s plot. As evening fell, the Marquis d’Esgrignon’s family fretted over debts and honor. The Duchess, who had stayed with them, advised practical measures: marry for money if necessary, since old privileges had been eroded by law. She produced the tiny phial as a jest, counseled Victurnien to take lands and a quiet life rather than Paris flirtations, and the brief romance dissolved with the crisis’s end.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Six months later Camusot became an assistant judge in Paris and later an examining magistrate. Goodman Blondet was named councillor to the Royal Court, kept the post until he secured a pension, then returned to his pretty house. Joseph Blondet took his father’s seat at the court until his death; with no hope of promotion he married Mlle. Blandereau and likely now leads, in the little flower-covered brick house, as dull a life as any carp in a marble basin. Michu and Camusot received the Cross of the Legion of Honor; Blondet became an Officer. M. Sauvager, deputy public prosecutor, was sent to Corsica, much to du Croisier’s relief; du Croisier certainly did not intend to give his niece to that functionary.Urged by President du Ronceret, du Croisier appealed the Tribunal’s finding to the Court-Royal and lost. Liberals across the department held little d’Esgrignon guilty; Royalists spun frightful stories of plots by “that abominable du Croisier” to compass revenge. A duel followed: chance favored du Croisier; the young Count was dangerously wounded, and his antagonist stood by his words. The affair embittered party strife, and the Liberals invoked it at every opportunity. Meanwhile du Croisier never carried his election and saw no hope of marrying his niece to the Count, especially after the duel.A month after the Tribunal’s decision was confirmed, Chesnel died, exhausted by the strain that had weakened him mentally and physically. He died at the moment of victory, like a faithful hound that brings the boar to bay and dies on its tusks. Though the great House was nearly ruined, the heir penniless and bored, and prospects of establishing himself gone, Chesnel’s exhaustion and bitter thoughts hastened his end. One comfort: at his sister’s entreaty the old Marquis restored their friendship. The great lord came to the Rue du Bercail and sat by his old servant’s bedside, unaware how much the servant had done and sacrificed. Chesnel sat up and repeated Simeon’s cry. The Marquis allowed burial in the castle chapel; they laid him crosswise at the foot of the tomb reserved for the Marquis himself, the last, in a sense, of the d’Esgrignons.Thus died one of the last representatives of Service in its original meaning: the relation between feudal lord and servitor. Found now only in remote provinces or among a few old royal servants, it honored a noblesse that inspired affection and a bourgeoisie that could conceive it. Such magnificent devotion is no longer possible. Noble houses have no servitors; France no longer has a King, hereditary peerage, or land irrevocably bound to historic houses to perpetuate glorious names. Chesnel was not merely an obscure great man of private life; he was a great fact. There is something solemn and sublime in his sustained self-devotion, rising above a single beneficent deed or a moment’s supreme effort. Chesnel combined homely burgher virtues with heroic noble ideals enlightened by solid education — virtues belonging to classes between the people’s poverty and the aristocracy’s greatness.Victurnien was not well regarded at Court; there was no chance of a great match or office. His Majesty refused to raise the d’Esgrignons to the peerage—the one royal favor that might have redeemed his position. He could not marry a bourgeois heiress during his father’s life, so he lived shabbily under the paternal roof, haunted by memories of two years’ splendor in Paris and the lost love of a great lady. He grew moody and depressed, vegetating with a careworn aunt and a half-broken father who blamed a wasting malady. Chesnel was gone.The Marquis died in 1830. The great d’Esgrignon, followed by the less infirm noblesse of the Collection of Antiquities, went to wait upon Charles X at Nonancourt, swelling the meager train of the fallen king—an act then seen as heroism amid revolt. “The Gaul has conquered!” were the Marquis’ last words.By that time du Croisier’s victory was complete. A week after his father’s death the new Marquis d’Esgrignon married Mlle. Duval. His bride brought three million francs; du Croisier and his wife settled the reversion of their fortunes upon her in the marriage contract. During the ceremony du Croisier declared the d’Esgrignon family the most honorable of all the ancient houses in France.Someday the present Marquis will have an income exceeding a hundred thousand crowns. He comes to Paris every winter and leads a jolly bachelor life, treating his wife with more than the indifference of the grand seigneur of olden times—he takes no thought for her.“As for Mlle. d’Esgrignon,” said Emile Blondet, source of these details, “if she is no longer like the divinely fair woman whom I saw by glimpses in my childhood, she is, at sixty-seven, the most pathetic and interesting figure in the Collection of Antiquities. She queens it among them still. I saw her on my last journey home to fetch the necessary papers for my marriage. When my father learned whom I had married he was struck dumb until I told him I was a prefect.“‘You were born to it,’ he said, with a smile.“As I took a walk around the town I met Mlle. Armande; she seemed taller than ever. I thought of Marius among the ruins of Carthage. Had she not outlived her creed and the beliefs that time destroyed? She is a sad and silent woman, with nothing of her old beauty left except her eyes, which shine with an unearthly light. I watched her on her way to mass, book in hand, and could not help thinking that she prayed to be taken out of the world.”LES JARDIES, July 1837.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Blondet (Judge) — The Old Maid; B. Nacquart,Member of the Royal Academy of Medicine.Dear Doctor—Here is one of the most carefully hewn stones in the second course of the foundation of a literary edifice which I have slowly and laboriously constructed. I wish to inscribe your name upon it, as much to thank the man whose science once saved me as to honor the friend of my daily life.De Balzac.ENVOIFelix de Vandenesse to Madame la Comtesse Natalie de Manerville:I yield to your wishes. It is the privilege of the women whom we love more than they love us to make the men who love them ignore the ordinary rules of common-sense. To smooth the frown upon their brow, to soften the pout upon their lips, what obstacles we miraculously overcome! We shed our blood, we risk our future!You exact the history of my past life; here it is. But remember this, Natalie; in obeying you I crush under foot a reluctance hitherto unconquerable. Why are you jealous of the sudden reveries which overtake me in the midst of our happiness? Why show the pretty anger of a petted woman when silence grasps me? Could you not play upon the contradictions of my character without inquiring into the causes of them? Are there secrets in your heart which seek absolution through a knowledge of mine? Ah! Natalie, you have guessed mine; and it is better you should know the whole truth. Yes, my life is shadowed by a phantom; a word evokes it; it hovers about me and within my soul are solemn memories, buried in its depths like those marine productions seen in calm weather and which the ocean’s storms cast in fragments on the shore.The mental labor which the expression of ideas necessitates has revived the old, old feelings which give me so much pain when they come suddenly; and if in this confession of my past they break forth in a way that wounds you, remember that you threatened to punish me if I did not obey your wishes, and do not, therefore, punish my obedience. I would that this, my confidence, might increase your love.Until we meet,Felix.CHAPTER I. TWO CHILDHOODSWhat genius fed on tears shall one day write the tale of tortures silently borne by souls whose tender roots find stony domestic soil, whose earliest buds are torn by those appointed to nurture them? Such hearts oppressed by caretakers would tell the true history of my childhood.What vanity or infirmity had I provoked as a newborn to earn my mother’s coldness? Sent to nurse in the country and forgotten for more than three years, I returned to a household where servants pitied my neglect and my siblings, taught by fear and flattery, turned childish cruelty into sport. No compact among children protected me; I was punished for my brother’s faults and denied any chance of proving injustice. Whether by imitation, desire to test power, or sheer lack of pity, they withheld fraternal tenderness.Disinherited of affection, I could not show love outwardly, though nature had made me loving. Many in such circumstances harden into hatred; in me the suppressed feelings formed a reservoir that later gushed into life. Constant torments developed moral resistance rather than submissiveness. Expecting fresh griefs, I lived in sullen resignation that smothered childhood gaiety, appearing foolish enough to seem to justify my mother’s prophecies. The certainty of injustice prematurely roused my pride and perhaps checked evil tendencies my upbringing might have encouraged.Though my mother neglected me, she sometimes spoke of my education as if to attend to it herself; the thought chilled me. Daily intercourse with her would have been torture; I blessed the neglect that let me stay alone in the garden, play with pebbles, watch insects, and gaze at the sky. My taste for contemplation was first awakened by an incident that illustrates my early afflictions. Once the governess forgot to send me to bed; I crouched under a fig-tree watching a star. When a garden faucet drenched the other children, Mademoiselle Caroline falsely accused me. I denied it, was punished, and forbidden to stay after dark. Tyrannical restraints enhance the charm of the forbidden; children think of nothing but what’s forbidden. I was often whipped for my star. Unable to confide in my kind, I told it my troubles in that inward prattle with which we stammer our first ideas. At twelve, long after school began, I still watched that star with indescribable delight.My brother Charles, five years older and my parents’ favorite, was robust and had a tutor; I, puny and ill, became a day pupil at five, taken to school by my father’s valet with a scanty lunch while others enjoyed rillons. My comrades teased me and flaunted their food. Ridiculed, I sometimes fought but had no protection. Once I was struck in the back by stones tied in a handkerchief. When the valet told my mother she exclaimed, “That dreadful child! he will always be a torment to us.”At school I sank into distrust of myself. The boys I liked were scamps, and pride held me aloof. The master, noting my gloom and unfriendliness, confirmed my family’s verdict. When I could read and write, my mother sent me to Pont-le-Voy to the Oratorians’ “class of the Latin steps” for dull lads. I remained eight years, seen rarely by my family, living the life of a pariah partly because I received but three francs a month—barely enough for pens and ink. Unable to buy playthings, I lost admission to games and refused to flatter the wealthy. I lived under a tree, reading books lent monthly by the librarian. How many griefs lay in that solitude; what genuine anguish filled my neglected life! At the distribution of prizes, where I won awards for theme and translation, neither parent was present. Instead of kissing the distributor, I burst into tears and threw myself on his breast; that night I burned my crowns in the stove. While the parents of other boys were in town, my parents, though only a few miles away, did not come.My unhappiness grew as my social spheres widened. I wrote passionate letters to persuade them to see me; they mocked my style. I implored my sisters in vain. As the distribution of prizes neared I doubled my entreaties and awaited them with a beating heart. The old porter called my comrades one by one; never did he call my name. Once, accusing myself to my confessor of cursing my life, he pointed to heaven and recalled “Beati qui lugent.” From my first communion I plunged into prayer, drawn to religious ideas; my ecstasy brought dreams that fed my imagination and strengthened my thought. I attribute those visions to the guardian angel that moulded my spirit, giving me the faculty of seeing the inner soul of things.My father, doubting the Oratorians, took me from Pont-le-Voy and at fifteen sent me to an institution in the Marais under Monsieur Lepitre. Examined, I—who had been in rhetoric—was placed in the third class. The sufferings of family and school continued at Lepitre. My father gave no money; Monsieur Lepitre, though sympathetic, could only help modestly. The Lepitre establishment in the Joyeuse mansion had a porter named Doisy who breakfasted with well-to-do pupils and smuggled in luxuries. Long I resisted Doisy’s blandishments, but finally I yielded to a café-au-lait and bread—small dissolutions but mighty to a chastened boy.About the end of the second year my parents came to Paris. My brother wrote that they would dine at the Palais-Royal and see the Théâtre-Français; I was excited but ashamed because I owed Doisy a hundred francs. I begged my brother to be Doisy’s mouthpiece. My father inclined to forgiveness, but my mother raged: “What should I be later if at seventeen I committed such follies? Did I mean to ruin my family?” Sent back to school in charge of my brother, I lost the dinner and the play. Such was my first interview with my mother after twelve years’ separation.When school ended my father left me under Monsieur Lepitre’s guardianship to study higher mathematics, a year of law, and philosophy. Though nineteen, my parents insisted on restrictive oversight. Lepitre sent a man-of-all-work to escort me to the law school. Paris alarmed them; I was watched as if I were about to commit a crime. The Palais-Royal, an Eldorado of delights, remained an asymptote to me, impossible to reach. Presented to an aunt on the Île Saint-Louis, the Marquise de Listomère, I dined under surveillance and once meant to escape to the Palais-Royal, but Lepitre’s coach and my mother’s arrival thwarted me. Fate interposed three times between me and that pleasure; once my mother appeared in a post-chaise and took me away when my life there seemed poised on the verge of ruin.Driven by repressed desires and weariness, study became a passion and nearly fatal to my health. At twenty, small and thin, I had read and thought so much that I knew life metaphysically, poised between puberty and manhood, ready to feel and to love.The journey from Paris to Tours chilled me with my mother’s coldness. At Orléans I fell at her feet, clasped her knees, implored her; she called me unnatural. At Blois I went to the parapet of the bridge to drown myself; the height prevented it. At home my sisters were at first surprised but soon showed relative kindness. I received a third-story garret; my mother left me without linen or clothes. She was tall, spare, card-playing, egotistical and insolent; her religion was duty. Despite her despotism, our love for our mother lasted until later judgment.In despair I took refuge in my father’s library and read all I could. Sometimes my eldest sister tried to comfort me, but often I desired to die. The return of the Bourbons stirred Touraine; the Duc d’Angoulême’s procession excited a festival and a ball at which I longed to be present. My mother, too ill to go, angrily resisted my request, then secretly had a bottle-blue coat made for me. In that costume I sneaked into a tent to see the duke. Suffocated by heat and dazzled by the spectacle, I felt disgust and withdrew to a corner, motionless and sullen. Mistaken for a sleepy child, a woman sat beside me; my senses were arrested. I kissed her white, rounded shoulder as a child might a mother’s breast. She cried, turned, and said, “Monsieur!” Her saintly anger and noble face struck me; tears fell. She moved away like a queen.That stolen kiss altered me. A new soul had burst its chrysalis. My star descended and became a woman. The first irruption of love in a man’s heart is strange: I had seen women before but none like her. Some conjunction made that woman unique to me at that hour. The thought that she lived in Touraine made the air delicious. My mother, fearing for my health, sent me to Frapesle, a château on the Indre belonging to a friend, offering the freedom I craved. I determined to search every chateau on foot, convinced she might live in any one of them.Leaving Tours, I walked across moors and by the Indre. At midday, beneath a walnut-tree, I saw the château whose white cambric dress gleamed among the vines—Clochegourde. Love filled the valley; the river Indre’s ribbon, poplars, oak-woods, and dim horizons became part of my vision. If you would see nature beautiful and virgin as a bride, go there in spring. My heart deceived me not—she lived there.My host at Frapesle carried me over his estate and, as we ascended, I kept looking toward the pretty castle. “It is Clochegourde,” he said; “a pretty house belonging to the Comte de Mortsauf, whose wife is a demoiselle de Lenoncourt. They never leave Clochegourde. Madame de Mortsauf is a woman who would hold the highest position wherever she might be.” When he added that she had been at the Duc d’Angouleme’s ball, I exclaimed, “It was she!”We crossed the river and reached Clochegourde at four. The building, modest yet effective, sits on a slope with a terraced garden by the Indre. Its five-windowed front, projecting end-windows, and little bell-tower with carved escutcheon give it an air of genteel neglect. The house, with its stables and greenhouses, seems dainty as a flower set in orchards and vineyards. I admired the groups of trees and felt my heart beat faster at approaching events that would change me.Warned by the watchdog, a servant told us the count had gone to Azay but Madame la comtesse was at home. My companion looked at me; I trembled lest he decline to see her in her husband’s absence, but he announced us. A golden voice bade us enter. I recognized her voice from the ball and, blushing, nearly fled; our eyes met. Too confused for speech, she returned to her embroidery and counted stitches while we were seated. Monsieur de Chessel explained who I was: a young man weakened by study whom he had met and led to Clochegourde to rest. Distrustful at first, Madame de Mortsauf offered me restoratives and a few kind words that gave me speech. I blushed like a girl and thanked her.She invited my companion to dine; as a man of the world he read the shades of meaning; I believed in her sincerity and was astonished when my host later remarked, “If you do not make it all right, I may find myself on bad terms with my neighbors.” His faith that I could please her gave me power and hope.At dinner she talked of harvest and vintage—subjects beyond me; but the music of her voice enthralled me. Her utterance made each syllable a charm; I stayed silent often only to hear her speak. Her way of pronouncing certain sounds was like a bird’s song; her speech led the listener into higher regions. Many times I continued a discussion simply to listen to the harmonies of her voice.Her inattention let me examine her. I saw the mole where the shoulder line began, the golden hair, the pure neck. Her face cannot be fully drawn; it required an artist to paint inner fires. Her soft fair hair caused her suffering, her brow was round and full of unuttered thoughts, her green-brown eyes were weak except on rare keen emotions when they sent forth a subtle gleam. A Grecian nose and curved lips gave her face harmony; her skin was like a white camellia warmed with rose. Her figure, though well developed and the mother of two, retained youthful freshness. She seemed simple, bashful, and dreamily grave through suffering; she charmed without striving. The rarity of her gestures and glances added solemnity to all she said; except when speaking to her children she seldom looked at anyone.On this morning she wore a rose-colored gown, a wide-collared hem, a black belt, and little boots; her hair twisted in a tortoise-shell comb. The constant emanation of her soul made Clochegourde’s life luminous; the salon, paneled and painted gray, with neat porcelain vases and a backgammon board, conveyed a calm and composed life. No apartment has given me such teeming impressions; many of my ideas in science and politics were born in that room. Through the windows the valley spread from Pont-de-Ruan to Azay; there the regularity of her occupations made itself felt.Had I first met her in the domestic gloom among her husband and children, I might have been different; but having stolen a kiss at the ball, having breathed her perfume, I longed for the companionship of the fields and nights, to paddle the river, to climb trees, to linger in the sun. Had she asked me impossible things, I would have sought them to obtain those other riches for her.When she mentioned Monsieur de Mortsauf I felt hatred and fear—hatred of obstacles and fear of the struggle and of her. “Here is Monsieur de Mortsauf,” she said. I rose, startled. The count entered with his little son. A child cried, “Here’s papa!” and a pale little girl, whom I thought six but learned was nine, came forward and sat on her mother’s lap. The child’s pallor and fragility revealed something of the grief upon the countess’s brow. Jacques, the son, showed the same weakness. Their frailty beside a mother so healthy made the cause of her silence plain.As the count bowed, his glance at me was uneasy and awkward—a distrust not born of malice but of uncertainty. I studied his features. Though forty-five, he seemed nearer sixty. A crescent of hair fringed the back of his bald head; his face suggested a white wolf with blood about its muzzle—his nose inflamed by long poisoning. His flat forehead and narrow pointed face bore signs of a life in the open air but not of vigorous intellect; his cheekbones were brown and prominent amid general pallor, and his light-yellow eye was bright without warmth, anxious without thought. His mouth was violent and domineering; his chin flat and long. Thin and very tall, he relied on the conventional value of his caste. The carelessness of country life left his dress neglected; his hands brown and wiry showed he wore gloves only on rare occasions, and his shoes were common.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Though ten years of exile and another decade of country life had altered him physically, the Count de Mortsauf still bore marks of his nobility: chivalric loyalty, rigid convictions, devotion to a cause, honest political antipathies, and a certain naive ignorance of contemporary France. He was the sort of upright man who was good for nothing—obstinate, ready to die at his post but unwilling to spend money. Even at dinner his slack cheeks and furtive glances at his children betrayed an inward torment: he had transmitted to them fragile constitutions. If he blamed himself, he refused to let others judge him. Harshly self-aware yet without greatness or charm to redeem his misery, his private life must have been full of irritability, visible in his angular features and restless eye. When his wife returned with the children clinging to her, unhappiness passed over the narrator like the sense of crossing a vault and feeling depths beneath; seeing the four together dimmed his evening.When conversation paused the count introduced his wife and mentioned family matters the narrator had not known. Asking the narrator’s age, the countess expressed surprise at her daughter’s age—perhaps having mistaken the narrator for a boy—another subtle tie between them. The narrator read her soul then: her motherhood trembled with a belated ray of hope. Observing his slight, delicate youth and nervous strength, she seemed inwardly to cry, “They too will live!” She looked at him searchingly; the ice between them melted. She had many questions but asked none. “If study has made you ill,” she said, “the air of our valley will restore you.” The count grumbled about modern education ruining children with ideas and sciences until they grew old before their time, adding that public schooling should be in the hands of religious bodies—words that later echoed in his distrust of talented men.He proposed a garden tour. The countess warned that Monsieur de Vandenesse had walked from Tours that morning, and the count rebuked the narrator for imprudence with kindly severity. The servant announced dinner; the company crossed to the dining room—Touraine tiles, Boulle buffets, family silver, Dresden china, flowers—a household scene that enchanted the narrator. He sat at the countess’s right hand and, in three hours, felt his life mingled with hers. A terrible kiss had bound them in a secret that inspired mutual shame; a glorious self-abasement took hold of him. He strove to please the count, fondled the dogs, amused the children, and would gladly have been claimed by them. Love has intuitions like genius: he perceived that gloom, discontent, and hostility would destroy his footing in that household.The dinner passed with inward happiness. He disregarded the countess’s apparent coldness and the count’s polite indifference. Love, like life, has an adolescence when it suffices unto itself. The rest of his visit was a dream until, by moonlight, he recrossed the Indre, watching white visions embellish meadows and hills and listening to a tree-frog’s melancholy note; from that night he never heard that cry without pleasure. A sense then came of a marble wall against which his feelings had dashed. Would it always be so? Unhappy memories rose, struggling with personal pleasure. Before reaching Frapesle he looked back at Clochegourde and saw beneath its windows Monsieur de Mortsauf’s little punt swaying by an ash-tree—the count’s fishing punt.Monsieur de Chessel, the narrator’s friendly host at Frapesle, raised his spirits by remarking on the unusual warmth of the count’s reception. Durand by birth, called Monsieur de Chessel, petty and jealous but favored by the crown, was kind to the narrator; with him he found peace and rest—hospitality contrasted with the neglect that had marked his life. The owners of Frapesle were associated with the dawn of his happiness. Monsieur de Chessel enjoyed ostentation, while the Comte de Mortsauf had been a Tourangean of limited means restored in some distinction by royal favor. His greeting to the narrator, the younger son of a ruined but ancient house, was meant to show contempt for a neighbor not of noble birth; Chessel understood this polite distance. Clochegourde and Frapesle, separated only by the Indre, lacked that everyday intimacy neighborly families should have.Jealousy was not the only cause of the count’s isolation. His early education, like that of great families, was interrupted by emigration; he missed later formative training and held to the immediate restoration of the monarchy. Exile became long idleness. When Condé’s army, in which he had served bravely, disbanded, he waited for another post and never sought a livelihood as other emigrants did. Daily postponed hopes, scruples of honor, and trials undermined his spirit. Long marches on poor rations, betrayals, and illness dissolved him where misery might harden others; he belonged to the sort destroyed by such trials.Tramping foreign roads, refusing to serve France’s enemies, he returned only when emigrants were allowed back and, seeing Strasburg on his crossing of the Rhine, cried “France!” Born to wealth, he now found himself poor, commanded without authority, turned into an embarrassment by his name because of his want of money. His unalterable opinions, memories, and wasted health left him unequipped for the sarcasms of a France he hardly knew. Half dead, he reached Maine, and by accident one of his farms had not been sold; a tenant still recognized him as owner. The Lenoncourts at Givry invited him to stay; his birth made him a suitable husband for their daughter. Mademoiselle de Lenoncourt accepted the marriage with the idea of living with her aunt, the Duchesse de Verneuil.Madame de Verneuil, close to the Duchesse de Bourbon, belonged to a pious circle led by Monsieur Saint-Martin, whose disciples practiced a mystical faith of spiritual progress, contempt of worldly suffering, and care of an inner angel. Active prayer and pure love marked this religion, a return to primitive Christianity. Mademoiselle de Lenoncourt remained devout. Cruelly tried by the Revolution’s horrors, the duchess acquired a passionate piety that shed celestial love on her niece. After the duchess’s death, Madame de Mortsauf received visitors at Clochegourde, and Saint-Martin corrected his last books there. The duchess had given Clochegourde to her niece; her sudden death threw gloom over the early days of the marriage and associated the estate with sorrow for the bride. The first period of her married life was not the happiest but the least troubled of her life.After exile’s trials Monsieur de Mortsauf dreamed of secure repose and found some happiness in agriculture. But the birth of their first child, Jacques, was a thunderbolt that destroyed past and future. The doctor declared the child lacked vitality; the count concealed this from his wife but sought further advice confirmed at Madeleine’s birth. These events and an inward sense of their cause amplified his diseased tendencies. His name seemed doomed; a pure young woman made miserable beside him and deprived of the joy of motherhood—this confluence crushed his spirit and completed his ruin.The countess, quick to discern past from present, read the future in their children’s fragility. Though making a man happy who feels himself to blame is difficult, she set herself to that impossible task, worthy of an angel. She became stoical: descending into an abyss from which she still saw the sky, she devoted herself to the care of one man as a sister of charity devotes herself to many. To reconcile him to himself she forgave what he could not forgive. The count grew miserly; she accepted privations. Like those whose knowledge breeds suspicion, he feared betrayal; she lived in solitude and submitted without murmur to his mistrust. With womanly tact she made him will to do right until he fancied the ideas his own, enjoying in him honors never his. After the experience of married life she resolved never to leave Clochegourde; she feared outbreaks that gossip would turn against her children. Thus, thanks to her, few suspected Monsieur de Mortsauf’s real incapacity; she wrapped his ruins in ivy. The embittered man found rest in his wife; his secret anguish was soothed by her balm.This brief family history, partly forced from Monsieur de Chessel, explained much, but the narrator’s prescience of love could not be misled by Madame de Mortsauf’s sublime attitude. One night, alone in his little bedroom, the full truth seized him; he could not bear to stay at Frapesle when he saw Clochegourde’s lighted windows. He dressed, left the château by a tower door, crossed the Indre by a little bridge, took the punt, and rowed before Clochegourde, where a brilliant window shone. Beneath her casement, charmed by nightingale and sedge-warbler, his passion fixed on her. Again and again he cried, “Will she be mine?” During preceding days the universe had enlarged to him, but in one night he found its center. On her his will henceforth fastened; he desired to be all in all to her, to soothe and fill her lacerated heart. That night he betrothed his soul to her with the faith of a poor Castilian knight.At daybreak he returned to Frapesle unobserved. After breakfast he went back to the meadows, to his punt and to the willows near Clochegourde. All was silent and palpitating: dragonflies hovered, cattle chewed the cud, the vineyard earth glowed. Running up the road, he met the count walking toward a riverbank gate and was invited to see the rye being cut. The count showed him yards, outbuildings, pleasure-grounds, orchards, vineyards, and the kitchen garden until an avenue by the river brought them to where the countess sat with her children. A woman is very lovely in such light and quivering shade. The count made him admire the view; the narrator might have thought himself in Switzerland. Approaching, the countess took Jacques upon her knees as he coughed violently.The count reproached his wife sharply for exposing Jacques to the river’s damp and letting him sit on a stone bench; she replied he was burning hot and had been suffocating. The count’s remark—“When they have brought such sickly children into the world they ought to learn how to take care of them”—was cruel, an instance of self-love that drove him to defend himself at his wife’s expense. She hurried into the portico. Monsieur de Mortsauf sat with bowed head in gloomy silence. The narrator’s position was awkward; the count neither spoke nor looked at him. At last he rose abruptly, and they took leave. The count suggested fetching a physician but the countess insisted Jacques’s cough was nervous and that she had soothed him with a lozenge and stories the previous night. “Poor woman!” the count said, taking her hand with a tearful look; “I knew nothing of it.” “Why be troubled when there is no occasion?” she replied. The narrator proposed learning agriculture; she motioned to the count as his master.Two months later the narrator learned she had passed that night in anxiety, fearing croup—while he, rocking in his boat with thoughts of love, imagined she might catch sight of him from her window adoring the gleam of a candle lighting her furrowed brow. The croup, prevalent at Tours, could be fatal. When they left the gate the count, in a voice of emotion, cried, “Madame de Mortsauf is an angel!” The narrator, still little acquainted with the family, inwardly asked what right he had to trouble such perfect peace.Eager to find a listener he could lord over, the count talked of the future the Bourbons would secure. The narrator listened to childish nonsense: the count’s ignorance of verifiable facts, fear of educated people, rejection of superiority, and scorn of progress. He discovered many sensitive fibers in the man requiring caution; conversation with him was a strain. When noticing the count’s failings, the narrator conformed with the same suppleness his wife showed to soothe him. Asking about prices and cultivation, he found the count glad to answer. On that first day the count said to his wife, “Monsieur Félix is a charming young man.” The narrator wrote to his mother to send his clothes and linen and resolved to remain at Frapesle for two and a half months before returning to Paris.The initial part of his stay, while he studied the count, was full of painful impressions. He found him irascible without cause, hasty and at times dangerous, alternating between flashes of the brave soldier he had once been—sudden will and light in the eyes—and deep melancholy, brutality, bitter complaints, cold hatreds, impulses like latent madness, childish moans, cries of despair, and unexpected fury. The narrator feared he might detect the look in his eyes and kill him. He was still young and tender; passion shook him like throes, and though he feared death, he would not die until he knew mutual love. He watched, made the children love him, and tried to identify with the family.Gradually the count restrained himself less in the narrator’s presence; the narrator learned the man’s sudden outbreaks. His future depended on the eccentricity of one variable will. Entering Clochegourde became a torment of uncertainty—“How will he receive me?”—and the narrator fell under the man’s dominion; the suffering taught him to understand Madame de Mortsauf. In forty days of trials they exchanged looks of comprehension; tried by the same fire, they discovered many things—actual bitterness, tacit joys, hopes alternately submerged and buoyant. Once, finding her pensive at sunset, he said, “Some days are hard to bear.” “You read my soul,” she answered. “We touch at many points,” he replied. “Surely we belong to the few born to highest joys and deepest sorrows.” She shuddered and left, noticing Madeleine’s cough.The countess showed no displeasure at his constant visits for two reasons: she was pure and her thoughts wandered into no forbidden regions; and the narrator amused the count and soothed him. Monsieur de Mortsauf proposed to teach him backgammon; he accepted. The countess betrayed compassion for his perilous position with a look that said, “You fling yourself into the lion’s jaws.” The narrator’s patience—born of a miserable childhood—ripened under this trial. The count delighted in jeering him for mistakes in the game; if he reflected the count complained of slowness, if he played fast the count was angry; if he forgot to mark points he was declared too rapid. When they played for money the count’s winnings gave him abject delight.A word from the countess could console him. But soon miseries multiplied: his money dwindled in losses. Though the count was present during his visits until late, he cherished hope that a moment might come to speak a word to touch his idol’s heart; to gain it he was forced to continue weary games that lacerated his feelings. There were moments of silence with sunlit meadows or moonlit sandbanks where they only said, “Night is beautiful!” But without money such evenings would end. He wrote to his mother for funds; she scolded him and sent enough to last a week. Where could he get more? His existence seemed to depend upon it. Thus, in the dawn of his first great happiness, he found the same sufferings that had assailed him elsewhere; in Paris he could abstain from certain wants, at Frapesle deprivation was active and sharp, pushing him to desperate thoughts that he subdued without losing self-respect.In this crisis he found a treatise on backgammon in Monsieur de Chessel’s library and studied it. Learning the game, he improved and soon could beat Monsieur de Mortsauf. But every win enraged the count, whose eyes glittered like a tiger’s; he flung down dice, bit the dice-box, and hurled insults. Such violence ceased only when the narrator learned to manage the game to be nearly equal until the last moment; the count never admitted his skill. The countess, understanding his maneuvers, gave him mute thanks; from that evening she always looked at him when she spoke. The narrator left elated: her look bathed him with light, and her farewell, “Adieu, monsieur,” haunted him. He felt reborn: her voice became palpable, an atmosphere of light and fragrance enveloped him. Her greeting the next day expressed unuttered feeling; he was initiated into the secrets of her voice.That day was decisive. After dinner they walked across a plain gilded by sunset; he held Madeleine’s hand, Madame de Mortsauf led Jacques, and the count, in front, suddenly struck the earth with his cane and said in a dreadful tone, “Such is my life!—but before I knew you,” looking at his wife. The countess turned pale. The narrator extolled the view and playfully proposed a game of backgammon; the count, distracted, heard bells and fell into reverie. Back at the salon, an indefinable dread possessed them. The count flung himself into an armchair, absorbed; the countess avoided interrupting. The narrator tried to coax the count to play; he dallyed, complained of giddiness and choking, then sighed. At last he consented. Madame de Mortsauf left to put the children to bed and to lead family prayers. All went well in her absence; the narrator allowed Monsieur de Mortsauf to win and his delight made him beside himself. This sudden swing from gloom to wild joy, a crazy laugh, distressed the narrator. Their intimacy bore fruit in that the count no longer restrained himself before him. Day by day he tried to extend his tyranny and feed his temper; moral disease appeared to seek empire.Presently the countess sat near the table, ostensibly to embroider by its light though anxiety placed her there. A bit of ill-luck changed the count’s face from gaiety to gloom; then he made a fatal throw. Instantly he sprang, flung the table at the narrator and the lamp to the floor, struck the chimney-piece, jumped about the room, and poured out a torrent of insults, imprecations, and incoherent words like those of a possessed man. Imagine the narrator’s position! The countess pressed his hand and bade him go into the garden. He fled before the count could see him. On the terrace, he paced and heard the count’s shouts and moans from the adjoining room. At intervals through that storm he heard the countess’s voice, rising like a nightingale after rain.He walked the acacia-shaded terrace waiting for her. For days an explanation seemed poised between them; a word might set it flowing. Perhaps she loved, as he did, these quiverings of spirit resembling fear and numbing sensibilities while they held their lives unuttered. An hour passed; he sat on the balustrade when her steps sounded. “Monsieur de Mortsauf is sleeping,” she said. “When he is thus I give him an infusion of poppies; the attacks are infrequent and this remedy never loses effect. Monsieur,” she continued with persuasive tone, “this accident has revealed a secret sedulously kept; promise me to bury the recollection of that scene. Do this for my sake, I beg you. I don’t ask you to swear; give me your word of honor and I shall be content.”“Need I give it to you?” he asked. “Do we not understand each other?” He promised. “You must not judge Monsieur de Mortsauf unfavorably; you see effects of his sufferings under emigration,” she urged. “Tomorrow he will forget all that he has said and done; you will find him kind and excellent as ever.”“Do not seek to excuse him, madame,” he replied. “I will do all you wish. I would fling myself into the Indre if I could restore his health and ensure you a happy life. The only thing I cannot change is my opinion. I can give you my life, but not my convictions; I can pay no heed to what he says, but can I hinder him from saying it? No. In my opinion Monsieur de Mortsauf is—”“I understand you,” she interrupted hurriedly. “You are right. The count is as nervous as a fashionable woman,” she added, trying to soften his madness, “but he is only so at intervals, once a year when the weather is very hot. Ah, what evils from the emigration! How many fine lives ruined! He would have been a great soldier—”“I know,” he said, interrupting to show the futility of deception.She stopped, laid a hand lightly on his brow, and looked at him. “Who has sent you here into this home? Has God sent me help, a true friendship to support me?” She paused, then as she laid her hand upon his, “For you are good and generous—” She raised her eyes to heaven as if invoking testimony and then rested them on him. Electrified, he acted with a social want of tact that some natures would call a brave desire to meet danger and avert evil.“Before we say more,” he said, voice shaken, “suffer me to purify one memory of the past.”“Hush!” she answered quickly, touching his lips and withdrawing her finger instantly. She looked at him with the glance of a woman too exalted for insult. “Be silent; I know of what you are about to speak—the first, the last, the only outrage ever offered to me. Never speak to me of that ball. If as a Christian I have forgiven you, as a woman I still suffer from your act.”“You are more pitiless than God himself,” he cried, forcing back tears.“I ought to be so; I am more feeble,” she returned.“But,” he insisted, “listen to me now if only for the first, the last, the only time in your life.”“Speak, then,” she said. “Speak, or you will think I dare not hear you.” Feeling the moment decisive, he spoke in a commanding tone; he told her that all other women had been nothing to him, but when he met her, a studious, not bold nature, he had been possessed by a frenzy no one could condemn. That no heart had been so filled with passion that conquers all things—even death. She stopped him. “And contempt?” she asked. “Did you despise me?” he cried. “Let us say no more,” she replied. “No, let me say all!” he begged. “It concerns my life, my whole being; it holds a secret you must know or I must die in despair. It also concerns you, who, unawares, are the lady in whose hand is the crown promised to the victor in the tournament!”Then he related his childhood and youth, not coldly but as a young man whose wounds still bled: cruel details of neglect and hope, the treasure of his hopes, the virgin gold of his desires, a burning heart kept alive beneath perpetual winter. When, bowed by his memories, he awaited her reply, she illumined the darkness with one look and one sentence: “We have had the same childhood!” she said, turning a face on which a halo of martyrdom shone.After a pause in which their souls seemed wedded in the thought, “I am not alone in suffering,” the countess told him, in the voice she used for her little ones, how unwelcome she had been as a girl when sons were desired. Her trials as a daughter bound to her mother had differed from a boy’s sending to school. Her youth’s desolate neglect seemed paradise compared to the millstone under which her soul was ground until her aunt saved her. After the duchess’s death she received Clochegourde and set up a home; but the duchess’s death left gloom.“You will live happy, I must die of pain. Life is what we make of it, and mine is made forever. No power can break the heavy chain to which a woman is fastened by this ring of gold—the emblem of a wife’s purity,” she said.They found themselves twins of one misfortune; she never dreamed of half-confidence between brothers. After a short sigh she told him of married life’s deceptions and disillusions, the rebirth of childhood's misery. Like him she had suffered under trifles; great sensitivity makes souls quiver at the least shock. When she married she possessed some girlish savings; these, when needed, she gave to her husband without telling him. He never saw himself as her debtor. How she went from trial to trial! Monsieur de Mortsauf habitually neglected household funds; when she asked he was surprised and spared her the mortification of petitioning for necessities. What terror filled her at his first outburst; what bitter reflections before admitting he was a wreck! What calamities had come of bearing children! What anguish at infants born almost dead! With courage she said in her heart: “I will breathe the breath of life into them; I will bear them anew day by day!” Then conceive the bitterness of finding her greatest obstacle in the heart and hand that should have sustained her. As each difficulty was conquered, new deserts opened until the day she thoroughly understood her husband’s condition, the constitution of her children, and the character of the neighborhood; a day when she trained her feet to trample mud and snow, braced her nerves to bullets, and made all her being passive obedience.“You would have to live here many months,” she said, “to understand what difficulties I have met improving Clochegourde; what persuasions to make him do what was important. You cannot imagine the childish glee he shows when anything I advise was not at once successful. All that turns out well he claims for himself. Yes, I need infinite patience to bear his complaints when I am half-exhausted trying to amuse his weary hours, to sweeten his life and smooth paths he himself has strewn with stones. The reward is that awful cry: ‘Let me die, life is a burden to me!’ When visitors are here and he enjoys them, he forgets gloom and is courteous. I cannot explain that want of loyalty in a man chivalrous. He is quite capable of riding to Paris to buy me ornaments, as he did before the ball. Miserly in his household, he is lavish upon me if I wish it. I would it were reversed; I need nothing for myself, but household wants are many. In my desire to make him happy, I began by letting him treat me as a victim—I who could then, by a few caresses, have led him like a child—but I was unable to play a disgraceful part. Now, the family’s welfare requires me to be calm and stern as Justice—and yet, I too have a heart that overflows with tenderness.”“But why,” he asked, “do you not use this influence to master him and govern him?”“If it concerned myself only I should attempt less to conquer the dogged silence that for days meets my arguments, nor answer his irrational remarks. I have no courage against weakness, any more than against childhood; they may strike me as they will, I cannot resist. Perhaps I might meet strength with strength, but I am powerless against those I pity. If I were required to coerce Madeleine in some matter that would save her life, I should die with her. Pity relaxes my fibres and unstrings my nerves. The violent shocks of the last ten years have broken me down; my feelings, so often battered, are numb; nothing can revive them; even the courage with which I once faced troubles begins to fail me. Yes, sometimes I am beaten. For want of rest—sea-baths to recover nervous strength—I shall perish. Monsieur de Mortsauf will have killed me, and he will die of my death.”“Why not leave Clochegourde for a few months? Surely you could take your children to the seashore.”“In the first place, Monsieur de Mortsauf would think he were lost if I left him. Though he will not admit his condition he is aware of it. He is both sane and mad, two natures in one, explaining many irrational actions. Besides, nothing would go right if I were absent. You may have seen in me a mother watchful to protect her young; harder still are cares imposed by Monsieur de Mortsauf, who constantly cries as he follows me, ‘Where is Madame?’ I am Jacques’ tutor and Madeleine’s governess; but that is not all, I am bailiff and steward too. Working an estate here is the most fatiguing of employments. We get small returns; farms are cultivated on shares needing close supervision. If I left, no servant would be in it in a week. So I am attached to Clochegourde as those leaden finals are to a roof. I have no reserves with you. The countryside is ignorant of our secrets, but you know them. Say nothing but what is kind and friendly, and you shall have my esteem—my gratitude. On those terms you are welcome at Clochegourde, where you will find friends.”“Ah!” he exclaimed, “I see I have never really suffered, while you—”“No, no!” she cried, with a smile that might melt granite. “Do not be astonished at my frank confidence; it shows life as it is, not as imagination pictures it. We all have defects and good qualities. If I had married a spendthrift he would have ruined me. If I had given myself to an ardent young man perhaps I could not have retained him; he might have left me, and I should have died of jealousy. For I am jealous!” she cried. “But Monsieur de Mortsauf loves me as much as he can; all that his heart contains of affection he pours at my feet, like Magdalen’s cup of ointment. Believe me, a life of love is an exception to earthly laws; all flowers fade; great joys have a morrow of evil—if a morrow at all. Real life is anguish; yet here there are smiles in the sky, few but compensating for grief. Women who are mothers live and love more through sacrifices than pleasures. Here I draw upon myself storms I fear may break upon my children; in doing so I feel something I cannot explain which gives secret courage. Time was when my children’s condition filled me with despair; to-day as they grow they grow healthier and stronger. And then, our home is improved and beautified, our means improving. Who knows but Monsieur de Mortsauf’s old age may be a blessing? Those who stand before the Great Judge with palms in their hands, leading to Him beings who cursed their lives, they have turned sorrows into joy. If my sufferings bring about my family’s happiness, are they sufferings at all?”“Yes,” he said, “they are; but they were necessary, as mine have been, to make us understand the true flavor of the fruit ripened on our rocks. Now, surely, we shall taste it together; surely we may admire its wonders, the sweetness of affection it has poured into our souls. Have we not followed the same star? Are we not more than brother and sister? That which heaven has joined we must not keep asunder. The sufferings you reveal are the seeds scattered by the sower for a harvest ripening in sunshine. Shall we not gather it sheaf by sheaf? What strength in me that I dare address you thus! Answer, or I will never again cross that river!”“You have spared me the word love,” she said sternly, “but you have spoken of a sentiment I know nothing of and which is not permitted me. You are a child; and again I pardon you, but for the last time. Understand, Monsieur, that my heart is intoxicated with motherhood. I love Monsieur de Mortsauf neither from social duty nor from desire to win blessings, but from an irresistible feeling that fastens all the fibres of my heart upon him. Was my marriage a mistake? My sympathy for misfortune led to it. It is women’s part to heal woes, to comfort those who rush into the breach and return wounded. How shall I make you understand me? I have felt selfish pleasure in seeing you amuse him; is not that pure motherhood? Did I not make you see just now the three children to whom I am bound, to whom I shall never fail, on whom I strive to shed a healing dew and the light of my soul without withdrawing or adulterating a single particle? Do not embitter the mother’s milk! Though as a wife I am invulnerable, you must never again speak thus to me. If you do not respect this simple command, the door of this house will be closed to you. I believed in pure friendship, a voluntary brotherhood more real than blood. I was mistaken. I wanted a friend who was not a judge, a friend to listen in moments of weakness when reproof is killing, a sacred friend from whom I had nothing to fear. Youth is noble, truthful, capable of sacrifice; seeing your persistency I believed in some divine purpose; I thought I should find a soul that would be mine, a heart to pour my troubles into when they deluged mine. Could I but have this friend, my life precious to these children might be prolonged until Jacques grew to manhood. But that is selfish! The Laura of Petrarch cannot be lived again. I must die at my post, like a soldier, friendless. My confessor is harsh, austere, and—my aunt is dead.”Two tears filled her eyes, gleamed in moonlight, and rolled down her cheeks; he caught them and drank them with avidity, moved by ten years of secret woe, wasted feelings, and ceaseless care—years of lofty heroism. She looked at him with gentle stupefaction.“It is the first communion of love,” he said. “Yes, I am now sharer of your sorrows. I am united to your soul as our souls are united to Christ in the sacrament. To love, even without hope, is happiness. What woman could give me a joy equal to receiving your tears! I accept the contract ending in suffering to myself. I give myself to you with no ulterior thought.
