I will be to you that which you will me to be—”She stopped him with a motion and said in a deep voice, “I consent if you will promise never to tighten the bonds that bind us.”“Yes,” he said; “but the less you grant the more evidence of possession I ought to have.”“You begin by distrusting me,” she replied with melancholy doubt.“No, I speak from pure happiness. Listen; give me a name by which no one calls you; a name to be ours only, like the feeling that unites us.”“That is much to ask,” she said, “but I will show I am not petty. Monsieur de Mortsauf calls me Blanche. One only person, my dear aunt, called me Henriette. I will be Henriette once more, to you.”He took her hand and kissed it. She left it in his hand with the trustfulness that makes a woman superior to men. She leaned on the balustrade and gazed at the river.“Are you not unwise to rush to friendship’s extremes? You have drained the cup at a draught. It is true a real feeling is never piecemeal; it must be whole. Monsieur de Mortsauf,” she added after a short silence, “is above all things loyal and brave. Perhaps for my sake you will forget what he said today; if he forgets to-morrow I will tell him what occurred. Do not come to Clochegourde for a few days; he will respect you more if you do not. On Sunday, after church, he will go to you. I know him; he will wish to undo the wrong, and he will like you better for treating him as a responsible man.”“Five days without seeing you, without hearing your voice!”“Do not put such warmth into your manner,” she said.They walked twice round the terrace in silence. Then she said with a command that she had taken possession of his soul, “It is late; we will part.” He wished to kiss her hand; she hesitated, then gave it and said entreatingly: “Never take it unless I give it to you; leave me my freedom; if not, I shall be simply a thing of yours, and that ought not to be.”“Adieu,” he said.She opened the lower terrace gate for him. Just as she was about to close it she opened it again and offered her hand, saying: “You have been truly good to me this evening; you have comforted my whole future; take it, my friend, take it.” He kissed it again and again; when he raised his eyes he saw tears in hers. She returned to the upper terrace and he watched her from the meadow. On the road to Frapesle he saw her white robe in moonbeam; moments later a light burned in her bedroom.“Oh, my Henriette!” he cried, “to you I pledge the purest love that ever shone upon this earth.” He turned at every step as he regained Frapesle. Ineffable contentment filled him. A way opened for the devotion swelling in youth and long inert in him. Like a priest entering a new life, his vows were taken; he was consecrated. A simple “Yes” had bound him to keep his love within the soul and never to lead this woman step by step to love. Noble feelings were awakened, and he heard their murmur. Before confining himself within a room he stopped beneath a heaven sown with stars, listened to the ring-dove plaints of his heart, and gathered in the air emanations of that soul which henceforth must ever seek him. How grand she seemed, with absolute forgetfulness of self, her religion of mercy to wounded hearts, her declared allegiance to her legal yoke. She stood serene upon her pyre of saint and martyr. He adored her face as it shone in darkness.Suddenly he fancied a meaning in her words, a mysterious sign making her sublime. Perhaps she longed that he should be to her what she was to the little world around her. Perhaps she sought to draw from him strength and consolation, putting him within her sphere, her equal, or perhaps above her. The stars—their light seemed to confirm his new destiny.Spirits quenched by tears, hearts misunderstood, saintly Clarissa Harlowe forgotten, children neglected, exiles innocent of wrong, all who enter life through barren ways, cease your complaints! You alone can know the infinitude of joy in that moment when one heart opens to you, one ear listens, one look answers. A single day effaces past evil. Sorrow, despondency, despair, and melancholy passed but not forgotten are links by which the soul fastens to its mate. Woman falls heir to all our past and gives them back ennobled; she explains former griefs as destiny’s payment for joys eternal which she brings when souls are wedded. Angels alone can utter the new name of that sacred love, and none but women, dear martyrs, truly know what Madame de Mortsauf became to him—to him, poor and desolate.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I can, but ~22,400 words is too long for one reply. Options: split chapter into chunks and edit sections for a ~1% reduction; give a compressed (10–30%) summary; or share an edit plan with cuts to implement on confirmation. For ~1%, pick 3–5 parts; I’ll propose sizes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As I read that letter I felt the maternal heart beneath my fingers; I understood why the countess had forbidden me to open it in Touraine—she feared I would fall at her feet and weep.I met my brother Charles, whom I had hardly known. He affected a haughtiness that kept us apart; kindness depends on the likeness of souls, and there was none between us. He used me as a foil for his social maxims. Solitude and my intimacy with Madame de Mortsauf sharpened my perceptions; I accepted my primogeniture more willingly, not as my brother’s dupe.I frequented the Duchesse de Lenoncourt’s alone; Henriette’s name was seldom mentioned there, except by the simple old duke whose welcome implied his daughter’s commendation. Just as I was overcoming the awkwardness of youth and learning to use Henriette’s maxims, the events of March 20 changed everything.My brother followed the court to Ghent; by Henriette’s advice I went there too with the Duc de Lenoncourt. The duke’s kindness became sincere protection when he saw my devotion to the Bourbons; he presented me to the king. Devotion counted at Ghent, and Louis XVIII judged men with a clear eye—I had the happiness of pleasing him.A letter from Madame de Mortsauf, brought by a Vendean emissary, enclosed a note telling me Jacques was ill. Torn between care of her son and household obligations, the countess needed a friend to lighten her burden. Though ambitious and eager to rival my brother—then at the Congress of Vienna—I resolved, moved by her sad face, to leave Ghent and return to Clochegourde in the king’s service. Fortune favored me: the emissary could not return and the king wanted a faithful messenger. The Duc de Lenoncourt requested the mission for me; after an audience with the king I set out, able to serve my sovereign and to go back to Henriette.I carried the king’s instructions successfully in Paris and in the Vendée. By late May, denounced to Bonapartist authorities, I fled, traveling on foot through parks and woods across the Bocage and Poitou. I reached Saumur, Chinon, and then the woods of Nueil, where the count found me and brought me safely to Clochegourde.“Jacques is better,” he said. When we entered the salon Henriette exclaimed, “Felix!” We looked at one another with the hunger of two souls making up for lost months. She had prayed for my safety and prepared my room. At dinner the news of Waterloo and the Allies’ march to Paris mattered politically, but for us small domestic joys prevailed: she told me, with shining pleasure, “You shall have ice to-day!”—an ingenuous proof of affection that stirred me. Her health and spirit revived in days; my heart opened in response. Absence had strengthened my love; reunion was like spring reviving a valley. Her recovery was delicate; she spoke of nights by Jacques’s bedside, of having lived for three months within herself, alternately listening for moans and a voice.When she spoke of visions—“While I slept my heart watched”—the count interrupted harshly, calling her “half crazy.” He wounded her; she asked whether he had no indulgence for her weakness as a woman. His habitual arrows pierced her; though he regretted the murmur, the pain revealed the long strain of his treatment.I tried to divert her with household matters. She told me of the success of her agricultural plans—new leases, planted trees, income—hoping that if the king returned their pension would be restored and Jacques’s fortune secured. She revealed a kind of spiritual insight: in fits of deep meditation she saw luminous or misty visions of her children, which she took as divine signs; she saw me brilliantly illuminated and heard a mental voice guiding me. I clung to her, crying that my obedience was due to none but her, and she smiled. She advised me, when the king returned, to go to Paris—“it may be degrading to beg, but dangerous to be out of the way of receiving favors.” She called me “my son” in her heart and took my arm contentedly.That evening I witnessed a scene that moved me: the household knelt in prayer, made solemn by Madeleine’s childish voice. The count presented me with backgammon; the countess brought her embroidery and said it had languished while her child was ill. That night, my love burning, I went to her door and listened to her breathing; foolish and dangerous, it was the impulse that urges some men to the brink of ruin.I plucked flowers and made bouquets; I spent days at Clochegourde and dined at Frapesle. The French army held Tours; though my presence seemed to strengthen the countess, she urged me to go to Paris by Châteauroux and Orléans to be near the king. I promised to write every night. The duke’s patronage was decisive: he told me the king had spoken of me and asked whether I lived. That night I was appointed master of petitions to the Council of State and given a private place in Louis XVIII’s service—a confidential position that became the source of my prosperity. Madame de Mortsauf had judged rightly; she guided and purified me. The post brought rooms at the Château, a carriage, allowances, and proximity to the sovereign—advantages as important as talent in that world.The king once teased me, calling me “Mademoiselle de Vandenesse” for my reserve, but he favored me. He arranged that I should have a colleague and leave for six months; I returned to Touraine a changed man—dressed with Parisian refinement and feeling the satisfaction of having repaid, in part, the woman whose counsels had shaped me. The new avenue to Clochegourde, the iron gate, the circular enclosure—small marks of prosperity—impressed me. I had hoped to surprise the countess, but she preferred to be prepared.When she saw the young man I had become she blushed, was pale, yet let me kiss her hand. She feared my being lost to the world; she begged that I promise never to abandon her. We voiced the impossible balances: I swore, “Sacredly... forever,” and she, half in jest, made me promise to remain a child at Clochegourde—she could refuse nothing to a child. In private she asked directly if I loved her “sacredly,” and I answered in vows that combined chivalry and hope, restrained by conscience. She told me to be a child there and a man with the king; in effect, she wanted my devotion within safe limits.The count, jealous and full of contradictions, resumed his complaints and oddities. He loved his wife yet made her life intolerable by petty tyranny and medical fancies—changing orders about windows, food, and temperature, inventing maladies, and expecting others to adapt. He attacked her for every imagined fault, blaming her when the children fell ill. The household suffered under his whims; servants were exhausted. Henriette had to shield the children, serving them at different hours to spare them his outbursts.After some days I pressed her: had she erred in making the count idle? She replied that her thoughts were consumed by the children’s health and that diversions would only shift the burden back upon her. She had even thought of silk-worm nurseries, but feared they would only increase his tyranny. She knew weakness breeds insatiable demands: “what you yield to them to-day they exact to-morrow.” Monsieur de Mortsauf delighted in getting the better of her but deceived himself about his goodness.One morning she dragged me into the vineyard, frantic with fear that the count “will kill me!” She felt the strain unbearable—never a respite, always walking among thorns. She dared not decide on separation after fifteen years’ marriage; her devotion to her children forbade desertion. She resolved to consult her confessor, the new Abbé Birotteau, for courage. When the count approached we hid; he complained of nausea and went to bed. We took our chance to go in a punt on the river—the quiet beauty of nature eased our spirits. The boat ride and mutual attention deepened our intimacy. Yet when a huntsman warned that the count was ill, her first thought was for her son; she breathed more freely learning it was the count. In that instant I realized: though she loved me, her love was of a different order—maternal, pious, not the tumultuous passion I had imagined. I reproached myself gently for not making more decisive claims, for fearing to bind what seemed to be love too tightly.The count’s illness grew grave. I hurried to Tours for Dr. Origet and obtained a surgeon; bleeding saved him from death. The countess blamed herself, thinking she had brought on the crisis; she wept and offered acts of contrition. The doctor prescribed nursing and a long convalescence; she and I shared the watches. He warned that the moral state of patient and nurses is potent: “a single word, a fear expressed, has the effect of poison.” Origet watched us with scrutiny but, seeing our devotion, he respected us.For fifty-two days the count hovered between life and death; Henriette and I each watched twenty-six nights. Our shared duty brought a new intimacy. The count’s room became tidy; we learned to serve each other in countless small attentions. She took my hand readily in service; we were, in effect, half-married in devotion. She entrusted me increasingly with household affairs so she could nurse. In the evenings we discussed estate business and children; I sat where a husband might sit, and she encouraged me with innocent pleasure. Illness stripped the world’s conventions; in that concentrated duty we revealed our true selves.Two small moments revealed deeper feelings, though never consummated: once she came quietly to my watch and I found her with moist, trembling hands; another time I surprised her weeping by the window. When the count showed signs of convalescence his old habits reappeared; he resumed quarrels over food and management, tormenting the household again. Yet the doctor’s orders gave Henriette authority; she governed him with calm firmness and sometimes yielded to pity. She had learned to be stricter, and I took joy in seeing her exercise control. But in private she would indulge him, and her compassion made me both happy and jealous.She once asked me to accompany her to church to return thanks for the count’s recovery; I left her at the door to visit friends, and she reproached me tenderly for being “a man.” She confessed such questions tortured her: could she abandon the old man who was the father of her children? If she failed in duty, the children would be exposed to his domination. She could not sacrifice them. The thought of marrying me, of turning away from duty for personal happiness, was morally impossible for her. She lived by feelings intertwined with conscience and religion. I, more impatiently passionate, could have sold eternity for a day; she resisted such compromises.One afternoon, while the count sat in the sun and his face extinguished all hope of earthly passion, I received a letter from Tours bearing the king’s signet—my recall. The countess read it and we felt sudden emptiness: we were necessary to one another and feared the future apart. She knelt and gave me a lock of hair—precious, symbolic—asking me to keep it. We kissed in a solemn, pure way; there was a sacrifice in the gesture. That evening I left; we parted at the walnut-tree where I had first seen her years earlier. She traveled with Madeleine as I returned to Paris.In Paris my life resumed: council work and my position kept me occupied. I wrote to the countess weekly; she answered twice a month. The letters and our duties made our attachment a disciplined devotion—a private religion whose observances preserved feeling. My story at court spread; my reserve and loyalty attracted notice. Society was polite and women kind; after the Duc de Berry’s marriage the court regained splendor and the city revived. I was drawn into salons but remained inwardly distant, sustained by the memory of Clochegourde.Five months after leaving, in midwinter, the countess wrote despairing of Jacques’s serious illness—though he was out of danger, precautions for his lungs troubled her. Then Madeleine fell ill: the little girl, once gay and blooming, became pale and anemic. The countess, exhausted by Jacques’s long illness, found no courage for these added cares. The household relapsed into old storms; Monsieur de Mortsauf regained power. She wrote that she felt “turned to stone by grief,” asking if I could love her now when she was “callous, ungrateful, turned to stone by grief.” She feared the loss of the children, the emptiness of life, and confessed that Clochegourde was very sad.Throughout these trials her nature remained noble and self-sacrificing. She had the courage to consult pastors and to bear suffering for her children’s sake. My love for her had been chastened into something combining worship, tenderness, and sorrow. I remained her friend, steward, and confidant, conscious that our intimacy had grown out of faithful service rather than conquest. The count’s illness had opened to me the inner life of the one woman I loved—her conscience, maternal heart, and piety—a love at once celestial and austere, which, in the end, prevented the consummation I desired but sanctified the devotion I offered.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It was when I felt most alive, full of morning light and crimson evenings, that I first met in the salons of the Élysée-Bourbon a celebrated woman of society who ruled by wealth, blood, and an “iron hand in a velvet glove.” Immensely rich and allied to an old British peer, she combined beauty, manners, and a wit that dazzled before it charmed. She reigned because she used pride and calculation as weapons.You know the English character: a distance and coldness, a Channel between them and anyone not properly presented. To them humanity is an ant-hill; only a few are admitted. The English make law and routine omnipotent; they polish fortifications around the Englishwoman, who becomes irresistibly attractive. Their customs train married women carefully in hypocrisy, forcing them between extremes—death or social station—with no middle way; this produces a special charm: an enthusiasm for love as life, meticulous care of person, and a delicate passion—Juliet without the Italian fervor.You, who envy them, know these white sirens: impenetrable yet fathomable, who believe love suffices love, who turn enjoyments to satiety by never varying them; a soul with one note only—an ocean of love in themselves. My relations with the Marchioness of Dudley had a damaging fame. At an age when senses rule and when my own had been repressed by the image of the saint at Clochegourde, that ideal shone so vividly I could resist seductions. It was this fidelity’s lustre that attracted Lady Dudley. My resistance inflamed her passion: like many Englishwomen she desired singularity; boredom drives them to the romantic and the difficult. The more I retreated, the more she pressed; curiosity and the struggle became triumph’s obligations. I might have been saved had someone repeated to me her cruel comment on my relations with Madame de Mortsauf: “I am wearied to death of these turtle-dove sighings.”Do not excuse me; a man has fewer means of resisting a woman than she has of escaping him. Manners forbid the brutality of repressing a woman; masculine pride ridicules a man’s modesty; social conventions grant women the monopoly of modesty so that they can bestow favors. Reverse the case, and man succumbs to sarcasm. Though I was protected by love, I still responded to pride, devotion, and beauty. When Arabella displayed the homage of a ballroom where she reigned, watched my glance to see if I approved her dress, trembled with pleasure at pleasing me, I was touched. She occupied a social position where I could not avoid her; in diplomatic circles invitations and rank carried her everywhere. With feminine cleverness she contrived to seat me beside her. On such occasions she whispered, “If I were loved like Madame de Mortsauf, I should sacrifice all.” She promised discretion and asked only to love me.After an evening when she made herself irresistible, she came to me. The scandal echoed through England; Lady Dudley abandoned her place and wealth, and endeavored to eclipse by sacrifice the virtue that had caused her ruin. She delighted, like the devil on the temple’s pinnacle, in showing me all the riches of her passionate kingdom.Read me indulgently: this involves one of those crises most men face. This slender, milk-white creature seemed yielding and gentle, but she had an iron nature; a hand that could bind without seeming to tire. Her body breathed fire; her love was African—desires like desert whirlwinds. What a contrast to Clochegourde! East and west: one drawing moisture, the other exuding soul. Have you reflected on English manners and morals? Is it not the deification of matter, a careful Epicureanism? England has a science of existence that makes women’s slippers exquisite, linens fragrant, tea served at fixed hours—matter becomes a polished pulp where the soul expires. Thus in British luxury I knew a woman who caught me by love excited by my indifference; I opposed stern continence, she opposed persistence. It was an electricity that carried us to the skies: a love monstrous and cruel, which laughs at the bodies it kills; love without memory, like the policy of the English nation. Most men yield to it.You see the problem: man is matter and spirit; animality ends and the angel begins. There is a struggle between the future destiny we sense and the influence of anterior instincts—carnal love and divine love. One man combines them, another abstains; some seek satisfaction across the sex, others idealize one woman who is their world. If you consider the different organisms, their dislikes and affinities, you will have indulgence for sorrows the world is pitiless toward. Lady Dudley gratified the instincts—the mistress of the body; Madame de Mortsauf was wife of the soul. The mistress’ love has limits; matter is finite and saturable; often I felt a void near Lady Dudley. Infinitude is the heart’s region—love had no limits at Clochegourde. I loved Lady Dudley passionately; she was magnificent in animality and superior in mind, her satire wide-ranging. But I adored Henriette. At night I wept with happiness; in the morning with remorse.Some women hide jealousy under angelic kindness; Arabella was over thirty, knowing how to feel and calculate; she pressed today’s juices and thought of the future. She could stifle a natural moan with the will of a huntsman. Without naming Madame de Mortsauf, Arabella sought to kill her in my soul; her passion increased with awareness of that invincible love. Intending to triumph by comparison, she watched me like a lioness with prey, guarding me captive. I wrote to Henriette under her eyes, but she never read a line; she never pried at my letters. She seemed to think, “If I lose him it shall be my own fault,” relying proudly on a love that would give me her life—she often said she would kill herself if I left her. “In India that is a distinction reserved for the higher classes,” she once said; “we should rise to greatness by unparalleled devotions. To love with our heads up, in defiance of law; to die for the idol we have chosen; to never betray that man; these are heights common women cannot reach.”Pride was her instrument; she flattered vanities by deifying them. She put me on high so she might live at my feet; the seductions of her spirit were expressed in acts of submission. How shall I describe those first six months when I was lost in enervating enjoyments, entangled in pleasures learned by experience but masked by transports of passion? These pleasures, the revelation of the poetry of the senses, bind young men to older women; they are the chain of the galley-slave, leaving an ineffaceable brand and filling the soul with distaste for pure, innocent love.Recalling my early dreams at Clochegourde, I found sophistries to excuse drinking that jeweled cup. Often, lost in lassitude, my soul thought these pleasures might abolish matter and let spirit soar. Sometimes Lady Dudley, profiting by my exaltation, wrung blasphemies from my lips against the angel at Clochegourde. Once a traitor, I became a scoundrel. I continued to write to Madame de Mortsauf in the tone of the lad she first knew, but her gift of second sight terrified me at the danger a slip of an indiscreet word might bring. Often, in pleasure, sudden horror seized me; I heard Henriette’s name above me like Scripture’s voice demanding, “Cain, where is thy brother Abel?”At last my letters went unanswered. Seized with anxiety, I wished to leave for Clochegourde. Arabella did not oppose it; she even proposed to accompany me to Touraine, thinking the journey might detach me from my rival; blind with fear and guilelessly unsuspecting, I did not see her trap. She proposed humble concessions: she would stay near Tours, disguised, refrain from daylight outings, meet me only evenings. I left Tours on horseback, choosing the road I had used on foot years earlier, and stopped beneath my walnut-tree. From there I saw Madame de Mortsauf on the terrace in white. Instantly I rode across country to her. She heard my horse and said calmly, “Oh, you here!”Those three words blasted me. She knew my treachery. Who had told her? Her mother, whose hateful letter she later showed me. The voice once so full of life revealed settled grief. The tempest of infidelity had torn through her soul, leaving a desert where verdure had been. I led my horse through the gate; he lay on the grass at my command and the countess came forward slowly, exclaiming, “What a fine animal!” She stood with folded arms lest I try to take her hand; I guessed her meaning.“I will let Monsieur de Mortsauf know you are here,” she said, and left me.I stood confounded, watching her noble, slow figure mount the terraces of her château, pride and torture combined. I cursed Arabella and wondered if my sin would destroy the calm life at Clochegourde. Presently the whole family came down; Jacques ran eagerly; Madeleine, a gazelle with mournful eyes, walked with her mother. Monsieur de Mortsauf greeted me warmly. Madame de Mortsauf stood with her back toward us, under pretext of showing the horse to Madeleine. “Ha, the devil! that’s what women are,” cried the count; “admiring your horse!” Madeleine turned and I kissed her hand, looking at the countess who colored.“Madeleine seems much better,” I said.“Poor little girl!” said the countess, kissing her forehead.“Yes, for the time being they are all well,” answered the count. “Except me, Felix; I am as battered as an old tower about to fall.”“The general is still depressed,” I remarked to Madame de Mortsauf.“We all have our blue devils—is not that the English term?” she replied.The party moved toward the vineyard with a sense something serious had happened. She had no wish to be alone with me, though I was her guest. The count confided to me that Madame de Mortsauf’s disposition had changed completely over six weeks; she, so gentle and devoted, was now extraordinarily peevish. Manette later told me the countess had fallen into a depression making her indifferent to the count’s provocations. No longer finding a soft surface to wound, he became uneasy, like a child whose prey ceases to move. He now needed a confidant, as the hangman needs a helper.“Try to question Madame de Mortsauf,” he urged. “Find out what is the matter. A woman always has secrets from her husband; perhaps she will tell you. I would sacrifice everything to make her happy; I would die for her. If I have not that angel at my side as I grow old I shall be the most wretched of men. I do desire to die easy. Tell her I shall not be here long. Yes, Felix, I am going fast. I hide the truth from everyone; why worry them? The trouble is in the stomach. I have discovered the true cause: my sensibility is killing me. All our feelings affect the gastric centre.”“Then do you mean,” I said, smiling, “that the best-hearted people die of their stomachs?”“Don’t laugh, Felix; too keen sensibility irritates the mucous membrane of the stomach… I have not six months to live.”I listened in terror. On meeting the countess I had noticed the yellow skin and feverish eyes. I led the count toward the house seeming to listen, but my thoughts were with Henriette; I wanted to observe her. We found her in the salon, listening to a mathematics lesson for Jacques while showing Madeleine an embroidery stitch. Formerly she would have laid aside every occupation on my arrival to be with me. My love was so deep I forced back grief and noticed the fatal yellow tinge on that celestial face, which resembled a painter’s halo. I felt the icy wind of death pass over me. When her eyes, no longer softened by former light, fell upon me I shuddered; the slender lines upon her forehead had deepened; her temples with violet veins seemed burning and concave; her eyes were sunken, circles browned like fruit in decay. I, who had sought to pour happiness into her soul, had embittered the spring she hoped would refresh life and renew courage. I sat beside her and asked, with tears, “Are you satisfied with your health?”“Yes,” she answered, plunging her eyes into mine. “My health is there,” she added, motioning to Jacques and Madeleine.Madeleine, now fifteen, had victoriously overcome anaemia and was blooming; she had a slim figure, an air of coquetry smoothing her magnificent black hair—a delicate medieval statuette, a bud of superb development. Jacques, fragile at seventeen, had a large head and feverish eyes, a voice deep with danger under the roses. It was Henriette’s intellect and soul devouring a body without stamina. Seeing him drawing, I shivered at death hidden beneath the roses and thanked my ignorance. Henriette’s maternal pleasure shone.“When I see my children thus, happiness stills my griefs,” she said. “If other affections fail, duties done and crowned with success compensate. Jacques will be like you, a man of highest education; Madeleine will have a noble heart; my motherhood, once tried, is happy now. God makes my joy blossom in these sanctified affections, and turns to bitterness those that might have led me astray—”“Good!” cried the abbé. Just then Jacques coughed.“This situation is intolerable,” I whispered.“Did I create it?” she asked. “Dear child,” she said aloud with cruel levity, “don’t you read history? France and England are enemies. Madeleine knows that; a broad cold sea separates them.”The vases on the mantel had given place to candelabra—perhaps to deprive me of filling them with flowers; I found them later in my room. When my servant arrived I went out to give orders; he had brought certain things I wished to place in my room.“Felix,” said the countess, “do not make a mistake. My aunt’s old room is now Madeleine’s. Yours is over the count’s.”Though guilty, I had a heart; those words were dagger thrusts coldly given at its tenderest spot. Moral suffering depends on sensitiveness. The countess had trod each round of life’s ladder; but for that reason the kindest woman could be cruel. I looked at Henriette, but she averted her head. I went to my room and burst into tears. Henriette heard me as she entered with a bunch of flowers.“Henriette,” I said, “will you never forgive a wrong that is indeed excusable?”“Do not call me Henriette,” she said. “She no longer exists, poor soul; but you may feel sure of Madame de Mortsauf, a devoted friend, who will listen and love you. Felix, we will talk later. If you have any tenderness for me let me grow accustomed to seeing you. When words will not rend my heart, if ever I recover courage, I will speak; not till then. Look at the valley,” she added, pointing to the Indre, “it hurts me, I love it still.”“Ah, perish England and all her women! I will send my resignation to the king; I will live and die here, pardoned.”“No, love her; love that woman! Henriette is not. This is no play, and you should know it.”She left, betraying in her tone the depth of her wounds. I ran after her and held her. “Do you no longer love me?”“You have done me more harm than all my other troubles put together. Today I suffer less, therefore I love you less. Be kind; do not increase my pain; if you suffer, remember that I live.”She withdrew her hand cold and moist from mine and darted through the corridor.At dinner the count subjected me to unexpected torture. “So the Marchioness of Dudley is not in Paris?” he said.I blushed but answered, “No.”“She is not in Tours?”“She is not divorced; she can go back to England. Her husband would be glad if she would return.”“Has she children?” asked Madame de Mortsauf in a changed voice.“Two sons,” I replied.“Where are they?”“In England, with their father.”“Come, Felix,” interposed the count; “be frank; is she as handsome as they say?”“How can you ask him such a question?” cried the countess. “Is not the woman you love always the handsomest?”“Yes, always,” I said firmly, with a glance she could not sustain.“You are a happy fellow,” said the count. “Ha! in my young days I should have gone mad over such a conquest—”“Hush!” said Madame de Mortsauf, reminding him of Madeleine by a look.After the table I followed the countess to the terrace. “How is it possible that some women can sacrifice their children to a man?” she exclaimed.“Yes, and such women would give even more if they had it; they sacrifice everything.”The world reversed before her; the grandeur of the thought struck her. She stood dumb, then rose grand and saintly.“Love her well, Felix,” she said with tears. “She shall be my happy sister. I will forgive her the harm she has done me if she gives you what you could not have here. You are right; I have never told you that I loved you, and I never have loved you as the world loves. But if she is a mother how can she love you so?”“Dear saint,” I answered, “I must be less moved than I am to explain how you soar above her. She is a woman of earth, daughter of decaying races; you are the child of heaven, an angel worthy of worship; you have my heart, she my flesh only. She knows this and it fills her with despair; she would change parts with you even for cruellest martyrdom. But all is irremediable: to you the soul, the thoughts, the pure love; to her the desires and joys of passing passion; to you remembrance forever, to her oblivion—”“Tell me that again!” she cried, sitting down and bursting into tears. “If that be so, virtue, purity, a mother’s love are not mistakes. Oh, pour that balm upon my wounds! Repeat the words that bear me back to heaven.” She turned to me, praying for a look—a sacred word—and forgave me for the sufferings of the last two months.“Henriette, there are mysteries in men’s lives of which you know nothing,” I said. “I met you at an age when heart feelings stifle desires; many scenes, the memory of which will kindle my soul to death, must have told you that age was drawing to a close… A love without possession is maintained by exasperated desire, but there comes a moment when all is suffering within—for in this we have no resemblance to you. We possess a power we cannot abdicate. Nature cannot long be silenced; some trifling accident awakens it to violence. No, I have not loved, but I have thirsted in the desert.”“The desert!” she said bitterly. “How he reasons! faithful hearts are not so learned.”“Henriette, do not quarrel with me for a phrase. No, my soul has not vacillated, but I have not been master of my senses. That woman is not ignorant you are the only one I ever loved. She plays a secondary part in my life; she knows it and is resigned. I have the right to leave her as men leave courtesans.”“And then?”“She tells me she will kill herself,” I answered, thinking the threat would startle her. But she smiled disdainfully. “My dear conscience,” I continued, “if you would take into account my resistance and the seductions that led to my fall you would understand the fatal—”“Yes, fatal!” she cried. “I believed in you too much. I believed you capable of priestly virtue. All is over. I owe you much; you have extinguished in me the fires of earthly life. The worst of the way is over; age is coming on. I am ailing now, soon ill; I can never be the brilliant fairy who showers you with favors. Be faithful to Lady Dudley. Madeleine, whom I was training to be yours, ah! who will have her now? Poor Madeleine!” she repeated.She looked at the warm setting sun, seized with compassion for our shipwrecks and turned back to memories of our pure past. It was her last hour of pleasure, enjoyed with the purity of her Catholic soul. She believed my words and saw where I placed her—in the skies.“My friend,” she said, “I obey God; his hand is in all this.”I did not know then how deep those words were. We slowly returned up the terraces. She took my arm and leaned on it resignedly, bleeding yet bandaged. “Human life is thus,” she said. “What had Monsieur de Mortsauf done to deserve his fate? It proves a better world exists. Alas, for those who walk in happier ways!” She judged life so truly that cold statements revealed disgust for all below. At the portico she dropped my arm and said, “If God has given us the desire for happiness, ought he not to take charge of innocent souls who have found sorrow only here? Either that must be so, or God is not, and our life is a cruel jest.”She entered quickly and I found her crouching on the sofa. “What is the matter?” I asked.“I no longer know what is virtue,” she replied; “I have no consciousness of my own.”We were silent, petrified. “If I am mistaken in my life she is right in hers,” she added at last. Thus her last struggle followed her last happiness. When the count came in she complained of illness. I implored her to tell me where she suffered; she refused and went to bed, leaving me prey to remorse. Madeleine went with her mother, and the next day I heard the countess had been seized with nausea from the day’s violent excitements. Thus I, who longed to give my life for hers, was killing her.“Dear count,” I said to Monsieur de Mortsauf, who made me play backgammon, “I think the countess very seriously ill. There is time to save her; send for Origet and persuade her to follow his advice.”“Origet, who half killed me?” cried the count. “No, no; I’ll consult Carbonneau.”Those days were anguish—the beginning of a paralysis of the heart—my vanity mortified, soul rent. One must have been the centre of all to understand the horror of the void about me. All things were the same, but the spirit that gave life to them was extinct. I now understood lovers’ desire never to see each other again when love has flown. To be nothing where once we were everything! I came at last to envy the dismal ignorance of my youth. My despair became so great that I thought the countess felt pity for it. One evening after dinner I made a last effort for forgiveness; I took Henriette to the punt.“Henriette,” I said, “one word of forgiveness, or I throw myself into the Indre! I have sinned—it is true; but am I not like a faithful dog? I return ashamed. Strike me, bruise me, but give me back your heart.”“Poor child,” she said, “are you not always my son?” She took my arm and rejoined her children, leaving me to the count, who began to talk politics.We returned to the garden scenes of family life, small domestic reconciliations that pierced me with remorse. The count, sometimes perceiving truth, joked that husbands are always suppressed. The countess answered with gentle dignity: “If virtue does not consist in sacrificing everything to our children and husband, what is virtue?” Her firmness and saintliness shone. In tender moments, she admitted to me, “I do wrong… I have many wrongs to repent of towards you; I wished to fill you with despair when I ought to have received you mercifully. Dear, you are kindness itself, and I alone can appreciate it.”She laid her head on my breast, pleading “Forgive me!” and spoke with a voice new and agonizing. She would be a “light from above,” sparkling and cold, unchanging. “Only let me not love the brother I have chosen without return. Love me, cherish me! The love of a sister has no dangerous tomorrow. Turn our doubtful attachment into this sweet and sacred love. I will begin tonight by taking Lady Dudley’s hand.”Our emotions were so great we barely noticed the heavy rain. When the storm eased we drove toward Clochegourde; Arabella, alerted by her dog, galloped to meet us on the moor. The dog’s barking had told Arabella I was in the carriage. We saw her in moonlight; she examined us swiftly and with English contempt disappeared into the underbrush. “Drive quickly to Clochegourde,” cried the countess for that cutting look was an axe blow.As we neared the moor Arabella galloped up crying, “Come! away! away!” I followed her to Saint-Cyr about midnight. “That lady is in perfect health,” said Arabella as she dismounted, and when I forbade her sarcasms about Madame de Mortsauf she replied with frank insularity and a vow: “If you betray me, I shall not be found alive; I was born in Lancashire, where women die for love.” She led me to her rooms where comfort spread its charms. She was not a moralist in Henriette’s sense; she mocked solemn religion and cultivated a practical, muscular morality. She could love and die for me, but she did not understand the sanctities that governed Henriette’s heart.“You poor child know only how to love; you don’t know how to live,” she said. “Still, I am capable of great efforts; I will learn to preach; you shall have no more kisses without verses of the Bible interlarded.” She used and abused her power when she saw my ardent eyes. She triumphed, and I complacently told myself the woman who loses all and makes love her only virtue is far above Catholic polemics. Arabella’s sharp, worldly monologue about women, virtue, and love ended with the same fatal distinction: she offered life and passion, while Madame de Mortsauf offered the soul and sacrifice. Between them I stood divided, consumed by remorse and desire, caught in the terrible contradiction of my own nature.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I can do this. Part two, A Distinguished Provincial at Paris, follows Lucien in Paris. Part three, Eve and David, returns to Angoulême. Often parts one and three are combined under the title Lost Illusions, while A Distinguished Provincial at Paris appears separately. Lucien’s story continues later in Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life.DEDICATIONTo Monsieur Victor Hugo,It was your birthright to be, like a Rafael or a Pitt, a great poet at an age when others are children; it was your fate, as with Chateaubriand and every man of genius, to struggle against jealousy lurking in newspapers and the subterranean places of journalism. For this reason I desired that your victorious name should help to win a victory for this work, which some say is an act of courage as well as a veracious history. If there had been journalists in Molière’s time, who can doubt they would have been within the province of the playwright? Why should Comedy, qui castigat ridendo mores, exempt one power when the Parisian press spares none? I am happy to subscribe myself your sincere admirer and friend,DE BALZAC.TWO POETSWhen this story opens, the Stanhope press and the modern ink roller were not yet common in small provincial printing houses. Even in Angoulême, connected to paper-mills and Parisian typography, the machinery was primitive: wooden presses, leather ink-balls, and a heavy movable marble “impression-stone.” This obsolete equipment, to which Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard clung with near-superstitious affection, figures in this chronicle.Séchard had been a journeyman pressman, nicknamed a “bear” in compositors’ slang—so called for the slow pacing of the pressman from ink-table to press. “Bears” called compositors “monkeys” for their nimble fingers. In the disastrous year 1793, Séchard, fifty and married, escaped the great Requisition that swept most workmen into the army. He was the only hand left in the printing-house; when the master died, leaving a widow but no children, the business seemed doomed because the solitary “bear” could not become a “monkey” and, as a pressman, could neither read nor write. A Representative of the People, hurried to publish Convention decrees, bestowed a master printer’s license on Séchard and requisitioned the establishment. Jérôme-Nicolas accepted the dangerous patent, bought the business from the widow with his wife’s savings, and took over the plant at half value—but he still had to print the Republic’s decrees without mistakes.At this crisis Séchard was fortunate to find a noble Marseillais unwilling to emigrate or risk his head, who agreed to work disguised in a provincial printer’s jacket. M. le Comte de Maucombe set up, read, and corrected the decrees that forbade citizens to harbor aristocrats; the “bear” printed and posted them, and both remained safe.In 1795, when the Terror had passed, Séchard needed a new jack-of-all-trades to be compositor, reader, and foreman; an Abbé who declined the oath succeeded the Count after the First Consul restored public worship. The Abbé later became a Bishop at the Restoration; in time the Count and the Abbé met again on the same bench in the House of Peers.By 1795 Jérôme-Nicolas had not learned to read or write; by 1802 he still had not. Yet by allowing a large margin for “wear and tear” in his estimates, he managed to pay a foreman’s wages. Once an easy-going journeyman, he became a terror to his “bears” and “monkeys.” Where poverty ceases, avarice begins. On glimpsing the possibility of making a fortune, covetousness sharpened a practical faculty for business—greedy, suspicious, and keen-eyed. He scorned theory; he could estimate at a glance the cost of printing per page or sheet for every kind of type. He proved to unlettered customers that large type costs more to move; if small type was discussed, he argued it was more difficult to handle. Setting up type was the one part of the craft he did not know; afraid of undercharging, he always made a heavy profit. He never took his eyes off compositors paid by the hour. If a paper manufacturer was in trouble, he bought up stock cheap and warehoused it. So he became his own landlord and owned the old house that had been a printing office for ages.He had every sort of luck. Left a widower with one son, he sent the boy to grammar school not so much for the child’s sake as to train a successor; and Séchard treated the lad harshly to prolong parental rule, making him work at case on holidays and telling him he must learn to earn his living to repay his father’s toil.The Abbé left and Séchard promoted one of his four compositors to foreman, chosen on the future bishop’s recommendation as an honest, intelligent man. These measures, Séchard thought, would tide him over until his son could take over what he expected to be a flourishing business.David Séchard’s school career was brilliant. Old Séchard, as a self-made man without education, entertained considerable contempt for book learning; yet when he sent his son to Paris to study higher typography, he urged him to save money in the “working man’s paradise” and made clear he was not to draw on the paternal purse. David learned his trade and completed his education without costing his father a farthing; Didot’s foreman became a scholar. At the end of 1819 he was summoned home to take the helm.Until then Nicolas Séchard’s establishment had enjoyed a monopoly of all regional official printing plus the prefecture and diocese—connections that should have been profitable. But at that moment the firm of Cointet Brothers, paper manufacturers, applied for the second printer’s license in Angoulême. Séchard had kept the license dormant during war-induced commercial atrophy but neglected to buy it outright; this parsimony threatened ruin. Séchard rejoiced when he heard the coming struggle would be fought by his son and not by himself—“a young fellow from the Didots will pull through,” he thought.The septuagenarian sighed for an easy life. Though his knowledge of higher typography was slight, he was master of “tipple-ography,” an art in which he indulged. Bound by the laws of etymology to be a dry subject, he suffered from an inextinguishable thirst. The habit altered his appearance: a large nose, veined cheeks like vine-leaves, and bloated purple patches gave his face a truffled look. His small gray eyes, under thick brows, glimmered with the cunning of avarice that had supplanted fatherly instinct. Those eyes retained their craftiness even in drink. Séchard resembled one of La Fontaine’s friars with a fringe of grizzled hair about a bald pate—short, corpulent, like an old-fashioned lamp burning much oil for a small wick; excess confirmed his body’s condition.For thirty years he had worn the municipal three-cornered hat still seen on provincial towncriers. His breeches and waistcoat were greenish velveteen, with an old brown greatcoat, gray cotton stockings, and shoes with silver buckles. This costume suited the man so completely it seemed part of his nature. If the old printer had been given to blind greed, it showed plainly in his manner of abdicating.Knowing his son had learned the business thoroughly at Didot’s, Jérôme-Nicolas had long plotted the bargain he meant to drive with David. All that the father made, the son was to lose, but in business Jérôme-Nicolas knew nothing of father or son. At first he regarded David as his only child; later he saw him as the natural purchaser of the business and therefore an antagonist whose duty it was to get the better of him. The transformation of sentiment into self-seeking, slow and veiled in more educated people, was swift and direct in the old “bear,” who showed the superiority of shrewd tipple-ography over book-learned typography.David returned home and the old man greeted him with the cordiality that cunning folk assume for business. He fussed over him—giving his arm, cautioning where to set his foot, warming his bed, making supper. After a sumptuous dinner and much potation, Jérôme-Nicolas began, between hiccoughs, with “Now for business.” David begged to postpone; the old “bear” would not. He had dragged the chain fifty years and would not wait another hour; his son would be “gaffer” tomorrow.A word about the premises: the printing-house had stood since Louis XIV’s reign at the angle of Rue de Beaulieu and Place du Mûrier. The ground floor was a single large room lit by a street casement and a sash window to the yard; a side passage led to the private office. In the provinces, typography excited curiosity, so customers entered through the street glass door and descended three steps because the workshop floor lay below street level. Newcomers, staring at sheets strung across the ceiling, often ran into cases or tie-bars; they watched compositors pick out type from the hundred and fifty-two compartments, read copy, and assemble lines, only to trip over a ream of damp paper or bump against a bench—delighting the boys, “bears,” and “monkeys.” No one passed through the shop without mishap. Two glass-windowed cages extended into the yard for foreman and master. The yard walls were decorated with trellised vines—tempting, given the owner’s reputation. One side held the kitchen, the other the woodshed, and a ramshackle penthouse against the hall housed paper trimming and damping; there forms—masses of set-up type—were washed. Inky streams from there mixed with kitchen waste and ran into the kennel in the street, so peasants thought the Devil was taking a wash inside the establishment.Above the shop, the house had three rooms on the first floor and two attic rooms. The first room served as dining-room and lobby; it was the same length as the passage below, minus the old wooden staircase, and lit by a narrow casement and a bull’s-eye into the yard. Its chief trait was a cynical simplicity born of money-making greed: bare whitewashed walls, a dirty brick floor never scoured, three rickety chairs, a round table, and a sideboard between bedroom and sitting-room doors. Windows and doors were dingy. Reams of paper or printed matter cluttered the floor; remains of Séchard’s dinners, empty bottles, and plates lay among packages.The bedroom, lit to the yard by a leaded window, was hung with old-world tapestry typical in provincial houses on Corpus Christi Day. Its furniture was the old Seigneur Rouzeau’s: a vast four-post bed with canopy, valances, and crimson serge quilt; a couple of worm-eaten armchairs, two tapestry-covered walnut chairs, an aged bureau, and a mantel clock. The room remained as Rouzeau had left it.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The sitting-room had been half-modernized by the late Mme. Séchard—powder-blue wainscot, sepia panels, six lyre-backed chairs—yet unfinished when she died; Jérôme-Nicolas, seeing no profit in completing it, left the mantel bare.With an air both timid and proud, Jérôme-Nicolas brought his son into that room and pointed to a foreman’s valuation of the printing plant. David read the inventory: three wooden presses, implements and accessories, valued at sixteen hundred francs. The old man interrupted, boasting of his presses as superior to the new cast-iron “Stanhopes” that David admired in Paris. He demonstrated their soundness by briskly operating each one, polishing a cheek with his coat like a dealer showing a horse, and declared them worth many more than the foreman’s estimate. He dismissed cast-iron presses as type-mauling, praised M. Vaflard’s heavy metal type as nearly new after long use, and held up packets of unused sorts to vindicate his claim. The inventory, in his view, undervalued every old tool and ornament—presses, chases, paste-pots, ropes across the ceiling—because “old tools are always the best tools” and provincial customers would not pay for fashionable innovations.David, trained at Didot’s and conscious of modern economy, found the valuation exorbitant—thirty thousand francs including license and goodwill, for premises his father had bought years before for ten thousand in assignats. The father, who equated bargaining with vigilance, pressed him to accept: the license and regular income from announcements and placards were real, he argued, and these provincial tastes would sustain the business. Attached to the valuation was a deed of partnership: Jérôme-Nicolas would let house and premises to the firm for twelve hundred francs a year, reserve a garret room for himself, divide profits equally until David paid the purchase money in full, then leave David as sole proprietor. David worked out the value of the license, goodwill, and paper stock and, thinking the debt possible to clear, accepted the conditions.Jérôme-Nicolas, at once elated and anxiously speculating about payment, tried to get some cash up front; David produced none. The old man moved his household items to the attic and, on the day David took possession of the three bare rooms of the printing-house, refused to contribute working capital, claiming he had nothing left after buying the place and that David’s education—paid for by his father’s sweat—should now be put to use. David, humiliated by the father’s niggardliness and contempt for books, endured and resolved to work, encouraged by the father’s reminder that Marion, a sturdy country girl, would handle dampening, cutting, cooking, and other indispensable tasks.Back in his country vineyard at Marsac, the old man’s mood swung from intoxication at the imagined thirty thousand francs to anxieties about its recovery. He paced his vines, counted his imagined coin, and then returned to Angoulême to inspect his presses and peek into the shop where he saw his son and the foreman reading books—mistaken by him for proof-sheets. Avarice made him rueful, but he was convinced his son’s education and honor would bring payment. Yet unease grew into real fear that the Cointet brothers, rival printers who cultivated conservative, clerical patronage, would steal the prefectural and diocesan work and ruin the Séchards.The Cointet brothers were deliberate in courting the clergy and monarchical customers: fasting on Fridays, frequenting the cathedral, publishing devotional books. They slandered David as a Liberal and immoral because of Jérôme-Nicolas’s reputation, gradually capturing the monopoly of official work. David’s circulation dwindled and rivals started a competing sheet of announcements. The brothers offered to buy the printing-paper and the departmental announcements; when David and his father balked, Jérôme-Nicolas proposed selling the Charente Chronicle for fear of complete ruin. After bargaining, the Cointets paid twenty-two thousand francs for the paper but insisted David pledge never to print a newspaper under penalty of thirty thousand francs in damages. The sale, while tempting immediate cash, was in effect fatal to the Séchard business: it ceded the crucial paper connection that fed much of their trade.Séchard senior, content to pocket money, still retained the premises and kept the rent at twelve hundred francs. He largely withdrew from town life thereafter, though his arrangement left openings for further maneuvering—he expected to appear in any bankruptcy as a privileged creditor for arrears of rent. The foreman, who had joined the rival concern, knew how much the father’s “generosity” was really worth.David’s own mistakes contributed to the downfall. Soon after taking over, he met Lucien Chardon, a former school companion now reduced to poverty. Lucien, about twenty-one and trained in scientific habits by a chemist father who had died unfulfilled, came from a once-comfortable family reduced to modest rentes. His mother, having sold the druggist’s shop, worked as a monthly nurse under the name Madame Charlotte; his sister Eve worked for a laundress. Facing destitution, Lucien accepted David’s generous offer of forty francs a month to learn the printer’s reader’s work—a post David did not really need but offered to save Lucien from despair.The two young men quickly bonded. Both were intelligent, literary, and drawn to natural science. David was moved by Eve’s beauty and by the family’s plight and promised support, convinced Lucien’s genius would yield fortune. Lucien recalled his father’s abandoned ideas: a chemical method to refine sugar and the notion of an American vegetable fibre to make paper à la chinoise, which could cut raw material costs. David seized on the paper-fibre scheme as a potential fortune and treated Lucien as a benefactor, diverting attention and capital to speculative experiments rather than practical improvements in the printing business.Their intellectual preoccupations left the printing-house neglected. Where a shrewd businessman would have invested in new type and machinery, hunted cheaper Paris orders, and modernized production, master and foreman were content with trickling business: while the Cointets were turning fifteen to twenty thousand francs, Séchard & Son scraped together barely three hundred francs a month—barely enough to pay the foreman, Marion, rent, and taxes, leaving David scarcely twelve hundred francs a year. The rivals, seeing David’s habits, did not crush the business outright but propped it up strategically, sending prospectuses and job-work as a way to keep the trade from falling into stronger hands—like running a sham opposition to deter better competition.Everything about the establishment spoke of Jérôme-Nicolas’s stinginess. The house had been worn by sun and rain, a sagging, worm-eaten roof, dingy casements, heavy shutters and iron bars; the workshop was lit at either end and dark in the middle, plastered with handbills, hung with cords across the ceiling, and crammed with stacks of paper, old presses, weighting slabs, rows of cases, and two dens where the master and foreman retired. It was the life of two friends who, devoted to thought more than trade, let their business decline while dreaming of scientific discoveries and literary fame.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One day in early May, 1821, David and Lucien stood at the shop window looking into the yard. It was nearly two; a few men went out to dinner; the apprentice had shut the street door and fastened the bell. As if the smells of paper, ink and presses had grown intolerable, David led Lucien into the yard and they sat beneath the trellised vines, keeping the office and door in view. Sunbeams braided the shoots, making an aureole about their heads and throwing their faces—and their characters—into sharp relief.David’s frame belonged to a fighter: broad shoulders, a well-developed chest, thick black hair and a high-colored, swarthy face. Yet his features, the lines about his mouth, the dimpled chin and steady, intent eyes, revealed a different truth: a thoughtful, strenuous melancholy. He was quick to see distant horizons and the end of winding ways; he could analyze joys still only ideas and then recoil from them. Behind the rough solidity showed flashes of genius smothered by the sense of social annihilation to which low birth and lack of fortune condemn many lofty minds. He loathed the handicraft of printing as ill-suited to his intellectual instincts, and his spirit retained the brooding seriousness of one who feels himself inexorably held down.Beside him stood Lucien, as graceful as a sculpted Bacchus. His profile was antique, his skin velvety white, blue eyes set in pearly whites, long lashes, pencilled brows; his damp, curling hair shone golden in the sunlight. A heavenly graciousness transfigured his temples; his small raised chin bore a matchless nobility; his lips curved in a sorrowful angel’s smile. His hands were shapely, his figure slender; a feminine impression might suggest disguise—an effect often found in keen-witted men. But beneath the beauty pulsed a restless, ambitious mind: the temperament of a Gascon—rash, brave, adventurous, ready to seize advantage and to excuse means by ends. He understood both good and evil, and this knowledge made him capable of stooping if success required it. For the moment, his baser impulses were tempered by youthful illusions and the enthusiasm of a man in love with glory; he struggled less with others’ baseness than with his own power.Both judged society by a lofty standard because their station lay so low; unrecognized power avenged itself by viewing the world from on high. Such flights left them more bitter and hopeless. Lucien had read much and compared; David had thought much and inwardly suffered. Despite David’s robust appearance, his mind was melancholic and shy; yet he had Northern persistence—seeing difficulties, vowing to conquer them, and never to yield. His virtue was softened by pity and inexhaustible indulgence. In their friendship David was the worshiper; Lucien ruled him with the assurance of a beloved. David gladly gave way, thinking of himself as the ox to Lucien’s eagle: “The ox for patient labor in the fields, the free life for the bird,” he resolved.For three years they had shared a destiny bright with promise. Together they devoured recent works—Schiller, Goethe, Byron, Scott, Jean-Paul—and warmed themselves at those fires. They tried their powers in many abortive creations, laying some aside and taking others up with renewed enthusiasm. Comrades in poverty and in love for art and science, they forgot the hard present while laying the foundations of future fame.“Lucien,” David said, producing a tiny volume he had just received from Paris, “do you know what I have just received?” He read André de Chénier’s Idyll Néere, then Le Malade, the Elegy on a Suicide, another elegy in the classic taste, and the last two Iambes. Lucien, seized with the discovery, cried out that it filled one with despair—a poet rediscovered by a poet. David told him that after Chénier had written these poems he thought he had written nothing worth publishing. Lucien read aloud the fragment L’Aveugle and two Elegies; on encountering the line, “If they know not bliss, is there happiness on earth?” he pressed the book to his lips and, overcome, both men wept—lovers and fellow-worshipers before the same altar.The vine-stems were changing; they covered the old house’s rifted walls—fluted columns, knots, bas-reliefs and little grotesques—fairy-work in a gloomy yard. Fancy had strewn flowers and crimson gems over the place; Chénier’s Camille became for David an Eve to worship, for Lucien a great lady to whom he paid homage. Poetry had shaken out her starry robe above the workshop where “monkeys” and “bears” were grotesquely busy among types and presses. Time slipped by unnoticed; five o’clock struck, yet neither hunger nor thirst disturbed them. Life had turned to a golden dream; the world’s treasures seemed at their feet. On the horizon lay the thin blue streak toward which Hope points in storm and stress; a siren voice called, “Come—spread your wings. Through that strip of gold or azure lies the way of escape from evil fortune!”At that moment the low glass door opened and Cérizet, an apprentice David had brought from Paris, emerged with a stranger—an author with a huge package of manuscript. “This, sir, is a monograph I desire to print,” he said to David. “Will you oblige me with an estimate?” David, without looking, replied that they did not take work on such a scale and advised him to see the Messieurs Cointet. Lucien picked up the roll and found a pretty type that might suit it; he invited the man to leave his commission and call again. The author then asked, “Have I the pleasure of addressing M. Lucien Chardon?” and pronounced himself fortunate to meet a young poet destined to greatness—adding that Mme. de Bargeton had sent him.Lucien flushed at the name and stammered thanks. David observed his friend’s embarrassment and left him talking with the country gentleman—a vain author preparing a monograph on silk-culture to be printed for his agricultural society. When the man had gone, David asked bluntly, “Lucien, are you in love with Mme. de Bargeton?” Lucien answered that he was passionately so. David warned that social prejudices set them as far apart as if she were in Pekin and he in Greenland. Lucien, eyes lowered, replied: “The will of two lovers can rise victorious over all things.”But then he declared he had perhaps sacrificed his love to David. He had told Mme. de Bargeton that he would never go to her house again unless another—“a man whose gifts are greater than mine”—were made welcome too: David Séchard, his brother and friend. He expected an answer when he went home; if negative, he would never set foot in Mme. de Bargeton’s house again. David, moved beyond his years, brushed away tears and wrung Lucien’s hand as the clock struck six. “Eve must be anxious; good-bye,” Lucien added abruptly, and hurried away. David watched him cross the workshop, exclaiming silently, “Heart of gold!”Lucien took the long route to L’Houmeau—along the Promenade de Beaulieu, the Rue du Minage, and Saint-Peter’s Gate—partly because Mme. de Bargeton’s house lay on the way. For two months he had taken this detour daily, delighted to pass beneath her windows though she knew nothing of it. Under Beaulieu’s trees he saw how far the suburb lay from the city. Custom had raised barriers harder to surmount than the descent to the river. Youth and ambition could throw a flying-bridge of glory across the gulf between the city and suburb, yet Lucien felt uneasy and feared his lady’s answer like any favorite who dreads a fall. To understand why a visit to Mme. de Bargeton was fraught required a sketch of Angoulême’s topography and manners.The old city perches on a crag like a sugar-loaf, overlooking the plain where the Charente winds. That crag—an outlying ridge—ends abruptly above the Paris-to-Bordeaux road; its ramparts, gates and ruined fortress recall it as a stronghold. But the rock’s very strength became modern weakness: Angoulême could not spread down to the river; shut in between ramparts and a steep slope, the old town stagnated. Government efforts—erecting a Prefecture, a Naval School, barracks—opened roads, but private enterprise had already extended life below. The suburb of L’Houmeau grew at the crag’s foot and along the riverside, where the river’s fall served paper-mills and industries established centuries before. Carriers, wheelwrights, posthouses, inns and trades that live by road or river clustered there to avoid the ascent. Tanneries, laundries, warehouses and brandy stores lined the quays. Thus L’Houmeau became a busy, prosperous second city, rivaling the upper town as residence of industry and wealth, though it remained an appendage of the city above.The noblesse and officialdom dwelt on the crag; trade and wealth flowed below. No love was lost between them. The upper town’s houses belonged to old families who lived on incomes, an autochthonous nation tolerant of no strangers; after two centuries a family from elsewhere might be adopted, but newcomers remained newcomers. Prefects and officials could invite these families to dinners, but were sternly kept from their hearths. Jealous and niggardly, marrying only among themselves and ignorant of modern luxury, they distrusted Parisian ambition such as sending a boy to the capital. Their thick-headed Royalism smacked of bigotry and stagnation; yet Angoulême boasted educational repute, and neighbors sent daughters to its schools and convents.Class sentiment held Angoulême’s two sections apart. The merchant classes below were rich while the nobles were usually poor; each derided the other. Under the Restoration the feud sharpened: the better families, devoted to the Government, grew more exclusive; “a man of L’Houmeau” became little better than a pariah. Thus a druggist’s son in Mme. de Bargeton’s salon was nothing less than a small revolution. The culpability for such mingling of classes spread over many literary and political figures—Lamartine, Victor Hugo, Béranger, Chateaubriand—because Mme. de Bargeton loved art and letters, an eccentric taste much deplored in Angoulême. To understand her influence on Lucien it is necessary, the narrator says, to sketch her earlier history: she was a woman born to shine, left by unlucky circumstances in the shade, whose taste for letters and art decisively shaped Lucien’s career.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################M. de Bargeton descended from Mirault, an alderman of Bordeaux ennobled under Louis XIII. His son served in Louis XIV’s household and married wealth; by Louis XV’s time his grandson dropped Mirault and was simply M. de Bargeton, and that branch later squandered the family fortune. Two brothers returned to trade, so the Mirault name lingered among Bordeaux merchants. The Bargeton lands in Angoumois and the town house in Angoulême were entailed and passed to the prodigal’s grandson. The Revolution stripped seignorial rights but left rents of about ten thousand francs a year. Had prudence prevailed earlier, the family might have risen higher; by 1805 the then M. de Bargeton judged himself fortunate to marry Mlle. Marie-Louise-Anaïs de Nègrepelisse, daughter of a reduced provincial noble.The Nègrepelisses were an old southern family—one had been among St. Louis’s hostages—and an elder branch became d’Espard by marriage. M. de Nègrepelisse, a younger son, lived off his wife’s property near Barbezieux, farming shrewdly, distilling brandy, and accumulating small savings. In the remote countryside his daughter Naïs’s taste for music and reading was shaped by circumstance and by the presence of Abbé Niollant, the Abbé Roze’s best pupil, who took refuge at the manor-house of Escarbas during the Revolution and brought his compositions. Hospitably received, he undertook Naïs’s education. Fluent in Italian and German, a musician and man of letters, he taught her languages, counterpoint, and explained masterpieces of French, German, and Italian literature; in enforced seclusion he also taught Latin, Greek, and touched on natural science.The Abbé’s artistic temperament and independent judgment, admirable in society, were a mixed blessing in private: his pride in his pupil flattered Naïs and encouraged fearless criticism and ready judgment—qualities useful in men but ill-suited to a woman destined for domestic life. Isolated from peers, she acquired a bold, cavalier air, a high opinion of herself, and a robust contempt for ordinary people. Though the Abbé urged modesty and graciousness, without social intercourse to enforce restraint her originality showed in manner and expression.M. de Nègrepelisse was miserly; he would have sacrificed books to save a bullock and refused to spend beyond his daughter’s allowance even for education. The Abbé died in 1802 before her marriage—he would likely not have approved of it—leaving her father anxious to manage her. Naïs held strong opinions about marriage: she wished to rule rather than obey, preferring the liberty of a consenting lover to the subjection she expected from ordinary provincial husbands.Fearing an unsuitable match, M. de Nègrepelisse resolved to marry his daughter for his own peace and sought a son-in-law who would be neither meddlesome nor demanding: a gentleman of breeding but limited cleverness, dull enough to let Naïs have her way and able to take her without a dowry. The only available candidate seemed M. de Bargeton: older, much worn by amorous dissipations, regarded as weak intellectually but with enough common sense to manage affairs and enough breeding to avoid social blunders in Angoulême. Naïs was instructed how to govern such a husband and welcomed the prospect: under the guise of conventional marriage she hoped for liberty to cultivate wit, beauty, and Parisian connections. M. de Bargeton saw the match as brilliant, expecting to profit from the Nègrepelisse estates; a dispassionate observer might have judged the elder M. de Nègrepelisse likely to outlive his son-in-law.At marriage Mme. de Bargeton was thirty-six; her husband fifty-eight and looking much older. She still appeared young and retained a taste for finery. Their income—about twelve thousand francs—placed them among the town’s few great fortunes. They had to remain in Angoulême until Mme. de Nègrepelisse’s inheritance arrived so they could go to Paris. The elder M. de Nègrepelisse kept them waiting so long that the son-in-law predeceased him, and the anticipated Paris life never came. Naïs’s intellectual gifts and inner resources profited her little in provincial isolation; deprived of stimulating society, her strong faculties hardened into affectations. Pride and enthusiasm, when untempered by greater minds, became exaggeration and petty pretension: knowledge stagnated, taste corrupted, and noble instincts were stifled by provincial meanness.Mme. de Bargeton began to overstate every feeling and to make spectacles of her emotions. She used inflated language and neologisms, indulged in ceaseless admiration, and reacted theatrically to trifles. Her enthusiasm was indiscriminate: she wept and rhapsodized over each sensation, magnified minor events, and exhausted herself in chronic admiration or dislike. She fancied herself heroic and picturesque, envied dramatic lives, and longed for any destiny more striking than her clear, simple existence. She adored Byron and Rousseau and any life that seemed romantic. Many called her a harmless lunatic; a shrewder observer might see her extravagances as fragments of grandeur broken off before fulfillment—love without a lover.The first eighteen years of her marriage were spent living on herself and distant hopes. Finding the income too narrow for a promised Paris life, she faced days among men who inspired neither passion nor intellectual companionship; no chance encounters relieved her fate until the Empire’s height roused new hopes. At a military ridotto she fell in love with an officer—a sub-lieutenant of good family—who, dreaming of glory, wore a miniature portrait she gave him at Wagram. On that battlefield a shell destroyed the portrait, and the soldier was killed, leaving her with a lasting veil of sadness. This grief deepened the sense of missed youth’s joys and reinforced an inner austerity. Pride saved her from suburban intrigues; she would have perished of degradation had she succumbed to the petty men of her town. She lived by poetry and by foreign essayists that reached France after 1815; they delighted but could not broaden her. Dignity curdled into stilted manner and affectation, and she ruled her circle with foibles that provincial courtiers adored.Into this stifled scene came Lucien—Baron Sixte du Châtelet—introduced by a newcomer who disturbed Mme. de Bargeton’s monotony. Born Sixte Châtelet, he took the particle after 1806 and belonged to the adaptable young men who skirted conscription by proximity to the Imperial court. He had been private secretary to an Imperial Highness—an office suited to his outward qualifications: handsome, adept at billiards and dance, passable as an amateur actor, ready with a ballad or quatrain, superficial in many arts. He could accompany a singer, improvise verse, sketch, copy landscapes, and color costumes—talents that served well where women held social influence. He claimed diplomacy as his forte; it fitted a man who could say little and keep mystery.Created a Baron and sent to Cassel as envoy-extraordinary, he was used by Napoleon as a courier in a European crisis; promised a ministerial post in Westphalia, he lost it when the Empire fell and went to Egypt with General de Montriveau. Misadventures followed—separation from his patron, wandering among Arab tribes, even being sold and resold as a captive—before he returned via an English vessel to Paris. His connections recommended him to the President of the Council, who placed him in M. de Barante’s department until a controllership appeared, which brought him to Angoulême.Baron Sixte arrived with the manners of a man of the world, habitually assuming the air of a man who had suffered travel and romantic experience. Socially on good terms with local authorities, he remained polite and slightly supercilious, like a man awaiting favors.
