He judged Angoulême’s men worthless and saw Mme. de Bargeton as the principal person to cultivate: she presided over a salon that seldom admitted strangers. He found music the key to her favor, procured a Mass by Miroir, learned it at the piano, and one Sunday played the organ while the town attended cathedral; his performance sent the ignorant into ecstasies and stirred Mme. de Bargeton’s admiration. By that pretext he gained entrée he could not have obtained by direct request.Thus Châtelet entered the queen of Angoulême’s circle and paid her marked attention. At forty-five, he saw her dormant youth ready to revive and possibilities to exploit—maybe a rich widow, maybe political connections through the Nègrepelisse family, perhaps even marriage. High-born Angoulême gasped: Mme. de Bargeton’s salon was a sacred preserve of gentility, usually only the bishop intimate there; a mere controller of excise in her circle shocked local notions of precedence and propriety. Those who understand petty vanities will see how the bourgeoisie revered the Hôtel de Bargeton: outwardly a miniature Louvre, inwardly it gathered the intellectual poverty and decayed gentility of the countryside, making the house both a spectacle and a prize to be courted.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Political opinion in the provinces came out as loud commonplaces: the Quotidienne was tepidly loyal, Louis XVIII was denounced as a Jacobin, and the women were judged awkward and spiritless. Yet the ruined nobility, with old manners and knowledge of tradition, masked many shortcomings; their antiquated exclusiveness was a form of social weight, a token of status to townsmen. Châtelet could hardly have tolerated this society but for his designs on Mme. de Bargeton. Some women, flattered by M. du Châtelet, saw in him what their men lacked and were soothed; purists thought he might be seen at Mme. de Bargeton’s but nowhere else.Du Châtelet cultivated the clergy and the queen of Angoulême, bringing books and reading poetry aloud, and tolerated Romantic enthusiasms with a patience unusual in an Imperial man. Mme. de Bargeton welcomed the literary renaissance, rejoiced in the Bourbon restoration, and pined for Paris and its great men. Du Châtelet, seeing his chance, told her that besides Victor Hugo there was “another sublime child” in Angoulême: a brilliant young poet from L’Houmeau named Lucien, whose verses, he claimed, surpassed the Parisian galaxy. The headmaster had shown the Baron some admirable lines; Mme. de Bargeton, starved for genius in her provincial circle, demanded to see the lad, and du Châtelet negotiated his appearance.For poor provincials the social distances were vast; Lucien’s heart leapt when his headmaster announced that the doors of the Hôtel de Bargeton would open for him. Only his sister Eve knew. Thrifty housekeeper and laundress, she devoted savings to thin shoes, a new suit, and a frilled shirt, and warned him about manners—no elbows on aristocratic tables. She watched him depart and waited until he reached the Promenade where du Châtelet awaited. For Eve, Lucien’s entry into Mme. de Bargeton’s house meant the dawn of success; she did not foresee that where ambition begins ingenuous feeling ends.The Hôtel was less grand than imagined: mellow country stone, plain rooms, a neat courtyard, and an interior that was sober and almost convent-like. Lucien climbed the old staircase, crossed an antechamber, and entered a wainscoted drawing-room lit by two wax candles where Mme. de Bargeton sat on a sofa. She did not rise but smiled on the new poet; he found her dignified and was enchanted by her exaltation. She wore a medieval coif of slashed black velvet; red-gold curls framed a white throat and an ardent face. Her refined fingers summoned him to sit close; M. du Châtelet ensconced himself nearby, and Lucien realized he was alone with her.Those three hours passed to Lucien like a dream. He adored her feminine exaltation and energy, and was blind to faded cheeks or paint; his imagination clung to dazzling particulars. He had brought no verses; she did not ask for them, meaning him to come again to read. M. Sixte du Châtelet, perceiving a rival and mortified, tried to assert himself—walking Lucien down to Beaulieu with tales and advice, bluster intended to destroy a rival—but the young poet was barely intimidated.Lucien returned often and grew used to condescension. When nobles called on Naïs and found him present, they greeted him with the graciousness of the well-bred toward inferiors; Lucien mistook civilities for kindness until their tone stirred his gall and a bitter Republicanism—a stage in many aspirants—began to ferment. Nevertheless, he would endure anything for Naïs.Naïs prophesied boundless fame for him. She flattered and exalted him, holding him near as reader and secretary, more attached than she expected. Her conduct mixed intimacy with capricious pride: sometimes lofty patroness, sometimes tender. The oscillation of dread and hope tormented Lucien—the torture of first love. After two months of patronage, confidences came: she softened address to “dear,” and he, bolder, called her Naïs; she rebuked him, and he then called her Louise—a privileged intimacy that delighted him.One evening she hid a portrait and told the sad tale of Cante-Croix’s stainless but cut-short love, provoking his jealousy and despair. When she raised scruples of duty or appearances he could not analyze; too boyish to reason, he vowed to live for her rather than die for her. Louise demanded verses for her album—pretext for quarrel in case of tardiness—but when she read his stanzas she was moved and changed. She praised the verses as evidence of genius and kissed him on the forehead. She spoke of perfecting him—teaching languages, refining manners—reasons to keep him perpetually in her orbit. She resumed music for his sake, played Beethoven to send him into ecstasy, and implied that such happiness might suffice; he answered “Yes.”Townsfolk buzzed. The week before, she had asked Lucien to dine with the Bargetons as a third, and the town reacted with outrage; some blamed liberal doctrines for moral decay. Du Châtelet, haughty, joked about Lucien’s mother being Madame Charlotte the monthly nurse—an apparent slur on the poet’s origins. Mme. de Chandour remonstrated with Mme. de Bargeton, who met gossip with composure: genius is often born of humble stock; if they had no money, what would they do? She turned misfortune into virtue with wit that disarmed critics.That evening she used caustic reasoning before a crowded salon, arguing that because nobles could not themselves produce Molières or Voltaires, they must accept that great men might be upholsterers’ or clockmakers’ sons; genius was not the preserve of birth. Her eccentric defense quelled the storm. When Lucien appeared before the whist-tables he was brought forward like a prince she meant to protect, and he faced the cold hostility of those who vowed to expel him with insolence.Naïs had won a victory but weakened her supremacy. Rumor spread; Amélie de Chandour, following du Châtelet’s counsel, set up a rival salon on Wednesdays. Habit prevailed with the habitués and the mistress of the staircase. The president of the agricultural society saved face by remarking that pre-Revolution nobles admitted men like Dulcos and Grimm—nobodies—but they never received tax-collectors; Châtelet, a tax-collector, remained unacceptable. Du Châtelet, affronted by the slur and envious of Lucien, declared he would possess Mme. de Bargeton; yet in a twist he sided with her and declared himself Lucien’s friend, feting the poet with a grand dinner. He invited the prefect, receiver-general, colonel, and other dignitaries and had Lucien recite “The Dying Sardanapalus.” Applause was plentiful; the headmaster extolled the lines as superior to Jean-Baptiste Rousseau. Sixte du Châtelet watched with the calculating interest of a rake waiting to strike.From then on vague reports of a great man in Angoumois circulated. Mme. de Bargeton was praised for her patronage, and she sought general sanction by arranging a soirée with ices, tea, and cakes—novelties in a town where tea was mostly medicine. She summoned the flower of aristocracy to hear Lucien read, warned him of the social cabal, and counselled that only the strong could survive. She proposed he abandon his patronymic for the noble name Rubempré, offering to secure royal sanction through her relation, the Marquise d’Espard. The words “King” and “Marquise” dazzled Lucien; baptism into a noble name seemed requisite.Mme. de Bargeton crossed social strata to show him that such a change would elevate him. She urged Lucien to renounce the equality rhetoric of ’89, fed his taste for social distinction, and tamed David’s commonsense. The once-rabid Liberal turned into a secret Monarchist: he longed for rank, luxury, and fame and swore to win laurels for his lady, even if blood must be spilled. To prove his courage he confessed his present hardships—the shackles of poverty borne with pride, long days of work for David, nights of study. His ardor recalled the colonel of twenty-six; Louise’s eyes softened; Lucien seized a hand and kissed it with the frenzy of lover and poet; she allowed him to press his lips to her forehead.Mme. de Bargeton’s arguments—men of genius must sacrifice family and law to grow—fell on Lucien’s worst inclinations and spread corruption in his heart. She invoked Palissy, Louis XI, Fox, Napoleon, Columbus, Caesar—praising the right of genius to set itself above law and take what it needed. For Lucien, with desires hot, all means seemed admissible; unconsciously he stood between prophetic promise and shameful ruin.Louise loosened his provincial bonds so effectively that he resolved on an experiment to secure her love without risk of rebuff. The forthcoming soirée offered a chance. Ambition and love intertwined: society’s banquet early aroused ambition, robbing youth of its original charm and corrupting generous thoughts into mercenary scheming. Lucien persuaded himself his plotting was for David’s sake.He wrote a long, impassioned letter to Louise—copied thrice—detailing his father’s thwarted genius, his own grinding poverty, praising his sister as an angel and David as a future Cuvier, friend, and father-figure. He implored her to do for David what she had done for him, to lift David into his deserved place. The letter was a blend of boyish casuistry, naive eloquence, and romantic pleading—the sort of unconscious revelation women love.Lucien left the letter with the housemaid and spent the rest of the day at the office reading proofs, supervising orders, and attending to the printing-house, saying nothing to David. Youth retains its reticence; perhaps he feared David’s clear eyes. Later, when he read Chénier’s poems with David, the secret rose in his heart under the sting of reproach, like an unhealed wound probed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Try to follow Lucien’s troubled thoughts as he left Angoulême. Had Mme. de Bargeton been offended? Would she receive David? Lucien measured the distance between a queen and her favorite; he knew how far he had climbed in five months, and though David might leap the same ground at once, Lucien did not yet grasp how completely the lower orders were excluded from that upper world. A second imprudence would ruin Mme. de Bargeton: once suspected of consorting with canaille, she would be shunned. Naïs might have broken moral law and been defended, but admitting humble people to her house was an unforgivable sin. The powerful are forgiven their faults; once they abdicate their rank they pay. And what was it but abdication to receive David?Lucien’s aristocratic instinct found other obstacles. Manner and bearing can be learned—Racine had a noble air, Corneille looked cumbersome—but David lacked the trifles Nature had given Lucien: a graceful figure, refined features, a certain ring to the voice. Lucien, of gentle blood on his mother’s side and with a high-arched foot, imagined Mme. de Bargeton’s repressed smile at David’s appearance. Not ashamed of his friend, he resolved to be prudent and risk no repeat of such experiments.So, after an hour of poetry and self-sacrifice came one of worldly wisdom and scheming. Back in L’Houmeau he wished he had not written the letter; he would take it back if he could. Down the vista of the future he glimpsed the inexorable law that nothing succeeds like success, and it cost him to step down from the first rung of the ladder he meant to climb. Images of his simple life rose—David generous and self-sacrificing; his mother proud in her lowly lot; his sister submissive and gracious; his own unspoiled conscience—and the past bloomed.He told himself it was nobler to hew his way through hostile society by repeated strokes than to reach the goal through a woman’s favor. Sooner or later genius would shine; others had tamed society; women would love him when that day came. He flung his schemes to the wind and blamed himself. Lucien was easily moved between extremes.He had none of the scholar’s taste for retreat; for a month he had felt shame at seeing POSTEL (LATE CHARDON), PHARMACEUTICAL CHEMIST in yellow letters on green over the shop. It offended him that his father’s name should be posted where every carriage passed. Every evening, after closing the ugly iron gate and going up to Beaulieu to give his arm to Mme. de Bargeton among the dandies, he chafed at the disparity between his lodging and his fortune.“I love Mme. de Bargeton; perhaps soon she will be mine, yet here I live in this rat-hole!” he thought as he descended into the little yard behind the shop. Bundles of boiled herbs lay along the wall, an apprentice scoured a caldron, and M. Postel, in a laboratory apron, inspected some chemical product while watching the door.A strong scent of camomile and peppermint pervaded the yard and the poor dwelling reached by a short ladder with ropes for handrails. Lucien’s room was an attic under the roof.“Good-day, sonny,” said Postel, the provincial tradesman. “Are you pretty middling? I have been experimenting on treacle, but it would take a man like your father to find what I am looking for. Ah! he was a famous chemist, he was! If I had only known his gout specific, you and I should be rolling along in our carriage this day.”Postel, thick-headed but kind, never failed to allude to Chardon senior’s unlucky secrecy—words that stabbed Lucien. He began to find Postel vulgar, though he blessed him for his kindness; honest Postel had helped the widow and children more than once.“Is there a letter for me?”“Yes, a letter that smells like balm! It is lying on the corner near my desk.”Mme. de Bargeton’s letter among physic bottles in a druggist’s shop! Lucien sprang to rescue it.“Be quick, Lucien! your dinner has been waiting an hour!” a sweet voice called through a half-opened window; but Lucien did not hear.“That brother of yours has gone crazy, mademoiselle,” said Postel, lifting his face.The old bachelor, a miniature brandy-cask of a man with a ruddy, pitted face, became ceremonious and amiable at the sight of Eve, showing thoughts of espousing his predecessor’s daughter. “Your sister is uncommonly pretty, and you are not so bad looking neither! Your father did everything well,” he often said.Eve was tall, dark-haired, blue-eyed; though vigorous she was gentle, tender, and devoted. Her frank innocence, simplicity, and spotless character won David’s heart; since they first met a repressed, single-hearted love had grown between them in the German fashion—quietly, without fervid protestations. In secret they thought of each other as if barred by something jealous; they hid feelings as if it wronged Lucien. David, lacking confidence, could not believe Eve could care for him; Eve, penniless and proud, was shy. Rumors said Marsac was worth more than eighty thousand francs; old Séchard had savings and bought bits of land, but perhaps David alone knew nothing of his father’s means—he cared little for the estate, seeing Marsac only at vintage time when his father boasted.David was a solitary student; his timid love sought encouragement. He stood in awe of Eve and was awkward, eager to hurry away. He repressed his passion; often he would reach the green iron railings of L’Houmeau and turn back. Eve read the little he showed and was proud of his reverence, but it was his faith in Lucien that drew her most. Their love’s mute delights were like wildflowers: glances like water-flowers, words vanishing like briar scents—tender blossoms of two rare natures. Eve saw strength beneath David’s weakness; a single word might bind them closer.Eve opened the door, and Lucien sat at the little trestle table. There was no tablecloth; the household owned three silver spoons and forks, and Eve laid them all for her dearly loved brother.“What have you there?” she asked when she set a dish and put the extinguisher on the portable stove.Lucien did not answer. Eve garnished a little plate with vine-leaves and set a jug of cream.“There, Lucien, I have had strawberries for you.”But Lucien was absorbed in his letter. Eve sat beside him without murmur; in a sister’s love there is pleasure to serve without ceremony.“Oh! what is it?” she cried as she saw tears in his eyes.“Nothing, nothing, Eve,” he said, and putting his arm about her drew her close and kissed her forehead and throat with surprising warmth.“You are keeping something from me.”“Well, then—she loves me.”“I knew very well that you kissed me for somebody else,” Eve pouted, flushing.“We shall all be happy,” cried Lucien, swallowing great spoonfuls of soup.“We?” echoed Eve, with the presentiment that had crossed David’s mind. “You will not care so much about us now.”“How can you think that, if you know me?”Eve clasped his hand, carried off the empty plate and brought the dish she had made. Lucien read his letter again instead of eating; Eve discreetly respected his silence. Here is the letter in essence: Mme. de Bargeton told Lucien she would not refuse David help in science; she believed merit had rights. Yet she warned Lucien of her circle’s prejudices and, fearing to commit herself rashly, attached a condition—she wanted to see his friend and judge him herself before accepting him into her company, as a mother might rightly watch over her protege.Lucien had no suspicion of the arts by which polite society frames a “Yes” on the way to a “No.” He took the note for victory. David should go to Mme. de Bargeton’s house! David would shine in the majesty of his genius! He raised his head proudly; his face radiated hope, and Eve told him he looked handsome.“If that woman has any sense, she must love you! And if so, to-night she will be vexed, for all the ladies will try all sorts of coquetries on you. How handsome you will look when you read your Saint John in Patmos! If only I were a mouse, and could just slip in and see it! Come, I have put your clothes out in mother’s room.”The mother’s room testified to self-respecting poverty: white curtains, a strip of cheap green carpet, a chest of drawers, a looking-glass, a few walnut chairs. A clock on the chimney-piece told of vanished prosperity. Eve’s adjoining closet held a narrow bed, a low chair, and a work-table; the door always stood open for air. Though poor, the atmosphere was sedate and studious.Lucien was tying his cravat when David’s step sounded in the little yard. The young printer appeared in a hurry.“Well, David!” cried Lucien, “we have gained the day! She loves me! You shall come too.”“No,” David said with confusion. “I came to thank you, but I have been thinking seriously. My life is cut out for me, Lucien. I am David Séchard, printer to His Majesty in Angoulême, my name on bills posted on every wall. For people of that class, I am an artisan, a craftsman who lives over a shop in the Rue de Beaulieu at the corner of the Place du Mûrier. I have not the wealth of a Keller nor the name of a Desplein—powers the nobles ignore. How can I prove my claim to this sudden elevation? I should only make myself a laughing-stock for nobles and bourgeoisie alike. As for you, your position is different. A foreman is not committed. You are gaining knowledge indispensable for later; you can explain your present work by your future. You can leave your place tomorrow and begin something else; nobody has pigeon-holed you. Take advantage of your social maiden fame to walk alone and grasp honors. Enjoy all pleasures. I wish you luck; I shall enjoy your success; you will be like a second self. Yes, in my mind I shall live your life. You shall have the holiday life; I will lead the tradesman’s life of sober toil, patient scientific research.“You shall be our aristocracy,” he continued, looking at Eve. “If you totter, I will steady you. If you suffer treachery, find refuge in our hearts; love there will never change. Influence and favor might fail us if we were two; we should stand in each other’s way; go forward, you can tow me after you if it comes to that. I shall dedicate my life to yours. The thing you did—risked losing your benefactress, your love perhaps, rather than forsake me—that little thing was so great—ah, well, Lucien, that alone would bind me to you forever if we were not brothers already. Have no remorse; be not concerned about seeming to take the larger share. This one-sided bargain is exactly to my taste. Suppose you give me a pang now and again, who knows that I shall not be your debtor all my life?”He glanced timidly at Eve; her eyes were full of tears; she saw all beneath the surface.“In fact,” he added, “you are well made, you have a graceful figure, you wear your clothes with an air, you look like a gentleman in that blue coat with yellow buttons and plain nankeen trousers; I should look like a workingman among those people, awkward and out of my element, or say foolish things—or say nothing. But you can take your mother’s name; call yourself Lucien de Rubempré; I am and shall be David Séchard. Everything tells for you there and against me. Women will worship that angel face of yours; won’t they, Eve?”Lucien sprang up and flung his arms about David. David’s humility had dispelled many doubts. It was one of those rare moments when forces in us are sweetly strung and every chord gives full resonance.Yet this noble goodness increased Lucien’s human tendency to take himself as center. He had all the faults of a spoiled eldest son: his mother and sister concentrated tenderness on him, David was devoted; Mme. de Bargeton was doing her best to foster the same tendency by inducing him to forget his family and friend. He was far from doing so yet; but there was reason to fear that as his sphere widened his thought would be how to maintain himself in it.When emotion subsided, David suggested Lucien’s Saint John in Patmos might be too biblical for such an audience. For his first appearance before the exacting Charente public, Lucien seemed nervous. David advised taking André de Chénier and substituting certain pleasure for dubious delight. Lucien was a perfect reader; listeners would enjoy him, and his modesty would serve him. Like most young people, they endowed the world with their own intelligence and virtues; youth not yet gone astray is pitiless for others’ sins and magnanimous in faith.The two lovers were left alone. David, embarrassed and timid, half wished, half feared Eve’s praise; he longed to run away, for modesty is not exempt from coquetry. He was afraid to utter a word that might seem to beg for thanks; everything put him in a false position, so he held his tongue and looked guilty. Eve guessed his agony and enjoyed the pause; when David wrung his hat as if to go, she smiled.“Monsieur David,” she said, “if you are not going to Mme. de Bargeton’s, we can spend the time together. It is fine; shall we take a walk along the Charente? We will have a talk about Lucien.”David longed to fling himself at her feet. Eve’s tone solved his difficulties: her suggestion was better than praise; it was the first grace offered by love.“But give me time to dress!” she said as David made to go.David went out; he, who had never known one tune from another, hummed. Honest Postel heard him and suspected Eve’s feelings for the printer.Small things that evening made a great impression on Lucien; his character was peculiarly susceptible to first impressions. Like all inexperienced lovers, he arrived so early that Louise was not in the drawing-room; M. de Bargeton was there alone. Lucien had begun to practice the small deceits lovers pay for married happiness—deceits through which a woman learns her power—but he had not met the lady’s husband.M. de Bargeton’s intellect was limited, poised between harmless vacancy and excessive stupidity. To be agreeable he adopted an opera-dancer’s smile as his sole expression. Satisfied, he smiled; dissatisfied, he smiled again. He smiled at good and evil alike; when obliged to express approval a complacent laugh reinforced the smile; otherwise he hardly spoke. A tête-à-tête embarrassed him, and he rescued himself by returning to the artless ways of childhood—thinking aloud about the smallest personal sensations. He never talked weather or commonplaces; he plunged you into intimate trivialities—“I took veal this morning to please Mme. de Bargeton, and my stomach has been uneasy since.” Such remarks permitted no discussion; a “Yes” or “No” and conversation fell dead. Then he implored help with a gaze that said, “You were saying?” He loved bores who talked about themselves and listened with delicate interest, so twaddlers credited him with more understanding than he showed. When others could find nobody to listen they went to him, sure of his eulogistic smile. Mme. de Bargeton’s rooms were always crowded, and generally her husband felt at ease. He watched arrivals, helped his wife bring guests, laid in wait for departures to take them to the door with that eternal smile. When conversation grew lively, he stood happy and mute, planted like a swan, listening to political talk or watching card-players without notion of the game; or he walked about taking snuff to promote digestion. Anaïs was the bright side of his life; she made it unspeakably pleasant. Stretched in his armchair he watched admiringly while she played hostess. He tried to see the point in her remarks, and often succeeded only after delay; his smiles appeared like a delayed explosion. His respect for his wife almost amounted to adoration. She had drilled him into military subordination; he yielded passive obedience. “Go and call on Monsieur So-and-So,” she would say, and he went forthwith, like a soldier at command. He stood at attention in her presence and waited motionless for orders.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################There was talk of naming the mute gentleman as a deputy. Lucien, who had not been long at the house, had scarcely begun to fathom M. de Bargeton. The old man, stretched in his great chair, seemed to see and understand everything; his silence gave him dignity, and Lucien, imaginative and uneasy, regarded him more as a sphinx than a host and wished to conciliate him.“I am the first comer,” Lucien said with extra respect.“That is natural enough,” M. de Bargeton replied in a tone Lucien took for an epigram and which made him imagine a jealous husband. When Lucien asked why people who live far off come earlier than those near by, Bargeton only returned to his immobility, snatching at a joke about “final causes” with an amused “Eh! eh!...” The conversation died; Lucien fumbled for topics—Mme. de Bargeton, the chandelier, his boots—trying to please the inscrutable man with small talk about a Japanese jar and the fact that Bargeton seldom left the city. The old man watched him like a suspicious cat, and each was afraid of the other.Relief came when the old man-servant announced “M. du Châtelet.” Baron Sixte du Châtelet entered with the ease of an inbred beau, dressed in dazzling white trousers, pumps, an eyeglass, and the air of faded elegance. He inspected Lucien coolly, his look saying, “I am satisfied,” and by his presence Lucien felt his own poverty eclipsed by the weight of fortune. M. de Bargeton, sparing of speech, kept a question in reserve and asked Châtelet if there was any news. Châtelet suggested M. Chardon—and, with malicious adjustment of his curl, invited Lucien to produce “some charming poet.” Lucien answered with deference, admitting Châtelet had preceded him in verse. The Baron dismissed his own work—“a few vaudevilles” and an “Epistle to a Sister of Bonaparte”—with affected indifference.Mme. de Bargeton appeared in full toilette: a theatrical Jewess’ turban, oriental clasp, cameos at her shoulders and bracelets. M. du Châtelet lavished fulsome compliments; she received them with gratification and only a civil nod for Lucien.Guests began to arrive. First the Bishop and his Vicar-General—one tall and attenuated, the other short and fat—grave and impassive, their silence taken for great intellect. Next came Mme. de Chandour and her husband Stanislas, a ci-devant slim man of forty-five who posed as a dandy: cravat points, awry coat-tails, starched shirt, massive studs—an eighteenth-century caricature whose grossness charmed women and provoked challenge from Châtelet. Mme. de Chandour, short and plump, loud-voiced and restless, talked without depth and wheezed with asthma.M. de Saintot, Astolphe, President of the Agricultural Society, arrived with his wife Élisa—Lili—solemn, pious, and card-playing at heart. Astolphe posed as a learned man: he compiled agricultural articles by pilfering others’ work, locked himself away to appear industrious, and in conversation dredged Cicero for apt quotations. His reputation rested on rumor and theatrical management of appearances.M. de Bartas, Adrien, boomed arrival with his bass voice and musical self-conceit. Obsessed with solfeggi, he longed to sing and, when praised, strutted from group to group for more compliments. M. Alexandre de Brébian produced heroic sepia sketches and together with de Bartas they arrived with wives in a cross-cornered arrangement that gossip exaggerated into impropriety. Their wives, Mesdames Charlotte de Brébian and Joséphine de Bartas—Lolotte and Fifine—were obsessed with fashion and Parisian airs, neglecting domestic matters. The husbands, dressed with an artist’s liberty, seemed provincial beside their wives’ doll-like finery.One curious pair were the Comte and Comtesse de Senonches: Jacques, a mighty hunter, lean, sunburned, jealous and suspicious; his wife Zéphirine, tall and fine but with a sallow complexion and affected languor masking passion. Francis du Hautoy, a friend of the house, had forsaken a consulship to be near her, managing the household and finances with devoted attention. The three formed a conjugal trinity so intimate that Du Hautoy’s role as a quasi-servant, confidant, and companion seemed indispensable; his mincing affectations—fussing over coughs and waistcoats—made him a pampered idol attended to by Zéphirine. Rumor fancied a closer tie between Françoise de la Haye, Zéphirine’s companion, and Du Hautoy, suggesting delicate mysteries despite chronological discrepancies.The Marquis de Pimentel, the largest landowner, arrived with his wife and, in their calèche, neighbors the Baron and Baroness de Rastignac, the Baroness’ aunt, and two charming, penniless daughters. These families, near the Court and immune to provincial gossip, received ostentatious yet jealous respect; Mme. de Bargeton attended to them most, and they remained dignifiedly aloof from Angoulême’s cliques.The Prefect and the General arrived last, accompanied by a country gentleman who had brought a treatise on silkworms. This man, secure in his estate but unused to polite society, quivered with awkward servility—shifting feet, half-rises, eager laughter at jokes, and anxious attempts to speak about silkworms—only finding a sympathetic ear late in the evening with Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard.Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard were a touching study in genteel poverty. Poor yet noble, their dress betrayed their pinched means. The daughter, Camille, a plump twenty-seven, was loudly praised by her mother for piano playing in the most awkward way; Mme. du Brossard plainly sought a husband for her daughter, boasting adaptability to any suitor. Their strained dignity and reliance on social formulas repaid them only with benevolent condescension.M. de Séverac, fifty-nine and a childless widower interested in silkworm nurseries, conversed with Mme. du Brossard, who absurdly claimed her daughter once understood the inverse ratio of the squares of distances—an anecdote that, in Lucien’s later reading, achieved comedic success.A few habitués and some eldest sons filtered in—shy, mute young men decked in jewelry, honored by invitation to the literary solemnity; a bold one ventured to chat with Mlle. de la Haye. The women settled in a circle and the men stood behind them. It was a quaint, heterogeneous assembly of wrinkled faces and varied costumes that made Lucien’s heart beat fast under their gaze.Mme. de Bargeton welcomed the illustrious Angoulême personages with ostentatious courtesy, but a petty sting awaited Lucien: apart from Louise, M. de Bargeton, the Bishop, and a few who wished to please the hostess, nobody called him M. de Rubempré; to the formidable audience he was simply M. Chardon. Lucien read his plebeian name in their movements and anticipatory provincial criticisms cut him deeply. He had not expected such pin-pricks and grew more out of humour with himself.Lucien, eager to begin and end his torment, waited for a chance, but the room buzzed with trivialities: Jacques told Mme. de Pimentel of his shooting; Adrien lectured Mlle. Laure de Rastignac on Rossini; Astolphe read a newspaper paragraph on a patent plow to the Baron. Unluckily for the poet, few in the room understood or wanted poetry. Many had come through misapprehension; certain words—“beauty,” “glory,” “poetry”—bewitched even coarse minds into attendance.When everyone had arrived and the low hum subsided—M. de Bargeton, obedient to his wife, made a slow circuit like a beadle tapping the pavement with his wand until silence—Lucien moved to the round table near Mme. de Bargeton. He felt a fierce thrill and announced in an uncertain voice that, to avoid disappointment, he would read the masterpieces of a great poet recently discovered—though André de Chénier’s poems had been published in 1819, no one in Angoulême had heard of him. The audience took the announcement according to convenience; it appeared to many a saving flourish of the hostess to soothe the poet’s self-regard and put the assembly at ease.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lucien began with Le Malade and won a murmur of applause; but when he followed with L’Aveugle the strain proved too great for most present. Only artists or temperamentally inclined listeners can follow such readings; to render poetry by voice so that an audience grasps it requires intense attention and a close rapport between reader and hearers. That sympathy was missing; Lucien seemed like an angel singing of heaven amid the chucklings of hell. A sensitive artist senses at once whether his audience listens, like a plant reviving or drooping to conditions; here the men who had come with their wives were absorbed in their own affairs, and every murmur registered in Lucien’s ear. He noticed gaps made by spasmodic jaw-work and teeth that seemed to grin defiance at him.Searching, like a dove for a resting place, he saw only rows of impatient faces. Their owners had come to discuss practical matters; except for Laure de Rastignac, the Bishop, and two or three young men, they looked bored. Those who understand poetry try to develop its quickened seeds; this glacial audience, so far from attaining the spirit, did not even hear the letter.Discouraged and damp with chill sweat, Lucien was steadied by a glowing glance from Louise, but his poet’s heart bled. Lili asked Fifine if she found it amusing. “Don’t ask me; I cannot keep my eyes open when anyone reads aloud,” Fifine answered. “I hope Naïs will not give us poetry often in the evenings,” said Francis. “If I must attend someone reading after dinner, it upsets my digestion.” “It was very well declaimed,” Alexandre observed, “but I like whist better.” After that quip some card-players decided the reader’s voice needed a rest, and a couple of couples slipped away to the card-room. But Louise, the Bishop, and pretty Laure begged Lucien to continue, and he caught his audience again with Chénier’s spirited reactionary iambes. Several, carried away by his impassioned delivery, applauded without understanding the sense; people of that sort mistake vociferation for meaning, as a coarse palate is tickled by strong spirits.During the interval, while they had ices, Zéphirine sent Francis to examine the volume and told Amélie that the poetry was in print. Amélie brightened. “Why, that is easily explained,” she said. “M. de Rubempré works for a printer. It is as if a pretty woman should make her own dresses,” she added, looking at Lolotte. “He printed his poetry himself!” the women murmured. “Then why does he call himself M. de Rubempré?” asked Jacques. “If a noble takes a handicraft, he ought to lay his name aside.” “So he did,” said Zizine, “but his name was plebeian; he took his mother’s, which is noble.” “Well, if his verses are printed, we can read them for ourselves,” said Astolphe.This stupidity complicated matters until Sixte du Châtelet condescended to inform these unlettered folk that the prefatory announcement was not oratorical flourish but fact: the poems had been written by a Royalist brother of Marie-Joseph Chénier, the Revolutionary leader. All Angoulême, except Mme. de Rastignac, her two daughters, and the Bishop—who had genuinely felt the grandeur of the poetry—were mystified and took offense at the hoax. There was a smothered murmur, but Lucien did not heed it. The intoxication of the poetry had seized him; far from the hateful world, he strove to render in speech the music that filled his soul, seeing the faces about him through a cloudy haze. He read the sombre Elegy on the Suicide, lines of a bygone taste pervaded by sublime melancholy; then he turned to the page where the line occurs, “Thy songs are sweet, I love to say them over,” and ended with the delicate idyll Néere.Mme. de Bargeton sat with a hand buried in her curls, heedless of the havoc she wrought, gazing with unseeing eyes, alone in her drawing-room, lost in delicious dreaming; for the first time in her life she had been transported to the sphere that was hers by nature. Judge, therefore, how unpleasantly she was disturbed by Amélie, who took it upon herself to express the general wish. “Naïs,” she broke in, “we came to hear M. Chardon’s poetry, and you are giving us poetry out of a book. The extracts are very nice, but the ladies feel a patriotic preference for the wine of the country; they would rather have it.” Astolphe added lightly to Châtelet, “The French language does not lend itself very readily to poetry, does it? Cicero’s prose is a thousand times more poetical to my way of thinking.” “The true poetry of France is song, lyric verse,” Châtelet answered. “Which proves that our language is eminently adapted for music,” said Adrien.“I should very much like to hear the poetry that has cost Naïs her reputation,” said Zéphirine; “but after receiving Amélie’s request in such a way, it is not likely she will give us a specimen.” “She ought to have them recited in justice to herself,” said Francis. “The little fellow’s genius is his sole justification.” “You have been in the diplomatic service,” said Amélie to M. du Châtelet, “go and manage it somehow.” “Nothing easier,” said the Baron.The Princess’ private secretary, used to petty manoeuvres, went to the Bishop and contrived to bring him forward. At the Bishop’s entreaty Naïs had no choice but to ask Lucien to recite his own verses for them, and the Baron received a languishing smile from Amélie as the reward of his prompt success. “Decidedly, the Baron is a very clever man,” she observed to Lolotte. But Amélie’s previous acid remark about women who made their own dresses rankled in Lolotte’s mind. “Since when have you begun to recognize the Emperor’s barons?” she asked, smiling.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lucien had proudly recited an ode, “TO HER!”, under Mme. de Bargeton’s protection, but the reception in the salon was chilly and calculating. His poem pictured an angel visiting earth, Love dazzled by light, and a luminous path marking the angel’s flight. Amélie coquetted; the Baron, judging taste, sneered it was a Christianized Ossian—full of swelling words and strained rhymes. Zéphirine called it obscure; Francis mocked the archangel’s armor as thin gauze. The women, hoping to be flattered like angels, pretended pleasure while remaining inwardly cold.Lolotte warned Adrien not to congratulate Lucien; Adrien obeyed. Zéphirine told Francis that love was a poem to be lived, not written; Stanislas agreed, flattering himself. Amélie, eager to humble Naïs, attacked Lucien’s humble origins—mocking his family’s trades—and the company joined in. Francis aimed a subtler blow by planting an idea with the Bishop: that Lucien’s mother, a modest woman of rare talents, supplied subjects for his verses and that he idolized her. Francis left the Bishop to make the intended allusion.Interest sharpened when the Bishop rejoined. Lucien, shy and unused to their manners, was embarrassed by being called alternately M. Chardon and M. de Rubempré. He misnamed M. de Senonches and provoked rude laughter and Mme. de Bargeton’s shame. Mme. de Bargeton philosophized about why women are drawn to greatness; the Marquise laughed and sided with Naïs. The Marquise and Marquis spoke lightly; Lolotte insolently asked Lucien if he worked quickly, as one asks a joiner about a box. Stung, Lucien found courage in Mme. de Bargeton’s defense: poetry did not grow in his head like grass in a courtyard. The Bishop, sanctifying poetry, laid a hand on Lucien’s head and called him a poet marked by Fate.Grateful, Lucien thanked the Bishop with a look—unaware the prelate would wound him next. When Lucien spoke of the poet’s suffering and the need for experience and deep feeling to make true characters, he hinted his work was only beginning. The Bishop’s joke—“Your excellent mother might assist you”—provoked delighted smiles of caste and M. de Bargeton’s laugh. Mme. de Bargeton retorted that a poet who turned to the Bible had a mother in the Church, mockingly challenging Lucien to declaim Saint John in Patmos or Belshazzar’s Feast; the women smiled at the Latin. Lucien faltered, sank, rose resolved to conquer this world, and prepared to declaim Saint John—but the card-tables called the company back. They returned to play, showing contempt, leaving Lucien and Mme. de Bargeton alone.Angoulême’s social world, conscious of its ignorance of poetry, clustered about the Pimentels and Rastignacs for guidance; the Marquis and Marquise’s verdict—“not bad for provincial poetry”—spread and became malicious. Music followed; duets and schoolroom songs filled the evening. Hurt by the company’s contempt, Mme. de Bargeton withdrew to her boudoir with the Bishop and Mlle. de Rastignac, who was enchanted by the verses. There, free of the salon’s eye, Mme. de Bargeton murmured that they had not understood him and repeated his lines with feeling. She urged Lucien to endure, insisting suffering is the price of immortality and that struggle gives the poet wings. She begged him, when he reached greatness, to remember those disinherited by fate, to sing of lives choked and unblossomed—allegories of souls that remember Heaven. The Bishop compared such a theme to the Song of Songs; Laure de Rastignac suggested it. The Vicar-General declared France’s sacred poem unwritten and prophesied success for the man who should write for religion. Mme. de Bargeton claimed the task for Lucien, seeing vision in his eyes.That quiet encouragement was short-lived. Amélie, Fifine, Adrien, and Francis reentered with Mme. de Rastignac to summon Naïs for music; they gleefully told the drawing-room M. de Rubempré would recite Saint John—“biblical” became mockery. Lucien excused a lapse of memory; when he reappeared, no one noticed—the poet’s halo had been plucked away. The landowners and pretentious set had no use for him; the women, jealous of Mme. de Bargeton, looked on with icy scorn.“So this is society!” Lucien thought as he walked the long way home to L’Houmeau, pacing to ease anger and muse on too-late answers. Far from disheartened, his ambition burned: like many who reach a higher sphere before belonging, he vowed any sacrifice to remain. As he strolled toward the Bordeaux road, he fancied Eve and David by the river and hurried to meet them.Eve had dressed simply; David, shy at her beauty, planned to speak of himself and their future. They walked across the Bridge of Saint Anne along the Charente until Eve broke the silence. David, thinking of Lucien, confessed anxiety: he feared Mme. de Bargeton would spoil Lucien, making him her plaything or, if indifferent, ruin him—Lucien loved pleasure and might not endure the privations required for great literary work. Eve calmly promised to work with her mother to support Lucien until success. Then David proposed marriage: he would marry Eve, give up his present life, and devote himself and any future fortune to supporting Lucien. He argued Lucien should not be a foreman nor live in L’Houmeau; together they could keep Lucien until he reached Paris.To justify this sacrifice, David revealed a plan born of his study of chemistry and the paper trade. Since the Empire’s fall, linen rags used for durable paper had grown scarce and dear, while cotton made soft, inferior sheets. If a cheap, durable paper could be made from a common vegetable fiber—like the Chinese use of bamboo—books could be far lighter and cheaper, widening the diffusion of knowledge. David sketched the paper-making process: rag-pickers gather rags; merchants sort them; mills wash and reduce them to pulp; the pulp is strained through a mould to set sheet size, then pressed and dried. European experiments toward substituting vegetable fibers and machine production had been slow; innovations such as web-paper and various inventors had begun exploring alternatives. David thought a local plant—reeds or other fibers—could be processed mechanically to replace costly linen rags. Though Chinese handwork gave a surface quality hard to imitate, an industrial solution could revolutionize European paper-making and thus literature’s reach.As David explained, Lucien arrived, distraught from his humiliation: the sneers, the Bishop’s epigram, the ridicule of his origins, and his fear Mme. de Bargeton might be both consolation and ruin. He believed she loved him as he loved her; he thought she would not abandon him. David and Eve, absorbed in their engagement and David’s plan, first did not grasp Lucien’s shock, but David’s warm description of a household—first floor for his wife, second for Lucien, rooms above the shed for Mme. Chardon—reassured him. The glow of aristocratic ambition faded; the burgher comforts and the brothers’ shared project appealed. They walked back together, reconciled, Lucien joining in the lovers’ joy.They returned late; Postel, uneasy, saw light in Eve’s room and asked Lucien if anything was wrong. Lucien said his mother had consented to Eve’s engagement; Postel slammed his window in despair that he had not earlier asked for Mlle. Chardon.David did not sleep in Angoulême; he walked overnight to his father’s house at Marsac. At sunrise, after brief domestic exchange, he declared his purpose: to marry Eve and asked his father’s consent and permission to add a second floor and rooms above the shed for their household. The practical vine-grower thought first of money, grumbled about taxes, and scoffed at book-learning. He consented to the marriage but had no money and assumed Eve must be rich. When David revealed she had nothing, the father proposed other, wealthier matches and railed at David’s education for leading him away from profitable advantage. He refused to fund building, though he would consent to the union and not press the rent; any improvements would be David’s responsibility.David left Marsac disappointed but resolved: he could not count on his father’s help; his plans must be achieved by his own means.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In Angoulême everyone talked about the Bishop’s epigram and Mme. de Bargeton’s witty retort; by evening the story had swelled into a triumph for Lucien. The rumor leaked into the bourgeois quarters: young men glanced after him, and phrases of admiration fed his vanity. Petit-Claud and others murmured that he was a lucky, good-looking fellow whom Mme. de Bargeton adored.Lucien, eager to see Louise, hoped that news of his sister’s forthcoming marriage would win him more favor after last night’s soirée. Mme. de Bargeton greeted him with passionate vehemence that overwhelmed the inexperienced lover: she yielded to his kisses, wiped his brow, called him a crowned poet, and declared she would make an oasis for him—busy and languid by turns—reminding him that he owed his laurels to her. Tears and raptures followed; Lucien kissed her hand, and the household—his mother, sister, David, and Louise—encouraged his self-regard. Compliments from friends and malice from enemies fixed him in an unreal world: a promising young man eager to believe flattering estimates.Louise, sweet and strategic, encouraged him but kept reserve. When Lucien told her that David and Eve were to be married, Louise brushed off any domestic scruples: lovers are each other’s whole family; the world outside did not matter. Her counsel—that he should be great and seek fame—made Lucien ecstatically happy. M. de Bargeton invited him to stay for dinner and read André Chénier, and under his and Louise’s coaxing Lucien lingered at the Hôtel de Bargeton. Cared for like a favored guest, he began to consider the luxuries he enjoyed as rightful for Lucien de Rubempré. Louise’s love and M. de Bargeton’s indulgence made him feel secure; he assumed a lordly air, tasted despotic sway, and liked being the town’s lion.Gossip turned his airs into apparent courtship. Amélie, with M. du Châtelet, and other jealous or envious observers concluded he had reached final terms with Mme. de Bargeton. They mocked the idea of a druggist’s son presuming to conquer a woman of Naïs’s rank, but some defended that love knows no class. By the next day every house in Angoulême discussed the intimacy between M. Chardon (Lucien) and Mme. de Bargeton. Though their real fault may have been a few kisses, society chose to believe scandal. Mme. de Bargeton paid for her social sovereignty: the town’s erratic judgments punished her more harshly than long toleration of other indiscretions.David had come with some savings from Paris, reserved to build an upper story on his father’s house and to marry Eve. He put up a timbered second floor for Lucien rather than strain the old walls, taking pleasure in making preparations for Eve’s household. The friends were blithely happy. Lucien, former printer’s foreman, now called himself M. de Rubempré, lodged sumptuously in contrast to his former attic, dined often with Mme. de Bargeton, and frequented the Bishop’s palace. He believed his name would be one of France’s great names, consoling himself for his meager stipend with the thought that his romance and verse—An Archer of Charles IX. and Marguerites—would yet bring fame and repay sacrifices.Eve and David deferred their wedding plans to accommodate Lucien: they painted rooms and bought furniture for him first. Lucien’s charm and gracious impatience made his cause so appealing that his friends and family centered comforts around him. This indulgence—common for youthful favorites—tends to ruin some, making them presume on a world predisposed to give to pleasant young things. Eve had meant to be pragmatic, but who could refuse a brother who appeared to long to sew for domestic life? David, sober and observant, watched Lucien with misgiving after his triumph; he feared Lucien would change. When he tested him, however, Lucien chose home pleasures over the great world, and David justified himself: “They will not spoil him for us!” They contrived modest country pleasures—picnics, walks, inexpensive dinners—and praised Lucien as the great man of Angoulême.The new home nearly complete, David went to Marsac to entreat his father to attend the wedding and perhaps contribute. But events in town then changed everything. Lucien and Louise had a spy in Châtelet, an envious man who watched with hatred and greed for scandal. Sixte—Châtelet—presented himself as Mme. de Bargeton’s humble admirer and confidant; he admired Lucien where expedient and disparaged him elsewhere. Naïs (Mme. de Bargeton) had gradually given him small attentions, for she no longer mistrusted her elderly suitor; but Châtelet had overreached. Love often flounders in provincial life: gestures are delayed, words substituted for action, and desire is forced into intellectual skirmishing. A system of petty espionage governs such towns—every house is transparent, every innocent act is ripe for scandal.Mme. de Bargeton’s situation was constrained by public life. She could not deny callers without arousing suspicion; doors stood open, servants came and went, the husband pervaded the house without suspecting any impropriety; visitors multiplied as curiosity increased. Any alteration in household routines would be treated as confession. To walk alone with Lucien would have been dangerous; to shut herself up with him would have been worse. Living so visibly, she could not satisfy a passion without exposing herself.Châtelet lay in wait. He watched Lucien into the de Bargeton house, followed with M. de Chandour, and contrived to feign neutrality while prompting others. He allied with jealous Amélie to lull suspicion in the pair and to manufacture a scene. Their polemics—arguments staged to draw attention—kept Mme. de Bargeton and Lucien in sight. Châtelet later claimed nothing suspicious was visible because the boudoir door stood open and servants came and went, but Stanislas vowed to cross the room on tiptoe the next day and, egged on by Amélie, did so.For Lucien, the next day he resolved to declare himself extravagantly: six months had elevated him in his own eyes to parity with Louise, and he intended to be her master. Mme. de Bargeton, however, resolved to baffle him. She valued herself as a sovereign, intending Lucien to win her by prowess; she wanted her love to be a reward for prolonged constancy and an incentive to his literary greatness. So when Lucien began vehemently, Louise responded in a lofty, didactic speech: do not sow regrets; my heart is already yours; the beloved should silence the clamor of the senses; if you cannot understand my elevated notion of love, you will never be worthy. Lucien, frantic, accused her of using words to avoid responding and flung himself at her feet, crying he had never loved anyone like her and begging proof that she was his.At that moment Stanislas came up unseen and found Lucien with his head on Louise’s knee—an appearance ripe for scandal. He turned on Châtelet, and the spies beat a precipitate retreat. Mme. de Bargeton, pale and trembling, learned that M. de Chandour and M. du Châtelet had been in the house; she told Lucien that if they had seen them as Stanislas said, she was ruined. Lucien, selfish in love, only exulted: “So much the better!”—a reply she received with a smile.In the provinces a story grows as it is told. M. de Chandour, elated, announced his part at the club and then house to house; Châtelet, claiming he had seen nothing, prodded Stanislas to gossip. Stanislas, thinking himself witty, embroidered the tale. The Angoulême nobility flocked to Amélie’s that evening, where exaggerated versions circulated among women who brandished accusations. Some expressed pity for Naïs yet agreed she had made herself ridiculous; others mocked the age difference. Châtelet defended her clumsily, doing more to stir than to quell rumor. Different voices created variations until the town buzzed.When the scandal seemed in full bloom, Châtelet went to Mme. de Bargeton with a professed offer of help. He suggested Stanislas must have been mistaken and urged Naïs not to let the dolt trifle with her life and honor. He presented himself as her friend and champion, ready to be her public defender; if necessary, he said, M. de Bargeton should press Stanislas for a formal retraction. He hinted that if the young man had behaved foolishly, a woman’s character ought not to hang on the word of a hare-brained boy. His obsequiousness allowed him to push Naïs toward Paris thoughts, and he begged her to dispose of him as she pleased.Naïs listened, weary of provincial life and drawn by the thought of Paris, but she did not disclose her intentions. Châtelet could not read the “high heart” folded in her silence.Later, after a whist game, Mme. de Bargeton spoke to her husband solemnly. She explained what had happened that morning: Lucien had thrown himself at her feet in passion, and Stanislas had come in just then. She described Stanislas’ claim that he had seen them in a compromising position, and she urged M. de Bargeton to go to Stanislas and demand satisfaction. She argued that public honor required a public retraction; that a gentleman should not vindicate his wife by fighting the offender, because that would be to publicize the scandal and the man defending her would himself be dishonored; but it would be right for M. de Bargeton to compel Stanislas to retract his insult publicly before witnesses. She planned to send Gentil to Escarbas to fetch her father as his second, and suggested pistols, for her husband was a good shot. Her aim was to restore respectability and reassert their standing.Touched and proud, M. de Bargeton took his hat and cane and set out that night for Stanislas’ house. Though inwardly anxious—worried what he should say—he found that, like many men of modest habitual reserve, he acted at crisis with a concise dignity that suited the occasion. He arrived at nine, saw Amélie and others gathered, and greeted them with a simple, ironic smile that suggested a grave purpose. The room fell silent, the pause before a storm; Châtelet looked from M. de Bargeton to Stanislas, who was accosted politely by the injured gentleman.Thus the scandal that springed from a few kisses and a momentary collapse of discretion had already mobilized gossip, jealousies, and schemes. Lucien’s vanity and Mme. de Bargeton’s open indulgence—combined with the petty malice of provincial society—made the pair vulnerable. Châtelet, eager to be her champion and in the bargain to profit by her fall, maneuvered to shape the narrative; others embellished for the sport of scandal. Mme. de Bargeton, refusing to renounce her dignity, insisted on public vindication; M. de Bargeton, though timid, accepted the role of a man of honor, and prepared to force the tongue of rumor into a public retraction. The impending confrontation at Stanislas’ house, with the town assembled, held consequences not only for reputations but for the fragile hopes Lucien and his friends had just begun to entertain.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Châtelet knew a late visit meant old M. de Bargeton would be in bed. Naïs had set that feeble arm in motion, and Châtelet, intimate with the household, took M. de Bargeton aside.“Do you wish to speak to Stanislas?”“Yes,” replied the old man, pleased to find a go‑between.“Very well; go into Amélie’s bedroom,” said the controller of excise, privately delighted at the prospect of a duel that might make Mme. de Bargeton a widow and cut off Lucien’s access to her. Châtelet then addressed M. de Chandour.“Stanislas,” he said, “Bargeton has come to call you to account, no doubt for the things you have been saying about Naïs. Go into your wife’s room and behave like gentlemen. Keep it quiet, be politely phlegmatic—act with British dignity.”A minute later Stanislas and Châtelet confronted Bargeton.Bargeton accused Stanislas of asserting he had found “Mme. de Bargeton and M. de Rubempré in an equivocal position.” Stanislas, flippant in tone, corrected the name and was not taken seriously. Bargeton, rehearsing his speech, demanded satisfaction. He announced his second, M. de Nègrepelisse, set pistols as the weapons, and named four o’clock the next morning for the duel. Stanislas, pale with conflicting shame and fear, yielded to the demand, hoping something would avert the meeting: “All right. Tomorrow morning.”They rejoined the drawing room where whispers spread and people sized up the participants. Some believed Stanislas’s pallor proof of deceit; others admired Bargeton’s dignity. Châtelet maintained a solemn air and lingered to reassure Stanislas and his wife that the younger man would have the advantage in a fight between ages. Amélie fainted and was taken upstairs. Bargeton, savoring his role, eventually left.Early next morning Angoulême soon echoed with news. Lucien sat at breakfast with David when Mme. Chardon arrived, frightened and breathless with gossip. A countryman had seen the duel from his cart: M. de Nègrepelisse had come to be Bargeton’s second; an officer lent service pistols and tested them; despite Châtelet’s opposition, the officer insisted on serviceable weapons. The seconds set twenty‑five paces. Bargeton fired first; the ball struck Chandour in the neck, who fell before he could return fire. The house surgeon predicted a permanent stiffness. Mme. Chardon warned Lucien to stay away from Angoulême, as friends of Chandour might call Lucien out.As she spoke, Gentil, Bargeton’s footman, arrived with a note for Lucien from Louise. She begged him to avoid the house that day, asking him not to show himself and urging him to spend the melancholy day with “your Beatrice,” who had been shaken by the event and had much to say. Lucien insisted Mme. de Bargeton was innocent.David, meanwhile, was engrossed in his own anxieties and satisfactions: his marriage was fixed for the day after tomorrow, and his first floor had been transformed into a fresh, white, bride‑like suite, furnished by the savings and efforts of mother and fiancée. Mme. Chardon and Eve had labored over linens, draperies, and even mattress piping—small vanities and homely comforts reflecting the couple’s love, but David had overspent. The cost exceeded his means; he had not enough for the planned house beyond the shed. He concealed his distress to spare Lucien, fearing his friend’s natural impulsiveness might collapse under the sacrifices.Lucien slipped away to Mme. de Bargeton’s. He found Naïs at table with her husband; Bargeton’s morning walk had sharpened his appetite, and he breakfasted with unaffected complacency after the duel. Lucien saw M. de Nègrepelisse, an old provincial noble who inspected him keenly. The father seemed to treat Lucien’s attachment as a flirtation; nevertheless, he gave Lucien an approving look.Louise left her father and husband and drew Lucien aside. Half glad and half melancholy, she told him she was going to Paris immediately; her father would take Bargeton back to his estate and keep him there during her absence. She planned to cultivate Mme. d’Espard (a Blamont‑Chauvry) — influential, connected to court — hoping such acquaintance could secure a place for Bargeton or otherwise elevate their prospects. If Bargeton could represent the Charente or receive some patronage, it would be a step toward the Paris life Louise craved: summers at the Escarbas, winters in Paris, surrounded by society worthy of a woman of quality.She then outlined to Lucien the glittering advantages Paris offered a young man of talent: salons where ministers, ambassadors, orators, and men of influence gathered; opportunities for sinecures or pensions; the way the monarchy rewarded letters and art. She urged him to follow her there at once, to put himself among great men, to keep his ideas from growing “rancid in the provinces” and to let his genius be seen. Mme. d’Espard’s salon, she promised, would give him introductions to those who could launch a career; the right patronage could secure distinction. She counselled a cautious strategy — being a religious, Royalist poet might be the way to court favor — and implored him to prepare for departure: “Would you rather not go?”To Lucien, who had always felt intellectually cramped in Angoulême, the suggestion was intoxicating. He imagined Paris as the Eldorado of provincial dreams, where publishers would greet genius with open purses and fame would follow instantly. He understood also that traveling with Louise would make their liaison irreversible; they would live together. Overcome, he embraced her passionately. But then he remembered with a pang his sister’s coming wedding — a domestic tie that represented his last duties at home. His outcry — “Great heavens! my sister is to be married the day after tomorrow!” — marked a last stir of boyish loyalty.The proud Nègrepelisse, appearing, rebuked Lucien with scorn: what had a tradespeople’s wedding to do with the “progress of our love”? He mocked Lucien’s supposed sentimental attachment to a wedding dinner and scolded him for wanting to be the best man over more exalted pleasures. Louise, wounded, fainted in the course of the argument. Lucien, contrite and desperate, cursed his family and clung to Louise, who, though she had been offended, forgave him but insisted he prove his resolve. She arranged for him to set out at midnight, to meet her on the road a short distance from Mansle, and to travel to Paris together.Lucien returned to David’s furnished rooms, his mind alight with ambition and misgivings. The riddle of finance loomed: where would the money come from? His departure would make the newly bought furnishings wasted, but perhaps his mother could take the rooms and relieve David. He made many convenient arguments to justify sudden flight. Desire, his guiding Jesuit, pressed him forward.He hurried to seek counsel from his sister and found the women in a painful position. Eve and Mme. Chardon had spent all their savings to furnish David’s first floor, and to prepare wedding clothes and linens. A ten‑thousand‑franc marriage‑settlement had been arranged for Eve, but the household’s ready money had been drained in doing honor to the match. Lucien proposed borrowing a thousand francs; Mme. Chardon promised to ask M. Postel for the loan.Eve, distressed, argued he ought not to leave for the wedding, begging him to delay his departure a few days; she feared the bad luck of him missing the wedding. She worried as well about Lucien’s slender wardrobe: only one good coat, two fine shirts, a handful of inadequate ties and waistcoats; Paris would necessitate new, costly clothes, forcing him to need more money. “Take two thousand francs,” she urged.Postel agreed to lend a thousand francs for six months but demanded a bill endorsed by David: Lucien offered no sufficient security. At that moment David, who had overheard, emerged quietly and, after a pause, offered to sign for the bill and to give Lucien the two thousand francs he would need. The Chardons felt shame for having assumed David’s resources, and David’s eyes were wet as he placed small wedding trinkets on the table he had recently bought for Eve. He declared he would put his name to the bill. Lucien, deeply moved and mindful of the sacrifice, embraced the young couple and implored them not to think ill of him. He professed that his destiny required entering Paris and that distance could not break their spiritual union.Behind the sentimental scene, practical anxiousness prevailed. David explained to Eve that if he endorsed the loan and surrendered two thousand francs, it would nearly exhaust his capital; half of his remaining funds were necessary to carry on the printing business. He argued that if it were only himself, he would be willing, but they were beginning married life and could not live in constant anxiety. Eve, distraught but resolute, told David to act as if he were alone: she would work to earn whatever was needed.Lucien prepared to depart with appalling haste. He took only a few garments and his manuscript — the waistcoat, the best overcoat, the famous coat, a couple of shirts — and arranged with David to send the rest by coach to a paper merchant in Paris to be collected there, so as to conceal his travel plans from Mme. de Bargeton. Mme. de Bargeton herself planned to leave Angoulême, and Châtelet, interested in exposing any impropriety, sent his man to Ruffec to watch every carriage change to learn whether Lucien accompanied her. Châtelet thought that if the poet went with her, he’d have proof of their intimacy.Lucien set out before dawn with David, who had hired a cabriolet and pretended business in Marsac to conceal their true journey. They spent part of the day with the old “bear” at Marsac, and then resumed their road. At dawn beyond Mansle they waited for Mme. de Bargeton. When the seventy‑year‑old carriage she commonly traveled in appeared, Lucien felt the fierce intensity of his decision. He sprang into David’s arms, and they watched Naïs’s carriage approach. David, whose heart was heavy with foreboding about Lucien’s prospects in Paris and his friend’s susceptibility to seduction and ruin, wished Lucien well with a prayer: “God grant that this may be for your good!” They parted as the carriage stopped and Mme. de Bargeton entered for the journey. David drove away, dread clutching him; he had terrible presentiments about Lucien’s fate in Paris.ADDENDUMTwo Poets is part one of a trilogy. The second part, A Distinguished Provincial at Paris, follows Lucien’s further adventures. Part three, Eve and David, continues their story. In some references parts one and three are combined under the title Lost Illusions.The following personages recurred elsewhere in Balzac’s Human Comedy: Bargeton, Mme. de; Cérizet; Mme. Chardon (née Rubempré); Châtelet, Sixte, Baron du; Châtelet, Marie‑Louise‑Anaïs de Nègrepelisse, Baronne du; Cointet, Boniface and Jean; Courtois and Mme. Courtois; Desplein; Gentil; Grozier, Abbé; Hautoy, Francis du; Maucombe, Comte de; Montriveau, General Marquis Armand de; Nègrepelisse, De; Petit‑Claud; Pimentel, Marquis and Marquise de; Postel; Prieur, Madame; Rastignac, Baron and Baronne de and their daughters; Rubempré, Lucien‑Chardon de; Séchard, Jérôme‑Nicolas; Séchard, David; Séchard, Mme. David; Senonches, Jacques de and his wife; Stanhope, Lady Esther.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MID STORY############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In late October 1800 a foreigner with a woman and a little girl stood a long while before the Tuileries, near the ruins where Catherine de’ Medici’s chateau once joined the Valois Louvre.The man stood with folded arms and bowed head, glancing between the consular palace and his wife, who sat on a stone. The woman, occupied with the nine- or ten-year-old child whose long black hair she toyed with, watched every look her companion gave. Their bond was more than love; misery—perhaps the strongest tie—stole their anxious attention.The stranger had a broad, serious head, thick hair streaked with white, noble features marred by hardness. Despite an erect, strong figure he looked over sixty. His worn clothes marked him as foreign. His wife’s faded face showed deep sadness, though she forced smiles when he looked. The little girl, sun-tanned and plainly Italian, had large black eyes and native grace. Passersby felt moved by this plain despair; when the stranger noticed attention he looked so savage that bold loungers hurried off.After a time the tall stranger wiped his face, drew a dagger from his breast, and gave it to his companion, saying in Italian, “I will see if the Bonapartes remember us.” He walked slowly to the palace entrance where a consular guard stopped him and refused to permit discussion. When the sentinel presented his bayonet, fortune favored the stranger: the guard was changing, and the corporal pointed to the post commander.“Let Bonaparte know that Bartolomeo di Piombo wishes to speak with him,” the Italian said to the captain.The officer insisted one could not see the First Consul without a written request. Bartolomeo persisted that the soldier must go to Bonaparte. The officer refused, citing regulations. Bartolomeo frowned, casting a terrible look as if charging him with the consequences of refusal. Then he remained under the portico. At that moment a carriage drew up and Lucien Bonaparte, minister of the interior, emerged.“Ah, Loucian, it is lucky for me I have met you!” cried the stranger in Corsican patois. Lucien recognized him and, after a word, took the Corsican away.Murat, Lannes, and Rapp were in the First Consul’s cabinet. As Lucien entered with Piombo, conversation ceased. Lucien took Napoleon by the arm and led him to a window recess. Napoleon signaled Murat and Lannes to retire; Rapp pretended not to see and lingered. Bonaparte opened the door suddenly and found his aide-de-camp close to the wall.“Do you choose not to understand me?” said the First Consul. “I wish to be alone with my compatriot.”“A Corsican!” replied the aide-de-camp. “I distrust those fellows too much to—”Bonaparte smiled and pushed him aside. “Well, what has brought you here, my poor Bartolomeo?” he asked.“To ask asylum and protection from you, if you are a true Corsican,” replied Bartolomeo roughly.“What ill fortune drove you from the island? You were the richest—”“I have killed all the Portas,” said the Corsican in a deep voice, frowning.The First Consul stepped back in surprise.“Do you mean to betray me?” cried Bartolomeo, with a dark look at Bonaparte. “Do you know that there are still four Piombos in Corsica?”Lucien seized his compatriot’s arm and shook it. “Did you come here to threaten the savior of France?” he said.Bonaparte signaled Lucien to be silent, then looked at Piombo. “Why did you kill the Portas?”“We had been friends,” the man replied. “The Barbantis reconciled us. The day after we drank to end our quarrels I left for Bastia. The Portas stayed in my house and set fire to my vineyard at Longone. They killed my son Gregorio. My daughter Ginevra and my wife had taken the sacrament that morning and escaped; the Virgin protected them. When I returned my house was ashes. I found Gregorio’s body by accident at night and knew the Portas had done it. I went to the woods, called together all the men who had ever served me—do you hear me, Bonaparte?—and we marched to the Portas’ vineyard. We arrived at five; at seven they were all before God. Giacomo says Eliza Vanni saved a child, Luigi. But I myself bound Luigi to his bed before setting fire to the house. I left the island with my wife and child without learning whether Luigi is alive.”Bonaparte listened with curiosity but not surprise. Lucien asked how many there had been.“Seven,” replied Piombo. “All of them persecuted you in olden times.”The brothers showed no hatred. “Ha! you are no longer Corsicans!” cried Piombo in despair. “Farewell. In other days I protected you,” he added reproachfully. “Without me your mother would never have reached Marseille,” he said to Bonaparte, who stood silent and thoughtful, his elbow on a mantel-shelf.“As a matter of duty, Piombo,” said Napoleon at last, “I cannot take you under my wing. I have become the leader of a great nation; I command the Republic; I am bound to execute the laws.”“Ha! ha!” said Bartolomeo scornfully.“But I can shut my eyes,” Bonaparte continued. “The tradition of the vendetta will long prevent law's reign in Corsica,” he added to himself. “But it must be destroyed, at any cost.”After a pause he spoke more personally. “Live here, in Paris,” he said to Bartolomeo. “We will know nothing of this affair. I will cause your property in Corsica to be bought to give you enough to live on for the present. Later, we will think of you. But remember, no more vendetta! There are no woods here to fly to. If you play with daggers you must expect no mercy. Here, the law protects all citizens; no one is allowed to do justice for himself.”“He has made himself the head of a singular nation,” said Bartolomeo, taking Lucien’s hand and pressing it. “But you have both recognized me in misfortune, and I am yours henceforth, for life or death. You may dispose as you will of the Piombos.”At these words his Corsican brow unbent and he looked pleasantly about him. “You are not badly off here,” he said, smiling, “You are all in red, like a cardinal.”“Your success depends on yourself; you can have a palace too,” Bonaparte replied, watching him keenly. “It will often happen that I shall need some faithful friend in whom I can confide.”A sigh of joy heaved the Corsican’s chest. He held out his hand to the First Consul. “The Corsican is in you still.”Bonaparte smiled and looked at the man who seemed to bring a breath from the island that had saved him—the land he would never see again. He signaled to his brother, who then took Piombo away. Lucien asked about the family’s finances. Piombo showed his wife and Ginevra sitting on a heap of stones.“We came from Fontainebleau on foot; we have not a single penny,” he said.Lucien gave him his purse and told him to come the next day so arrangements could be made for their comfort. The value of Piombo’s Corsican property, if sold, would scarcely maintain him honorably in Paris.Fifteen years elapsed between Piombo’s arrival in Paris with his family and the following event, which would be scarcely intelligible without this account of the preceding circumstances.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Servin, a respected painter, was the first in Paris to open a studio specifically for young ladies who wanted to learn painting. About forty, of strict morals and devoted to his art, he had married a general’s dowerless daughter and guarded the respectability of his classes carefully.
